The verse shows the theory meeting its hardest phonological datum head-on: even the acoustic differences that *matter to identity* are lodged in the dhvani, with the sphota borrowing them only in the loose way the moon borrows the water's trembling.

translation: But after the manifestation of the word, the modified sounds bring in the difference of speed of delivery; the sphota-self is not divided by them.
commentary: The vaikrta half. Once the word is manifested — urdhvam abhivyakteh, 'above' or after manifestation — a second class of sound-properties comes into play: the vaikrta dhvanayah, modified sounds, which carry vrtti-bheda, the differences of delivery (the tradition enumerates drutā, madhyamā, vilambitā — fast, medium, slow). These affect nothing criterial: the same word said quickly is the same word. The division of labour completed across 1.76-77 is elegant and does real explanatory work. Contrastive sound-properties (quantity, the phonemic pattern) belong to the prakrta dhvani and determine which sphota is manifested; non-contrastive ones (tempo, voice) belong to the vaikrta dhvani and determine only how long the manifesting takes. The sphota itself — tair na bhidyate — is divided by neither. A modern reader will recognize the shape of the phonemic/phonetic distinction, discovered here from the manifestation side.

translation: The sounds effect a preparation of the sense-organ alone, or of the word alone, or of both: these are the three positions among the proponents of manifestation.
commentary: Having committed to abhivyakti — the word is manifested, not produced — Bhartrhari must say what the sounds actually do, and he opens with a clean doxography. Manifestation requires that something be made ready: either the dhvanis prepare the *ear* (as collyrium prepares the eye, sharpening the receiver until the ever-present word registers), or they prepare the *word* (bringing the object itself into manifestable condition, as grinding releases fragrance), or both at once (as light readies both the eye it strikes and the pot it falls on). The three options exhaust the logical space once manifestation is analyzed as a relation between a receiver and an object, and the next two verses supply the perceptual precedents for each. The care taken here is anti-dogmatic: the sphota doctrine is not hostage to one mechanism, and Bhartrhari characteristically secures the doctrine by showing every available mechanism leads to it.

translation: There is preparation of the sense-organ alone by concentration, collyrium and the like; but preparation of the object for the apprehension of its smell.
commentary: Precedents for the first two positions, drawn from accepted perceptual lore. That the receiver alone can be prepared is shown by samadhana and anjana — the yogin's concentration and the doctor's eye-salve improve the eye while leaving the object untouched. That the object alone can be prepared is shown by scent: crushing sandalwood readies the wood's own gandha for apprehension while doing nothing to the nose. Both patterns are familiar, so neither samskara-model is ad hoc when applied to sound and word. The verse's method deserves notice: the manifestation-theorist is assembling an empirical taxonomy of how hidden things come to cognition, exactly as a naturalist would, and the word is being placed within a general theory of manifestation rather than treated as a unique mystery.

translation: If the eye acts by reaching its object, then by light the preparation of both — object and organ — is accepted; that is the pattern for sound.
commentary: The precedent for the third position, and the one the tradition tends to prefer for the word. On the prapyakaritva theory of vision — the sense reaches out to its object, a live doctrine among Naiyayikas and others — light plays a double role, readying the eye that operates by it and bathing the object that becomes visible in it. Sa kramo dhvaneh: that, says the verse, is the sounds' way — they condition the hearer's faculty and bring the word to manifestable condition at once. The double model fits the phenomenology of 1.84 (repetition ripens the hearer's grasp: receiver-side) and the criterial role of the prakrta pattern (the sounds determine which word appears: object-side), which is presumably why it stands last, in the position of the favoured view. Bhartrhari still does not legislate; the argument continues into 1.81 with the question of whether the dhvani is separately perceived at all.
contested: whether 1.80 endorses the both-samskara view or merely completes the doxography is not marked in the karika; its final position and the analogy's aptness suggest quiet preference, and we read it so

translation: By some, the apprehension of the sound is accepted as undivided from the form of the sphota; by others the sound is construed as never itself sensed, or as independent.
commentary: The question beneath the mechanism: what, in hearing, is actually *heard*? Three answers. On the first, the dhvani is grasped only aviBhagena, in non-separation from the sphota — we never hear bare sound, only word-clothed-in-sound, the two arriving as one phenomenal package (as one sees the lamp's light only in seeing the lit room). On the second, the dhvani is asamvedya, never itself an object of awareness at all — a pure vehicle, doing its manifesting work entirely offstage, inferred by theorists but absent from experience. On the third it is svatantra, independent — separately audible, as when one hears a voice too distant to make out words: sound without sphota, so sound in its own right. The phenomenological dispute matters because it fixes what the 'transfer' of 1.49 and 1.76 amounts to: only on the first and third views is there something *in experience* whose properties could be misattributed to the word. Bhartrhari again reports without sentencing; his own account of learning and error (1.85-86) will draw on the resources of all three.

mula_deva: आवृत्त्या न तु स ग्रन्थः प्रत्यावृत्ति निरूप्यते ॥ ८२ ॥
mula_iast: āvṛttyā na tu sa granthaḥ pratyāvṛtti nirūpyate || 82 ||
translation: As a Vedic lesson or a verse comes to be mastered through repetition, and yet that text is not discerned anew at each repetition —
commentary: First half of an argument-pair completed by 1.83-84, introducing the phenomenon that most embarrasses part-assembly theories: learning by repetition. A student masters an anuvaka by reciting it again and again; sodhatva, the being-mastered, dawns gradually across repetitions. Yet the text itself — sa granthah — is not a different object at each pass, na pratyavrtti nirupyate: the tenth recitation does not present a tenth text, but the same text, better possessed. So repetition improves the *grasp* while its object stays fixed — which is exactly the structure manifestation-theory attributes to hearing a word: the sounds' repeated strokes ripen apprehension of one unchanging sphota. The example is chosen from the tradition's daily pedagogy, where every reader has felt the process from inside.

translation: — so, by cognitions that cannot be separately reported, yet are conducive to the final grasp, the word's own form is ascertained once the sound has illuminated it.
commentary: The apodosis. The intermediate cognitions in learning — each pass through the verse, each stroke of sound — are anupakhyeya, not separately reportable: the student cannot say what exactly the seventh recitation added, and the hearer of a word cannot report the deliverance of each phoneme as it lands. But they are grahana-anuguna, conducive to the grasp; their work is real though silent, and it terminates in avadharana, the settled ascertainment of the word's svarupa. This gives the sphota theory its account of process without surrendering the unity of the object: the *ripening* is gradual and many-staged, the *ripened grasp* seizes one undivided thing. The phenomenology is precise — comprehension does in fact arrive as an all-at-once click after a subpersonal accumulation, not as a felt summation of parts — and the next verse states the mechanism in its classical form.

translation: The seed having been sown by the sounds, the word is ascertained in a cognition brought to ripeness by repetition, together with the final sound.
commentary: The classical statement of how the partless word is grasped through serial sounds — the verse later doxographers quote for the sphota theory's answer to its standing objection. Each dhvani deposits a bija, a seed or trace, in the hearer's buddhi; the sequence is a sowing, avrtta-paripaka, ripening through the repeated strokes; and antyena dhvanina saha, together with the last sound, the whole word is ascertained at once. The final phoneme is not a final part completing a sum — it is the occasion on which a cognition prepared by all the traces seizes the entire sphota. The seed-and-ripening vocabulary does deliberate double duty: it is agricultural, and it is the yogacara-adjacent idiom of traces in awareness, and both registers say the same thing — what accumulates is a readiness in the cognizer, not an assembly of the object. The Mimamsaka has a rival account (the last phoneme aided by memory-traces of the preceding ones conveys the meaning) that is formally parallel while dispensing with the sphota; the argument between the two schools turns on whether the *object* of the final cognition is a mere phoneme-series remembered, or a unity over and above it — and 1.85 gives Bhartrhari's phenomenological evidence for the latter.

translation: And when one thinks that the parts of the word exist in the interval, and that what is non-existent exists — that is the apprehender's incapacity; that, in fact, is the very means of the grasping.
commentary: The theory's boldest inversion: the appearance of phonemic parts is itself diagnosed as error — but productive error. In the antarala, the interval before the final ascertainment, the hearer takes part-cognitions to be cognitions of real parts of the word; this, says the verse, is asaktih, an incapacity of the apprehender, taking the non-existent as existent. And then the turn: sa grahanopaya eva sah — that very incapacity is the means of grasping. The mistake is not an obstacle the theory must explain away; it is the mechanism, the ladder the mind climbs. One cannot seize the undivided word directly, so cognition proceeds through a staged fiction of parts that ripens (1.84) into the true unitary grasp, whereupon the fiction has discharged its office. The structure — truth reached only through a scaffolding of error that the attained truth then retires — recurs at the grandest scale in this philosophy: the world's diversity is to Brahman what the phonemes are to the word. That parallel is not decoration; 1.86 immediately generalizes it.
contested: the double sah in grahanopaya eva sah is emphatic but syntactically loose; some construe 'that incapacity is precisely his means of grasping', others make the error-cognition itself the subject — the doctrine is unaffected but the sentence resists tidy parsing

translation: The imitation of differentiation by cognition, and the disturbance of speech, are constant; speech has a form contaminated by sequence, and cognition rests upon its objects.
commentary: The generalization: the productive error of 1.85 is not an occasional mishap but the permanent condition of mind and speech. Jnana imitates bheda — cognition, whose nature is undivided illumination, perpetually mimics the differentiation of what it grasps; vak suffers upaplava, disturbance, dhruvam, constantly — speech never appears except krama-upasrsta, contaminated by sequence. The two clauses are chiastic: cognition, undivided, borrows division from its objects; speech, whose essence (the sphota, ultimately the sabda-tattva of 1.1) is undivided, borrows division from its manifesting medium. Neither ever presents its true face in ordinary transaction. This is the kanda's epistemological low point, deliberately placed — if distortion is constant, how is truth ever touched? — and the answer has already been given in miniature: as in 1.85, the distortion is the upaya. The doctrine that ordinary cognition is irreducibly word-involved (and so sequence-involved, and so distortion-involved) returns in force at 1.123ff., where it becomes the master-thesis of the kanda.

translation: As the grasp of the first number is a means to the cognition of further numbers even though they differ, so is the hearing of one word a means to the cognition of another.
commentary: An analogy from arithmetic for a fact of linguistic learning: counting to one is how one learns to count at all. The grasp of adya-samkhya, the first number, is the upaya through which the numbers beyond it — genuinely different items — become cognizable; no one grasps 'seven' who has not first mastered the schema that 'one' inaugurates. So too sabdantara-sruti: hearing one word opens cognitive access to other words not yet heard. The point serves the manifestation theory at a specific joint. If words were learned one-by-one as brute associations, each would be an independent conquest; in fact linguistic competence is systematic — mastering some words confers a capacity for the rest, as mastering early numbers confers the number-series. A theory on which all words are variously-manifested configurations of one underlying word-nature (and ultimately of the one sabda-tattva) predicts this systematicity; a theory of atomistic pairings does not.
contested: the verse is compressed and its target unstated; we read it as an argument from systematicity of acquisition, but it can also be construed more narrowly, as explaining how the phoneme-schema learned in one word transfers to others — the arithmetic simile supports either

translation: The manifestors, distinct for each among phoneme, word and sentence — even though these differ absolutely, their powers appear as though commingled.
commentary: A precision about levels. The theory recognizes sphotas at three grains — varna, pada, vakya — and each has its own vyanjakas, its own manifesting sounds; and the manifested items are atyanta-bhinna, absolutely distinct: the sentence-sphota is not a sum of word-sphotas any more than the word was a sum of phonemes (1.73). Yet in the single stream of speech these manifesting powers appear sankirna iva, as if commingled — one and the same stretch of sound seems simultaneously to be manifesting phonemes, words and the sentence, and the hearer cannot assign portions of the acoustic flow to levels. The iva is again load-bearing: the commingling is apparent, an artifact of one medium serving three manifestations at once. The verse matters structurally because it prevents a regress-objection — 'if the word is partless, what are the phoneme-sphotas doing in it?' Answer: nothing *in* it; they are distinct manifestables sharing manifestors, and hierarchy among sphotas is a hierarchy of alternative unities, not of containment. The second kanda's argument that the sentence is the true unit stands on exactly this ground.
contested: the wisdomlib transcription prefixes this karika's first line with the sequence 'rdhnyam a-', which yields no construable Sanskrit and is evidently a transcription artifact; we translate the evident text (pratyekam vyanjaka bhinnah...) and note the corruption rather than silently repairing the source

translation: Just as, by its earlier perceivings — from afar, or in deep gloom — sight makes the object other than it is and determines it quite otherwise,
commentary: Protasis of a pair completed in 1.90, supplying the perceptual model for staged misapprehension. A figure approaches from a distance or looms in darkness: sight does not withhold judgement until conditions improve — it delivers a series of determinate but wrong verdicts (a post, then a man, then finally the traveller it is), each earlier darsana anyathakrtya, making the object otherwise, and adhyavasyati, determining it with full assertoric confidence. The progression to truth passes through committed error, not through cautious suspension. The example is the stock-in-trade of Indian error-theory (the post/man, the shell/silver), but its use here is distinctive: where the epistemologists deploy it to classify illusion, Bhartrhari deploys it to show that serial correction is a *normal mode of successful cognition* — the endpoint is veridical, and the errors were its road. The application to sentence-hearing follows immediately.

translation: — so, while the sentence is being manifested by the causes of its manifestation, cognition first proceeds in the form of a seizing of parts.
commentary: The apodosis, and the sentence-level restatement of 1.85. As the sounds do their manifesting work, the hearer's buddhi runs ahead in bhaga-avagraha-rupa, the form of part-seizure — it takes words, phrases, apparent constituents, as so many determinate grasps, exactly as the distant-viewer takes the post and then the man. These part-graspings are the anyatha-determinations of 1.89: committed, useful, and destined to be superseded when the vakya-sphota is seized whole. The two verses together complete the theory's phenomenology of comprehension at sentence scale: hearing is a controlled hallucination of structure that converges on an undivided meaning-whole, the intermediate 'words' having the same standing in the final grasp that the intermediate 'man at a distance' has once the friend is recognized — scaffolding, retrospectively cancelled but prospectively indispensable. The second kanda will inherit this result as its starting point.

translation: As there is a fixed order of succession in the transformation of milk or of a seed, so is the sequence in the cognitions of comprehenders fixed.
commentary: A determinism about the stages. Milk does not become cheese except through curd; the seed reaches ear-of-grain through sprout and stalk, aniyama excluded — natural transformation has anupurvi-niyama, fixed serial order. So too the hearer's cognitions: the part-seizures of 1.90 are not a random walk that happens to end at the whole but a niyata krama, a law-governed ripening (the paripaka of 1.84 now given its natural-kind analogues). The point defends the theory against a charge of arbitrariness — if the intermediate graspings are errors, why do all competent hearers commit the *same* ones, in the same order, and converge on the same whole? Because manifestation is a causal process with the same fixity as any other maturation: same sounds, same prepared faculty, same staged path. Error with lawlike trajectory is process, not accident. The organic imagery also quietly reasserts the egg of 1.51: language-understanding belongs with growth, not with construction.

translation: Among those very units, if they have parts, the difference of form arises from the sequence of the sound; if they are partless, then the construing of a difference of parts is a device.
commentary: A dilemma posed and both horns absorbed. Take the units — phoneme, word, sentence (tesv eva, those of 1.88). Either they have parts or they do not. If bhagavat, then their apparent internal form-differences (the 'g' seeming to precede the 'au' in gauh) arise dhvaneh kramat, from the sound's sequence — the ordering belongs to the manifesting stream, projected onto the unit (1.49, 1.76). If nirbhaga, partless, then bhaga-bheda-prakalpanam, the very positing of parts, is abhyupaya — a device, the analyst's and the hearer's working fiction (1.85's upaya, now named as method). Either way, no real internal sequence belongs to the linguistic unit itself: the first horn locates sequence in the medium, the second concedes it only as fabrication. This is the argumentative pincer that lets Bhartrhari be serene about the part-whole antinomy of 1.72-74 — the dispute over which decomposition is 'real' loses its object once both decompositions are seen to be projections, differently sourced, onto undivided manifestables.

translation: The sphota is held by tradition to be the universal, manifested by its several individuals; by some, its individuals are construed as the sounds.
commentary: A rival systematization from within the school, reported with its refinement. On this view the sphota is a jati, a universal: gauh-said-by-Devadatta and gauh-said-by-Yajnadatta are vyaktis, individual instances, and the word itself is the type they instantiate — anekavyakti-abhivyangya, manifested by its many particulars, as cowhood is by cows. The refinement (kaiscit): the vyaktis in question just *are* the dhvanis, so that the sound-word relation becomes a special case of the particular-universal relation, and manifestation of the sphota by sounds becomes the familiar manifestation of a jati by its instances. The economy is attractive — one metaphysical relation instead of two — and this jati-sphota reading became a standing position in the later school (with vyakti-sphota views opposed to it). Bhartrhari records it as smrta, received doctrine, without making it his own statement; his own emphasis throughout has been on the sphota as *undivided meaning-bearer*, a functional characterization neutral between type-theoretic construals. The next two verses take up what the jati-reading must answer: how eternal universals can have transient, law-governed manifestation.
contested: whether 1.93 reports a view Bhartrhari endorses, tolerates, or merely catalogues is a classical crux; smrta suggests received authority, but the kaiscit of the second line marks at least the refinement as partisan, and we read the whole as doxography

translation: Of the unchanging word, the sound — modified by causes — comes to be the condition for apprehension, like a light.
commentary: The manifestation relation restated with its cleanest analogy, in answer to the standing worry: how can the changing reveal the changeless without infecting it? The dhvani is vikrta nimittaih — modified by its causes: the speaker's organs, effort, pace — while the sabda is avikara, unchanging. The sound's role is nimittatvam upalabdhau, being the condition of apprehension: prakasavat, like a lamp. A lamp may be bright or dim, steady or guttering; these vicissitudes affect how well the pot is seen, perhaps whether it is seen — they do not put flicker into the pot. So the sound's modifications condition the apprehending (speed, clarity, audibility) without transferring change to the word apprehended; where transfer *appears* to occur, 1.49 and 1.76 have already diagnosed it as figurative. The lamp analogy also silently answers 1.78's mechanism question in favour of the both-sides model of 1.80: light conditions eye and object at once. That the light-comparison recurs at every load-bearing joint of the kanda (1.46, 1.55, here) reflects its unique fitness: illumination is the one ordinary relation in which a mediator reveals without constituting.

translation: Nor is manifestation settled by rule only for non-eternal things; manifestation of the eternal universals by their substrates is likewise accepted.
commentary: An objection dissolved by precedent. The objector holds that abhivyakti is at home only among anitya things — the pot manifested by the lamp is itself a transient item, and manifestation-relations, being causal and dated, seem to belong to the world of the produced; how can the *eternal* be manifested without being dragged into time? The reply is that the objector's own ontology already contains the counterexample: universals, nitya by everyone's admission, are manifested (vyaktir isyate) by their asrayas, the substrates in which they inhere — cowhood becomes apprehensible exactly where a cow stands, though cowhood neither arrives nor departs. Dated, located manifestation of the undated is thus a relation the realist schools (Nyaya-Vaisesika above all) are committed to independently. The sphota theorist claims nothing stranger: sounds stand to the eternal word as the cow stands to cowhood — occasioning its apprehension here and now without lending it their transience. The argument is dialectically tidy: it does not prove the word eternal, it removes the alleged incoherence in saying so.

translation: Connection with place and the rest is observed even for embodied things; even under the alternative of difference of place, there is no difference between sound and word.
commentary: The companion objection: manifestation seems to localize the word — the utterance happens *here*, so is the eternal word here rather than there? The first line defuses the assumption that location-talk tracks real division: even kayavat things, bodies, stand in place-connections that multiply without multiplying them (one body, many successive places; one fire, many hearths — the traditional illustrations run this way). The second line applies it: under desa-bheda-vikalpa, the alternative that utterances occur in different places, there is still no bheda between dhvani and sabda beyond what the theory already grants — the sounds are many and located; the word they manifest is not parcelled among the locations. As with time in 1.76, so with place: spatial predicates attach to the word upacarena, by transfer from its manifestors. The pair 1.95-96 thus closes the classical trio of worries — change, time, place — and with them the manifestation doctrine's defensive perimeter is complete.
contested: the illustrative force of kayavatam api is compressed; we take it as 'even bodies multiply place-connections without internal division', but some construe it as conceding location to embodied things precisely to contrast the word's non-location — the verse's conclusion is the same on either construal

translation: As the fitness between an apprehension and its object is established and fixed, just so is that between sphota and sounds, in the relation of manifested and manifestor.
commentary: The relation itself, finally named as primitive. Why do *these* sounds manifest *this* word? The answer offered is yogyata, fitness — the same fixed, unexplained aptitude that pairs sight with colour and never with sound. No one asks in virtue of what the eye is fit for the visible; that fitness is siddha and niyata, established and fixed, a bedrock of the cognitive order. The vyanya-vyanjaka relation between word and its sounds has, Bhartrhari claims, the same standing: constitutive, regular, not further derivable. The move is philosophically self-aware — every theory must stop somewhere, and stopping at a fitness already admitted everywhere in epistemology is cheaper than inventing new machinery. It also quietly explains the *rule-boundness* of manifestation (only the right phoneme-pattern manifests gauh) without conceding that the word is assembled from that pattern: fit, not composition.

translation: And in ordinary experience, the illuminating condition even of things similarly apprehended — smells and the rest — is fixed, standing distinct for each substance.
commentary: Support for 1.97 from the economy of perception. Smells resemble one another; yet the nimitta that releases each — the crushing of this sandal, the burning of that resin — is niyata and pratidravyam avasthita, fixed and lodged substance by substance. Similar manifestables, dedicated manifestors: nature is full of such assignments, and no one finds them mysterious enough to demand a compositional analysis (nobody thinks the fragrance is *made of* crushings). So likewise similar words each have their own dedicated sound-patterns, and the dedication is no argument that the pattern constitutes the word. The example continues the naturalist's method of 1.79-80: the word-sound relation is being normalized, placed in a family of manifestation-relations the opponent already accepts, so that scepticism about it becomes scepticism about perception at large.

translation: And the illuminated object conforms to the differences of its illuminators — that is directly seen in a reflection when the oil, water and the like differ.
commentary: The transfer-doctrine of 1.49 receives its everyday proof. One face, reflected in water, in oil, in a sword-blade: the pratibimba differs — dim, dark, elongated — as the medium differs, and the differences are patently the medium's contribution, since the face has not changed. Prakasya anuvartate prakasakanam bhedan: the revealed conforms to its revealers. Pratyaksam: this is not speculation but something seen. Applied: the word heard through a child's voice, a shout, a drawl 'conforms' likewise — its apparent duration, pitch and colour are the sounds' vicissitudes worn borrowed. The verse arms the theory with a lived, checkable case where everyone already distinguishes the thing from its medium-conditioned appearance, and 1.100 will push the same example to its limit: media can even misrepresent *size*.

translation: In mirrors of contrary measure — a gem-facet, a blade-surface and the like — there is no possibility of entities really of like form with the mountain reflected.
commentary: The reductio that secures 1.99. A mountain shows in the facet of a jewel. If reflections were real duplicates — entities sarupa with their originals — the facet would have to contain a mountain-sized thing, which is absurd: viruddha-parimana, the measures contradict. So a reflection is not a second entity resembling the first; it is the original *as appearing under* an alien condition, and its size-in-the-mirror is a fact about the mirroring, not about anything's real dimensions. Applied to the word: the sphota 'in' a quick utterance is not a short word-duplicate, nor a long one in slow speech — there are no duplicates at all, only the one word under different manifesting conditions (1.75-77). The mirror-mountain is the strongest of the reflection-series because it makes quantitative transfer patently unreal, and duration was exactly the quantitative property whose transfer to the word (1.76) most needed the demonstration.

translation: Therefore, for phonemes, sentences, words and the rest, whose time is undivided, the time of functioning and their own time are distinguished according to the difference of the sound.
commentary: Tasmat — the conclusion of the whole movement since 1.75. All linguistic units, at every grain, are abhinna-kala, temporally undivided in themselves; the felt durations belong to nada. Hence a final clean distinction: vrtti-kala, the time of functioning — how long the manifesting takes, a real property of the sound-process — versus sva-kala, the unit's 'own time', which exists only as the figurative shadow the vrtti casts (the upacara of 1.76). Long and short vowels, quick and slow recitations, differ in vrtti-kala; no sphota differs from any other in sva-kala, since none has any. The verse closes the temporal dossier with the theory in equilibrium: sequence and duration fully acknowledged as data, fully assigned to the medium, the meaning-bearer standing outside time as 1.48 required. What remains for the kanda is doxography (1.102-113), the cosmological deepening (1.114-118), and the great theses on knowledge and word (1.123ff.).

section: Rival Accounts of Sound and Word
translation: What is generated by the organs through conjunction and disjunction — that is the sphota; the sounds born from that word are what others call the dhvanis.
commentary: A terminological about-face, reported deadpan: for *some* theorists (the tradition identifies phonetician-grammarians whose view survives here and in the Mahabhasya's margins), 'sphota' names the *first-produced sound* — the one generated by the articulators' contacts and releases (samyoga-vibhaga, the standard physics of sound-production) — while 'dhvani' names the sabda-ja sabdah, the sounds born *from* that sound: the spreading, echoing continuum that carries it to distant ears. On this usage the sphota is itself acoustic and produced; what Bhartrhari has been calling sphota — the partless meaning-bearer — is not in this picture at all. He reports the usage without protest because half of his own apparatus survives translation into it: the first-sound fixes identity (prakrta), the propagated train varies freely (vaikrta, as 1.103 will say in this idiom). But the metaphysical core does not survive, and the sequence 1.102-113 is precisely a tour of what theories look like when the undivided word is left out.
contested: whose view 1.102 reports is uncertain; candidates run from early phoneticians to the audumbarayana-line of speculation, and the karika's anonymous 'anyaih' leaves it open — what is clear is that the terminology here inverts Bhartrhari's own

mula_deva: अल्पे महति वा शब्दे स्फोटकालो न भिद्यते ।
mula_iast: alpe mahati vā śabde sphoṭakālo na bhidyate |
translation: Whether the word is small or great, the time of the sphota does not differ; but the subsequent train of sounds has the nature of increase and decrease.
commentary: The produced-sphota theory of 1.102 developing its own version of the invariance thesis. Even with the sphota reduced to the first acoustic event, a distinction imposes itself: that first event's time 'does not differ' with loudness — alpa or mahat, whispered or bellowed, the initial articulated form is what it is — while the para sabda-santana, the after-train, waxes and wanes (pracaya-apacaya) with the force of utterance, which is why a shout carries and a whisper dies. Loudness, on this analysis, is a property of propagation, not of articulation. Bhartrhari's interest in reporting it is transparent: even theorists who deny the transcendent word are driven to split the invariant from the variable within speech — the structure of prakrta versus vaikrta (1.76-77) reappears inside pure acoustics. The rival theory thus testifies, despite itself, that no account of language survives without an invariance-core; the schools differ only over how deep the invariant lies.
contested: sphota-kala here must mean the duration of the initially-produced sound if 1.102's terminology is still in force, and we read the verse as continuing that reported view; a reader who takes 1.103 as Bhartrhari's own voice must instead take it as restating 1.75-76, which the 'para santana' clause fits less well

mula_deva: दूरात् प्रभेव दीपस्य ध्वनिमात्रं तु लक्ष्यते ।
घण्टादीनां च शब्देषु व्यक्तो भेदः स दृश्यते ॥ १०४ ॥
mula_iast: dūrāt prabheva dīpasya dhvanimātraṃ tu lakṣyate |
ghaṇṭādīnāṃ ca śabdeṣu vyakto bhedaḥ sa dṛśyate || 104 ||
translation: From afar, only the sound is discerned, like the glow of a lamp; and in the notes of bells and the like, that difference is plainly seen.
commentary: Empirical vindication of the sphota-dhvani split from two everyday observations. First: a voice heard across a valley — one hears *that* someone speaks without hearing *what*: dhvani-matram, bare sound, reaching the ear while the word does not, exactly as a lamp's prabha announces the lamp from a distance at which the flame itself cannot be made out. The manifestor, then, is separable in experience from what it manifests — settling against 1.81's second position (that the dhvani is never itself sensed). Second: the bell. Struck once, it gives its note and then the long after-hum; hearers effortlessly distinguish the strike-sound from its lingering resonance — vyakto bhedah, the difference is patent. That is the alpa/mahat train of 1.103 made audible in a single event: initial form, then propagating tail. Both observations are chosen for being no one's theory: they are data any account must accommodate, and they already contain the distinction the sphota-theorist needs.
contested: which distinction the bell 'plainly shows' — strike versus resonance (our reading), or the sphota/dhvani distinction proper carried into non-linguistic sound — turns on how far 1.102's reported terminology still governs; the example works either way, which may be why it was chosen

translation: From the striking of the substance, in the amplification even the long and prolated arise distinct; but the sounds born when the vibration has ceased are the differentiators of delivery.
commentary: The physics pressed one step further, to apportion vowel-quantity itself. Dravya-abhighata, the striking of the sounding body — articulators on air, hammer on bell — yields the pracaya, the amplified initial sound, and within *that*, dirgha and pluta arise as distinct: quantity, being criterial (1.76), belongs to the primary production. But the nadas born kampe uparate, once the vibration has ceased — the after-resonances, the bell's hum, the voice's trail — are vrtter visesakah, differentiators only of delivery: they mark how the utterance decays, never which word it was. The verse thus locks the earlier division (1.76-77: prakrta bears contrast, vaikrta bears style) onto an explicit acoustic mechanism: contrastive properties ride the driven phase of the sound, stylistic ones ride its free decay. As physics of vibration this is remarkably observant — driven versus decaying oscillation is a real and fundamental distinction — and as linguistics it completes the assignment of every temporal property of speech to a definite layer of the medium.
contested: pracitau is difficult — we take it as locative of pracaya, 'in the amplification', following 1.103's vocabulary, but it has been construed as dual ('the two accumulated ones') with dirgha-pluta, changing the syntax though not the doctrine

translation: Even while the vibration of the organ persists unsettled, further sounds are generated from the sphota itself, as flame from another flame.
commentary: The propagation mechanism, with a simile that repays pressing. While the karana still trembles — the articulation not yet settled — apare dhvanayah, further sounds, arise sphotad eva: from the first-produced sound itself (1.102's usage), not from fresh strokes of the organ. Each ring of sound begets the next outward ring, jvala jvalantarad iva, as one flame kindles another — the torch passed, not the torch stretched. The model is serial regeneration: sound crosses space by continually re-producing itself in the medium, each phase the cause of its successor. As acoustics, this is the wave-picture in the only idiom available — local excitation propagating by self-replication, the sounding source needed only to seed it. Doctrinally the verse explains how the word reaches far-off hearers without the original articulation reaching them: what arrives is a great-grandchild of the first sound, faithful in form (flame lights flame without loss of fire-nature), degraded only in the free properties — which is why the distant hearer of 1.104 gets dhvani-matra, the form's carrier worn thin.
contested: sphotad eva here reads naturally only under 1.102's reported terminology (sphota = first-produced sound); a reader taking sphota in Bhartrhari's own sense must treat the generation as manifestation loosely phrased, and the flame simile fits production better than manifestation — evidence that 1.102-106 form one reported block

translation: By some, it is air, by some the atoms, by some cognition that is held to attain the condition of the word; for the divergence of views among the doctrines is unsettled.
commentary: The doxographic frame widens to first principles: what *is* the stuff that 'becomes' word? Three candidate substrates, each with its school: vayu, air — the phonetician's answer, speech as wind shaped (1.108-109 will unfold it); anavah, atoms — an atomist refinement in which sound-particles accumulate (1.110-111); and jnana, cognition — the idealist answer, word as a modification of awareness itself (1.112-113, where Bhartrhari's own sympupathies will surface). The second line is a shrug with method in it: darsana-bhedo hi pravadesv anavasthitah — the divergence among doctrines settles nowhere. Bhartrhari's tolerance is again strategic. His own doctrine lives at a level these disputes do not touch: whatever the substrate-story, the manifested word's undividedness, its transfer-properties, its yogyata with sounds (1.97) stand unaffected — exactly as 1.28 declined the nitya/karya fight and 1.74 the part/whole fight. The catalogue that follows is thus not indecision but a demonstration that the sphota doctrine is compatible with every physics on offer, and hostage to none.

translation: (108) Air, set in motion by the effort that follows the speaker's wish, striking upon the places of articulation, attains the condition of the word. (109) Through the potency of its cause, possessing the properties of velocity and accumulation, it divides by its impact even bodies that possess solidity.
commentary: The vayu doctrine given its full statement, and it is a mechanist's credo. The causal chain runs unbroken from mind to matter: iccha, the wish; prayatna anuvartin, effort conforming to it; air labdha-kriya, endowed with motion; abhihata sthanesu, striking the eight classical places of articulation — and the shaped collision *is* the word: sabdatvam pratipadyate, air attains wordhood. Nothing non-physical intervenes between intention and utterance except intention itself. 109 then insists the agent is robust enough for the role: this moving air carries vega and pracaya, velocity and mass-accumulation, from the samarthya of its cause, and at full sannipata it splits even solid bodies — the gale that breaks trees is the same stuff that, finely governed, becomes a vowel. The argument-by-escalation answers the obvious sneer (how can thin breath be the mighty word?) with observed pneumatic violence. Bhartrhari reports the view fairly and without adoption; its inadequacy for him lies not in the physics but in what the physics cannot reach — air shaped at the lips has still to be *grasped as one word* by a hearer, and the whole dossier of 1.82-92 waits on the far side of any collision.
contested: sāravatyo 'pi mūrtayah — 'even solid bodies' — is our construal of saravat as 'possessing pith/solidity'; the line has also been read as 'even the solid forms (of the articulators) are divided', making the wind shape itself by cutting against the organs, which fits the phonetic context more closely though it strains vibhajyante

translation: (110) The atoms, because they possess all powers, functioning by division and by combination, transform themselves as shadow, light, darkness and word. (111) When their own power is being manifested, impelled by effort, the atoms called 'word' accumulate like clouds.
commentary: The atomist refinement, stated in two strokes. Where the vayu theory made word a *state* of one continuous stuff, this view grinds the substrate finer: anavah, atoms, sarva-saktitvat — omnipotent in the small, each carrying every power — take on word-being as one parinama among siblings: chaya, atapa, tamas, sabda. The company the word keeps in that list is deliberate and strange: shadow, glare, darkness — the quasi-substances of Indian physics, borderline entities that are somehow *there* without ordinary thinghood. Word belongs, for this school, to precisely that twilight ontology. 111 gives the dynamics: under prayatna the sabda-akhyah paramanavah — 'the atoms called word', a name conceding that wordhood is a functional designation, not a natural kind — pracyante abhranive, mass like clouds: scattered potency condensing into audible weather. The cloud-simile earns its place: clouds are aggregates with edges only from a distance, form without unity — which is exactly the standing this theory can give the word, and exactly why it cannot be Bhartrhari's: a cloud of word-atoms has no principled oneness for the hearer's single grasp (1.84) to seize. The purvapaksa is thus allowed to paint its own limitation.
contested: bheda-samsarga-vrttayah we take as 'functioning by division and combination' (the atoms' two modes); it has also been construed as 'whose functioning involves difference-and-contact with one another', a physics of collision — the cosmology is unaffected

translation: (112) Now this inner knower, standing in the subtle self-of-speech, turns itself, for the manifestation of its own form, into wordhood. (113) Attaining the condition of mind, ripened by the inner fire, it enters the breath-wind; and then it is uttered forth.
commentary: The third doctrine, and the temperature changes: the anonymous reportage of 1.108-111 gives way to vocabulary that is Bhartrhari's own. The subject now is the antarah jnata, the inner knower, already standing suksme vagatmani, in a subtle speech-self — language is not something the knower produces but a dimension it inhabits — and the verb is vivartate (112d): it *appears as* wordhood, the same appearance-without-transformation that 1.1 predicated of Brahman and 1.48 of the sphota. And the purpose: vyaktaye svasya rupasya, for the manifestation of its own form — speech as the self's self-disclosure, not as signal-engineering. 113 then supplies what neither rival could: a descent-path connecting this metaphysics to their physics. The word-nature attains mano-bhava (mind-condition), is ripened tejasa — by the inner heat, the old physiological fire of the Upanisads — enters prana, and is samudirya, uttered forth. Mind, fire, breath: the idealist keeps the whole pneumatic chain of 1.108 as the *last* stage of a process that begins in awareness. The three-stage descent — knower's subtle speech, mental word, breath-borne utterance — is the embryo of the pasyanti/madhyama/vaikhari hierarchy made explicit at 1.142, and from here to the kanda's end the reported view and the author's voice are one.
contested: whether 1.112-113 still belong to the doxography of 1.107 (as the 'jnana' candidate) or mark Bhartrhari's own resumption is a real question; the vivarta vocabulary and the seamless continuation into 1.114-118 argue for the latter, and we read the passage as the author speaking through the third position — the doxography ending by dissolving into the doctrine

translation: The breath, having come to rest upon the principle of the inner organ, pervaded by that principle's properties, appears — by the agency of the fire itself.
commentary: The descent of 1.113 examined at its middle joint, where mind and wind interlock. Prana, rising to carry the word out, is not raw air by the time it sounds: it has gone asrayatam, taken the antahkarana-tattva as its resort, and is tad-dharmena samavista — suffused, occupied, by the inner organ's properties. The breath that reaches the lips is mind-conditioned matter, wind already carrying form. And the connective is again tejasa eva vivartate: through the ripening fire, it *appears* — the vivarta verb now applied to the physiological process itself, so that even the pneumatic stage of speech is appearance-of-the-inner rather than fresh production. The verse answers, in passing, the question the vayu-doctrine of 1.108 could not: *why* shaped breath means anything. Because what shapes it from within is the mind's own structure riding down; the wind is expressive because it is invested, not because collisions are eloquent. The mechanist's whole chain is retained and re-read as the outermost garment of self-manifestation.
contested: tejasaiva vivartate is elliptical — we supply 'appears (as utterance) through the fire', continuing 1.113's cooking-fire; others read tejas here as the visual/luminous element into which breath transforms, making the verse a piece of element-physics; the vivarta verb tells against mere element-transformation

translation: Dividing the knots of itself by the differentiated forms of audible sound, the breath, having manifested the phonemes, dissolves into those very phonemes.
commentary: The last event of utterance, rendered with a violence and a pathos the doctrine has earned. The breath ascends carrying the undivided word; at the places of articulation it is svatmano granthin vibhajan — dividing the knots of itself. The knots (granthi, the Vrtti's own word for the involutions of 1.1) are the word's condensed unity as lodged in the moving prana; articulation unties them into sruti-rupaih prthagvidhaih, the differentiated audible forms. Then the extraordinary final clause: the breath, varnan abhivyajya, having manifested the phonemes, varnesv eva upaliyate — dissolves into those very phonemes. The carrier does not survive its delivery; prana spends itself utterly into what it manifests, as if the messenger became the message's letters. This is the production-side answer to 1.54's hearing-side vanishing (words unnoticed, meaning taken): at both ends of speech, the medium consummates itself by disappearing into what it serves. Cosmologically the verse is the microcosm of dissolution-in-manifestation — the one, descending into difference, is wholly *in* the difference, nothing held back — which is why the next verse must immediately insist that the source itself is nonetheless never exhausted.
contested: upaliyate admits both 'dissolves into' (our reading — the breath is used up in the phonemes) and 'merges/hides within' (the breath persists concealed in them); the first suits the physiology, the second the metaphysics of latency, and the word may be chosen to carry both

translation: The word whose functioning is unceasing is not apprehended because of its subtlety; it is made manifest through its own occasioning cause, as air is by a fan.
commentary: The insistence 1.115 required: behind each spent utterance stands an unspendable source. The sabda meant here is ajasra-vrtti, of unceasing operation — the inner word-nature of 1.112, never silent, only suksma, too subtle for apprehension. Its non-appearance is thus an epistemic fact, not an ontological absence — and the simile makes the point with perfect economy: vyajanad vayur iva, as air by a fan. Air is always present, always in some motion; the fan does not create it but pracyate, concentrates and quickens it past the threshold of feeling. So utterance stands to the ever-functioning word: articulation is the fan, a sva-nimitta that raises the perpetual subtle operation to gross perceptibility for a moment, after which the word no more ceases than the air does when the fan stops. The verse retro-illuminates the whole manifestation dossier: this is why abhivyakti and not production was the right category from 1.44 onward — one does not produce what never pauses; one occasions its detection.

mula_deva: तस्य प्राणे च या शक्तिर्या च बुद्धौ व्यवस्थिता ।
mula_iast: tasya prāṇe ca yā śaktiryā ca buddhau vyavasthitā |
translation: The power of it that stands in the breath, and the power that stands settled in the intellect — that very power, turning itself about in the places of articulation, attains differentiation.
commentary: A unification, gathering the descent (1.112-115) into a single subject. What stands in prana and what stands in buddhi are not two agencies but ya ca ... ya ca ... saisa — one sakti doubly stationed, and it is *that* which, vivartamana sthanesu (the vivarta participle again, now in the very throat), bhedam prapadyate, arrives at differentiation. The verse forecloses a dualist misreading the descent-story invited: mind-word and breath-word are not successive entities handed a baton but one power viewed at two stations, its 'motion' from one to the other being appearance, not transit. Read against 1.2, the architecture is exact — as Brahman's many saktis are apthak, non-separate, from it, so the word-power's stations in intellect and breath are non-separate from the one power. Differentiation happens at the sthanas, the outermost edge, and even there it is something the power *presents*, not something it suffers. Everything since 1.44 is here in one sentence: one word-power, two inner stations, appearance of difference at the threshold of sound.

translation: The power residing in words themselves is what binds this universe; and this self, whose essence is the flash of insight, whose eye is that power, is perceived in the form of difference.
commentary: The crescendo before the kanda's final theses, in which the linguistics is folded back into the cosmology without remainder. First line: visvasya asya nibandhani — the sakti lodged sabdesv eva, in words themselves, is the *binder of this universe*. Nibandhana has been this kanda's word for 'ground' since 1.13 (words ground the realities of practice) and 1.15 (word-forms ground the classes of things); now the grounding is total — the world hangs together wordwise, coherence itself being linguistic. Second line: the atman, pratibha-atma, whose essence is pratibha — the immediate flash of understanding, here named for the first time and destined to be the second kanda's answer to what sentence-meaning is — sees *through* that word-power (yan-netra, 'whose eye is it') and accordingly pratiyate bheda-rupah, is perceived in the form of difference. The construction is precise about where difference lives: an undivided seer, seeing through the instrument of the word-power, appears divided *because its eye is the differentiating instrument* — the eye colours the seen, and the world's plurality is the tint of the lens. The verse thus answers 1.1 across the whole arc of the kanda: how does the one Brahman-word appear as the processional world? Through its own power-of-words serving as the very organ of its self-perception.
contested: yannetrah is a bahuvrihi we resolve as 'whose eye/instrument is that (power)'; others take netra as 'leader' — 'led by which power' — softening the perceptual metaphor; and whether bheda-rupah pratiyate describes the atman as (falsely) perceived in difference or as (really) manifesting difference reopens, deliberately, the vivarta/parinama question of 1.1

translation: Since the differences beginning with the note sadja are explained and given form by word, therefore all the varieties of objects rest upon the measures of word.
commentary: An argument from the hardest case: music. The notes of the scale — sadja and its fellows, the seven svaras — are pure audition, the least conceptual of objects, difference given to the ear with no help from thought. And yet, the verse observes, even these are vyakhyata and rupyate sabdena — explained, and *given their form*, through word: the notes are individuated as notes, held apart as a system of seven, only through their names and the theory that names them. The untrained ear hears continuous pitch; the named scale is what carves it into sadja-versus-rsabha. If even audible difference at its most immediate is word-fixed, then a fortiori artha-vidhah sarvah, all the varieties of objects, rest on sabda-matras, the measures of word. The verse converts 1.15's thesis (classes rest on word-forms) from assertion to argument by finding the a fortiori case, and it opens the final ascent: from here to 1.131 the kanda claims everything — cosmos, practice, cognition, salvation — for the word.

translation: This is the transformation of the word — so those who know the tradition understand; from the Vedic hymns first of all this universe unrolled.
commentary: The cosmological warrant, cited from scripture. Amnaya-vidah, the knowers of the received text, understand this world as sabdasya parinamah — and the second line gives their proof-text in paraphrase: chandobhya eva prathamam, from the metres, the hymns, first of all, this universe vyavartata, turned forth. The allusion gathers a genuine Vedic lineage — the cosmogonies in which creation proceeds from vac or through the sacrificial metres. Two cautions for the reader. First, parinama here sits in reported speech: it is the amnaya-knowers' formulation, and Bhartrhari's own considered verb has been vivartate since 1.1 — the juxtaposition quietly registers that scripture speaks the language of real transformation while the analysis has argued for appearance. Second, the verse is doing legitimating work: the extravagant claims of 1.119 and 1.121-123 are being shown to be not a grammarian's professional inflation but the oldest teaching of the tradition itself.
contested: the wisdomlib transcription reads parinapo, an evident typo for parinamah, and we translate accordingly; more substantively, whether Bhartrhari endorses the parinama idiom here or flags it as scriptural shorthand for what is strictly vivarta divides readers, and the kanda's own usage supports the latter

translation: All procedure-knowledge in the world rests upon word — which even the child comprehends, through the residues deposited in it before.
commentary: From cosmos to cradle. Itikartavyata — 'the thus-it-is-to-be-done-ness' of things, know-how, the whole practical intelligence by which life is conducted — is sarva sabda-vyapasraya, wholly word-dependent. The counterexample any critic reaches for is the infant: the bala acts purposively — turns to the breast, grasps, avoids — before language. Bhartrhari's answer is purva-ahita-samskara: the child comprehends through residues laid down before — in prior lives, on the tradition's reckoning; in any case, antecedently to this learning. Infant know-how is not word-free cognition but the operation of word-formed traces not presently audible: an innate endowment that is itself linguistic in structure. The move is only as strong as one's tolerance for its premise, but its shape is exact and modern — practical capacities that precede overt language are explained by an inner, inherited language-likeness rather than admitted as non-linguistic. What 1.123 will call the interweaving is here traced beneath the threshold of speech acquisition.

translation: The first disposing of the organ, the driving of the breath upward, the striking upon the places of articulation — none occurs without a conceiving in word.
commentary: The dependence turned back on speech itself, completing a circle with deliberate care. Utterance is a motor act: the initial karana-vinyasa (tongue and lips taking position), the upward samirana of breath, the abhighata at the sthanas. Each of these, the verse notes, is itself a piece of itikartavyata — a procedure — and so by 1.121 requires sabda-bhavana, a conceiving or intending in word, to occur at all. One cannot *say* anything without inwardly word-forming the saying of it. The regress this threatens (does the inner wording itself need a prior wording?) is not an oversight but the doctrine: it terminates, non-viciously, in the ajasra-vrtti word of 1.116 — the unceasing inner operation that no act initiates because it never began. Speech does not start from silence; audible speaking is a local intensification (the fan of 1.116) of a wording that is always already under way. The verse thus welds the physiology of 1.108-115 to the epistemology about to be announced: even the body's speech-machinery runs on the very medium it outputs.

mula_deva: न सोऽस्ति प्रत्ययो लोके यः शब्दानुगमादृते ।
mula_iast: na so'sti pratyayo loke yaḥ śabdānugamādṛte |
translation: There is no cognition in the world that does not follow upon word; all knowledge appears as if interpenetrated by word.
commentary: The most celebrated karika of the work, and the thesis for which Bhartrhari stands in the history of philosophy. Na so 'sti pratyayo loke yah sabda-anugamad rte: no cognition whatsoever without the accompaniment of word. Not: no *communicated* cognition, no *reflective* cognition — none. And the second line specifies the mode: sarvam jnanam appears anuviddham iva sabdena, as if interwoven, shot through, with word — anuviddha is the weaver's and jeweller's word, thread through cloth, thread through pearls. Cognition is not first accomplished wordlessly and then labelled; the wording is *in* the seeing, as the thread is in the fabric — remove it and no fabric remains. The iva registers phenomenological honesty (the interweaving is how it *appears*; introspection cannot get behind it to check) rather than hesitation, since by 1.124 the dependence is argued to be constitutive. Against this stands the great counter-tradition — the Buddhist doctrine that perception proper (nirvikalpaka pratyaksa) is precisely wordless, conception arriving after — and the battle line drawn here runs through a millennium of Indian epistemology, Dignaga and Dharmakirti on one side, the grammarians and (with qualifications) Bhatta Mimamsa and Nyaya distributing themselves around the other. Bhartrhari's ground: attend to any actual cognition and the attending is already articulate — the allegedly pure percept is a theoretical posit, never a datum. The kanda's arc closes onto its opening: if all knowing is word-woven and the world is what is known, then the sabda-tattva of 1.1 is not mysticism but the epistemology taken at full strength.

mula_deva: न प्रकाशः प्रकाशेत सा हि प्रत्यवमर्शिनी ॥ १२४ ॥
mula_iast: na prakāśaḥ prakāśeta sā hi pratyavamarśinī || 124 ||
translation: If that eternal word-formedness were to depart from awareness, the light would not shine — for it is that which makes recognition possible.
commentary: The transcendental argument for 1.123 — from assertion to necessity in one verse. Suppose the vag-rupata, the word-formedness, sasvati, standing, were utkramet, to withdraw from avabodha, awareness. Then na prakasah prakaseta: the light would not illuminate — cognition would lose precisely what makes it *cognition of* something. The reason: sa hi pratyavamarsini — it, the word-form, is the recognizer, the power of pratyavamarsa, that taking-as, that inner 'this is such-and-such', by which a bare impingement becomes an apprehension. Light that reveals nothing determinate is not light; awareness stripped of the articulating thread would be a shining that shines on nothing, which is no shining at all. So the interweaving of 1.123 is not an empirical regularity that might have exceptions but a condition of the possibility of cognition as such: to be aware is to be articulately aware, and articulation is the word's work. This verse is the philosophical hard core of the entire kanda — later Kashmir Saiva thinkers (Utpaladeva, Abhinavagupta) took pratyavamarsa/vimarsa from exactly here and made it the essence of consciousness itself, divinized: awareness's self-touching is speech. The debt is explicit in their citations.
contested: pratyavamarsini we render 'that which makes recognition possible', taking pratyavamarsa as the determining 'taking-as' within awareness; the term admits shades from 'reflective judgement' to 'introspection', and how intellectualist one makes it decides how strong a thesis the verse proves — the weakest reading (all *judgement* is verbal) is nearly trivial, the strongest (all *sentience* is verbal) is the one the Saiva tradition embraced. The Vrtti supports the strong reading: it extends the word-association into sleep and into indeterminate first-cognitions (which still grasp their objects 'as this or that'), leaving no wordless stratum of awareness at all

translation: It is the binder of all sciences, crafts and arts; by its power everything that is brought forth is differentiated.
commentary: The scope of pratyavamarsini vak, spelled out horizontally. Sarva-vidya-silpa-kalanam upabandhani: sciences, crafts, arts — the theoretical, the technical, the aesthetic — all are 'bound together' by the word-power; upabandhani continues the nibandhana-line of 1.13, 1.15, 1.118 (word as the fastening of the world), now applied to the whole space of human competence. The second line adds the productive side: whatever is abhinispanna, brought to completion — the pot thrown, the hymn composed, the dance performed — vibhajyate tad-vasat, is differentiated, given its internal articulation, under the word's control. A craft-object has parts, stages, a right order; that articulation is word-borne (1.121's itikartavyata at work), and so is the finished thing's very identity as *this* kind of artifact. The verse quietly generalizes the kanda's linguistics of manufacture: as the sentence's parts were projections of analysis (1.73), the artifact's rationale is projected onto matter by the word-guided maker. Culture, in the widest sense, is language differentiating itself into media.

translation: This it is that operates as the consciousness of transmigrating beings, without and within; sentience in all the kinds of beings does not step beyond its measure.
commentary: The widest claim of the sequence: the word-power is coextensive with sentience itself. Sa esa samsarinam samjna — it is the samjna, the consciousness or awareness-function, of all transmigrators — bahir antas ca, operating outward (in speech, in practice) and inward (in thought, in the silent taking-as of 1.124). And then the universal quantification: caitanyam sarva-jatisu, sentience in every order of beings — down through animals, to whatever stirs — tan-matram avyatikrantam, does not overstep its measure. Wherever there is consciousness at all, there is word-formedness; the beast's cognition too is anuviddha, at however dim a grade. This universalizing is required by the system: if word-interweaving held only for humans, it would look like an artifact of learned language, and 1.121 already blocked that reading for infants; extending it through all jatis makes vag-rupata a feature of caitanya as such, not of human acculturation. The verse is the bridge over which 1.1's metaphysics and 1.123's epistemology become one doctrine — consciousness, wherever found, is the word-essence functioning; samsara is the word talking itself through the forms of life.
contested: samjna we render as 'consciousness' (its upanisadic sense), though 'name/designation' — its grammatical sense used throughout 1.59-69 — is deliberately audible: beings' very awareness is their 'naming'; the pun resists translation and either English choice loses half

mula_deva: प्रविभागे यथा कर्ता तया कार्ये प्रवर्तते ।
mula_iast: pravibhāge yathā kartā tayā kārye pravartate |
translation: As, in the state of differentiation, the agent proceeds by means of it toward the deed, so, in non-differentiation, it is that very power which stands as the deed itself.
commentary: A compact metaphysics of action, closing the loop between word and work. Pravibhage, when differentiation reigns — the ordinary condition, agent here, act there — the karta proceeds toward his karya *by* the word-power (taya): intention, plan, procedure, all word-woven (1.121-122); language is the instrument through which agency reaches its deed. But avibhage, in non-differentiation — at the source, before the carving into agent and act — sa eva, that very power, karyatvena avatisthate: *stands as the deed*. Where there is no distance between doer and done, the word-power does not mediate the deed; it is it. Instrument in the divided state, identity in the undivided — the pattern is 1.5's exactly (the Veda as means *and* likeness of Brahman), now generalized to all action whatever. The verse also glosses the old Vedic intuition that the primal creation is a speaking (1.120): at the origin there is no creator wielding speech as tool; the speaking is the creating. Human action, mediated by word, is the divided image of that unmediated word-act.

mula_deva: स्वमात्रा परमात्रा वा श्रुत्या प्रक्रम्यते यथा ।
mula_iast: svamātrā paramātrā vā śrutyā prakramyate yathā |
translation: Whether as its own measure or as the measure of another — however it is set going by the utterance, just so does it become established; for it is by utterance that the object is determined.
commentary: How word-dependence cashes out in semantic practice: things stabilize as they are said. Whatever is prakramyate srutya — launched, set going, by the utterance — tathaiva rudhatam eti, in just that form attains rudhi, established currency (the grammarians' term for conventionalized meaning). The opening alternative sva-matra va para-matra va we read as: whether the utterance presents a thing in its own measure or in the measure of another — directly or through transfer, literally or figuratively — either way, the presented determination is what hardens into the thing's standing identity, tayā hy artho vidhiyate, for the object is *ordained* by utterance. Vidhiyate is strong and chosen: the verb of injunction and ordination. Objects do not first possess determinate contours which speech then reports; the contour settles along the lines speech first draws, and repeated saying is what fixes it (rudhi). This supplies the mechanism for 1.119's claim that all object-kinds rest on word-measures — and 1.129 immediately supplies the limiting case, where saying determines a 'thing' with no thing under it at all.
contested: sva-matra para-matra is elliptical and our construal (own measure/another's measure = literal/transferred presentation) is one of several; the pair has also been taken as 'in its own form or that of another word' (phonological adaptation) — the verse's point, that establishment follows utterance, survives any of these

translation: Even where the occasioning ground is utterly not so, by resort to the utterance one sees the determination of a thing-form — as in the fire-brand circle and the like.
commentary: The limiting case that proves 1.128: language determining an object where reality supplies none. The alata-cakra, the fire-brand circle, is the tradition's shared example of pure appearance — whirl a torch and there *is* no circle, atyantam atathabhute nimitte, the ground being utterly otherwise (a single moving point). Yet vastvakara-nirupana drsyate: a thing-form is determined, seen, held — and, the verse says, sruty-upasrayat, by resort to the word. We *say* 'circle', and the saying furnishes the form under which the non-thing is apprehended as a thing. The example is aimed with polemical precision: the Buddhist uses the alata-cakra to show that persistent objects are constructions; Bhartrhari accepts the analysis and identifies the constructing power — it is vac, the word, that builds the circle out of the whirl. Perception supplies moving light; the word supplies the cakra. And if the word can constitute the object *there*, where nothing underlies it, its form-giving role in ordinary cognition (1.123-124, 1.128) can hardly be denied — the illusion is not an embarrassment to the theory but its cleanest exhibition.

translation: Moreover, they declare the word standing within the speaker's self to be the great bull — union with which is what is sought.
commentary: The kanda's soteriology begins, and it begins with a citation. The 'great bull' is the mahan rsabha of the old texts — the allusion is to the Vedic figure of speech as the great bull (catvari srnga, 'four horns...', RV 4.58.3, is the passage the tradition connects here, whose bull the exegetes read as vac). What the seers so named, the verse says, is the sabda antar avasthita — the word stationed *within the speaker's self*: not the audible fourth part but the inner totality of speech, the ajasra-vrtti word of 1.116, the vagatman of 1.112. And with it sayujyam isyate — union, the technical term of Vedic soteriology (con-yoking, being joined-with), is what is sought. The religious end is thus declared to be union with the inner word — not with a deity beyond language but with language at its own root, which by 1.1 *is* the ultimate. The verse authorizes 1.131's conclusion: if union with the inner word is the goal, then the discipline of the word is the path, and grammar's claim to be dvaram apavargasya (1.14) stops being hyperbole and becomes the system's plain consequence.
contested: the rsabha allusion is certain in tenor but its exact source and the compound maha-antam-rsabham admit variant resolutions; we follow the standard identification with the Vedic bull-of-speech imagery, and note that the karika's api prahuh marks it explicitly as received teaching being adduced, not argued

translation: Therefore that perfecting of the word is the attainment of the supreme self; one who knows the principle of its functioning enjoys the deathless Brahman.
commentary: The conclusion of the whole kanda's argument, marked tasmat. If the ultimate is word-essence (1.1), if all cognition is word-woven (1.123-124), if consciousness across beings is the word-power functioning (1.126), and if the sought union is with the inner word (1.130) — then sabda-samskara, the perfecting or refinement of the word (the grammarian's daily work: samskara is what grammar *does* to speech), simply is siddhih paramatmanah, the attainment of the supreme self. And the knower — pravrtti-tattva-jna, one who knows the principle of the word's functioning, its descent from undivided source through mind and breath to audible difference and its return — tad brahma-amrtam asnute, enjoys that deathless Brahman. Asnute, 'eats, enjoys': attainment as tasting, recalling the paramo rasa of 1.12. Every honorific claim of 1.11-22 is hereby redeemed as doctrine: grammar as highest tapas, as therapy, as the door of liberation — these follow, given the identifications the kanda has argued. The Brahmakanda ends its constructive arc here; what remains (1.132-155ish) is consolidation — the necessity of tradition, the divisions of speech, and the closing frame.

translation: Never does anyone accept a tradition to be without an author; when all traditions perish, the seed remains — hence the triple Veda stands established.
commentary: A return to the philosophy of tradition (1.27-43) at a deeper station, with a puzzle set on its face. First line: no agama circulates as ownerless — every current of teaching is received as *someone's*, akartrkam pratipadyate no one. Second line: sarvagamapaye bijam — when all traditions pass away (in the dissolution between world-ages, on the cosmology in play), a seed persists, from which the triple Veda re-establishes itself (trayy evato vyavasthita). The two lines look opposed — everything authored, yet something survives all authors — and the resolution is the doctrine of 1.28 writ cosmic: what is eternal is not any authored text-body but the vyavastha, the seed-order that re-flowers identically each age; the 'authors' each cycle are the rsis who *see* it again (1.37), not composers. The verse thereby threads between Mimamsa (authorless Veda) and Nyaya (authored, by God): authored in every manifestation, authorless as the seed. Bija completes the metaphor-chain of 1.84 and 1.111 — trace-seed in the hearer, cloud-seed in the atoms, now world-seed across dissolutions: at every scale, the word persists as latency between manifestations.
contested: the first line can be read as the opponent's premise (nobody accepts an authorless tradition — so how is the Veda authorless?) answered by the second, or as Bhartrhari's own concession en route to the seed-doctrine; we take the latter, but the dialectical cut differs between readers

translation: When the doctrines have set, and other authors are no more, the world does not transgress the rite declared by sruti and smrti.
commentary: The empirical companion to 1.132's cosmology. Vadas — the philosophical systems, with their founders and their fortunes — asta yatesu, set like stars; their authors pass, their schools scatter. Yet the karma declared in sruti and smrti, the ritual and practical order, lokah na vyativartate — the world does not step across it; the agnihotra outlives every philosophy of the agnihotra. The observation is sociological and, within its horizon, accurate: doctrines are mortal in a way that rites are not, argument-driven allegiance decaying faster than practice-driven transmission. Its argumentative work: to display, within recorded history, the same asymmetry 1.132 asserted across cosmic dissolutions — teachings-with-authors perish, the practice-bearing core persists — so that the seed-doctrine is not exotic metaphysics but the long shape of ordinary cultural time. It also silently continues 1.30-31: the immunity of the unbroken practice to tarka is here shown, not argued — the vadas that would have judged it are the ones that have set.

mula_deva: ज्ञाने स्वाभाविके नार्थः शास्त्रैः कश्चन विद्यते ।
mula_iast: jñāne svābhāvike nārthaḥ śāstraiḥ kaścana vidyate |
translation: If knowledge were natural, there would be no purpose whatever in the sastras; if dharma is the cause of knowledge, then tradition is its ground.
commentary: A dilemma posed to the naturalist about knowledge, each horn closing toward tradition. Suppose jnana svabhavika — cognition arises naturally, needing no instruction: then the sastras are pointless; yet no party to the debate, least of all the naturalist writing books, treats them so — reductio by performative fact. Alternatively, grant what the tradition holds: that dharma conditions knowing — the knower's moral-ritual formation is causally upstream of his cognitive reach (the yogic epistemology of 1.37 presupposed exactly this). Then, since dharma is not established by unaided reason (1.30), amnayo nibandhanam: tradition is knowledge's ground, at one remove. Either the sastras are idle (which practice refutes) or they are load-bearing via dharma (which makes agama foundational). The verse formalizes what the kanda has assumed since 1.14 called grammar a purifier: disciplines do not merely inform; they *qualify the knower* — and any epistemology that ignores the knower's formation has, on this view, mistaken its subject.
contested: the second pada's syntax (dharmo jnanasya hetus cet...) can be read as a genuine conditional the author endorses, or as continuing the opponent's alternatives; the wisdomlib transcription's nivandhanam for nibandhanam is the v/b alternation common in the source and we read nibandhanam

translation: And reasoning that does not conflict with Veda and sastra is an eye for those who do not see; for from the mere form alone the meaning of a sentence does not stand settled.
commentary: The rehabilitation of tarka — the kanda's last word on reason, and a deliberately generous one. After the demolition of 1.32-42, reasoning returns with a licensed office: veda-sastra-avirodhi tarkah, reasoning that keeps concord with Veda and sastra, is caksur apasyatam — an *eye for the sightless*, for those without the rsi's direct vision (1.37-38), which is to say for everyone now. And the second line gives the reason tradition itself requires this eye: rupa-matrat kevalat, from the bare form alone, vakyarthah na avatisthate — the meaning of a sentence does not settle. Texts do not interpret themselves; the received word, precisely because it is received as *form*, demands construal, and construal is reasoning's proper province. So the settlement: tarka is blind as a foundation (1.30) and indispensable as an instrument — sovereign nowhere, employed everywhere, its concord with the tradition being the condition of its safe use, as the frame of a lens is to its glass. That sentence-meaning outruns sentence-form is, moreover, no small aside: it is the thesis-seed of the entire second kanda, where what a vakya means will be argued to be an undivided pratibha that the parsed form merely occasions. The kanda's epistemological ledger thus closes balanced: vision above, tradition around, reason within.

translation: Non-intention of what is present, subservience to another, indication of a meaning through a mark — such manifold interpretive principle is articulated by reasoning.
commentary: What the licensed tarka of 1.135 actually does, shown by its instruments. The verse names three canons from the interpreter's working kit: sato 'vivaksa — a real feature of the situation may be *unintended*, present but not meant (the speaker mentions the man's staff without meaning staffness); pararthya — an element may stand wholly in service of another, its sense exhausted in subordination (the Mimamsa principle governing subsidiary and principal); vyaktir laingiki — a meaning may be delivered only through its linga, its indicatory mark, never said outright.
