Three predications of the one word-essence, each a resolution of a standing antinomy. Astitva-nastitva-samarthye samavasthite: the capacities for existence *and* non-existence stand established in it — presence and absence alike are its powers (how else could language speak of the non-existent, negate, counterfactualize? — the capacity to present absence is as positive a power as the capacity to present being; the hare's horn lives in speech). Akrame krama-nirbhase: sequenceless, *appearing* as sequence — the whole dossier 2.19-27 sealed into an epithet. Vyavahara-nibandhane: and precisely as such — powers of being and non-being, timeless ground of temporal show — it is the nibandhana of vyavahara, the fastening of all practice: the term that has tracked 'ground' since 1.13 here takes its widest object; everything transacted, said, done, negated, planned, hangs from this. The verse is Kanda II's answer to 1.1: there, Brahman-as-word from whom the world proceeds; here, analyzed to its working parts — the one whose paired capacities and sequence-show *are* the world's practicability. What such a ground cannot be made to do is behave as one item within the practice it grounds — which is exactly the deflation about to be visited on the padartha-realists.
contested: the locative absolute construction leaves the verse's syntactic completion open (the sentence continues into the discourse rather than closing); we render it as free-standing predication, as the tradition does

translation: Since the means of knowledge is the comprehension itself: in the positing of word-meanings as existent, if a word-meaning is added or subtracted, meaninglessness results.
commentary: Return to semantics, with the criterion that governs the remainder of the kanda: samprataya-pramanatvat — the pramana here is the *comprehension itself*; what a sentence means is answerable, finally, to what competent hearers actually understand, not to what a theory computes from parts. Then the test applied against padartha-realism. If word-meanings are existents that compose (padartha-astitva-kalpane), composition should be monotonic: add an item (abhyuccaya), the meaning should grow by it; drop one (tyaga), it should shrink by it. The facts refuse: anarthakyam prasajyate — add or subtract against the sentence's one meaning and you get not more or less meaning but *meaninglessness* (or a different whole altogether): 'bring the cow' plus a stray genitive is not an enriched command but noise; idioms lose everything by one substitution; the same word added here completes, there destroys. Sentence-meaning behaves like a gestalt, not a sum — tolerance zero at its boundary, holistically re-formed within it. The argument is the empirical mirror of 2.15's ontological trilemma, and its premise is the school's deepest methodological commitment: linguistics answers to comprehension (the pratipattr of 2.17-18), the theorist's ledger of parts having no authority against what understanding actually does.
contested: we construe abhyuccaye tyagat as the add/subtract test (either operation, against the fixed comprehension, yields anarthakya); the compressed ablatives admit other pairings — e.g. 'because of abandonment (of the general sense) in the aggregation' — which would echo 2.15 instead; the comprehension-criterion in pada a is secure on any parsing

translation: By the word 'rajan' the king-meaning is understood under a differentiated form —
commentary: A half-verse (the source transmits it split), opening the compound-analysis: in 'raja-purusa', 'king's man', the element rajan- delivers its sense bhinna-rupena, under a differentiated form — not the free-standing king of 'the king dines' but king-as-qualifier, bent toward the purusa it possesses. The lexical 'same word' contributes differently under construction — the isolationist's fixed deposit (2.15, 2.17) failing again, this time inside a single compound.

translation: — while in the derivational analysis, a word resembling a finite verb is employed for what stands otherwise.
commentary: The completion: grammar's vrtti-analysis paraphrases the compound by a clause — 'rajnah purusah', or with a verb-like element supplied ('the man *who belongs to* the king') — employing akhyata-sadrsam padam, a word resembling a finite verb, for what in the compound stands anyatra, otherwise (verblessly, fused). The paraphrase is faithful to the meaning and *false to the form*: the compound contains no verb, yet its analysis requires one. Moral, in the kanda's running key: analysis is translation into the analyst's idiom, not excavation of hidden constituents — the clause 'in' the compound is exactly as real as the phonemes 'in' the sphota (2.10). The pair 35ab/cd thus exhibits, at the smallest scale, both directions of the general doctrine: meaning shifting under construction (ab), and construction-analysis fabricating parts the meaning never had (cd).
contested: the source splits 2.35 across two pages and the halves' connection is loose; some editions read them as one karika on vrtti (compound-formation) generally — our joint reading follows the evident sense of the received halves

translation: As, when 'asvakarna' is said, a certain particular thing is understood without any horse at all — such is the cognition in all cases.
commentary: The rudhi-compound as the revealing limit-case, generalized with sudden boldness. Asvakarna, 'horse-ear', names a tree (the sal); when it is said, vinaiva asvena — without any horse whatsoever entering the comprehension — kascid eva visisto 'rthah, a certain quite particular thing (the tree), is understood: the 'members' contribute nothing; the whole word delivers its whole meaning at a stroke. So far, standard: every school parks such compounds in the 'conventional' bin and moves on. Then the last quarter detonates: sarvesu pratyayas tatha — the cognition is like that *in all cases*. What the opaque compound wears openly — meaning attaching to the undivided whole, 'parts' semantically idle — is on this view the true structure of *every* understanding: 'raja-purusa' too is grasped as one word-one meaning, its apparent member-contributions being the analyst's after-the-fact bookkeeping (2.10, 2.13, 2.35cd); the transparent compound differs from the opaque only in *inviting* an analysis, not in being composed. The generalizing move is the kanda's signature (as 1.119 generalized from music): find the case where partition visibly fails, then argue the 'normal' cases differ in salience, not in kind. Comprehension everywhere is asvakarna-comprehension — whole to whole, at once.
contested: none textually; doctrinally, whether sarvesu extends to all *compounds* or all *words and sentences whatsoever* divides readers — the kanda's argumentative arc (toward the undivided vakyartha) demands, and we adopt, the wider scope

translation: It is for the sake of understanding another meaning in sentences that the resemblance is constructed; for some, the status of conventional word is acknowledged within the science itself.
commentary: Two housekeeping clarifications that protect 2.36's generalization. First: when analysis paraphrases — when grammar 'finds' horse and ear in asvakarna, king and man in rajapurusa — the sadrsya, the resemblance between member-look-alikes and free words, is parikalpyate, *constructed*, arthantara-gateh, for the sake of getting at another meaning: paraphrase is a bridge built from known words toward the unfamiliar unit's sense, a heuristic of glossing (the teaching-function of 2.13 again), not a discovery that the unit was secretly a sentence. Second: kesamcit rudhi-sabdatvam sastre eva anugamyate — for some theorists, being-a-conventional-word is acknowledged *within the science itself*: Panini's own system treats certain formations as unitary and underived (avyutpanna), their 'derivations' declined even by the derivers. The point of citing it: the concession the opponent makes at the margin (some words are just wholes) is the thin edge of 2.36's wedge — once *any* word's meaning attaches whole-to-whole with idle parts, partition has lost its claim to be constitutive of meaning as such, and retaining it elsewhere is a choice of bookkeeping, not a finding of fact.
contested: arthantara-gateh we take as 'for the sake of reaching the other (=the unit's) meaning' — paraphrase as bridge; it can be read 'because (the compound) conveys a different meaning (than its members)' — the two shade into each other and both serve the verse's deflationary office

translation: Even things taken up that are afterwards to be discarded — these they declare to be means; and a fixed rule about means does not necessarily stand.
commentary: The pedagogy of fictions, stated as a general maxim and closing the movement. Upadaya api ye heyah — what is *taken up precisely to be let go*: the ladder-entities of the whole discussion (phoneme-divisions, word-meanings, derivational members, the constructed resemblances of 2.37) — tan upayan pracaksate: these, the tradition declares, are *means*. The formulation compresses 1.85 (error as the very means of grasping), 2.13 (divisions for the unlearned), and every iva of both kandas into a rule of method: a scaffold is not refuted by the finished building; it is *discharged*. And the rider does real work: upayanam ca niyamo na avasyam avatisthate — among means there is no necessary fixity: no single ladder is privileged; different learners, different analyses, different fictions may serve (the darsana-tolerance of 1.74 and 2.26 as pedagogy). The maxim shields the school from a tu-quoque it plainly invites — 'your sphota-talk, your levels, your similes: are they not also constructions?' Answer: yes, and offered as upaya, discardable at attainment; the difference between the grammarian and his opponent is not that one uses fictions and the other facts, but that one knows which of his instruments are ladders. The verse is the kanda's methodological self-consciousness at full candor — and the traditional resonance (upaya-heya structures run through Buddhist method too) is surely heard, and meant.
contested: none of weight; the verse reads as the maxim it is — its interest is entirely in scope: whether Bhartrhari means it reflexively of his own doctrine (we take it so, with 1.74 and 2.26 as warrant) or only of the opponent's apparatus

mula_deva: संसृष्टा वा विभक्ता वा भेदा वाक्यनिबन्धनाः ॥ ३९ ॥
mula_iast: saṃsṛṣṭā vā vibhaktā vā bhedā vākyanibandhanāḥ || 39 ||
translation: Somehow or other, some person comes to an understanding of the meaning; the differences — whether fused or divided — are fastenings relative to the sentence.
commentary: Deflationary realism about the variety of comprehension, extending 2.38's tolerance from theories to *hearers*. Artham kathamcit purusah kascit sampratipadyate: some person, somehow, arrives at the meaning — the double indefinite (kascit, kathamcit) is the doctrine: routes to comprehension vary irreducibly by hearer, occasion, and preparation; there is no single canonical processing-path any more than a single canonical analysis (2.38). And the differences among construals — samsrsta va vibhakta va, the meaning taken fused (whole-wise) or divided (part-wise) — are vakya-nibandhanah: fastenings *relative to the sentence*, ways of tying the one vakyartha down, not structures in it. The learned hearer may pass through elaborate division, the native speaker through none (2.13's avidvan/vidvan gradient); both arrive, and what they arrive at is identical. The verse quietly performs an important disarmament: the holist does not deny that part-wise comprehension *happens* — people do parse, gloss, construe — he denies that its happening reveals the meaning's composition; parsing is one kathamcit among others. With hearers, theories, and analyses all pluralized around a single invariant comprehension, the ground is fully prepared for naming that invariant — pratibha — and 2.40 begins the approach.
contested: vakya-nibandhanah we render 'fastenings relative to the sentence' (bheda-construals anchored to, and answerable to, the one vakya); the compound also admits 'grounded in the sentence' — either way the bhedas are relational, the vakya prior

translation: When the connection 'that is this' is set moving by the mind, then sometimes even a single phoneme is the conveyor of the sentence-meaning.
commentary: The extreme datum that only the holist can accommodate, offered as the movement's QED. Comprehension consists in an abhisambandha of the form so 'yam — '*that* is *this*': the incoming utterance identified with a content already alive in the hearer (the recognition-structure of 1.124, pratyavamarsa, here at sentence scale). When the mind prakramyate, is set moving toward that identification — context primed, expectation shaped — tada ekah api varnah: then even a *single phoneme* conveys the entire vakyartha, kvacit, on occasion. The phenomenon is common coin: the shared joke's first syllable; the servant who moves at 'ga—'; the pupil for whom the sutra's opening sound unrolls the whole. For compositional theories this is catastrophe — one phoneme has no parts to compose with, no word-meanings to sum; the meaning conveyed (a full sentential content) is unconstructible from the vehicle. For the manifestation theory it is the expected limit-case: if sounds are manifestors of a whole already standing in the comprehender (2.18, 2.30), then *how much* sound is needed is an empirical matter of the hearer's preparedness — normally much, with priming little, in the limit one varna, the fan of 1.116 barely waved. The verse ends the movement by inverting 2.28's regress: the atomist could not build the word from his parts; the holist retrieves the sentence from less than one of them.
contested: kvacit guards the claim ('sometimes, somewhere') and we keep its modesty; the traditional illustrations vary, and some readers take the varna here as a pratyahara-style code-sound (technical abbreviation) rather than ordinary speech — the argumentative use is identical

translation: Only so much meaning as is expressed by the word in isolation — of just that much meaning, they say, is the word when standing in a sentence the expressor.
commentary: The abhihitanvaya position — the Bhatta Mimamsa semantics, the holist's most serious rival — stated with scrupulous fairness at the head of a long examination. The thesis: a word's semantic contribution is *invariant*; kevalena padena, in isolation, 'gauh' expresses cow-in-general — and vakyastham, stationed in a sentence, it expresses tavatah eva, exactly that much, no more or differently. Sentence-meaning then arises downstream: words severally denote their meanings (abhihita), and the denoted meanings connect (anvaya) by expectancy, fitness, proximity. The picture is compositionality at full strength: fixed lexical deposits, syntax as the connective operation on them — the very picture 2.15 and 2.17 attacked, now given its own voice for systematic testing. The verses that follow (2.42-56) will let it and its variants state their best cases, and the objections will not be cheap: Bhartrhari's doxographic conscience — argue the rival to its strongest before dissolving it — is nowhere better displayed than in this stretch.

translation: But the surplus that arises when connection has taken place — that, resting on the several words together, they call the sentence-meaning itself.
commentary: The rival's account of what the sentence adds. Grant each word its fixed deposit (2.41); when sambandha occurs — the deposits connecting — anyad adhikyam upajayate: a *surplus* arises, something none of the words separately expressed: the relatedness itself, cow-as-being-brought, the state of affairs over and above its ingredient terms. Tam vakyartham eva prahuh: that surplus, they say, *is* the sentence-meaning — aneka-pada-samsraya, resting on the many words jointly, since no one of them delivers it. The account has genuine elegance: it explains compositional productivity (new sentences understood from old words: the deposits are old, only the connection is new) and gives the sentence a meaning of its own without positing a sentence-entity. Its exposed joint, which the sequel probes: the surplus is *generated* — it arises (upajayate) from the connecting of meanings — so its unity is derivative, an assembly-product; and everything Kanda II has argued about assembly (2.15's homeless generals, 2.28's non-adhering parts, 2.34's non-monotonicity) now stands waiting for it.
contested: whether 2.42 states the Bhatta abhihitanvaya specifically or a generic connection-semantics is debated; the adhikya ('surplus') formulation matches the Mimamsa doctrine of the sentence-meaning as vakyartha-over-padarthas closely enough that we read it so

translation: But that (sentence-meaning), though resting on many words, is brought to completion at each division; by others it is construed as like a universal in the aggregate, or like a number.
commentary: Two refinements of the surplus-doctrine, each trying to repair the assembly-problem. First (echoing 2.18 verbatim — pratibhedam samapyate): even granting the vakyartha rests on many words, it completes *at each*: the relational surplus is wholly present wherever it is present at all — a concession the connection-theorist is driven to by the phenomenology (comprehension does not feel like watching a relation assemble) and which quietly surrenders half his picture. Second, the type-theoretic repairs (paraih, by others): the sentence-meaning is jativat samudaye — like a *universal* residing in the aggregate: as cowhood is wholly in each cow yet 'of' the herd only via its members, the vakyartha inheres whole in the word-collection; or samkhyavat — like a *number*: as ten is a property of the group that no member has and no member lacks a share in generating, the sentence-meaning is a collective property, real, unitary, and irreducibly of-the-many. These are the opponent's best structures for a one-over-many meaning — and Bhartrhari has chosen them for their instructive failure: universals and numbers are precisely the entities whose one-in-many character is *unexplained* in the rival ontologies (the Vaisesika's own scandals), so the repair grounds the sentence's unity in structures as problematic as itself. The holist's alternative — the unity is primitive, the plurality constructed (2.30-31) — begins to look like the *cheaper* metaphysics.
contested: the referents shift rapidly and paraih is our pivot: we read three positions (per-division completion; jati-model; samkhya-model), the latter two as others' refinements — some read the whole verse as one position with two similes

translation: (44) Others know the sentence-meaning as a general, conformable to all the particulars; through contact with other meanings it takes on the form of the particular. (45) The relation delimits that hovering condition of it — of the general awaiting its particulars — settling it upon the particular.
commentary: The anvitabhidhana-adjacent refinement (the direction Prabhakara Mimamsa would systematize): meaning enters *general* and is *specified in construction*. On this view (apare, others), what a word first gives the sentence is a samanya conformable to every particular it might become — 'bring' awaits a bringable, 'cow' awaits a role — sarva-bheda-anugunyam, fitted to all differentiations, committed to none. Arthantara-samsargat, through contact with the other meanings, it bhajate bheda-rupatam, takes on particular form: 'cow' under 'bring' becomes cow-as-patient-of-bringing. (45) adds the dynamics with a remarkable image: before settlement the general exists in pariplavamanata — a *hovering*, a floating unfixity (the word suggests a boat riding loose) — bheda-anakanksatah... which the sambandha avacchinatti, cuts off, delimits, visese nivesayan, installing it in the particular. Comprehension as progressive fixation of hovering indeterminates: each word casts a shifting field of possibility that the others successively pin. Bhartrhari reports this with evident respect — it is the rival picture *closest* to his own (specification-in-context, meanings alive only in construction, 2.17), differing at one joint only: for this theorist the hovering generals are real semantic individuals that get connected; for Bhartrhari the 'generals' and their 'fixation' are both analytical shadows of one undivided vakyartha manifesting (2.18). The next verse probes the connective itself.
contested: the attribution is anachronistic either way (Prabhakara postdates); we mark the view as the ancestor-type of anvitabhidhana without the school-name. Pariplavamanata is the verse's coinage and our 'hovering' its nearest English

mula_deva: कार्यानुमेयः संबन्धो रूपं तस्य न दृश्यते ।
mula_iast: kāryānumeyaḥ saṃbandho rūpaṃ tasya na dṛśyate |
translation: The relation is inferable from its effect; a form of it is not seen. Therefore they acknowledge it to be utterly beyond thinghood.
commentary: The connective interrogated, and the rival semantics pays its ontological bill. Every connection-theory (2.42-45) trades on sambandha — but what *is* it? Karya-anumeyah: knowable only by inference from its effect (the unified comprehension); rupam tasya na drsyate — no form of it is ever presented; nobody perceives the relating, only the related-having-been. The theorist must therefore concede (pratijanate, they acknowledge) that the relation is atyantam asattva-bhutam — utterly beyond sattva, beyond thinghood: not an entity among entities but at most a structure attributed backward from its supposed work. Bhartrhari lets the concession stand without immediate comment because its dialectical yield is complete: the rival account of sentence-unity now rests on an imperceptible, non-thing connective inferred from the very unity it was invoked to explain — a circle with an invisible center. Against it, the holist's primitive (one self-manifesting vakyartha, 2.31) posits no machinery at all between items, since there are no items to connect. The verse also plants a flag the third kanda will march to: the Sambandha-samuddesa's full theory of relation as paratantrya, essence-less dependence, begins in this asattva-concession.
contested: who concedes — the rival driven to it, or Bhartrhari stating his own doctrine of relation through their mouths — is nicely undecidable, and the verse works as both refutation and foreshadowing; we read it as the former with the latter intended

translation: The thing-to-be-accomplished is fixed relative to its means; the action has fixed means. That fixity, simply through proximity, shines forth as settled.
commentary: Deflation of the connective completed: what work was left for sambandha to do, niyama and sannidhi already do. The semantic roles come *pre-fixed*: sadhye sadhane niyatam — what is to be accomplished is fixed relative to its means (a bringing needs a brought, an agent, an instrument); kriya niyata-sadhana — the action-meaning arrives with its argument-places built in (the karaka-structure that Panini's grammar maps). Given meanings so structured, sannidhana-matrena — through *mere proximity*, the words' co-presence in one utterance — sa niyamah san prakasate: the fixity, already real, simply *shines forth as settled*. Note the verb: prakasate, manifestation-vocabulary — nothing is assembled; an antecedent structure is lit up when its expressors stand together. The connection-theorist's sambandha is thus idle: his 'relating operation' names nothing beyond meanings-with-slots meeting slot-fillers in proximity — and 'meeting in proximity' is an occasion for manifestation, not an act of synthesis. This is the holist absorbing the best insight of 2.44-45 (specification-in-construction) minus its ontology: expectancy (akanksa) is real as the *shape* of the one meaning, not as hooks between many. 2.48 immediately cashes this in grammatical coin.

translation: The noun operates there as requiring, in the capacity of subordinate; the occasioning elements, as things-to-be-accomplished, await the action-word.
commentary: The karaka-architecture of the sentence, in one couplet — 2.47's abstract niyama shown as the actual grammar of Sanskrit. Nama, the noun, pravartate gunabhavena sakanksam: operates as *subordinate* (guna, the dependent member, 2.4's third mark) and as *requiring* — a noun is intrinsically unfinished, a role-filler seeking its role. The nimittani — the occasioning elements, the nominal contributors read as sadhyatvena, under the aspect of what-is-to-be-accomplished — kriyapadam apeksate: await the action-word. The verb is the sentence's gravitational center: nouns orbit it, their cases the names of their orbits (karakas), their expectancy the pull. This is why the akhyata-view (2.1's first answer) was *almost* right: the verb is indeed where sentence-unity is organized — wrong only in taking the organizer for the unity itself (the verb too is an abstraction from the one vakyartha; it is the *structure's* center, not the meaning's substance). The verse also explains, retroactively, why 2.4 defined the vakya as karma-pradhana: principality of action is the syntactic face of the meaning's one-ness — a single sadhya with its sadhanas is how an undivided content articulates when projected into word-roles (2.7).
contested: nimittani ('the occasioning elements') is loose — we take it as the nominal karaka-bearers collectively; some construe it narrowly as the pre-verbal conditions (the karakas qua causal factors of the action); the architecture asserted is the same

mula_deva: सन्त एव विशेषा ये पदार्थेषु व्यवस्थिताः ।
mula_iast: santa eva viśeṣā ye padārtheṣu vyavasthitāḥ |
translation: The particulars that stand settled in the word-meanings, really existent, are followed along in order — the sentence is not an expressor.
commentary: The opposite extreme now states its case: the sentence eliminated entirely (the padartha-realist's terminus, counterpart to 2.41's opening). On this view the visesas — the particular determinations that comprehension delivers — santa eva, *really existent*, stand already vyavasthita in the word-meanings themselves; comprehension kramad anugamyante, follows them along in sequence, one deposit after another; and the conclusion is drawn without blinking: na vakyam abhidhayakam — *the sentence expresses nothing*; there is no sentence-level semantics at all, only a parade of word-facts that the hearer strings. This is the mirror-image of the holism it opposes — where Bhartrhari dissolves the words into the sentence, this theorist dissolves the sentence into the words — and Bhartrhari grants it the courtesy of full statement because its existence proves something structurally: the middle positions (2.42-45), trying to have real word-deposits *and* a real sentence-surplus, are unstable, sliding under pressure toward one pole or this one. 2.50-52 will let this eliminativism run to its own dissolution: if only word-order strings the deposits, and order is no word (krama being time's property, not speech's), the view ends with no expressor anywhere — an instructive collapse the holist will collect.
contested: santa eva we read with visesah ('really existent particulars', the eliminativist's realism); it can be construed adverbially ('being so, they are followed'); the view's identity — late Mimamsa? a Nyaya strand? — is unrecorded, and the karika keeps it anonymous

mula_deva: शब्दानां क्रममात्रे च नान्यः शब्दोऽस्ति वाचकः ।
क्रमो हि धर्मः कालस्य तेन वाक्यं न विद्यते ॥ ५० ॥
ते सन्निधाने व्यज्यन्ते न तु वर्णेष्वयं क्रमः ॥ ५१ ॥
mula_iast: śabdānāṃ kramamātre ca nānyaḥ śabdo'sti vācakaḥ |
kramo hi dharmaḥ kālasya tena vākyaṃ na vidyate || 50 ||
te sannidhāne vyajyante na tu varṇeṣvayaṃ kramaḥ || 51 ||
translation: (50) And if (the sentence is) mere order of words, there is no further word to be the expressor; for sequence is a property of time — hence the sentence does not exist. (51) And the possible particulars, unrealized in the word-meanings, are manifested in co-presence; but this order is not in the phonemes.
commentary: The eliminativism of 2.49 driven to collapse, then the holist's harvest. (50): if what 'strings the deposits' is krama-matra, mere order, note what order is: kramo hi dharmah kalasya — sequence is a property of *time*, not of speech (2.24 established it is not even securely that); it is no sabda at all. So the eliminativist has: word-deposits, plus a non-verbal, non-entity ordering — nanyah sabdo 'sti vacakah, no further word exists to do the expressing of the sentence-content — tena vakyam na vidyate: the sentence, on his own premises, *does not exist*. The reductio is complete and Bhartrhari states it in the opponent's own voice, letting the position pronounce its own emptiness. (51) then gathers what survives: yes — sambhavino bhedah, the possible particulars, avibhavitah in the isolated word-meanings (the hovering generals of 2.44), vyajyante sannidhane, are *manifested in co-presence* (2.47's sannidhi, the verb again vyaj-): co-utterance occasions the determinate whole. But — na tu varnesu ayam kramah — this manifesting order is not *in* the phonemes: the sequence that matters is the mind's traversal (2.25), not an acoustic property; the utterance's serial body is occasion, never bearer. Between the two verses the last rival has dissolved and every salvageable insight has been re-housed in manifestation-theory: deposits→hovering possibles; connection→co-presence; order→cognitive traversal; sentence→the one manifested whole.
contested: 50's first pada is compressed (sabdanam krama-matre ca) and we supply the conditional; 51's closing na tu varnesv ayam kramah has also been read 'nor does this order (of manifestation) hold among the phonemes' — i.e. manifestation is not phoneme-by-phoneme — which is the same doctrine from the other side

mula_deva: पदाख्या वाक्यसंज्ञा च शब्दत्वं नेष्यते तयोः ॥ ५२ ॥
mula_iast: padākhyā vākyasaṃjñā ca śabdatvaṃ neṣyate tayoḥ || 52 ||
translation: The designation 'word', and the term 'sentence', settle upon mere order of phonemes and of words; wordhood is not accepted of those two.
commentary: The nominalist coda, reported as the last word of the partitive tradition — its most austere and most self-undermining form. On this view 'pada' and 'vakya' are mere labels: pada-akhya settles on krama-matra of phonemes, vakya-samjna on krama-matra of words — names for stretches of ordering, convenience-terms like 'forest' for trees or 'army' for men, and sabdatvam nesyate tayoh: *wordhood is not granted* to either — word and sentence are not themselves linguistic realities at all, only the phonemes are. The position is the exact linguistic analogue of the Buddhist prajnapti-doctrine (composites as designation-only), and Bhartrhari's economy in refuting it is total: he has already done so. If only phonemes are real and phonemes are meaningless severally (2.12, 2.28-29), and order is no entity (2.50), then meaning has no bearer whatsoever — the view saves ontology by losing language. The deeper irony he leaves implicit: the nominalist's 'mere designation' (akhya, samjna) is itself a *linguistic* act — his theory denies wordhood by means of words, dissolving the very instrument of its own statement. The catalogue of rivals thus ends where 2.28-29 predicted: partition, pursued honestly, terminates in the unnameable — and the way back up is the holist's (2.53-62, where the argument turns constructive).
contested: whose nominalism this is remains unrecorded; the prajnapti-parallel is structural, not an attribution. Some read the verse not as a rival but as the holist's own concession (word/sentence as designations upon the krama of manifestors) — the sequel's constructive turn tells against that reading

mula_deva: समानेऽपि तु शब्दत्वे दृष्टः संप्रत्ययः पदात् ।
mula_iast: samāne'pi tu śabdatve dṛṣṭaḥ saṃpratyayaḥ padāt |
translation: But even where wordhood is equal, comprehension is seen to arise from the word; it does not arise phoneme by phoneme — hence they know the meaning to belong to the word.
commentary: The constructive turn: from the rubble of the rivals, the minimal argument that meaning attaches to wholes. Concede the nominalist his level playing field — samane 'pi sabdatve, phonemes and words equally 'sound', nothing acoustic privileging either. The *facts of comprehension* (2.34's criterion, the school's constant court of appeal) then discriminate: samprataya drstah padat — understanding is observed to arise from the word; prativarnam tv asau nasti — it does not arise per-phoneme: 'g', 'au', 'h' severally deliver nothing, no fraction of cow arrives with each (1.84 already established the all-at-once arrival with the last sound). Atah padasya artham viduh: therefore the meaning is *the word's* — the semantic unit is fixed not by ontology or acoustics but by where comprehension actually happens. The argument's beauty is its neutrality: it uses no sphota-metaphysics, only the observable non-additivity of understanding — and it sets the pattern the next verses iterate upward: exactly as meaning belongs to the word over the phonemes' heads, it will belong to the sentence over the words' (2.60-61), by the same evidence, at the next grain. The ladder of 2.28 is being climbed in reverse, comprehension lighting each rung.
contested: none of weight; the verse is deliberately ecumenical — even a padartha-realist signs it, which is its strategic point

translation: As its constituent phonemes, without any expressible content of their own, become meaning-possessed when combined — so also is the sentence accepted.
commentary: The parity-ladder, ascending. The word's phonemes are severally anarthaka (2.12, 2.53) — vina vacyena kenacit, with no expressible of their own — yet samuditah, combined, they are arthavantah: the combination is where meaning lives. Vakyam apy evam isyate: accept the same of the sentence — its words, severally, stand to the vakyartha as phonemes to the padartha: contributing no independent deposits, meaning-possessed only as the ensemble. The argument is strictly ad hominem in structure and the more binding for it: every school grants the phoneme-to-word case (no one thinks 'g' means a third of cow); parity of evidence (comprehension arising only at the whole, 2.53) then forces the word-to-sentence case on pain of arbitrariness — what non-question-begging difference marks the levels? The partitive theorist who resists must find in words a semantic self-sufficiency he already denies phonemes, against identical distributional facts (words 'meaning something alone' being citation-artifacts, 2.17). The holism thus propagates up the grains by the opponent's own concessions — the strategy 2.10 inaugurated (apoddhara accepted for stems forces it for words) here reaching the sentence.
contested: isyate ('is accepted/desired') marks this as doctrine being *ratified*, not merely reported; some hear in it lingering doxography — but 2.55-56 continue in the same ratifying voice and we read the siddhanta as speaking

translation: Meaningless (severally), by way of being means; or meaning-possessed through the word-meaning: uttered in sequence, they declare them to express a sentence-meaning of distinct character.
commentary: The two available descriptions of the words, and the invariant they share. Within the ratified holism, what shall we *say* words are? Either anarthakani upayatvat — meaningless severally, their standing that of *means* (the upaya-doctrine of 2.38: taken up to be discharged, semantic scaffolding); or arthavanti padarthena — meaning-possessed, if one insists, but through the word-meaning as analytically assigned (the abstraction of 2.10 credited back to them as a facon de parler). The choice is bookkeeping (1.74, 2.39): on either description, kramena uccaritani — uttered in their sequence — they express a vakyartha *bhinna-laksanam*: of *distinct character*, differently-marked — a meaning whose nature is other than the word-meanings' own, not their sum, aggregate, or relational surplus (contra 2.42) but a content of a different logical kind. The phrase is the movement's yield in two words: sentence-meaning differs from word-meanings not in *size* but in *kind* — which is why no operation on padarthas (adding, connecting, specifying) was ever going to construct it, and why the kanda must next say positively what kind of thing it is. The answer — indivisible, instantaneous, pratibha — is now one movement away.
contested: bhinna-laksanam we press as 'of distinct (logical) character'; a flatter reading — 'having a different definition' — is available; the argumentative arc from 2.34 onward (non-constructibility in principle) supports the pressed reading

translation: Whether the aggregates are held eternal, or the universal is what is construed, they declare the single sentence's single-meaningedness to be without deviation.
commentary: The invariance-result, sealed across the surviving metaphysical options. Two construals of the sentence-unit remain live from the wreckage: nityatve samudayanam — the aggregates themselves eternal (each sentence-whole a nitya unit, the samudaya elevated from heap to real); or jater va parikalpane — the universal-construal (the jati-sphota line of 1.93, 2.43: the sentence as type). Bhartrhari's point: choose either — ekasya ekarthatam ahur vakyasya avyabhicarinim — the *one* sentence's *one*-meaningedness holds without deviation (avyabhicarin, the term for a mark that never strays from what it marks). The eka-artha of 2.4's definition, flagged there as the loaded fourth condition, is here discharged at full metaphysical weight and shown *robust*: unitary meaning is not hostage to the token/type dispute, exactly as Kanda I's manifestation-doctrine was not hostage to the substrate-dispute (1.107) or the nitya/karya dispute (1.28). The method is constant across the work: fight to the death over the structure (undividedness), stay serene over the ontology beneath it. With unity thus secured against all comers, the kanda can afford its most disarming gesture — 2.57-58 next, where the whole war is recast as two legitimate directions of description.
contested: none textually; the pairing samudaya-nityatva / jati-parikalpana is our resolution of the compressed alternatives, following the earlier appearances of both options (2.43, 1.93)

mula_deva: पदानां संहिता योनिः संहिता वा पदाश्रया ॥ ५८ ॥
mula_iast: padānāṃ saṃhitā yoniḥ saṃhitā vā padāśrayā || 58 ||
translation: (57) The differences are constructed as posterior to non-difference by the sentence-theorists; the word-seers, however, hold the non-differences to be posterior to difference. (58) And the word-as-base relation is described according to the difference of functioning: either the continuous utterance is the womb of the words, or the continuous utterance rests upon the words.
commentary: The whole controversy, mapped from above — Bhartrhari's most explicit statement that the war of the kanda is between two *directions of construction*, not two sets of facts. (57): the vakya-vadins (his own side) hold bhedah abheda-purvakah kalpitah — differences are constructed, and construction runs *from* non-difference: the undivided sentence is primitive, words are carved (2.10). The pada-darsins, word-seers, hold abhedan bheda-purvan — non-differences (the sentence-unities) posterior to difference: words primitive, sentences built. Each side's primitives are the other's constructs; the datum — speech understood — is common. (58) then gives the dispute its technical grammatical form, where it had lived all along: samhita, the continuous utterance (the sandhi-fused stream actually spoken), versus the padas (the word-forms isolated by analysis, as in the pada-patha of Vedic recitation against its samhita-patha) — is the stream the *yoni*, the womb, of the words (padanam samhita yonih: analysis extracts them from it), or does the stream *rest on* the words (samhita va padasraya: recitation fuses them into it)? Vrtti-bhedena varnyate: described either way *according to the difference of functioning* — the reciter building samhita from memorized padas, and the analyst deriving padas from heard samhita, are both real procedures, and each grounds one metaphysics. The echo of 1.74 is deliberate and structural (what is primary for some is for others the reverse); but where 1.74 left the antinomy standing, 2.59-62 will now argue that the two directions are *not* symmetric after all — eternality breaks the tie, and it breaks toward the stream.
contested: 58's alternatives compress the samhita/pada-patha background, which we supply as the tradition does; pada-prakrti-bhava is the technical name of the issue (the Rk-Pratisakhya's own phrase), and whether prakrti there means 'base' or 'source' is itself the ancient form of the same dispute

translation: And if the word-recension is something else, an indicator of the continuous text — where eternality holds, how could the word be a product, given that its character is (mere) indication?
commentary: The tie of 2.58 broken, on the tradition's own deepest commitment. Consider the pada-amnaya, the word-recension — the pada-patha, that venerable apparatus in which the Veda is transmitted word-by-word, sandhi dissolved. What is its standing? Anyah samhitaya nidarsakah: it is *other* than, and an *indicator* of, the samhita — a pointer toward the continuous text, a laksana (the verse's own word: its character is that of showing). Now the argument: nitye — where eternality holds, and for the Veda it holds by the whole tradition's confession (1.132, 1.144) — katham karyam padam: how could the word be a *product*? If the eternal object is the samhita (and it is the samhita that is *recited*, the pada-patha being a human teaching-instrument attributed to named authors — Sakalya's, the tradition says, and what has an author is karya, 1.132), then the padas are downstream: extracted, indicated, didactic — exactly the apoddhara the holist claimed (2.10). The word-seer's direction of construction would make the eternal rest on the authored; the sentence-seer's makes the authored point at the eternal. Only one direction is consistent with the tradition's own reverence — and it is the holist's. The argument is ad verecundiam only in dress; structurally it is 2.38's upaya-doctrine given a philological body: the pada-patha is the paradigm ladder, and everyone already climbs it as one.
contested: the syntax of laksana-darsanat is compressed: we take it as 'given that its (the pada's) character is seen to be indication'; it can be read 'because (only) the laksana (the indicator) is seen (to be karya)' — same force. That the verse presupposes the pada-patha's authored status is the traditional understanding and the argument needs it

translation: As the cognition of the word-meaning is not separately felt at each phoneme, so in the words the discerning of the sentence-meaning is not separately felt.
commentary: The phenomenological parity-argument, stated in its final polished form — the experiential twin of 2.54's structural ladder. At the word-level: prativarnam asamvedyah padartha-pratyayah — the cognition of the word's meaning is *not felt phoneme by phoneme*; introspect as one may, there is no experience of one-third-of-cow at 'g' (1.83's anupakhyeya cognitions: real perhaps as process, absent as experience). Padesu evam — in the words, likewise: asamvedyam vakyarthasya nirupanam — the discerning of the sentence-meaning is not separately felt word-by-word. This is an empirical claim about comprehension's phenomenology, and it is carefully calibrated: not that nothing happens at each word (2.18 said the whole re-completes there; 1.90 mapped the part-seizures), but that no *separable installment of the sentence-meaning* is experienced as such — what one feels at 'Devadatta' mid-sentence is the still-resolving whole, not a detachable Devadatta-meaning banked and held. The hearer's experience is of one content clarifying, never of contents accumulating — and since comprehension is the pramana here (2.34, 2.53), how it presents *is* the primary evidence of how it is. The rival must now explain away the phenomenology; the holist merely describes it.
contested: asamvedya ('not separately felt') is the load-bearing term: the objector may grant unfelt processing while denying that unfelt entails unreal — Bhartrhari's implicit reply is the pramana-point (2.34): what never shows in comprehension cannot be *comprehension's* structure, only a theory's

translation: The sentence-meaning enters into the words as they function together; just so does the word-meaning into the phonemes functioning together.
commentary: The positive image that replaces deposit-and-assembly, completing 2.60's negative. Vakyarthah samnivisate padesu saha-vrttisu: the sentence-meaning *enters into* — installs itself in, comes to reside in — the words, and the words so entered are saha-vrttisu, *functioning together*: the locative absolute carries the doctrine — only in joint functioning, never severally, are the words the meaning's seat. And downward the same: padarthah into the phonemes saha-vrttisu. The direction of predication is the whole point, and it inverts the rival's at every joint: meaning is not *built up from* the units; it *descends into* them — the whole, primitively real (2.30-31), takes lodging in its manifestors as they operate in concert, the way (the system's oldest images) fire lodges in the churned wood or the reflection in the water. A word 'has meaning', on this picture, in the derivative sense in which a windowpane 'has' the light that fills it while the shutter is open: really present, wholly borrowed, gone when the joint functioning stops (citation, 2.17). Samnivisate also quietly answers where the 'surplus' of 2.42 went: nothing arises over the words; something antecedent *arrives in* them.
contested: none of substance; the verse's force is entirely in its verb and its locative — both are as we render them

mula_deva: सूक्ष्मं ग्राह्यं यथाऽन्येन संसृष्टं सह गृह्यते ।
mula_iast: sūkṣmaṃ grāhyaṃ yathā'nyena saṃsṛṣṭaṃ saha gṛhyate |
translation: As a subtle graspable, conjoined with another, is grasped along with it — so too a phoneme, bound up with another phoneme, is an expressor.
commentary: A concessive coda: even the phoneme recovers a kind of expressorhood — the right kind — under the completed doctrine. The model: a suksma grahya, a subtle perceptible — the tradition's examples are the faint scent, the thin thread, the soft tone — too slight to be grasped alone, is grasped anyena samsrstam saha, conjoined-with-and-alongside another: perception carries the subtle in on the back of the salient. So the varna: samsbaddho 'nyena varnena, bound up with its fellows, vacakas tatha — it *is* an expressor, in that conjoined mode: not the bearer of a meaning-fragment (2.53 stands), but a genuine co-carrier of the whole that only the ensemble delivers. The verse does quiet justice-work at the close of the movement: 2.40 showed one phoneme sufficing (primed manifestation); 2.53 showed no phoneme sufficing (no per-phoneme deposits); the apparent tension resolves in the conjunction-model — expressive power is real at every grain and *inalienably collective*, the phoneme expressing as a finger lifts: only as the hand's. With this, the descent-and-parity movement (2.53-62) closes: meaning belongs to wholes, installs itself downward through joint functioning, and every lesser unit shares expressorhood exactly to the degree it shares the functioning. The kanda now stands ready for its center: what the one meaning is, and how it flashes.
contested: the simile's base (what the 'subtle graspable' is) is unstated and the traditional examples vary; nothing turns on the choice — the samsarga-structure (grasped-along-with) is the entire cargo

translation: As some meaning is discerned from the uttering of the word, so from the proximity of the phonemes that same meaning is apprehended.
commentary: A bridging verse, securing the levels against a last confusion before the great exegetical excursus begins. Meaning is discerned 'from the word's utterance' — and the word's utterance just *is* the phonemes in sannidhya, co-presence (2.47, 2.51). The two descriptions name one event: no stage exists at which phoneme-proximity has done its work and a further word-act follows; apprehension from the word and apprehension from the concerted phonemes are the same apprehension under coarser and finer description. The verse blocks a reifying misstep the ladder-arguments (2.53-62) might invite — as if the word were a second station where meaning is *again* received. There is one reception, describable at any grain; the grains are the analyst's (2.10), the reception is the hearer's. With the levels thus flattened into one manifestation-event, Bhartrhari turns (2.64ff.) to the hardest test his holism faces: the Mimamsa science of ritual exegesis, whose entire apparatus appears to run on exactly the word-level meanings he has dissolved.

section: The Exegetical Test: Word-Meanings in Ritual Science
translation: (64) When something is already obtained by capacity, and the explicit text serves (only) restriction — if by that utterly particular (text) the general were annulled, (65) then, when 'let him sacrifice' has obtained substance in general by the mark of capacity, the substance would be excluded by the word 'rice-grains' — and there would then be no substitute.
commentary: The test-case, drawn from the deep machinery of Mimamsa: the doctrine of pratinidhi, ritual substitution. The injunction vriihibhir yajeta, 'let him sacrifice with rice-grains'. Analysis: yajeta alone, by samarthya, capacity (a sacrifice *needs* some substance), already obtains dravya-in-general; the explicit sruti 'vriihi' then serves niyama, restriction — it selects *which* substance, among the antecedently-possible, shall be used. Now the argument (64): if the particular utterance *annulled* the general (atyantam badhyate) — if 'rice' obliterated the obtained substance-in-general rather than restricting within it — then (65) when rice is unavailable, na syat pratinidhih: no substitute could be licensed. But the ritual science *does* license substitutes (nivara-grains when vriihi fails — the standing doctrine): therefore the general survives inside the particular; 'rice' specifies substance-in-general without destroying it. The exegetical practice thus *presupposes* a layered semantics — general meaning obtained by capacity, particular meaning by explicit text, the second restricting, not replacing, the first. The relevance to the kanda's war is exact and double-edged, and Bhartrhari will work both edges: the layering looks like word-meaning realism (the Mimamsaka's reading), yet the *general's survival under specification* is precisely the holist's hovering-samanya (2.44-45) — and 2.66 begins extracting that second edge.
contested: the transcription's samarthya-laksanam (65) we read as 'obtained by the mark of capacity'; the argument is the classical pratinidhi-nyaya of the Mimamsa (cf. the sastra's treatment of substitution), which the karikas compress to near-stenography — our expansion follows the traditional case

translation: Therefore the word 'vrihi', positing riceness as the surplus, being (itself) for the sake of what is (already) obtained, does not annul substancehood, since there is no conflict.
commentary: The resolution of the test-case, and its terms are the holist's. What does 'vrihi' in the injunction *do*? It prakalpayati adhikam vriihitvam — posits riceness as the *addition*, the specific difference — while dravyatvam na badhate: substancehood, already obtained by capacity (2.64), stands unannulled, avirudhatvat, since genus and specification do not conflict: rice *is* a substance; the particular arrives as a determination *of* the general, not a rival to it. The exegetical rule falls out: where the explicit text specifies within the obtained, substitution survives (the general remains to be re-specified when rice fails); where it *conflicts*, annulment (badha) operates. Note what this concedes and what it wins. Conceded: semantic stratification is real — comprehension does distinguish the obtained-general from the text-specific (the Mimamsaka's layering, 2.64). Won: the strata behave exactly as the hovering-account (2.44-45) said — the general persisting *in* its specification, delimited, not discarded (2.15's trilemma dissolved in ritual practice itself: nothing was thrown away; the hovering was pinned). The opponent's own science, run on his own cases, exhibits the holist's semantics. The next verses (2.67-69) generalize the mechanism: specification-without-annulment, and the principled *non-intention* (avivaksa) of what a word could but does not mean here.

translation: (67) And even when, by that (word), the co-occurrent substancehood is delimited, other particulars are not seen there, because they are not possible; (68) nor are all the particulars indicated by the action-word, as the general is — for a word is not the expressor of everything that exists.
commentary: Two boundary-theorems for the specification-mechanism of 2.66. First (67): when 'vrihi' delimits its sahacarin, its co-occurrent substancehood, the *other* particulars that substance-in-general might have taken (barley, millet) are simply *not seen* there — asambhavat, because after specification they are no longer possible; they do not linger as suppressed candidates needing annulment. Specification closes possibilities without expending badha on each: the pinned general (2.45) just *is* general-as-riced, the alternatives dead by delimitation, not by execution. Second (68): the converse boundary — the action-word (yajeta), which obtains the general by capacity, does *not* thereby indicate all the particulars under it: na hi sarvesam satam sabdo 'bhidhayakah — *a word is not the expressor of everything that exists* (a maxim with reach far beyond ritual: expression is selective by essence; to mean the genus is not to mean, even faintly, each of its members). Between them the theorems fix the semantics' shape: upward, specification does not drag the whole genus-tree into meaning; downward, the genus-word does not scatter into its instances. Meaning moves at the grain the utterance sets — which is the anti-compositionalist moral in exegetical dress: content is not inherited along ontological lines (genus-member, whole-part) but fixed by the utterance's own act.
contested: 68's samanyavat we read 'as the general (is indicated)' — the genus is indicated, its members are not; some construe 'like (words for) universals' as a comparison-class. The maxim of 68d is textually secure and is the verses' exportable yield

translation: As the qualities, white and the rest, though present, are unintended there — so is there non-intention of the particulars that co-occur with substancehood.
commentary: The doctrine of avivaksa — principled non-intention — completing the boundary-theorems with the concept the whole exegetical tradition runs on. The rice-grains actually used are *white* (or red, or old); these gunas are santah, really present — and avivaksitah, *unintended*: the injunction 'with vrihi' says nothing of them; a sacrificer with discolored rice has not deviated. Presence in the object is one thing; presence in the meaning another — vivaksa, speaker's intention (the term that settled 1.155), is what admits features into the said, and its refusals are as constitutive as its grants. So too (the verse generalizes) all the particulars riding with substancehood: co-occurrent, real, unmeant. For the kanda's argument this is the last piece of the exegetical semantics and the most holist of all: what a sentence means is fixed neither by its words' full lexical potential nor by its objects' full property-set, but by the one intentional act the utterance embodies — the vakyartha as a *selection*, whole and indivisible, from which the analyst's 'word-meanings' inherit exactly the determinations the act granted and no others (sato 'vivaksa, 2.136 of Kanda I already named the canon). The Mimamsa exegete, master of avivaksa, has been practicing sentence-holism all along.
contested: none of substance; the verse applies 1.136's canon and the tradition's standing doctrine — its work is consolidation

translation: Lest there be a substitute in the absence (of what the text names), for the obligatory rite — or for an undertaken optional one — 'omission' is (instead) prescribed.
commentary: A precision from the casuistry, marking where substitution's license ends — and thereby what its license was. Some textual elements tolerate no pratinidhi: where what the text names is absent and the element was of the annulling kind (2.66's contrast-class — specification that *conflicts* rather than restricts, or an element textually essential), the science prescribes not substitution but lopa, omission — for the nitya rite (obligatory, which must proceed: it proceeds *without* the element) or the kamya rite already undertaken (optional, but binding once begun). The theoretical cargo: substitution was never a general permission to improvise; it was licensed *exactly by* the survival of the general under specification (2.64-66) — where no general survives (the element being sruti-essential, not capacity-obtained), nothing remains to re-specify, and the only options are omission or abandonment. The casuistry thus confirms the semantics from its negative side: the exegetical operations track, case by case, *which layer of meaning the text's authority entered at* — capacity-obtained layers flex (substitute), text-fixed layers do not (omit). A hermeneutics this fine-grained about layers is the kanda's best witness that comprehension itself is layered manifestation, not flat deposit-summing.
contested: the verse's compressed alternatives (ma bhut pratinidhih / lopa upadisyate) we resolve per the standard Mimamsa doctrine of lopa vs. pratinidhi; the transcription's ity(a)padisyate we read as upadisyate

translation: For one by whom the sentence-meaning is construed as the qualified action itself — for him, when substance fails and a substitute stands in, that would be a different action.
commentary: The exegetical dossier turned, at last, into a direct argument between the kanda's live semantic theories. Consider the theorist for whom the vakyartha is visista eva kriya — the *qualified action as such*: not action-plus-qualifications assembled, but the single qualified whole (rice-sacrificing as one indivisible act-meaning). For him, the verse observes, substitution has a startling consequence: dravyabhave pratinidhau — rice failing, nivara standing in — tat syat kriyantaram: the performed rite is *a different action* (nivara-sacrificing, not rice-sacrificing-with-replacement). The whole being indivisible, vary anything and you have another whole. Yet the ritual science treats the substitute-performance as *the same rite* discharged (the sacrificer has fulfilled vriihibhir yajeta, not violated it) — the practice sides with layering (2.64-66): the rite's identity rides the capacity-obtained stratum (sacrifice-with-substance), which the substitute preserves. The verse is Bhartrhari at his most honest: it is *his own* holism, in its naive form (the undifferentiated qualified-whole), that the practice embarrasses — and the embarrassment is instructive, forcing the refinement the kanda has been building anyway: the one vakyartha is not a *blank* unity but a unity with internal svabhava-structure (obtained strata, specifications, avivaksa: 2.64-70) — undivided, yet not undifferentiated; one act of meaning whose articulation is real *as* articulation-of-one. Holism that cannot say 'same rite, different rice' is too crude; the layered manifestation-holism now on the table can.
contested: whether 2.71 is an objection Bhartrhari answers (as we read: it refines his holism) or a position he endorses against the substitution-practice (making lopa the 'correct' outcome) is a genuine crux; the dossier's drift (2.64-70 validating the practice) supports the refining reading

translation: And when a word whose meaning is known has had its object conveyed, one like 'pika', not known — 'what is that?' — is inquired after.
commentary: A small phenomenological datum with a long reach: the experience of the unknown word. In a sentence otherwise understood — vijnata-artham padam, the known words having conveyed their object — an unknown item ('pika', the tradition's stock example: a Vedic bird-name opaque to later speakers) arrests the hearer: kim iti anuyujyate, 'what is that?' is asked. The datum cuts several ways and Bhartrhari wants them all. Against flat holism: the inquiry is *word-shaped* — the hearer localizes his ignorance to 'pika', which he could not do if comprehension had no word-articulated structure (2.71's lesson again: the one meaning has real joints). Against deposit-atomism: the rest of the sentence *was* understood — comprehension did not wait on the missing deposit but completed around the gap, holding a determinate *place* open (a something-the-sentence-predicates, awaiting species): exactly the hovering-general structure (2.44), here generated by the sentence for a word, inverting the usual direction. The question 'what is pika?' is thus itself evidence of the layered vakyartha: the sentence has already given 'pika' its role-meaning (some bird-agent, say); what is sought is only the residue. Word-meaning as *role-first, filler-later* — the sentence assigning, the lexicon merely filling — is the datum's moral, and it is the holist's.
contested: none textually; the pika-example's traditional setting (nirukta discussions of unknown Vedic words) is assumed rather than stated by the karika

translation: (73) And that which, obtained by capacity, is attached for the sake of the individual — in such attachment it is the explicit text that annuls both indicatory mark and syntactic connection; (74) but a 'white' and the like, not (antecedently) obtained, understood through proximity — that, brought in by effort into the sentence, has a character quite distinct from the explicit text.
commentary: The hierarchy of evidences — the Mimamsa's celebrated ranking of sruti, linga, vakya (explicit text, indicatory mark, syntactic connection) — cited at its decisive joint and read semantically. (73): where meanings obtained by capacity are being attached to their individuals (anusanga, carrying-over of elements across clauses), conflicts are settled by rank: srutir eva badhika lingavakyayoh — the explicit text annuls mark and connection; what is *said* outranks what is *indicated* outranks what is merely *construed together*. (74) then marks the other grade: a qualifier like 'white', aprapta, not obtained by any capacity, gathered sannidhanena, by mere proximity — yatna-prapita, brought in by effort — is sruti-dharma-vilaksana, of a character quite distinct from explicit-text status: present in the comprehension, lowest in authority, first to yield. For the kanda, the ranking is one more stratigraphy of the single vakyartha (2.71's refinement compounding): comprehension is one, but its contents carry *grades of textual warrant* — said, marked, construed, proximate — and the exegete's conflict-rules are a map of those grades. A semantics of flat deposits has no room for warrant-grades (every word a deposit, all deposits equal); the manifestation-holism has exactly room — the one meaning arrives with its determinations differentially anchored, and badha among evidences is the grammar of that anchoring. The opponent's crown jewels keep testifying for the defense.
contested: 73 compresses the sruti-linga-vakya doctrine (the classical exposition is the Mimamsa's krama-sutra context) and our reading supplies that frame; 74's yatna-prapita ('brought in by effort') is read by some as pejorative ('dragged in'), by others as neutral ('secured by interpretive labor') — the grade-distinction is the point either way

translation: But if the sentence is accepted as undivided with undivided meaning, then, since all of that has the standing of explicit text, nothing will be in conflict with the text itself.
commentary: The dossier's first summit: the holist collects the entire hierarchy at a stroke. If — as the kanda has argued — the vakya is abhinnam eva, undivided, its artha abhinna, one, then tat sarvam sruti-bhutam: *everything* in the sentence-meaning has the standing of sruti, of the explicitly said — na srutya eva virotsyate, nothing in it can conflict with the text, being text through and through. The ranking of 2.73-74 (said > marked > construed > proximate) is thereby revealed as an artifact of *analysis*: grades of warrant attach to the analyst's extracted items (his lingas, his construals, his carried-over qualifiers) — to the padartha-layer, the scaffolding (2.10, 2.38); the meaning itself, taken whole as uttered, is all equally *said*. The exegete's conflicts are conflicts among his abstractions; the utterance never disagreed with itself. This is simultaneously a compliment and a demotion of the Mimamsa science: its rules are the correct *statics of the scaffold* — indispensable for the avidvan-analyst working part-wise (2.13) — and idle at the level of the undivided meaning, where there are no ranked parts to collide. The verse thus closes the test the excursus opened (can holism survive exegetical practice?) with the strongest possible verdict: the practice is holism's *own* casuistry, conducted one level down.
contested: none of weight; the verse's sweep — sarvam sruti-bhutam — is as bold as we render it, and the tradition reads it so

translation: And where there is an aggregate of sentences with mutually-requiring parts, for the establishing of a single meaning — there a (separate) sentence-meaning does not exist either.
commentary: The holism propagated one level *further up*, with perfect consistency and startling consequence. Sentences themselves aggregate: the mantra-sequence, the extended injunction, the narrative — vakyanam samudayah — whose members are sakanksa-avayava, mutually requiring (the kanda's own mark of unity, 2.4), jointly establishing eka-artha-prasiddhaye, one meaning. There, the verse rules, vakyarthah api na vidyate: the *individual* sentence-meaning does not exist — it dissolves into the discourse-meaning exactly as padarthas dissolved into it (2.13). The sentence, so long defended as the real unit against the word, was never an absolute: it is the unit *where expectancy closes* (2.4) — and where expectancy spans sentences, the unit is the span. The 'sentence' of the kanda's title was always a variable: the maha-vakya, the great sentence, is whatever whole comprehension actually takes undivided — a verse, a chapter, in the limit (the tradition would say, and Kanda I's 1.1 implies) the one Veda, the one Word. The verse guards the system from making a new atom of the vakya — the same relativism of grain (1.88, 2.10) that dissolved the parts dissolves any absolute whole short of the whole itself. Analysis is scaffolding at *every* level; only the sabda-tattva is no one's abstraction.
contested: api ('either/also') carries the argument — as word-meanings do not exist (as independent reals), so sentence-meanings likewise, within the larger unity; some soften to 'the sentence-meaning is not (complete) there' — the radical reading is the consistent one and we keep it

translation: 'This operation is incidental'; 'this is obtained by single performance'; 'this, by repetition and differentiation'; 'here, annulment and combination' —
commentary: The opening of a remarkable passage (2.77-86): ten verses of pure quotation-catalogue, the working idiom of the ritual exegete recited at speed — each phrase a term of art from the Mimamsa toolbox, strung in oratio recta as if overheard at the sacrificial session. Here: prasangika, the incidental operation (discharged en passant by a rite performed for another purpose); tantrena labhyate, obtained by tantra, single-performance-serving-many (one threshing serves all the grains); avrtti and bheda, repetition and differentiation (when must an element be re-performed for each?); badha and samuccaya, annulment and combination (when do provisions exclude, when cumulate?). The rhetorical strategy unfolds across the catalogue and is stated at 2.87: every one of these operations is *word-meaning-dependent* — each presupposes that 'this element', 'this provision', can be isolated, weighed, transferred; the exegete's entire practice runs on padartha-realism. Bhartrhari recites the catalogue *fairly and at length* precisely because the concession is real and the resolution (2.87-88) must carry its full weight.

mula_deva: ऊहोऽस्मिन् विषये न्याय्यः सम्बन्धोऽस्य न बाध्यते ।
mula_iast: ūho'smin viṣaye nyāyyaḥ sambandho'sya na bādhyate |
translation: 'Modification is proper in this domain'; 'the connection of this is not annulled'; 'this is extension of the general'; 'here the particular is extended' —
commentary: The catalogue continues with the transfer-operations. Uha, modification — the adaptation of mantras when a rite is transposed (new deity, new substance: the formula's 'rice' re-worded to 'nivara'); the exegete judges where uha is nyayya, proper. Atidesa, extension — the carrying of provisions from the archetype-rite (prakrti) to its derivatives (vikrti): sometimes samanyasya atidesa, the general rule extended; sometimes visesa atidisyate, a particular detail carried across. Each operation is a controlled *edit of meaning under identity of frame* — the rite remains 'the same' while its determinations are re-filled — and thus each presupposes the layered semantics of 2.64-71: only where a general survives its specifications can specification be re-done (uha), only where structure detaches from filling can structure travel (atidesa). The exegete manipulating transfer is manipulating exactly the strata the kanda's refined holism recognizes — a point banked silently for 2.88.

translation: 'Here, being-in-need is the qualification'; 'in this, the meaning is not divided'; 'this one's competence is obtained from the teaching'; 'his exclusion is in the other rite' —
commentary: The catalogue's third stretch: the doctrine of adhikara, competence — who may perform, who is addressed. Arthitva, being-in-need (desire for the fruit) as the qualifying condition; the scope-rulings ('the meaning is not divided' — one provision, not distributed); competence sastrat prapta, conferred by the teaching itself; and vyudasa, exclusion — this performer barred from that other rite. The exegete here is doing *semantics of address*: determining, from the injunction's words, the class of its addressees — reading subject-position out of sentence-meaning. That address is part of meaning (an injunction *to someone*) while no word in the sentence names the addressee is itself quiet evidence for meaning outrunning deposits — the vakyartha including, as structure, what no padartha supplies.

translation: 'This order is obtained from the text'; 'this, from the order of utterance and the rest'; 'this order here has force'; 'in this one, order is not intended' —
commentary: The krama-doctrine: in what sequence must the rite's elements proceed? The sources are ranked (another hierarchy, sibling to 2.73's): order from explicit sruti; order from utterance-sequence (the mantras' own order projected onto acts) and the other lesser sources (performance-order of the archetype, and so on); orders that are balavan, forceful, binding; and orders avivaksita, not intended at all — mere textual accident, no rule (1.136's canon and 2.69's avivaksa, applied to sequence itself). For the kanda this last is the resonant entry: even the exegete, sequence's greatest taskmaster, must distinguish *meant* order from *merely present* order — conceding that krama as such carries no authority until intention ratifies it. Sequence unintended is noise even in scripture: the ritual science's own avivaksita-krama is 2.24-27's doctrine (order as contributed, not found) speaking in the opponent's dialect.

translation: 'This is connected with the limbs of another'; 'this does not occasion the subsidiaries'; 'this occasions them'; 'this here is inseparable' —
commentary: The catalogue's structural stretch: anga-relations, the rite's part-whole engineering. Elements connected para-angaih, with another's limbs (serving a different principal than proximity suggests); elements aprayojaka, not occasioning the subsidiaries (present without commanding a retinue); their opposites, prayojaka; and the nantariyaka, the inseparable — what cannot be omitted without the whole failing. The exegete is here mapping *dependence-structure*: which parts serve which wholes, what can detach, what cannot. It is the sentence's own architecture (2.48's karaka-orbits, 2.4's guna-pradhana) rediscovered in the rite — unsurprisingly, since the rite is *known only through its injunctions*: ritual structure is sentence-structure enacted, and the exegete's anga-map is a syntax written in offerings.

translation: 'This is the principal, this the remainder'; 'this, moreover, is the order of application'; 'this assists it directly'; 'this qualifies it from afar' —
commentary: Continuation of the dependence-map, now graded by intimacy. Pradhana and sesa, principal and remainder (the Mimamsa's master-pair: everything in the rite is either end or auxiliary-to-end); viniyoga-krama, the order of application (which auxiliary is deployed to which principal, and in what precedence); and the two grades of service — saksat upakari, the direct assistant, touching the principal's own accomplishment, versus arad visesaka, the qualifier *from afar*, contributing at a distance through intermediaries. The two grades are the exegete's version of the semantic distinction the kanda drew at 2.73-74 (warranted nearly versus remotely): contribution is not flat; it has metric structure, near and far, and the science measures it. A theory of meaning as flat deposit-summing has no natural home for 'assistance from afar'; a theory of one meaning differentially articulated (2.71, 2.75) does.

translation: 'In this, the difference lies in the operation of the capacity, while here the fruit is what differs'; 'this difference is born of the connection'; 'there, the difference is not intended' —
commentary: The individuation-stretch: when is there *one* element, when two? The exegete's tests: sometimes sakti-vyapara-bheda, difference in the capacity's operation (one item, two workings); sometimes phala-bheda, difference of fruit (two results, hence two rites); sometimes bheda sambandhat jata, difference generated merely by the connection (relational, not intrinsic — the item differs only qua related); and sometimes, again, aviviaksita — present difference, unintended, hence null for the science. This is a working ontology of *aspects*: the exegete individuates by operation, by outcome, by relation, and by intention — never by bare form. Individuation answering to function and intention rather than to phonetic or physical identity is precisely how the kanda individuated its own objects (the word one under many utterances, 1.71; the sentence one under many construals, 2.39) — the catalogue keeps testifying that expert practice everywhere runs on the semantics the theory articulates.

mula_deva: इदं गौणमिदं मुख्यं व्यापीदं गुरु लध्विदम् ॥ ८४ ॥
mula_iast: idaṃ gauṇamidaṃ mukhyaṃ vyāpīdaṃ guru ladhvidam || 84 ||
translation: 'This is negation of the predicated, while this here is exclusionary negation'; 'this is secondary, this primary'; 'this is pervasive'; 'this weighty, this light' —
commentary: The logical stretch, headed by the tradition's most consequential distinction: prasajya-pratisedha versus paryudasa — negation that *denies a predication* ('he does not eat' — the act negated as such) versus negation that *excludes toward an affirmation* ('he eats not-this' — a positive selection wearing negative dress). The pair, born in ritual exegesis (does 'na' prohibit the act or redirect it?), became common property of every Indian logical school; its presence here marks the catalogue's reach into semantics proper. With it: gauna and mukhya, secondary and primary senses (metaphor's grammar, to which the kanda returns at length later); vyapin, the pervasive provision (holding across the whole domain); guru and laghu, weighty and light (the maxim-calculus of interpretive economy: prefer the lighter assumption). Negation's two kinds are the entry to watch: they are *indistinguishable in wording* — 'na' plus verb — and distinguished wholly by the one intended sentence-meaning (prohibition versus redirected injunction). No deposit-semantics reads them apart; comprehension of the undivided vakyartha does, every time an exegete rules.

translation: 'The limb-and-limbed relation of this is by differentiation'; 'this is optioned manifoldly'; 'this is restricted'; 'here fitness arises for it' —
commentary: The modal stretch: how provisions *bind*. Anga-angi-bhava bhedena, the part-whole relation obtaining under differentiation (the same element limb to one principal, not another); vikalpa, option — where texts permit alternatives, bahudha, manifoldly (and the science's deep discomfort with vikalpa, its rule that option is a last resort, is itself a preference for determinate meaning); niyama, restriction (the alternative closed); yogyatva, fitness arising — the element becoming eligible in context. Option, restriction, fitness: the deontic modalities of the rite, and, transposed, of the sentence (what may, must, can fill this role) — the karaka-fitness (yogyata) that sentence-comprehension weighs (2.44-47) here governing offerings. The catalogue is nearly complete; its cumulative effect — the sheer *density* of part-wise operations in expert practice — is about to be conceded in full (2.87) and absorbed (2.88).

translation: 'Of this, the difference is inferred from a mark seen in another sentence'; 'this word-meaning is divided out, having been abstracted from the word' —
commentary: The catalogue's last and most self-referential entries. First: difference established vakyantare drstat lingat — from a mark seen *in another sentence*: the exegete reads across texts, one passage's indication individuating another's provision (intertextual inference, the linga of 2.73 working at distance). Second — and with this the catalogue closes on the kanda's own key-term: ayam sabdad apoddhrtya padarthah pravibhajyate — 'this word-meaning is divided out, having been *abstracted* from the word'. The exegete himself says apoddhrtya: his practice's own idiom confesses that padarthas are extracts (2.10's doctrine in the practitioner's mouth). The catalogue thus ends by quoting the opponent performing the holist's analysis: ten verses of part-wise operations, and the last operation named is the abstraction that generates the parts. Nothing remains but to state the moral both ways — the objection (2.87: all this collapses if words express nothing) and the resolution (2.88: all this stands, as the statics of abstraction, within the undivided meaning).

mula_deva: सर्वे ते न प्रकल्पेरन् पदं चेत् स्यादवाचकम् ॥ ८७ ॥
mula_iast: sarve te na prakalperan padaṃ cet syādavācakam || 87 ||
translation: (87) Thus, the properties in sentences that are fastened to word-meanings — all of them would fail to be construed, if the word were non-expressive; (88) but when, though the sentence-meaning is undivided, abstraction is made according to the difference of capacities, what was stated is not contradicted — by way of the division into other (subordinate) sentences.
