commentary: The excursus resolved, both edges honored. (87) states the objection at the full weight the ten-verse catalogue earned it: the dharmas operative in sentences — prasanga and tantra, uha and atidesa, adhikara, krama, anga-structure, the negations, the modalities: everything 2.77-86 recited — are padartha-upanibandhanah, *fastened to word-meanings*; sarve te na prakalperan — all of them would fail, the entire science with them, padam cet syad avacakam, if the word expressed nothing. The concession is total: no exegesis without word-meanings. (88) then resolves without retreat: avibhakte 'pi vakyarthe — the sentence-meaning remaining undivided — apoddhrte sakti-bhedat — abstraction being performed *according to the difference of capacities* (the saktis: the real articulations of the one meaning, 2.71's structure, 2.83's operations) — yatha-uktam na virudhyate: nothing stated is contradicted, the whole practice standing, vakyantara-vibhagena, by way of division into subordinate sentences — the analyst's re-casting of the one utterance as many part-statements (2.35cd's paraphrase-clauses, the exegete's provision-sentences), each a legitimate *station of the scaffold*. The settlement, in one sentence: word-meanings are real as capacities of the undivided meaning and fictional as its components — and the exegetical science is the complete, correct engineering manual of the scaffold, true of the vakyartha *as analyzed*, silent on it as it is. Both kandas' method is hereby vindicated at once: analysis everywhere (or the sciences die, 87), constitution nowhere (or comprehension lies, 88). The excursus that began as holism's hardest test ends as its most detailed confirmation.
contested: 88's vakyantara-vibhagena admits two construals — 'by division into other sentences' (the analyst's part-statements, our reading) or 'by the divisions belonging to other sentences' (transferring articulations across contexts); the settlement's substance — undivided meaning, capacity-wise abstraction, practice preserved — is identical on either

translation: Just as, of a single fragrance, a construction of difference is made across flowers and the rest — so too in the sentence a difference of meaning is prescribed.
commentary: A new movement opens with a delicate analogy about projected difference. One gandha — fragrance as such, or a single perfume — is met in flower, in oil, in unguent; and the mind parikalpana, constructs difference: jasmine-scent, oil-scent, as if the fragrance itself were many. The differences belong to the substrates and the occasions; the projected plurality is a construction upon one thing variously housed. Tatha vakye 'pi: so in the sentence — artha-bheda vidhiyate, difference of meaning is *prescribed*, laid on: the padarthas, the itemized senses, are the jasmine-scent and oil-scent of one undivided vakyartha, differentiated by its housings (the words) rather than in itself. Vidhiyate is drily chosen: prescription is the analyst's act (2.10), not the meaning's structure. The analogy also opens the movement's real theme — the psychology of similarity and misconstrual (2.90-94): how one thing partially apprehended generates confident pluralities, which is both how analysis works and how it errs.

translation: As in the gavaya, or in the man-lion, enveloped in a single cognition, one apprehends a part as simply similar to another kind —
commentary: The similarity-psychology, opened with the tradition's two best specimens. The gavaya, the wild ox, is the upamana-example of every epistemology primer: the townsman told 'a gavaya is like your cow' meets one and *recognizes via similarity*. The narasimha, the man-lion — Visnu's terrible avatara — is the fused kind: one being, leonine above, human below. In both cases the object arrives eka-jnana-avrta, wrapped in a *single* cognition — one seeing of one thing — and within that single seeing a *part* presents as sadrsam jatyantarasya, similar to another kind altogether: the gavaya's dewlap recalls the cow, the man-lion's mane the lion. The datum Bhartrhari wants: similarity-to-another is apprehended *inside an undivided grasp of one* — the comparison does not dismember the object into a cow-part plus a remainder; the man-lion is not half a man. Partial resemblance, wholly registered, within unbroken unity: precisely the structure the sentence needs (2.93-94 will apply it), where 'shared words' between two sentences are dewlaps — real resemblances of undivided wholes, not shared components.
contested: the verse runs syntactically into 2.91 (yatha ... 2.92 tatha); we mark the suspension with a dash. Narasimhe is read by some as 'in (the word) narasimha' (the compound as example) — the perceptual reading fits eka-jnana-avrte better

translation: — but the part that is unfamiliar, unseen before, he beholds; and to that extent the bewildered man everywhere arrives at non-comprehension.
commentary: The error-half of the psychology. The provincial meeting the man-lion knows lion-manes; the human torso in that setting is aprasiddham, adrstam — unfamiliar, never seen *so* — and before it he is mudhah, bewildered: tavati asamvidam pratipadyate, to just that extent he arrives at non-comprehension, and (sarvatra) the bewilderment spreads over the whole: not knowing the part, he cannot close his grasp of the thing. The observation is exact: comprehension of wholes tolerates familiar-part-resemblance smoothly (2.90) and is *globally* disrupted by one unfamiliar region — the unknown does not politely subtract itself; it unsettles the entire object (as the pika-word did the sentence, 2.72, the parallel Bhartrhari is building toward). Confidence in wholes, fragility at unfamiliar parts: both facts will transfer to sentences in 2.92-94 — where they explain, respectively, why analysis works on familiar material and why 'similarity of parts' between sentences can seduce the analyst into inventing shared components (the fallacy 2.92 names).
contested: asamvidam ('non-comprehension') is our reading of the transcription; the line has been construed 'he attributes (to it) whatever he has not comprehended' — the psychology (local unfamiliarity, global unsettling) is common to both

translation: So, through the presence of a 'pika' and the like, in a sentence utterly distinct, one fancies — as of something similar — the cognition of a meaning that is not there.
commentary: The fallacy diagnosed, closing the triptych. A sentence containing the opaque 'pika' (2.72) is atyanta-vilaksana, utterly distinct — *its* meaning is its own, undivided, partly unmanifest to this hearer. But the hearer, like 2.90's provincial, finds familiar dewlaps: the *other* words, met before in other sentences. And by that yoga, that co-presence, he manyate — fancies — sadrsasya iva samjnanam asato 'rthasya: a cognition, as it were of the similar, of a meaning *that is not there* — he projects into this sentence the meanings those look-alike words 'had' elsewhere, assembling a pseudo-comprehension out of remembered fragments. The diagnosis cuts deep against deposit-semantics: what the atomist calls 'the word's standing meaning, carried into each sentence' is here anatomized as the *hearer's transfer by resemblance* — real psychology, illegitimate semantics; the analogical shadow of other wholes mistaken for a component of this one. Word-meanings, once more (2.17), are citation-artifacts — but now with the error-theory supplied: they are *memories of other sentences*, reified. What guards against the fallacy is what guarded the provincial: completing the acquaintance — hearing the whole as the whole it is.
contested: asato 'rthasya ('of a meaning that is not there') is pointed: not-present-in-this-sentence, rather than non-existent absolutely; some press the stronger reading (fancied meanings are unreal as such) — the diagnosis stands on the weaker

mula_deva: एकस्य भागे सादृश्यं भागे मेदश्च लक्ष्यते ।
mula_iast: ekasya bhāge sādṛśyaṃ bhāge medaśca lakṣyate |
translation: (93) Of one thing, similarity is marked in one part and difference in another — of a partless illumination, by a mind itself partless; (94) just so is similarity settled in one part and difference in another even of sentences whose properties are utterly distinct — though parts there are none.
commentary: The movement's resolution: partial similarity *without parts* — the apparent contradiction dissolved by the psychology just built. (93) states the general form with maximal paradox: one nirbhaga prakasa, one partless illumination (a color-expanse, a face, any unitary presentation), is marked similar-*here*, different-*there* — by a cetas, a mind, itself nirbhaga, partless (2.25-27: the sequenceless capacity). Neither term of the comparison has parts; yet 'part-wise' comparison happens — because *aspect-discrimination is the mind's act upon wholes*, not a reading-off of components: the partless cognition self-articulates (sva-saktya, 2.25) in registering respects of likeness. Two faces are alike 'about the eyes' though neither face is an assembly. (94) then delivers the application the movement was built for: so with sentences — atyanta-bhinna-dharmanam, of utterly distinct properties (each vakyartha its own undivided whole, 2.92), bhaga-abhave 'pi, *though parts there are none*: similarity settles 'in one part' (the shared-seeming words), difference 'in another'. Inter-sentential resemblance — the deposit-theorist's master-datum: 'the same word recurs with the same meaning across sentences' (1.71) — is thus accounted whole-to-whole: sentences resemble *in respects*, as faces do, and the 'recurrent word-meaning' is a respect of resemblance among undivided meanings, not a shared brick. The atomist's strongest evidence is hereby not denied but re-parented: recurrence is real, and it is a similarity-fact, not a composition-fact.
contested: 93's simile-base (the partless prakasa) is unspecified — light, cognition, or the sphota itself; all three work and the tradition wavers. The doctrine of 94 — respect-wise resemblance without composition — is the movement's secured yield on any choice

translation: If the form of the words is destroyed, how could there be the construction of a boundary? And when no boundary is grasped in the word, how is the meaning intended to be discerned?
commentary: An objector's double question, sharp enough that Bhartrhari devotes the next movement (2.96-111) to the machinery it demands. If — as the holism now says — the words' own forms are dissolved into the sentence (rupa-nasa: no independent word-reals), then: katham avadhi-kalpana — how is a *boundary* constructed at all? Where does one sentence (or word, in analysis) end and the next begin? And agrhita-avadhau sabde — the word/utterance having no grasped boundary — katham artho vivacyate: how is the meaning to be discerned/intended? Comprehension surely requires *delimitation*: the hearer must take *this much* as the unit to be understood (2.4's expectancy-closure presupposed a where-it-closes). The objection is the practical shadow of 2.76: if units dissolve upward and downward, the theory owes an account of segmentation — how speech is *portioned* by hearers and analysts if the portions are not objectively there. Bhartrhari's answer, unfolding across 2.96-111, will be drawn from grammar's own devices: tantra (single-utterance-serving-many), ekasesa (one-remainder), the sandhi-doctrine — cases where the science itself manufactures and dissolves boundaries at need, showing delimitation to be operational, not ontological.
contested: vivacyate we read with the transcription as from vi-vac (be discerned/expressed); a variant tradition reads vivaksyate ('is intended') — the objection is the same: no boundary, no determinate unit of meaning

translation: Like a commingling of forms, it stands settled in the word elsewhere; that form, among the various forms, is not measured out by single-performance.
commentary: The answer begins, with grammar's strangest boundary-phenomena as evidence. Forms samsarga iva, as-if-commingled: in the continuous utterance (samhita), word-shapes interpenetrate — sandhi fuses edges, one sound serving two words at once (the pararupa of 2.101 ahead) — the 'boundary' being genuinely *shared territory*. And such a form, standing among many forms, na tantrena parimisyate — is not measured out by tantra, single-performance: one cannot always utter a shared form once and have it count for each sharer; the commingled is not the common. The verse's work is to put on the table cases where wordhood's edges are *objectively indeterminate* in the utterance — fusion, sharing, overlap — so that the objector of 2.95 cannot pretend boundaries are simply given in speech and merely reported by grammar. They are not given; they are *managed* — by devices (tantra here glimpsed, ekasesa and the rest to come) whose very existence shows segmentation to be the grammar's engineering upon a continuum, exactly as the holist said (2.58: samhita as womb).
contested: the verse is among the kanda's most compressed and our construal — commingled forms resisting tantra-measurement — follows the movement's arc into 2.97-101; alternative readings take tantrena with what follows ('by the single utterance, that form is not exhausted among its various (occurrences)'), same evidentiary point

translation: In that non-difference, there operates as it were a commingling of differences; therefore the form, not being other than the other form, is (nonetheless) divided out.
commentary: The metaphysical gloss on 2.96's phenomena, stated as the movement's theorem. Tasmin abhede — in that non-difference, the fused continuum of actual utterance — bhedanam samsarga iva vartate: a commingling *of differences* operates, as-it-were: the analyst's word-forms are 'in' the stream the way currents are in a river — distinguishable flows of what is one water. Therefore: rupam rupantarat ananyat — the form is *not other* than the other form (at the fusion, the 'end' of one word and the 'start' of the next are numerically one stretch of sound) — and yet pravibhajyate, it *is divided out*: analysis assigns the shared stretch twice, to each word, without scruple and without falsity. Division-of-the-undifferent: grammar's daily sandhi-practice performs, at the phonetic joint, exactly the operation the holist claims for all analysis (2.10, 2.89) — real distinction drawn where no real otherness stands, valid as procedure, silent as ontology. The objector's boundary (2.95) is thus answered in kind: boundaries are *drawn*, expertly and reproducibly, upon the boundariless — and their reproducibility (the science's rules) is what their objectivity consists in.
contested: ananyat ('not other') we take at the fusion-point specifically; read globally ('the form is nothing other than the other form') the verse asserts full identity of all word-forms in the one utterance — the stronger claim is congenial to the kanda but the sandhi-context suggests the local one

translation: (98) In the science, sometimes oneness is relied upon even for the conveyor, while sometimes its difference from the conveyed is established — of grasper and grasped. (99) In 'u' oneness is relied upon and the respective-correspondence constructed; in the mention of lrn and lrt, difference is constructed together with the two graspables.
commentary: The evidence made technical: Panini's own metalanguage manipulates the identity and difference of *its own signs* at convenience. (98) states the practice: the sastra sometimes relies on the *oneness* of a pratyayaka, a conveying sign (one mention serving many conveyed items), sometimes on its *difference* from and among the conveyed — the grahana-grahya relation (mention and mentioned) engineered per rule. (99) gives the worked examples from the grammar's depths: the single technical 'u' relied on as one and distributed yatha-samkhyam, by respective correspondence (one sign, many assignments in order); the mention of lrn and lrt (the conditional and second-future affix-names) constructed as *different*, each with its own graspable. The argument: if word-forms were self-delimiting reals, the science could not treat one sign as one here and as many there, could not fuse and split mention at need — but it does, ubiquitously and without paradox, because signs *have* the individuation their use assigns (2.83's aspect-individuation, now in the metalanguage). Grammar's meta-practice is the finest-grained confirmation available: even where language talks about language, boundaries and identities are operational postings, not discoveries — and what holds of the mention holds of the mentioned (1.65's regress collapsed into evidence).
contested: the examples are stenographic and our unpacking (u-pratyahara distributed; lrn/lrt differentiated) follows the grammatical tradition's cases; specialists locate the precise sutras variously — the meta-linguistic moral is unaffected

translation: The form 'yasya' (in the rule) is the an-form expressing its name-bearers; for there is no possibility of mention by way of what is (merely) being understood.
commentary: A precision on how mention works, guarding the meta-practice of 2.98-99 from a misreading. In a rule like Panini's (the tradition points to 6.4.148, yasya iti ca), the cited form functions as the *an-form* — the technically-shaped sign-body — abhidhayaka of its samjnins, expressing the items it names. The principle in the second line: na hi pratiyamanena grahanasya asti sambhavah — mention cannot proceed by way of the merely-understood: what a rule *grasps* (grahana) must be a *form given in the rule*, not a content the reader would understand anyway; the metalanguage operates on exhibited shapes, not on inferred senses. This looks anti-holist — mention riding forms! — and is in fact the division of labor the settlement (2.88) drew: within the scaffold, forms are the currency (the science individuates by shape, cites by shape, 2.98-99 engineers shape-identity); the scaffold's forms are themselves postings within the undivided (2.97). Rule-craft is form-craft; comprehension is whole-craft; the kanda's care is to keep each sovereign in its own house.
contested: the sutra-context of yasya/an is disputed among the grammarians themselves and our identification follows the common gloss; the verse's principle — grahana requires exhibited form — is clear regardless of the rule intended

translation: And this 'u', undivided, is the fastening of different sentences; and its mention is made under difference — like the single subsequent-form belonging to both.
commentary: The boundary-sharing phenomenon named at last in its classical form. The undivided 'u' (the technical sign of 2.99, or the sandhi-vowel) serves as bhinna-vakya-nibandhanam — the *fastening of different sentences/rules*: one form, anchoring plural contexts, mentioned bhedena, under difference, as many as its employments. The simile seals it: pararupam iva dvayoh — like the pararupa, the 'subsequent form' of sandhi doctrine, where two adjacent vowels resolve into *one* sound that grammatically *belongs to both* words (the single 'e' of a fusion being the end of one word and the beginning of the next, by rule). One phonetic body, two grammatical citizenships: the limit-case of everything the movement has argued. Boundaries not only can be drawn on the undifferent (2.97) — the science *assigns the same stretch to two units at once* when the economy of description demands. After pararupa, the objector's 'where does the word end?' (2.95) has its answer: where the grammar, for its purposes, posts the end — including, when useful, in two places at once.
contested: whether the 'u' here is the pratyahara-sign continued from 2.99 or the sandhi-vowel is undecidable and immaterial — the pararupa-simile carries the doctrine either way

translation: And by the one who sets aside the lengthening of the base before pluta, and likewise the coalescence of the two vowels, difference among words is relied upon to the utmost.
commentary: The counter-practice acknowledged: grammar also *maximizes* boundaries when its purposes so run. Certain operations are vyudasyat, set aside — blocked: the pluta (protracted) vowel does not trigger the base-lengthening (anga-vivrddhi) that its shape might invite; vowel-coalescence (samahara acoh) is barred at certain junctures — and such blockings are effected precisely by *relying on difference to the utmost* (atyantam asritah bhedah): treating the words as utterly separate, their edges rigid, so that cross-boundary operations cannot see across. Read with 2.101, the pair completes the demonstration: the same science that fuses boundaries (pararupa: one form, two words) also *hardens* them (blocking: two words, no interaction) — per rule, per need. Boundary-strength is a *parameter of description*, tuned case by case — which is unintelligible if boundaries are facts of the speech, and exactly intelligible if they are postings of the analysis (2.97). The objector's segmentation (2.95) has now been shown, from the science's own casebook, to be manufactured in both directions: dissolved where description profits, absolutized where it must.
contested: the specific blockings compressed here are variously identified (the pluta-contexts of Panini 6.1, the samahara-exceptions); our rendering keeps the operations generic — the tuning-of-boundaries moral is the verse's evident office

translation: As, in words like ardharca, difference of form (obtains) according to sequence — so, by single-performance, when there is one-wordhood, the hearing of the different is otherwise.
commentary: A final pair of boundary-cases, from the lexicon's own oddities. Ardharca, 'half-verse' — the traditional head of a gana (the ardharcadi class) of words wavering between genders and, emblematically here, between *analyses*: read krama-wise, as sequence (ardha + rca), its form divides one way; read tantra-wise, as one word uttered once, the sruti — the hearing — of the 'different' components is anyatha, otherwise: the parts are not heard *as* parts at all. One phonetic body; two hearings; the difference made not by the sound but by the descriptive frame (sequence-analysis versus unit-citation) under which it is taken. The compound-word — language's own boundary-dissolving device, where yesterday's phrase is today's word — is the natural last witness: lexicalization is boundary-*history*, edges fading as analysis-frames shift, and no acoustic fact marks the transition. Segmentation, once more, follows the taking, not the sound (2.58's vrtti-bhedena, now at the lexical scale).
contested: the verse is elliptical to the edge of riddle; our construal — ardharca-class words as frame-dependent in their part-hearing — follows the gana-tradition and the movement's argument; readers who take ardharcadi merely as 'words with variable gender' get a thinner but compatible point (form-difference without meaning-difference, frame-assigned)

translation: In the domain of the continuous utterance, the phonemes, unchanging in their own form, go as it were to the condition of other words, through the assumption of another capacity.
commentary: The movement's doctrine crystallized in one verse — the samhita as the place where identity and difference trade places. In samhita-visaya, the continuous utterance, the varnas are svarupena avikarinah: unchanged in their own form — the sounds are what they are, the stream is one. And yet sabdantaratvam yanti iva: they *go, as it were, to being other words* — the same stretches, under sandhi and re-segmentation, belong now to this word, now to that (2.101's double citizenship) — sakty-antara-parigrahat, through the assumption of *another capacity*. Nothing in the sound changes; its office changes. Sakti, the kanda's deepest word (2.88: abstraction follows capacity-difference), here does its finest work: wordhood is a capacity *worn* by stretches of the invariant stream, and re-segmentation is capacity-exchange, not sound-change. The iva, as ever, holds the ontology honest: they do not *become* other words (there are no word-things to become); they present under other offices. The objector of 2.95 receives here the theory his question deserved: boundaries are the seams of capacities, and capacities are assigned by the whole (the one vakya, the one samhita) to its own presented articulations.
contested: none of weight; the verse is the movement's clearest and the tradition treats it as summary

mula_deva: आत्मत्यागादृते भिन्नं ग्रहणं स क्रमः श्रुतौ ॥ १०५ ॥
mula_iast: ātmatyāgādṛte bhinnaṃ grahaṇaṃ sa kramaḥ śrutau || 105 ||
translation: It is seen, among graspable things, that through modification of the sense-faculty and the rest, the grasping differs without the thing abandoning its own self; that is the way with the heard.
commentary: The epistemological warrant for 2.104, drawn from perception at large. Among grahya vastuni, graspable things generally, grasping varies indriya-adi-vikarena — with modifications of the faculty and its conditions: the same white shell grasped yellow by the jaundiced eye, the same taste sweet or flat by the fevered tongue — bhinnam grahanam atma-tyagad rte: the grasping differs *without the thing's abandoning its self*. Perceptual variation is routinely receiver-side; no one infers a new shell from a new seeming. Sa kramah srutau: that is the pattern with the heard — the one utterance-stream grasped now as these words, now as those (2.103-104), under different analytic 'faculties' (the frames, the trainings, the purposes of hearers and analysts), remains self-same throughout; the variation of segmentation is variation of grasping. The verse generalizes the kanda's constant move (1.75-77: delivery-properties are grasping-conditions; 2.25: sequence is the cognition's act) into a maxim: *difference of apprehension never by itself certifies difference in the apprehended* — the principle on which every reduction of parts to postings has quietly run, here stated as common epistemology.
contested: srutau we take as 'with respect to the heard (utterance)'; some take it 'in the Veda' (sruti), making the verse about scriptural construal — the maxim covers both and the movement favors the acoustic reading

translation: Through connection with the act of denoting, even among words unmodified, the form — one and the same — shines forth with utter difference.
commentary: The converse of 2.104-105, completing the chiasmus: there, invariant sound wearing different word-capacities; here, invariant word-form shining as utterly different *through its denoting-employments*. Abhidhana-kriya-yoga — connection with the act of denoting: the same form 'gauh', unmodified (avikrtesu sabdesu), deployed of the beast, of the constellation, in citation, in metaphor — tad eva ekam rupam atyanta-bhedena prakasate: the *very same one* form radiates with utter difference. Neither sound nor lexical shape changes; the act it serves in changes, and with it everything comprehension receives. Prakasate keeps the register honest — manifestation, not transformation: the differences are modes of the one form's shining, occasioned by employment (the vyapara-individuation of 2.83). Between 2.104 and 2.106 the full doctrine of linguistic identity stands: sameness of sound underdetermines wordhood (capacity decides); sameness of word-form underdetermines meaning (employment decides) — identity at every level is office-identity, and office is conferred by the undivided act of speaking. The Vedic recitation-sciences supply the next verse's confirmation.
contested: none of substance; the verse's placement (before the saman-example) marks it as the principle 2.107 instantiates

mula_deva: ऋचो वा गीतिमात्रे वा साम द्रव्यान्तरं न तु ।
mula_iast: ṛco vā gītimātre vā sāma dravyāntaraṃ na tu |
translation: The saman is (nothing but) the rks, or mere melody — not, however, a further substance; by difference of melody, those very rks, modified, are severally grasped.
commentary: The confirmation from sacred musicology — the tradition's own limit-case of one-form-many-shinings. What *is* a saman, a chant of the Samaveda? The doctrine the verse ratifies: the samans are the rks — the Rgvedic verses themselves — sung; or say, mere giti, melody, laid upon them; na tu dravyantaram: *not a further substance*, no third thing over verse and tune. Giti-bhedat, by difference of melody, ta eva rcah, those very verses, vikrtah, modified (stretched, repeated, ornamented by the chant's syllable-insertions), vigrhyante — are severally grasped, counted as distinct samans, catalogued, named, transmitted as separate sacred items. An entire Veda — its canon, its schools, its ritual employments — individuated by *mode of rendition* upon unchanged texts: office-identity (2.106) practiced at scripture's own scale, with the tradition's full authority behind the na dravyantaram. If the samans can be many while being the rks, the analyst's words can be many while being the one utterance (2.104): the sacred case licenses the semantic one.
contested: rco va gitimatre va compresses a debate the Mimamsa also conducts (is the saman the verse, the melody, or the composite?); the karika's own verdict — no further dravya — is what the argument needs, and both remaining options serve it

translation: When, by a device, the utterances of different words having a single remainder are gathered — in uttering them by single-performance, correctness in the science is accepted.
commentary: The ekasesa doctrine arrives — grammar's most audacious boundary-device, and the movement's crowning exhibit. Panini's rule (1.2.64ff.): when several identical forms would occur together ('ramas ca ramas ca'), *one remains* — ekasesa, single-remainder — 'ramau', 'ramah': one uttered form standing for many words. The verse: such gatherings, upayat, by device — sruti-samhare, in the gathering-up of the (plural) utterances — uttered tantrena, by single-performance (the ritual term of 2.77 now naturalized in grammar): sadhutvam isyate, correctness is accepted in the science. Weigh what the science thereby countenances: *several words* — really several, by its own count: each with its case, its reference, its role — *whose utterance is one form uttered once*. The deposit-theorist's minimal assumption — one word-token, one meaning-contribution — is breached by rule: here are meanings without severally-uttered vehicles, vehicles bearing plural citizenship not at their edges (2.101) but in toto. Only office-identity absorbs this smoothly: the one form serves many word-offices, the offices conferred by the construction's needs — the device is 'correct' because correctness was always adjudicated at the whole (2.109 next states exactly that condition).
contested: the transcription wavers (samhare/samharo); the ekasesa-identification is the tradition's and certain. Upayat ('by device') quietly re-invokes 2.38: ekasesa as upaya, the scaffold's own scaffolding

translation: And having taken up a single utterance even for those possessing difference of form, the uttering must be done by single-performance; otherwise they are not correct.
commentary: The ekasesa-doctrine extended and its condition stated with legislative force. Extended: even rupa-bhedavatam api — even where the gathered words *differ in form* (the dvandva-adjacent cases the grammarians debate: 'pita-mata' gathered as 'pitarau', 'parents', the mother uttered nowhere yet meant) — a single sruti is parigrhya, taken up, and the uttering *must* be tantrena, by single-performance. Then the condition, unblinking: anyatha te na sadhavah — otherwise (uttered severally where the device is prescribed) they are *not correct*. Mark the inversion this completes: correctness — sadhutva, Kanda I's dharma-bearing property (1.27, 1.141) — here *requires* the non-utterance of words that are nonetheless fully meant and grammatically counted. The severally-spelled-out 'correct' forms would be solecism; the fused device is the norm. Against any semantics that pins meaning to uttered vehicles, the science's own norm of correctness legislates meaning-without-vehicle wherever ekasesa runs. The scaffold does not merely tolerate the holist's ontology; at these joints it *mandates* it.
contested: which form-different gatherings fall under the rule is the grammarians' own long dispute (the ekasesa of 'pitarau'-type is contested in the tradition); the karika legislates the principle and leaves the casebook to the sastra

translation: And of sentences of like form, it is taught by the science: from utterance by single-performance, a single correct form is obtained.
commentary: The device propagated to the sentence-level — completing the movement at the grain where the kanda lives. Sarupanam vakyanam: sentences of like form — the shared refrain, the repeated mantra serving multiple ritual stations, the common formula meant severally in several connections. The science teaches (sastrena pratipaditam): uttered tantrena, once-for-many, ekam rupam sadhu upalabhyate — *one* correct form is obtained, and it discharges them all. Whole sentences, then — each with its own vakyartha, its own station, its own fruit — borne by one uttered body: the ekasesa-logic (2.108-109) at full scale. The theoretical harvest is the movement's QED: if one utterance can *be* many sentences (as office), then sentencehood was never in the utterance — it was always the office conferred by the employment-whole (the rite, the discourse, 2.76's larger unity) upon the uttered form. The boundary-question of 2.95 dissolves in its final form: not only are edges posted, whole *units* are posted — one body, many sentences; many bodies (the substitute-cases), one rite; the mapping engineered by the science at every grain, answerable only to the undivided purposes it serves.
contested: none of weight; the sentence-level tantra is the ritual-exegetical practice (one recitation serving many applications) and the verse cites it as settled

translation: As one thing comes to have many forms through the taking-up of the reed and the like — just so, through single-performance, many may come to have a single form.
commentary: The movement's closing simile, chosen for its perfect symmetry. One breath, taken up through nalika-adi — the reed-pipe and its kin (the flute's bores, the instruments' channels) — becomes many-formed: one wind, many notes, the plurality conferred by the apertures it is passed through. Yatha — and tatha, inversely: through tantra, single-performance, bahunam eka-rupata — the many (words, sentences, rites) come to *one* form: plurality gathered into a single uttered body, the unity conferred by the device it is passed through. One-to-many by instrumental taking-up; many-to-one by performative gathering: the two operations the whole movement has documented (2.96-110), here shown to be the same operation run in opposite directions — form and office trading multiplicities under devices. Beneath both, unmoved, the invariant: the breath; the utterance-stream; the undivided meaning. The simile quietly re-sounds 1.116's fan and the vamsa-flute of the old cosmologies: manifestation as one wind through many stops — the kanda's technical grammar of boundaries ending on the system's oldest music.
contested: nalika-adi we take as the wind-instrument's reed/pipe (the traditional gloss); some read 'the (moulding-)tube' of metallurgy — one metal, many castings; either instrument serves the one-through-channels figure

translation: As there is separate occurrence of sentences whose words are of like form, so, in the absence of another sentence, these would have separate meaningfulness.
commentary: A hinge-verse, turning the movement's results toward the kanda's next great topic (the abhidheya/prayojana distinction of 2.113). Sentences pada-sarupanam — of like word-forms — occur prthak, separately: the same wording, uttered in two settings, is *two sentences* (office-individuation, 2.110 inverted: one form, many units — here each unit its own occurrence). And vakyantara-abhave — where no other sentence stands (no larger discourse absorbing them, 2.76) — syad esam prthag-arthata: they *would have* separate meaningfulness, each its own vakyartha, notwithstanding identity of wording. Same words, different sentences, different meanings: the last nail in form-semantics, driven with the movement's accumulated hammer — if meaning rode wording, like wording would compel like meaning; it does not (the repeated formula means per station, 2.110; the quoted sentence means otherwise than its original; the rehearsed line otherwise than the avowal). What fixes each occurrence's meaning is its *employment-whole* — and the anatomy of that fixing, the difference between what a sentence's words denote and what the sentence is *for*, is precisely where the text now turns.
contested: the double conditional (occurrence separate; meaning separate *in the absence of a larger sentence*) we read as stated; some construe vakyantara-abhave as 'since there is no (real) other sentence' — a denial that re-wordings are ever distinct — against the movement's drift, and we decline it

translation: The expressible is the word's meaning; the purpose is the sentence's meaning — whoever (holds otherwise), for him no connection among sentences is intelligible.
commentary: The distinction on which the kanda's remaining semantics is built, legislated in a half-line each. Abhidheyah padasya arthah: the *expressible* — the denotable, the lexical content — is the word's meaning: what analysis assigns the extracted units (2.10), the scaffold's currency (2.88). Vakyasya arthah prayojanam: the sentence's meaning is *purpose* — prayojana: what the utterance is deployed to accomplish, its point, its fruit-in-view. The category-difference is the doctrine: word-meanings are contents; sentence-meaning is *telos* — not a bigger content assembled from small ones but a different kind of thing altogether (2.55's bhinna-laksana, now named): the one act-of-meaning whose service the contents are abstracted as serving. And the argument appended: whoever holds otherwise — meaning as content all the way up — tasya na sambandho vakyanam upapadyate: for him *connection among sentences* is unintelligible. Discourse-coherence (the samudaya of 2.76, the paragraph, the rite's liturgy) is teleological through and through: sentences connect by subserving ends within larger ends (2.82's viniyoga at discourse-scale); mere contents, stacked, connect not at all — no proposition reaches for its successor; purposes do. The kanda's two questions — what unifies the sentence? what unifies sentences? — receive here one answer: prayojana, the undivided point, at every scale the principle of unity that content-semantics forever lacks.
contested: yasya tasya we render 'whoever (holds otherwise), for him' with the standard supplement; the ellipse can be filled differently ('of whatever [word], connection... is not intelligible [without purpose]') — the abhidheya/prayojana legislation, the verse's substance, is textually secure

translation: There, it is the action-words themselves that mutually depend; and the connection, attached to the action-word, is thereby understood.
commentary: The teleology of 2.113 given its grammatical mechanics — how purpose-connection actually shows in the texture of discourse. Tatra — there, among connected sentences — kriya-padani eva vyapeksante parasparam: it is the *action-words* that mutually depend: verb reaches for verb across sentence-edges ('having-bathed... he-offers... he-recites...' — the rite's liturgy; 'she left... he followed' — the narrative's spine); the inter-sentential expectancy that 2.113 said content cannot ground runs concretely along the verbs. And the sambandha, kriya-pada-anusakta, attached to the action-word, atah pratiyate — is understood therefrom: discourse-connection is *read off* verbal dependence. The mechanics ratify the metaphysics: verbs are grammar's purpose-bearers — each names a sadhya, a to-be-accomplished (2.48) — so purposes-within-purposes (2.113's coherence) surface as verb-upon-verb dependence; the karaka-architecture that organized the single sentence (nouns orbiting the verb) iterates upward as sentences orbiting principal actions (2.6's tinanta qualifying tinanta, now at discourse-scale, its promise redeemed). One structure, three scales — noun to verb, clause to clause, sentence to sentence — and at every scale the same principle: unity flows from the end in view, and the end wears the verb.
contested: none of substance; the verse applies 2.6 and 2.48 at discourse-scale as we render, and the tradition reads it so

translation: On the construction of word-meaning as the individual: either repetition, or restatement; but the meaning, complete in each, operates in them as co-present.
commentary: A dilemma for individual-denotation, resolved by completion-per-each. If words denote vyaktis, individuals, then in 'gauh gauh' (or in a plural: 'the cows') the second occurrence is either avrtti, repetition — the same individual denoted twice, redundantly — or anuvada, restatement — reference back, adding nothing. Either way the second token is semantically idle, which affronts the economy every school professes. The holist's escape re-uses 2.18: pratyekam tu samapto 'rthah — the meaning is *complete in each* occurrence — and sahabhutesu vartate, operates in them as co-present: neither token is a fraction or an echo; each is a station of the whole meaning, their plurality the utterance's mode of presenting one content under co-presence (as each glance sees the whole painting, 2.8). The dilemma dissolves because it presupposed deposit-arithmetic — count the tokens, count the contributions — and the whole-at-each principle refuses the count.

translation: Though the sentence-meaning is free of construction, the constructions rest upon the bringing-into-being; on this topic the doctrines of the ancients have gone many ways.
commentary: The pivot into the kanda's center. The vakyartha itself is avikalpa — free of conceptual construction: one, undivided, given whole (everything since 2.7 has argued it). The vikalpas — the constructions, the itemized word-meanings and relations analysis reports — bhavana-asrayah: rest upon *bhavana*, the bringing-into-being — the cognitive realization-process in the hearer (2.27's bhavana-samaya, where the sequenceless capacity works). Constructions belong to the coming-to-understand, not to the understood: process-artifacts, real as process, projected as content. And the honest doxographic register resumes: atra adhikarane, on this very topic — the relation of constructed articulation to unconstructed meaning — the purvesam vadah, the ancients' doctrines, bahudha gatah, have gone many ways. The next verses (2.117-135) canvass them: pratibha by practice, convention, the denotation-catalogue — the tradition's whole semantics laid out before Bhartrhari's own resolution (2.143ff.). The avikalpa/vikalpa pair also silently aligns the kanda with the perception-debate (nirvikalpaka/savikalpaka): sentence-understanding as the grammarian's pratyaksa, given whole before construction — against the Buddhist, for whom construction *is* the verbal; the battle of 1.123 rejoined on new ground.
contested: bhavana we render 'bringing-into-being' (the realization-process); the term also means 'meditative cultivation' and 'the efficacy-toward-being' of Mimamsa verb-theory — Bhartrhari plausibly hears all three; the Mimamsa resonance matters at 2.124-125

translation: By others, every word is held to be a cause of intuition through practice — as in the conveying of things as they are to children and to animals.
commentary: Pratibha enters — through a reported view, as the kanda's great terms always do. Aparaih smrtam: others hold that sarvo 'pi sabdah, every word, is pratibha-hetuh abhyasat — a cause of *pratibha*, intuitive flash, through *abhyasa*, practice, repeated exposure (the term from 1.35: the jeweller's untransmittable expertise). Their evidence: balanam ca tirascam ca — children and *animals*: the calf finds the udder at a call, the dog answers its name, the infant obeys before it can parse — yathartha-pratipadane, in being brought to things as they are. Comprehension without grammar, lexicon, or inference: word acting on prepared life directly, waking a knowing that was never taught propositionally. The view's radicalism: it makes *practice-formed intuition* the entire mechanism of understanding — no deposits, no assembly, just trained flash — dissolving semantics into a natural history of responsiveness. Bhartrhari will not leave it here (2.118-119 immediately press its edges), but he has chosen his reporters carefully: the animal-and-infant datum is *unanswerable* by deposit-semantics (what lexicon does the calf consult?), and pratibha, entering as another school's word, will by 2.143-152 have become his own doctrine's name — abhyasa-formed intuition generalized into the very form of sentence-understanding, and (at 2.146-152) of skill and instinct across the living world.
contested: whose view 2.117 reports is unrecorded; the abhyasa-mechanism suggests naturalist strands the tradition preserves namelessly. That Bhartrhari ultimately *adopts* pratibha (2.143) while here attributing it is his standard doxographic choreography (cf. 2.30), not evidence against adoption

translation: And that practice, by some, is held to be convention, without received tradition — the showing that 'immediately after this, this is to be done'.
commentary: The practice-doctrine given its conventionalist reading, and quietly its most subversive one. Kaiscit: some construe the abhyasa of 2.117 as samaya — *convention*, agreement — and anagama, *without received tradition*: not the beginningless transmission Kanda I sanctified (1.27-31, 1.141) but mere settled usage, upadarsanam, an ostensive showing: 'anantaram idam karyam asmat' — after this (word), this is to be done. Language-learning as drill: word paired with expected act, the pairing exhausted by regularity — no eternal sambandha (1.23), no sista-lineage, just coordination grown stable. The view is the ancient ancestor of every use-theory of meaning: to understand a word is to know what to do next. Bhartrhari reports it without immediate rebuttal because its half-truth is one he needs (comprehension *is* trained responsiveness; the calf of 2.117 proves it) while its frame — convention *instead of* tradition — collides with his deepest commitments; 2.119 lets the collision surface on the conventionalist's own terrain: the words whose 'expected act' no drill could ever exhibit.
contested: anagamah we read as qualifying the abhyasa ('a practice without agama') — the conventionalist's denial of tradition; it has been read as a separate charge ('and that practice is un-traditional') in Bhartrhari's own voice, making the verse rebuttal rather than report — the sequel favors report

translation: 'All words have a meaning' — such is the mark of the conveyable; for 'apurva', 'deity' and 'heaven', they say, it is the same as for 'cow' and the rest.
commentary: The conventionalist's ostension-model tested against the words that break it — and the tradition's own semantics stated in the testing. The maxim: asti arthah sarva-sabdanam — all words have meaning; meaningfulness is the pratyayya-laksana, the defining mark of the conveyable (a 'word' that conveyed nothing would not be a word, 2.53). Now the test-set: apurva — the unseen ritual potency, the efficacy that bridges rite and remote fruit (Mimamsa's central posit); devata — the deity as injunction addresses it; svarga — heaven, the promised fruit no living speaker has seen. For these, they say, the semantics is samam gavadisu — *the same as for 'cow'*: meaning attaches in the ordinary way. But no upadarsana (2.118) ever paired 'svarga' with an exhibited heaven; no drill, no ostension, no expected-next-act exhausts these meanings. If they mean as 'cow' means — and the whole exegetical science (2.64-88) requires it — then meaning-as-such cannot be constituted by exhibition-drill: the conventionalist's mechanism fails precisely where scripture's core vocabulary lives. The verse thus turns the maxim of universal meaningfulness into an argument: semantics must be of a kind that covers the unexhibitable — which trained-flash pratibha (waked by words, not shown by objects) and eternal-sambandha tradition can claim, and samaya-drill cannot.
contested: whether 2.119 is the conventionalist's own boast (our reading: 'the same as for cow', which 2.120 then dismantles) or already the rebuttal is delicately poised; either way the apurva-set is the fulcrum, and the tradition reads the verse as the test it plainly is

mula_deva: न स शब्दस्य विषयः स हि यत्नान्तराश्रयः ॥ १२० ॥
mula_iast: na sa śabdasya viṣayaḥ sa hi yatnāntarāśrayaḥ || 120 ||
translation: But the grasping of the form through practice in observing usage — that is not the word's object; for that rests upon a different effort.
commentary: The discrimination that saves pratibha from its conventionalist reading — task 2.118 set, here discharged with one clean cut. Two things practice yields, and they must not be confused. Prayoga-darsana-abhyasat, from practice in observing usage, comes akara-avagraha — the grasping of the *form*: the learner's growing command of shapes, collocations, the sound-habits of the community (2.118's drill, real and necessary). But: na sa sabdasya visayah — that is not the *word's object*, not meaning; sa hi yatna-antara-asrayah, it rests on a *different effort* — the effort of form-acquisition, distinct in kind from the semantic waking the word performs (2.117). Learning the language and understanding the utterance are two achievements: drill builds the instrument; meaning happens when the built instrument is played (the fan of 1.116 must exist — and its existing is not the breeze). The conventionalist's error is a level-confusion: he watched the form-drill and called it semantics. With the cut made, both truths stand in order: abhyasa conditions comprehension (against intellectualism), meaning outruns every drill (against conventionalism, 2.119's test-words now accounted: their *forms* are drilled like any; their meanings wake as all meanings do — by pratibha, whose theory now has the field). The catalogue of denotation-doctrines (2.121ff.) follows as the last survey before that theory is named in full.
contested: yatna-antara ('a different effort') we take as form-learning versus meaning-grasp; some construe 'dependent on another's effort' (the teacher's) — the level-cut survives either construal

translation: Some differences are illuminated by words as their expressors; certain others, arising-in-consequence, are (also) thought to be word-meaning.
commentary: The denotation-catalogue opens with its governing distinction: the *expressed* versus the *entailed*. Kecid bhedah — some determinations — prakasyante sabdaih tad-abhidhayibhih: are illuminated by words as their proper expressors — what the word is *for*, its abhidheya (2.113). Kamscit — others — anunispadinah: *arising-in-consequence*, following along — determinations that come to cognition with the expressed without being expressed: say 'cow' and the hearer has also, willy-nilly, singular number, presence, generic shape. Are these consequences *meaning*? Manvate — some think so, stretching sabdartha to cover the whole cognitive wake; others (the verses following) draw the line tight. The distinction is the catalogue's instrument: each doctrine surveyed (2.122-135) is a different placement of the expressed/entailed boundary — jati-theorists parking individuals as anunispadin (2.122), form-theorists parking shapes (2.123), action-theorists parking usage (2.124) — and Bhartrhari's eventual verdict (2.134-135: comprehension varies per hearer; the boundary is drawn by vivaksa and construal, not found) is already latent in the fact that the boundary *can* be disputed at all.
contested: anunispadin ('arising-in-consequence') is the catalogue's coinage; 'concomitant', 'supervenient', 'resultant' all serve — the term marks the entailed-but-unexpressed, and our rendering keeps its processual flavor

translation: When the word conveys the universal, the individual comes along as an adjunct; the universal-word does not lean upon the differences residing in the individual.
commentary: The jati-doctrine, catalogued first as the grammarians' default (1.93's kin, Vajapyayana's line in the tradition's memory). The word expresses the universal: 'gauh' means cowhood. The vyakti, the individual — since cowhood stands nowhere but in cows — anusangini, comes along as adjunct: entailed, not expressed (2.121's boundary drawn at the genus). Consequence, stated as the doctrine's signature: na tad-vyakti-gatan bhedan jati-sabdo 'valambate — the universal-word does not *lean on* the differences residing in the individual: its semantic weight never rests on this cow's color, size, or station; those enter comprehension (if at all) as the adjunct's baggage, available to context, invisible to the word. The doctrine's strength is 2.69's avivaksa made systematic (unmeant particulars, principled); its strain shows where language *does* reach individuals ('bring *this* cow') — deixis, names, the whole apparatus the vyakti-theorists (Vyadi's line) built their rival case on. The catalogue holds both in suspension; the kanda's own resolution (individuation by employment, 2.104-106) has already dissolved the question's presupposition — that the word, rather than the utterance-act, is where denotation is fixed.

translation: Nor does the expressor convey the shapes of pots and the like; because it is installed upon the bare thing, the cognition of those is inseparably-consequent.
commentary: The boundary-instrument applied to a subtler case: shape. Say 'ghata', pot. The hearer's cognition arrives *shaped* — a bulging-bellied, narrow-necked something stands in it. Did the word convey the akara? The doctrine here catalogued answers no: na akaran pratyayati vacakah — the expressor does not convey the shapes; vastu-matra-nivesitvat, it is installed upon the *bare thing* — the pot as such, unconfigured — and tad-gatih nantariyaki: the shape-cognition is *inseparably-consequent* (nantariyaka, 2.81's term for the rite's inseparable elements): you cannot cognize pot bare, so shape rides in of necessity — entailed by the cognitive act's own conditions, expressed by nothing. The analysis is fine: it distinguishes what the word semantically carries from what cognition must supply to host the carriage — the imagery, the default configuration, the mind's obligatory furnishing. A hearer's rich pot-image is thus no evidence of a rich pot-meaning: semantics thin, hosting thick. The verse arms the catalogue's eventual moral (2.134-135): since hosting varies per mind while the word stands one, the seeming variety of 'what the word brings' is the variety of its receptions.
contested: vastu-matra ('the bare thing') is the doctrine's load-bearing posit — thing-sans-configuration — and its coherence is precisely what rival schools (Buddhist apoha-theorists above all) deny; the karika catalogues without defending it

mula_deva: क्रिया विना प्रयोगेण न दृष्टा शब्दचोदिता ।
mula_iast: kriyā vinā prayogeṇa na dṛṣṭā śabdacoditā |
translation: An action enjoined by word is never seen apart from employment; the employment, however, arising-in-consequence, is understood as the word's meaning.
commentary: The boundary-instrument at the verb — where Mimamsa lives. Kriya sabda-codita, action word-enjoined ('let him sacrifice'), is never seen vina prayogena, apart from *employment*: an enjoined action exists only as deployed — by an agent, with means, in performance; the bare act-type never walks alone. So what does the verb mean? The doctrine catalogued: the prayoga, the employment — arising anunispadi, in-consequence (the act-type cannot be hosted without it, 2.123's structure at the verb) — *is understood as* the word's meaning: comprehension takes the verb to mean the deployed act, agent-and-means included, because that is the only form in which action is cognizable. The placement is the inverse of 2.122-123: there the entailed was excluded from meaning (individual, shape); here the entailed is *absorbed into* meaning (employment) — the catalogue exhibiting, by design, that the expressed/entailed boundary migrates doctrine by doctrine. The Mimamsa resonance is loud: bhavana, the verb's efficacy-toward-being, with its trio of what-whereby-how, is exactly an employment-semantics of the verb — and 2.125 next catalogues its restriction.
contested: we read prayoga as 'employment/deployment' (the act as performed); the word also means 'usage' (the linguistic employment), yielding 'usage is understood as meaning' — a use-theory entry in the catalogue; the kriya-context favors the performative sense

mula_deva: नियतास्तु प्रयोगा ये नियतं यच्च साधनम् ।
mula_iast: niyatāstu prayogā ye niyataṃ yacca sādhanam |
translation: But the employments that are fixed, and the means that is fixed — of these, expressibility by the word is followed by others.
commentary: The employment-semantics restricted to its lawful domain. Not every deployment enters the verb's meaning — only niyatah prayogah, the *fixed* employments, and the niyatam sadhanam, the *fixed* means: what the injunction's own economy determines (the rite's constitutive performances, its textually-settled instruments — 2.47's niyama, 2.64-70's obtained-and-specified strata). Of *these*, aparaih anugamyate, others hold, sabda-abhidheyatvam — expressibility by the word: the verb means its act *cum* its fixed circumstantials; the variable ones (this priest, that morning) stay outside as mere hosting (2.123). The restriction rescues 2.124 from absurdity (else 'sacrifice' would mean every accident of every performance) and completes a small system: meaning = act-type + textually-fixed frame; entailment = whatever performance adds. It is the exegete's sruti/linga stratigraphy (2.73-74) reappearing as verb-semantics — and its lesson for the catalogue is the constant one: every workable denotation-doctrine ends by indexing meaning to *fixity*, to what the utterance-economy settles — fixity being precisely what the undivided vakyartha distributes (2.88), not what word-atoms possess.

translation: Or the aggregate is the expressible — a cumulation free of alternative-construction; or else an unreal commingling is, by some, held to be the word's meaning.
commentary: The catalogue reaches the composite-doctrines — and, in its second line, crosses a threshold. First: samudayo 'bhidheyah — the *aggregate* is what is expressed: word-meaning as the whole bundle (thing + universal + shape + number...), taken avikalpa-samuccaya, as a cumulation *without* alternative-construction: all-at-once, no either/or among aspects — the maximalist placement of 2.121's boundary (everything the cognition hosts, meaning owns). Then the leap: asatyo va api samsargah — or an *unreal commingling*: the word's meaning is a samsarga, a mixture — of thing and form, referent and concept — that is *asatya*, not real: a constructed composite answering to nothing's actual structure, thrown up by language itself. With that entry the catalogue admits semantic *irrealism*: meanings as useful unrealities — and Bhartrhari's own deep teaching (the vikalpas as bhavana-artifacts, 2.116; the constructed circle of 1.129) stands suddenly among the catalogued, wearing another school's name. The next verse completes the irrealist family and adds its subtlest member.
contested: the source's numbering staggers here (126ab with 127cd printed together); we follow the evident karika-boundaries. Asatya-samsarga is glossed by some as the Buddhist-leaning entry (meaning as constructed fusion), by others as a grammarian's own limit-doctrine — the catalogue's silence on attribution is likely deliberate NOTE: wisdomlib paginates 2.126-127 as half-verses with a duplicated '2.127cd' label; commentary for each verse is attached to its 'ab' page and covers the full verse.

translation: Or that real which has the unreal as its adjunct is the word's fastening; or the word itself, having come to the condition of identifying-speech, attains expressibility.
commentary: The irrealist family completed by its two profoundest members. First: asatya-upadhi yat satyam — the *real with unreal adjunct*: the word fastens (nibandhana) to something genuinely real — but only as dressed in unreal upadhis, the limiting-conditions construction supplies (the satya here inevitably recalling 1.1's Brahman under avidya's upadhis: word-meaning as reality-wearing-constructions, semantics as the microcosm of vivarta). Second, stranger and destined for a future: sabdo va api abhijalpatvam agatah yati vacyatam — or the *word itself*, come to the condition of *abhijalpa*, identifying-speech, attains expressibility: the word means... itself-as-fused-with-its-object; meaning is the word in a special reflexive condition. The term abhijalpa is defined in the very next verse — the catalogue pausing, uniquely, to unpack one of its own entries: a signal of weight. Between them the two doctrines bracket the deepest question the catalogue holds: whether the unreality in meaning belongs to the *object-side* (real thing, constructed dress) or to the *word-side* (the word itself, self-fused, doing duty as its own meaning) — and Bhartrhari's system, which identifies word and meaning at the root (2.31), has stakes in both.
contested: the source's segmentation of 126-127 is disturbed (halves printed under both numbers); our reconstruction follows the sense-units. Abhijalpa is rare enough that 2.128's definition is the tradition's anchor for it

translation: [Second half-lines of 2.126 and 2.127 respectively — see the commentary at 2.126ab and 2.127ab, which treat each verse entire.]
commentary: A note on the source rather than on the text. The wisdomlib edition paginates verses 2.126 and 2.127 as half-verses, and — evidently in error — assigns the identical label '2.127cd' to two different pages: one carrying the second half-line of 2.126 (asatyo vapi samsargah sabdarthah kaiscid isyate), the other the second half-line of 2.127 (sabdo vapy abhijalpatvam agato yati vacyatam). Because this edition keys its commentary to the source's own page labels, a single entry necessarily serves both pages. The substantive commentary for each verse is given complete at 'Verse 2.126ab' and 'Verse 2.127ab', where each karika is translated and discussed in full. Readers consulting the source directly should be aware that its labelling here cannot be relied upon.
source_anomaly: duplicate label in source; lines merged in document order

translation: When, through the connection 'that is this', the word's form is made one with the meaning — that word they declare to be abhijalpa, identifying-speech.
commentary: The promised definition, and one of the kanda's psychological gems. Take the recognition-structure so 'yam iti abhisambandhat — 'that is this' (2.40's own formula: the incoming identified with the possessed): when by it the sabdasya rupam, the word's form, ekikrtam arthena, is *made one with* the meaning — when 'cow' and cow have fused in the speaker's cognition so thoroughly that the word is transparently its thing, thing wordlike, word thinglike — tam abhijalpam pracaksate: that fused condition of the word is abhijalpa. The definition names ordinary semantic consciousness: the mature speaker does not experience word and meaning as paired items (only the analyst, and the learner mid-drill, do); he experiences *one* — the meant-word, the worded-thing (1.50's double shining, lived as fusion; the identification samjna/consciousness pun of 1.126 enacted). The abhijalpa-doctrine of 2.127 then reads: what a word expresses is itself-in-fusion — meaning as the word's achieved transparency. And the fusion explains the catalogue's whole disarray (2.129-130 draw exactly this): because word and meaning present as one, every school's boundary-dispute (which side owns what) is a quarrel over joints that lived speech does not exhibit — the joints appear only under vivaksa-driven analysis, which is where the next verse takes the ledger.
contested: none of substance in the definition; its reach — whether abhijalpa names a special condition (citation-like) or ordinary comprehension as such — divides readers, and we take the ordinary-condition reading with the movement's drift

translation: (129) Since their non-separate meaningfulness is unswerving by established currency, some one form, somewhere, stands as principal; (130) in the world, the word operates having assumed the form of the meaning; in the science, however, the two-formedness is divided out according to intention.
commentary: The fusion-doctrine made stratigraphic, and the catalogue's quarrels dissolved in two strokes. (129): tayoh aprthag-arthatve — word and meaning being non-separately meaningful (the abhijalpa-fusion), and that non-separation avyabhicarin, unswerving, secured by rudhi, established currency (2.128's 'that is this' hardened by usage, 1.128) — still, kimcid eva kvacid rupam pradhanyena avatisthate: some one form, in some context, stands as *principal* — now the thing-face (ordinary talk), now the word-face (quotation, grammar): salience rotates within the fusion without breaking it. (130) then draws the two-register conclusion: loke — in the world — the word operates artha-rupatam pratipannah, *having assumed the meaning's form*: transparent, self-effacing (1.54's unnoticed instrument; the world sees through words to things). Sastre tu — in the science — ubhaya-rupatvam pravibhaktam vivaksaya: the *two*-formedness is divided out, by intention: the analyst's vivaksa splits the fusion, holds word-face and thing-face apart, and works the seam (svam rupam, 1.59-68; the grahana-craft of 2.98-100). Between loka and sastra the whole catalogue is adjudicated: every doctrine surveyed was a sastra-posture — a way of splitting for a purpose — while lived meaning is the unsplit fusion; the boundary of expressed and entailed (2.121) is drawn by vivaksa, wherever the analysis needs it, and *that is all the drawing there is*.
contested: 129's syntax is dense (aprthag-arthatve rudher avyabhicarini as locative frame) and our construal follows the movement; some take rudhi as subject ('currency being unswerving in their non-separation'). The loka/sastra division of 130 is textually pristine and is the passage's yield

translation: Whether from incapacity or from omnipotence, the fixed construction of action and the rest, for one single meaning, is constructed by words alone.
commentary: The ledger's radical closing entry before the perception-turn: even the categories are wordwrought. The parikalpana — the construction of kriya-adi, action-agent-instrument-and-the-rest (the karakas, the very architecture of 2.48): for ekasya arthasya, one single meaning (the undivided vakyartha — one event, say), a *fixed* construal into doer-doing-done is laid down — sabdaih eva prakalpita, constructed *by words alone*. The event itself is one; that it comes carved as agent-acting-on-patient is the contribution of the sentence-form that presents it (active or passive, this the karta, that the karman — carvings the same event tolerates interchangeably). And the alternative etiologies, offered with a shrug: asakteh — from *incapacity*: language cannot present the undivided except through role-fictions (the mind's part-scaffolding, 1.85, 2.13); sarva-sakteh va — or from *omnipotence*: the word-principle (whose sakti binds the universe, 1.118) freely articulates the one as many. Weakness or sovereignty — the two readings of vivarta itself (1.1's contested edge) — and Bhartrhari declines to choose here as there. Either way the theorem stands: the categorial grid — action, agent, instrument, the furniture of every sastra including his own — is of language, not read off the world; Kanda III's samuddesas (padartha by padartha) will spend six hundred verses honoring exactly this theorem: each category examined *as what words posit*.
contested: asakteh/sarva-sakteh we read as genuine alternatives (incapacity of speakers/language, or omnipotence of the word-power); some assign the first to language and the second to the meaning — the theorem (categories as word-constructions) is unaffected

translation: Or: the meaning that is the object of cognition, fastened to the external thing — that, known as the external thing, is by some held to be the word's meaning.
commentary: The catalogue's epistemological entry — the doctrine that meanings are *mental contents mistaken for things*. The artha that words convey is buddhi-visaya, an object *of cognition* — a content in the mind — bahya-vastu-nibandhana, fastened to (occasioned by, answerable to) the external thing; and it is jnatah bahya-vastu iti — *known as* the external thing: comprehension systematically takes the inner content for the outer object. What 'cow' gives you is a cognition-shape, taken as the beast. The doctrine (kaiscid isyate — the Buddhist resonance is unmistakable: meaning as buddhi-pratibhasa, the apoha-tradition's ancestor) is catalogued without polemic because Bhartrhari needs its insight while rejecting its frame: that comprehension traffics in cognition-side contents is his own teaching (2.18's siddhi in comprehenders; 2.123's hosting; pratibha ahead) — that this convicts meaning of *error* (mistaking inner for outer) presupposes the very inner/outer ledger his word-monism dissolves (2.31: word and meaning two faces of one; 1.129: the 'external' circle itself word-built). The next verse presses the entry to its idealist limit — and there the kanda's own metaphysics looks back at the objector.
contested: the doctrine's attribution (Buddhist vijnana-leaning strands, or a grammarian's own buddhyartha-view) is the classical question; the karika's kaiscit preserves the ambiguity and we do too

translation: Those that appear in cognition, possessed of form, knowable, fastened to distinct memory — (so far as they so appear); but beyond that, it is mere awareness, otherwise.
commentary: The idealist limit, stated with a precision that leaves the catalogue behind. Whatever presents — the akaravantah, the formed ones: samvedyah, knowable contents, vyakta-smrti-nibandhanah, fastened to distinct memory (recognizable, re-identifiable — the whole apparatus of stable objects) — ye pratyavabhasante: they *appear*, and their being formed-knowable-memorable is exactly their manner of appearing. Atah anyatha: beyond that — behind, beneath the appearing — samvin-matram: *mere awareness*, awareness-only, unformed, unfastened. The verse's economy is severe: objecthood analyzed without remainder into appearance-structure; what is not appearance-structure is bare samvit. Whether this is the catalogued opponent's terminus (vijnana-vada naming itself) or Bhartrhari's own floor showing through (the samvit here and the sabda-tattva of 1.1 differing, on his account, in name only — awareness whose essence is word, 1.126) is genuinely undecidable and probably meant to be: at the limit, the grammarian's word-monism and the idealist's awareness-monism face each other as mirror-doctrines, quarreling over whether the ultimate light is verbal — the one dispute (1.123-124 already staked it) neither can cede. The catalogue ends here, on its deepest entry; 2.134 steps back into daylight with the observation that dissolves catalogues as such.
contested: the verse's voice — opponent's conclusion or author's floor — is the crux stated above; the transcription's pratyavabhasanta we read as present middle. Samvin-matram is the only occurrence of the term in the kanda and its weight is accordingly disputed

translation: As the sense-faculty, coming into contact, is a displayer according to its variety, just so from the word the comprehension of the meaning is manifold.
commentary: The daylight-observation that adjudicates the whole catalogue: *display varies with the displayer*. The indriya, meeting its object, upadarsakam vaicitryena — displays according to its own variety: the same lamp shows differently to the keen eye and the dim, the jaundiced and the clear (2.105's maxim, now aimed at semantics); no one concludes the lamp is many. Tathaiva sabdat — just so from the word: pratipattir anekadha, comprehension is *manifold* — the same utterance waking richer and poorer, thicker and thinner uptakes per hearer (the child, the expert, the exegete, the provincial of 2.91). The application to the catalogue is immediate and lethal: each denotation-doctrine (2.121-133) canonized *one uptake-profile* — the genus-grasper's, the shape-hoster's, the employment-reader's, the idealist's — and legislated it as 'the word's meaning'. But uptake-profiles are receiver-facts (2.123's hosting, 2.135 next: division per own-cognition); the word, like the lamp, is one, and the doctrines were a physiology of hearers mistaken for a semantics of words. What remains constant under all the variety — the one thing every uptake is an uptake *of* — is the undivided vakyartha; and the flash in which any hearer, at his own grain, seizes it whole now finally receives its name (2.143: pratibha).

translation: Set going by the speaker in one way entirely, among differing comprehenders the word's meaning is divided out, each according to his own cognition.
commentary: The catalogue's verdict completed on the speaker's side, and semantics resituated once for all between two minds. Vaktra anyathaiva prakrantah: launched by the speaker *in one way entirely* — the utterance leaves as one determinate act, the speaker's vivaksa whole in it (1.155's criterion; 2.130's intention). Bhinnesu pratipattrsu: among differing comprehenders it sva-pratyaya-anusarena pravibhajyate — is divided out, each according to his own cognition: every hearer carves the one launch at his own grain, with his own furnishing (2.123), his own training (2.117), his own purposes — the manifold of 2.134 given its mechanism. Between the one launching and the many carvings, where does 'the meaning' live? The kanda's whole architecture answers: in neither ledger as the doctrines kept it — not in word-atoms (dissolved), not in hearer-constructions (variable), but in the undivided act that launching and uptake both answer to: the vakyartha as the invariant of communication, seized whole (never carved the same way twice) by the flash each comprehension is. The two-mind picture also quietly prices error and depth: uptake can fall short of launch (the mudha of 2.91), exceed it (the exegete's construals), or meet it — and meeting it is not matching carvings but sharing the whole: the samepratibha waked. The stage for 2.143 is fully set; 2.136-142 first generalize the receiver-relativity to *seeing itself*, so that when pratibha is named it will be named for perception and speech alike.
contested: anyathaiva ('in one way entirely / quite otherwise') is pointed: some read it as 'otherwise (than the hearers take it)' — launch and uptake never coinciding; our reading ('in one determinate way') keeps the invariant the movement needs, and the two shade together in any case

translation: Even regarding a single visible object, seeing divides, each separately; and with the passage of time, even the same one sees it otherwise again.
commentary: Receiver-relativity generalized to perception — the first of the movement's two supports (2.137 gives the word-side) for reading comprehension-variety as unalarming. Ekasminn api drsye 'rthe: one visible thing, publicly there. Darsanam bhidyate prthak: the seeings divide, observer by observer — the painter, the timber-merchant and the botanist before one tree; the lover and the anatomist before one face. Kalantarena ca eko 'pi: and across time even the *same* seer — tam pasyaty anyatha punah: sees it otherwise again; yesterday's tree is not today's to the same eyes grown older. If seeing itself — the pramana of pramanas — is thus per-seer and per-time without anyone doubting the tree's unity, then comprehension's per-hearer variety (2.134-135) convicts language of nothing: it exhibits the common condition of all uptake of the one by the many. The verse is 2.105's maxim (difference of grasping certifies no difference in the grasped) turned from defense into framework — and it prepares the movement's harder edge (2.138-141): if all seeing is perspectival, the authority of *any* seeing, including perception's, needs auditing — which the seers' case (2.139) and the sky's false floor (2.140) then conduct.

translation: And of even a single word, by the fixed occasioning conditions, the meaning is constructed manifoldly — by one (hearer) and by many.
commentary: The word-side support, completing the pair. Ekasya api sabdasya: one word, self-same. Nimittaih avasthitaih — by the occasioning conditions, themselves *fixed* (the contexts, constructions, employments: 2.148's nimitta-bheda, the settled apparatus of use) — arthah bahudha parikalpyate: the meaning is constructed manifoldly — ekena bahubhis ca: by one hearer (across occasions, moods, ages: 2.136's diachrony imported) and by many (across the room: 2.135's synchrony). Note the care: the nimittas are avasthita, *fixed* — the variety is not anarchy; construals are occasioned, rule-shadowed, answerable (the same fixities the exegete's science maps, 2.64-88) — and yet within the fixities, parikalpyate: construction, per mind, irreducibly. Law without uniformity: the shape of every practice the kanda has audited (grammar, exegesis, perception) and now of meaning itself. With both supports standing — seeing divides (2.136), construal divides under fixed occasions (2.137) — the movement can draw its severe epistemological conclusions: what *never* divides is not given to ordinary seers and speakers at all (2.138-139), the given teems with false floors (2.140), and the wise must therefore audit even perception by reason (2.141) while leaving the world's working namings undisturbed (2.142). Then, against that chastened background, the one positive absolute the kanda will assert: the flash (2.143).

translation: (138) Therefore, for those who have not seen reality, seeing and speech alike are at fault, full of traps, forever unsettled; (139) and the seers' vision, which is somewhat settled in reality — by it there is no worldly practice, nor is it fastened to words.
commentary: The movement's severe theorem: between fallible practice and settled vision there is no overlap. (138): for adrsta-tattvanam, those who have not seen reality — all of us in the ordinary case — both darsana and vacana, seeing and saying, are sa-aparadham, at fault, bahu-cchalam, full of traps (chala: the debater's snare — our very perceptions and phrasings bait us), nityam eva anavasthitam: *forever unsettled* — the per-seer, per-time, per-hearer divisions of 2.136-137 now pronounced as chronic condition, not occasional noise. (139): and the rsis' darsana — which is kimcid avasthitam tattve, somewhat settled in reality (the vision-knowledge of 1.37-38, its authority intact) — na tena vyavaharah asti: by it *no worldly practice runs* — the settled seeing does not transact, buy, teach, or litigate; and na tat sabda-nibandhanam: nor is it fastened to words — what the seer sees whole does not parcel into speech (1.145's dream-mode showing; the param padam beyond the three, 1.142).
