The theorem cuts both ways with equal edge: practice cannot borrow vision's settledness (the market runs on trapped seeing and unsettled saying — and runs); vision cannot fund practice (settled, it falls silent). Between them stands everything the two kandas have built: language as the unsettled-but-lawful medium (2.137) in which unseeing beings transact — redeemable not by importing the seers' silence but by the discipline of the word itself (1.14, 1.131). 2.140 next gives unsettled seeing its emblem; 2.141-142 give the wise their protocol for living in it.
contested: kimcid ('somewhat') in 139 is a studied qualification — even the rsis' vision is only *somewhat* settled, or settled *in some respect*; readers who take it as 'a certain vision, settled' remove the hedge; we keep it — the theorem is harsher and more Bhartrharian with it

mula_deva: नैव चास्ति तलं व्योन्मि न खद्योतो हुताशनः ॥ १४० ॥
mula_iast: naiva cāsti talaṃ vyonmi na khadyoto hutāśanaḥ || 140 ||
translation: The sky is seen as if it had a floor; the firefly, like a fire-carrier; and there is no floor in the sky at all, nor is the firefly fire.
commentary: The emblem: perception's confident deliverances of what is not there. Talavat drsyate vyoma — the sky *looks floored*: the vault, the dome, the blue ceiling every eye reports and no sky possesses; khadyotah havyavad iva — the firefly like a fire-bearer: a spark, to all seeing, and no fire at all. Na eva ca asti talam vyomni, na khadyotah hutasanah: flatly — no floor, no fire. The examples are chosen as *universal and incorrigible* illusions: not the sick eye's jaundice (2.105) or the fool's fancy (2.91) but percepts every healthy observer shares and no correction dispels — one still *sees* the dome after learning there is none. Perception at its best, unanimous and stable, can thus be false to fact: unanimity is no truth-mark, stability no floor (the pun is the verse's own). This is 1.129's alata-cakra radicalized — there, construction where nothing was; here, construction *canonized in every normal seeing* — and it discharges the movement's last debt: if even agreed perception needs auditing, the variety of comprehension (2.134-137) was never the scandal; the scandal would be trusting any uptake, however shared, without the yukti that 2.141 now prescribes.
contested: none; the verse is limpid and the tradition quotes it widely — its work is emblematic, and the floor/settledness pun (tala/avasthita) may be heard or not without loss

translation: Therefore the wise man should examine even a perceptible object by reasoning; he should not posit the object seen merely on the authority of seeing.
commentary: The protocol, drawn with tasmat-force from the emblem. Pratyaksam api artham — even the *perceptible*, even what stands before the eyes — vidvan ikseta yuktitah: the wise man should examine by reasoning; na darsanasya pramanyat drsyam artham prakalpayet: he should not posit the seen object on seeing's authority alone — the dome and the firefly's fire being exactly what such positing yields. Mark the placement of this rationalism: Kanda I demoted tarka against agama (1.30-42: reasoning cannot found dharma); here tarka is *promoted* against unaudited perception — and the two rulings are one epistemology, not a reversal: reasoning is corrective, never foundational (1.135-137: the eye for the sightless, sovereign nowhere, employed everywhere) — it audits the given (perceptual or textual) without replacing it. The verse's quiet radicalism is its scope: pratyaksam api — the pramana every school treats as archetype is made a defendant; where 2.140 showed *that* seeing errs incorrigibly, 2.141 draws the duty: conviction is earned by cross-examination, not conferred by vividness. For the kanda's own subject the transfer is immediate: comprehension (speech's 'perception') likewise binds only as audited — the exegete's science (2.64-88) being exactly that audit, institutionalized.
contested: none of substance; the verse is the tradition's standing citation for grammarian rationalism, and its harmony with 1.30-42 (corrective vs. foundational tarka) is as we state

translation: The naming that worldly speakers, in practice, give to things whose reality cannot be told — the truly wise man should not construct alternatives against it.
commentary: The protocol's companion, guarding it from misuse — audit the given, but do not harass the working namings. Things are, in their tattva, asamakhyeya — *untellable*: reality outruns designation (the seers' settled vision itself sabda-free, 2.139; the untellable expertise of 1.35). Yet laukikaih, worldly speakers, vyavahare, in practice, samakhyanam — name them, and the namings *work*: the market's 'cow', the household's 'fire', the whole serviceable taxonomy of the unseeing. Tat prajnah na vikalpayet: against *that*, the truly wise man constructs no alternatives — he does not correct the world's speech toward metaphysical exactitude, does not replace 'the sky's dome' with disquisitions at the well. The pairing with 2.141 is exact and deliberate: examine even the perceived (rigor inward, where conviction is formed); leave the world's namings standing (charity outward, where practice runs) — for the namings never claimed tattva; they claimed serviceability, and by that claim they are true (vyavahara-satya, the working truth the whole system has honored since 1.28's vyavastha). The wise man's silence before working speech is thus not condescension but accuracy about what kind of instrument language is — and it is the exact temper in which Kanda III will examine the categories: each padartha audited as *posited by words for practice*, none arraigned for failing to be the untellable.
contested: na vikalpayet we render 'should not construct alternatives against (it)'; the verb also yields 'should not subject it to (skeptical) analysis' — the charity-ruling is the same; note the deliberate echo and inversion of 1.29's nanarthika vyavastha (the sista's codification purposeful) — here the *world's* codification receives the same protection

translation: When the meanings have been grasped in their separation, an intuition altogether other is born; that, brought about by the word-meanings, they call the sentence-meaning.
commentary: The kanda's summit: the vakyartha named at last, and its name is *pratibha* — the flash. The mechanics first, stated with all the movement's accumulated care: viccheda-grahane arthanam — when the meanings (the padarthas, the scaffold's items) have been grasped *in their separation* (the analyst's and learner's part-wise passage: 1.90's seizures, 2.13's ladder) — pratibha *anyaiva* jayate: an intuition *altogether other* is born — other in kind, not in degree; not the parts' sum, echo, or relational closure but a new indivisible arising (2.55's bhinna-laksana cashed): the understanding itself, the click, the whole dawning at once. Tam vakyartha iti ahuh: *that* they call the sentence-meaning — not a proposition the flash grasps: the flash *is* the meaning, comprehension and content one event (2.31's identity, lived). And the genetic clause, holding both truths without strain: padarthaih upapaditam — *brought about by* the word-meanings: the parts are real causes (upapadita — occasioned, ushered) of what they neither contain nor compose; scaffolding that precipitates the edifice (1.84's ripening; 2.18's per-word re-completion; 2.38's discharged means — every earlier structure converging here). This is the doctrine the opening catalogue promised under 'buddhy-anusamhrti' (2.1) and the whole kanda has been assembling: sentence-meaning as an *event in the comprehender* — indivisible, occasioned, other — the flash in which the trained mind (2.117's abhyasa) meets the launched whole (2.135) and the undivided shows itself through its own dismantled ladder. The verses following will defend its ineffability (it cannot be told to another — only waked), distribute it across the living world (the bird's nest-craft, the beast's know-how: pratibha as the very form of practical intelligence), and ground it finally in the word-essence itself: the flash as the sabda-tattva's self-recognition in a mind — 1.142's sabda-purva yoga arriving at its terminus.
contested: anyaiva is the verse's hinge and we press it fully ('altogether other' — the flash as novel indivisible); a softer reading ('quite different [from the padarthas]') is possible but under-reads the kanda's architecture. That pratibha is here *defined* as vakyartha (not merely compared to it) is the tradition's own reading and ours

translation: It cannot in any way be told to others as 'this is that'; established by its own operation within each self, it is not describable even by the one who has it.
commentary: Pratibha's first mark: ineffability, and of a peculiarly strict kind. It cannot be told to others idam tad iti — under the 'this is that' formula, the very structure of identifying-communication (2.40, 2.128's so 'yam): to convey a content one must pair it with something the hearer already holds, and the flash pairs with nothing — it *is* the pairing-event, not a term in one. Pratyatma-vrtti-siddha: established by its own operation *within each self* — self-certifying, self-locating, irreducibly first-personal. Then the harder clause, which distinguishes this from ordinary privacy: kartrapi na nirupyate — it is not describable *even by its own possessor*. Not merely 'I cannot convey it to you' but 'I cannot state it to myself': the flash is not an inner object one inspects and then reports badly; there is nothing standing at a distance to inspect. This is the exact structure of 1.35's connoisseur — the jeweller who knows and cannot say what he knows, asamakhyeya — now generalized from expertise to *every act of understanding a sentence*. Every comprehension is connoisseurship. Note what this costs and buys: it costs the theory any hope of *displaying* the vakyartha (one can only occasion it in another, by uttering — which is what speech is); it buys the explanation of why every attempt to state a sentence's meaning yields another sentence, needing its own flash, forever.
contested: kartra ('by the agent/possessor') we read as the one in whom the pratibha arises; some construe 'even by the speaker' (the utterer cannot describe what he wakes) — both readings make ineffability structural rather than merely practical

translation: Unexamined, it effects as it were a close conjoining of the meanings; and, as though all-formed, it operates as their objecthood.
commentary: Pratibha's second mark: it does the work that connection-theories invented sambandha to do (2.42-46), and does it without machinery. Upaslesam iva arthanam sa karoti — it effects, as it were, a *close conjoining* of the meanings: in the flash the items cohere — the cow is brought, the agent acts, the parts hold together — precisely the relatedness the abhihitanvaya theorist had to purchase with an imperceptible, non-thing connective (2.46). And avicarita — *unexamined*: the conjoining is not a judgement one makes but a coherence one finds already made; scrutiny arrives afterward, upon a whole already unified. Sarva-rupyam iva — as though *all-formed*, wearing every relevant form at once (the whole complete in each aspect, 2.18) — visayatvena vartate: it operates *as their objecthood* — the flash is not a cognition *of* objects standing apart; it is that in which the meanings have their standing-as-objects at all. Both iva's are exact: the conjoining is real-as-presented, not an assembly performed; the all-formedness is completeness, not a sum of forms. Where the rivals posited relations between semantic atoms, pratibha *is* the relatedness — self-arising, prior to inspection, constituting rather than linking its terms.

translation: Whether generated directly by the word, or through the following of realization — in the matter of what-is-to-be-done, no one at all transgresses it.
commentary: Pratibha's third mark: it is *practically sovereign*. Two genetic routes are conceded: saksat sabdena janitam — generated directly by the word (the hearer's flash on being spoken to: the kanda's home case); or bhavana-anugamena — through the following of bhavana, realization and cultivation (2.116's process, 2.117's abhyasa). Either origin, one authority: itikartavyatayam — in the domain of what-is-to-be-done (Kanda I's term for practical know-how, 1.121, there declared wholly word-dependent) — na kascid ativartate: *no one transgresses it*. Not 'no one should' — no one *does*: when the flash says what is called for, action follows its dictate; deliberation neither precedes nor overrides it, because deliberation is itself conducted in flashes. This is the doctrine's turn from semantics toward life. Comprehension, know-how and conduct converge on a single phenomenon: the immediate, untellable, self-certifying seizure by which any agent knows what is to be done. The next verses press exactly this convergence outward — first to the whole human world, then, far more radically, to the animal one.

translation: The whole world regards it as a means of knowledge; and through it the undertakings even of animals are extended.
commentary: The sovereignty of 2.146 given its two-fold evidence. First, epistemic standing: pramanatvena tam lokah sarvah samanupasyati — the *entire* world regards pratibha as a pramana, an authoritative means of knowing: not a private feeling but the thing everyone in fact acts on and treats as knowledge — the schools' formal lists (perception, inference, testimony) notwithstanding, the working epistemology of every agent is the flash. Second, and with a jolt: samarambhah pratayante tirascam api tadvasat — undertakings *even of animals* are extended by its power. Tiryanc — beasts, 'the horizontal ones' — has appeared before (1.126: sentience in all kinds; 2.117: children and animals comprehending), but here it becomes the argument's centre of gravity: if the flash is what animals act by, then pratibha is not an achievement of language-users at all — it is the form of practical intelligence as such, and human sentence-comprehension is one dialect of a capacity running through everything alive. The verses following make good on this with a series of examples remarkable for their observational precision.

translation: As, from the ripening of particular substances, powers such as intoxication are seen to arise without effort — so are the intuitions of those who possess them.
commentary: The naturalist model for pratibha's arising, chosen with care. Certain substances, on paripaka — ripening, fermentation — develop mada-adi-saktayah, powers such as intoxication: the grape left to itself becomes wine; nobody *makes* the power, and no ingredient contains it. Ayatna-jah: they arise *without effort* — unwilled, unassembled, the outcome of a thing's own maturation under conditions. Pratibhah tadvatam tatha: so with the intuitions of those who have them. Every clause carries doctrine. Ripening: the flash comes of maturation (abhyasa, 2.117; the seed-and-paripaka of 1.84), not of construction. Effortless: it is not produced by the will that awaits it — one cannot decide to understand; understanding dawns (2.145's avicarita). And *emergent*: intoxicating power is not found in the grape's parts, as the vakyartha is not in the padarthas (2.143's anyaiva) — the fermented whole has a capacity its ingredients lack. Bhartrhari here naturalizes the flash without deflating it: pratibha is a real power of a matured being, as wine's potency is of a matured substance — which is precisely why it can belong to birds as well as to brahmins, as the next verses insist.
contested: tadvatam ('of those who possess them') is neutral as to the possessors' kind — deliberately, given 2.147's animals and 2.149's cuckoo; some construe 'of those who possess (the requisite ripening)', which tightens the analogy further

translation: Who modifies the voice-function of the male cuckoo in spring? By whom are creatures like the spider taught in the making of nests and the like?
commentary: Two questions, and their form is the argument. Spring comes; the kokila's song changes — its svara-vrtti, its very voice-function, transformed, on time, without instruction: kah vikurute, *who* does this? The spider and its kin build — kulaya-karane kena siksitah: by *whom* taught? The expected answers are all unavailable. Not imitation (the hatchling spider has watched no one). Not inference (2.34's reasoners, deliberating, could not derive a web). Not agama in the narrow sense (spiders keep no lineage of sistas). Not trial and error (the first web holds). The tradition around these verses reaches for karma and vasana — ripened residues of prior lives — and Bhartrhari's own bhavana-vocabulary (2.146, 2.151) accommodates that. But the verse's philosophical force does not depend on the metaphysics of rebirth: it depends on the *phenomenon*, undeniable and undomesticated by any epistemology of the day — determinate, complex, immediately-known, unlearned competence. Naming it pratibha is less an explanation than a placement: whatever the cuckoo has, the hearer of a sentence has too, and the schools admitting only perception, inference and testimony have no cell for either. A modern reader will recognize the questions as those that eventually produced ethology; Bhartrhari's use of them is to widen the concept of knowing until it is large enough to hold both the web and the word.
contested: jantvadayah we take with the tradition as spiders and such web/nest-builders (kulaya covers both); the list is deliberately open — the argument wants the whole class of unlearned crafts

translation: In the activities of eating, affection, aversion, flight and the rest — established by kind and by descent — who is the employer, for beasts and birds?
commentary: The interrogation widened from craft to the whole repertoire of animal life. Not merely nest-building but the basic modes: ahara, feeding; priti and apadvesa, affection and aversion (the animal's evaluative world: what it goes toward, what it flees); plavana-adi, springing and flight. And the specification that matters: jaty-anvaya-prasiddhasu — *established by kind and by descent*, running true in the lineage, each species with its settled repertoire, transmitted without teaching. Kah prayokta: *who is the employer* — who deploys these activities, sets them going at the right moment toward the right object? The question is put in the grammarian's own idiom: prayoktr is the term for the speaker, the one who employs words (1.130's inner word standing in the prayoktr's self; 1.147's speaker intending gauh). The choice is exact and discloses the design: as the speaker's inner word deploys itself into audible speech, so *something* deploys the beast's inheritance into timely action — and the answer is not a further agent but the pratibha itself, the ripened power (2.148) that is its own employer. Life's know-how and speech's comprehension are one structure at two grains — which is why 2.151 can say, with a straight face, that this too comes from agama.
contested: the transcription's apadvesa/plavana we read as aversion and springing-flight; the exact list varies in the manuscripts and nothing turns on its members

translation: This arises precisely from tradition, which is followed by realization; but tradition is distinguished according to nearness and remoteness.
commentary: The audacious answer to 2.149-150, and the verse where the kanda's naturalism and Kanda I's traditionalism become a single doctrine. Whence the spider's web, the cuckoo's spring song, the beast's fear? Bhavana-anugatad agamad eva jayate: from *agama* — received tradition — followed by bhavana, realization. The claim sounds absurd only if agama is confined to texts. Bhartrhari's move is to widen it: what is handed down and ripened *in a lineage* is agama, whether the vehicle is a recension of the Veda or the inheritance of a species (2.150's jaty-anvaya-prasiddha was already the tell). Then the calibration that makes the widening precise rather than glib: asatti-viprakarsabhyam agamas tu visisyate — tradition is *distinguished by nearness and remoteness*: the brahmin's Veda is proximate agama, recited yesterday, present to consciousness; the spider's is remote — sedimented past all recollection, ripened into the body's own certainty, and for that reason *more* unerring, not less (the web never fails; the recitation may). This is Kanda I's deepest thesis (1.30: even the seers' knowledge is agama-purvaka; nothing is known without inheritance) extended to its limit: *all* competence is inherited and ripened, and the difference between scripture, expertise and instinct is one of distance, not of kind. The six-fold classification follows immediately, and this verse is its ground.
contested: reading agama here as 'tradition/inheritance' in the wide sense is required by the context (spiders have no scripture) and is the tradition's own gloss; a narrower reading forces the verse into incoherence, which is itself an argument for the wide one

translation: From nature, from conduct, from the practice of yoga, from the unseen, from what is brought about, and from what is conveyed by the distinguished — thus they know intuition to be sixfold.
commentary: The classification, and with it the movement's close: pratibha in six kinds, ordered — not accidentally — from the animal to the sage. Svabhava, nature: the cuckoo's song, the spider's web (2.149-150) — flash as the ripened endowment of a kind. Carana, conduct: the flash bred by a way of life, by observance and habit of living. Abhyasa, practice: the jeweller's eye, the grammarian's ear (1.35, 2.117) — expertise. Yoga: the flash of disciplined contemplation, the yogin's seeing (1.37's unclouded mind). Adrsta, the unseen: what karma ripens unaccountably — the sudden knowing no biography explains. And visistopahita, conveyed by the distinguished: the flash *transmitted* by an eminent one, teacher to pupil, seer to hearer — the only member of the six that passes between persons, and it does so by *waking* rather than telling (2.144's ineffability strictly preserved: the master does not describe the flash, he occasions it). Read against 2.151, the six are the spectrum of agama by distance: nature is tradition at its most remote and most certain, transmission from the distinguished its nearest and most fragile, the middle terms marking the intervals. Read against 2.143, the whole is the kanda's answer to its opening question — what is a sentence's meaning? A flash of the sixth kind, waked in a hearer by a speaker; the same flash, at bottom, by which the bird knows its nest. The Vakyakanda's central argument is complete: sentence-meaning is not a thing assembled out of words but an event in a living mind, continuous with all knowing-how, occasioned by speech and ineffable in it.
contested: the six members are stable in the tradition though their glosses vary; visistopahita is the contested one — 'conveyed by (contact with) the distinguished' (our reading, making it transmitted pratibha) versus 'endowed by a distinguished cause', which loses the transmission-sense the sequence seems to want

section: What the Word Does Not Convey
mula_deva: गोशब्दो न त्वसौ तेषां विशेषाणां प्रकाशकः ॥ १५३ ॥
mula_iast: gośabdo na tvasau teṣāṃ viśeṣāṇāṃ prakāśakaḥ || 153 ||
translation: (153) As the word 'cow' is employed with respect to an object marked out by the substances conjoined with it, and yet is not the illuminator of those particulars — (154) so, though the word operates upon cows and the like which are commingled with shape, colour and limbs, it does not take those in.
commentary: A new movement opens, and its method is subtractive: to fix what the word conveys by cataloguing what it does not. The object 'cow' is met samyogibhih dravyaih laksite — marked out by the substances conjoined with it: the beast comes with horns, dewlap, tether, field, all present in the encounter, all serviceable for pointing it out. And yet: na tv asau tesam visesanam prakasakah — the word is *not the illuminator of those particulars*. (154) makes the point structural rather than incidental: akara-varna-avayavaih samsrstesu — shape, colour and limbs being *commingled* in every cow one meets (there is no unconfigured cow to encounter) — sabdah pravartamano 'pi na tan angikaroti: the word, though it operates upon such commingled objects, *does not take them in*, does not accept them into what it says. The distinction being drawn is between the object's furniture and the word's cargo: everything the thing has is present to the hearer, and almost none of it is *meant* (2.69's avivaksa, 2.123's inseparable hosting, now made the organizing principle of a whole movement). The verses following (2.155-163) will test the principle at its edges: parts, quantity, measure, the conditions under which usage lapses — mapping precisely how little the word carries and how much the encounter supplies.

translation: The word that is employed with respect to a thing distinguished by configuration, colour and the rest — its operation is not found in that thing's limb.
commentary: The subtraction's first theorem: word-meaning does not distribute to parts. 'Cow' is employed of a thing that is samsthana-varnadaih visistam — distinguished by configuration and colour: a whole individual, determinate in every respect. Yet na tasya avayave pravrttir upalabhyate: the word's operation is *not found in its limb* — the cow's leg is not a cow, and 'cow' has nothing to say about it. The observation looks trivial and is not: it establishes that denotation is grain-fixed and non-inheriting, the semantic counterpart of 2.68's maxim (a word is not the expressor of everything that exists). If word-meaning flowed to parts, 'cow' would half-mean 'hoof', and every term would leak into its object's mereology. It does not: the word settles at exactly one grain — the grain its employment sets (2.104-106) — and both the whole's furniture (2.153-154) and the parts beneath it lie outside. The theorem also guards the sphota-doctrine's flank: undivided meaning attaching to undivided object, with no partitive echo on either side.

mula_deva: बिन्दौ च समुदाये च वाचकः सलिलादिषु ॥ १५६ ॥
mula_iast: bindau ca samudāye ca vācakaḥ salilādiṣu || 156 ||
translation: Indifferent to number, measure and configuration, the expressor operates — with respect to a drop and to an aggregate alike — in the case of water and the like.
commentary: The theorem tested on mass-terms, where it shows most cleanly. 'Salila', water: the word is samkhya-pramana-samsthana-nirapeksah — *indifferent* to number, to measure, to configuration; and it operates equally bindau ca samudaye ca — upon a *drop* and upon an *ocean*. One term, spanning every quantity and shape its stuff can take, with no adjustment and no strain. For the atomist semantics this is awkward (what fixed deposit is common to drop and sea?); for the subtraction-doctrine it is the expected result: quantity, extent, shape are the *encounter's* furniture, never the word's cargo — the drop and the ocean differ in everything except what 'water' says of them. The example also quietly indicts the vyakti-theorist (2.122's rival): if words denoted individuals, 'water' would need an individual, and neither drop nor sea nor the sum of them presents one. What the word settles on is the stuff as such — and 'as such' is precisely the residue left when number, measure and configuration have been subtracted.
contested: salilādisu ('water and the like') marks the class of mass-terms without theorizing it; whether Bhartrhari means to distinguish mass from count nouns systematically, or merely to exhibit indifference-to-quantity in a favourable case, is not stated

translation: Of that which stands settled in oil and the like, delimited by processing and the rest, one says that a single portion is that very thing — its residing in the parts (is thus admitted).
commentary: The counter-case, honestly admitted, and it complicates 2.155's theorem. Take taila, oil, samskara-adi-paricchinne — delimited by processing and such (this pressing, this vessel, this batch: the stuff individuated by its history). Of such a mass one says ekadesam tattvena — a single portion *is that very thing*: any ladleful of the oil is oil, wholly and without remainder — tasya avayava-vartita, its residing in the parts is thereby granted. Mass-terms *do* distribute to their parts, exactly where count-terms (2.155's cow and its leg) do not. Bhartrhari records the asymmetry without smoothing it, and it repays notice: what a word reaches is not fixed by any general rule of mereology but by the *kind of standing* its object has under that employment — divisible stuffs distributing, articulated wholes not. This is the movement's method working honestly: the subtraction (what the word omits) yields different residues for different terms, and the residue is settled by the employment, never by the ontology alone. Later semantics would call this the mass/count distinction and make heavy weather of it; the verse simply lets the two behaviours stand side by side.
contested: the syntax is compressed and our construal (oil's portion being oil, hence part-residence granted for such terms) follows the contrast with 2.155-156; some read the verse as reporting an opponent's over-extension of part-residence, to be corrected — the asymmetry it records is real on either reading

translation: The naming that is employed as bound up with a particular meaning — on the departure of that meaning, its employment ceases.
commentary: The subtraction given its operational test: what a word *really* carries is what its usage cannot survive losing. Yena arthena abhisambaddham abhidhanam prayujyate — the naming is employed as bound to a certain meaning; tad-artha-apagame tasya prayogo vinivartate: that meaning departing, the employment *ceases*. This is the criterion the whole movement needs, and it is a dependency-test of exactly the modern kind: vary the object and watch the word — where usage lapses, meaning was; where usage persists, meaning was not. Lose the cow's colour and 'cow' stands (so colour was never carried, 2.153-154); lose the cowness and 'cow' falls (so cowness was). The test converts the doctrine of avivaksa from a claim about intention into an observable of usage — and 2.159-160 apply it immediately, first to the features that come and go without disturbing the word, then to the one whose loss silences it.

translation: But those possible properties which the word does not take within itself when employed — it does not, as a rule, require their presence.
commentary: The test's negative half. Sambhavino dharman — the *possible* properties: those a thing of this kind might have, and this one does (the cow's horns, hair, hooves, the colour of the day) — the word, when employed, does *not take them within*: antarniya na prayujyate, they are not carried inside the naming. Hence: tesam sannidhyam niyamena na vyapeksate — the word does not, *as a rule*, require their presence. The consequence is exactly the usage-behaviour 2.158 made criterial: a hornless cow is a cow, a shorn one is a cow, the word does not falter — because it never asked. Niyamena, 'as a rule', is precise and not a hedge: these features may be relevant on occasion (a context may make horns matter), but no rule of the word requires them; their bearing is the employment's business, not the term's. What we are watching is the emergence, from usage-observation, of something like the necessary/accidental distinction — arrived at not by metaphysics but by asking which losses stop the mouth.

translation: As the word 'cow' is seen (to operate) even where hair, hooves and the like deviate — not so does it operate when the universal is dissociated.
commentary: The test's positive half, and the movement's sharpest single stroke. Roma-sapha-adinam vyabhicare 'pi — hair, hooves and the rest *deviating*: absent, atypical, lost — drsyate go-sabdah: 'cow' is still seen in use, unembarrassed. Na tatha jater viprayoge pravartate — *not so* when the jati is dissociated: let cowness go and the word will not follow; no usage survives it. Two features, two fates, one test (2.158): the deviating ones were never carried; the one whose loss silences the word was its whole cargo. This is the jati-doctrine (2.122) not asserted but *derived* — reached by watching where speech breaks — and it is the movement's yield: after everything has been subtracted (the conjoined substances, the shape and colour, the limbs, the number and measure, the possible properties), the residue is the universal, and the residue is the meaning. Vyabhicara, deviation, is the test's technical name and would become the standard tool of Indian logic for isolating the invariable; here it is turned on semantics with complete clarity: meaning is what does not deviate.

translation: Difficult it is for anyone in the world to see all the parts; but from certain parts seen, the whole object is inferred.
commentary: A pivot from what words carry to how *objects* are given — the movement's second half, on the gap between what is presented and what is taken. Durlabham kasyacil loke sarva-avayava-darsanam: seeing *all* the parts is beyond anyone — no one ever sees the whole cow (the far side, the interior, the moment before and after are never given); perception is always partial. Kaiscit tv avayavair drstaih arthah krtsno 'numiyate: yet from *some* parts seen, the whole object is inferred — and effortlessly, without felt inference, the cow simply *there* in her entirety. The observation is doing two jobs. It secures the earlier subtractions: if the object is never wholly given anyway, the word's indifference to its furniture (2.153-156) is no defect but a fit with the epistemic situation. And it prepares 2.163's principle: comprehension routinely delivers wholes on partial warrant — which is what hearing a word does. The verse belongs with 1.90's part-seizures and 2.90's man-lion: Bhartrhari's persistent interest in how the mind completes what is shown, and how much of the given is in fact supplied.

translation: So too, in the case of jasmine, lotus and the like, whose qualities are constant companions of their scent, the determination of the qualities is observed.
commentary: The completion-principle exemplified, with the tradition's favourite evidence: scent. The jasmine's fragrance reaches one in the dark; and with it, not fragrance alone but the flower — its whiteness, its form, its whole quality-complex — avadharanam, *determined*, settled, present to the mind. The mechanism: gunanam sahacarinam nitya-sambandhinam — the qualities are constant companions of the scent, invariably linked; so one companion given, the company follows. This is co-occurrence hardened into cognitive delivery: what has never been met apart is never taken apart. The example serves the movement precisely because the delivery is *not* the scent's doing — the scent conveys scent; the flower arrives by the mind's completion along invariable association (2.161's inference-without-inferring, made specific). Transferred to language, as 2.163 immediately transfers it: the word conveys its narrow cargo (the jati, 2.160), and everything else the hearer finds in his comprehension — the shape, the colour, the whole imagined cow — arrives as the scent's company does: by association, not by the word.

translation: Therefore, when there is comprehension from the word of the possible meaning, the object whose dissociation has not been seen is understood by way of its being connected.
commentary: The movement's conclusion, gathering both halves. Tasmat: from the subtraction (words carry only the non-deviating, 2.158-160) and the completion (minds deliver wholes from parts by invariable association, 2.161-162) it follows: sambhavino arthasya sabdat sampratyaye sati — when the word has conveyed its possible meaning — adrsta-viprayogo 'rthah sambandhitvena gamyate: the object *whose dissociation has never been seen* is understood by way of *being connected*. Two channels, cleanly separated. The word gives what it gives: the invariant (2.160). Everything else in the hearer's comprehension — the cow's shape, her four legs, the world of default furnishing — comes by sambandhitva: it rides in because it has never been met apart, exactly as the flower rides in on its scent. Note the criterion's elegance: adrsta-viprayoga, 'not seen dissociated' — not *impossible* to dissociate, merely never yet met apart; the completion is defeasible, an association-effect, not an entailment (the hornless cow of 2.159 defeats it and the word survives). The movement thus ends with a two-channel semantics that a modern reader can recognize: strict semantic content, thin and invariant; and a generous pragmatic penumbra supplied by the hearer's world-knowledge. The kanda's next topic — number and the case-endings — is the natural test of exactly this boundary.
contested: sambhavinah arthasya we take as 'the possible meaning' — that which the word can convey (recalling 2.159's sambhavino dharmah); some construe 'the existent object'. The two-channel result is unaffected

translation: (164) The case-endings might be either expressive or manifesting of duality and the rest; or the aggregate might be the expressor of a numbered object. (165) Or, without any expression of number, they say that certain words such as 'go' convey, by difference of their own form, objects already accompanied by difference of number.
commentary: Number — the perfect test of the two-channel doctrine (2.163), since number is precisely a determination that sometimes seems said and sometimes seems merely there. Three accounts are canvassed. (i) The vibhaktis, the case-endings, are vacikah — *expressive* of duality and the rest: 'gavau' says two, the dual ending denoting the number outright. (ii) They are dyotikah — *manifesting*: the ending does not denote number but lights up a number already in the meaning — the illuminator/expressor distinction (Kanda I's whole apparatus) applied to inflection, and destined for a long career in Indian semantics (the dyotaka-doctrine would become the standard account of particles). (iii) The samudaya, the aggregate — stem-plus-ending taken as one unit — is the expressor of the numbered object: no division of labour at all, the whole form denoting the whole content (2.36's asvakarna-principle at the level of inflection). Then (165) the fourth and strangest: without any number-expression whatever, some words (the tradition instances 'go') convey numbered objects sva-rupa-bhedena — by *difference of their own form*: the form-difference simply *is* the number-difference, nothing denoting anything. The four exhaust the logical space, and the exhaustiveness is the point: number's status is genuinely undecidable by inspection, which is what a two-channel semantics predicts for exactly such borderline determinations.
contested: 165's kamscid ahur gavadayah is grammatically awkward and our construal (certain words convey number by their own form-difference) follows the sense; the verse has also been read as reporting that certain *theorists* say this. Either way the fourth option is a form-difference account without denotation

translation: Those words which have eternal connection, whose capacities are known in isolation — their meaning is divided out by agreement and difference.
commentary: The method for adjudicating 2.164-165, and it is the method of 2.12: distributional analysis. Where words are nitya-sambandha — eternally connected with their meanings (Kanda I's thesis, 1.23) — and vaiveke jnata-saktayah — their capacities *known in isolation*, established independently — then anvaya-vyatirekabhyam tesam artho vibhajyate: their meaning is divided out by *agreement and difference*: covary the form and watch the meaning; where they track, credit a unit; where they do not, do not. The verse is doing careful methodological work. It states the *conditions* under which the distributional method may be trusted — independently-known capacities, stable connection — and thereby marks that the method is not universally applicable: it is an instrument with a domain, not a metaphysics. This matters immediately, because 2.167 will exhibit exactly the cases where the conditions fail, and the whole point of the pair is that the analyst's chief tool has an edge past which it does not cut.

translation: And where the construing of those two can be made without deviation, there is a fixed rule; but there is no such fixed rule in the case of nut, sap and the like.
commentary: The method's limit, exhibited in the grammar's own machinery. Where anvaya and vyatireka can be applied avyabhicarena — without deviation, tracking perfectly — niyamah, a fixed rule obtains: the segmentation is secure, the unit real (as 'pac-' is, recurring with cooking wherever it recurs). But: na tv evam niyamo nut-sab-adisu — no such fixity for nut, sap and their kind. These are Panini's augments and technical elements — nut (the n-augment), sap (the class-marker of the first conjugation) — formatives that appear and vanish by rule with *no* constant meaning tracking them: sap marks a conjugation class and means nothing at all. The method finds them by distribution and finds no meaning to credit them with. So the analyst's own inventory contains units that are real *as units* and empty *as meanings* — 2.12's anarthaka parts (the '-bha' of vrsabha) now met not in the lexicon's oddities but at the heart of the derivational system. The moral for 2.164's dispute: one cannot settle whether the dual ending *denotes* two merely by noting that it covaries with twoness — covariance certifies a unit, never a meaning; sap covaries with everything in its class and means nothing. And the moral for the kanda: the tool that generates padarthas cannot, by itself, tell which of them are semantic — which is precisely why the meanings had to be sought at the whole (2.143).
contested: the transcription's nut-sab-adisu we read as the grammatical augments/markers nut and sap; some editions read differently, but the class intended — meaningless formatives isolable by distribution — is not in doubt

mula_deva: सम्भवे वाभिधानस्य लक्षणं तु न कल्प्यते ।
mula_iast: sambhave vābhidhānasya lakṣaṇaṃ tu na kalpyate |
translation: When direct expression is possible, indication is not to be assumed; for the capacities of words, in combination, are fixed relatively.
commentary: Two maxims, the second considerably deeper than the first. (i) Sambhave va abhidhanasya laksanam tu na kalpyate: where abhidhana — direct expression — is available, do not posit laksana, indication (secondary or transferred meaning). This is the standard interpretive economy of the tradition (do not reach for metaphor while the literal serves), and it will govern the coming discussion of secondary meaning. (ii) The ground offered for it, and it is the movement's most compressed insight: apeksikyah hi samsarge niyatah sabda-saktayah — the capacities of words, *in combination*, are fixed *relatively*. Sakti — the word's power, the thing every semantics has been trying to locate — is here declared apeksiki: relational, index-bound, determinate only against the combination it stands in. Not: each word has a fixed power and combination merely displays it. Rather: powers are *fixed by* relation — the same word carrying different capacity in different company, and carrying determinate capacity only *because* of company (2.17's particular-words looking general in citation; 2.104's capacity-assumption in the stream). The economy-maxim then follows naturally: if capacity is settled relationally, then in any given combination the capacity simply *is* what the combination fixes — and there is no room to import laksana while the relationally-fixed abhidhana is doing the work.

translation: No syntactic connection of meaning is seen in the case of 'kupa', 'supa', 'yupa'; therefore it is understood that the aggregate alone expresses a different meaning.
commentary: The relational-capacity doctrine of 2.168 exhibited in a case that has become famous. Kupa (well), supa (broth), yupa (sacrificial post) — three words sharing '-upa', differing in one phoneme, and their meanings share *nothing*: na anvayah arthasya drsyate, no syntactic or semantic connection whatever is seen. The rhyme is total and the sense is null. Hence: sanghatasya eva arthantara-vacitvam gamyate — it is the *aggregate alone*, the whole word, that expresses its wholly different meaning. The example does the work of a hundred arguments. Distributional method (2.166) would find '-upa' as a recurrent segment; the segment means nothing; the three words are not variations on a theme but three unrelated wholes that happen to sound alike. Phonetic overlap is thus proved semantically inert — which is 2.11's point (word-parts resembling other words) driven home with maximum economy, and which vindicates 2.36's generalization (comprehension is whole-to-whole everywhere, the transparent compound differing from the opaque only in inviting analysis). Kupa-supa-yupa is the kanda's emblem of the fact that meaning does not live in the pieces.

translation: Explanations differ in the operations of deriving words; among the many possible meanings, some one occasion is accepted.
commentary: Etymology entered — and immediately relativized. Anvakhyanani bhidyante sabda-vyutpatti-karmasu: *explanations differ* in the business of deriving words; the etymologist's craft (the nirukta-tradition's whole enterprise) does not converge — the same word receives incompatible derivations from different authorities, each defensible. And the reason: bahunam sambhave arthanam nimittam kimcid isyate — many meanings being *possible*, some one occasion is *accepted*: the derivation does not discover the word's meaning; it selects one nimitta, one occasioning ground, from among several the form permits. Etymology is thus a choice under-determined by evidence — the analyst picking which possible history to credit, as the exegete picks which analysis to run (2.38's non-fixity of means: 'a fixed rule about means does not necessarily stand'). This is not a complaint. Bhartrhari's tradition prizes nirukta (1.10's angas), and he is not dismissing it; he is placing it — as upaya, a discardable instrument whose value is what it teaches, not what it reveals about the word's real constitution (2.13's prakriya-bheda for the avidvan). The next verses exhibit the multiplicity concretely.
contested: nimittam kimcid isyate we read as the etymologist's selection among possible grounds; some construe 'some particular occasion is (what is) desired (by the deriver)' — the under-determination is the point on either reading

translation: Vaira, Vasistha, Girisa, and likewise Ekagarika and the rest, have been explained somehow, by some, through confusions of ground and limit.
commentary: The multiplicity exhibited, with a rueful precision. Four specimens whose derivations the tradition contests: vaira (enmity — from vira? from a lost base?); vasistha (the seer's name — from vasin, from vasistha as superlative?); girisa (Siva, mountain-dweller — giri-sa, but sa from which root, and why?); ekagarika (a term the tradition glosses variously — 'burglar', 'one who keeps a single house'). Kaiscit kathamcid akhyatah: explained *by some*, *somehow* — the two indefinites doing withering work. And the diagnosis: nimitta-avadhi-sankaraih — by *confusions of ground and limit*: the etymologist mixes up which element is the occasioning ground and where the analysis is to be cut, and by shuffling those two variables generates as many derivations as he pleases. Bhartrhari has isolated exactly the degrees of freedom that make etymology unfalsifiable: choose a base, choose a boundary, and any form yields any sense. The verse is his most economical demonstration that the analyst's tools generate their own results — and it lands with special force here, since the whole kanda's argument has been that the parts analysis finds are the analysis's own postings (2.10, 2.97, 2.167).

translation: As the designation of a road by trees, ant-hills and mountains is not incompatible — so with 'cow' and the rest, by different accompanying things.
commentary: The movement closes with a simile that redeems what 2.170-171 seemed to demolish. A road is designated by its landmarks: 'the way past the great tree', 'the road by the ant-hill', 'the mountain road' — three designations, three grounds, one road, and aviruddham: *not incompatible*. No one asks which landmark is the road's true name; each picks the road out, from where the speaker stands, by what lies to hand. Tatha gavadinam bhinnaih sahacaribhih: so with 'cow' and the rest, by *different accompanying things* — a word may be occasioned by any of its object's companions (its gait, its lowing, its horns), and rival etymologies are rival landmarks, all serviceable, none constitutive. The simile completes the movement's whole argument with a single image. The word does not carry its object's furniture (2.153-160); the mind supplies the rest by association (2.161-163); the analyst's derivations pick among associations for their occasioning grounds (2.170-171) — and none of this impugns the word, because designation was never a matter of *containing* the thing. It is a matter of *pointing successfully from where one stands*. That the pointing succeeds while every account of how it succeeds diverges is not a scandal; it is the ordinary condition of language, and 2.142 has already told the wise man to leave the world's namings undisturbed.

translation: And a designation is made otherwise by those who see difference of state, from the determination of a single portion — as in the case of the kimsuka and the like.
commentary: The landmark-simile (2.172) given its human dimension: designators differ because *seers* differ. Avastha-bheda-darsibhih — by those who see a difference of state: the kimsuka tree (the flame-of-the-forest, a stock example precisely because it is a different sight in each season — bare, budded, blazing red) receives its designation ekadesa-avadharanat, from the determination of a *single portion*: whoever names it names it from the aspect he has met. The traveller who saw it in flower and the one who saw it bare have named the same tree by different grounds, and neither has erred. This completes the etymological relativism (2.170-171) by locating its source not in the etymologist's carelessness but in the structure of encounter itself: nobody meets the whole (2.161), everybody names from a portion, and the portions differ. The kimsuka also carries an old resonance the audience would hear — it is the tree of the parable in which several men describe it from their several seasons and each is mocked by the others — which is exactly the moral: the mockery is misplaced.
contested: the kimsuka's traditional parable-associations are assumed rather than stated by the karika; the verse needs only that its appearance varies by state and that names are given from states

translation: By some, a different derivation has been shown here for 'gauh' — from 'girati', from 'garjati', from 'gam', from 'gavate', or indeed from 'gata'.
commentary: The relativism made concrete on the tradition's most-derived word. 'Gauh' — cow — has been referred to: girati, she swallows (the grazing beast); garjati, she bellows (the lowing beast); gam, to go (the wandering beast); gavate, a rare root the etymologists press into service; gata, gone/motion. Five derivations, five grounds, one animal — each a landmark on the road (2.172), each picking the cow out by a real companion of hers (her feeding, her voice, her going). Kaiscid, 'by some': the anonymity is now familiar and is the point — the derivations have authorities, and the authorities do not agree, and the disagreement has never once impeded anyone's use of the word. That is the datum. Etymology is a genuine discipline producing incompatible results and costing nothing, because the word's working never depended on it: derivation reconstructs a possible occasion for a name whose life is entirely in its employment (2.104-106). The verse is the kanda's cheerful demonstration that the search for a word's 'true meaning' in its origin is a search for a landmark, not a foundation.

translation: Or the word 'gauh' operates upon cows simply from its own form, as 'gauh'; either everything is derived, or not — and by some it is accepted in both ways.
commentary: The alternative to derivation, and then the tradition's characteristic settlement. First: gaur ity eva svarupad va — 'gauh' operates on cows simply *from its own form*, underived, the word standing on itself with no history to lean on (the avyutpanna, the underivable, that Panini's own system concedes for certain forms, 2.37). Then the general question posed in its starkest form: vyutpadyate na va sarvam — is *everything* derived, or nothing? The two schools are the tradition's real ones: the vyutpatti-vadins who hold every word analyzable to root and affix, and the a-vyutpatti-vadins who hold that stems are simply given. And the settlement: kaiscid ca ubhayatha isyate — by some it is accepted *both ways*. Not a compromise but a principled dual-standing: the same word is underived as *used* (its life needing no history) and derived as *taught* (its analysis serving the learner, 2.13). This is 1.74 and 2.38 arriving in etymology — the two directions of description, each valid for its purpose, neither the fact of the matter — and it is why the kanda can honour the nirukta-tradition (2.170's craft) while denying it any authority over meaning.

translation: And instruction by way of the general is resorted to in the science for the sake of brevity; as with another universal, its particulars are (still) the conveyors.
commentary: A methodological note that will govern the movement on roots and preverbs now beginning. Samanyena upadesah — instruction by way of the *general* — is what the sastra resorts to laghv-artham, *for brevity*: the grammar teaches 'the root pac', 'the affix tin', stating rules over general categories because stating them over every instance would be endless (laghava, economy, is the Paninian science's governing virtue). But the brevity is a device, and the verse says so: jaty-antaravad asyapi visesah pratipadakah — as with any other universal, its *particulars* are the conveyors: what actually conveys, in any utterance, is the particular form in its particular employment; the general is the teacher's shorthand for a family of particulars, not an entity doing semantic work. The point matters for what follows: the grammar will be seen to treat dhatu and upasarga as separate general items, and the whole question (2.180-190) will be whether that separation is more than pedagogical economy. Bhartrhari has answered before asking: instruction is general for brevity's sake; conveyance is always particular.

mula_deva: अर्थान्तरे च यद् वृत्तं तत्प्रकृत्यन्तरं विदुः ।
mula_iast: arthāntare ca yad vṛttaṃ tatprakṛtyantaraṃ viduḥ |
translation: And what has occurred in another meaning, they know as a different base; though its form is alike, it is not carried over to another in established currency.
commentary: A principle of lexical identity, drawn from the same soil. Artha-antare yad vrttam — what has occurred with a *different meaning* — tat prakrty-antaram viduh: they know it as a *different base*: sameness of meaning-behaviour, not sameness of shape, individuates a stem. And the consequence: tulya-rupam na tad rudhau anyasmin anusajyate — though the form be alike, it is not carried over into another word's established currency: the identical syllables occurring in a rudhi-word (a conventionalized term, 2.37) do not drag their other life along. This is kupa-supa-yupa (2.169) stated as a rule of the lexicon rather than exhibited as an oddity: homophony is not identity, and the etymologist who reasons from shared shape to shared base has confused a landmark with a road (2.172). The principle also anticipates the movement's central case: if 'pra-' in one verb means one thing and in another means nothing recoverable, then by 2.177 they are not the same item at all — which is precisely the reef on which preverb-semantics will run.
contested: vrttam we take as 'has occurred/operated' (the form used with another sense); the compressed syntax also permits 'what has been formed (as a compound) in another meaning', narrowing the rule to vrtti-formations — the individuation-principle survives either way

translation: The two roots 'ji' and 'yaj', distinct and fixed to different domains, have been somehow set forth by some as one; for conveying is a various thing.
commentary: A specimen of the etymologist's over-reach, chosen for its audacity. The roots ji (to conquer) and yaj (to sacrifice) are bhinnau — *distinct*, niyatau visayantare — each fixed to its own domain, sharing nothing but a formal resemblance that phonological ingenuity can manufacture. And yet kaiscid kathamcid uddistau: by *some*, *somehow*, they have been set forth as one — the derivers, having the two freedoms 2.171 identified (choose a base, choose a boundary), unified what usage keeps forever apart. Bhartrhari's comment is a single wry clause: citram hi pratipadanam — 'for conveying is a various thing', or more bluntly, exposition is a wonderful business. The dryness is the argument. He does not refute the derivation; he lets its existence testify. If the method can identify conquering with sacrificing, the method's outputs are not constraints on meaning — and 2.177 has just given the rule the derivers violated: different meaning-behaviour, different base, whatever the shapes suggest.
contested: the identification of the two roots as ji and yaj follows the transcription's aviji-yaji; the specific etymological claim being mocked is not recoverable, and the verse's force is entirely in the mockery

translation: And thus one should proceed as with 'bala', 'vaya' and 'jitvari' and the like; in accepting both difference and non-difference, there is no contradiction whatever.
commentary: The settlement of 2.175 formalized as a maxim, with a handful of examples the tradition disputes (bala, vaya, jitvari — words whose analyses divide the grammarians). Evam upacaret: proceed thus — accepting bheda-abheda-abhyupagame na virodho 'sti kascana: in accepting *both* difference and non-difference there is *no contradiction whatever*. Two words may be one base and two bases, according to the purpose of the description: one, for the economy of a rule (2.176's samanyena upadesa); two, for the fidelity of a usage-account (2.177's individuation by meaning-behaviour). The maxim is the kanda's constant (1.74, 2.38, 2.58, 2.175) at its most explicit — bhedabheda not as a metaphysical doctrine to be defended but as a *methodological permission* to describe the same material twice, incompatibly, without incoherence, because descriptions answer to purposes and the material answers to neither. It is the exact license the coming analysis of root-and-preverb will need: they are two (the grammar says so, and rules require it) and one (usage says so, and meaning requires it), and the verse has pre-authorized both.

translation: The construing of root and preverb as separate, in the science, is for the sake of settling 'at' and the rest; but the root itself is of that very kind.
commentary: The movement's thesis, stated at once. In the sastra, dhatu and upasarga — root and preverb ('pra-', 'sam-', 'ud-') — are prthaktvena prakalpanam, construed as *separate* items. Why? Ad-adinam vyavastha-artham: for the sake of settling the augment 'at' and the rest — that is, for strictly formal purposes: the augment a- of the past tense must be positioned (does one say pra-agacchat or pra-a-gacchat?), sandhi must be regulated, accent assigned, and none of that can be stated without treating the preverb as a separable unit. The separation is *rule-driven*, exactly as 2.176 said instruction is general for brevity's sake. And then the counter-claim that governs everything following: dhatur eva tu tadrsah — *the root itself is of that very kind*: what actually conveys is the root already-as-so-qualified, the preverbed root as one meaning-unit; the separation that the grammar's formal operations require does not correspond to any division in the meaning. Two items for the rule-writer; one for the speaker. It is 2.10's apoddhara-doctrine arriving at the innermost joint of the word — and the reason it must arrive here is that if analysis *anywhere* found a real semantic seam, this is where it would be.

translation: For thus, a rule is taught for 'samgramayati' as having its preverb; and such particular actions are undertaken by whole complexes.
commentary: The evidence for 2.180, from the grammar's own practice. Samgramayati — 'he fights, wages battle': the form is derived from samgrama, itself sam + grama, and the grammar teaches its rules sopasargat — *with its preverb*: the sastra cannot state the derivation by taking the root alone and adding the preverb's contribution; it must treat the preverbed complex as the base from the start. The grammarian's own procedure thus concedes what 2.180 claimed: at the point where a rule must actually apply, the unit is the complex. And the generalization: kriya-visesah sanghataih prakramyante tathavidhah — such particular actions are undertaken *by whole complexes*: the specific action a verb names (waging-battle, not going-together-with-a-village) is delivered by the sanghata, the complex as a whole, not composed from separable donations. Note that 'wage battle' is precisely *not* recoverable from sam + grama by any calculus — the meaning is the complex's own (2.169's kupa-supa-yupa at the verbal root). The grammar keeps root and preverb apart for the augment's sake (2.180) and keeps them together for the rule's sake here: the two treatments are 2.179's licensed double description, and only the second tracks meaning.

translation: Thus the operations relating to root and preverb are internal; the action, being of that very kind, comes into connection with the means.
commentary: The doctrine given its technical seat. Karyanam antarangatvam evam dhatupasargayoh — the operations concerning root and preverb are *antaranga*, 'internal': the Paninian term for rules that apply at the innermost layer, before the outer ones get a chance (antaranga rules take precedence over bahiranga, and the metarule governing this is among the system's most consequential). Bhartrhari's use is exact and shrewd: the preverb's union with its root happens *inside*, in the derivation's core, complete before the word meets anything else. Hence: sadhanaih yati sambandham tathabhuta eva sa kriya — the action, being *already of that kind* (preverbed, particular, its sense settled), then comes into connection with the means, the karakas — the agent, instrument, object, everything the sentence supplies. The order is the argument. By the time the verb reaches the sentence, there is no root-plus-preverb to be summed; there is one action-meaning, which the sentence then furnishes with participants (2.48's architecture presupposes exactly this: the verb arrives whole and the nouns orbit *it*). The grammar's own layering of rules thus certifies the semantics: what is settled antaranga is settled before semantics at sentence-scale begins.
contested: antarangatva is the technical priority-notion and we render it 'internal'; whether Bhartrhari invokes the full paribhasa-machinery or the plainer sense ('these operations belong to the inner constitution') is undecidable, and the argument works on the plainer sense alone

translation: Being accomplished among things fit for employment, the object to be split is (further) specified; and prior to connection with the means, the action does not arise at all.
commentary: A pair of claims about how verbal meaning comes to be determinate. First: prayogarhesu siddhah san bhettavyo 'rtho visisyate — the object-to-be-split (bhettavya: the standard example of a verb's patient, 'what is to be split') is *already accomplished* among things fit for employment, and is then further specified: the log exists before the splitting is spoken of; the verb's participants are drawn from a world of usable things and then narrowed by the utterance. Second, and it is the striking one: prak ca sadhana-sambandhat kriya naiva upajayate — *prior to connection with the means, the action does not arise at all*. There is no bare action waiting to be supplied with participants. 'Splitting' as such, unagented, unpatiented, uninstrumented, is not a meaning that exists and then gets furnished; the action-meaning *comes into being* with its karaka-connections. Read against 2.182, the picture is precise: the preverbed root arrives with its *kind* fixed internally (waging-battle, not going-with-village), and its *being an action at all* is constituted in the sentence, by the means-connection. Kind from within, existence from without — which is why the verb is the sentence's centre (2.48) and yet not the sentence's meaning (2.1's first view, refused).
contested: bhettavyo 'rthah we take as the illustrative patient ('the to-be-split thing'); some read it abstractly ('the meaning that is to be analysed is specified'), making the verse about semantic decomposition — the second claim (no action prior to means-connection) is textually secure and is the load-bearing one

translation: As root-hood, and the condition of being an object, belong to a root by the commencement of its coming connection with the means — so let the other case be seen as well.
commentary: The retroactive constitution made explicit, and then extended. Dhatoh sadhana-yogasya bhavinah prakramat — from the *commencement* of the root's *coming* (bhavin: future, prospective) connection with the means — dhatutvam karma-bhavas ca: root-hood itself, and the condition of being an object, accrue. This is remarkable: a root is a root *in virtue of* the means-connection it is heading toward; the item's very categorial status is conferred by a relation not yet realized. (The grammar behaves exactly so: an element is called a dhatu because of what it will govern; a stem is called a karman because of the action it will stand under.) Tatha anyad api drsyatam: *so let the other case be seen too* — the invitation is to read the root/preverb relation by the same light: as root-hood is conferred prospectively by connection, so the preverbed root's unity is conferred by the meaning it is going to deliver, not assembled from what its pieces antecedently were. Categories in this grammar are functional and forward-looking — an item is what its role will make it (2.104's capacity-assumption, now temporalized) — which is why no analysis of the pieces will ever yield the whole they were destined to constitute.

translation: (185) As lac-juice and the like, applied at the time of the seed, benefit the fruits by transformation of colour and the rest — (186) so, from the connection standing in the intellect, the difference of root and preverb, having been made internal, shines forth at the time of the word.
commentary: The movement's great simile, and one of the most delicate images in the work. The horticultural practice: lac-juice and other dyes applied *at the time of the seed* — bija-kalesu sambaddhah — work their effect not then but far downstream, varna-adi-parinamena phalanam upakurvate: benefiting the *fruits* by a transformation of colour and the rest. Nothing is visible at sowing; the whole efficacy is latent, carried invisibly through the plant's growth, and emerges in the fruit as its colour. So (186): buddhistat abhisambandhat — from the connection *standing in the intellect* (the union of root and preverb accomplished inwardly, in the speaker's mind, antaranga in the fullest sense) — abhyantarikrto bhedah — the difference *having been made internal*, taken inside, absorbed — pada-kale prakasate: shines forth *at the time of the word*. The preverb's contribution is real and is never separately present: it was mixed in at the seed, and what appears at the word is one colour. The simile answers precisely the question that would otherwise sink the doctrine — if root and preverb are semantically one, why does the preverb make a difference? — and answers it without conceding a seam: the difference is *in* the unified meaning as the dye is in the fruit's redness, constitutive and unlocatable. It also re-sounds the kanda's deepest structure: latency ripening into manifestation (1.84's seeds, 2.148's fermentation), here at the scale of a single word's inner history.
contested: laksarasa-adayah we take as lac-juice and similar dyes used in horticulture (the traditional gloss); the exact practice is obscure to us, but the simile's logic — applied at the seed, manifest in the fruit — is fully carried by the text

translation: Sometimes possible differences, not exhibited by the roots alone, are manifested in connection with a preverb — 'pra', 'para' and the rest.
commentary: The phenomenon the doctrine must account for, stated fairly. Sambhavino bhedah kevalair anidarsitah — *possible* differences which the bare roots do not exhibit: har- alone gives nothing determinate; pra-har- is to strike, a-har- to fetch, sam-har- to gather, vi-har- to sport. The preverbs pra, para and the rest *do* something, undeniably and systematically. And the verb chosen for what they do is the movement's whole answer in one word: vyajyante — they are *manifested*. Not produced, not added, not composed: manifested — the technical term of Kanda I's entire apparatus (the sounds manifesting the sphota, 1.44-49; the flower's scent released by crushing, 1.79). The differences were *sambhavin*, possible, latent in the root's own semantic range; the preverb does not import them from outside but occasions them — the dye at the seed (2.185-186), the fan raising the ever-present air (1.116). This is why the doctrine of 2.180 does not have to deny the obvious: the preverb's contribution is total and the seam is still fictional, because manifesting is not contributing. The next verse canvasses the alternatives with characteristic fairness.

mula_deva: स वाचको विशेषाणां सम्भवाद् द्योतकोऽपि वा ।
mula_iast: sa vācako viśeṣāṇāṃ sambhavād dyotako'pi vā |
translation: That (preverb) is the expressor of the particulars; or, since it is possible, it is the manifestor; or it is employed as a co-operating factor for the sake of infusing capacity into the root.
commentary: The three accounts of what a preverb does, catalogued without immediate adjudication — and the catalogue is the tradition's live one. (i) Vacaka: the preverb *expresses* the particular — 'pra' denotes forth-ness and the verb's sense is compounded of root-sense plus preverb-sense. This is the compositional account, and it is the one 2.180-186 have been dismantling. (ii) Dyotaka: it is the *manifestor* — sambhavat, 'since it is possible', i.e. because the particulars are already possible in the root, the preverb need only light them up (2.187's vyajyante given its technical name; the dyotaka-doctrine, first canvassed for case-endings at 2.164, here at its most consequential home — this is the account the grammatical tradition would ultimately make its own, and this verse is among its earliest clear statements). (iii) Sahakarin: a *co-operating factor*, employed sakty-adhanaya, for the *infusing of capacity* into the root — a third way between the first two: the preverb neither denotes nor merely reveals but *conditions*, adding potency to the root's own power (as heat conditions the seed without being the plant). Bhartrhari lets all three stand; but the middle one is his (2.187's verb was vyajyante), and 2.189-190 will supply the evidence that only manifestation-or-conditioning can absorb.
contested: which of the three Bhartrhari holds is a classical question; our reading takes (ii) as favoured on the strength of 2.187's vyajyante and 2.185-186's simile, with (iii) as a friendly variant — the tradition after him divides, and the dyotaka-account's eventual dominance is not itself evidence about the karika

translation: And where going and the like is understood from 'stha' and the rest taken alone, there, by inference of two kinds, the preverb 'pra' and the rest is said to have that same property.
commentary: Evidence for the manifestation-account, from the roots that need no preverb. Sthadibhih kevalaih — by roots such as stha *alone*, without any preverb — gamanadi tu gamyate: going and the like is (nonetheless) understood: bare roots already deliver directional and modal particulars that one might have credited to preverbs. So the particulars are in the root's range natively — which is (i)'s embarrassment: if 'pra' *denotes* forth-ness, why does the bare root sometimes deliver it? Then the inference: tatra anumanad dvividhat tad-dharma pradir ucyate — from a two-fold inference, the preverb pra and the rest is said to have *that same property*: reasoning from the bare root's capacity (it can deliver the particular unaided) together with the preverbed form's behaviour (it delivers it reliably), one concludes that the preverb shares the root's kind of work rather than contributing a separate content — it is a manifestor of what was there, tad-dharma, of the same character. The argument is genuinely evidential rather than doctrinal: it turns on a distributional fact (bare roots covering preverb-territory) that only the dyotaka or sahakarin accounts predict. And the next verse supplies the mirror-image evidence.
contested: anumanad dvividhat ('by a two-fold inference') is unexplained by the karika; our reconstruction (from the bare root's capacity, and from the preverbed form's behaviour) is one plausible filling among several the commentators offer

translation: And in the non-employment of 'adhi' and 'pari', so far as a different action is seen — the root that expresses that action is, apart from those two, meaningless.
commentary: The mirror-evidence, and the movement's closing stroke — the preverbs that cannot be removed. Some roots simply do not occur bare: adhi-i (to study), pari-dha (to don) and their kind. In the *non-employment* of adhi and pari — aprayoge — yavad drstam kriyantaram, so far as a different action is seen (i.e., the distinct actions these complexes name), tasya abhidhayako dhatuh saha tabhyam anarthakah — the root that expresses that action, *apart from those two, is meaningless*: strip 'adhi' from 'adhi-i' and no residue survives that means studying, or anything at all in that domain. Here the compositional account (2.188's (i)) fails completely and visibly: one cannot sum root-sense and preverb-sense when the root has no sense to contribute. And here 2.180's thesis is vindicated at its strongest: dhatur eva tu tadrsah — the root *itself is of that very kind*, i.e. the real unit is and always was the preverbed complex, the grammar's separation being for the augment's sake (2.180) and nothing more. The obligatory preverb is thus to the movement what kupa-supa-yupa (2.169) was to the earlier one: the case where the analysis's own units are exposed as postings. Root and preverb are two for the rule-writer, one for the speaker — and in adhi-i they are not even two for the rule-writer, since neither piece can be given a meaning to be summed.
contested: saha tabhyam is awkward ('together with those two... meaningless') and we construe it as 'apart from those two' following the argument's evident need; some read 'the root, together with those two, expresses that action — (alone it is) meaningless', which yields the same doctrine by a smoother route

translation: Just so, certain self-meaning suffixes, occurring within another complex, are commingled with what is meaningless and merely restate the base's meaning.
commentary: The preverb-doctrine (2.180-190) extended to the svarthika suffixes — those affixes that, by the grammar's own account, add *nothing*: the -ka of many a form, the affixes taught as sve arthe, 'in their own meaning', which is a courteous way of saying they mean what the stem already meant. Prakrty-artha-anuvadinah: they *restate* the base's meaning (anuvada, 2.115's term for idle repetition). And they are anarthakena samsrstah — *commingled with the meaningless*: the complex contains formatives that neither denote nor manifest. This is the movement's most awkward datum honestly placed: language is full of morphology doing no semantic work at all (2.167's nut and sap already showed it in the derivational core). The verse does not explain it away; it registers that the analyst's inventory contains items real as units and null as meanings, and that the word nonetheless means. Which is precisely the kanda's thesis from another side: if meaning were assembled from the pieces, meaningless pieces would be subtractions — and they are not; they are simply present, and the whole means what it means.

mula_deva: आगमा इव केऽपि स्युः संभूयार्थस्य वाचकाः ॥ १९२ ॥
mula_iast: āgamā iva ke'pi syuḥ saṃbhūyārthasya vācakāḥ || 192 ||
translation: Some particles are manifestors; some express a separate meaning; and some might be like augments — expressors of a meaning (only) in combination.
commentary: The nipatas — particles — canvassed in three accounts, the same triad the preverbs received (2.188) and for the same reason: particles are the hard case for any denotational semantics. (i) Dyotakah, manifestors: 'eva', 'ca', 'hi' do not denote anything but light up a determination already in the sentence-meaning (the account that would become the tradition's standard for particles, and the one Bhartrhari's system favours). (ii) Prthag-artha-abhidhayinah: expressors of a *separate* meaning — the particle denotes a content of its own (conjunction, emphasis, limitation) which then combines. (iii) The third and most interesting: agama iva — *like augments*: sambhuya arthasya vacakah, expressors of meaning *only in combination* — as an augment (the nut of 2.167) has no independent existence or sense and yet the form containing it means, so the particle contributes only as absorbed, never as a term. The three placements track exactly the possible relations of a formative to meaning: revealing, denoting, or constituting-only-in-combination — and it is characteristic that Bhartrhari lists them without decision. The next verses supply the evidence that only the first and third can absorb.

translation: Whether above or before, the manifestor-hood is not differentiated, when they are employed — even in the case of different meanings, in every way.
commentary: Evidence for the dyotaka-account, from positional freedom. Uparistat purastad va — whether the particle stands *after* or *before* (the preverb pra- prefixed, the particle ca postposed, the Vedic freedom of placement) — dyotakatvam na bhidyate: its manifestor-hood is *not differentiated*: the work it does is the same work regardless of where in the utterance it falls. And sarvatha, in every way, bhinnarthesv api — even where the meanings differ. The argument turns on a fact about denotation: a denoting element contributes a content that must be *composed* with the rest, and composition is order-sensitive (this is why word order and case are doing labour). A manifesting element illuminates a whole that is already there, and illumination is order-indifferent — the lamp lights the room from any corner.
