'Gauh' expresses the beast — sasnadiman pindah, the mass with dewlap and the rest (the standard definition-by-marks). And tatha sa eva go-sabdah bahike 'pi vyavasthitah: *that very same word* 'gauh' stands settled also upon a *Bahika* — the man from Bahika, called an ox: 'gaur vahikah', the tradition's stock case of figurative speech, roughly 'the Punjabi is an ox' (dull, stolid). Note what the verse refuses to say. It does not say the word is *transferred*, or *applied metaphorically*, or *used in a secondary sense*. It says sa eva — *that very same word* — vyavasthitah, *stands settled*: the same word, equally settled, on both. This is 2.250-251 taken with full seriousness: if the word is one and omni-significant, then its standing on the ox and its standing on the man are not two operations, one primary and one derived, but one plenitude carved by two occasions. The next verse says so outright, and in doing so dismantles the primary/secondary distinction that every rival school treats as foundational.

translation: But of that very word, which is all-capable and has many properties, secondariness and primariness are described by reason of a difference in established currency.
commentary: The verse that relocates the entire gauna/mukhya distinction — and it is one of the most consequential in the kanda. Sarva-sakteh tu tasya eva sabdasya aneka-dharmanah: of that very word — *all-capable*, sarva-sakti, and possessed of many properties. Then: prasiddhi-bhedad gaunatvam mukhyatvam ca upavarnyate — secondariness and primariness are *described* by reason of a difference in *established currency*. Every word is load-bearing. Sarva-sakti restates 2.250's sarvarthya: the word can do anything. Upavarnyate — *described*, not discovered (the kanda's verb for postings: 2.10's apoddharah upavarnyate). And prasiddhi-bheda: the difference is one of *currency* — of which use is commoner, more familiar, more settled in the community (2.128's rudhi). So: 'gauh' is primary of the ox and secondary of the man not because the ox-meaning is the word's own and the man-meaning borrowed, but because one use is more current than the other. The distinction is *statistical and social*, not semantic. Every rival school — Mimamsa, Nyaya, the poeticians — builds on gauna/mukhya as a real division in how words mean; Bhartrhari has just made it a fact about usage frequency. If the word is one and omni-significant, there is no other place for the distinction to live.
contested: prasiddhi we render 'established currency' (familiarity of use); reading it as 'well-known-ness' or 'renown' shades toward the same deflation. The verse's force — that the gauna/mukhya distinction is currency-based, not capacity-based — is our reading and it is the one 2.250-252 require

translation: One mantra, likewise, is settled — with respect to the self, with respect to the deity, with respect to the rite — as having all meanings without commingling, its capacities distinct.
commentary: The doctrine confirmed from the tradition's own most authoritative practice. A single mantra is read adhyatmam — with respect to the self (the psychological/spiritual construal), adhidaivam — with respect to the deity (the theological), adhikratu — with respect to the rite (the ritual): the three standard registers of Vedic exegesis, in which one text yields three complete and non-competing meanings. And the description: asankarena sarvarthah bhinna-saktir vyavasthitah — having *all* meanings, *without commingling*, its capacities *distinct*, standing settled. Every term matches 2.250-253: sarvartha (omni-significance), bhinna-sakti (capacities differentiated by the nimitta — here the register of reading), asankarena (no blurring: each construal complete and clean, none the metaphorical shadow of another). The exegetical tradition has always insisted that the adhyatma-reading is not a figurative extension of the adhiyajna-reading; both are the mantra's own. Bhartrhari's point is that the tradition is right, and that its rightness is general: what the mantra does conspicuously, every word does — one plenitude, many carvings, none primary.

translation: By some, an adherence of cowness in the Bahika is held to follow from the occasioning ground; the mere object is transposed, while the word stands settled in its own meaning.
commentary: A rival account of the Bahika-case, reported for contrast. Kaiscid: some hold that go-tva-anusanga bahike — an *adherence of cowness* in the Bahika — follows nimittat, from the occasioning ground (the shared property: dullness, stolidity): the universal cowness is somehow carried over and made to inhere in the man. On this view: artha-matram viparyastam — the *mere object* is transposed, shifted, misassigned — sabdah svarthe vyavasthitah: while the *word* stands settled in its own meaning, unmoved. The word keeps its ox-meaning; what moves is the object it gets applied to. This is the transference-account (the poeticians' laksana in embryo, and the Naiyayika's too): meaning fixed, application stretched. Bhartrhari reports it because it is the natural alternative to his own, and because its very structure concedes his point in miniature — it must posit a *transposition*, an artha-matram viparyastam, some machinery of carrying-over, precisely because it began by fixing the word's meaning. Take 2.250's plenitude instead and no carrying is needed: the word was always able to mean the man; currency alone made the ox familiar.

mula_deva: अर्थमात्रं विपर्यस्तं स्वरूपे तु स्थितिः स्थिरा ॥ २५६ ॥
mula_iast: arthamātraṃ viparyastaṃ svarūpe tu sthitiḥ sthirā || 256 ||
translation: So too the own-form of words adheres in all meanings; the mere object is transposed, but the standing in the own-form is firm.
commentary: The rival account (2.255) generalized — and, in generalizing, revealed. Tatha svarupam sabdanam sarvarthesu anusajyate: the *own-form* of words adheres in *all* meanings — whatever a word is applied to, its svarupa (1.50's self-presenting face, 1.59-68's grammatical object) goes along: the word 'gauh' is the same word in every use, and its identity is not touched by what it is applied to. Artha-matram viparyastam — the mere object is transposed (2.255 verbatim) — svarupe tu sthitih sthira: but the standing in the own-form is *firm*. Read as the rival's doctrine, this is the transference-account's foundation: word-identity is form-identity, applications wander, meanings shift, the word abides. But read against 2.250-253 the same sentence says something else entirely: what is invariant across every use is the word's *form*, and nothing else — not a primary meaning, not a core sense, not a fixed deposit. Which is precisely the omni-significance thesis: the word is one *as form*, and its meanings are as many as its occasions. The rival's own foundation, stated exactly, is the holist's conclusion.

translation: But those who trace out the path of difference say that the oneness of the two words, secondary and primary, is on account of their own-form — even where there is utter difference.
commentary: The doxographic placement of 2.256, and it is precisely calibrated. Bheda-marga-anudarsinah — those who *trace out the path of difference*: the analysts, the theorists whose method is division (2.57's pada-darsins, whose primitives are the parts). Their doctrine: sabdayoh gauna-mukhyayoh ekatvam svarupatvat — the *oneness* of the two words, the secondary and the primary, is *on account of their own-form*; atyanta-bhede 'pi — even where there is *utter difference* (the meanings having nothing whatever in common: ox and man). So even the difference-tracers concede unity — but only formal unity: for them, 'gauh'-of-the-ox and 'gauh'-of-the-man are two words that happen to share a shape, held together by nothing but svarupa. Bhartrhari's own position agrees on the datum and reverses the direction: they are not two words sharing a form but *one word* whose plenitude two occasions have carved (2.250-253). The difference between the doctrines is exactly the difference between the two darsanas of 1.74 and 2.57 — parts first, or whole first — and here, as always, the verse states the rival fairly and lets the reader see that both accounts save the phenomena while only one avoids positing a transference (2.255).

translation: And thus another samidheni-verse adheres in the repetition; and mantras obtain difference by their application, as in modification.
commentary: Ritual confirmation of the nimitta-doctrine, from the fire-kindling verses. The samidhenis are the verses recited while kindling the sacrificial fire; the rite requires a fixed number, and the number is made up by *repeating* verses. The question the exegetes faced: when a verse is repeated to make the count, is the repetition the *same* verse or *another*? The verse's answer: samidhenyantaram anusajyate avrttau — *another* samidheni adheres in the repetition: the repeated verse counts as a distinct one. Same words, same form, two verses — individuated by their ritual office. And the generalization: mantrah viniyogena labhante bhedam uhavat — mantras obtain *difference by their application*, as in uha (the modification of mantras when transposed to a derivative rite, 2.78). Vinyoga — application, deployment to a ritual station — is what individuates. This is 2.104's capacity-doctrine and 2.110's tantra-cases returning at the semantic level: identity of form settles nothing; office decides how many there are. And the ritual science, which must count exactly, counts by office.

translation: Those are just other recensional texts; or some one alone is recited; or there is recitation of meaningless things, and the remainder is understood as something else.
commentary: The alternatives canvassed for the repetition-problem, and their disarray is the point. (i) Tani amnayantarani eva: the repeated verses are just *other recensional texts* — different amnayas, so no repetition at all, merely distinct verses that happen to coincide. (ii) Pathyate kascid eva tu: only *some one* is recited — one verse, the repetitions being mere re-utterances of it. (iii) Anarthakanam patho va: or there is *recitation of meaningless things* — the repeated tokens carrying no meaning, present only to make the count; sesas tu anyah pratiyate — and the remainder is understood as *something else*. Three accounts of what happens when the same words are said twice in a rite, and they disagree about the most basic question: how many things are there? The disarray is not incompetence. It is what 2.250-258 predict: identity and number are conferred by office, and where offices are contested (is the second recitation a new station or the same one served twice?), the count is contested — with nothing in the words to settle it, because the words never carried the count.

translation: By others, the own-form of the word is described as the meaning, and the recitation as (something) else; there is utter difference of all, but (identity) of the possessors, from connection with that.
commentary: A further account, and it draws the movement's threads to a point. Aparaih: by others — sabda-svarupam arthah — the *own-form* of the word is (its) meaning: the position 2.256 stated, now held as doctrine: what a word means, at bottom, is itself (2.127's abhijalpa-doctrine at its limit; 1.50's self-presentation made the whole story). Pathah anyaih upavarnyate: the *recitation* is described as something else — the utterance-event distinguished from the form recited. Then the summary: atyanta-bhedah sarvesam — there is *utter difference* of all of them (form, meaning, recitation, application: every item the analysis distinguishes stands utterly apart from every other) — tat-sambandhat tu tadvatam: but (identity/unity accrues) to *the possessors* from connection with that. The construal is difficult, and the difficulty is doctrinal: once analysis has cut form from meaning from recitation from office, nothing reassembles them except a sambandha — the connective whose emptiness 2.46 exposed. The verse is a last exhibit of the same pattern the kanda has traced from its first movement: cut, and you will need a knot; the knot will be a nothing; and the thing you cut was never cut.
contested: the syntax of the second line is genuinely obscure and our construal is one of several defensible; the transcription may also be at fault. What is clear is the contrast: utter difference among the analysed items, unity supplied only by connection

translation: One savitri is for the sacrament, another is employed in the rite, another in the sequences of muttered prayer — yet it is understood as one and the same.
commentary: The movement's most beautiful confirmation, and its subject is the most familiar verse in the tradition. The Savitri — the Gayatri — is deployed in three quite different offices: samskara-savitri, the one whose recitation makes the boy twice-born at initiation; the one employed karmani, in the rite; the one used japa-prabandhesu, in the sequences of muttered repetition. Three offices, and by 2.258's doctrine of vinyoga three verses. Anya... anya... anya: *another*, another, another — the verse says it three times. And then: sa tu eka eva pratiyate — *yet it is understood as one and the same*. Both halves are true and the verse asserts both without reconciling them. Individuated by office, one in itself: this is not a paradox to be resolved but the structure the whole kanda has been describing — the one appearing as many through conditions (2.250-251), the many being the one carved by nimittas, and comprehension holding both without strain, as every brahmin holds that the Savitri he mutters is the Savitri that made him. The verse is 1.2 (the one, resting on its differentiated powers, functioning as if divided) come home to the tradition's own daily practice.

translation: The operation of words upon the meaning's own form is held to be from their own form; the operation of the sentence-form upon the sentence-meaning is without dependence on anything else.
commentary: The self-sufficiency thesis, stated at both grains. Artha-svarupe sabdanam svarupad vrttih isyate: the operation of words upon the meaning's own form is *from their own form* — a word reaches its meaning by being what it is, not by way of some intermediary connection (2.46's inferred non-entity, dismissed). And the sentence: vakya-rupasya vakyarthe vrttih anya-anapeksaya — the sentence-form's operation upon the sentence-meaning is *without dependence on anything else*: not on its words, not on their meanings, not on a connective. Anya-anapeksaya is emphatic and it is the movement's terminus. The sentence does not reach its meaning *through* the words — this is why the flash is anyaiva (2.143), why the words are abhyupaya (2.248), why the strung sequence is not the meaning (2.249). Form and meaning stand to each other as the two faces of one thing (2.31), at word-grain and at sentence-grain alike, and nothing mediates because there are not two things to mediate between. The verse is the kanda's most compact statement of what it has been arguing since 2.7: the sentence means directly, and everything the analysis inserts between the sentence and its meaning is the analysis's own furniture.

translation: Those by whom the many-meaningedness of a single word is admitted — for them the construction of secondary and primary is made by attainment and non-attainment.
commentary: The doxographic ledger for the gauna/mukhya distinction, opened with the position the kanda holds. For those who admit anekarthatvam ekasya — the many-meaningedness of a *single* word (2.250's examiners, and Bhartrhari with them) — the gauna-mukhya-prakalpana is siddhy-asiddhi-krta: made by *attainment and non-attainment*. That is: a use is primary where the meaning is attained — arrives, lands, is simply there for the hearer — and secondary where it is not attained directly but reached with effort, through the occasion's help. The distinction survives, but as a fact about *how the meaning arrives*, not about what the word owns: 2.253's currency-doctrine given its cognitive face. Note that this leaves the word entirely undivided: the same plenitude, arriving easily here (the ox: well-worn channel) and with labour there (the man: channel less worn). The verses following canvass the rivals' versions of the same distinction, and the contrast will be instructive — each of them locates in the *word* what this one locates in the *arrival*.

translation: Or: that which, depending on the object and the context, or joined with other words, conveys the meaning — others know as secondary.
commentary: The rivals' first definition of the secondary, and it is a good one. Apare: others know as *gauna* that which conveys its meaning artha-prakarana-apeksah — *depending* on the object and the context — or sabdantaraih saha yuktah — *joined with other words*. Secondary use, on this account, is context-dependent use: the word that cannot deliver alone and needs the occasion's help. Note that the two channels named are exactly 2.251's — the same nimittas that, on Bhartrhari's account, carve *every* meaning out of the plenitude. So the rival's definition of the secondary is the holist's description of the ordinary: what this theorist marks as the deviant case (meaning determined by context and company) is, for the kanda, how words always work — the isolated word being the citation-artifact (2.17), not the norm. The definitions are diverging exactly where the theories diverge, and the divergence is visible in what each treats as needing explanation.

translation: That whose well-established own meaning is understood on the mere uttering of it, unmixed, is to be known as primary — grounded in the mere form.
commentary: The rivals' complementary definition of the primary. Suddhasya uccarane — on the uttering of the word *unmixed*, bare, without context or company — svarthah prasiddhah yasya gamyate: whose *well-established* own meaning is (nonetheless) understood — sa mukhya iti vijneyah, that is to be known as primary; rupa-matra-nibandhanah, grounded in the *mere form*. So the primary is the meaning the bare form delivers, and the test is citation: say the word alone and see what comes. It is a clean criterion and it is exactly the one the kanda has spent two hundred verses undermining. Prasiddhah is the tell — 'well-established', the same prasiddhi that 2.253 identified as *currency*: what the bare word delivers is not its own meaning but its commonest one, the channel most worn by use. The rival takes the citation-response for the word's nature; Bhartrhari takes it for a statistic (2.17: words look general in citation because citation is a context too, and a thin one). Both agree on what happens when you say 'gauh' to a Sanskritist. They disagree about whether that tells you anything about the word.

translation: But that which is, as it were, forcibly yoked by the employment of another — they consider not-established, as settling upon a secondary meaning.
commentary: The rivals' account of the secondary completed, and its vocabulary is revealing. Yah tu anyasya prayogena yatnad iva niyujyate — that which is *forcibly*, as it were with effort, yoked by another's employment: the word conscripted, pressed into a service not its own by the speaker's design — tam aprasiddham manyante gauna-artha-abhinivesinam: they consider it *not-established*, settling upon a secondary meaning. Two words carry the account's whole picture. Yatnad iva: *as if by force* — the secondary use is violence done to the word, a wrenching. Abhinivesin: *settling upon*, with a connotation of clinging or insisting, the word made to squat on a meaning that is not its home. This is metaphor understood as coercion, and it is the standard view — the poeticians would build an entire theory on the word's 'incapacity' in its primary sense forcing a secondary. Bhartrhari's alternative needs no violence: the word could always mean the man (2.250's sarvarthya); the occasion carved that meaning rather than the worn one; nothing was forced, and the felt effort (2.263's asiddhi) is the effort of a channel not yet worn, not the strain of a word being bent.

mula_deva: निमित्तं तत्र मुख्यं स्याद् निमित्ती गौण इष्यते ॥ २६७ ॥
mula_iast: nimittaṃ tatra mukhyaṃ syād nimittī gauṇa iṣyate || 267 ||
translation: Where one word, operating in its own meaning, has its meaning depended upon by another — there the ground would be primary, and the grounded is held secondary.
commentary: The rivals' most precise formulation, and it is a relational one. Consider two words: one svarthe pravartamanasya — *operating in its own meaning*; the other yasya artham yah avalambate — the one that *depends upon*, leans on, the first's meaning. Then: nimittam tatra mukhyam syat — the *ground*, the one leaned on, would be primary; nimitti gauna isyate — the *grounded*, the leaner, is held secondary. In 'the Bahika is an ox', the ox-meaning is the ground (it supplies the dullness); the man-meaning leans on it; so ox is primary, man secondary. The definition is elegant and it makes the distinction structural rather than statistical — which is precisely its vulnerability, and Bhartrhari has already placed the charge: the leaning is a fact about *this* use, in *this* company; reverse the currency (let 'gauh' be commonly said of stolid men and rarely of cattle) and the leaning reverses with it. Nothing in the word ordains which of its meanings is the ground. The rival has described the phenomenology of a particular figurative act with real precision and mistaken it for the anatomy of the word.

translation: The two which operate in different, contradictory meanings — as with 'pura' and 'arat' — their determination too depends on the object and the context.
commentary: The auto-antonyms, and they finish the movement's case. Pura means both 'formerly' and 'hereafter'; arat means both 'near' and 'far'. Bhinne 'rthe virodhini: the two meanings are not merely different but *contradictory* — each word carries its own opposite, and no shared property, no leaning, no ground-and-grounded relation (2.267) can connect them. So which is primary? The question has no answer, and the verse says so: tayoh api avadharanam artha-prakarana-apeksam — *their* determination too depends on the object and the context. Nothing but the occasion decides, and the occasion decides completely. For the rival accounts this is a hard case demanding special treatment; for 2.250-251 it is simply the ordinary structure with the primary/secondary illusion stripped away — one word, a plenitude including contradictories, carved by nimitta, and no meaning more its own than another. The auto-antonym is to the gauna/mukhya distinction what the freak compound (2.216) was to composition: not an anomaly but the general case, visible because nothing obscures it.
contested: pura and arat are our reading of the transcription's purarad iti; both are standard examples of words bearing opposite temporal/spatial senses, and the argument requires only that the two senses be contradictory

section: The Sentence as the Unit of Connection
translation: When the abstraction of word-meanings from the sentence's meaning has been constructed, how is connection with another word intelligible for any one of them?
commentary: A question that closes a circle opened at 2.10. Grant the apoddhara: vakyasya arthat padarthanam apoddhare prakalpite — the abstraction of word-meanings *from the sentence's meaning* having been constructed (the kanda's own doctrine, stated as accomplished). Then: sabdantarena sambandhah kasya ekasya upapadyate — how is *connection with another word* intelligible for any *one* of them? The force is this: the analyst's padarthas are extracts from one meaning; extracts do not connect — they were never apart. To speak of one word-meaning connecting with another is to speak as if they had been independently there and then joined, which is exactly what the abstraction-doctrine denies. So the whole vocabulary of syntactic connection (2.42's samsarga, 2.47's expectancy, every account of how words combine) is revealed as belonging to the scaffold and not to the meaning: within the analysis, the extracts must be re-tied, because the analysis cut them; in the meaning, nothing is tied because nothing was cut (2.249's chinna-grathita). The verse is the kanda's final disposal of the connection-problem — not solved but dissolved, by noticing that only the analysis ever had it.
contested: the transcription wavers between kasya and tasya; the interrogative reading (our choice) makes the verse the reductio it evidently is

translation: And that single word which is seen somewhere, its verb-of-existence having been discharged — they call that another sentence entire; it does not connect with anything else.
commentary: The one-word sentence, and it seals the movement. Ekam padam drstam kvacit carita-asti-kriyam: a *single word* seen somewhere with its asti-kriya — its verb of existence — *discharged*, already performed, absorbed (the elliptical 'Fire!', 'Water!', the injunctions and exclamations in which one word carries a whole predication). What is it? Tad vakyantaram eva ahuh: they call it *another sentence entire* — not a word but a sentence, complete, whose verb is done. Na tad anyena yujyate: and *it does not connect with anything else* — being complete, it has no expectancy left (2.4's nirakanksata, the mark of the sentence), so there is nothing for connection to do. The verse is the perfect complement to 2.269. There, the analyst's extracts cannot connect because they were never separate; here, the complete utterance does not connect because it needs nothing. Between the two, the entire connective apparatus has been shown to have no domain: not among the abstractions (which were one), not among the wholes (which are self-sufficient). And the one-word sentence makes the kanda's title-doctrine visible to the naked eye: sentencehood is not a matter of how many words there are. It is a matter of whether the expectancy has closed — which is to say, whether a flash has been occasioned.

mula_deva: यच्च कोऽयमिति प्रश्ने गौरश्व इति चोच्यते ।
प्रश्न एव क्रिया तत्र प्रक्रान्ता दर्शनादिका ॥ २७१ ॥
mula_iast: yacca ko'yamiti praśne gauraśva iti cocyate |
praśna eva kriyā tatra prakrāntā darśanādikā || 271 ||
translation: And when, at the question 'who is this?', 'a cow' or 'a horse' is uttered — there the question itself is the action commenced: seeing and the like.
commentary: The one-word sentence (2.270) explained by its context. Asked ko 'yam, 'who is this?', one answers gauh — a single word, and unmistakably a complete sentence. Where is its verb? Prasna eva kriya tatra prakranta darsanadika: *the question itself* is the action commenced — the seeing and the like. The interrogative has already deployed the verb; the answer's single word steps into a predication that the question built and left open. So the one-word utterance is not a word doing a sentence's work but a *sentence whose remainder was supplied by the discourse* — 2.76's larger unity in miniature: the unit is the exchange, and 'gauh' is complete within it because expectancy closed there. This is also the cleanest possible demonstration of 2.113's thesis: sentence-meaning is prayojana, purpose — and the purpose here was set by the asking. What the answer means is fixed by what was wanted.

translation: Neither excess of properties nor deficiency is what occasions (secondariness); some hold excess also, and sometimes deficiency, on account of established currency.
commentary: A demolition of the standard criteria for figurative use, and it is brisk. When 'gauh' is said of the Bahika, the man has *fewer* of the cow's properties (no dewlap, no horns) — so deficiency, nyunata, looks like the mark of secondariness. But some hold adhikatvam, *excess*, to be the mark instead (the case where the figure attributes more than the primary bears: 'he is a lion' claiming a courage beyond any lion's). Bhartrhari's verdict: naiva adhikatvam dharmanam nyunata va prayojika — *neither* is what occasions it; and the reason for the disagreement is given in the last word: prasiddheh — from *established currency*. The theorists disagree because they are reading off usage, and usage varies. The distinction they are trying to ground in a property-count has no property-count to stand on; it stands, as 2.253 said, on how worn the channel is. Every attempt to make gauna/mukhya a structural matter runs into this: the structures point both ways, and only currency is constant.

translation: Where a universal-word is employed even without the universal, on account of a property resembling that of the relatum — others know that as secondary.
commentary: Another rival definition, and a careful one. A jati-sabda — 'gauh', a word whose office is a universal — is employed antarena api jatim, *even without the universal* being present: the Bahika has no cowness. What licenses it? Sambandhi-sadrsad dharmat — a property *resembling* that of the relatum: the man's stolidity resembling the ox's. So the account is resemblance-based: the universal absent, a similar property present, the word extended on the strength of the resemblance. Apare vidus tam gaunam: others know *that* as secondary. It is the definition most readers would give unprompted, and Bhartrhari's objection is not stated here because it has already been made: 2.90-94 showed that resemblance is respect-wise apprehension of undivided wholes, not shared components — so 'a resembling property' is not a thing two objects both have but a way one cognition takes two. Build metaphor on resemblance and you have built it on a cognitive act, which is where the kanda locates meaning anyway (2.143). The rival is closer to the holist than he knows.

mula_deva: मन्यन्ते स गवादिस्तु गौण इत्युच्यते क्वचित् ॥ २७४ ॥
mula_iast: manyante sa gavādistu gauṇa ityucyate kvacit || 274 ||
translation: Where, as if by a transposition of the meaning, one thinks there is as it were an otherness of meaning — that use of 'cow' and the like is somewhere called secondary.
commentary: A third rival definition, and its hedges are its confession. Viparyasad iva arthasya — as if by a *transposition* of the meaning (2.255's artha-matram viparyastam) — yatra arthantarataam iva manyante: where one *thinks* there is *as it were* an otherness of meaning. Count the qualifications: iva, iva, manyante, kvacit — 'as if', 'as it were', 'one thinks', 'somewhere'. The definition cannot state its own content without four hedges, because what it is trying to name is not a fact about the word but an impression in the hearer: the sense that something has been shifted. Bhartrhari records the hedges rather than smoothing them, and they make his case for him. Secondariness, on every rival account canvassed (2.264-267, 2.273-274), turns out to be describable only in terms of how the use *strikes* someone — forced (2.266), leaning (2.267), resembling (2.273), transposed (here) — which is to say: in terms of the arrival, not the word. Which is 2.263's position, held by the very theorists who deny it.

translation: (275) As plough, sword, pestle and the like are understood, in their operations, as fixed in the character of instruments, endowed with form and capacity — (276) and their capacities do not arise through a different action; from their form alone their being-for-that-purpose is understood as a rule — (277) just so, the word, standing established by its form and capacity from its production, having a fixed purpose, is (nonetheless) employed elsewhere by capacity.
commentary: The instrument-simile, and it is the movement's most careful piece of work. Consider the plough, the sword, the pestle: each is niyata sadhanatvena — *fixed* in its instrumental character, rupa-sakti-samanvita, endowed with form and capacity. And the two facts about them. (276): their capacities do not arise kriyantarena, through some *other* action — the sword's cutting-power is not conferred on it by being used to cut; rupad eva tadarthyam niyamena pratiyate: from its *form alone* its being-for-that-purpose is understood, as a rule. Look at a sword and you know what it is for. (277) applies it: tathaiva rupa-saktibhyam utpattya samavasthitah sabdah niyata-tadarthyah — just so the word, established by form and capacity from its very production, has a *fixed purpose* — saktya anyatra prayujyate: and yet *by capacity* it is employed *elsewhere*. That last clause is where the simile turns. A sword has a fixed purpose and can still be used to prop a door; the use is not the sword's purpose and is entirely within the sword's power. So with the word: fixed purpose (the current meaning, prasiddhi), and a capacity that outruns it (2.250's sarvarthya). The 'secondary' use is neither a violation (2.266's forcing) nor a different word — it is the instrument's own power, deployed off its accustomed office. The simile grants the rivals everything they wanted about fixity and takes back everything they built on it.
contested: 277's utpattya ('from its production') is difficult — we take it as 'from the moment of its arising', i.e. the word's form-and-capacity are constitutive rather than acquired; some read it with niyata-tadarthyah, 'having a purpose fixed by its derivation', which would make the point etymological and sit oddly with 2.170-175

translation: Where its being-for-that-purpose is determined by the mere hearing, they consider that meaning primary; secondary, where it is (only) brought about.
commentary: The criterion restated in the instrument-simile's terms, and now it is exact. Sruti-matrena — by the *mere hearing*, the word alone, nothing else deployed — yatra asya tadarthyam avasiyate: where its being-for-that-purpose is determined: mukhyam tam artham manyante, that meaning is considered primary. And gaunam yatra upapaditam: secondary where it is *brought about* — where the meaning has to be produced, occasioned, worked for by the context. Upapadita is the verse's precision: it is 2.143's own word for what the padarthas do to the sentence-meaning (padarthaih upapaditam — 'brought about by the word-meanings'). So the 'secondary' meaning stands to its occasion as the sentence-meaning stands to its words: occasioned, not contained. Which means the distinction primary/secondary is a distinction between meanings that need less occasioning and meanings that need more — a gradient, not a boundary. The sword recognized at sight versus the sword recognized as a door-prop once you see the door: same sword, same powers, different amount of context required.

translation: In the sense of the cvi-affix, 'go', 'yusmat' and 'mahat' stand in a meaning other than their own; there the becoming-that of the other meaning is seen as primary even so.
commentary: A grammatical case that complicates the gradient, and Bhartrhari lets it. The cvi-affix forms the 'becoming' verbs: agni-bhavati, 'it becomes fire'; suklibhavati, 'it becomes white'. In such formations go, yusmat, mahat — cow, you, great — stand svarthad arthantare sthitau: in a meaning *other than their own*, since the form no longer names a cow but a becoming-cow. And yet: arthantarasya tad-bhavah tatra mukhyah api drsyate — the *becoming-that* of the other meaning is seen *as primary even so*. The construction's whole point is transformation into the other, so the 'other' meaning is exactly what the form is for — its primary office, not a stretch. The case matters because it shows the gradient is not a simple function of distance from the citation-meaning: here the word is used at maximum distance from its bare sense and the use is fully primary, because the *construction* fixes the purpose (2.277's tadarthya, conferred by form). Grammar can build a form whose primary meaning is the departure. Which is one more demonstration that primariness is an office, not a possession.
contested: the cvi-construction is standard; whether Bhartrhari cites it as a genuine counter-case to his gradient or as a case the gradient absorbs (the construction being its own nimitta) is undecidable — we read it as the latter

translation: The base attains greatness and the state of being white; but that (base), being depended upon by way of difference, is what occasions secondariness.
commentary: The cvi-case analysed, and the analysis locates secondariness precisely. In suklibhavati the prakrti, the base ('sukla'), pratipadyate the state of being white — the base does deliver its own content; there is no mystery about what it contributes. But: bhedena apeksita sa tu gaunatvasya prayojika — that base, *being depended upon by way of difference*, is what occasions the secondariness. The phrase bhedena apeksita is the key: the base is leaned on *as differentiated*, as a term standing apart within the construction, and it is that standing-apart-and-being-leaned-on which generates whatever secondariness the form has (2.267's nimitta/nimitti relation, now located in the derivational morphology rather than between two words). The verse's interest is methodological: it shows that even where a construction's primary purpose is 'becoming other' (2.279), the analyst can find a secondariness inside it — by dividing. Divide, and a leaner and a leaned-on appear; and where they appear, gauna appears. Secondariness is a shadow cast by apoddhara.

translation: Words such as 'agni' and 'soma', which are grounded in their own form, are employed together with their name-bearers; secondariness in them is on account of non-establishment.
commentary: Proper names as the test case for the currency-doctrine. Agni, Soma: words svarupa-nibandhanah, grounded in their own form — used as *names* (of a man called Agnidatta, of a boy called Soma), samjnibhih samprayujyante, employed together with their name-bearers. When 'Agni' names a man, is that secondary? The verse: aprasiddheh tesu gaunata — secondariness in them is *on account of non-establishment*: because the naming-use has not (yet) become current for this hearer. Not because a fire-meaning is being stretched to a man; not because of resemblance (the boy is not fiery) or leaning or transposition — none of the rivals' machinery applies to proper names, which is exactly why they are the test. What makes the use feel secondary is only that the channel is unworn. And once it is worn — once you know the man — 'Agni' delivers him with no felt stretch at all, sruti-matrena (2.278). The name-case isolates the currency-variable from every other, and the currency-variable alone accounts for the phenomenon. This is 2.253's thesis at its cleanest.

translation: But where an Agnidatta is himself a fire — there the word, having its own meaning subordinated, attains secondariness on account of operating on the meaning of 'datta'.
commentary: The mirror-case, constructed with some wit. Take a man named Agnidatta ('fire-given'), and now suppose he *is* a fire — or rather, consider the word 'agni' inside the compound-name when the compound is being used of its bearer. There: svartha-upasarjanah sabdah — the word has its own meaning *subordinated*, made upasarjana (the technical term for the dependent member, 1.62, 2.226); datta-artha-vrttitvat gaunatvam pratipadyate — it attains secondariness *because it operates on the meaning of 'datta'*: 'agni' inside 'Agnidatta' is not naming a fire, it is qualifying a giving. The verse pairs with 2.281 to complete the demonstration: the same word 'agni' is secondary in one direction (name of a man: unworn channel) and secondary in another (member of a name-compound: subordinated office), and the two secondarinesses have nothing in common — one is a fact about currency, the other about grammatical rank. The category 'gauna' is not one category. It is a bag into which theorists have swept every case where a use does not arrive with the ease of the commonest one.

translation: When the operation of deriving words has been commenced, on account of difference of occasioning ground, the presence and absence of the augment 'sut' stand settled in 'Hariscandra' and the like.
commentary: The doctrine confirmed from the grammar's own augment-rules, and the case is exquisitely apt. Hariscandra: the name takes the augment sut (the s in hari-s-candra) — or does not, in otherwise parallel compounds. Why? Nimitta-bhedat, from *difference of occasioning ground* — the augment's presence or absence tracks which derivational ground the analyst credits, which is to say, which story he tells about the compound's formation. Sabda-vyutpatti-karmani prakrante: when the operation of deriving has been *commenced* — the augment's fate is settled inside the derivation, by the derivation's own choice of nimitta. So even the phonological shape of the word depends on which of the possible occasioning grounds the grammar elects (2.170's under-determination, 2.171's confusions of ground and limit — now shown to have audible consequences). This is as far as the point can be pressed: the analyst's choice of nimitta does not merely re-describe the word, it *changes the form* the rules produce. Postings all the way down, and the postings have teeth.

translation: A word that has obtained its refinement in the case of a seer's name and the like, when it is joined with another — there the internal refinement does not withdraw in respect of the external meaning.
commentary: A rule about the persistence of grammatical form across changes of reference. A word that has prapta-samskarah — obtained its *refinement*, its grammatically-formed shape — rsy-adau, in the case of a seer's name and such (names having their own formation-rules), and which is then anyena yujyate, joined with another (used of something else, transferred to a new bearer): tatra antarangah samskarah bahye 'rthe na nivartate — there the *internal* refinement does not withdraw in respect of the *external* meaning. The form, once made, stays made: the word carries its morphology into every new office, and the new referent does not unmake it. This is 2.256's svarupa-invariance given its grammatical statement, and it is the exact complement of everything the movement has argued: what is fixed about a word is its form; what is free is its meaning. The rivals had it backwards — they fixed the meaning (primary sense, the word's own) and let the application wander (2.255's transposition). Bhartrhari fixes the form (antaranga, made once, never withdrawing) and lets the meaning be carved by occasion. The grammar's own antaranga-doctrine testifies for him.
contested: antaranga/bahya we read as internal (formal) versus external (referential); the technical antaranga-paribhasa (inner rules take precedence) is surely being invoked, but the verse's own contrast is the form/meaning one

translation: Even when the meaning determined is utterly contrary, the word operates there as primary, according to the comprehension.
commentary: The movement's conclusion, and it is radical. Atyanta-viparito 'pi yada yo 'rtho avadharyate: even when the meaning determined is *utterly contrary* — the man taken for a post, the shell for silver, the rope for a snake, the word's referent as wrong as it can be — yatha-sampratyayam sabdas tatra mukhyah pravartate: the word operates there *as primary*, *according to the comprehension*. Say 'snake!' of a rope in the dusk, and 'snake' is not being used figuratively. It is being used primarily, of what the speaker took to be a snake. Error does not make speech figurative; it makes it false. And that distinction — which the whole movement has been clearing ground for — is what separates the gauna from the mithya: secondariness is a matter of currency and occasioning (2.253, 2.278), falsity is a matter of the world. Yatha-sampratyayam, 'according to the comprehension', is the standing criterion (2.34): what the word does is fixed by what was understood, not by what was there. The verses following ask the harder question this opens — how, then, do we ever tell the rope from the snake?

translation: Although the determination of the meaning's reality depends on cognition, not every cognition arises in respect of it as if unestablished.
commentary: The guard against the obvious misreading of 2.285. Yes: arthatattva-avadharanam pratyaya-adhinam — the determination of the meaning's *reality* depends on cognition; there is no access to what is real except through some cognition of it (2.132-133's ledger). But: na sarvah pratyayah tasmin asiddha iva jayate — *not every* cognition arises in respect of it *as if unestablished*: cognitions are not all equally shaky; some arrive with the character of the settled, some with the character of the doubtful, and the difference is *in* them, not imposed afterward. This blocks the slide from 'all access is cognitive' to 'no cognition is better than another' — the slide that 2.285's tolerance might seem to license. Bhartrhari has already refused it in Kanda I (1.39's undoubted cognition; 1.141's audit) and refuses it again here. The dependence of reality-determination on cognition is not the collapse of the distinction between veridical and erroneous; it is the statement that the distinction must be drawn *within* cognition. The next verses draw it.

translation: The seeing in the case of water is like the seeings of a mirage and such; the seeings being alike, water is not a mirage.
commentary: The distinction drawn, and the verse's structure is its argument. First line: darsanam salile tulyam mrgatrsnadi-darsanaih — the *seeing* in the case of real water is *like* the seeing of a mirage: phenomenally indistinguishable; the thirsty man's experience is the same in both, which is why he runs in both. Second line: tulyatve darsananam na jalam mrgatrsnika — the seeings being alike, *water is not a mirage*. The flat refusal is the point. Indistinguishability of the experiences does not entail identity of the objects; the sceptic's inference (we cannot tell them apart, therefore there is no difference) is invalid, and the verse simply declines it. This is the exact counterweight to 2.140's emblem (the sky's false floor): there, perception's confident deliverance of what is not there; here, the reminder that its equally confident deliverance of what *is* there is not thereby impugned. Phenomenal parity is a fact about us. The difference between water and mirage is a fact about them — and the next verse says how we get at it.

translation: The non-common effect that is well established for a rope and a snake — by that, the difference of the two is to be discriminated, even though the seeing is alike.
commentary: The criterion, and it is a pragmatic one. Rope and snake, in the dusk, are one seeing. What distinguishes them? Yad asadharanam karyam prasiddham rajju-sarpayoh: the *non-common effect* well established for each — the snake bites, the rope does not; the rope ties, the snake will not. Tena bhedah paricchedyah — *by that* the difference is to be discriminated — tayoh tulye 'pi darsane: even though the seeing is alike. The test is differential efficacy: not how it looks but what it does, and specifically what it does that the other cannot. This is the tradition's arthakriya-criterion (2.33's own vocabulary, the Buddhists' central test of the real) deployed against phenomenal scepticism — and it is exactly the audit 2.141 prescribed: examine the perceptible by reasoning, do not posit the seen on seeing's authority. Note that the criterion is available *within* cognition (one cognizes the biting, or its absence) — so 2.286's constraint is honoured: the distinction between the veridical and the erroneous is drawn inside cognition, by cognition's own differential yields, without appeal to an outside we do not have.

translation: And where an occasioning ground for the transposition of a well-established meaning is seen — the difference that is marked from that, they declare to be the unreal.
commentary: The definition of the unreal, arrived at by the same route. Prasiddha-artha-viparyasa-nimittam yac ca drsyate — where an *occasioning ground* for the transposition of a well-established meaning is *seen*: where one can identify what produced the misjudgement (the dusk, the coiled shape, the fear, the distance) — yah tasmat laksyate bhedah tad asatyam pracaksate: the difference marked from *that* is what they declare *unreal*. The unreal is not defined by how it appears (2.287: appearances are parity) nor by decree, but by *diagnosability*: an error is a taking whose occasioning ground can be exhibited. The rope-snake is unreal because one can say why it looked that way; the water is real because no such account is forthcoming — one looks for the mirage's cause and finds instead a pond. This is a criterion of remarkable modernity — error identified by the availability of a debunking explanation — and it completes the epistemology of 2.285-288 without ever leaving the circle of cognition. It also, quietly, explains 2.140's dome: we can say why the sky looks floored, and that is why it is not.

mula_deva: न तत्र प्रतिघातादि कार्यं तद्वत् प्रवर्तते ॥ २९० ॥
mula_iast: na tatra pratighātādi kāryaṃ tadvat pravartate || 290 ||
translation: And where, in a painting, high and low are of like form with mountains and the like — there the effect of obstruction and the rest does not operate as it does with those.
commentary: The criteria applied to the case that will matter most, and it is chosen with care. In a citra, a painting, nimna-unnate — the depths and heights, the modelled recession — are sarupam parvatadibhih: of *like form* with real mountains: phenomenal parity again (2.287), and the painter's whole art consists in achieving it. But: na tatra pratighatadi karyam tadvat pravartate — there the effect of *obstruction* and the rest does not operate as it does with those: you cannot climb the painted mountain, it does not block the wind, it stops nothing. Asadharanam karyam (2.288) is absent; the debunking ground is available (it is a painted surface, 2.289); therefore the painted mountain is unreal, however like. The example is the movement's terminus and it is doing more than it says. The kanda has repeatedly used the picture-analogy for the sentence (2.8-9: the painting one in form, described by its differences; 2.52: the figure executed in stages) — and here the painting is the emblem of the real-seeming unreal. The suggestion, left unstated, is the one Kanda I made at 1.129 with the fire-brand circle: what language builds has exactly this status — vivid, coherent, useless for climbing — and knowing it is what 2.238's untrue path means by standing on the false to seek the true.

mula_deva: न तथाऽलातचक्रस्य विच्छिन्नं स्पृश्यते हि तत् ॥ २९१ ॥
mula_iast: na tathā'lātacakrasya vicchinnaṃ spṛśyate hi tat || 291 ||
translation: (291) As the continuity of touch by the hand exists for a real wheel — not so for a firebrand-circle; for that is touched as interrupted. (292) And as, in cities, there is touching and enclosure by ramparts, walls and terraces — those are not so in gandharva-cities. (293) As much purpose as is accomplished by real deer, cattle and the like — that much does not exist even in a clay one; therefore they are the domain of the 'ka' suffix.
commentary: Three applications of the effect-criterion (2.288), and the third is a small joke with a doctrine inside it. The firebrand-circle (1.129's emblem): a real wheel gives sparsa-prabandha, a *continuity of touch* to the hand laid on it; the whirling brand gives interrupted contact — vicchinnam sprsyate — the hand finds gaps where the eye found a circle. Touch audits sight, and the circle fails. The gandharva-city, that shimmering mirage-town of the tradition: real cities enclose and resist by rampart and wall; the phantom city does not — no obstruction, no shelter. And the clay deer: it accomplishes less than a living one — na tavat mrnmaye 'pi asti — and the verse's conclusion is a grammatical pun. Tasmat te visayah kanah: *therefore they are the domain of the 'ka' suffix* — the affix that forms diminutives and likenesses (mrga-ka, 'a little deer', 'a mere deer-thing'). The clay deer is deficient in efficacy, and the grammar marks the deficiency morphologically: language has a suffix for the not-quite-real. The point is serious under the wit — the effect-criterion is not the philosopher's invention; it is already sedimented in the language's own machinery for marking simulacra.
contested: kanah we take as the affix ka (the diminutive/similitude-forming suffix, Panini 5.3.96ff.); the reading makes the verse's conclusion a grammatical observation, which fits the kanda's habit of finding its doctrines in the sastra's own postings

translation: A great region is covered by well-established mountains and the like; but a reflection is seen as standing within a small extent of space.
commentary: The effect-criterion applied to the reflection — the image the work has used at every load-bearing joint (1.49, 1.99-100). A real mountain avriyate mahan desah — *covers a great region*: occupies, occludes, takes up the space its size requires. The reflected mountain is alpa-desantara-avastham drsyate — seen as standing within a *small extent*: the whole range in the palm-sized mirror, in the sword's blade, in the eye. Size, which for the real thing is an efficacy (it covers), is for the reflection a mere appearance (it does not). This is 1.100's argument (the mountain in the gem-facet: viruddha-parimana, contradictory measures) now stated as an effect-test rather than a reductio: not merely that duplicate-entities would be absurd, but that the reflection *fails the criterion* — it does not do what mountains do. The verse quietly completes a long arc. The reflection has served throughout as the model for the sphota's borrowed properties; here it is examined in its own right and found unreal by the same test that found the painted mountain unreal (2.290). The instrument of the great similes has been audited by the criterion the similes' user gave us.

mula_deva: न ते स्वप्नादिषु स्वस्य तद्वदर्थस्य साधकाः ॥ २९५ ॥
mula_iast: na te svapnādiṣu svasya tadvadarthasya sādhakāḥ || 295 ||
translation: And as real poisons and the like are the occasion of death and such — those, in dreams and the like, are not accomplishers of that same purpose for oneself.
commentary: The criterion's last and most intimate application. Mukhyah visadayah — *real* poisons — are marana-adi-nimittam, the occasion of death and the rest: that is what poison does, and doing it is what makes it poison. Na te svapnadisu svasya tadvad arthasya sadhakah — those, in dreams and such, are not *for oneself* accomplishers of that purpose: the dreamt poison kills the dreamt man and leaves the dreamer breathing. The example completes the series (firebrand, phantom city, clay deer, reflection, painting) with the case where the stakes are one's own life, and the effect-criterion holds there as everywhere: what does not do it, is not it. Note svasya — *for oneself*: the qualification is exact and it concedes what the dream-world genuinely has. Within the dream the poison works; the dreamt man does die. What the dream lacks is efficacy across the boundary, for the dreamer. Efficacy is thus always efficacy-within-a-domain — which is precisely the vyavahara-relativity the kanda has honoured throughout (2.142's working namings; 1.28's vyavastha), and which keeps the criterion from collapsing into a naive realism it never wanted.

translation: That which, through differences residing in place, time and sense-faculty, is seen otherwise — it is determined according as the world's established currency has it.
commentary: The final adjudication, and it hands the verdict to a court the kanda has been deferring to since 1.27. Desa-kala-indriya-gataih bhedaih — through differences residing in *place*, *time*, and *sense-faculty* (the three sources of perceptual variation catalogued at 2.105 and 2.136) — yad drsyate anyatha: what is seen *otherwise*, variously, by different observers at different places and times. How is it settled? Yatha prasiddhir lokasya tatha tad avasiyate: *according as the world's established currency has it*, so is it determined. Prasiddhi — the same currency that decided primary and secondary (2.253, 2.281) — now decides real and apparent. This is neither scepticism nor dogmatism but the position 2.142 staked: the world's working namings are not to be second-guessed by the wise, because they are not claims about tattva; they are the settled outcome of countless audits by countless observers with hands to touch the wheel (2.291) and walls to walk into (2.292). Loka-prasiddhi *is* the effect-criterion, applied over generations and sedimented. The epistemology ends where the kanda's authority-doctrine began: in the unbroken practice of the many.

translation: And the cognition that is born of an impairment, and the cognition that is non-worldly — by neither of those two is there any practice; words are grounded in the world.
commentary: The boundary of the linguistic, drawn with a firm hand. Two kinds of cognition fall outside. Upaghata-jam jnanam — cognition born of *impairment*: the jaundiced eye's yellow, the fevered tongue's bitterness, the madman's certainty — private, undersharable, failing the world's audit (2.296). And jnanam alaukikam — the *non-worldly* cognition: the seer's vision, settled in reality (1.139: 'by it there is no worldly practice, nor is it fastened to words'). Na tabhyam vyavaharo 'sti — by neither is there *practice*: the impaired cannot transact because nobody shares his yellow; the seer cannot transact because what he sees does not parcel into speech. And the conclusion, which is the kanda's charter: sabdah loka-nibandhanah — *words are grounded in the world*. Language lives in the middle band — above the private, below the ineffable — and its home is the loka, the shared, audited, currency-settled domain of ordinary transaction. This is why the sciences describe avidya (2.233) and why that is no disgrace: the constructed middle is exactly where speech works, and a word that reached past it would not be a word.

section: The Lamp and the Unintended Meaning
translation: (298) As a lamp, employed for whatever purpose among pots and the like, effects the illumination also of what is other than that, by way of accompaniment — (299) so, among commingled objects, the word, employed for whatever purpose, conveys also others than that occasioner.
commentary: A new movement, on the meanings a word delivers without being meant to — and the lamp returns to carry it. A lamp is brought in for the pot: yena arthena prayujyate, for whatever purpose it is employed. But it lights the mat, the wall, the cat: tato 'nyasya api sacivyat — of what is *other* than that, by way of *accompaniment*, sa karoti prakasanam. Illumination has no way of restricting itself to what it was wanted for; it falls on everything in reach. Tatha: so the word. Samsargisu arthesu — among *commingled* objects, meanings that come mixed together (2.153-154's inescapable furniture) — the word employed for one purpose tasmat prayojakat anyan api pratyayayati asau: conveys *others* than the occasioner too. This is the mechanism behind the whole penumbra of 2.161-163 (the flower arriving on its scent) now given its proper simile: not association merely, but *illumination overspilling its errand*. The lamp-image has served the work at every scale (1.46's fire in the wood, 1.94's unchanging thing lit by changing light, 2.134's faculty displaying by its variety); here it explains why speech always says more than it means to.

translation: (300) As the churning of the two fire-sticks, undertaken for the sake of fire, being a single means, generates smoke also, which was not intended — (301) so the word too, when some conveyable meaning is intended, illuminates even an unintended meaning, from proximity.
commentary: The same doctrine with a sharper simile, and the sharpening matters. The lamp (2.298) lights the mat because the mat is *there*; nothing about the lamp requires a mat. But the fire-drill: nirmanthanam aranyoh agny-artham upapaditam — the churning of the fire-sticks, undertaken *for fire's sake* — dhumam api anabhipretam janayati eka-sadhanam: generates *smoke* as well, unintended, *being a single means*. Smoke is not an accident of the surroundings; it is what fire-making does. The unintended is here the *same operation's* other yield — you cannot have the fire without it. Tatha sabdo 'pi: so the word — kasmimscit pratyayye 'rthe vivaksite, some conveyable meaning being *intended* — avivaksitam api artham prakasayati sannidheh: illuminates the *unintended* meaning too, from proximity. The pair of similes brackets the phenomenon exactly: some overspill is circumstantial (the lamp's mat) and some is constitutive (the fire's smoke) — and speech has both. Avivaksita, the unintended, has been the movement's technical term since 1.136 and 2.69, always as what meaning *excludes*; here it returns as what meaning cannot help delivering. Intention governs what is said and does not govern what arrives.

mula_deva: तथा शब्दोऽपि सम्बन्धी प्रविवेक्तुं न शक्यते ॥ ३०२ ॥
mula_iast: tathā śabdo'pi sambandhī pravivektuṃ na śakyate || 302 ||
translation: Just as an object utterly commingled cannot be abandoned — so a word that is a relatum cannot be separated out.
commentary: The reason the overspill cannot be stopped, stated as a symmetry. Atyanta-samsrstah artho na sakyate tyaktum — an object *utterly commingled* cannot be abandoned: you cannot fetch the cow and leave her colour behind; the furniture comes because it is not detachable (2.154's samsrsta, now given its consequence). Tatha sabdo 'pi sambandhi pravivektum na sakyate: so too a word that is a *relatum* — that stands in relation, as every uttered word does — cannot be *separated out*. The symmetry is the argument: as objects come in bundles, so words come in company, and neither the bundle nor the company can be dissolved by wanting. The speaker who wishes to say just this much and no more is in the position of one who wishes to bring the cow without her colour. Pravivektum — 'to separate out' — is the apoddhara-vocabulary (2.10's extraction), and its appearance here is precise: the analyst can extract a word in his ledger; the speaker cannot extract it in the world. What analysis does on paper, utterance cannot do in the air.

translation: Even when meaningless things are in proximity, and even when these are illuminated, the occasioning meaning of a word is commended, though there be no difference of form.
commentary: A qualification that keeps the doctrine from dissolving into noise. Grant that words illuminate the unintended (2.299-301) and that proximity cannot be prevented (2.302). Does the word then simply mean everything in its vicinity? No: prayojakah arthah sabdasya sasyate — the *occasioning* meaning of the word is *commended*, singled out, held up — even where vyarthanam sannidhane, meaningless things stand in proximity and are themselves lit; and even rupa-abhede 'pi, where there is *no difference of form* to mark the intended from the incidental. The distinction between what a word is *for* and what it merely *reaches* survives, and it survives without any formal marker: nothing in the sound tells you which of the lit things was the errand. What tells you is the prayojaka — the occasioning purpose (2.113's prayojana, the sentence's meaning). So the overspill is real and the intention is sovereign: the lamp lights the cat, and the lamp was brought for the pot, and no inspection of the light will tell you which — only knowing what was wanted.

translation: (304) Sometimes the subordinate-and-principal status of meanings is unintended; sometimes even their proximity is no cause in the comprehension. (305) And what is not taken up by the word is understood in some case; sometimes the principal meaning itself becomes the indicator of another.
commentary: Four observations that dismantle any tidy mapping from form to content. (304): sometimes the *hierarchy* of meanings — which is guna, which pradhana — is avivaksita, unintended: the sentence does not say which of its contents is central, and the hearer must find it. And sometimes proximity itself is akaranam pratipattau — *no cause* in comprehension: things stand adjacent and contribute nothing, the lamp's light falling on what nobody notices. (305): and yad anupattam sabdena — what is *not taken up* by the word at all — tat kasmimscit pratiyate: is understood in some case (the bahuvrihi's unuttered possessor, 2.219; the question's unstated verb, 2.271). And kvacit pradhanam eva artho bhavaty anyasya laksanam: sometimes the *principal* meaning itself becomes the *indicator* of another — the central content demoted to a mere signpost toward what was actually meant. Take the four together and there is no rule connecting what is uttered, what is proximate, and what is comprehended: content can be present without being meant, meant without being present, central without being the point. Which is exactly what one expects if comprehension is a flash occasioned by an utterance (2.143), and exactly what no compositional calculus can tolerate.

translation: The finite verb is what somehow exhibits the meaning of a secondary suffix; there the reverse of the subordinate-and-principal relation is seen.
commentary: A grammatical instance of 2.305's inversion. The taddhita, the secondary suffix, carries a meaning; and sometimes it is the akhyata, the *finite verb*, that exhibits it — yat kincid upadarsakam, somehow displaying what the suffix would have said. And the consequence: guna-apradhana-bhavasya tatra drsto viparyayah — there is seen *the reverse* of the subordinate-and-principal relation: the element the grammar files as principal is doing the subordinate's work, and vice versa. The rank the system assigns is not the rank the utterance enacts. This is 2.229-231's ledger-shuffling (base-meaning borne by suffix, suffix-meaning by root) reaching the syntactic categories themselves — and it lands with particular force because pradhana/guna is the axis on which the entire analysis of the sentence turns (2.4's definition, 2.48's architecture, 2.82's ritual hierarchy). If even *that* inverts under employment, then the categories are offices conferred by use, from top to bottom, with nothing underneath them but the flash they serve.
contested: the verse is compressed and the specific construction it has in mind is not recoverable; our reading takes it as a general observation about verbs discharging taddhita-meanings, which the guna/pradhana inversion in the second line requires

translation: In the designation of genders and numbers, proximity is no cause.
commentary: A half-verse, and it applies 2.304's second observation to the grammar's own metalanguage. When the science *designates* genders and numbers — when a rule mentions 'the feminine', 'the dual' — sannidhanam akaranam: the *proximity* of the actual gendered or numbered things is no cause: the rule is not made true by there being feminine things about, and the mention does not reach out to them. Metalinguistic designation floats free of its subject matter's presence, exactly as 2.100 said (mention proceeds by exhibited form, not by what is understood). The half-verse is doing housekeeping before the movement's last topic — the semantics of measure-words — but its placement is not idle: it reminds the reader that everything about to be said concerning 'short' and 'long' is being said in a metalanguage whose relation to its objects is itself a posting.
contested: the source paginates 2.307 as two half-verses; we key commentary to each half as the corpus labels them

translation: It is the measure itself, in 'short' and the like, that is understood, though not taken up.
commentary: The complement, and it opens the movement's final case. Say 'short' — hrasva — of a vowel. What is understood? Pramanam eva — the *measure itself*: a determinate quantity, one matra. And: anupattam pratiyate — *though not taken up*: no word for the measure occurs; 'short' does not contain 'one matra'; the quantity arrives unuttered (2.305's anupatta, now in the grammar's most technical vocabulary). Measure-terms are thus the purest specimens of the movement's thesis: what they convey is precisely what they do not say. And they are chosen for a further reason. Vowel-quantity was the datum that most embarrassed the timeless-word doctrine back at 1.76 — the one acoustic difference that is genuinely contrastive — and the resolution there was that duration belongs to the manifesting sound and is transferred to the word figuratively. Here the same territory is re-entered from the semantic side: what does the *word* 'short' mean? The next verses answer, and the answer is that it means by indicating, not by containing.

mula_deva: दीर्घप्लुताभ्यां तस्य स्याद् मात्रया वा विशेषणम् ।
mula_iast: dīrghaplutābhyāṃ tasya syād mātrayā vā viśeṣaṇam |
translation: (308) And that half which is seen of a short (vowel) — that, even in its absence, is expressed just as such, because the short serves as the indicator. (309) Or its qualification would be by the long and the prolated, or by the matra; or there is indication of the universal — since in every way it is like 'seven-leaf'.
commentary: The measure-word's semantics, canvassed to exhaustion, and the last simile is the movement's signature. The problem: the pluta (three matras), the dirgha (two), the hrasva (one) are defined by reference to one another, and half a short is spoken of though no such vowel exists. (308): hrasvasya ardham — the half of a short — is expressed *even in its absence*, tasya asannidhav api, because hrasvasya laksanarthatvat: the short serves as the *indicator*, the measuring-rod; one speaks of half of it as one speaks of half a cubit, without there being any such thing lying about. (309) offers the alternatives: qualification by the long and prolated (defining each by the others, the system closing on itself); or by the matra (an abstract unit doing the measuring); or jater va laksana — *indication of the universal*. And then: sarvatha sapta-parnavat — *in every way, like 'seven-leaf'*. The saptaparna is a tree whose name says seven leaves and whose leaves are not seven: the name indicates without describing, points without containing, and everyone finds the tree. That is the measure-word, and — the simile's reach is deliberate — that is every word: sarvatha, in every way. Language names as saptaparna names.
contested: saptaparna (the Alstonia tree, 'seven-leaved' and not seven-leaved) is the traditional example of a name whose components misdescribe its bearer; whether sarvatha extends the comparison to all words (our reading, with 2.36's precedent) or only to measure-terms is undecidable from the karika

mula_deva: गन्तव्यं दृश्यतां सूर्य इति कालस्य लक्षणे ।
mula_iast: gantavyaṃ dṛśyatāṃ sūrya iti kālasya lakṣaṇe |
translation: 'One must go — look at the sun!' — in indicating the time, this, 'let the time be known', is expressed together with its means.
commentary: The movement closes on a small scene, and it gathers everything. Someone says: gantavyam, drsyatam suryah — 'we must go; look at the sun!' What is meant? Kalasya laksane — in *indicating the time*: jnayatam kalah — 'let the time be known' — sopayam abhidhiyate: expressed *together with its means*. Nobody is being told to look at the sun for the sun's sake; the sun is the means, and the errand is the hour. And notice how much of the kanda is in this one utterance. The meaning is the purpose (2.113: vakyartha is prayojana) — knowing the time, which no word in the sentence names (2.305's anupatta). The sun is the indicator, meaning by pointing rather than containing (2.309's saptaparna). What is uttered is the means and what is meant is the end, delivered together, undivided, in a single flash to any hearer who is standing there (2.143). And the flash is practical through and through — itikartavyata, what is to be done (2.146: 'in the matter of what is to be done, no one transgresses it'). The Vakyakanda's whole doctrine, in a sentence someone might say at a doorway: language points at the sun and delivers the hour.

translation: In 'he pierces with a non-bow', a general is indicated by way of the particular — a general which is the substratum of the capacity, and which is somehow the conveyor.
commentary: A curious idiom opens the movement on contextual determination. 'Vidhyati adhanusa' — he pierces with a non-bow: the phrase denies the instrument and yet an instrument is understood; something does the piercing, and what is conveyed is instrumentality-in-general, samanyam asrayah sakteh — a general that is the *substratum of the capacity*: the bare role of instrument, waiting for a filler. Visesena nidarsyate: the general is indicated *by way of the particular* — one names a bow, negates it, and what survives is the role the bow would have played. This is the hovering-general of 2.44-45 generated by an unlikely route, and the verse's yah kascit pratipadakah — 'which is *somehow* the conveyor' — is the honest shrug the case deserves. What conveys the role? Not the word 'dhanus', which is negated; not the negation, which negates nothing else. The role arrives from the construction, and no word in it says so. Every subsequent verse in this movement is a variation on that discovery.

mula_deva: उपघातपरे वाक्ये न श्वादिभ्यो न रक्षति ॥ ३१२ ॥
mula_iast: upaghātapare vākye na śvādibhyo na rakṣati || 312 ||
translation: 'Guard the ghee from the crows' — even a child, so instructed, in a sentence whose purport is harm, does not guard it from dogs and the like.
commentary: The movement's most famous example, and one of the great examples in the world's philosophy of language. Kakebhyo raksyatam sarpih — 'guard the ghee from the crows'. The word says crows. The child — balo 'pi coditah, *even a child*, so instructed — guards it from dogs. Why? Upaghata-pare vakye: the sentence's *purport* is harm; crows were named because crows were the salient threat, and what was meant was: keep the ghee safe. And na svadibhyo na raksati — the litotes is exact: it is not the case that he fails to guard it from dogs. No inference is performed, no rule of pragmatics consulted; the child simply understands, and would be astonished to be told that 'crows' meant crows. The example demolishes literal-meaning semantics with a single household scene, and it is chosen for the child on purpose: this is not sophisticated interpretation but the baseline competence of anyone who understands anything (2.117's balanam tirascam ca — children and animals). The sentence's meaning is its purpose (2.113), and the purpose was never crows.

translation: The washing of the cups and likewise the scouring of the pots, though unstated in explicit form, is understood, from being an auxiliary of eating.
commentary: The companion example, from the other side. Say 'prepare the meal' and the praksalanam saravanam, the washing of the cups, and the marjanam sthalinam, the scouring of the pots, are anuktam api rupena — *unstated in explicit form*, no word for them anywhere — and pratiyate, understood: bhujy-angatvat, *from being an auxiliary of eating*. Where 2.312 showed a named thing not meant, this shows unnamed things meant. Together they close the field: what is said and what is meant come apart in both directions, and neither direction troubles a child. The mechanism named here is angatva — being an *auxiliary* — the Mimamsa's own relation (2.81-82's anga-structure): the meal's meaning carries its subsidiaries with it because a meal *is* a structure of subsidiaries, and understanding the end is having the means. This is 2.146's practical sovereignty: comprehension delivers the whole itikartavyata, the entire what-is-to-be-done, and the words are the occasion of that delivery rather than its inventory.

translation: Word-meanings are differentiated by the sentence, by the context, by the object, by propriety, by place and time — not from the form alone.
commentary: The movement's thesis, stated flatly and comprehensively. Sabdarthah pravibhajyante — word-meanings are *differentiated*, carved out (the vocabulary of 2.250-251: the plenitude divided by nimittas) — and the list of carvers is given: vakyat, by the *sentence* (the word's meaning fixed by its host); prakaranat, by the *context* (the standing situation of discourse); arthat, by the *object* (what is there to be talked about); aucityat, by *propriety* (what it would be fitting to mean); desa-kalatah, by *place and time*. And the denial that gives the verse its force: na rupad eva kevalat — *not from the form alone*. The bare word-form determines nothing. This is the direct contradictory of 2.265's rival criterion (the primary meaning is what the bare form delivers, rupa-matra-nibandhanah), and its placement after 2.312-313 is not accidental: the crows and the unwashed pots are the evidence, this is the thesis. The next two verses give the full inventory of what does the carving, and it is the most systematic list of contextual factors in the ancient world.

translation: (315) Connection, dissociation, association, opposition, the object, the context, an indicatory mark, the proximity of another word, (316) capacity, propriety, place, time, gender, accent and the rest — these are the causes of the recollection of the particular, where the word-meaning is undelimited.
commentary: The catalogue — fourteen factors, and it has no rival in antiquity. Samsarga, connection (what the word is joined with); viprayoga, dissociation (what it is explicitly kept from); sahacarya, association (the company it customarily keeps); virodhita, opposition (what it is contrasted against); arthah, the object; prakaranam, the context; lingam, an indicatory mark; anyasya sabdasya sannidhih, the proximity of another word; samarthyam, capacity (what could do the job); aucitii, propriety; desah, place; kalah, time; vyaktih, gender; svaradayah, accent and the rest. And the frame that makes the list a doctrine rather than an inventory: sabdarthasya anavacchede — *where the word-meaning is undelimited* — these are visesa-smrti-hetavah, the *causes of the recollection of the particular*. Every term is exact. The word-meaning is *undelimited* in itself (2.250's sarvarthya, the plenitude); these factors *delimit*. And they do so by causing *smrti*, recollection — not by adding content but by waking what the hearer already has (2.117's abhyasa, 2.152's ripened endowment). The list is the operational face of the kanda's entire semantics: one omni-significant word, fourteen kinds of occasion, and a trained mind in which the particular flashes.
contested: the fourteen are stable in the tradition though their glosses vary; vyakti we render 'gender' (the grammatical sense, given the company of svara) though it can mean 'individual' — both are contextual determinants and nothing turns on it

translation: Even on the difference-view, words having distinct meanings, being alike in form, come to manifestation as fixed in comprehensions, by the context and the rest.
commentary: The catalogue's results shown to be neutral between the schools — the kanda's habitual generosity at a movement's close. Bheda-pakse 'pi: *even on the difference-view* — even for the theorist who holds that 'gauh'-of-the-ox and 'gauh'-of-the-man are two distinct words that merely share a shape (2.257's bheda-marga-anudarsinah), rather than one word with a plenitude (2.250) — the same machinery is required.
