commentary: The movement's last technical case, and it admits a complication the earlier analysis suppressed. Bhuji-dvandva-ekasesabhyam yatra anyaih saha sisyate — where, by the eating-dvandva and the ekasesa, something is taught *along with others*: the two gathering-devices operating together (2.108's single-remainder inside a dvandva) — tatrapi laksanarthatvat dvidha vakyam samapyate: *there too*, because the purpose is *characterization* (2.380's principle), the sentence is completed *two-fold*. The sentence closes twice — once distributively, once collectively — and both closures are real. This is 2.387's dvidha samapty-ayoga (a two-fold completion is not possible) apparently contradicted, and the contradiction is only apparent: there, of *number*, which cannot complete twice; here, of *characterization*, which can. The verse is a fine demonstration of how carefully the movement has been discriminating: what completes twice and what cannot depends on what kind of thing is completing, and no general rule about sentences settles it.

translation: By some, a completion of the several sentences is accepted, each severally; by that, sentences joined with another form are comprehended.
commentary: The doctrine of 2.388 generalized into a position. Kaiscid isyate: some accept vakyantaranam pratyekam samaptih — a *completion of the several sentences, each severally*: where a compressed utterance carries several predications (the dvandva with its distributed action, the ekasesa with its several referents), it is not one sentence with a complex meaning but *several sentences*, each complete. And the consequence: tena rupantarena yuktanam vakyanam sangrahah — by that, sentences *joined with another form* are comprehended: the several sentences are grasped through the single form that gathers them. So the ekasesa's one uttered word (2.108-109) is, on this account, several sentences wearing one body — 2.110's doctrine (one form, many sentences) stated as a semantics rather than a ritual convenience. The position is available to Bhartrhari and he does not quite claim it; but it fits everything he has argued about offices and bodies (2.104), and it explains the two-fold completion of 2.388 without paradox: two completions because two sentences, sharing a form.

translation: The difference-sentences are not things expressible by the sentence, any of them; but when that has been uttered, one comprehends those differences and others so.
commentary: The movement's closing correction, and it pulls the doctrine of 2.389 back into the kanda's frame. Na vakyasya abhidheyani bheda-vakyani kanicit: the *difference-sentences* — the several predications the analysis distributes out of the compressed form — are *not things expressible by the sentence*, not any of them: the utterance does not *say* them; they are not its content. But: tasmin tu uccarite bhedan tatha anyan pratipadyate — when *that* has been uttered, one comprehends those differences, *and others*, so. The distributed predications are what the hearer *gets*, not what the sentence *says* — and 'and others' (anyan) concedes that he gets more besides, unpredictably, per hearer (2.299's overspill; 2.135's division per own-cognition). This is the kanda's settled position applied one last time: the utterance occasions a flash whose articulation the analyst can spread across several sentences, and none of those sentences is what was said. What was said was one thing. What arrived was one thing. The several sentences are the ledger.

mula_deva: तेषां तदानीं भिन्नस्य किं पदार्थस्य सत्तया ॥ ३९१ ॥
mula_iast: teṣāṃ tadānīṃ bhinnasya kiṃ padārthasya sattayā || 391 ||
translation: For those for whom the entire sentence-meaning is completed at each division — for them, at that moment, of what use is the existence of a separate word-meaning?
commentary: The objection turned back on the holist, and it is a sharp one. If, as 2.18 held, the krtsnah vakyarthah samapyate pratibhedam — the *entire* sentence-meaning completes at *each* division, each word — then tesam tadanim bhinnasya kim padarthasya sattaya: at that moment, what use is the *existence* of a separate word-meaning? The whole being present at every word, the word-meanings are not merely non-constitutive (which the kanda has argued at length) but *idle* — they do nothing, explain nothing, and might as well not be. The objection is a reductio of the holist's own most radical thesis, and it deserves its force: a doctrine on which the parts contribute nothing owes an account of why speech has parts at all. The verses following do not dodge it; 2.394-397 will answer with the sadharana-doctrine, and the answer will be that the word-meanings are real as *shares* in a common thing rather than as contributions to a sum.

mula_deva: अथ तैरेव जनितः सौऽर्थो भिन्नेषौ वर्तते ।
पूर्वस्यार्थस्य तेन स्याद्विरोधः सह वा स्थितिः ॥ ३९२ ॥
mula_iast: atha taireva janitaḥ sau'rtho bhinneṣau vartate |
pūrvasyārthasya tena syādvirodhaḥ saha vā sthitiḥ || 392 ||
translation: (392) Now if that meaning, generated by those very ones, operates upon the differentiated — then, of the prior meaning, there would be either contradiction with it, or co-standing. (393) On co-standing, there would be contradictoriness of the qualified and the unqualified; but a deviating connection follows, on the abandonment of the meaning.
commentary: The objection of 2.391 pressed into a dilemma. Suppose the sentence-meaning, generated by the word-meanings, then *operates upon the differentiated* — comes back to bear on the very items that produced it. Then the prior meaning (the word-meaning as it stood before) either contradicts the new one or co-stands with it. (393) takes both horns. On co-standing: viruddhata syad visista-avisistayoh — there is *contradictoriness of the qualified and the unqualified*: the word-meaning as bare (before the sentence) and as qualified (after) cannot both stand, being incompatible determinations of one item. On abandonment: vyabhicari tu sambandhah tyage 'rthasya prasajyate — a *deviating connection* follows: if the bare meaning is abandoned, then the word's connection with its meaning is not invariable, and the eternal bond of 1.23 and 2.332 fails. Contradiction or deviation: the dilemma is real, and it is the same one 2.15 posed against the deposit-theorist (the general obscured in the particular: where does it go?), now aimed at the holist. Bhartrhari has arranged for his own position to face the argument he used against everyone else.

translation: A single common expressible, standing in each word, is the meaning-self in the group and in the group's members — indicating the proximity.
commentary: The answer, and it introduces the model that will carry the movement: sadharana, the *common*. Ekah sadharano vacyah — a *single common* expressible — pratisabdam avasthitah: standing *in each word*: not divided among them, not summed from them, but present in each as a common thing is present to each of its sharers. Sanghe sanghisu ca arthatma: it is the meaning-self *in the group and in the group's members* — both, without division. And its office: sannidhana-nidarsakah — *indicating the proximity*: it is what makes the words' co-presence into a togetherness rather than a heap. The model dissolves 2.392-393's dilemma at a stroke. There is no prior bare meaning that must be either contradicted or abandoned, because the word never had a meaning of its own to lose — it had a *share* in a common one, and shares are not diminished by being shared. What the word-meaning *is*, on this account, is the sentence-meaning as standing in this word: 2.391's 'idle' padartha turns out to be the vakyartha itself, seen from where one word stands.

mula_deva: यथा साधारणे स्वत्वं त्यागस्य च फलं धने ।
mula_iast: yathā sādhāraṇe svatvaṃ tyāgasya ca phalaṃ dhane |
translation: (395) As, in common property, ownership, and the fruit of relinquishing, and the satisfaction, are undiminished in the wealth — so the connection is with the meaning, not with its possessor. (396) In the meaningfulness of phonemes, by that very meaning in the possessor, oneness with the aggregate stands by way of difference.
commentary: The sadharana-model given its illustration, and it is drawn from law. Consider sadharana dhana — *common property*, held jointly by brothers. Each has svatva, *ownership* — full ownership, not a fraction of it; each has tyagasya phalam, the *fruit of relinquishing* (the merit of giving it away — and each gets the whole merit, not a share); each has pritih, *satisfaction* — avikala, *undiminished*. The wealth is one, the ownings are several, and no owning is a portion. This is the exact structure the holist needs: the sentence-meaning is one, the words' meanings are several, and each is the whole thing owned from a position, not a slice. And the doctrinal formulation: sambandho 'rthe na tadvata — the connection is *with the meaning*, not with its *possessor*: what a word is bonded to is the meaning itself, not to the word-as-meaning-possessor. (396) applies it downward: phonemes' meaningfulness, likewise, is oneness with the aggregate standing bhedena, by way of difference — each phoneme owning the word's meaning from where it stands. The joint-property analogy is one of the most illuminating in the work: it explains how many can each have all of one thing, which is precisely what the flash-doctrine requires and what every deposit-semantics finds impossible.

translation: By a single lamp alone, all see the common wealth; likewise, by a single case-ending, number is expressed.
commentary: The lamp returns to seal the sadharana-model, and the pairing of images is exact. Ekena eva pradipena sarve sadharanam dhanam pasyanti: by *one* lamp, *all* see the *common* wealth — one illumination, many seers, one object, and nobody's seeing is a fraction of anybody's. Tadvat ekena supa samkhya abhidhiyate: *likewise*, by a *single case-ending*, number is expressed. The application is precise: the ending does not distribute a number-contribution among the words it governs; it lights the whole and each sees it entire (2.375's collective seeing, 2.374's lamp as the tantra-model, now doing semantic work). Between the joint-property (2.395) and the lamp, the movement has its two figures for how one thing can be wholly many's: ownership without division, and illumination without division. Both were needed. Property answers 'how can each have all?'; the lamp answers 'how does one act reach all at once?' — and together they explain how a single utterance delivers one undivided meaning to every word in it and every hearer of it.

mula_deva: नार्थवत्ता पदे वर्णे वाक्ये चैवं विशिष्यते ।
mula_iast: nārthavattā pade varṇe vākye caivaṃ viśiṣyate |
translation: Meaningfulness is not thus distinguished in the word, in the phoneme, and in the sentence; but from practice, a different commencement is seen, as though contradictory.
commentary: The consequence of the sadharana-model for the whole architecture of grains. Na arthavatta pade varne vakye ca evam visisyate: meaningfulness is *not distinguished* in the word, in the phoneme, and in the sentence — not, that is, in the way the analysis supposes, with each grain owning its own quantum. On the common-property model there is one meaning and three positions from which it is held, and 'meaningfulness' names the same thing at every grain. Then the concession: abhyasat prakramo 'nyah tu viruddha iva drsyate — but *from practice*, a *different commencement* is seen, *as though* contradictory: usage proceeds as if the grains had distinct meanings, and the appearance of conflict between the grains (the word's meaning versus the sentence's) is an artifact of the practice, not of the semantics. Iva, as always, holds the line. The kanda has now answered 2.391's challenge fully: word-meanings exist, are not idle, and are not contributions — they are the one meaning, owned from a place.

translation: Without application, a word is not the illuminator of its own meaning; they call the connection with the expressing of meaning a door.
commentary: A new movement, on what activates a word, and its thesis is stated at once. Viniyogad rte sabdo na svarthasya prakasakah: *without application* — viniyoga, the ritual and grammatical term for deploying an element to an office (2.258's vinyoga individuating mantras) — a word is *not the illuminator of its own meaning*. An unapplied word illuminates nothing; it sits inert, a form without office. And the figure: artha-abhidhana-sambandham uktidvaram pracaksate — the connection with the expressing of meaning they call a *door*: the bond between word and meaning is a doorway, standing open, through which nothing passes until someone walks. The image is exactly calibrated to the kanda's position. The eternal connection (1.23, 2.332) is real and is not sufficient: it is a standing possibility, a door, and utterance with intent is the walking through. This is why the plenitude (2.250) needs nimittas, why the lamp needs bringing (2.397), why the word means everything and says one thing.

translation: As the eye, when directed, becomes fit for seeing — so a word, when intended, becomes the expressor of a meaning.
commentary: The doctrine of 2.399 given its simile, and the simile is chosen with great care. Yatha pranihitam caksuh darsanaya upakalpate: as the eye, when *directed* — pranihita, aimed, laid upon its object — becomes *fit* for seeing. The eye is always an eye; open, it is capable of every visible thing; and it sees *this* because it was turned this way. Tatha abhisamhitah sabdo bhavaty arthasya vacakah: so a word, when *intended* — abhisamhita, aimed by the speaker's intent — becomes the expressor of *a* meaning. Every element of the kanda's semantics is in the analogy. The word's omni-significance is the eye's openness to all colours (2.250's sarvarthya); the intention is the aiming; the meaning that results is what was in the line of sight — carved from the plenitude by a directing, not selected from a list. And the simile explains, incidentally, why the word's capacity is not diminished by use: an eye that has looked at a pot is no less able to look at a cloth. The vivaksa of 1.155 has become, here, the whole engine of signification.

translation: The connection of instrument and object is seen as mediated by an action; therefore the fixing of expression is of the expression and the expressible.
commentary: The structural point behind 2.400's simile. Kriya-vyavetah sambandho drstah karana-karmanoh — the connection of *instrument* and *object* is seen as *mediated by an action*: an axe and a tree are not related until there is a felling; the karaka-relations exist only within an action (2.183: prior to connection with the means, the action does not arise). By parity: abhidha niyamah tasmat abhidhana-abhidheyayoh — the *fixing* of expression is therefore of the *expression and the expressible* — i.e. the word-meaning relation, like the instrument-object relation, is constituted within an act: the act of abhidha, expressing. There is no standing relation between word and meaning waiting to be used; there is a door (2.399) that becomes a relation when someone walks through it. This is the metaphysics behind the eye-simile: relations of this kind are not found but *made*, in and by the act that has them as its terms — which is the Sambandha-samuddesa's thesis (Kanda III) in embryo, and which is why the third kanda must exist.

translation: Among many single expressions, all effecting one meaning — the word stands upon that which the speaker intends.
commentary: The intention-doctrine's decisive case. Bahusu eka-abhidhanesu sarvesu ekartha-karisu: among *many* single expressions, *all* effecting *one* meaning — the synonyms, several words any of which would do (2.330's paryayah): yat prayokta abhisandhatte sabdah tatra avatisthate — the word stands upon *that which the speaker intends*. Where several words could serve and one is chosen, nothing but the intention distinguishes them — the meaning being identical, the choice is the speaker's and no fact about the language settles it. This is the cleanest possible demonstration that abhisandhi, intention, is doing real work: it is the only variable left when the semantic contribution is held constant. And it explains 2.399's door-figure: the eternal connections stand open for all the synonyms at once, and what determines which door is walked is not the doors.

translation: (403) In the practice of the Vedic words, some say they are meaningless and operate on their own form alone, in the conveying to others. (404) Because of the difference in the act of expression, which conveys the meaning, those who see them as one consider them (different) by difference of application.
commentary: The doctrine applied to the recitation of scripture. (403): amnaya-sabdanam abhyase — in the *practice*, the drill, of the Vedic words (the rote recitation by which the Veda is transmitted) — kecid ahuh anarthakan: some say they are *meaningless* — svarupa-matra-vrttin, operating on their *own form alone*: the reciter is not saying anything, he is producing forms; meaning does not enter until the words are deployed in a rite. And (404): the ekatva-darsinah — those who see them as *one* (one word, whether recited or applied) — account for the apparent difference abhidhana-kriya-bhedat and niyoga-bhedat: by the difference of the *expressing act* and of *application*. The two accounts of what happens when a boy chants a hymn he does not understand: either the words are meaningless there (and become meaningful when applied), or they are one and the same words differing only in their office. Both are versions of 2.399: unapplied, a word illuminates nothing — whether one says it is then a different word or a meaningless one is bookkeeping (2.179's licensed double description).

translation: Those who deal in manifoldness declare their utter manifoldness — as of dice and the like, from their connection with a single class.
commentary: The contrary school, and its analogy is worth examining. Nanatva-vyavaharinah — those who *deal in manifoldness*, whose method is division (2.57's pada-darsins) — tesam atyanta-nanatvam prahuh: declare their *utter manifoldness*: the recited word and the applied word are simply two words, not one word twice. And the simile: aksadinam iva eka-jati-samanvayat — as of *dice* and the like, from their connection with a *single class*: dice are many, and their oneness is only their sharing a kind — there is no one die of which the several are appearances. So words: many tokens, unified by nothing but a common type, no single word standing behind them. The analogy is well chosen for the position and it is exactly what Bhartrhari denies (2.250's one word carved by nimittas). Note that the two schools have now been running in parallel since 2.57, and neither can refute the other by the phenomena — which was 1.74's finding and has never been retracted. What separates them is which primitive they will pay for.

mula_deva: विषये यतशक्तित्वात् स तु तत्र व्यवस्थितः ॥ ४०६ ॥
mula_iast: viṣaye yataśaktitvāt sa tu tatra vyavasthitaḥ || 406 ||
translation: Intention arising from employment does not exist in the mantra-words; because their capacity is fixed with respect to their domain, that stands settled there.
commentary: A limit-case for the intention-doctrine, and Bhartrhari concedes it. Prayogad abhisandhanam mat-padesu na vidyate: intention arising from employment does *not exist* in the mantra-words — the reciter does not aim them; he utters what is given, and often does not know what it means (2.403's meaningless drill). So 2.400's directed eye is absent. What then makes them mean? Visaye yata-saktitvat sa tu tatra vyavasthitah: because their *capacity is fixed* with respect to their *domain*, it stands settled there — the mantra's power is lodged in it by its office in the rite, independently of any reciter's intention (1.138's mantra-efficacy: form-bound, working whether or not understood). The concession is significant: intention is the engine of ordinary signification (2.400-402) and is *not* the whole story — scripture works without it, its capacities fixed by domain rather than aimed by minds. Which is exactly what a tradition holding an authorless Veda must say (1.144), and Bhartrhari says it rather than forcing the doctrine to cover everything.

translation: The knowledge of manifoldness alone comes by the object, the context and the rest; there is never, in any way, an operation of words meant for other meanings upon a different meaning.
commentary: The movement's closing thesis, and it is a strict one. Nanatvasya eva samjnanam artha-prakaranadibhih: the *knowledge of manifoldness alone* comes by the object, the context, and the rest — the fourteen factors (2.315-316) tell you *which* meaning is in play, and that is all they do. And the denial that follows: na jatv arthantare vrttih anyarthanam kathamcana — there is *never*, in any way, an operation of words *meant for other meanings* upon a *different* meaning. A word aimed at one meaning does not stray into another; the context does not bend a word's sense, it *identifies* which of the word's senses is being used. The distinction is fine and it is the movement's whole point: context is not a force acting on meanings but a light showing which door was walked through (2.399). This closes the door on any account of figurative language as sense-stretching (2.266's forcing) and it protects the eternal bond (2.332): the word never means what it was not, because everything it could mean, it always could.

section: The Word as Sentence; Existence and Predication
translation: (408) But that sentence which has the form of a word, grounded in existence — let this reflection be there, granted, and not in the word that is a member of a sentence. (409) Just as, by meaningless phonemes, a qualified meaning is expressed — so, by meaningless words, a qualified meaning is expressed. (410) But the knowledge that arises in the interval, with respect to the word-meanings, is a means to the comprehension, on account of the non-ascertainment of the commencement.
commentary: Three verses that state the kanda's architecture one last time, now in terms of existence. (408): a pada-rupam vakyam — a sentence *having the form of a word* (the one-word sentence, 2.270, 2.326) — is astitva-upanibandhanam, *grounded in existence*: its whole predication is the assertion that something is. Grant the reflection *there*, the verse says — kamam vimarsah tatra — and *not* in the word that is a *member* of a sentence: the isolated word that constitutes a sentence has a predication; the word inside a sentence has none. (409) then draws the parity that has been the kanda's constant: as anarthakaih varnaih — by *meaningless phonemes* — a qualified meaning is expressed, so padaih anarthakaih — by *meaningless words*: the words within a sentence are as semantically null as the phonemes within a word, and the sentence means all the same. (410) rescues the intermediate cognitions from that nullity: the knowledge that arises antarale, *in the interval*, concerning the word-meanings, is pratipatteh upayah — a *means* to the comprehension: not a contribution but a ladder (2.38's discardable means; 1.85's productive error), and it is needed prakrama-anavadharanat, because the commencement is not ascertained — because one does not know, mid-utterance, where the whole will land.

translation: As each successive meaning-self is accompanied by the prior meanings, so, in other meaning-things, the commingling alone is what is commenced.
commentary: The phenomenology of the interval (2.410) described. Purvaih arthaih anugatah — accompanied by the *prior* meanings — yatha arthatma parah parah: each successive meaning-self, one after another, comes attended by what preceded it: no word arrives bare; each is met by a mind already shaped by the words before it. And the conclusion: samsarga eva prakrantah tatha anyesu artha-vastusu — the *commingling alone* is what is *commenced*, in the other meaning-things as well. What is under way through the interval is not a series of meanings but a *commingling* — a single process of fusion whose stages are not stages of a sum. This is why 2.410 could call the interval-cognitions a means without calling them constituents: what they are doing is not accumulating but converging, and 2.143's flash is the convergence, not its total.

translation: But when, by some, the means is admitted along with the to-be-accomplished meaning — the re-hearing of the means is only for the sake of fixing the substratum.
commentary: A technical case: why sentences state what their verbs already imply. When a verb is uttered, its means are implied (2.183: the action does not arise apart from means-connection; 2.47: the roles come pre-fixed). So why does the sentence bother to name them? Sadhananam punah srutih — the *re-hearing* of the means: adhara-niyama-artha eva — *only for the sake of fixing the substratum*: the verb implies *some* agent, and the noun tells you *which*. So the nouns are not adding the roles; the roles were there in the verb (2.48's architecture). They are specifying the fillers. This is 2.66's structure exactly — vrihi restricting the substance-in-general that yajeta obtained — and it shows the same layering: what the sentence *says* and what it *fixes* are different offices, and most of a sentence's words are doing the second.

translation: In the absence of the fixing of the substratum, its implication does not exist; its possibility is from capacity, but the explicit hearing is for the exclusion of others.
commentary: The complement to 2.412, and it is a precision about implication. Adhara-niyama-abhavat tad-aksepah na vidyate: in the absence of a *fixing* of the substratum, the *implication* of it does not exist — an unfixed role is not implied at all; there is no floating 'some agent' hovering until named. Sambhavah tasya samarthyat: its *possibility* is from *capacity* — the verb's nature makes an agent possible (an action of that sort could have one). Srutih tu anya-nivrttaye — but the *explicit hearing* is for the *exclusion of others*: naming the agent does not create him, it excludes the alternatives. So the sentence's words are, one and all, exclusion-devices: they narrow a plenitude of possibility that the verb's capacity opened. This is 2.250-251's semantics at the level of syntax — the omni-significant carved by nimittas — and it explains why a sentence's meaning is not the sum of its words: subtraction does not sum.

translation: An action, distinct from other actions, having a fixed substratum and means, once commenced — the differences are causes of awakening for the comprehenders.
commentary: The result of 2.412-413's exclusion-work, and it recasts the whole sentence as a waking-device. Kriya kriyantarad bhinna — an action *distinct from other actions*, individuated by the exclusions the words performed — niyata-adhara-sadhana, having its substratum and means now *fixed* — prakranta: commenced. And the office of everything that did the fixing: bhedah sambodha-hetavah pratipattrnam — the *differences* are *causes of awakening* for the comprehenders. Sambodha: awakening (2.331's pratibodha-abhyupayah, words as means of awakening). The words' distinctions do not build the meaning; they wake it — narrowing until the hearer's ripened capacity (2.152's pratibha) catches, and the flash occurs. The verse gathers the movement: exclusion, fixing, and waking are one process, and the meaning was never among the words that performed it.

mula_deva: प्रकाशते तदन्येषां वाक्यं वाक्यार्थ एव च ॥ ४१५ ॥
mula_iast: prakāśate tadanyeṣāṃ vākyaṃ vākyārtha eva ca || 415 ||
translation: But to others, the sentence and the sentence-meaning both shine forth as undivided, without sequence of words, from words that possess sequence.
commentary: The doctrine stated in its final form, and the symmetry is the point. Sabdebhyah kramavadbhyah — from words *possessing sequence*: the utterance is serial, its words come in order, nothing about the vehicle is undivided — prakasate avibhagam apada-kramam: there shines forth what is *undivided*, *without word-sequence*. And what shines: tad anyesam vakyam vakyartha eva ca — to others, *both* the sentence *and* the sentence-meaning. Both terms of the relation are undivided; only the vehicle has parts. This is 1.48's structure (the sequenceless born as if in sequence) at the sentence's scale, and it completes the parallel the kanda has been drawing since 2.30: as the word-sphota is manifested by sequential sounds, the sentence-sphota is manifested by sequential words, and in neither case does the manifestor's seriality touch the manifested. The one word 'prakasate' carries the whole doctrine: not built, not summed, not connected — *shone forth*.

mula_deva: स्वरूपं विद्यते यस्य तस्यात्मा न निरूप्यते ।
नास्ति यस्य स्वरूपं तु तस्यैवात्मा निरूप्यते ॥ ४१६ ॥
mula_iast: svarūpaṃ vidyate yasya tasyātmā na nirūpyate |
nāsti yasya svarūpaṃ tu tasyaivātmā nirūpyate || 416 ||
translation: That which has an own-form — of it, the self is not described; but of that which has no own-form, of that alone is the self described.
commentary: A paradox stated with deliberate sharpness, and it concerns the limits of description. Svarupam vidyate yasya tasya atma na nirupyate: that which *has* an own-form — a determinate nature of its own — of it the *self* is *not described*: what is fully itself cannot be caught in a description, since description would have to stand outside it and there is no outside (2.144's pratibha, not describable even by its possessor; 1.35's untellable expertise). Nasti yasya svarupam tu tasya eva atma nirupyate: but of what *has no* own-form — of *that alone* is the self described. What can be described is exactly what has no nature of its own — the constructed, the relational, the item that is nothing but its place in a scheme (the sambandha of 2.46, asattva-bhuta, beyond thinghood, and knowable only by inference from its effect). So description tracks unreality. The verse is the epistemological ground of 2.233's claim that the sciences describe only avidya: they describe because their objects are constructions, and what is not a construction is not available for describing. It is also the deepest reason the vakyartha must be a flash: it has an own-form, and so it can only be waked, never told.
contested: the verse is aphoristic and admits an idealist reading (only the unreal is describable, hence description as such is of the unreal) and a narrower semantic one (self-standing items resist definition; relational ones are definable); we take the first as the kanda's own, with 2.233 and 2.421-422 as warrant

translation: Others know the determination of the meaning's form to be wordless; for the memory that arises from words has the form of an appearance of the meaning.
commentary: The consequence of 2.416 for semantics. Asabdam apare arthasya rupa-nirdharanam viduh: others know the *determination of the meaning's form* to be *wordless* — what fixes a meaning is not itself verbal (2.16's objection, once feared, now embraced: the sentence-meaning is asabda). And the reason: artha-avabhasa-rupa hi sabdebhyo jayate smrtih — the *memory* that arises from words has the form of an *appearance of the meaning*: what words produce in a hearer is not a word but a showing — an avabhasa, a shining-forth of the thing (2.415's prakasate). So the terminus of a verbal process is non-verbal: words wake an appearing, and the appearing is what meaning is. This does not sever the word-meaning bond (2.332 secured it) — it locates the bond's *effect*: the eternal connection delivers, at its far end, something that is not language. Which is why 1.123's thesis (all cognition is word-interwoven) and this verse are compatible: the appearing is word-woven in its structure and wordless in its presentation.

translation: It is in one way that the burnt man, from connection with fire, experiences the burning; in quite another way that the meaning of burning and the rest is understood by the word 'burning'.
commentary: The gap between the meant and the had, put with unusual directness. Anyatha eva agni-sambandhad daham dagdho 'bhimanyate: it is *in one way entirely* that the *burnt man*, from connection with fire, experiences the burning — the searing itself, undergone. Anyatha daha-sabdena dahady-arthah pratiyate: in *quite another* way is the meaning of burning understood by the *word* 'burning'. The word does not burn. What it wakes is an appearing (2.417), and the appearing of burning is not a burning — no hearer of the word is scorched. The verse marks the limit of the whole semantic enterprise with a directness the kanda has earned: language reaches everything and touches nothing; it can wake an appearance of any experience and cannot supply one. This is also why 2.416 said what it said: the burning has an own-form and is therefore indescribable in the strict sense — what the word conveys is not it, but an appearance answering to it.

translation: (419) As the effect of the sense-faculties — whose realities are separately lodged, and which severally follow their own objects — is not construed apart from the body; (420) so, of all the words, which severally settle on their own meanings, when they are divided out from sentences, there is no meaningfulness.
commentary: One of the finest similes in the work, and it answers 2.391's challenge definitively. (419): the indriyas — the senses — have prthag-nivista-tattvanam, their *realities separately lodged* (eye in the socket, ear in the head: really distinct organs); and they are prthag-artha-anupatinah, *severally following their own objects* (eye to colour, ear to sound, no overlap). So they are as separate as things can be. And yet: yatha karyam rte dehan na kalpate — their *effect* is not construed *apart from the body*: an eye out of a head sees nothing; the organ's separateness is real and its functioning is not its own. (420): tatha padanam sarvesam prthag-artha-nivesinam — so of *all* words, which *severally settle* on their own meanings — vakyebhyah pravibhaktanam arthavatta na vidyate: *divided out* from sentences, there is *no meaningfulness*. The simile grants the atomist everything he asked for — words really are distinct, really do have their own meanings, as eyes really are distinct organs with their own objects — and takes back the only thing that mattered: none of it functions in isolation. The word in the sentence is the eye in the head.

mula_deva: दर्शनस्यापि यत्सत्यं न तथा दर्शनां स्थित्म् ।
mula_iast: darśanasyāpi yatsatyaṃ na tathā darśanāṃ sthitm |
translation: (421) The form of commingling is grasped among meaning-things that are commingled; here the reality is not told, because of the seeing of what is not a word-meaning. (422) Even of the seeing, what is true is not established as the seeing is; that formless thing is described by way of the form of the commingling of things.
commentary: Two verses at the edge of what the kanda can say. (421): samsarga-rupam samsrstesu artha-vastusu grhyate — the *form of commingling* is grasped among commingled meaning-things: what one gets is the fusion, not the items. And: na atra upakhyayate tattvam apadarthasya darsanat — *here the reality is not told*, because of the seeing of *what is not a word-meaning*: what is seen is no padartha at all, so no word-meaning-talk reaches it (2.416: what has own-form is not described). (422) goes further and turns on the seeing itself: darsanasya api yat satyam na tatha darsanam sthitam — even of the *seeing*, what is *true* is not established as the seeing *is*: the seeing's own truth is not the seeing as it presents. Vastu-samsarga-rupena tad arupam nirupyate: that *formless* thing is described by way of the *form of the commingling of things*. So the formless is described in the only available idiom — the idiom of commingled things — and the description is therefore not of it. This is the kanda's most rigorous moment of self-limitation: it says that its own central object cannot be said, and says why, and continues.
contested: 422 is among the most difficult verses in the work and our construal is one of several; the transcription's sthitm for sthitam is a clear fault. What is not in doubt is the verse's direction: the formless described through the form of fusion, and the description acknowledged as inadequate

translation: Since a meaning is expressed either as accompanied by existence, or as standing in a withdrawn self — therefore a sentence is employed.
commentary: The definition of the sentence, reached from the side of existence. A meaning is expressed in one of two ways: astitvena anusaktah — *accompanied by existence*: 'the pot is', the predication affirming; or nivrttya atmani sthitah — *standing in a withdrawn self*: 'the pot is not', the predication denying, the meaning present but with existence withheld (2.240-245's negation-dossier, now given its positive statement). Atah vakyam prayujyate: *therefore a sentence is employed*. The inference is the definition: a sentence is what does *this* — what places a meaning in relation to existence, either way. This is why a word cannot be a sentence unless its existence-verb is discharged (2.270's carita-asti-kriya, 2.408's astitva-upanibandhanam), and it is the deepest answer the kanda gives to its title-question. A vakya is not a string of a certain length or structure. It is an assertion of being or non-being.

mula_deva: सत्यो वा विपरीतो वा व्यवहारे न सोऽस्त्यतः ॥ ४२४ ॥
mula_iast: satyo vā viparīto vā vyavahāre na so'styataḥ || 424 ||
translation: Without the accompaniment of an action, a word-meaning is not understood — whether real or contrary; therefore it does not exist in practice.
commentary: The corollary, and it is severe. Kriya-anusangena vina na padarthah pratiyate: without the *accompaniment of an action*, a word-meaning is *not understood* — satyo va viparito va, whether *real or contrary*: neither a true one nor a false one; not at all. A meaning unattached to any action, any predication, any being-or-not-being, does not present itself to anyone. Atah na so 'sti vyavahare: *therefore it does not exist in practice*. Note the qualification: vyavahare — *in practice*. The bare word-meaning is not declared unreal simpliciter; it is declared absent from practice, which by 2.297 (sabdah loka-nibandhanah: words are grounded in the world) is where words live. So the padartha is an item with no place in the only domain language occupies — the exact status the kanda has assigned it since 2.10: real in the ledger, absent from the transaction.

mula_deva: सदित्येव तु यद्वाक्यं तदभूदस्ति नेति वा ।
mula_iast: sadityeva tu yadvākyaṃ tadabhūdasti neti vā |
translation: But that sentence which is just 'sat' — 'it was', 'it is', or 'not' — is not conceived apart from the connection with the expressing of an action.
commentary: The limit-case tested: the barest possible sentence. Sad iti eva yad vakyam — a sentence that is just *'sat'*, 'being' — tad abhut asti na iti va: 'it was', 'it is', or 'not'. Even here, at the minimum, kriya-abhidhana-sambandham antarena na manyate — it is not conceived *apart from* the connection with the *expressing of an action*: the existence-verb is an action-word, and 'sat' predicates only because it carries one. There is no assertion so thin that it escapes the verb. The verse forecloses an obvious escape from 2.423-424 — the thought that pure existence-claims might be predication without action — and in doing so it makes the action-centred account of the sentence complete: every sentence has a verb, even the ones that are only a verb, and the verb is what makes the sentence a sentence rather than a naming.

translation: (426) In the meaning expressible by a finite verb, grounded in the means, expectancy does not withdraw without the expressing of existence. (427) But from principality, the action is first divided out of the meaning; the auxiliaries are employed for the sake of the to-be-accomplished; its fruit is the occasioner.
commentary: The architecture of the sentence, stated completely. (426): in what a finite verb expresses — sadhana-upanibandhane, *grounded in the means* — na akanksa vinivartate vina sattva-abhidhanena: *expectancy does not withdraw* without the *expressing of existence*: the sentence does not close (2.4's nirakanksata, the mark of the vakya) until being has been predicated. So existence-assertion is the closure-condition. (427): pradhanyat tu kriya purvam arthasya pravibhajyate — from *principality*, the *action* is *first* divided out of the meaning: when the analyst carves, the verb comes off first, being the principal (2.4's karma-pradhanam). Sadhya-prayuktani angani: the *auxiliaries* are employed for the sake of the *to-be-accomplished*. Phalam tasyah prayojakam: its *fruit* is the *occasioner*. The chain is complete and it runs backwards from the end: the fruit occasions the action, the action requires the means, the means are named by the nouns — and the sentence closes when existence is predicated of the whole. Purpose at the top (2.113), existence at the bottom, and everything between is auxiliary.

translation: It is the speaker alone who intends the character of to-be-accomplished and means; or he desires the constructing of the meaning's connection.
commentary: The architecture of 2.427 handed to the speaker. Prayokta eva abhisandhatte sadhya-sadhana-rupatam: it is the *speaker alone* who *intends* the character of to-be-accomplished and means — which element shall be the action and which the instrument is not read off the world; it is conferred by the intending (2.131's theorem: the karaka-grid is constructed by words alone). Artthasya va abhisambandha-kalpanam prasamihate: or he *desires the constructing* of the meaning's connection. The same event can be carved as a cooking-with-fire or a fire's-burning-under-a-pot; the world does not choose, the speaker does. This is 2.400's directed eye applied to syntax rather than to reference: intention aims not only *at* a meaning but *into* an articulation — deciding what shall count as the action. And the next verse gives the demonstration.

translation: In 'I do the act of cooking', the cooking-action is expressed as the object; but the cooking, in the form of the instrument, is understood as the to-be-accomplished.
commentary: The demonstration, and it is elegant. Take paci-kriyam karomi — 'I do the cooking'. Here the *cooking* — which is an action — is karmatvena abhidhiyate, expressed *as the object*: it has become the patient of 'do'. And yet paktih karana-rupam tu sadhyatvena pratiyate — the cooking, in the *form of the instrument*, is understood as the *to-be-accomplished*: it is still what is being brought about. One and the same cooking is simultaneously object (of 'do'), instrument (of the doing), and the sadhya (what is accomplished) — three karaka-roles at once, assigned by how the sentence was built. Nothing in the cooking determines which; the speaker's construction does (2.428). This single example refutes any view on which the karakas are read off events: an event's roles are as many as the sentences one can make about it, and the sentence-maker assigns them.

translation: That which is intended by speakers, with whatever assistance — since the meaning is all-capable, that stands settled just so.
commentary: The movement closes by folding syntax into the omni-significance doctrine. Yo 'sau yena upakarena prayoktrnam vivaksitah — whatever is *intended* by speakers, with whatever *assistance* (in whatever role, contributing however) — arthasya sarva-saktitvat sa tatha eva vyavasthitah: *since the meaning is all-capable*, that stands settled *just so*. Sarva-saktitva — the same all-capacity 2.253 predicated of the word (sarva-sakti) and 2.250 of its meaning (sarvarthya) — is here predicated of the *meaning* to explain the karaka-freedom of 2.429: the cooking can be object, instrument, or accomplished because the meaning can be anything, and the speaker's vivaksa selects. The doctrine has now been stated at every level of the language: the word means everything and says one thing; the meaning plays every role and takes one; and in both cases the carving is intention's, working on a plenitude. Kanda III will spend twelve hundred verses examining the categories this carving produces — and 2.430 is its charter.

section: Connection, Assistance, and the Word's Sovereignty
translation: (431) In derivative formations, a connection is sometimes expressed from afar; what is unattached is sometimes understood as attached. (432) Of the commingled there is discreteness, and commingling of the discrete; of the many-natured, oneness — and manifoldness in the reverse case. (433) Whether from the meaning's being all-natured, or from its being self-less, it stands settled; because capacity is utterly fixed, the word alone is the ground.
commentary: Three verses that state the movement's thesis by first cataloguing the inversions language performs. (431): in the vrttis, connection is expressed arat, *from afar* — relations asserted between things not adjacent; and aslisto yo 'nupaslistah — what is *unattached* is understood as *attached*. (432) makes the inversions systematic and total: samsrstanam vivekitvam — of the *commingled*, discreteness (language separates what is fused); samsargas ca vivekinam — and commingling of the *discrete* (it fuses what is separate); nanatmakanam ekatvam — of the *many-natured*, oneness; nanatvam ca viparyaye — and manifoldness in the reverse. Every possible inversion, and language performs them all. Then (433) the conclusion, with its startling either/or: sarvatmakatvad arthasya nairatmyad va vyavasthitam — whether because the meaning is *all-natured* (able to be anything, 2.430's sarva-saktitva) *or* because it is *self-less* (having no nature of its own to resist) — it stands settled thus. Bhartrhari does not choose between plenitude and emptiness; either grounds the same result. And the result: atyanta-yata-saktitvat sabda eva nibandhanam — because capacity is *utterly fixed*, the *word alone* is the ground. The word is sovereign because the meaning offers no resistance, whether from having every nature or none.
contested: 433's sarvatmakatvad / nairatmyad va is the crux: the first is the kanda's own sarvarthya-doctrine (2.250), the second a nairatmya-formulation with unmistakable Buddhist resonance. That Bhartrhari treats them as interchangeable grounds for one conclusion is remarkable and, we think, deliberate

mula_deva: न स्वशक्तिः पदार्थानां संस्प्रष्टुं तेन शक्यते ॥ ४३४ ॥
mula_iast: na svaśaktiḥ padārthānāṃ saṃspraṣṭuṃ tena śakyate || 434 ||
translation: The word is an indicator of the thing; it is not the expressor of the assistance. The own-capacity of word-meanings cannot be touched by it.
commentary: A precise limitation on what the word does. Vastu-upalaksanam sabdah: the word is an *indicator of the thing* — upalaksana, a mark that points out without describing (2.309's saptaparna: naming without characterizing). Na upakarasya vacakah: it is *not the expressor of the assistance* — of the contribution one meaning makes to another, the relational work that goes on among the items. And: na sva-saktih padarthanam samsprastum tena sakyate — the *own-capacity* of word-meanings cannot be *touched* by it. The word points at things; it does not reach into their powers, does not manipulate what they can do, does not express the assistance they render one another in a construction. This is why the karaka-relations must be conferred by the speaker (2.428) rather than expressed by the words: the words are indicators, and indicators indicate — they do not perform the joinery. The verse limits the word's sovereignty of 2.433 in exactly the right place: sovereign over what is meant, powerless over what the meant things are.

translation: Conjunction, a property of the relata, is expressed by its own word; connection and inherence are understood by way of being relata.
commentary: The taxonomy of relation, and it discriminates carefully. Samyoga — *conjunction*, the Vaisesika category (two substances in contact) — is sambandhi-dharma, a *property of the relata*, and svasabdena abhidhiyate: expressed by *its own word*: 'conjunction' names it, as any word names any thing, because it is a thing — a property residing in its terms. But sambandhah samavayas ca — *connection* as such, and *inherence* (the Vaisesikas' other relation, binding qualities to substances) — sambandhitvena gamyate: are understood *by way of being relata* — i.e. not named directly but grasped only through the terms they relate. The distinction is exact: conjunction is a property and so nameable; relation-as-such is not a property of anything and so is reachable only obliquely (2.46: the relation is inferable from its effect, its form is never seen, it is utterly beyond thinghood). The verse is the third kanda's Sambandha-samuddesa in miniature — and it shows Bhartrhari treating the rival school's categories with technical care before dissolving them.

mula_deva: लक्षणाद् व्यवतिष्ठन्ते पदार्था न तु वस्तुतः ।
mula_iast: lakṣaṇād vyavatiṣṭhante padārthā na tu vastutaḥ |
translation: Word-meanings stand settled by way of characterization, not in reality; from the assistance, that very meaning is somehow followed along.
commentary: The status of the padartha, stated flatly. Laksanad vyavatisthante padarthah na tu vastutah: word-meanings stand settled *by way of characterization*, and *not in reality* — laksana, the marking-out by which analysis fixes an item, is what gives a word-meaning its determinacy, and the determinacy is the marking's, not the thing's. And the concession: upakarat sa eva arthah kathamcid anugamyate — from the *assistance*, that very meaning is *somehow* followed along. Kathamcid — 'somehow': the shrug is the verse's honesty. The word-meanings do have a life, traceable through the assistance they render in constructions; but the tracing is of something the characterization posited, and how it manages to be followed is not further explained. This is 2.10's apoddhara-doctrine in its final formulation: the padartha is a creature of laksana, real in the practice, and its reality *in* the practice is not nothing — it is just not vastu.

mula_deva: वाक्यार्थे योऽभिसम्बन्धो न तस्यात्मा क्वचित् स्थितः ।
mula_iast: vākyārthe yo'bhisambandho na tasyātmā kvacit sthitaḥ |
translation: The connection which is in the sentence-meaning — its self does not stand anywhere; in the practice of word-meanings, they call that its self.
commentary: The connection dissolved, and its ghost given an address. Vakyarthe yo 'bhisambandhah — the connection that is *in* the sentence-meaning (the relatedness by which the items cohere, 2.145's upaslesa) — na tasya atma kvacit sthitah: its *self does not stand anywhere*. It is nowhere: not in the words (which only indicate, 2.434), not among the meanings (which are characterizations, 2.436), not as a thing (2.46's asattva-bhuta). And then: vyavahare padarthanam tam atmanam pracaksate — *in the practice* of word-meanings, they call *that* its self: the practice has a name for this nothing, treats it as an item, and transacts with it. So the connection is a posting with no referent, indispensable to the practice and absent from the world — which is the sharpest thing the kanda has said about the central concept of every rival semantics.

mula_deva: पदार्थे समुदाये वा समाप्तो नैव वा क्वचित् ।
mula_iast: padārthe samudāye vā samāpto naiva vā kvacit |
translation: Whether completed in the word-meaning, or in the aggregate, or nowhere at all — its self is divided out by way of the difference of word-meaning-forms.
commentary: The three options for the connection's location, and their equivalence. Padarthe — completed in the *word-meaning* (each item carrying its own relatedness); samudaye va — or in the *aggregate* (the relation belonging to the collection); samapto na eva va kvacit — or *nowhere at all* (2.437's verdict). Whichever: tasya atma pravibhajyate padartha-rupa-bhedena — its self is *divided out* by way of the *difference of word-meaning-forms*. The connection, wherever one places it, gets its apparent articulation from the items it supposedly relates — which is circular in exactly the way 2.46 diagnosed: the relation is known only from its terms, and the terms are individuated by the relation. Bhartrhari lists the three placements without choosing because the circularity infects all three. What escapes it is his own account: no relation, one meaning, and the 'items' its capacities (2.394's sadharana-model).

translation: (439) The difference which, for the sake of analysis, is the ground of comprehension — by that, in the differentiation, something else, having mutually-requiring parts, is described. (440) The division of a single thing possessing many capacities is followed; for a sentence's single-meaningedness is understood even by a fraction.
commentary: The analysis's status, and then a striking empirical claim. (439): the *difference* posited anvakhyanaya, *for the sake of analysis*, and serving as pratipatti-nibandhanam, the ground of comprehension (the ladder, 2.410) — by it, tena anyad upavarnyate: *something else* is described: sakanksa-avayavam bhede — 'having mutually-requiring parts', which is 2.3-2.4's definition of the sentence. So the analytic definition of the vakya describes not the sentence but *something else* — the analysis's own construct. (440) then names what is really there: anekasakteh ekasya pravibhagah anugamyate — the *division* of a *single* thing possessing *many capacities* is what is being followed (the sadharana-model, 2.394-396: one meaning, many capacity-positions). And the evidence: ekarthatvam hi vakyasya matraya api pratiyate — the sentence's *single-meaningedness* is understood *even by a fraction*: hear part of a sentence and you already grasp that it has one meaning — the unity is given before the completion, which no summative account can explain (2.40's single phoneme conveying the whole).

translation: The external meaning, whether existent or non-existent, is divided out from the meaning of the comprehension; but the division, having made it external, has the mark of the abstraction of capacities.
commentary: The relation of comprehension to the world, and it is the movement's most careful formulation. Sampratyayarthad bahyo 'rthah san asan va vibhajyate: the *external* meaning — *whether existent or non-existent* — is *divided out from* the meaning of the comprehension. The order is the point: what is primary is the sampratyaya-artha, the meaning-in-comprehension (2.328's criterion: what is understood *is* the meaning); the 'external' object is then carved off from it, by us, and its existence or non-existence is a further question that the carving does not settle. And: bahyikrtya vibhagas tu sakty-apoddhara-laksanah — the division, *having made it external*, has the mark of the *abstraction of capacities*: externality itself is an apoddhara, an abstraction, and what it abstracts are capacities (2.88's settlement: abstraction according to capacity-difference). This is as close as the kanda comes to an explicit idealism, and it stops precisely where 2.132-133 stopped: the external is a division within the comprehended, and whether anything answers to it is not a question the semantics can reach.

translation: Even among many finite verbs that are mutually requiring, there is single-sentence-hood; hence the exclusion of accent-suppression from the finite verbs is also meaningful.
commentary: The doctrine of 2.348 (rule and exception as one sentence) given its grammatical proof. Bahusv api tinantesu sakanksesu eka-vakyata: even among *many finite verbs*, if they are *mutually requiring*, there is *single-sentence-hood* — verb-count does not individuate sentences; expectancy does (2.6's tinanta qualifying tinanta). And the evidence from the sastra: tinantebhyo nighatasya paryudasah tatha arthavan — the *exclusion* of accent-suppression *from* the finite verbs is *thus meaningful*: Panini's nighata-rule (8.1.28: a verb loses its accent after a preceding word in the same sentence) has an exception for verbs following verbs — and that exception only makes sense if multiple verbs *can* stand in one sentence. The grammar's exception presupposes the doctrine. This is the kanda's favourite move (2.5's vocative, 2.59's pada-patha): a semantic thesis confirmed by a rule that would be unstatable without it.

mula_deva: एकतिङ् यस्य वाक्यं तु शास्त्रे नियतलक्षणम् ।
mula_iast: ekatiṅ yasya vākyaṃ tu śāstre niyatalakṣaṇam |
translation: But that sentence which has one finite verb, whose definition is fixed in the science — for it, there is no purpose in the mention of 'not-tin', because of the difference of sentences.
commentary: The alternative position and its cost. Suppose one holds eka-tin yasya vakyam — that a sentence has *one* finite verb, by a definition niyata-laksanam sastre, fixed in the science (the operational definition of 2.3, adopted as metaphysics). Then: tasya atin-grahanena artho na vidyate — for such a theorist, there is *no purpose* in the mention of 'not-tin' (the atin of the nighata-rule's exception) — vakya-bhedat, *because of the difference of sentences*: on his view, two verbs simply *are* two sentences, so the rule's exception is idle — no verb ever follows a verb within a sentence, and the exception excludes nothing. The argument is a clean reductio from grammatical economy: Panini does not legislate vacuously; his exception has work to do; therefore the one-verb definition is false. The verse thus turns the science's own laghava-principle (2.343) against a definition the science itself uses (2.3) — which is exactly what 2.3 warned: rule-definitions are for the rules' purposes and settle nothing beyond them.

mula_deva: मृगः पश्यत यातीति भेदाभेदौ न तिष्ठतः ॥ ४४४ ॥
mula_iast: mṛgaḥ paśyata yātīti bhedābhedau na tiṣṭhataḥ || 444 ||
translation: In cases joined with another finite verb, or again in the joined-and-joined — 'the deer! look! it goes' — difference and non-difference do not stand.
commentary: The hard case, honestly displayed. Mrgah pasyata yati — 'The deer! Look! It goes' (or 'Look at the deer going'): three predications, or one utterance? Tinanta-antara-yuktesu — in cases joined with *another* finite verb — yukta-yuktesu va punah, or in the *joined-and-joined* (chains of them): bheda-abhedau na tisthatah — *difference and non-difference do not stand*: neither the claim that these are separate sentences nor the claim that they are one can be maintained. The utterance is genuinely indeterminate between one sentence and three, and no criterion settles it — not verb-count (2.442-443 disposed of that), not expectancy (each closes and none does), not the speaker's intent (he intended the whole scene). Bhartrhari lets the indeterminacy stand. It is the same undecidability he confessed at 2.26 (whether the sequencing cognition is one or many: niruktir na avatisthate) and at 1.74, and its recurrence at the kanda's climax is not a failure: it is what a doctrine of undivided meaning carved by analysis *predicts*. Where the carving is optional, the count is.

translation: That whose what-is-to-be-done is desired, from capacity, for the meaning — having an expectancy not characterized by words, that is called 'of completed meaning'.
commentary: A definition of completion that severs it from words entirely. Itikartavyata arthasya samarthyad yasya kanksyate — that whose *what-is-to-be-done* (the practical remainder: what one must now do) is *desired*, from *capacity*, for the meaning — asabda-laksana-akanksam: having an expectancy *not characterized by words* — samapta-artham tad ucyate: *that* is called 'of completed meaning'. So a sentence is complete when its verbal expectancy has closed even though a *practical* expectancy remains open: 'the deer is going' is a complete sentence and leaves you wanting to know what to do about it. Asabda-laksana-akanksa — wordless expectancy — is the crucial coinage: the sentence's completion is measured against words, and the residue it leaves is measured against action. This distinguishes semantic closure from practical closure, and it explains why sentences can be complete and yet demand more: the more they demand is not linguistic (2.146's itikartavyata, sovereign and untransgressed).

translation: But in the mere analytic statement of the reality, however much meaning is carried along — even without the employment of that, the sentence is completed from the hearing.
commentary: The complement of 2.445, for the case of pure statement. Tattva-anvakhyana-matre tu — in the *mere analytic statement of the reality*: description for its own sake, no injunction, no practical residue — yavan arthah anusajyate, however much meaning is carried along: vina api tat-prayogena srutervakyam samapyate — *even without the employment of that*, the sentence is completed *from the hearing*. A descriptive sentence closes with its utterance; nothing further is wanted; there is no itikartavyata to leave open. Between 2.445 and 2.446 the two kinds of sentence are distinguished by what they leave: the practical sentence closes verbally and opens actionally; the descriptive closes altogether. And note which is treated as the special case: it is the *descriptive* one that needs the qualification 'even without employment'. For this school the default sentence is the one that tells you what to do — which is what a tradition whose paradigm text is a body of injunctions would naturally think, and what 2.113's prayojana-doctrine requires.

translation: 'While walking about, study here'; 'muttering, do the walking-about' — though the purposiveness is not different, a difference is understood from the word.
commentary: A beautiful demonstration that syntax means something even when semantics does not change. Two instructions: cankramyamano 'dhisva atra — 'while walking about, *study* here'; and japan cankramanam kuru — 'while muttering, *do the walking-about*'. The same two activities, the same pairing, the same practical upshot: the man walks and recites. Tadarthyasya avisese 'pi — though the *purposiveness is not different* — sabdad bhedah pratiyate: a *difference is understood from the word*. What differs is which activity is principal and which subordinate — and that difference is real, is understood, and is contributed by nothing but the grammatical construction (2.428: the speaker intends the sadhya-sadhana structure). The example is decisive against any view on which sentence-meaning is a function of the states of affairs described: here the states of affairs are identical and the meanings differ. What differs is the *articulation*, and articulation is the speaker's.

translation: The differences of action, having fruits, grounded in other actions, are uncounted; by the designations of sequence, they have a single finite verb for their exhibition.
commentary: The verb's compression, stated as a fact about the world it reports. Kriya-bhedah phalavantah kriyantara-nibandhanah asamkhyatah: the differences of action — each *having its fruit*, each *grounded in other actions* — are *uncounted*: any action is indefinitely divisible into sub-actions, each with its own result, each depending on others (the cooking is a fetching, a lighting, a stirring, a waiting). And: kramoddesaih eka-akhyata-nidarsanah — by the *designations of sequence*, they have a *single finite verb* for their exhibition. One verb, 'he cooks', displays an unbounded manifold. This is the sadharana-model (2.394) at the verb: the single verb does not sum its sub-actions, it *is* them, wholly, from where it stands. And it explains 2.415's undivided shining: the vakyartha can be undivided while its analysis is inexhaustible, because analysis of a whole is not decomposition into parts.

translation: (449) The whole action, its differences withdrawn, is expressed in the finite verb; the constructing of a division of the differences is not possible from the hearing. (450) 'The kings will sacrifice with the horse-sacrifice'; 'the brahmins sit the session' — a difference is not understood from the finite verb's form.
commentary: The consequence of 2.448, with evidence. (449): nivrtta-bheda sarva eva kriya akhyate abhidhiyate — the *whole* action, its differences *withdrawn*, is expressed in the finite verb; sruteh asakya bhedanam pravibhaga-prakalpana: the constructing of a division of the differences is *not possible from the hearing* — listen as hard as you like to 'he cooks' and no sub-action is audible in it. (450) supplies two cases where the distribution question is live and the verb is silent: asvamedhena yaksyante rajanah — 'the *kings* will sacrifice with the horse-sacrifice' (each king his own? or one joint rite?); satram asate brahmanah — 'the brahmins *sit* the session' (the sattra is by definition collective, but the verb does not say so). Na akhyata-rupad bhedah pratiyate: a difference is *not understood from the finite verb's form*. The verb form is identical in the distributive and collective cases; only samarthya (2.371) tells them apart. Language does not encode the distinction that ritual practice most needs, and the exegetes have been supplying it from capacity ever since.

translation: The action which, heard once, is not repeated among the seventeen — in the Prajapatya rites it attains difference, from capacity.
commentary: The doctrine at work in a real ritual crux. In the Prajapatya rites, seventeen items are involved and an action is sakrt sruta — *heard once*, uttered a single time, anavrtta, not repeated. Does it apply once, or seventeen times? Sa bhedam pratipadyate samarthyat: it *attains difference*, from *capacity* — the action distributes across the seventeen, though nothing in the utterance says so, because what the action *is* requires it (2.371's criterion; 2.372's eating completed in each). The case is chosen because the stakes are exact: get this wrong and the rite is malperformed. And it demonstrates the movement's thesis in the domain where the tradition is most careful: the verb, uttered once, undivided (2.449), distributes by capacity and not by any linguistic mark — which is why the exegetical science exists and why it reasons from samarthya rather than from grammar.

mula_deva: प्रतिस्वतन्त्रं वाक्यं वा भेदो न प्रविभज्यते ॥ ४५२ ॥
mula_iast: pratisvatantraṃ vākyaṃ vā bhedo na pravibhajyate || 452 ||
translation: The eating stands severally with respect to Devadatta and the rest; or the sentence is independent for each — the difference is not divided out.
commentary: Two descriptions of the distributed case, offered as equivalent. Devadattadisu bhujih pratyekam avatisthate: the *eating* stands *severally* with respect to Devadatta and the others — one sentence, its action distributing (2.372). Prati-svatantram vakyam va — *or* the sentence is *independent for each*: several sentences, one per eater, sharing a form (2.389's position). Bhedo na pravibhajyate: the *difference is not divided out* — nothing distinguishes the two descriptions; no fact settles whether this is one sentence distributing or several sentences coinciding. This is 2.444's undecidability again, and its recurrence is now clearly systematic: wherever the kanda's own doctrine (undivided meaning, analytic carving) applies, the count of sentences is a function of the carving. Bhartrhari does not treat this as a problem to be solved. It is the doctrine's signature, and its consistency across every application is the best evidence for it.

translation: But in the uttering of sentences, one form is grasped; while in the comprehension of the differentiated, another form is understood.
commentary: The two-sidedness that generates 2.452's undecidability, named. Uccarane tu vakyanam anyad rupam nigrhyate: in the *uttering* of sentences, *one* form is grasped — the utterance is one, a single stretch of sound, however many predications it carries (2.110's tantra: one form, many sentences). Pratipattau tu bhinnanam anyad rupam pratiyate: but in the *comprehension* of the differentiated, *another* form is understood — the hearer's grasp articulates into as many as the case requires. Utterance-side unity, comprehension-side plurality: and since sentencehood is defined by neither the sound (2.104) nor the count of words (2.326) but by the closing of expectancy in a mind, the two sides can disagree without contradiction. The verse explains, at last, why the kanda's central question — what is a sentence? — has been answered with a phenomenon rather than a definition. Sentences are individuated where they happen, and they happen in comprehenders.

translation: One comprehensive sentence is expressed by way of the general — as in 'in the agent' — and that is divided out among the animal and the rest.
commentary: The mechanism of generality in rule-sentences. Ekam grahanakam vakyam samanyena abhidhiyate: *one comprehensive sentence* is expressed *by way of the general* — the example is Panini's kartari, 'in (the sense of) the agent' (the rule-fragment governing a whole class of affixations) — and tad ca pasvadisu vibhajyate: *that* is *divided out* among the animal and the rest: the single general provision distributes across all its instances. This is 2.176's economy (instruction by the general, for brevity) shown to be a case of 2.448's compression: one utterance, indefinitely many applications, no division audible. And it closes the circle the movement opened at 2.371: how a single utterance distributes over a plurality — whether of eaters, of kings, of sacrificial items, or of grammatical instances — is settled by capacity and purpose, never by form, and the grammar's own most general rules are the clearest case.

translation: If the expectancy of that were to withdraw upon a single hearing — then it could not in any way attain connection with another.
commentary: A closing argument, and it is a conditional whose antecedent the kanda denies. Yadi akanksa nivarteta tad-bhutasya sakrc-chrutau: *if* the expectancy of that were to *withdraw* upon a *single hearing* — if one utterance discharged the expectancy completely and finally — na eva anyena abhisambandham tad upeyat kathamcana: then it could *not in any way* attain connection with *another*. A fully discharged sentence is inert: nothing can be added to it, no further clause can join it, no exception can limit it (2.348's rule-and-exception unity would be impossible; 2.442's multi-verb sentences would not exist). But such joinings manifestly occur. Therefore expectancy is not discharged by a single hearing — it remains live, reaching across utterances, closing only where the whole discourse closes (2.76's maha-vakya). The verse defends, by reductio, the most expansive consequence of the kanda's doctrine: sentencehood has no fixed upper bound, and what looked like a completed sentence is a member of a larger one whenever a larger one is being said.

section: One Form, Many Meanings: The Womb of Sentences
translation: Therefore the ground is single in form and manifold in meaning — the womb of the sentences of division, standing as though not other than them.
commentary: The conclusion of 2.455's argument, and it names the sentence's source with the kanda's oldest metaphor. The ground — upanibandhanam, the fastening (the term for what meaning rests on since 1.13) — is eka-rupam aneka-artham: *single in form*, *manifold in meaning* (2.250's one word with many meanings, now at the sentence's scale). And its office: yonih vibhaga-vakyanam — the *womb* of the sentences of *division*: the analytic sentences, the several predications the analysis distributes (2.389-390), are born from it. Yoni is 2.58's word (samhita as the womb of the words) and 1.3's (the six modifications as wombs of differentiation) — the vocabulary of birth rather than of composition. And the closing qualification: tebhyah ananyad iva sthitam — standing as though *not other* than them: the source is not a further item behind its progeny; the division-sentences are it, differently seen. Iva, as always, holds the ontology honest.

translation: Sometimes an action operates in its assistance by way of the individual-portions; sometimes the general-portion alone of it is what accomplishes the meaning.
commentary: The distribution-doctrine (2.371-455) restated in terms of portions. Kvacit kriya vyakti-bhagaih upakare pravartate: sometimes an action operates, in its *assistance*, by way of the *individual-portions* — reaching its object through the particulars (the eating, per eater, 2.372). Samanya-bhaga eva asyah kvacid arthasya sadhakah: sometimes the *general-portion alone* of it is what accomplishes the meaning — the action engaging only the type, indifferent to instances (the lamp's lighting, 2.374). The vocabulary of bhaga, portion, is doing careful work: these are not two actions nor two parts of one action, but two *aspects* under which one action engages a plurality. The sadharana-model again (2.394-397): one thing, wholly present at several positions, and which position bears the weight is settled by capacity.

translation: The differences that are separated by time, and those in 'ustrasika' and the like — in the commencement of the class-portion, the word's self is not differentiated by them.
commentary: The invariance of the word under two kinds of variation. Kala-bhinnah ca bhedah — differences separated *by time*: the same word uttered on different occasions, its instances scattered across the years. And ye ca api ustrasikadisu — those in 'ustrasika' and the like: the traditional class of words whose formation or reference is irregular. Prakrame jati-bhagasya sabdatma taih na bhidyate: in the *commencement of the class-portion*, the word's *self is not differentiated* by them. Whether the word is met now or a century hence, in a regular formation or an odd one, its atman is one — 2.256's svarupa-invariance (what is fixed about a word is its form) given its temporal and morphological range. The verse guards the sadharana-model from an obvious objection: if the word's meaning is carved fresh at every occasion, is it the same word? Yes — the carving varies, the word does not.

translation: Among differences of a single number, actions differentiated by class and the rest are applied by way of difference, on a single hearing of that word.
commentary: A refinement: distribution can be selective. Eka-samkhyesu bhedesu — among differences that are *of a single number* (items counted as one group) — bhinnah jaty-adibhih kriyah: actions *differentiated* by class and the rest — bhedena viniyujyante: are applied *by way of difference* — tac-chabdasya sakrc-chrutau: on a *single hearing* of that word. One utterance, one number, and yet the several actions distribute selectively across the group by class — some members getting one treatment, some another, all from a single hearing. Nothing in the utterance encodes the sorting; capacity and class do it (2.371's samarthya-laksanam). The verse is a fine specimen of how much the exegetical tradition asks of a single sentence, and of why the kanda has insisted that the words are not carrying the load: no distribution of deposits could produce a selective sorting that the utterance never mentions.

mula_deva: क्रमो वा यौगपद्यं वा यौ लोको नातिर्त्तते ॥ ४६२ ॥
क्रमे विभिद्यते रूपं यौगपद्ये न भिद्यते ।
mula_iast: kramo vā yaugapadyaṃ vā yau loko nātirttate || 462 ||
krame vibhidyate rūpaṃ yaugapadye na bhidyate |
translation: (460) As, with dice and the like, the actions of breaking, eating and playing are distinct — though there is no difference in the time of employment, they stand separately for each. (461) The single-performance of dice and of lute-players is a means; likeness of form is the way; their sequence, being divided, is the once-hearing bound up with that. (462) Two means stand settled in the employment of words: sequence, or simultaneity — which the world does not transgress. (463) In sequence the form is differentiated; in simultaneity it is not; but the action, even in simultaneity, follows the form of sequence.
commentary: Four verses on the two modes of linguistic employment. (460): aksa — 'dice', and the pun is deliberate, since aksa also names other things — take distinct actions: bhanji (breaking), bhaksi (eating), divi (playing); prayoga-kala-abhede 'pi, though the time of employment does not differ, prati-bhedam prthak sthitah, they stand separately for each. (461) gives the machinery: tantra, single-performance, and tulya-rupata, likeness of form, as the means; the sakrc-chruti, the once-hearing, bound to the divided sequence. Then (462) the doctrine: dvau abhyupayau sabdanam prayoge samavasthitau — *two* means stand settled in the employment of words: kramo va yaugapadyam va — *sequence* or *simultaneity* — yau loko na ativartate: which the world does not transgress. Every utterance is either serial or simultaneous; there is no third. And (463) the asymmetry: krame vibhidyate rupam — in sequence the *form* is differentiated; yaugapadye na bhidyate — in simultaneity it is not. Kriya tu yaugapadye 'pi krama-rupa-anupatini: but the *action*, even in simultaneity, *follows the form of sequence*. Even where the words come at once, what they mean unrolls in order — sequence expelled from the vehicle reappears in the meaning, which is 2.24-27's doctrine (krama as the mind's contribution) confirmed from an unexpected side.

translation: The two capacities, of difference and of commingling, stand as though distinct from the word; even in simultaneity, in employment with several, the hearing is differentiated.
commentary: The two capacities named. Bheda-samsarga-sakti dve — the capacity of *difference* (to be carved into distinct meanings) and of *commingling* (to fuse into one) — sabdad bhinne iva sthite: stand *as though distinct* from the word. The iva is essential: they are not two properties the word has but two aspects of its one omni-capacity (2.433's atyanta-yata-saktitva). And the consequence: yaugapadye 'pi anekena prayoge bhidyate srutih — even in *simultaneity*, in employment with several, the *hearing* is differentiated: the word uttered once, at once, is nonetheless heard as several where several are meant. This is 2.463's asymmetry given its mechanism: simultaneity in the vehicle does not prevent differentiation in the uptake, because the differentiating is not the vehicle's work.

translation: Where one meaning, undivided, is intended in the form of difference — there the aggregate is favoured by the property of the parts.
commentary: A case where the whole borrows from its parts. Abhinnah bheda-rupena yah eko 'rtho vivaksitah — where *one* meaning, *undivided*, is intended *in the form of difference*: the speaker means one thing and means it as many (a group as a group-of-these). Tatra avayava-dharmena samudayah anugrhyate: there the *aggregate* is *favoured* — assisted, benefited — by the *property of the parts*: the whole takes on characteristics that belong to its members, and the taking-on is what makes the group-as-many available to speech. This is the reverse of the kanda's usual direction (wholes lending to parts, 2.61's samnivisate); here the parts lend upward. And it is licensed by the same model: on the sadharana-account (2.394), whole and parts are one thing at different positions, so lending can run either way.
