What is not licensed is composition — the aggregate is *favoured* by the parts' property, not *made of* it.

translation: But in the explicit stating of its difference, either it is completed severally, or the hearing, differentiated by the expression, stands in a difference of sentences.
commentary: The two descriptions of 2.465's case, and they are 2.452's pair again. Bheda-nirvacane tu asya — in the *explicit stating* of its difference (where the speaker spells out the members) — pratyekam va samapyate: either it is *completed severally* — one sentence, distributing; or srutih vacana-bhinna va vakya-bhede 'vatisthate: the *hearing*, differentiated by the expression, stands in a *difference of sentences* — several sentences, one per member. Undecidable, as always where the carving is optional (2.444, 2.452). The recurrence at this point is worth marking: the kanda is closing, and it has not resolved this question anywhere. It has instead shown, case after case, that the question has no answer *and that nothing depends on it* — which is a philosophical result rather than an evasion, and the strongest possible confirmation that the unit was never in the utterance.

translation: There, either that word 'aksa' is employed as ending in the singular, or severally, by way of plurality; the division is according to the hearing.
commentary: The two options exhibited on the aksa-example (2.460). Either eka-vacana-antah — the word ends in the *singular*, one form for the group; or pratyekam bahutvena — *severally*, by way of *plurality*, the plural form spelling out the members. And the settlement: pravibhagah yatha-sruti — the division is *according to the hearing*: whichever was uttered, the division follows. Yatha-sruti is a modest and exact criterion: the grammar does not decide whether this group is one or many; the speaker's chosen number does, and the hearer follows. Which is 2.385's doctrine (number conferred by vessels) at the level of morphology — the singular and the plural are two vessels, and the same stuff is one or many according to which one it is poured into.

translation: Even in those sentences which are two-placed, for those who see oneness, the own-capacity of a single thing possessing many capacities is divided out.
commentary: The oneness-doctrine applied to sentences with two loci. Dvisthani yani vakyani — sentences that are *two-placed*: predications reaching two subjects, two sites, two applications. Tesu api ekatva-darsinam — even in those, for *those who see oneness* (Bhartrhari's own party, 2.57's abheda-purvaka) — anekasakteh ekasya sva-saktih pravibhajyate: the *own-capacity* of a *single* thing possessing *many capacities* is *divided out*. Not two sentences, not one sentence with two halves: one capacity, carved. The formulation gathers the movement's whole vocabulary — anekasakti (2.440), sva-sakti, pravibhajyate (2.314's carving) — and applies it where the temptation to count is strongest. Two places, one meaning, and the twoness is the division of a capacity rather than the presence of a pair.

translation: (469) Or there might be an employment, characterized by single-performance, of two utterly distinct things; the means there is the commingling, which is differentiated among the comprehenders. (470) Two words, previously apprehended by way of difference, alike in hearing, are again conveyed by single-performance to the comprehender by the speaker. (471) Even when one alone is intended, the other arises-in-consequence; without any intention, the word shines forth in the form of capacity.
commentary: Three verses on homonymy under tantra, and the last is the movement's most interesting concession. (469): two *utterly distinct* things may be employed tantra-laksanah, by single-performance — one utterance serving both — and the samsarga, the commingling, is the means, pratipattrsu bhidyate, *differentiated among the comprehenders*: different hearers get different fusions. (470): two words bhedena avagatau purvam — previously apprehended *by way of difference* (known separately, as distinct words) — tulya-sruti punah, *alike in hearing*, are conveyed tantrena by the speaker: one utterance, both words, deliberately. That is punning, and the verse describes it exactly. Then (471) the concession: ekasya api vivaksayam anunispadyate parah — even when *one alone* is intended, the *other* arises-in-consequence (2.121's anunispadin: the entailed, unbidden); vina abhisandhina sabdah sakti-rupah prakasate: *without any intention*, the word shines forth *in the form of capacity*. The second meaning comes whether the speaker wants it or not — because the word's capacity is not under his control (2.400's eye can be aimed; what falls in the periphery cannot be excluded). Intention governs what is said and not what arrives (2.301's smoke), and here that doctrine reaches its sharpest formulation: the pun is not always the punster's.

translation: (472) The many capacities of a single thing are sometimes heard simultaneously; fire is applied, even in one place, by way of both illuminating and burning. (473) In a sentence whose meaning is differentiated by the capacity of repetition, even when heard once, it stands by way of division — either from a mark or from the property of single-performance.
commentary: The doctrine's culminating illustration, and it is the work's oldest image put to a new use. (472): aneka-saktih ekasya yugapat sruyate kvacit — the *many capacities* of a single thing are sometimes heard *simultaneously*; and the example: agnih prakasa-dahabhyam ekatra api niyujyate — *fire* is applied, *even in one place*, by way of *both* illuminating *and* burning. One fire, two capacities, exercised at once, in the same act, on the same occasion — and no one thinks the fire is two, or that the burning is a secondary use of the lighting (2.253's gauna/mukhya dissolved by the plainest fact about fire). So with the word: many capacities, exercised at once, one word. (473) then applies it to the sentence heard once and meaning severally — standing vibhagena, by way of *division*, either lingat, from a *mark*, or tantra-dharmat, from the *property of single-performance*. The fire-image is the perfect close to the movement, and it is chosen for a reason: fire has been the work's figure for the word since 1.46 (fire latent in the fire-sticks), and here it is the figure for omni-capacity exercised without division.

translation: In the samprasarana-designation, by the two marks of phoneme and sentence, the division is construed there in one and the same sutra.
commentary: The doctrine confirmed from the grammar's own metalanguage. Samprasarana is Panini's term for the vowel-substitution of semivowels (y→i, v→u, r→r). The verse observes that in the samprasarana-samjna, the division is effected lingabhyam varna-vakyayoh — by the *two marks* of *phoneme* and *sentence*: two different kinds of indicator, operating at different grains — and tatra sutre ekasmin eva kalpate: the division is construed *in one and the same sutra*. One rule, two grains of marking, both required and both present. This is 2.472's fire in the sastra: a single item exercising several capacities at once, and the grammar's own most technical machinery presupposing exactly the multi-capacity account the kanda has argued. The verse is characteristic of the work's method at its most confident: after four hundred verses of philosophy, the confirmation is a footnote to a sutra.

translation: So too in 'reduplication before a vowel', the designation by way of single-remainder, characterized by the means of single-performance and the rest, has been described in the Bhasya itself.
commentary: A second confirmation, and this one names its authority. Dvirvacane aci — 'reduplication before a vowel' (Panini 6.1.1-2 territory): the rule's formulation employs ekasesa, the *single-remainder* device (2.108-110), and is tantropaya-adi-laksanah, characterized by the means of single-performance and the rest. And: bhasya eva upavarnitah — it has been described *in the Bhasya itself*. Patanjali got there first, and Bhartrhari says so. The citation is doing more than crediting: it establishes that the whole apparatus the kanda has built — tantra, ekasesa, one form serving many offices — is not the author's invention but the tradition's own, present in the Mahabhasya and only made explicit here. Which is exactly the claim 1.30 requires of any teaching: it must be agama-purvaka, preceded by tradition, and even the grammarian's boldest semantics is a drawing-out of what the gurus had already done.

translation: Having, for the most part, reached grammarians who delight in brevity and have grasped little learning — when the Sangraha had gone to its setting —
commentary: The famous historical passage begins, and its first word is a lament. The Sangraha — Vyadi's vast lost work, the tradition's great compendium — has *gone to its setting*, astam upagate: the sun-verb, the same used at 2.133 for doctrines that set like stars and at 1.18 for differentiations that set like the sun. The book is gone. And the reason it went: the grammarians it reached were samksepa-rucin — *delighting in brevity*, wanting the short version — and alpa-vidya-parigrahan, having *grasped little learning*. A great work perished because its inheritors preferred summaries. Prayena — 'for the most part': the qualification is scholarly and it is bitter. This is the only extended historical narrative in the work, and it is placed at the very end of the Vakyakanda, after four hundred verses arguing that meaning lives in a flash waked by tradition in a ripened mind — which is precisely what a tradition loses when its heirs want it brief.
contested: the Sangraha's authorship (Vyadi) and its loss are known only from this passage and later report; nothing of it survives, which makes 2.476-482 the principal evidence for a major chapter of the tradition's history

translation: (477) — and when, thereafter, the Mahabhasya had been composed by the teacher Patanjali, the seer of the ford, as the binding-together of the seeds of all reasonings — (478) that work, unfathomed because of its depth, and as though on the surface because of its excellence — in it, for those whose understanding is unformed, no settled conclusion stood at all.
commentary: The second act of the history, and its praise is exact. Patanjali is guruna tirtha-darsina — the *teacher*, the *seer of the ford*: tirtha is the crossing-place, and to see the ford is to know where the river can be passed. His Mahabhasya is sarvesam nyaya-bijanam nibandhane — the *binding-together of the seeds of all reasonings*: not a commentary but a seed-bank, and bija is the work's word for what survives dissolutions (1.132's world-seed). Then (478) the diagnosis of its difficulty, in a phrase of great beauty: alabdha-gadhe gambhiryad — *unfathomed* because of its *depth*; uttana iva sausthavat — and *as though on the surface* because of its *excellence*: the very perfection of its exposition made it look shallow, so that readers waded in and drowned. Tasmin akrta-buddhinam na eva avasthita niscayah: in it, for those whose understanding was *unformed*, *no settled conclusion stood at all*. The book was too good to be read by the unready, and being unread it was misread.

translation: (479) When the seer's work had been swept away by Baiji, Saubhava and Haryaksa, followers of dry reasoning, with the Sangraha as their cover — (480) the grammatical tradition which had fallen away from Patanjali's pupils, at that time stood established among the Southerners in the mere book. (481) Having obtained the tradition from the mountain, by those following the seeds of the Bhasya — by Candracarya and the rest — it was again brought to many branches.
commentary: The near-death and the recovery, and it is the most cited historical passage in Sanskrit grammar. (479) names three men — Baiji, Saubhava, Haryaksa — as suska-tarka-anusarinah, *followers of dry reasoning*: tarka without agama, exactly what 1.30 and 1.137 declared groundless; and they operated sangraha-pratikancuke, with the Sangraha *as their cover* — invoking the authority of the great work while destroying its teaching. Arse viplavite granthe: the *seer's work was swept away*. (480): the tradition bhrastah, *fallen* from Patanjali's pupils, survived kale daksinatyesu grantha-matre — among the *Southerners*, in the *mere book*: text without living transmission, the letter surviving where the flash had gone out (2.152's transmitted pratibha, broken). And (481) the rescue: parvatad agamam labdhva — having obtained the tradition *from the mountain* — bhasya-bijanusaribhih, by those *following the seeds of the Bhasya* (477's bija) — candracarya-adibhih punah, by *Candracarya and the rest*: sa nito bahu-sakhatvam, it was brought again to *many branches*. The whole doctrine of Kanda I is enacted here as history: tradition is not a text but a living inheritance (2.151's agama ripened by bhavana), it can be broken, and its recovery requires both the book and the mountain.
contested: 'from the mountain' (parvatat) is the great crux: a place-name, a mystical source, a teacher's name, or a lineage-designation have all been proposed, and nothing decides. Candracarya is plausibly the Buddhist grammarian Candragomin. The three named opponents are otherwise unknown

translation: (482) Having studied those paths of the courses of reasoning, and his own school, this compendium of the tradition was composed by our teacher. (483) Here, of some of the ways, the bare substance has been cited; in the third kanda the examination will be thorough. (484) One obtains discrimination of understanding through differing traditional views; how much can be raised up by one who runs after his own reasoning? (485) For those who imagine this and that without the ancient traditions, and who have not waited upon their elders, knowledge does not become very gracious.
commentary: The kanda closes on four verses that gather the whole work's method. (482): the compendium was composed by gurunasmakam — *our teacher* — having studied both the nyaya-prasthana-margan, the paths of reasoning, and svam darsanam, *his own school*: both, and in that order. (483) is a promise and a pointer: here vastu-matram udahrtam, the *bare substance* of some views has been cited; kande trtiye nyaksena bhavisyati vicarana — in the *third kanda* the examination will be *thorough*. The Padakanda is announced. Then (484-485) the closing counsel, and it is the work's own epistemology addressed to its reader. Prajna-vivekam labhate bhinnair agama-darsanaih — one obtains *discrimination of understanding* through *differing* traditional views: the doxography that has filled two kandas was not scholarly display but the method itself; discrimination comes from holding many inherited views, not from any one. Kiyad va sakyam unnetum sva-tarkam anudhavata — *how much can be raised up* by one who *runs after his own reasoning*? And the last word: vidya na atiprasidati — knowledge does *not become very gracious* — to those who imagine without the puranair agamaih, the ancient traditions, and who are anupasita-vrddhanam, *who have not waited upon their elders*. Prasidati: knowledge is not seized but *graced*, and it graces those who have sat with the old. After four hundred verses arguing that meaning is a flash in a ripened mind, the kanda ends by naming what does the ripening: not argument, but sitting with those who know.
contested: 'our teacher' (guruna asmakam) in 482 is disputed — whether Bhartrhari refers to a teacher of his own or, as some hold, to himself in the third person; the Vrtti-authorship question is entangled with it and remains open

mula_deva: द्विधा कैश्चित्पदं भिन्नं चतुर्धा पञ्चधापि वा ।
mula_iast: dvidhā kaiścitpadaṃ bhinnaṃ caturdhā pañcadhāpi vā |
translation: The word is divided by some in two ways, in four, or even in five — having been abstracted from sentences, just as stem, suffix and the rest are.
commentary: The Padakanda opens by conceding, in its first breath, everything the Vakyakanda argued. The word — the object this entire kanda will anatomize across fourteen samuddesas and twelve hundred verses — is apoddhrtya eva vakyebhyah: *abstracted from sentences*, and the comparison seals it: prakrti-pratyayadivat, *just as stem and suffix are* — which is 2.10 verbatim, where the extraction of words from sentences was said to have exactly the standing of the extraction of stems from words. So the kanda's subject is announced as a construct. The divisions canvassed are the tradition's: dvidha, twofold (noun and verb); caturdha, fourfold (Yaska's nama, akhyata, upasarga, nipata — the Nirukta's opening classification); pancadha, fivefold (adding the karmapravacaniya of 2.199-204). Kaiscit, 'by some': even the count is disputed, as 2.341 noted when it priced the reduction that would abolish the fourfold scheme. The verse is a masterstroke of intellectual honesty at a book's threshold: here is my subject, and it does not exist except as my analysis makes it. What follows is not thereby idle — the scaffold's statics are the science (2.75, 2.88) — but the reader is told at the outset in what spirit to take twelve hundred verses about word-meanings.

translation: In the abstraction of word-meanings: either the universal, or else substance. The two word-meanings of all words are described as eternal indeed.
commentary: The kanda's program, stated with the economy of a table of contents. Padarthanam apoddhare — in the *abstraction* of word-meanings (3.1.1's apoddhrtya sustained: what follows concerns extracts) — the candidates are two: jatir va dravyam eva va, either the *universal* or *substance*. Every word, on analysis, means a type or a thing; there is no third. And both are nityau eva upavarnitau — described as *eternal indeed*: whichever a word means, what it means does not come and go (1.23's nitya sambandha requiring an eternal relatum, and 3.1.13's dravya turning out to be eternal in a sense the Vaisesikas would not recognize). The two options name the kanda's first two samuddesas — Jati and Dravya — and the fact that they are canvassed as *alternatives* rather than combined is the tradition's long dispute (Vajapyayana for the universal, Vyadi for the substance, as the Mahabhasya reports). Bhartrhari will not choose in the way either party wanted: 3.1.6 is about to relocate the whole question by asking what a word means *first*, before it means anything about the world at all.
contested: nityau eva ('eternal indeed') is emphatic and its scope is disputed — whether both candidates are eternal, or whether the verse reports that each party claims eternality for its own candidate; we take the first, with 3.1.13's dravya-doctrine as warrant

translation: For some, by association, the universal is the indicator of capacity; among khadira-woods and the like that are incapable, a capable one is substituted.
commentary: The first argument for the universal, and it is drawn — characteristically — from ritual practice rather than from metaphysics. The injunction prescribes a khadira post for the sacrifice. Why khadira? Not for khadira-ness as such: jatih sakty-upalaksanam — the universal is the *indicator of capacity*, sahacaryena, *by association*: khadira-wood is durable, and the universal is named because it reliably accompanies the durability the rite needs. The proof is the substitution-rule: khadiradisu asaktesu — among khadiras that are *incapable* (rotten, unsound) — saktah pratinidhiyate: a *capable* one is *substituted*. If khadira-ness itself were what the injunction wanted, a rotten khadira would satisfy it and a sound non-khadira would not; ritual practice says the reverse. So the universal named is a *mark* for a capacity meant. This is 2.64-66's pratinidhi-doctrine (substitution proving that the general survives its specification) redeployed: here it proves that what a universal-word conveys may be a capacity the universal merely flags.

translation: The binding, whose fruit is non-independence, is taught as (governed) by measure and the rest; therefore, even where the universal is expressed, what lacks capacity is not taken.
commentary: The argument of 3.1.3 given its grammatical anchor. The rite's verb is bandhi — *binding* (the victim bound to the post), and its fruit is asvatantrya-phalah: *non-independence* — the binding's whole purpose is that the bound thing not get free. Such a binding is sisyate pramanadi iva — taught as governed by *measure* and the rest: the post's dimensions are prescribed, and dimensions are prescribed because they serve the holding. Hence: atah jaty-abhidhane 'pi sakti-hinam na grhyate — therefore, *even where the universal is expressed*, what *lacks capacity* is *not taken*. The reasoning is tight and it is the samuddesa's opening move: the injunction's own apparatus (the measures, the substitution-rule) shows that its universal-word is in service of a capacity, so universals as word-meanings are, at least here, instrumental rather than terminal. What a word means and what a word is *for* have come apart again (2.113's abhidheya versus prayojana, now inside a single noun).

translation: But if 'binds' were intended as mere contact, then the teaching of measure and the rest would be a mark of a substratum of capacity.
commentary: The alternative construal, and it reaches the same destination. Suppose samslesa-matram badhnatih — 'binds' means *mere contact*, nothing about holding fast: the verb merely joins victim to post. Then the prescriptions of measure would not be explained by the binding's own sense — and yet they are prescribed. So: sakty-asraye tato lingam pramanady-anusasanam — the teaching of measure and the rest would then be a *mark* (linga) of a *substratum of capacity*: the dimensions would be evidence that something capacity-bearing is wanted, even though the verb does not say so. Either way, on both construals of the verb, capacity is what the injunction is after and the universal-word is its indicator. Bhartrhari's technique here is worth naming: he does not argue that his opponent's reading is wrong; he shows that both readings deliver his conclusion. The same move closed 1.28 (eternal or produced, no beginning either way) and 2.433 (all-natured or self-less, the word is sovereign either way).

translation: Its own universal is first expressed by all words indeed; thereafter comes the construction of superimposing that upon the forms of the object-universals.
commentary: The samuddesa's central doctrine, and one of the most consequential verses in the work. Sva jatih prathamam sabdaih sarvair eva abhidhiyate: *its own universal* is expressed *first* by *all* words — every word, before it means anything about the world, means *itself*: the word-universal, sabdatva, the type of which its utterances are tokens (1.71's recurrence-datum: the same word across sentences). Only thereafter: tatah artha-jati-rupesu tad-adhyaropa-kalpana — the *construction of superimposing* that upon the forms of the *object*-universals. The word's own type is laid over the object's type, and reference is that superimposition. Every word is thus doubly universal, and the second universal is a projection of the first. The verse gathers the whole work: it is 1.50's double luminosity (the word shows itself and its meaning) given priority-order; it is 2.127-128's abhijalpa (word and meaning fused in cognition) given its genesis; it is 1.59-68's svam rupam (grammar's technical practice of citing word-forms) shown to be not a special usage but the *primary* one, of which ordinary reference is derivative. And adhyaropa — superimposition — is the term the Vedantic tradition would make its own: what we take to be the world's joints are the word's own type projected outward. The Padakanda's fourteen categories are about to be examined, and the first thing said about all of them is that they are projections of sabdatva.
contested: prathamam ('first') can be temporal (the word-universal grasped before the object-universal, in each act of hearing) or logical (prior in the order of explanation, without any felt priority in experience). The kanda's method favours the logical reading, but the doctrine is stronger on the temporal one, and Bhartrhari does not disambiguate

mula_deva: यथा रक्ते गुणे तत्त्वं कषाये व्यपदिश्यते ।
mula_iast: yathā rakte guṇe tattvaṃ kaṣāye vyapadiśyate |
translation: (7) As the reality in the red quality is designated in the dye, and, from the proximity of what is conjoined with it, is grasped also in cloth and the like — (8) so, from the word-meaning connection, the universal standing in the word serves, in the designating of the object-universals, for the effect of the universal.
commentary: The superimposition of 3.1.6 given its simile, and the simile is a dyer's. (7): redness — rakte gune tattvam — is really *in* the dye, kasaye vyapadisyate, designated there: the dye is what is red, properly speaking. And samyogi-sannikarsat — from the *proximity of what is conjoined* with it — vastradisu api grhyate: it is *grasped also* in cloth and the like: the cloth is called red, and the redness it is called by is the dye's, transferred by contact. Nobody thinks the cloth manufactured its own redness. (8) applies it: sabdartha-sambandhat sabde jatih avasthita — from the word-meaning connection, the universal *standing in the word* — vyapadese artha-jatinam jati-karyaya kalpate: serves, in the *designating* of object-universals, *for the effect of the universal*. The object-universal is the cloth's redness: real as designated, borrowed by contact, the dye's own. This is the sharpest formulation the samuddesa gives of 3.1.6: what we call the universal *in things* is the word's universal, worn by the thing through the contact that reference is. And the choice of a dyeing-simile rather than a mirroring one is deliberate — dye does not merely appear on cloth; it *takes*, and the cloth is thereafter red.

translation: In the single-remainder of universal-words, that is held to be the universal of universals; in 'word-universals', that universal is among the word-universals.
commentary: The regress opened, and opened deliberately. If every word expresses its own universal (3.1.6), then the word 'jati' expresses *its* own universal — and when universal-words are gathered by ekasesa (2.108's single-remainder: one form standing for many), sa jatinam jatir isyate: that is held to be the *universal of universals*. And the same again one level up: sabda-jatayah iti atra — in the expression 'word-universals' — taj-jatih sabda-jatisu: that universal is *among the word-universals*. Universals of universals of universals, without evident end. Bhartrhari states the regress without alarm because his doctrine is not embarrassed by it: on the sarva-sakti account (2.433, 2.472) the word can do anything, including take itself as object indefinitely, and each level is a carving of the same plenitude rather than a new entity. It is the *realist* about universals for whom the regress is a bill — each level a further real thing, inhering in the last — and the tradition's third-man problems are precisely there. The verse hands the bill over.

translation: The word-universal in words, whose mark is distinct from the words — that universal operates transcending even word-universal-ness.
commentary: The regress's terminus, and it is not a top level but a dissolution. Ya sabda-jatih sabdesu sabdebhyo bhinna-laksana: the word-universal *in* words, whose *mark is distinct from* the words — the type is not the tokens, and its criterion is other than theirs (2.104's capacity worn by the invariant stream). And then: jatih sa sabda-jatitvam api atikramya vartate — that universal *operates transcending even word-universal-ness*: it exceeds the very category it was invoked to fill. The formulation is careful and its direction is unmistakable. What is reached at the end of the ascent is not the highest universal in a hierarchy but something that has left the hierarchy — atikramya, having *stepped beyond*. This is the sabda-tattva of 1.1, met from the other end: the Brahmakanda began with the word-essence and descended; the Padakanda begins with word-meanings and ascends to the same, and finds that it is not a padartha at all. The samuddesa on universals thus reaches, in its tenth verse, the point where universals stop being the right category — which is what one should expect from an author for whom every category is a posting.

translation: Even in the expressing of object-universals, all words are universal-expressers — since word-meanings stand characterized by operation.
commentary: The doctrine of 3.1.6 stated as a thesis about all words whatever. Artha-jaty-abhidhane 'pi — *even* where a word is expressing an *object*-universal (the ordinary case: 'cow' meaning cowhood) — sarve jaty-abhidhayinah: *all* are universal-expressers. There is no class of words that escapes into pure particularity, because the expressing of the object-universal is itself the superimposition of the word's own (3.1.6-8). And the ground offered: vyapara-laksanah yasmat padarthah samavasthitah — since word-meanings stand *characterized by operation*: what a padartha *is* is what it *does* — vyapara, the functioning, the office (2.104's capacity, 2.83's individuation by operation). And offices are repeatable by nature: to be characterized by an operation is to be the sort of thing that recurs, which is to be a universal. So universality is not a feature some meanings have; it is what having a meaning *is*, once meanings are understood functionally. The argument is elegant and it is the Padakanda's charter for treating every category as it will.

mula_deva: जातौ पदार्थे जातिर्वा विशेषो वापि जातिवत् ।
mula_iast: jātau padārthe jātirvā viśeṣo vāpi jātivat |
translation: When the universal is the word-meaning, either a universal, or even a particular as though a universal, is what is required by words; therefore they are universal-expressing.
commentary: The thesis defended against its obvious counterexample. If words express universals (3.1.11), what of words that plainly pick out particulars — proper names, demonstratives, 'this cow'? The reply: jatau padarthe — when the universal is (taken as) the word-meaning — sabdaih apeksyate jatih visesah va api jativat: what words *require* is either a universal, or *even a particular as though a universal* — jativat, in the manner of a universal. The particular, to be meant, must be meant *as* something: as this-cow, as Devadatta — that is, under a characterization that is repeatable in principle even if instantiated once (the office-structure of 3.1.11). So particulars enter language only in universal dress. Atas te jati-vacinah: therefore words are universal-expressing, without exception. The doctrine anticipates a long argument in Indian philosophy about whether the particular as such is sayable, and Bhartrhari's answer is the one his whole system requires: it is not — not because particulars are unreal, but because saying is a universal-making operation (3.1.6's superimposition), and what will not wear the word's type cannot be said.

mula_deva: द्रव्यधर्मा पदार्थे तु द्रव्ये सर्वोऽर्थ उच्यते ।
mula_iast: dravyadharmā padārthe tu dravye sarvo'rtha ucyate |
translation: But when substance is the word-meaning, every meaning is called substance; from resting on the properties of substance, everything is held to be substance.
commentary: The rival option (3.1.2's dravyam eva va) shown to be equally total. Take the other horn: dravya-dharma padarthe — when substance is the word-meaning — dravye sarvo 'rtha ucyate: *every* meaning is called substance. And the reason: dravya-dharmasrayat — from *resting on* the properties of substance: whatever a word means is treated as bearing properties, standing in relations, being qualified — which is to be a substance, whatever else it may be. Even universals and actions, once spoken of, take adjectives and cases and behave as things. Atah sarvo 'rtha isyate: therefore *everything* is held to be substance. Read together with 3.1.11-12, the pair is the samuddesa's most striking result: on the jati-option, *everything* words mean is a universal; on the dravya-option, *everything* is a substance. Both options are unrestricted, which means neither is a discovery about the world — each is a way of taking whatever is said (1.74's two darsanas; 2.179's licensed double description). The great dispute between Vajapyayana and Vyadi is not a dispute about the facts. It is a choice of ledger.
contested: the verse's first pada (dravya-dharma padarthe tu) is awkward and our construal supplies the option-structure from 3.1.2; some read dravya-dharma as a compound qualifying padartha ('the word-meaning having substance-properties'), which yields the same doctrine by a rougher route

translation: Or the universal would be the property of recurrence in all universals; the general character of the property of exclusion is held to be the universal in the particular.
commentary: The two definitions of the universal, and the verse is doing more than cataloguing. Anuvrtti-dharma va jatih syat sarva-jatisu: the universal would be the property of *recurrence* — anuvrtti, the running-along, the same-again across instances — in all universals: cowhood is what recurs in cows. That is the realist's definition, and it is the tradition's default. Then: vyavrtti-dharma-samanyam visese jatir isyate — the *general character of the property of exclusion* is held to be the universal *in the particular*: vyavrtti is the turning-away, the excluding, the not-being-other — and on this second definition what makes a cow a cow is its exclusion from non-cows. Bhartrhari records both without deciding, and a reader who knows what came next in Indian philosophy will feel the ground move: vyavrtti is *apoha*, and the Buddhist logicians would build their entire semantics on it — the word 'cow' meaning nothing but exclusion-from-non-cows, universals dissolved into negations. That the option is already here, stated neutrally as one of two available definitions, is among the most valuable pieces of information the Vakyapadiya carries about the state of the question in its own time.
contested: whether vyavrtti-dharma-samanya here anticipates the developed apoha-doctrine or merely names a definitional alternative is a genuine question of intellectual history; the karika is neutral, and reading Dignaga backwards into it would be an error we flag rather than commit

translation: And when place has been constructed by difference of the properties of the things conjoined with it, in those places there exists a general even of ether.
commentary: A hard case for the recurrence-definition, and it is chosen with precision. Akasa — ether, space — is by every school's account *one*, partless, all-pervading: it has no instances, so nothing recurs in it, and by 3.1.14's first definition it can have no universal. And yet we speak of 'places' — desa — and places are akasa: 'here' and 'there' are ether, differentiated. How? Samyogi-dharma-bhedena dese parikalpite: place is *constructed* by difference of the properties of the things *conjoined* with it — the jar's place is where the jar is; the difference is the jar's, lent to the space. And then: tesu desesu samanyam akasasya api vidyate — in those places, a *general* even *of ether* exists: once places are so constructed, ether has instances after all, and place-hood recurs among them. The verse is a small demonstration of the samuddesa's method: universals appear wherever differentiation is constructed, even in what is by definition undifferentiable — which suggests that universals track our constructions rather than the world's joints, and that 3.1.6's superimposition-doctrine is the honest account of what has happened.

translation: As places, being relata, belong to pots and the like which are not places — so places, being relata, belong even to ether, which is not a place.
commentary: The construction of 3.1.15 shown to run in both directions, and the symmetry is the argument. A pot is adesa — *not a place*; it is a thing, not a location. And yet desah sambandhinah: *places* belong to it as *relata* — the pot has a place, is somewhere, occupies. Where did that come from? From relation, sambandha: being conjoined with the space it sits in. Akasasya api adesasya — and ether *likewise* is not a place (it is undifferentiated, 3.1.15) — desah sambandhinah tatha: places belong to it too, and by the same route: from what is conjoined with it. So neither the pot nor the ether is intrinsically a place, and both have places, and the places are wholly a matter of the relation between them. Placehood belongs to neither relatum and appears between them — which is exactly the status 2.46 gave relation itself (inferable from its effect, its form never seen, utterly beyond thinghood). The verse is the Sambandha-samuddesa reaching back into the Jati-samuddesa: what looks like a property of things turns out to be a posting of the relation, and the relation is not a thing.

translation: (17) A cognition resting on distinct things runs along among things conjoined; among things inherent, the grasping of difference withdraws. (18) Therefore the places of the conjoined are constructed as secondary; from the non-discrimination of the sites, primariness belongs to the inherent.
commentary: Two ways of being related, and the criterion is what the cognition does. (17): among samyogi — things *conjoined*, in mere contact (the pot and its space) — bhinna-vastv-asraya buddhih anuvartate: a cognition resting on *distinct things* runs along: one keeps seeing two, the contact never fusing them. But among samavayi — things *inherent*, bound by samavaya (the Vaisesika's intimate relation: quality in substance, universal in particular) — bhedasya grahanam vinivartate: the grasping of difference *withdraws*: one does not see the redness *and* the cloth; one sees red cloth. (18) draws the consequence for place: samyogi-desanam gaunatvam parikalpyate — the places of the *conjoined* are constructed as *secondary*; and mukhyatvam samavayinam — *primariness* belongs to the *inherent*, avivekat pradesebhyah, from the *non-discrimination* of the sites. Where difference is not grasped, place is primary; where it is, place is borrowed. Note what has just happened: the primary/secondary distinction — which 2.253 reduced to established currency — is here made to track a *phenomenological* fact about whether a cognition discriminates. The two treatments are compatible: currency is sedimented discrimination.

translation: That well-known configuration which they know as having the form of recurrence — some, however, declare it, in the form of exclusion, to be substance-hood.
commentary: The two definitions of 3.1.14 given their partisans, and the second is startling. Akrti — the *configuration*, the shape-universal, a term the tradition has used for the universal since Panini's own akrtyadhikarana debates — is known prakhyatam anupravrtti-rupam: as having the form of *recurrence* (3.1.14's anuvrtti: the same-again across instances). That is the standard view. Kecid vyavrtti-rupam tu dravyatvena pracaksate: but *some* declare it, in the form of *exclusion*, to be *substance-hood*. Two moves at once. The universal is redefined as exclusion (3.1.14's vyavrtti, the apoha-shaped option) — and then identified with dravyatva, substance-hood: what makes a thing a substance is its exclusion from what it is not. So the two candidates of 3.1.2, jati and dravya, collapse into each other under the exclusion-definition: universals *are* substances, both being nothing but not-being-other. The verse is a compressed report of a position that would have large consequences, and Bhartrhari gives it without comment — which, given 3.1.13's finding that both options are unrestricted, is consistent: if everything is a universal and everything is a substance, their identification costs nothing.

mula_deva: भिन्ना इति परोपाधिरभिन्ना इति वा पुनः ।
नैकत्वं नापि नानात्वं न सत्त्वं न च नास्तिता ।
mula_iast: bhinnā iti paropādhirabhinnā iti vā punaḥ |
naikatvaṃ nāpi nānātvaṃ na sattvaṃ na ca nāstitā |
translation: (20) 'They are different' — or again 'they are not different' — this is an adjunct imposed by another; this elaboration arises only among the selves of things when they are commingled. (21) Neither oneness, nor manifoldness, nor existence, nor non-existence, is found in the self-realities of things when they are uncommingled.
commentary: Two of the most severe verses in the work, and they dissolve the samuddesa's own subject. (20): bhinnah iti — 'they are different'; abhinnah iti va punah — 'or again, they are not different': *both* are para-upadhih, an *adjunct imposed by another* — neither difference nor identity belongs to things; both are laid on from outside. And the location: ayam prapancah samsrstesu eva bhavatmasu jayate — this *elaboration* arises *only* among the selves of things *when they are commingled*: difference and identity appear where things have been brought into relation — by us, in cognition, in speech (3.1.16's places, appearing between relata) — and nowhere else. Then (21) the full list of what goes: na ekatvam na api nanatvam na sattvam na ca nastita — *neither oneness, nor manifoldness, nor existence, nor non-existence* — atma-tattvesu bhavanam asamsrstesu vidyate: is found in the *self-realities* of things *uncommingled*. Unrelated, a thing is not one, not many, not existent, not non-existent. The four negations are the catuskoti's own four corners, and their appearance here — in a grammarian's treatise on universals, applied to the uncommingled thing-in-itself — is not accidental. Bhartrhari's point is his own, though: what the four corners fail to apply to is what has not been *related*, and relating is what cognition and speech do. The categories are artifacts of commingling; strip the commingling and no category applies, including existence.
contested: whether 21's asamsrstesu atma-tattvesu names a real unrelated substratum (things as they are apart from our relating) or is a reductio (there is no such condition to describe) is the deepest question the passage raises, and the karika does not settle it. The Madhyamaka resonance is unmistakable and its direction of influence is unrecoverable

mula_deva: संसृष्टाः पुरुषार्थस्य साधिका न तु केवलाः ॥ २३ ॥
mula_iast: saṃsṛṣṭāḥ puruṣārthasya sādhikā na tu kevalāḥ || 23 ||
translation: (22) The conclusion is that the being-all-powers is of one alone; the construction of a difference of selves among things would be purposeless. (23) Therefore all the powers — substance and the rest — of distinct characters, being commingled, are what accomplish the human end, and not when taken alone. (24) And just as the wholeness of the sense-faculties and the rest is their very self, so it is understood also in the commingling of relatum and relation.
commentary: The positive doctrine that 3.1.20-21 cleared ground for, and it is the Brahmakanda's metaphysics arriving in the Padakanda. (22): sarva-sakty-atma-bhutatvam ekasya eva iti nirnayah — the *conclusion* is that being-all-powers belongs to *one alone* (1.2's Brahman resting on its differentiated powers; 2.433's sarva-saktitva); bhavanam atma-bhedasya kalpana syad anarthika: the construction of a *difference of selves* among things would be *purposeless* — not false merely, but idle, doing no work. (23): tasmat — therefore — dravyadayah sarvah saktayah bhinna-laksanah: all of them, *substance and the rest* — which is to say the very categories this kanda's fourteen samuddesas will examine — are *powers*, of distinct characters; and samsrstah purusarthasya sadhikah na tu kevalah: *commingled*, they accomplish the human end, and *not when taken alone*. The categories are powers of the one, functional only in combination. (24) gives the model: as the samagrata, the *wholeness*, of the sense-faculties is atma-bhuta — their very *self*, not something added to them (2.419's eyes, which are nothing outside the body) — so with sambandhi-sambandha-samsarga, the commingling of relatum and relation. The passage is the Padakanda's charter: everything it is about to anatomize is a power of one thing, real in commingling, nothing alone.

translation: (25) Nothing whatever comes to be of which there is no universal; the universal is the occasioner of the causes, for the manifestation of its own self. (26) Universals, having taken their station in causes both eternal and non-eternal, come somewhere to manifestation in effects, again and again.
commentary: A causal role for the universal that inverts the usual picture. (25): na tad utpadyate kimcid yasya jatir na vidyate — *nothing* comes to be of which there is *no universal*: nothing is produced except as a something, an instance of a type; bare particulars are not born. And then the inversion: atma-abhivyaktaye jatih karananam prayojika — the universal is the *occasioner of the causes*, for the *manifestation of its own self*. Not: the causes produce the thing, which then has a universal. Rather: the universal deploys the causes so as to manifest itself — the type is the agent, the causes its instruments, the individual its appearing. (26) extends it: jatayah karanesu padam krtva nityanityesu — universals, *having taken their station* in causes eternal and non-eternal — punah punah abhivyaktim upayanti: come to manifestation *again and again*. The eternal type, stationed in the causal order, surfacing repeatedly as instances. This is the manifestation-doctrine of Kanda I (1.44-49: the sphota manifested by sounds it is not made of) transposed to ontology entire: individuals are to their universals what utterances are to the word — occasions of appearing, not constituents.
contested: prayojika ('occasioner') gives the universal genuine causal agency, which is a strong reading; a weaker construal ('is the occasion for the causes') keeps the causes primary. The abhivyakti-vocabulary of 26 and the parallel with Kanda I's manifestation-doctrine support the strong reading and we adopt it

translation: The action that is being brought about — there too the universal is the means; in the completion of its own substratum, it is the occasioner of the actions.
commentary: The doctrine of 3.1.25-26 extended from things to actions. Nirvartyamanam yat karma — the action that is *being brought about*, in process of accomplishment — jatih tatra api sadhanam: there too the *universal* is the *means*. And: svasrayasya abhinispattau sa kriyanam prayojika — in the *completion of its own substratum*, it is the *occasioner of the actions*. The cooking-universal deploys the cookings; what makes this stirring part of a cooking is the type it is realizing, and the type is what drives the process to its completion. Note how far this is from the Vaisesika's inert universals sitting in their instances: Bhartrhari's jati is dynamic, purposive, the engine of its own instantiation (3.1.25's atma-abhivyaktaye, 'for the manifestation of its own self'). And note the term sadhanam — *means*: the seventh samuddesa is titled Sadhana-samuddesa, and it will treat the karakas. Universals as means is thus not a metaphor here but a placement: the jati belongs among the factors that bring actions about.

mula_deva: विधौ वा प्रतिषेधे वा ब्राह्मणत्वादि साधनम् ।
mula_iast: vidhau vā pratiṣedhe vā brāhmaṇatvādi sādhanam |
translation: In injunction or in prohibition, brahminhood and the like is the means; the universal of number, resting on what rests on the individual, is what differentiates the universal.
commentary: The doctrine in ritual application, and then a fine piece of stratification. Vidhau va pratisedhe va — in *injunction* or in *prohibition* — brahmanatvadi sadhanam: brahminhood and the like is the *means*: what qualifies a man for the rite, or bars him from it, is the universal he instantiates, not his individuality (the adhikara-doctrine of 2.79, now grounded). Then the second line's stratification: samkhya-jatih visesika jateh — the *universal of number* is what *differentiates* the universal — vyakty-asrita-asrita, *resting on what rests on the individual*: number is a universal that inheres not in the individual directly but in what inheres in it. So there is a hierarchy: individual, then the universal in it, then number as a universal of *that*. Universals of universals (3.1.9's regress) here doing real grammatical work: the plural of a universal-word is a number-universal riding a quality-universal riding a substance. The verse shows the machinery the grammar needs and the ontology it costs — which is the samuddesa's constant theme.

translation: As the face itself, manifested by water and the like, is what is expressed — so the universal itself, manifested by substances, is what is expressed.
commentary: The reflection returns, and this time to a constructive purpose. Yatha jaladibhih vyaktam mukham eva abhidhiyate: as the *face itself*, manifested by water and the like, is what is *expressed* — pointing at a reflection, one says 'that is my face', and one is right: the reflection is not a second face one names by courtesy; the face is what is named, manifested there (1.49's borrowed properties; 2.294's reflection failing the effect-test, but naming succeeding). Tatha dravyaih abhivyakta jatih eva abhidhiyate: so the *universal itself*, manifested by *substances*, is what is expressed. Point at a cow and say 'cow': what is named is cowhood, manifested in this beast as the face is in the water. The simile does exactly the work the jati-vadin needs — it explains how a word can name a universal while one is looking at a particular — and it is chosen for a further reason: the water manifests the face without the face being *in* the water, which is precisely how 3.1.25-26 want universals to stand to their instances. Manifestation, not inherence.

translation: (30) As a difference residing in the sense-faculty, though unseen in the objects of the senses, serves — apart from any grasping of the faculty — for a difference of cognition; (31) so, for some, individuals, without any grasping of their own form, come to be the occasioning ground of the differences in the cognition of the general.
commentary: The samuddesa's subtlest argument, and it turns on a genuine observation about perception. (30): the sense-faculty has its own differences — the eye's constitution differs from the ear's, and within a faculty from moment to moment — and these are indriya-gato bhedah, residing in the *faculty*, adrsto indriya-arthesu, *unseen in the objects*: nothing in the pot corresponds to my eye's condition. Yet indriya-grahanad rte — *apart from any grasping of the faculty* (one never perceives one's own eye in perceiving) — the difference jnana-bhedaya kalpate: serves for a *difference of cognition*: my seeing differs though neither its object nor its organ is given to me. (31) applies it: tatha atma-rupa-grahanat kesamcid vyaktayo vina — so, for some, *individuals*, *without any grasping of their own form* — samanya-jnana-bhedanam upayanti nimittatam: come to be the *occasioning ground* of the *differences in the cognition of the general*. The individuals are never themselves grasped (3.1.12: particulars enter only in universal dress), and they nonetheless make my cowhood-cognition here differ from my cowhood-cognition there. Ungrasped, they condition; like the eye, they work by not appearing. It is an elegant rescue of the individual's reality from a doctrine that denies it can ever be meant.
contested: 31's atma-rupa-grahanat vina is syntactically awkward and our construal ('without grasping of their own form') follows the parallel with 30's indriya-grahanad rte; an alternative reads it as 'from the grasping of the self-form', reversing the sense — but that loses the analogy the pair is built on

mula_deva: सत्यासत्यौ तु यो भावौ प्रतिभावं व्यवस्थितौ ।
सत्यं यत्तत्र सा जातिरसत्या व्यक्तयः स्मृताः ॥ ३२ ॥
mula_iast: satyāsatyau tu yo bhāvau pratibhāvaṃ vyavasthitau |
satyaṃ yattatra sā jātirasatyā vyaktayaḥ smṛtāḥ || 32 ||
translation: (32) But of the two entities, the real and the unreal, which stand in each thing — what is real there is the universal; the individuals are held to be unreal. (33) Existence alone, being differentiated by the difference of its relata among cows and the like, is called the universal; in it all words stand established.
commentary: The samuddesa's culmination, and it is as radical as anything in the work. (32): every thing has two entities standing in it — satya and asatya, the *real* and the *unreal*: satyam yat tatra sa jatih — what is *real* there is the *universal*; asatya vyaktayah smrtah — the *individuals* are held to be *unreal*. The realist's world is inverted at a stroke: types are what there is, tokens are appearance. Then (33) the final identification, which dissolves even the types: satta eva sambandhi-bhedat bhidyamana gavadisu jatir ity ucyate — *Existence alone*, being *differentiated* by the difference of its *relata* among cows and the like, is *called* the universal. There are not many universals: there is *satta*, Being, one; and what we call cowhood is Being as differentiated by its relata (3.1.20's difference imposed from outside; 3.1.16's places appearing between). The plural universals are Existence carved. And the last line brings the whole kanda home: tasyam sarve sabdah vyavasthitah — in *that*, *all words* stand established. Every word, whatever it seems to name, is established in Being — which is 1.1's sabda-tattva reached by the Padakanda's own road, and which explains 3.1.6's superimposition at last: the word's own universal is laid over the object's because both are the one Being, differently carved. The Jati-samuddesa began by asking what universals are and ends by saying there is only one, and it is not really a universal.
contested: 32's satya/asatya we render 'real/unreal'; the pair can be softened to 'true/false' or 'abiding/transient', and how strongly one takes the individuals' unreality is the classical crux of the samuddesa. 33's satta-doctrine (the maha-samanya, Being as the sole universal) is the Bhartrharian signature and is not in doubt

mula_deva: तां प्रातिपदिकार्थं च धात्वर्थं च प्रचक्षते ।
mula_iast: tāṃ prātipadikārthaṃ ca dhātvarthaṃ ca pracakṣate |
translation: And they declare that to be the meaning of the nominal stem and the meaning of the root; it is eternal, it is the great Self — the affixes 'tva', 'tal' and the rest express it.
commentary: Satta — Being, the sole universal reached at 3.1.33 — now claims the whole of grammar. Tam pratipadika-artham ca dhatv-artham ca pracaksate: they declare *that* to be the meaning of the *nominal stem* and the meaning of the *root* — both, the two great classes of meaning-bearing element the science recognizes: what nouns are built on and what verbs are built on alike mean Being. Then: sa nitya, it is *eternal*; sa mahan atma — it is the *great Self*, and the phrase is deliberate, importing the upanisadic register into a discussion of stems and roots. And the grammatical clincher: tam ahuh tvatal-adayah — the affixes *tva* and *tal* and the rest *express* it: these are the abstract-noun suffixes (gotva, 'cowness'; gota, the same), and what they do, on this account, is not manufacture an abstraction but *say Being* — 'cowness' means Being-as-carved-among-cows (3.1.33). The verse is the samuddesa's boldest identification: the abstract suffix is the grammar's own name for the mahan atma, and every stem and every root has been meaning it all along.

translation: That very one, having attained sequence among the particulars, is called action; in the withdrawal of the form of sequence, that same is spoken of as substance.
commentary: The unification, and it is one of the great verses of the work. Sa eva — *that very one*, satta, Being — prapta-krama visesesu kriya abhidhiyate: *having attained sequence* among the particulars, is called *action*. And: krama-rupasya samhare tat sattvam iti kathyate — in the *withdrawal* of the form of sequence, that same is spoken of as *substance*. Action and substance — the two great categories, verb-meaning and noun-meaning, the whole dichotomy the grammar is built on (2.195's asattva-bhuto bhavah versus sattva) — are *one thing* under two aspects: Being with sequence, Being with sequence withdrawn. There are not two kinds of meaning; there is Being, and whether we meet it as verb or noun depends on whether krama is in or out. And krama, the whole work has argued, is the mind's contribution (2.25's sequencing power; 2.27's sequenceless capacity generating ordered appearance). So the noun/verb distinction — the deepest structural fact about language — is a difference in how we take one thing, not a difference in what there is. This is 3.1.2's alternatives (jati or dravya) dissolved: both are satta, sequenced or not.

translation: That same one attains the six states among the modifications of being; by its own powers, in sequence, it thus appears in cognition.
commentary: The doctrine of 3.1.35 given its classical furniture. Sa eva bhava-vikaresu sad-avasthah prapadyate: that same one — Being — attains the *six states* among the *modifications of being*: the sad-bhava-vikara of Yaska's Nirukta, which 1.3 already installed as the wombs of differentiation: is born, exists, changes, grows, declines, perishes. Kramena saktibhih svabhih: *in sequence*, by its *own powers* — the powers are Being's, the sequence is the mode of their exercise (3.1.35's prapta-krama). Evam pratyaya-bhasate: thus it *appears in cognition*. Note the verb: appears — the six states are how Being shows, not six things that happen to it. And note the closing of a circle: 1.3 introduced the six vikaras at the Brahmakanda's opening, resting on Brahman's kala-sakti; here, twelve hundred verses later, the same six are Being's own powers exercised in sequence. The Padakanda's ontology and the Brahmakanda's cosmology have turned out to be one doctrine approached from opposite ends.

translation: Even the sequence belonging to it is its very self, wherein this seeing of time, in the form of before-and-after and the rest, stands as though divided.
commentary: The precision that keeps 3.1.35-36 from incoherence. If Being is sequenceless and action is Being *with* sequence, whence the sequence? Atma-bhutah kramo 'pi asyah: *even the sequence* belonging to it is *its very self* — not an addition, not an alien imposition, but Being's own (3.1.24's samagrata, the faculties' wholeness being their self). Yatra idam kala-darsanam paurvaparyadi-rupena pravibhaktam iva sthitam: wherein *this seeing of time*, in the form of before-and-after and the rest, stands *as though divided*. The iva does its usual office. Time's divisions are how the seeing takes it; the sequence that is Being's own self is undivided, and paurvaparya — before-and-after — is a division laid on. So sequence is real (it is Being's self) and its articulation into befores and afters is not (that is darsana, seeing). The verse threads a needle the whole work has been approaching: 2.24 argued that time admits no intrinsic divisions and 2.25 located sequence in the cognition; here the two findings are reconciled by distinguishing sequence-as-such (real, Being's own) from ordered sequence (constructed). The Kala-samuddesa, the ninth, will take this up at length.

translation: On the admission of the disappearance of things, that same one is their non-existence; in a disappearance that has attained sequence, it is understood as 'it perishes'.
commentary: Being made to account for non-being — the hardest thing the doctrine must do. Tirobhava-abhyupagame bhavanam — on the admission of the *disappearance* of things (granting that things do vanish) — sa eva nastita: *that same one* — Being — *is* their *non-existence*. The formulation is deliberately paradoxical and it is exact: what we call a thing's absence is Being, carved so as to exclude it (3.1.33: Being differentiated by its relata). Absence is not a further entity opposed to Being; it is Being under a determination. And the verb-form: labdha-krame tirobhave nasyati iti pratiyate — in a disappearance that has *attained sequence*, it is understood as 'it *perishes*': the same non-existence, sequenced (3.1.35's rule), presents as an action, the perishing. The verse thus delivers what 2.33 promised — that the capacities for existence *and* non-existence stand established in the one — and it disposes of the negation-problem that occupied 2.240-245: there is no cancelled deposit to account for, because the negative sentence's content is Being, carved to exclude, and nothing was ever added to be taken away.

translation: Fallen away from the prior property, and not having attained the subsequent station — in that interval, from the resort of the differences, birth is spoken of.
commentary: Birth analysed as an interval, and the analysis is subtle. Purvasmat pracyuta dharmat — *fallen away* from the prior property; aprapta ca uttaram padam — and *not having attained* the subsequent station: the thing is between, no longer what it was and not yet what it will be. Tad-antarale bhedanam asrayat janma kathyate: in *that interval*, from the *resort of the differences*, *birth* is spoken of. Birth is not an event that happens at an instant; it is the name for a gap — the interval where the differences take their stand, where a thing is neither of its two determinations. And this is precisely 3.1.20-21's condition: uncommingled, a thing is neither one nor many, neither existent nor non-existent — and here that condition is located not in some inaccessible thing-in-itself but in the ordinary interval of becoming. Everything that comes to be passes through the state to which no category applies. The verse gives the catuskoti a home in the biology of change.

mula_deva: आश्रयः स्वात्ममात्रा वा भावा वा व्यतिरेकिणः ।
mula_iast: āśrayaḥ svātmamātrā vā bhāvā vā vyatirekiṇaḥ |
translation: The resort is either mere portions of its own self, or things that are distinct from it, or its own powers — these being the causes of the seeing of difference in Existence.
commentary: What the 'resort of the differences' (3.1.39) might be, canvassed in three options. Asrayah sva-atma-matra va — the resort is *mere portions of its own self*: Being's own aspects, nothing external; bhava va vyatirekinah — or *things distinct* from it: real others, standing outside; sva-saktayo va — or its *own powers* (the sakti-doctrine of 1.2 and 3.1.22-23). And all three are sattayah bheda-darsana-hetavah — *the causes of the seeing of difference in Existence*: whichever they are, what they do is cause the *seeing* of difference. Note the term: darsana, seeing (3.1.37's kala-darsanam). Bhartrhari canvasses three metaphysical accounts of what differentiates Being and does not choose — because on his method the choice is a ledger-choice (1.74, 2.179), and what all three deliver is the same: difference is *seen*, and its causes are what they are. The samuddesa's characteristic tolerance at the level of ontology, rigour at the level of phenomenology.
contested: the three options are compressed and their individuation is our reading; some take sva-atma-matra and sva-saktayah as one option variously phrased, yielding a two-fold canvass (own aspects versus real others), which is the more usual dichotomy

translation: In its manifestation among earth and the rest, it does not depend on configuration; the universal, its substratum being uninterrupted, stands even in a non-eternal substratum.
commentary: A defence of universals against the transience of their instances. Prthivy-adisu abhivyaktau na samsthanam apeksate: in its manifestation among earth and the rest, it does not depend on *configuration* — the universal is not the shape; a lump of earth and a pot are both earth, and earthness cares nothing for their forms (against any akrti-doctrine that identifies the universal with configuration, 3.1.19's prakhyata akrti). And the second line: anucchinnasrayat jatih anitye 'pi asraye sthita — the universal, its *substratum being uninterrupted*, stands even in a *non-eternal* substratum. The pots come and go; the *series* of pots does not; and the universal rides the unbroken succession rather than any member of it. This is 1.28's vyavastha-nityata exactly — beginninglessness as regular arrangement rather than as any item's permanence, the analogy there being living beings, each produced and the series without a first term. The eternal universal needs no eternal instance, only an unbroken supply.

translation: Some declare every universal to have an uninterruptible substratum; they are settled in the view that there is no simultaneity of everything at the dissolution.
commentary: The doctrine of 3.1.41 pressed to a cosmological commitment. Anucchedyasrayam eke sarva jatim pracaksate: some declare *every* universal to have an *uninterruptible* substratum — not merely unbroken in fact but incapable of interruption. Why so strong? Because of the pralaya, the world-dissolution: if at some moment *all* substrata perished together, every universal would lose its footing at once and could not return (3.1.41's succession requires that the chain never fully break). So these theorists are settled: na yaugapadyam pralaye sarvasya iti vyavasthitah — there is *no simultaneity* of everything at the dissolution: the destruction is staggered, something always remaining, the chain never quite parting. This is the seed-doctrine of 1.132 (when all traditions perish, a seed remains) transposed to ontology, and it shows how much metaphysical weight the universals' eternality is made to carry: rather than let the chain break, the school revises its cosmology.

translation: For those who see oneness, when the differences have dissolved into the primal nature, the universals — their own substrata — attain the state of substance.
commentary: The alternative cosmology, for those who can afford it. Prakrtau pravilinesu bhedesu — when the *differences* have *dissolved* into the *primal nature* (prakrti, the Samkhya's unmanifest matrix) — ekatva-darsinam: for those who *see oneness* (the abheda-purvaka party of 2.57, Bhartrhari's own). What happens to universals then? Dravya-sattvam prapadyante svasraya eva jatayah — the universals, *being their own substrata*, attain the state of *substance*. Where all differentiated instances have dissolved, the universals do not lose their footing: they *become* their own footing — cease to need instances and stand as substance. And by 3.1.35 this is exactly right: substance is Being with sequence withdrawn, and at the dissolution sequence has withdrawn. So the pralaya is not a catastrophe for universals but their return to what they always were: satta, undifferentiated, before its relata carve it (3.1.33). The verse resolves 3.1.42's anxiety by not sharing it — those who see oneness need no unbroken chain, because the chain was never what held the universal up.

translation: Brahminhood and such entities stand present in all living beings; manifested, they are the accomplishers of their own effects — such too is the received teaching.
commentary: A doctrine of remarkable reach, reported as smrti. Brahmanatvadayah bhavah sarva-pranisu avasthitah: brahminhood and the like *stand present in all living beings* — not in brahmins only: every universal is everywhere, latent, in everything that lives. What distinguishes a brahmin is not that he has brahminhood and others lack it but that in him it is abhivyaktah, *manifested*. And manifested, svakaryanam sadhakah — they are the *accomplishers of their own effects* (3.1.25's prayojika: the universal deploying the causes for its own manifestation). Iti api smrtih: *such too is the received teaching* — the report-marker, and Bhartrhari does not press it further. The doctrine follows strictly from 3.1.33 (there is only satta, carved by relata) and 3.1.25-27 (universals as self-manifesting powers): if all universals are one Being differentiated, then all of them are latent wherever Being is, which is everywhere. Its social implications are not drawn here, and it would be anachronistic to draw them for the text; but the metaphysics is unambiguous, and it is the metaphysics that the verse states.

translation: By some, a manifestation of universals is accepted even in paintings and the like; but those that rest in living beings are the occasion, in their attainment, of merit and demerit.
commentary: A boundary drawn on 3.1.44's universality. Kaiscid citradisu api abhivyaktih jatinam isyate: by some, a manifestation of universals is accepted *even in paintings* — the painted brahmin has brahminhood manifested in him, the painted cow cowhood: manifestation does not require flesh (and 2.290 already granted the painting its likeness while denying it efficacy). But the qualification that matters: prany-asritas tu tah praptau nimittam punya-papayoh — those that rest in *living beings* are, in their attainment, the occasion of *merit and demerit*. Only the living manifestation carries dharmic weight: honouring a painted brahmin earns nothing, killing a painted cow costs nothing. So the boundary is not ontological but ethical — the painting has the universal and not the consequences — and it is drawn by exactly the criterion 2.288-290 used to sort the real from the unreal: asadharanam karyam, the effect that only the genuine article produces. Here the effect is merit, and merit is what the painting cannot do.

translation: But the knowledge of those, in men distinguished from us, they know to be through every sense-faculty — as, from practice, among the particulars of gems and silver and the like, for those who know them.
commentary: How universals are known, and the answer returns to the work's oldest example. Asmad-visistanam — of men *distinguished from us*, those beyond our grade (the rsis of 1.37-38, whose knowledge of past and future is not different from perception) — jnanam tasu sarvendriyam viduh: their knowledge of universals is *through every sense-faculty*: not inferred, not reasoned to, but perceived, and by any sense whatever. And the analogy that makes it credible: abhyasat mani-rupyadi-visesesu iva tad-vidam — as, *from practice*, among the particulars of *gems and silver*, for *those who know them*. The jeweller of 1.35 returns, and his epistemic situation is now given its metaphysical import: what he perceives, unable to say how, is the universal — and what the seer perceives is the same, at every grade. Abhyasa, practice, is what closes the gap (2.117's pratibha-hetuh abhyasat; 2.152's six-fold flash). The doctrine of universals thus rests, in the end, not on argument but on connoisseurship — the trained eye that sees types where the untrained sees only this stone.

translation: The reality of difference which resides in the scent of jasmine, lotus and the like — in the world it is not expressed by cognitions of that being, because of its non-eternality.
commentary: A limit on what universals language reaches. There is a genuine bheda-tattvam — a *reality of difference* — residing in the scent of jasmine as against lotus: the noses of the world discriminate them infallibly, so the difference is real. And yet: tad-bhava-pratyayaih loke na abhidhiyate — in the world it is *not expressed* by cognitions of that being: there is no word for it, no abstract noun, no 'jasmine-scent-ness' that ordinary usage employs. Why? Anityatvat — because of its *non-eternality*: the scent is too fleeting to have earned a universal-word. The verse concedes something important: real differences may go unnamed, and language's inventory of universals is not a map of the world's distinctions but of the ones usage has settled on (2.253's prasiddhi; 2.296's loka-prasiddhi deciding what is real). What we have words for is what has stayed put long enough to be worth a word.

translation: But the universals such as man-lion and the like are not expressed by their own words; in them stands a hearing that is, as it were, other, resting on like-formed parts.
commentary: The man-lion returns (2.90's specimen) and now as a problem for universals. Asva-sabda-abhidhanah — *not expressed by their own words*: narasimha-adi-jatayah, the universals of man-lion and the like, have no word of their own; 'narasimha' is a compound, borrowing from 'nara' and 'simha'. So a real kind (the tradition grants the man-lion its reality) with no proper name, denoted only by pieces that name other kinds. And what stands in such a word: sarupavayaveva anya tasu srutir avasthita — a *hearing* that is, as it were, *other*, resting on *like-formed parts*. The compound's members sound like the words for man and lion and are not those words (2.177's rule: different meaning, different base; 2.169's kupa-supa-yupa). So the man-lion's universal is reached by a word whose parts merely resemble words. The verse is a fine specimen of the samuddesa's method: a case where the smooth doctrine (words express universals) meets a kind that has to be named obliquely, and the obliquity is described rather than smoothed.

translation: In the delimiting of the universal's state, number, or number-hood itself, serves for assistance through commingling, even where there is remoteness.
commentary: Number's role in fixing universals. Jaty-avastha-paricchede — in the *delimiting of the universal's state* (which cowhood: the one, the many, the herd) — samkhya samkhyatvam eva va — *number*, or *number-hood* itself — upakaraya kalpate: serves for *assistance*; and the striking qualification: viprakarse 'pi samsargat — *even where there is remoteness*, through commingling. Number assists the universal even at a distance — the singular or plural of a word conditioning which state of the universal is meant, though number is not part of the universal and stands well away from it (3.1.28's stratification: number as a universal riding on what rests on the individual). The verse is preparing the Sankhya-samuddesa (the eleventh) and, more immediately, the discussion of vivaksa that follows: if number assists from a distance, then whether it assists at all in a given utterance is a matter of what the speaker means it to do.

translation: Indication is the operation, in the refining of the word, for the accomplishing of the effect; the capacities of number, action and the rest are seen even where the hearing is the same.
commentary: The grammar's own use of the foregoing. Laksana sabda-samskare vyaparah karya-siddhaye: *indication* is the *operation*, in the *refining of the word* (sabda-samskara: 1.131's term, the grammarian's whole business), *for the accomplishing of the effect*: the science indicates in order to get its operations right, and indication is a doing, not a describing (2.436: word-meanings stand by way of characterization, not in reality). And: samkhya-karmadi-saktinam sruti-samye 'pi drsyate — the capacities of *number*, *action* and the rest are *seen* even where the *hearing is the same*: identical forms carrying different capacities, sorted by nothing audible (2.104's capacity-assumption in the invariant stream). So the grammar's indications are postings that track capacities the sound does not carry — which is why the science is necessary and why its categories are not discoveries.

translation: Nothing that is a substance-entity is spoken of at all without number; therefore, in the designation of everything, number would be unintended.
commentary: A dilemma about number, sharply put. Na vina samkhyaya kascit sattva-bhuto 'rtha ucyate: *nothing* that is a substance-entity is spoken of *without number* — Sanskrit has no numberless noun; to say 'cow' is to say one cow, and there is no way to mention a thing without counting it. But then: atah sarvasya nirdese samkhya syad avivaksita — therefore, in the designation of *everything*, number would be *unintended*: if number is unavoidable, it cannot be meant — what one cannot help saying, one does not thereby intend (1.136's canon of sato 'vivaksa, the non-intention of the actual; 2.69's unintended qualities of the rice). The reasoning is exact and it produces a genuine puzzle: the grammar's number-apparatus is obligatory and therefore, on the tradition's own principle, semantically idle. What is forced is not meant.

translation: For some, either oneness or manifoldness is unintended, for that serves for the expressing of the universal; but duality would be intended.
commentary: The puzzle of 3.1.51 resolved by a discrimination, and it is a fine one. Ekatvam va bahutvam va kesamcid avivaksitam: for some, either *oneness* or *manifoldness* is *unintended* — the singular and the plural are the forms one uses when saying nothing about number, tad hi jaty-abhidhanaya, for *that* (either of them) serves for the *expressing of the universal*: 'the cow is an animal' uses a singular and means cowhood; 'cows are animals' uses a plural and means the same. Neither number is doing number-work. Dvitvam tu syad vivaksitam: but *duality* would be *intended* — the dual is never idle. Sanskrit's dual is used only when exactly two are meant; there is no generic dual. So the obligatory numbers (singular, plural) are unmeant and the optional one (dual) is meant, and the criterion is precisely 3.1.51's: what one could have avoided saying, one intended. The verse is a small triumph of the vivaksa-doctrine (1.155, 2.400): meaning is what was aimed, and grammar's forced categories are outside the aim.

translation: 'If these two should fall ill, this medicine is to be given' — in such an indicating of the meaning, duality would be unintended.
commentary: The rule of 3.1.52 (the dual is always meant) immediately qualified, and the qualification is exact. 'If these two fall ill, give this medicine': the sentence's purpose is laksana, *indicating* a condition — it tells you when to administer, not how many patients matter. Would one withhold the medicine from a third? Obviously not; and would one wait until both were ill? Not that either. So dvitvam syad avivaksitam — *duality would be unintended*, even in the dual, whose whole distinction was that it is never idle. What overrides the rule is purpose: where the sentence is indicating rather than specifying, the number is carried along and not aimed (2.301's smoke: what an operation cannot help producing, it does not intend). The verse guards 3.1.52 from being taken as a formal rule about the dual. It is not. It is an application of vivaksa, and vivaksa answers to what the sentence is *for* (2.113), which the dual's morphology cannot settle.

translation: For number expressible by 'one' and the rest, auxiliary-status in the action is accepted; for in 'he digs with two', it is resorted to from the form.
commentary: The contrary case, where number *is* doing work. Ekadi-sabda-vacyayah samkhyayah karmani angatvam isyate: for number expressible by 'one' and the rest, *auxiliary-status* in the action is accepted — angatva, the Mimamsa relation of a subsidiary to its principal (2.81-82): the number is a genuine contributing factor to the act. The evidence: khanati dvabhyam — 'he digs *with two*' (two spades, two hands) — rupad dhi sa asrita: from the *form* it is resorted to. Digging with two is a different action from digging with one; the number enters the act's own description. So numbers sort into two classes by the same criterion as everything else in this samuddesa: those the action needs (auxiliary, intended) and those it merely carries (unintended, 3.1.51).
