(21): karanatvadibhih jnatah kriya-bheda-anupatibhih — *known* by *instrument-hood* and the rest, which *follow upon* the differences of *action*; svatantryam uttaram labdhva pradhane yanti kartrtam: having obtained a *subsequent independence*, they *attain agency* with respect to the *principal*. The subordinate karakas regain agency at the next level up: within its own operation, the axe is svatantra. So the system is a nested hierarchy of agents, each independent in its own sphere and demoted in the sphere above.

mula_deva: यथा राज्ञा नियुक्तेषु योद्धृत्वं योद्धृषु स्थितम् ।
तेषु वृत्तौ तु लभते राजा जयपराजयौ ॥ २२ ॥
mula_iast: yathā rājñā niyukteṣu yoddhṛtvaṃ yoddhṛṣu sthitam |
teṣu vṛttau tu labhate rājā jayaparājayau || 22 ||
translation: (22) As, among those appointed by the king, the state of being fighters stands in the fighters; but when they act, it is the king who obtains victory and defeat. (23) So, among all those appointed by the agent, doing one work — agency, subsequent, is not in conflict with instrument-hood and the rest.
commentary: The political simile, and it is the samuddesa's finest. (22): rajna niyuktesu — among those *appointed by the king* — yoddhrtvam yoddhrsu sthitam: the *state of being fighters* stands *in the fighters*: the soldiers do the fighting, entirely and without remainder; the king is not fighting. Tesu vrttau tu labhate raja jaya-parajayau: but *when they act*, it is *the king* who obtains *victory and defeat*. The doing is theirs; the outcome is predicated of him. (23) applies it: tatha kartra niyuktesu sarvesu eka-artha-karisu — so among *all those appointed by the agent*, *doing one work* — kartrtvam karanaditvaih uttaram na virudhyate: *agency*, *subsequent*, is *not in conflict* with instrument-hood and the rest. The axe is an agent (it cuts) and an instrument (in the woodsman's sentence), as the soldier is a fighter and the king's instrument, and no contradiction arises because the two predications belong to different levels. The simile explains what 3.7.20-21 asserted, and it explains why we say 'the king conquered' without lying and 'the soldiers fought' without contradicting ourselves.

translation: But when the operation is not resorted to, the occasioning ground is held to be the hetu; when the state of being a limit is resorted to, they know the mark to be in the case of the mark.
commentary: Two karakas distinguished by what they do not do. Anasrite tu vyapare nimittam hetur isyate: when the *operation is not resorted to* — where the ground contributes without any operation being attributed to it — the occasioning ground is held to be *hetu*, the cause (Panini's karana in its causal sense, and the ablative of cause: 'he suffers *from* illness'). Asrita-avadhi-bhavam tu laksane laksanam viduh: when the state of being a *limit* is resorted to, they know the *mark* to be in the case of the *mark*. So hetu and laksana are the two karakas that operate without operating: one contributes causally without a vyapara of its own, the other locates by being a boundary (3.6.2's avadhi: the limit from which direction separates). The verse is fine taxonomy: having established that every karaka is an agent with its own complete operation (3.7.20), it identifies the two that are exceptions — and they are the two that do not act.

translation: The hetu has substance and the rest for its domain; the karaka has a fixed action. An agent depending on another agent is held to be the hetu in the action.
commentary: The hetu/karaka distinction sharpened. Dravyadi-visayo hetuh: the *hetu* has *substance and the rest* for its *domain* — it can be anything: a substance, a quality, an action, so long as it grounds. Karakam niyata-kriyam: the *karaka* has a *fixed action* — a karaka is defined by its determinate contribution to a determinate act (3.7.1's samarthyam in the completing). And the causative: karta kartr-antara-apeksah kriyayam hetur isyate — an *agent depending on another agent* is held to be the *hetu* in the action. This is the grammar of causation: when Devadatta makes Yajnadatta cook, Yajnadatta is an agent who depends on another agent, and Devadatta is the hetu. So the causative construction is analysed as an agent-over-agent relation — which is exactly 3.7.22's king and soldiers, now given its morphological home in the nic-affix.

mula_deva: हेत्वर्था तु क्रिया तस्मान्न स प्रतिनिधीयते ॥ २६ ॥
mula_iast: hetvarthā tu kriyā tasmānna sa pratinidhīyate || 26 ||
translation: For an action, a substitute for the instrument is seen; but the action is for the sake of the hetu — therefore that one is not substituted.
commentary: A test that discriminates the two. Kriyayai karanantasya drstah pratinidhih tatha: for an *action*, a *substitute for the instrument* is *seen* — the axe failing, use a hatchet; instruments are replaceable (3.1.78's substitution-rule discriminating what may be substituted from what may not). Hetv-artha tu kriya: but the *action* is *for the sake of* the *hetu* — the hetu is not a means the action uses; the action exists for it. Tasmat na sa pratinidhiyate: *therefore* that one is *not substituted*. One does not substitute a different reason for acting and call it the same act. The test is the same one 3.1.78-79 ran on universals and qualities (what stands near the capacity cannot be replaced; what stands at a remove can), and it places hetu and karana precisely: the instrument serves the action and is fungible; the hetu is what the action serves and is not.

translation: The hetu is the accomplisher of the meaning by way of the contrary and by way of the favourable; and purposiveness, by way of the favourable, is what follows upon hetu-hood.
commentary: The hetu's two modes. Pratilomya-anulomyabhyam hetur arthasya sadhakah: the *hetu* is the *accomplisher of the meaning* by way of the *contrary* and by way of the *favourable* — one acts *because of* a thing one wants (favourable) and *because of* a thing one flees (contrary): both are reasons, and the ablative of cause covers both ('from desire', 'from fear'). Tadarthyam anulomyena hetutva-anugatam tu tat: and *purposiveness* — tadarthya, being-for-that, the dative's sense ('for the sake of') — *by way of the favourable*, is what *follows upon hetu-hood*. So the dative of purpose is a special case of the hetu: the favourable reason, taken as goal. The verse quietly unifies two of Panini's cases (the ablative of cause and the dative of purpose) under one karaka-analysis, distinguished only by direction — and the unification follows from 3.7.26's finding that the hetu is what the action is *for*.

translation: Everywhere the innate capacity stands as long as the substance does; but at the time of the action, from its manifestation, it becomes assisting through its substratum.
commentary: The temporality of capacity, and it settles a real puzzle. Sarvatra sahaja saktih yavad-dravyam avasthita: *everywhere*, the *innate* capacity stands *as long as the substance* does — the axe's cutting-power is in it while it is an axe, whether or not anyone is cutting; capacities do not come and go with their exercise. Kriya-kale tu abhivyakteh asrayad upakarini: but *at the time of the action*, from its *manifestation*, it becomes *assisting* through its *substratum*. So the capacity is permanent and its *assisting* is occasional — it is always there and it helps only when manifested (1.44's manifestation-doctrine: the sphota permanent, the sounds occasioning). This resolves how a karaka can be both a standing property of a thing (the axe is an instrument even in the shed) and an occasion-relative posting (3.7.12's whatever-whenever-wherever): the capacity stands, the assisting happens.

mula_deva: सर्वदा स तु सन् धर्मः क्रियाकाले निरूप्यते ॥ २९ ॥
mula_iast: sarvadā sa tu san dharmaḥ kriyākāle nirūpyate || 29 ||
translation: The wall's capacity is in obstructing, that of a sword and the like in cutting; that property, being always existent, is determined at the time of the action.
commentary: The doctrine of 3.7.28 in two homely cases. Kudyasya avarane saktih: the *wall's* capacity is in *obstructing* — a wall is for stopping, and it is for stopping when nothing is being stopped. Asyadinam vidarane: that of a *sword* and the like in *cutting*. Sarvada sa tu san dharmah: that property, being *always existent* — the wall does not acquire obstruction-power when someone walks into it. Kriya-kale nirupyate: it is *determined* at the *time of the action* — nirupyate: ascertained, fixed, brought into determinacy (3.3.54's sva-rupena anirupitam: the object's own form left undetermined by the word). So the capacity is eternal and its determination is occasional, and the karaka-assignment records the determination. The examples are chosen for their obviousness: nobody thinks the wall becomes obstructive on impact, and nobody can say what the wall's obstruction *is* except by reference to a walking-into that has not yet happened.

translation: As the Varuna-nooses of the demons, conjoined with their own limbs, are made manifest — so are the capacities of substances made manifest for those who seek to conquer.
commentary: The samuddesa's most vivid image for manifestation. The varuna-pasa — Varuna's nooses, the god's binding weapon of the Vedic hymns, which the demons carry sva-anga-samyoginah, *conjoined with their own limbs*: the noose is in them, part of them, unseen until it binds. Vyajyante: they are *made manifest* — the noose does not arrive; it appears out of what was already there. Dravyanam saktayah tatha vijigisunam: so the *capacities of substances*, for those who *seek to conquer*. The image completes 3.7.28-29 with a picture: capacities are latent bindings, carried in the thing, showing when the occasion draws them out. And vijigisunam — for those who seek to conquer — quietly returns the samuddesa to its own subject: the capacities manifest *for* someone with a purpose, and a purpose is what makes an event into a structure of agent and instrument and object. The karaka-system is the world's latent nooses, drawn out by an intention (3.7.2's kvacit kimcid vivaksyate: somewhere, something is intended).

mula_deva: छेद्यं प्रति व्याप्रियते शक्तिमान् गृह्यते तदा ॥ ३१ ॥
mula_iast: chedyaṃ prati vyāpriyate śaktimān gṛhyate tadā || 31 ||
translation: When the sword, by its own sharpness, weight, hardness and shape, is engaged toward what is to be cut — then it is apprehended as capacity-possessing.
commentary: The manifestation of 3.7.30 given its physics. Taiksnya-gaurava-kathinya-samsthanaih svaih: by its *own* *sharpness*, *weight*, *hardness* and *shape* — four properties, each permanently the sword's (3.7.28's sahaja sakti yavad-dravyam avasthita: the innate capacity standing as long as the substance). Asir yada chedyam prati vyapriyate: when the *sword* is *engaged toward* what *is to be cut*. Saktiman grhyate tada: *then* it is *apprehended* as *capacity-possessing*. The verse locates the apprehension precisely: not when the sword exists (it always has the four properties), and not after the cutting (that is too late), but when it is *engaged toward* — vyapriyate, in the middle of its being brought to bear. Capacity is apprehended in the engaging. And the four properties are named because they are what the engaging draws on: the karana-hood is not a fifth property alongside them but their being brought to bear on something.

translation: Some hold the means of the action to be produced beforehand from another occasioning ground; some hold it innate; others hold the action to be prior.
commentary: Three positions on the means' origin, catalogued. Prak nimittantara-udbhutam kriyayah kaiscid isyate: some hold the means *produced beforehand from another occasioning ground* — the sword's cutting-power was made by the smith; capacities have causal histories. Sahajam kaiscit: some hold it *innate* — the capacity is the thing's own, unmade (3.7.28's sahaja). Kriya anyaih purvam isyate: *others* hold the *action* to be *prior* — the capacity is constituted by the action rather than preceding it, so that the sword becomes an instrument by cutting. Three genuine options, and Bhartrhari does not choose. He does not need to: 3.7.28-31 have established that whatever the capacity's origin, it is *apprehended in the engaging*, and that is all the karaka-system requires. The origin-dispute is 3.1.107's substrate-dispute again — canvassed, tolerated, and shown not to touch the doctrine that depends on it.

translation: The operation itself is first, somewhere not resorting to anything at all; the capacity, in a single substratum, draws downward, like a current.
commentary: The third option of 3.7.32 developed, with an image. Pravrttir eva prathamam kvacid api anapasrita: the *operation itself* is *first*, somewhere *not resorting to* anything at all — the doing is primary, leaning on no prior capacity: sometimes there simply *is* a going, and the goer is read off it. Saktir eka-adhikarane sroto-vad apakarsati: the *capacity*, in a *single substratum*, *draws downward*, *like a current*. The image is exact: a river's current is not a thing in the water that makes it flow; it is the flowing, and it carries what is in it. So capacity is the operation's own momentum, drawing its bearer along — which inverts the ordinary picture (thing has power, power produces act) into: act runs, and what runs in it is called its power. The verse is the samuddesa's most process-metaphysical moment, and it is offered as one option among three.

mula_deva: अपूर्वं कालशक्तिं वा क्रियां वा कालमेव वा ।
mula_iast: apūrvaṃ kālaśaktiṃ vā kriyāṃ vā kālameva vā |
translation: Some, in some fashion, call that entity of such a character the unprecedented, or the capacity of time, or action, or time itself.
commentary: The current of 3.7.33 named, and the names do not agree. Apurvam: the *unprecedented* — the Mimamsa's technical term for the unseen ritual potency that bridges rite and remote fruit (2.119's test-word). Kala-saktim va: or the *capacity of time* (1.3's kala-sakti, on which the six modifications rest). Kriyam va: or *action*. Kalam eva va: or *time itself*. Tam evam-laksanam bhavam kecid ahuh kathamcana: some, *in some fashion*, call *that entity of such a character* by these names. Kathamcana — 'in some fashion': the shrug is 2.171's (kaiscit kathamcid akhyatah, of the etymologists' derivations). Four schools, four names, one phenomenon: the drawing-onward that makes a sequence of events into an action. Bhartrhari records the disagreement and adds nothing, because 3.6.16 already ruled that where every candidate for a first term turns out to be another differentiated item, the reasonable course is to stop looking.

translation: For others, six capacities are eternal, endowed with difference and non-difference; for the accomplishing of action they stand established among objects, like universals.
commentary: The doctrine that will organize the rest of the samuddesa. Nityah sat saktayah anyesam: for *others*, *six* capacities are *eternal* — the six karakas (agent, object, instrument, recipient, source, locus) taken as permanent powers, not as occasional postings. Bheda-abheda-samanvitah: *endowed with difference and non-difference* — they are distinct from each other and not distinct from what has them (1.2's saktis, apthak and yet functioning prthaktvena iva). Kriya-samsiddhaye arthesu jativat samavasthitah: *for the accomplishing of action* they stand established *among objects*, *like universals*. The comparison to universals is the point: as cowhood stands in cows eternally and manifests in each (3.1.25-26's jati as self-manifesting power), so agenthood stands in things and manifests when they act. The six become a fixed ontological furniture — and the samuddesa will spend its remaining verses both using this scheme and undermining it.

translation: And by difference of substance, configuration and the rest, they are seen as though unlimited; but in the settling of the reality, one does not transgress the six capacities.
commentary: The six defended against their apparent multiplicity. Dravya-akaradi-bhedena tah ca aparimita iva drsyante: by *difference of substance*, *configuration* and the rest, they are seen *as though unlimited* — every instrument is a different instrument, every agent a different agent; the world's roles look innumerable. Iva: as though. Tattva-sayyam tu sat saktih na ativartate: but in the *settling of the reality*, one does *not transgress* the *six* capacities. The unlimited variety reduces to six modes. This is the ordinary structure of the work's reductions (2.250's one word carved by nimittas into unlimited meanings; 3.1.33's one satta carved by relata into all the universals) — and its application here yields the karaka-system's central claim: however many participants an event has, they occupy six slots, and the slots are exhaustive.

translation: From difference of occasioning ground, one and the same capacity is understood as differentiated; they call agency alone six-fold — it is the ground of that operation.
commentary: The reduction pressed one step further, and this is the samuddesa's deepest structural claim. Nimitta-bhedad eka eva bhinna saktih pratiyate: from *difference of occasioning ground*, *one and the same* capacity is understood as *differentiated* — the six are not six powers but one, carved by nimittas (2.250-251's mechanism exactly). And the identification: sodha kartrtvam eva ahuh: they call *agency alone* *six-fold*. Not: there are six capacities of which agency is one. Rather: there is *agency*, and it appears sixfold. Tat-pravrtteh nibandhanam: it is the *ground of that operation*. This completes 3.7.18 (in the mere completing, agency exists in every karaka whatever): if every karaka is an agent, then the six karakas are six modes of *one* thing, agency, differentiated by the occasions on which it is attended to. Instrument-hood is agency-under-a-nimitta. The whole architecture of Sanskrit syntax reduces to one power, sixfold-appearing.

mula_deva: तत्त्वे वा व्यतिरेके वा व्यतिरिक्तं तदुच्यते ।
mula_iast: tattve vā vyatireke vā vyatiriktaṃ taducyate |
translation: Whether in reality or in separation, that is spoken of as separate; the world has the word for its authority, and it is followed along by the science.
commentary: The methodological placement of 3.7.37's reduction. Tattve va vyatireke va vyatiriktam tad ucyate: *whether* in *reality* or in *separation* — whether the six are really distinct or only construed as distinct (3.7.15's undecided ontology) — that is *spoken of as separate*: language treats them as six regardless. And the ground of the treatment: sabda-pramanako lokah — the *world* has the *word* for its *authority*: people's ontology is their grammar (2.328's principle: what a word means is what is understood, and there is no other criterion). Sa sastrena anugamyate: *it* is *followed along by the science* — and the grammar follows the world, rather than correcting it (2.142's tat prajnah na vikalpayet; 3.6.25's lokah tatra anugamyate). So the six karakas are in the sastra because they are in the loka, and they are in the loka because that is how words work. The reduction to one agency (3.7.37) is true and changes nothing, because the science's business is the world's usage.

translation: But in the highest sense there is no oneness whose character is distinct from separateness; reality itself shines forth in the form of separateness and oneness.
commentary: The reduction of 3.7.37 given its metaphysical honesty. Paramarthe tu na ekatvam prthaktvad bhinna-laksanam: in the *highest sense* there is *no oneness* whose character is *distinct from separateness* — the 'one agency' of 3.7.37 is not a unity standing over against the six; oneness and separateness are not two conditions (3.6.26's finding: neither exists without the other, and in the highest sense the difference does not exist at all). Prthaktva-ekatva-rupena tattvam eva prakasate: *reality itself* *shines forth* in the *form of separateness and oneness*. So one must not read 3.7.37 as saying that the truth is one agency and the six are illusion. The truth shines as both — six, and one — and neither is the correction of the other. This is 3.2.12-13's paired tetralemmas applied to the karaka-system: everything true of it is true both ways, and the pair is the doctrine.

mula_deva: द्यौः क्षमा वायुरादित्यः सागराः सरितो दिशः ।
mula_iast: dyauḥ kṣamā vāyurādityaḥ sāgarāḥ sarito diśaḥ |
translation: (40) That separateness which is beyond doubt is not different from oneness; that oneness which is beyond doubt is not different from separateness. (41) Sky, earth, wind, sun, oceans, rivers, directions — these are portions of the inner-organ reality, standing outside.
commentary: (40) states the mutual non-difference with a symmetry that is itself the argument: yat prthaktvam asandigdham tad ekatvan na bhidyate — that *separateness* which is *beyond doubt* is *not different from* oneness; and the mirror. Whatever is indubitably many is not other than one, and whatever is indubitably one is not other than many. Then (41) delivers, without warning, one of the most remarkable sentences in the work: dyauh ksama vayur adityah sagarah saritah disah — *sky*, *earth*, *wind*, *sun*, *oceans*, *rivers*, *directions* — antah-karana-tattvasya bhagah bahir avasthitah: are *portions of the inner-organ reality*, *standing outside*. The cosmos is the inner organ's parts, externalized. This is 3.7.6's doctrine (the intellect's operation superimposed on objects) at maximum scale, and 3.6.23's declined option (a property of the inner organ shining forth outwardly) here affirmed without the hedge. The list is deliberately elemental — the things anyone would call most obviously *out there* — and they are named as the mind's own portions standing outside.
contested: 41's antah-karana-tattvasya bhagah admits a weaker reading ('portions of the reality that is [known by] the inner organ') and a stronger ('portions of the inner-organ-reality itself'). The stronger is the natural construal and coheres with 3.7.6 and 3.7.3; we adopt it while noting that the verse stands without argument here

translation: That very one stands, single, in the form of the division of time; for that portion, being without prior and posterior, is marked by another's form.
commentary: The cosmos of 3.7.41 given its temporal aspect. Kala-viccheda-rupena tad eva ekam avasthitam: *that very one* stands, *single*, in the *form of the division of time* — the same inner-organ reality that stands outside as sky and sun stands also as time's divisions. Sa hi apurvaparo bhagah: for that *portion* is *without prior and posterior* — the portion itself has no before and after (3.1.37's sequence being Being's own self, undivided; 3.2.8's no difference of time in the real). Para-rupena laksyate: it is *marked by another's form* — its temporal divisions come from elsewhere, as the mountain's sides come from the sun (3.6.12). So time's articulation is conferred on what has none, exactly as space's was (3.6.13's bhaga-prakalpana-sakti), and both articulations are of one thing which is the inner organ's own reality. The Kala-samuddesa, two samuddesas ahead, will take this up at length.

translation: For separation is seen even where there is no separation, when there is no syntactic connection; therefore the meaning of the case-ending is held to be other than the meanings of 'tree' and the rest.
commentary: The argument that case-meanings are distinct from stem-meanings. Drsto hi avyatireke 'pi vyatireko 'nvaye 'sati: *separation* is seen even where there is *no separation*, when there is *no syntactic connection* — one can distinguish things that are not in fact distinct, wherever the construction does not link them. Vrksadyarthan layas tasmad vibhakty-artho 'nya isyate: therefore the *meaning of the case-ending* is held to be *other than* the meanings of 'tree' and the rest. The reasoning: the stem 'vrksa' gives a tree; the ending gives a role; and since the role can vary while the tree stays fixed ('the tree', 'to the tree', 'from the tree'), the two meanings are separable — which the distributional method (2.12's anvaya-vyatireka) certifies. This is the grammatical basis for the whole samuddesa: karaka-meanings are borne by the endings (3.7.13), and here that is established by method rather than asserted.
contested: the transcription's vrksadyarthan layah is corrupt; we translate the evident sense (the meanings of 'tree' etc. being distinguished from the case-meaning) and flag the fault

translation: The karaka is the general; the seventh and the rest are the wombs of its differences: six, by the difference of what is called karman and so on — but the seventh is the difference of the remainder.
commentary: The system's formal architecture, and it is Panini's. Samanyam karakam: the *karaka* is the *general* — karaka is the genus, and the individual roles are its species (Panini 1.4.23: karake, the heading under which all the role-definitions fall). Tasya saptadya bheda-yonayah: the *seventh* and the rest are the *wombs of its differences* — the case-endings are what generate the distinctions. Sat karma-akhyadi-bhedena: *six*, by the difference of what is called *karman* and so on — the six karakas proper. Sesa-bhedas tu saptami: but the *seventh* is the *difference of the remainder*. This last is precise and it is Panini's own architecture: the genitive (sasthi) is not a karaka at all; it is the sesa, the *remainder* case (2.3.50: sasthi sese), used where no karaka-relation holds. So the system has six roles plus a residue-case for everything else — and the residue is where relation as such lives (3.3.3: the word-meaning connection understood through the sixth case; 2.197-198's genitive for relations whose action has withdrawn).

mula_deva: निर्वर्त्यं च विकार्यं च प्राप्यं चेति त्रिधा मतम् ।
mula_iast: nirvartyaṃ ca vikāryaṃ ca prāpyaṃ ceti tridhā matam |
translation: The object is held to be three-fold: what is to be produced, what is to be modified, and what is to be reached. Among these, the karman is what is most desired; but another is constructed four-fold.
commentary: The object's classical taxonomy. Nirvartyam ca vikaryam ca prapyam ca iti tridha matam: the *object* is held to be *three-fold* — nirvartya, *what is to be produced* (the mat that the weaving makes: 3.7.7's janyamana karman, the object that does not exist until the act completes); vikarya, *what is to be modified* (the gold that the smith works: it exists and is changed); prapya, *what is to be reached* (the village one goes to: it exists and is unchanged). The three exhaust the ways an action can bear on an object. Tatra ipsitatamam karma: among these, the *karman* is *what is most desired* — Panini's own definition (1.4.49: kartur ipsitatamam karma, the object is what the agent most desires), and note that it is defined by the *agent's desire*, not by any feature of the thing (3.4.3's dravya defined by pronominal referability; 3.7.3's karakas grounded in buddhi). Caturdha anyat tu kalpitam: but *another* is constructed *four-fold* — a rival taxonomy, unnamed. The object's classification is doctrine-relative, as everything in this kanda has been.

translation: What is reached by way of indifference; what is not desired by the agent; what is not named by other designations; and whatever has another as its antecedent —
commentary: The object's residual categories, and they qualify 3.7.45's definition sharply. Panini defined the karman as ipsitatamam — *what is most desired* by the agent. But four classes of object are manifestly not desired. Audasinyena yat prapyam: what is reached by way of *indifference* — the grass one treads on while walking to the village (Panini's own example at 1.4.51's akathitam ca provisions); yat ca kartuh anipsitam: what is *not desired* by the agent — the poison one swallows unknowingly, the thief one meets on the road. Samjnantaraih anakhyatam: what is *not named by other designations* — the residue that no other karaka-term covers (the akathita, 'unstated', of Panini's rule). Yat ca api anya-purvakam: and whatever has *another as its antecedent*. So the definition by desire fails for most objects, and the grammar has had to build a residue-category to catch them. The verse is the samuddesa's honest report that its central karaka is defined by a criterion its own instances violate — and 3.7.3's answer stands ready: the karakas are buddhi-postings, and the postings need not answer to the definition.

translation: That for which a transforming material cause — whether existent or non-existent — is not resorted to: of that they declare the state of being what-is-to-be-produced.
commentary: The nirvartya defined by an absence. Sati va avidyamana va prakrtih parinamini: a *transforming material cause* — whether *existent or non-existent* (the clause is careful: it does not matter whether one holds the material really transforms) — yasya na asriyate: *for which* it is *not resorted to*. Tasya nirvartyatvam pracaksate: of *that* they declare the state of being *what-is-to-be-produced*. So the nirvartya-karman is the object in whose production no material is being attended to: 'he makes a mat' does not present the reeds; the mat simply comes to be. And the contrast is with vikarya (3.7.48), where the material *is* attended to. The distinction is thus not between two kinds of event but between two ways of construing one — whether the stuff is in view — which is 3.7.3's thesis (the karaka-practice is grounded in a state of the intellect) applied to the object's own taxonomy.

mula_deva: निर्वर्त्यं च विकार्यं च कर्म शास्त्रे प्रदर्शितम् ॥ ४८ ॥
mula_iast: nirvartyaṃ ca vikāryaṃ ca karma śāstre pradarśitam || 48 ||
translation: But when the material cause is intended, it is what-is-to-be-modified; by some, otherwise. Both what-is-to-be-produced and what-is-to-be-modified have been exhibited as the object in the science.
commentary: The complement of 3.7.47, and the criterion is named. Prakrteh tu vivaksayam vikaryam: when the *material cause* is *intended*, it is *what-is-to-be-modified*. Vivaksa — intention: the samuddesa's constant (3.7.2's kvacit kimcit vivaksyate; 3.7.19's difference by intention). Whether an object is nirvartya or vikarya depends on whether the speaker means to bring its material into view: 'he makes an ornament' (nirvartya, the gold unattended) versus 'he works the gold into an ornament' (vikarya, the gold in view). Same goldsmith, same afternoon. Kaiscid anyatha: *by some*, *otherwise* — the criterion is disputed. Nirvartyam ca vikaryam ca karma sastre pradarsitam: both have been *exhibited as the object* in the *science* — Panini's system recognizes both, and it does not tell you which is which, because the assignment is the speaker's.

mula_deva: तन्निर्वर्त्यं विकार्यं च कर्म द्वेधा व्यवस्थितम् ॥ ४९ ॥
mula_iast: tannirvartyaṃ vikāryaṃ ca karma dvedhā vyavasthitam || 49 ||
translation: What, being non-existent, comes to be; or what, being existent, is made manifest by the birth — that is the object established two-fold, as what-is-to-be-produced and what-is-to-be-modified.
commentary: The two-fold object given its metaphysical alternatives, and they are 3.3.43's dilemma. Yad asat jayate: what, *being non-existent*, *comes to be* — the asatkarya-vada option, the Vaisesika's: the mat was not, and now is. Sad va janmana yat prakasyate: or what, *being existent*, is *made manifest by the birth* — the satkarya-vada option, the Samkhya's: the mat was in the reeds and the weaving revealed it. Tan nirvartyam vikaryam ca karma dvedha vyavasthitam: *that* is the object established *two-fold*. The verse maps the grammar's two objects onto philosophy's two theories of causation — and note what this implies: the choice between satkarya and asatkarya, over which the schools fought for centuries, is here a choice of *construal*, available to any speaker, and Sanskrit has a case-assignment for each. The metaphysical dispute is a difference in vivaksa (3.7.48), and both parties speak grammatically.

translation: Something arises from the destruction of the material — like ash from wood; something by the arising of another quality — like the modification of gold and the rest.
commentary: The vikarya's two modes. Prakrty-uccheda-sambhutam kimcit: *something* arises from the *destruction of the material* — kastha-adi-bhasmavat, *like ash from wood*: the wood is gone, and the ash is what became of it; the material is consumed in the modification. Kimcid gunantara-utpattya: *something* by the *arising of another quality* — suvarnadi-vikaravat, *like the modification of gold*: the gold persists entire and takes a new shape (3.2.4's suvarnadi bhinnam svaih akaraih apayibhih: gold differentiated by its own perishing configurations, and the gold never other). So the vikarya splits: destructive modification, where the material does not survive, and qualitative modification, where it does. And the two examples are the tradition's standing pair — ash and gold — precisely because they mark the limit: at one end the material is annihilated, at the other it is untouched, and the grammar's single category vikarya covers both. What unites them is only that the material is in view (3.7.48), which is a fact about the speaker.

translation: Where an accomplishment of the particulars belonging to the action is not understood, either from seeing or from inference — that is called what-is-to-be-reached.
commentary: The third object, defined by a double absence. Kriya-gata-visesanam siddhih yatra na gamyate: where an *accomplishment* of the *particulars belonging to the action* is *not understood* — nothing about the object shows that the action happened to it. Darsanad anumanad va: either from *seeing* or from *inference* — neither observation nor reasoning finds a mark. Tat prapyam iti kathyate: *that* is called *what-is-to-be-reached*. The village one goes to is unchanged by the going; look at it and infer as you like, and no trace of the arrival is in it. So the prapya-karman is the object that the action leaves as it found it — which makes it, of the three, the one whose objecthood is *purely* a construal: the nirvartya at least comes to be, the vikarya at least changes, and the prapya does nothing at all except get reached. Its karman-hood is entirely the speaker's posting (3.7.3).

translation: Everywhere a gaining of particulars exists, through seeing and the rest; for some, the establishing of that manifestation is in the case of seeing, poison and so on.
commentary: An objection to 3.7.51, and it is a good one. Visesa-labhah sarvatra vidyate darsanadibhih: *everywhere* a *gaining of particulars* exists, through *seeing* and the rest — even the prapya *does* acquire something: being-seen, being-reached, being-arrived-at. Look hard enough and every object is marked by the action that took it. Kesamcit tad-abhivyakti-siddhih drsti-visadisu: for *some*, the *establishing of that manifestation* is in the case of *seeing*, *poison* and so on — where the object visibly registers the act (the poisoned man shows the poison; the seen thing is, on some accounts, altered by being seen). So the third category may collapse: if every object gains a particular, none is merely reached. The objection presses 3.7.51's double absence, and the next verse answers by naming what the prapya does gain.

translation: Coming-to-appearance, manifestation, and being-endured: these particulars of the object that is being reached stand established in the accomplishing of the action.
commentary: The answer to 3.7.52: the prapya's gains are named, and they are all relational. Abhasa-upagamah — *coming-to-appearance*: the object comes into view; vyaktih — *manifestation*: it becomes manifest; sodhatvam — *being-endured*, being-put-up-with, being-undergone. Iti karmanah visesah prapyamanasya kriya-siddhau vyavasthitah: these *particulars* of the object *that is being reached* stand established *in the accomplishing of the action*. Every one of the three is a fact about the object's standing-in-relation to the act, not about the object: coming-to-appearance is appearing to someone, manifestation is manifestation to a cognition, being-endured is enduring an action. So 3.7.52 is granted its datum and denied its conclusion: the prapya does gain particulars, and they are relational particulars, conferred in the act and belonging to no thing (3.6.14's things without place, parts, motion, support; 3.7.14's nimitta-bhava as a leaning).

translation: Among what-is-to-be-produced and the rest, having first experienced independence, the object comes to be, thereafter, in the operation of other agents.
commentary: The object's own agency, and this is 3.7.18's doctrine reaching the karman. Nirvartyadisu tat purvam anubhuya svatantratam: among the nirvartya and the rest, *having first experienced independence* — the object is *svatantra*, independent, before it is an object: the wood is doing its own thing, the village stands on its own. Svatantrya is Panini's own mark of the agent (1.4.54: svatantrah karta), and it is here predicated of the object in its prior condition. Kartrantaranam vyapare karma sampadyate tatah: in the *operation of other agents*, the object *comes to be*, *thereafter*. Objecthood is a demotion suffered when another agent's operation arrives (3.7.20's nyakkrta-saktayah: capacities subordinated by the agent). So the karman is an agent that has been overtaken. Every object in every sentence was independent a moment before the verb reached it, and its objecthood is the grammar's record of a subordination.

translation: Even in the separation from that operation, the object, standing in its own operation, obtains in some cases the rules taught in the science that rest upon it.
commentary: The object's retained agency, and it has grammatical consequences. Tad-vyapara-viveke 'pi: even in the *separation from that* operation — even when the other agent's action is withdrawn or not in view — sva-vyapare vyavasthitam: the object stands *in its own operation*: the wood goes on being wood, doing what wood does (3.7.20's sampurnaih svaih vyaparaih samanvitah: furnished with their own complete operations even when subordinated). Karma apadistan labhate kvacit sastrasrayan vidhin: the *object* obtains, in some cases, the *rules taught* in the science that *rest upon it*. Because the object retains its own agency, the grammar can address rules to it — and this is exactly what the karma-kartari construction does (the next verses' subject): 'the rice cooks itself', where the object's own operation surfaces and it takes the agent's endings. The doctrine that every karaka is an agent (3.7.18) is not a metaphysical flourish; it is what makes a whole class of Sanskrit constructions statable.

translation: The object, its being-directed having ceased, stands in a portion of its own action; the objecthood withdrawing, it stands in its own agency.
commentary: The karma-kartari construction analysed, and the analysis is exact. Nivrtta-presanam karma: the *object*, its *being-directed having ceased* — presana: the sending, the directing by another agent; when that withdraws — sva-kriya-avayave sthitam: it *stands in a portion of its own action*. Nivartamane karmatve sve kartrtve 'vatisthate: the *objecthood withdrawing*, it stands *in its own agency*. So 'pacyate odanah' — 'the rice cooks' — is not a passive with a suppressed agent; it is the object having reverted to the agency it always had (3.7.54's svatantrata, experienced first) once the cook is out of view. The construction's morphology (the passive endings with an active sense) records exactly this: objecthood withdrawn, agency resumed. And the doctrine that makes it intelligible has been in place since 3.7.18: agency was never the subject's privilege; it was every karaka's, differently attended to.

translation: Those they know as simply other roots — like 'pac' and 'sidhyati'; though there is difference, from likeness of form there is a construction of oneness.
commentary: A deflationary account of the karma-kartari's morphology. Tani dhatv-antarani eva viduh: those they know as *simply other roots* — the 'cooks' of 'the cook cooks' and the 'cooks' of 'the rice cooks' are two verbs, not one verb in two constructions. Paci-sidhyati-vat: *like* 'pac' (to cook, transitive) and 'sidhyati' (to be accomplished, intransitive) — two roots that mean nearly the same and are undeniably two. Bhede 'pi tulya-rupatvat ekatva-parikalpana: though there is *difference*, from *likeness of form* there is a *construction of oneness*. We take them for one verb because they sound alike (2.169's kupa-supa-yupa: phonetic overlap is semantically inert; 2.177's rule: different meaning-behaviour, different base). So the elegant analysis of 3.7.56 is offered a rival: there is no reversion of agency, just two verbs that rhyme. Bhartrhari reports it without deciding — and the report is itself instructive, since the two accounts are 2.57's two darsanas (whole-first or parts-first) meeting in a single conjugation.

translation: In a single portion and in an aggregate of operations, 'pac' and the rest proceed by their own nature, endowed with like form.
commentary: The rival account of 3.7.57 developed. Eka-dese samuhe ca vyaparanam pacadayah: in a *single portion* and in an *aggregate* of operations — whether the verb names one act or a complex of them — svabhavatah pravartante: they proceed *by their own nature*: the roots simply do what they do, without a scheme of role-assignments organizing them. Tulya-rupa-samanvitah: *endowed with like form*. So on this view the verb-family's unity is entirely superficial, and its members are separate items that happen to share a shape and a rough sense. The account is the atomist's (2.52's nominalism: word and sentence as mere designations upon order), and its virtue is economy: no reverting agency, no subordination-hierarchy, just roots. Its cost is that it explains nothing about why the forms cluster as they do — which is why 3.7.59-60 return to the structural analysis.

translation: (59) Subordinating and being-subordinated are understood in the pure root; subordinating and being-subordinated are found in the causative as well. (60) They call it the fifth state; in the causative, in the object-as-agent — when the directing has ceased, the causative affix is stated of the root in its natural meaning.
commentary: The structure of 3.7.20-21 found at every morphological level. (59): nyag-bhavana nyag-bhavanam rudhau suddhe pratiyate — *subordinating* and *being-subordinated* are understood in the *pure root*: even the simple verb contains the hierarchy — something subordinates, something is subordinated (nyak: down, the same root as 3.7.20's nyakkrta). And: nyanta 'pi pratipadyate — they are found in the *causative* as well: the nic-affix adds a level and the same structure repeats. So subordination is not a feature the causative introduces; it is present in the bare root and iterated by the affix (3.7.25's karta kartr-antara-apeksah: an agent depending on another agent). (60): avastham pancamim ahuh nyante tam karma-kartari — they call it the *fifth state*, in the *causative*, in the *object-as-agent*; nivrtta-presanad dhatoh prakrte 'rthe nij ucyate: when the *directing has ceased*, the causative affix is stated of the root *in its natural meaning*. The verse tracks a construction where the causative morphology appears without causative sense, because the directing (3.7.56's presana) has withdrawn — the morphology outliving its semantics, which is 2.229-231's ledger-shuffling in the verb's own paradigm.
contested: 60's avastham pancamim ('the fifth state') is not further identified and the commentators differ over what series it numbers; we translate the phrase and leave the enumeration open

translation: 'Sidhyati' states the meaning of 'pacati' only with the causative affix; that causative-ending stands in the natural meaning of 'pacati'.
commentary: The two roots of 3.7.57 shown to be related after all, and by a morphological argument. Braviti pacater artham sidhyatir na vina nijam: 'sidhyati' (is accomplished) states the meaning of 'pacati' (cooks) *only with the causative affix* — sadhayati, the causative of sidh, means what pacati means: one has to add nic to get from the intransitive to the transitive sense. Sa nyantah pacater artho prakrte vyavatisthate: *that causative-ending* stands in the *natural* meaning of 'pacati' — what the one root needs a causative to say, the other says in its bare form. So pac contains, in its natural meaning, what sidh requires an affix to reach: transitivity is built into pac and added to sidh. This is 3.7.59's doctrine (subordinating and being-subordinated present in the pure root) given its evidence — the hierarchy is inside pac already, which is why it needs no nic, and its being inside is visible precisely by comparison with a root that lacks it.

translation: For some, that operation of Devadatta and the rest which is in a transitive verb — that same is intended, without Devadatta and the rest, in mats and the like.
commentary: The karma-kartari's semantics restated. Kesamcid devadattader vyaparo yah sakarmake: for some, that *operation* of Devadatta and the rest which is in a *transitive* verb — the weaving that Devadatta does. Sa vina devadattadeh katadisu vivaksyate: *that same* is *intended*, *without* Devadatta and the rest, in *mats* and the like. 'The mat weaves itself' intends the very operation that 'Devadatta weaves the mat' intends — the same weaving, with the weaver simply not meant (3.7.56's nivrtta-presana: the directing having ceased). Vivaksyate: *is intended* — the agent's absence is a fact about the speaker's aim, not about the event. So the construction subtracts nothing from the action; it withdraws attention from one of its participants, and the mat, which was an agent all along (3.7.54's svatantrata experienced first), surfaces.

translation: The object, its directing having ceased, is the occasioner of its own agent; when there is connection with another directing, in the causative it is expressed by the 'la'-affix.
commentary: The construction's morphology tracked through a further level. Nivrtta-presanam karma svasya kartuh prayojakam: the *object*, its directing having ceased, is the *occasioner of its own agent* — the mat, freed from the weaver, occasions its own agency (3.7.56 verbatim, and prayojaka is 3.1.25's word for the universal deploying its causes). Presanantara-sambandhe nyante lena abhidhiyate: when there is connection with *another* directing — a second agent arriving above the first — in the *causative* it is expressed by the *la*-affix: the personal endings (Panini's lakaras) carry it. So the sequence is: object directed (accusative), directing withdrawn (object becomes agent), new directing added (causative, and the la-affix marks the new arrangement). Each level of subordination is a morphological layer, and the layers stack exactly as 3.7.59's nyag-bhavana predicted. The grammar is doing hierarchy-bookkeeping, and the hierarchy is 3.7.22's king and soldiers.

translation: Where an object attains agency in 'sadrsa' and the like — there, in the bringing-about of a state, objecthood belongs to the counter-action.
commentary: A further case, and it is technical. Sadrsadisu yat karma kartrtvam pratipadyate: where an *object* attains *agency* in 'sadrsa' (resembling) and the like — verbs of resembling, of becoming-like, where what is compared behaves now as object, now as agent. Apatty-apadane tatra visayatvam pratikriye: there, in the *bringing-about of a state* (apatti: coming-to-be-such), *objecthood* belongs to the *counter-action*. Where the construction expresses a becoming-like, the objecthood shifts to the reciprocal act — resembling being a relation each relatum bears to the other (3.7.19's parents, both agents, roles assigned by intention). The verse extends the karma-kartari analysis to a family of verbs where the agent/object assignment is symmetrical in the world and asymmetrical in every sentence — which is the samuddesa's constant finding since 3.7.19.

translation: And when a word is fetched from somewhere and so construed, there would be object-located-state-hood for the expressers of seeing and the rest.
commentary: A construal-dependent classification. Kutascid ahrtya padam evam ca parikalpane: when a *word* is *fetched from somewhere* and *so construed* — supplied from context, imported into the analysis (2.327's unuttered word supplied by the cognition). Karma-stha-bhavakatvam syad darsanady-abhidhayinam: there would be *object-located-state-hood* for the expressers of *seeing* and the rest. Karma-stha-bhavaka is the grammarians' term for verbs whose state resides in the object rather than the agent: 'he sees the pot' — where does the seeing's result lodge? In the pot (which becomes seen) or in the seer? The classification decides how the verb's affixes behave. And the verse's point is that the classification follows the *construal*: fetch a word from context, construe accordingly, and the verb changes class. Nothing in seeing settles where seeing lodges (3.7.3's buddhi-grounding, at the level of verb-taxonomy).

translation: Where the seeing of a particular is, there the action stands settled; but for others, the settling of the action is made manifest by words alone.
commentary: Two criteria for locating an action. Visesa-darsanam yatra kriya tatra vyavasthita: where the *seeing of a particular* is, *there* the action stands settled — locate the action where its result shows: the cooking is in the rice, because the rice softens; the difference is visible, so the action is there (3.7.51's criterion for the prapya, run in reverse). Kriya-vyavastha tu anyesam sabdaih eva prakasyate: but for *others*, the settling of the action is made manifest *by words alone* — the action is where the grammar says it is, and no observation is relevant. Two schools: one locates actions by their visible effects, the other by the sastra's assignments. And the samuddesa has been arguing throughout that the second is right (3.7.13: the assistance is understood by the case-endings alone) — not because words are authoritative over the world but because the location of an action is not a fact the world contains (3.7.8's nirihesu api bhavesu kalpana-upanibandhanam: even among the striving-less, it rests on construction).

translation: For time, state, road and place, in connection with all intransitive verbs having other actions included within them, objecthood arises.
commentary: How intransitive verbs acquire objects. Kala-bhava-adhva-desanam: for *time*, *state*, *road* and *place* — the four that Panini's rule (1.4.51's kala-adhvanor atyanta-samyoge and its kin) makes accusative even with intransitives: 'he sleeps a month', 'he goes a league'. Antarbhuta-kriyantaraih sarvaih akarmakaih yoge: in connection with *all intransitive* verbs having *other actions included within them* — antarbhuta: absorbed, included (3.3.47's antarbhava, the self-bearing of 'asti'). Karmatvam upajayate: *objecthood arises*. So 'he sleeps a month' has an object because the sleeping has an occupying-of-time included in it, and the month is that included action's object. The intransitive verb is not really intransitive: it contains a second action, and the accusative belongs to that one. The analysis is elegant and it is the samuddesa's method throughout — where the grammar assigns a role that seems unearned, find the included operation that earns it.

translation: Having attained, as it were, the state of being a locus, those — time and the rest — again, in the case of substance-objects, attain a subsequent objecthood of a different level.
commentary: The levels of 3.7.67 sorted. Adharatvam iva praptah te: having attained, *as it were*, the state of being a *locus* — time and place naturally present as loci ('he sleeps *in* the night', locative), and the iva marks that this is their default construal. Punah dravya-karmasu: *again*, in the case of *substance-objects* — where the verb already has a substance for its object ('he studies the book a month'). Kaladayah bhinna-kaksyam yanti karmatvam uttaram: time and the rest attain a *subsequent objecthood* of a *different level* — bhinna-kaksya: of another compartment, another tier. So a sentence can carry two objects at different levels: the book at one, the month at another, and they do not compete because they are objects of different included actions (3.7.67's antarbhuta-kriya). Kaksya — level, compartment — is the samuddesa's term for the hierarchy 3.7.20-23 built, and here it does taxonomic work: objecthood is stratified, and the strata do not collide.

mula_deva: लस्य कर्मणि भावे च निमित्तत्वाय कल्पते ॥ ६९ ॥
mula_iast: lasya karmaṇi bhāve ca nimittatvāya kalpate || 69 ||
translation: Therefore a root joined with those objects, which are not substances, is intransitive; it serves as the occasioning ground for the 'la'-affix in the object and in the state.
commentary: The consequence for morphology. Atah taih karmabhih dhatur yuktah adravyaih akarmakah: *therefore* a root joined with *those objects* — which are *not substances* (time, place, road, state: 3.7.67's four) — is *intransitive*. The verb has an accusative and is still akarmaka, because its accusative is not a substance-object. Lasya karmani bhave ca nimittatvaya kalpate: it serves as the *occasioning ground* for the *la*-affix *in the object and in the state* — that is, it takes the passive/impersonal endings, which is what intransitives do. So the grammar's classification tracks *substance*-objecthood, not accusatives: a verb with a temporal accusative counts as intransitive for affixation. The verse is fine technical bookkeeping and its interest is that the category 'transitive' turns out not to mean 'takes an accusative' — which is exactly what one expects if the karakas are postings assigned per operation (3.7.12) rather than features read off surface case.

translation: Or every unstated object is understood as of a different level; by difference in the indication of the root's meaning, that is not what is most desired.
commentary: The akathita placed. Sarvam va akathitam karma bhinna-kaksyam pratiyate: *every unstated object* is understood as of a *different level* — akathita: 'not stated', Panini's own term (1.4.51: akathitam ca — 'and what is unstated' is called karman), the residue-category that catches every object no other rule assigns. Bhinna-kaksyam: they belong to another tier (3.7.68's stratification). Dhatv-artha-uddesa-bhedena tan na ipsitatamam kila: by difference in the *indication of the root's meaning*, *that* is *not what is most desired* — kila: 'so they say', the reportive particle carrying a shade of irony. So the akathita objects fail Panini's own definition of karman (3.7.45's ipsitatamam, what is most desired), and the grammar catches them in a residue-rule anyway. The verse's kila registers the awkwardness: the definition is by desire, most objects are not desired, and the system works by a catch-all. 3.7.46 already listed the undesired classes; here their status is named.

translation: The principal object is what is stated, which is the occasioner of the action; what is joined with the action for the accomplishing of that is remembered as unstated.
commentary: The two objects distinguished by office. Pradhana-karma kathitam: the *principal object* is what is *stated* — yat kriyayah prayojakam: which is the *occasioner of the action*: the object the action is *for*, whose desirability drives the whole undertaking (3.7.45's ipsitatama, now confined to this one). Tat-siddhaye kriya-yuktam anyat tu akathitam smrtam: what is joined with the action *for the accomplishing of that* is remembered as *unstated*. So in 'he milks the cow of milk' (the classic double-accusative), the milk is the principal — it is what he wants — and the cow is akathita: joined to the action in service of getting the milk. The distinction is teleological through and through: which object is principal depends on which is wanted, and the other is machinery. This is 2.113's prayojana-doctrine (sentence-meaning is purpose) reaching down into the accusative case.

translation: In 'nayati' and the like, as in 'duhi' and the rest, objecthood rests on what is not stated; but when the finite verb is not employed, the remainder is held to obtain, by rule.
commentary: The double-object verbs, and their two families. Duhy-adi-vat nayaty-adau karmatvam akatha-srayam: in *'nayati'* (leads) and the like, as in *'duhi'* (milks) and the rest, *objecthood* rests on *what is not stated* — both families take two accusatives, and in both the second is akathita (3.7.70-71). Akhyata-anupayoge tu niyamat sesa isyate: but when the *finite verb is not employed* — in nominal constructions, participles, compounds, where no finite verb governs — *the remainder* is held to obtain, *by rule*: the genitive, Panini's sesa-case (3.7.44: sasthi sese), takes over. So 'he milks the cow of milk' has two accusatives, and 'the milking of the cow' has a genitive: withdraw the finite verb and the karaka-relations lapse into the residue-case. This is 2.197-198's doctrine exactly — the genitive expresses relations whose generating action has withdrawn — now shown operating in the double-object constructions.

translation: For 'duhi' and the rest, whose causative-meaning is included within, objecthood is established by the prior rule as though they ended in the causative; and the restriction is likewise as for causative-ending forms.
commentary: The double-object verbs explained by a hidden causative. Antarbhuta-nij-arthanam duhy-adinam: of *'duhi'* and the rest, whose *causative-meaning is included within* — antarbhuta again (3.7.67's included actions): 'milks' contains a 'causes-to-flow'; the causative is in the root's own meaning without any affix. Nijantavat siddham purvena karmatvam: *objecthood* is *established by the prior rule* *as though they ended in the causative* — the grammar treats them like causatives because they are causatives semantically, and causatives take two objects (the one caused, and the thing done). Nijantam niyamas tatha: and the *restriction* is likewise as for causative-ending forms. So the double accusative of 'duhi' is not an idiosyncrasy: it is the ordinary causative pattern, with the causative buried in the root. This completes 3.7.61's finding (pac contains what sidh needs a nic to say) — the roots' meanings carry morphology invisibly, and the case-assignments reveal it.

translation: As for the instrument, in its own compartment, there is no resort to excellence — so for the object too, in its own compartment, there would be no surpassing.
commentary: An objection to the ipsitatama-definition, from the grammar's own logic. Karanasya sva-kaksyayam na prakarsa-asrayo yatha: as for the *instrument*, in its *own compartment*, there is no resort to *excellence* — one instrument is not more an instrument than another; the karaka-roles do not come in degrees. Karmano 'pi sva-kaksyayam na syad atisayas tatha: so for the *object* too, in its own compartment, there would be *no surpassing*. But Panini's definition is ipsitatamam — the *most* desired: a superlative, which requires degrees. So the definition demands a gradation the category cannot support: if objecthood does not admit of more and less, 'most desired' picks out nothing. The objection is sharp, and it targets the definition that 3.7.45, 3.7.70 and 3.7.71 have all leaned on. The answer follows.

translation: But since, for the object, when the state of being wished-to-be-attained is resorted to, a surpassing is present — therefore it is resorted to; hence there is an utter difference from the prior object.
commentary: The answer to 3.7.74, and it relocates the gradation. Karmanas tu aptum istatve asrite atisayo yatah: for the *object*, when the state of *being wished-to-be-attained* is *resorted to*, a *surpassing* is *present* — the degrees are not in objecthood but in *desiredness*: one may want the milk more than the cow, and that is a real gradation. Asriyate tatah: *therefore* it is resorted to — the definition invokes desire precisely because desire admits of degrees where objecthood does not. Atyantam bhedah purvena karmana: hence there is an *utter difference* from the *prior* object — the ipsitatama (the milk) differs in kind from the akathita (the cow), because the first is wanted most and the second is machinery (3.7.71). So 3.7.74's objection is granted and disarmed: objecthood has no degrees, desire does, and the superlative in Panini's definition qualifies the desire. The definition survives by being read exactly.

mula_deva: णिजन्ते च यथा कर्ता सक्रियः सन् प्रयुज्यते ।
mula_iast: ṇijante ca yathā kartā sakriyaḥ san prayujyate |
translation: And as, in a causative-ending form, an agent possessing action is employed — so in 'nayati' and the like, an agent devoid of action is also employed.
commentary: The parallel completed, and its second half is the odd one. Nijante ca yatha karta sakriyah san prayujyate: in a *causative-ending* form, an agent *possessing action* is employed — in 'he makes Devadatta cook', Devadatta is an agent and he is cooking: the caused agent acts. Nayaty-adau tatha karta niskriyo 'pi prayujyate: in *'nayati'* (leads) and the like, an agent *devoid of action* is *also* employed — 'he leads the goat to the village': the goat is grammatically an agent of a sort (it is the one led, and the leading is its going), and it does nothing; it is dragged. Niskriya: without action (3.7.8's niriha, without striving). So the pattern that 3.7.73 established — the double-object verbs behaving as causatives — extends to cases where the 'caused agent' has no agency whatever. The grammar constructs an agent out of the inert, exactly as 3.7.8 said it does: independence and dependence rest upon construction, even among things that are without striving.

mula_deva: भेदवाक्यं तु यत् ण्यन्ते नीदुहितप्रकृतौ च यत् ।
mula_iast: bhedavākyaṃ tu yat ṇyante nīduhitaprakṛtau ca yat |
translation: But the analytic sentence which is used for a causative-ending form, and that which is used for the base of 'ni' and 'duh' — from its being another word, there is no contact of it with the root.
commentary: The paraphrase disowned. Bheda-vakyam tu yat nyante: the *analytic sentence* used for a *causative-ending* form — the gloss 'he causes X to cook' by which the grammar explains 'pacayati'; ni-duhita-prakrtau ca yat: and that used for the *base* of 'ni' and 'duh' (the double-object verbs of 3.7.72-73). Sabdantaratvat na eva asti samsparsah tasya dhatuna: *from its being another word*, there is *no contact* of it with the *root*. The paraphrase is a different word entirely and touches the root not at all. This is 2.35cd's doctrine (the vrtti-analysis employing a verb-like element for what stands otherwise, the paraphrase faithful to the meaning and false to the form) and 2.37's (the resemblance constructed for the sake of reaching the meaning). So the causative analyses of 3.7.61-76, however illuminating, are glosses: they explain by substituting, and what they substitute has no contact with what it explains. The samuddesa audits its own method at the point where it has leaned hardest on it.

translation: Just as one and the same ablative is exhibited in the science by way of difference — so one and the same object too is expounded by way of difference.
commentary: The taxonomies placed. Yatha eva ekam apadanam sastre bhedena darsitam: as *one and the same* ablative is exhibited in the *science* by way of *difference* — Panini divides apadana into kinds (the moving source, the fixed source, the source of fear, the source of learning) and it is one karaka throughout. Tatha ekam eva karma api bhedena pratipaditam: so *one and the same* object too is *expounded* by way of *difference* — the nirvartya, vikarya and prapya of 3.7.45 are one karman, divided for exposition. So the object's three-fold taxonomy is a teaching-device, not a discovery of three objecthoods (2.176's instruction by the general for brevity's sake; 3.7.36's unlimited-seeming capacities reducing to six). The verse tidies the samuddesa's own apparatus: everything it has distinguished about the karman is distinction *in the science*, and the karman is one.

mula_deva: निर्वर्त्यो वा विकार्यो वा प्राप्यो वा साधनाश्रयः ।
mula_iast: nirvartyo vā vikāryo vā prāpyo vā sādhanāśrayaḥ |
translation: Whether what-is-to-be-produced, or what-is-to-be-modified, or what-is-to-be-reached — the substratum of the means, since actions alone are what is to be accomplished, is expressed as having an accomplished form.
commentary: The object's grammatical form explained. Nirvartyo va vikaryo va prapyo va sadhana-asrayah: whatever kind it is, the object is the *substratum of the means*. Kriyanam eva sadhyatvat: since *actions alone* are *what is to be accomplished* — only actions are sadhya; nothing else is ever brought about (3.7.1's definition: the means is capacity in the completing of *actions*). Siddha-rupo 'bhidhiyate: it is expressed as having an *accomplished* form. So the object, however unaccomplished it may be in fact (the mat not yet woven, 3.7.7's janyamana karman), is grammatically *siddha* — presented as a standing thing, a noun, complete. The sadhya/siddha distinction is the grammar's deepest: verbs present the to-be-accomplished, nouns the accomplished, and every participant in an action is nominally presented as though finished. This is why 3.7.7 needed buddhi to hold the unmade mat in place: the grammar has no way to present an object as unfinished.

translation: As, in the case of things not conducive, a desire of the agent arises from greed — so, in the case of poison and the like, it proceeds from fear and the rest.
commentary: The ipsitatama-definition rescued at its hardest case. 3.7.46 listed the undesired objects, and poison is the standing example: 'he eats poison' has an object that nobody most-desires. The answer: ahitesu yatha laulyat kartur iccha upajayate — in the case of things *not conducive*, a *desire* of the agent arises *from greed*: the glutton eats what harms him, and there *is* a desire — a bad one. Visadisu bhayadibhyah tatha eva asau pravartate: in the case of *poison* and the like, *that* (desire) proceeds *from fear* and the rest. The suicide's desire for poison comes from fear; the desire is real, its source is dread. So the undesired objects are desired after all, under desires the agent would disown — and the definition holds, at the cost of making 'most desired' cover what one takes in terror. It is an honest rescue: it saves the rule and shows what the rule then means.

translation: (81) Where the capacity of a substance, in the principal and the other, is separately in the two actions — there the capacity resting on the subordinate follows the principal. (82) The capacity whose domain is the principal is expressed by the affix; when it is in the subordinate, then, though unstated, it likewise shines forth.
commentary: How karaka-roles propagate between clauses. (81): where a substance has a capacity in *two* actions — one principal, one subordinate — guna-asraya saktih pradhanam anurudhyate: the capacity resting on the *subordinate* *follows the principal*: the role assigned in the main clause governs the role in the subordinate one. (82) gives the morphology: pradhana-visaya saktih pratyayena abhidhiyate — the capacity whose domain is the *principal* is *expressed by the affix* (the finite verb's endings carry it); yada gune tada tadvat anukta api prakasate — when it is in the *subordinate*, then, *though unstated*, it *likewise shines forth*: the subordinate clause's role is not expressed and is understood, carried over from the principal. This is why 'having cooked, he eats' needs no agent for the cooking: the eater's agency propagates backward. The mechanism is 2.305's anupatta (what is not taken up by the word is nonetheless understood) operating across clause-boundaries.

translation: The object not stated for 'paci', in a gerund expressing the state — for 'bhuji', though another capacity is stated, that property of it shines forth.
commentary: The propagation of 3.7.81-82 in a worked case. Pacau anuktam yat karma: the *object not stated* for 'paci' (cooks) — in 'pakva bhunkte', 'having cooked, he eats', the cooking's object is not said. Ktvante bhava-abhidhayini: in a *gerund* expressing the *state* — the ktva-affix, which forms the gerund and expresses the action without an agent of its own. Bhujau sakty-antare 'pi ukte: for 'bhuji' (eats), *though another capacity is stated* — the eating has its own object, said. Tat tad-dharmah prakasate: *that property* of it *shines forth* — the unstated cooking-object is understood as the same as the stated eating-object. One cooks what one eats, and no word says so. The verse shows the propagation working across a difference of role (the rice is the cooking's nirvartya and the eating's prapya) — and it works because the roles ride on the buddhi's holding of the situation (3.7.3), not on the words.

mula_deva: इषेश्च गमिसंस्पर्शाद् ग्रामे यो लो विधीयते ।
mula_iast: iṣeśca gamisaṃsparśād grāme yo lo vidhīyate |
translation: And the 'la'-affix which is prescribed for 'isi', from its contact with 'gami', in 'the village' — there, by 'usi' alone, a separation of the going-object is effected.
commentary: A worked case of case-assignment under verb-contact. The construction is 'gramam icchati' — 'he wants the village', meaning he wants to *go* to the village: iseh gami-samsparsat, from *'isi'* (wanting) having *contact with* *'gami'* (going) — the wanting has a going absorbed in it (3.7.67's antarbhuta-kriyantara), and the village is the *going's* object, taking the accusative that 'icchati' alone could not license. Tatra usina eva nirbhogah kriyate gati-karmanah: there, by *'usi'* alone, a *separation* of the *going-object* is effected. The technical detail is obscure in transmission; the principle is not. When two actions fuse in one verb, the case-assignments come from the buried one — which is 3.7.73's finding about 'duhi' (the double accusative coming from an included causative) applied to a different fusion.
contested: the verse's technical apparatus (the la-affix, usi, nirbhoga) is compressed to the edge of recoverability and the transcription may be at fault; we translate the evident structure and note that the specific Paninian derivation intended is not confidently reconstructible

translation: In 'having cooked, it is eaten' — for some, 'pacati' does not require the rice; that one is understood by inference.
commentary: The propagation of 3.7.83 disputed. Paktva bhujyate iti atra: in 'having cooked, it is eaten'. Kesamcid na vyapeksate odanam pacatih: for *some*, 'pacati' does *not require* the rice — the cooking-verb has no unstated object waiting to be supplied; it is simply a cooking. So 'apeksa', the expectancy that 3.7.83 relied on, is denied. Sa asau anumanat pratiyate: *that one* is understood *by inference* — the rice arrives, but by inference from the situation rather than by any grammatical expectancy. The difference matters: on 3.7.83's account the object is *carried over* by the construction; on this account it is *inferred* by the hearer, and the construction is silent. This is 2.149's mechanism (the corrupt form conveying by inference, through a recovery so practiced it vanishes) offered as a rival to grammatical propagation — and the two are hard to distinguish, since both deliver the rice and neither shows its working.

translation: There, for 'abhi-nivis', the object which is expressed in the finite verb — in the gerund, even where there is locus-hood, they do not desire the locative there.
commentary: A case where the propagation is grammatically visible. The verb abhi-nivis (to enter into, to settle upon) takes its object in the accusative by a special rule of Panini's (1.4.47: abhinivisas ca — the locus of abhinivis is called karman, not adhikarana). Tatra abhinivisau karma yat tinante 'bhidhiyate: the *object* which is expressed in the *finite verb*. Ktvante adhikaranatve 'pi na tatra icchanti saptamim: in the *gerund*, *even where there is locus-hood*, they do *not desire the locative* there. So the special accusative propagates into the gerund: even though the thing entered is by nature a locus, and gerunds might be expected to revert to the natural case, the rule's assignment carries across. The verse is evidence for 3.7.81-82's propagation-doctrine against 3.7.85's inference-account: here the carried-over role is *marked*, by the refusal of a case the situation would otherwise take.

translation: That object whose substratum is accomplished, but which is not yet augmented by the attaining — being differentiated by the coming-about of the state of being edible and the like — that is what is desired.
commentary: The ipsitatama located with precision. Yan nirvrtta-asrayam karma: that object whose *substratum is accomplished* — the thing is there; the rice exists. Prapteh apracitam punah: but which is *not yet augmented by the attaining* — it has not yet been got; the eating has not happened. Bhaksyadi-visaya-apattya bhidyamanam: being *differentiated* by the *coming-about* of the state of being *edible* and the like — apatti: coming-to-be-such (3.7.64's term). Tad ipsitam: *that* is what is *desired*. So the desired object is the existing thing not yet attained, taken under the aspect it will have when attained: the rice, not yet eaten, wanted *as edible*. Every clause is doing work: existing (else there is nothing to want), unattained (else wanting is idle: 3.3.63-64's already-pregnant woman), and aspectually differentiated (one wants it *as* something). The definition by desire, which 3.7.74 attacked and 3.7.75 rescued, is here given its exact content.

translation: From the root's operating in another meaning; from the object's being included within by the root's meaning; from established usage; from non-intention — the action becomes objectless.
commentary: The four ways a transitive verb goes intransitive, and they are a complete taxonomy. Dhatoh arthantare vrtteh: from the *root's operating in another meaning* — the verb used in a sense that has no object ('he sees' meaning he understands). Dhatv-arthena upasangrahat karmanah: from the *object's being included within* by the root's meaning — the object absorbed into the verb, so that nothing is left to state (3.3.47's antarbhava: the self-bearing of 'asti', whence it takes no accusative). Prasiddheh: from *established usage* — the verb simply is not used with an object, whatever its sense allows (2.253's prasiddhi: currency deciding). Avivaksatah: from *non-intention* — the speaker does not mean to bring an object into view, and there is none (3.7.62's vina devadattadeh: the agent unmeant). Karmano akarmika kriya: the action becomes *objectless*. Four routes — semantic, absorptive, conventional, intentional — and only the first two are about the verb. The other two are about usage and the speaker, which is the samuddesa's constant finding.

mula_deva: भेदा य एते चत्वारः सामान्येन प्रदर्शिताः ।
mula_iast: bhedā ya ete catvāraḥ sāmānyena pradarśitāḥ |
translation: These four differences that have been exhibited in general are again differentiated manifoldly, by difference of occasioning ground and the rest.
commentary: The taxonomy's status. Bhedah ya ete catvarah samanyena pradarsitah: these *four differences* that have been exhibited *in general* — 3.7.88's four routes to intransitivity. Te nimittadi-bhedena bhidyante bahudha punah: they are *again* *differentiated manifoldly*, by *difference of occasioning ground* and the rest. The four are not a bottom; each subdivides by nimitta, and the subdivision has no evident end (3.7.36's aparimita iva: seen as though unlimited, before the reduction to six). So the samuddesa reports its own taxonomies as provisional — general exhibitions that further analysis will multiply — which is 3.4.1's charter (kecit padarthah vaksyante samksepena yatha-agamam: certain word-meanings, in brief, according to tradition) honoured in the working.

mula_deva: विवक्ष्यते यदा तत्र करणत्वं तदा स्मृतम् ॥ ९० ॥
mula_iast: vivakṣyate yadā tatra karaṇatvaṃ tadā smṛtam || 90 ||
translation: When the complete accomplishment of the action is intended as immediately following upon whose operation — then instrument-hood is remembered there.
commentary: The instrument defined, and the definition is temporal. Kriyayah parinispattih yad-vyaparad anantaram: the *complete accomplishment* of the action *immediately following* upon whose *operation* — the karana is the participant whose contribution is the *last* one, after which the act is done: the axe's stroke, and the tree is felled. Vivaksyate yada: when it is *intended* so. Tatra karanatvam tada smrtam: *then* instrument-hood is remembered *there*. This is Panini's own definition (1.4.42: sadhakatamam karanam, the most effective means) recast: what makes something 'most effective' is that the accomplishment follows immediately on it. And vivaksyate is the samuddesa's constant: the immediacy is a matter of what the speaker construes as immediately-prior, not of any causal fact. The verse sets up 3.7.91, which says this outright.

mula_deva: स्थाल्या पच्यत इत्येषा विवक्षा दृश्यते यतः ॥ ९१ ॥
mula_iast: sthālyā pacyata ityeṣā vivakṣā dṛśyate yataḥ || 91 ||
translation: In reality it is not to be specified; for no thing stands settled — since this intention is seen: 'it is cooked with a pot'.
commentary: The samuddesa's most direct statement of its thesis, and it is delivered as a shrug. Vastutah tad anirdesyam: *in reality* it is *not to be specified* — there is no fact about which participant is the instrument. Na hi vastu vyavasthitam: for *no thing stands settled* — nothing in the world is an instrument (3.6.14's things without place, parts, motion, support; 3.7.8's nirihesu api bhavesu kalpana-upanibandhanam). And the evidence, which is a single ordinary sentence: sthalya pacyate iti esa vivaksa drsyate yatah — since *this intention* is *seen*: '*it is cooked with a pot*'. The pot. Not the fire, not the heat, not the cook's stirring — the *pot*, which does nothing but sit there, is Sanskrit's perfectly ordinary instrument of cooking. If the pot can be the karana, then the karana is not the most effective cause, and 3.7.90's definition tracks nothing but what the speaker chose to put in the instrumental. Sthalya pacyate is the samuddesa's kupa-supa-yupa: one everyday phrase, and the realist account of the karakas is finished.

translation: But in the instruments, they undergo refinements again and again, and particular applications, for the accomplishing of the principal.
commentary: The instruments' ritual dimension. Karanesu tu samskaran labhante punah punah: in the *instruments*, they undergo *refinements* *again and again* — samskara: the preparation, purification, making-fit that the rite performs on its implements (1.146's therapeutic triad; 3.3.35's samskara of the object by the word). Viniyoga-visesan ca: and *particular applications* — viniyoga, the deployment to a ritual station (2.258's mantras obtaining difference by their application). Pradhanasya prasiddhaye: for the *accomplishing of the principal*. So instruments are worked on, repeatedly, and assigned to stations, all in service of the rite's principal act.
