The verse returns the abstract karana-analysis to the sacrificial ground it came from: the ladle is consecrated, and the consecrating is what makes it the instrument — which is 3.7.91's thesis in ritual dress. Nothing is an instrument; things are *made* instruments, and in the rite the making is visible and liturgical.

translation: And among the instruments, in their own compartments, no excellence exists; but the state of possessing a surpassing, resorted to from outside, is the mark there.
commentary: The objection of 3.7.74 revisited for the instrument, and answered as the object's was. Sva-kaksyasu prakarsah ca karananam na vidyate: among the *instruments*, in their *own compartments*, no *excellence* exists — one instrument is not more an instrument than another; instrument-hood does not grade (3.7.74's premise, granted). Asrita-atisayatvam tu paratah tatra laksanam: but the state of *possessing a surpassing*, resorted to *from outside*, is the *mark* there. Panini's definition is sadhakatamam — the *most* effective — and the superlative must qualify something. It qualifies not instrument-hood but effectiveness, which is conferred paratah, from outside: by the action's needs, by the speaker's construal. So the karana's definition works exactly as the karman's did (3.7.75: objecthood has no degrees, desire does): the superlative attaches to a gradable property that is not the role itself.

translation: Even in their independence, the employers assist only from a distance; for the operation of all is separated by the instrument.
commentary: The agent's remoteness, and it explains the whole hierarchy. Svatantrye 'pi prayoktarah arad eva upakurvate: even in their *independence*, the *employers* — the agents, svatantra by Panini's definition (1.4.54) — assist *only from a distance*: arat, from afar (2.82's arad visesaka, the qualifier from afar). The agent does not touch the work. Karanena hi sarvesam vyaparo vyavadhiyate: for the *operation of all* is *separated by the instrument* — the instrument stands between every agent and every act. The woodsman does not fell the tree; the axe does, and he swings the axe. So agency is constitutively mediated: to be an agent is to act *through*, and what one acts through is the karana. This is 3.7.22's king and soldiers given its mechanism — the king obtains victory precisely because he does not fight — and it explains why 3.7.90 could define the instrument as what the accomplishment immediately follows: the instrument is where the action touches the world, and the agent is always one remove back.

mula_deva: सिद्धौ सत्यां हि सामान्यं साधकत्वं प्रकृष्यते ॥ ९५ ॥
mula_iast: siddhau satyāṃ hi sāmānyaṃ sādhakatvaṃ prakṛṣyate || 95 ||
translation: In the accomplishing of the action there is excellence; but in the agent there is only subordination. For when the accomplishment exists, the general means-hood is surpassed.
commentary: The hierarchy's paradox, stated exactly. Kriya-siddhau prakarso va: in the *accomplishing of the action* there is *excellence* — the instrument, being nearest the accomplishment (3.7.90), has the surpassing. Nyag-bhavas tv eva kartari: but in the *agent* there is *only subordination* — nyag-bhava: the being-pressed-down (3.7.59's nyag-bhavana). The agent is *subordinated*? The independent one, the svatantra? Yes — and the reason: siddhau satyam hi samanyam sadhakatvam prakrsyate — when the *accomplishment* exists, the *general means-hood* is *surpassed*. Measured against the accomplishment, the agent's contribution is the most distant and the least effective (3.7.94's arat), so in the ranking-by-effectiveness he comes last. The grammar's most independent karaka is efficacy's least. This is the samuddesa's finest structural irony, and it is not a paradox: independence and effectiveness are different scales, and the karta tops one by placing last on the other.

mula_deva: अस्यादीनां तु कर्तृत्वे तैक्ष्ण्यादि करणं विदुः ।
mula_iast: asyādīnāṃ tu kartṛtve taikṣṇyādi karaṇaṃ viduḥ |
translation: But in the agency of a sword and the like, they know sharpness and the rest to be the instrument; and in the independence of sharpness and the rest, the self stands two-fold.
commentary: The regress of instruments, and it does not terminate. Asy-adinam tu kartrtve taiksnyadi karanam viduh: in the *agency* of a *sword* and the like — when we say 'the sword cuts', the sword is agent — *sharpness* and the rest are the *instrument*: the sword cuts *by* its sharpness (3.7.31's four properties). So the sword, promoted to agent, needs an instrument of its own, and its own property serves. Taiksnyadinam svatantratve dvaidha atma vyavatisthate: and in the *independence* of sharpness and the rest — promote the sharpness to agent ('the sharpness cuts') — the *self* stands *two-fold*: it must now be both agent and (through some further property) instrument. The regress is 3.7.20-21's nesting run downward without a floor: every agent needs an instrument, every instrument can be promoted to agent, and each promotion generates a new instrument. Dvaidha atma — the self standing two-fold — names the splitting that each step requires.

translation: Even when there is a difference of self, one and the same object stands thus; from its resting on that, agency, though there is difference, is therefore what annuls.
commentary: The regress of 3.7.96 stopped. Atma-bhede 'pi sati evam ekah arthah sa tatha sthitah: even when there is a *difference of self* — even granting the splitting — *one and the same* object stands thus: the sword and its sharpness are not two things; the splitting was analytical (3.7.15's vyatirekam upasritya: the construction resorting to separation, whatever the metaphysics). Tad-asrayatvad bhede 'pi kartrtvam badhakam tatah: from its *resting on that*, *agency*, though there is difference, is *therefore what annuls*. Agency wins: where sharpness and sword are one thing, the agency-assignment overrides the instrument-assignment, and the regress stops because there is nothing further to promote. Badhaka — annulling: the badha-machinery (2.66, 2.73's hierarchy of evidences) adjudicating between two karaka-assignments to one thing. So the regress is real as analysis and idle as ontology, and the grammar stops it by ranking.

translation: And as instrument-hood is understood from proximity — just so, even in the absence of proximity, it is understood from the accomplishing of the action.
commentary: The instrument's criterion loosened. Yatha ca sannidhanena karanatvam pratiyate: as instrument-hood is understood from *proximity* — the axe in hand, the pot on the fire (3.7.91's sthalya pacyate). Tatha eva asannidhane 'pi kriya-siddheh pratiyate: *just so*, even in the *absence of proximity*, it is understood from the *accomplishing* of the action. Distant things are instruments too: one conquers by an army not present, cooks by a fire one is not touching. So proximity was never the criterion; the accomplishment's dependence was (3.7.90's parinispattih yad-vyaparad anantaram — and 'immediately following' is a matter of construal, not of distance). The verse removes the last physical residue from the karana's definition: instrument-hood is a functional and construal-relative posting, and nothing about nearness survives in it.

translation: From the coming-about of what is little, or from its not ceasing — they declare the establishing of instrument-hood for 'little' and the like.
commentary: A hard case: adverbial instrumentals. Stokasya va abhinirvrtteh anivrtteh ca tasya va: from the *coming-about* of what is *little*, or from its *not ceasing*. The construction is 'stokena mukta' — 'released by a little', 'he escaped by a hair' — where 'little' takes the instrumental and is not an instrument in any recognizable sense: no thing is being used. Prasiddhim karanatvasya stokadina pracaksate: they declare the *establishing* of instrument-hood for *'little'* and the like. Two accounts are offered, and both are functional: the littleness *came about* (and its coming-about is what the escape followed on: 3.7.90's criterion), or it *did not cease* (and its persisting is what the escape depended on). Either way, the instrumental marks what the accomplishment hung on — which in this case is a quantity, not a thing. The verse shows the karana-slot accepting what is not remotely an instrument, because the slot tracks dependence and not instrumentality.

translation: It is distinguished by the difference and the non-difference of the properties from what possesses them — as it is differentiated by a difference in the delimitation of the action's limit.
commentary: The general principle behind 3.7.99. Dharmanam tadvata bhedad abhedac ca visisyate: it is *distinguished* by the *difference* and the *non-difference* of the *properties* from *what possesses them* — a property may be taken as one with its bearer or apart from it (3.7.96-97's sword and sharpness), and the karaka-assignment follows the taking. Kriya-avaccheda-visesad bhidyate yatha: *as* it is differentiated by a difference in the *delimitation of the action's limit* — where one draws the action's boundary decides what falls inside it as participant and what stands outside as its instrument. So two variables govern every karaka-assignment: how one takes the property's relation to its bearer, and where one cuts the action. Both are construals (3.7.3's buddhy-avastha-nibandhanah), and between them they generate the whole taxonomy the samuddesa has been cataloguing.

translation: (101) From the obtaining of capacity previously from another; from the bringing-about of subordination; from their operating in dependence on that; from the turning-back of those who have set out — (102) from the non-observation of a substitute; and from its being seen in separation — even where the assistance is from a distance, independence is said of the agent.
commentary: Six marks of agency, gathered into a definition. The agent is identified by: (i) prag anyatah sakti-labhat — the others *obtaining capacity previously from* him (he empowers them); (ii) nyag-bhava-apadanat — his *bringing about their subordination* (3.7.20's nyakkrta); (iii) tad-adhina-pravrttitvat — their *operating in dependence* on him; (iv) pravrttanam nivartanat — his *turning back those who have set out* (he can stop what he started: the mark of control); (v) adrstatvat pratinidheh — the *non-observation of a substitute* for him (agents are not substitutable: 3.1.78's test, which discriminates what stands near the capacity from what stands at a remove); (vi) prativeke ca darsanat — his *being seen in separation* (he is identifiable apart from the act). And the conclusion: arad api upakaritve svatantryam kartur ucyate — *even where the assistance is from a distance* (3.7.94's arat), *independence* is said of the *agent*. Six operational marks, no metaphysics: the karta is whoever exhibits these, and 3.7.103 will say what that means.

mula_deva: धर्मैरभ्युदितैः शब्दे नियमो न तु वस्तुनि ।
mula_iast: dharmairabhyuditaiḥ śabde niyamo na tu vastuni |
translation: By the properties that have arisen, the restriction is in the word, not in the thing; when the agent's property is intended, the agent is understood from the word.
commentary: The six marks of 3.7.101-102 placed, and the placing is the samuddesa's thesis. Dharmaih abhyuditaih sabde niyamah na tu vastuni: by the *properties* that have *arisen*, the *restriction* is *in the word*, *not in the thing*. The six marks do not identify a thing that is the agent; they govern when the *word* takes agent-morphology. Kartur dharma-vivaksayam sabdat karta pratiyate: when the *agent's property* is *intended*, the *agent* is *understood from the word*. So agency is a fact about what is intended and expressed, and the marks are the conditions under which the expression is licensed. Na tu vastuni — not in the thing: the phrase is 3.7.91's (vastutah tad anirdesyam, na hi vastu vyavasthitam) applied now to the karta as it was there to the karana. Neither end of the karaka-system rests on anything.

translation: And when a difference is constructed, by states of the intellect, for one and the same thing — objecthood, instrument-hood and agency arise.
commentary: The samuddesa's culminating statement, and it gathers a hundred verses. Ekasya buddhy-avasthabhih bhede ca parikalpite: when a *difference* is *constructed*, by *states of the intellect*, for *one and the same thing* — one thing, and the buddhi's states carve it. Karmatvam karanatvam ca kartrtvam ca upajayate: *objecthood*, *instrument-hood* and *agency* *arise*. All three, from one thing, by cognitive states. This is 3.7.3 (the practice of means is grounded in a state of the intellect) now demonstrated rather than asserted, and it explains everything the samuddesa has found: why the same cooking is object, instrument and sadhya at once (2.429); why both parents are agents and the roles are assigned by intention (3.7.19); why the pot is the instrument of cooking (3.7.91). Buddhy-avastha — states of the intellect: not arbitrary, not private, but *states*, which come and go and can be occupied. The karaka-system is a map of the positions a mind can take up toward an event.

translation: The prior non-existence before the arising is grounded in a state of the intellect; not distinguished from another existent thing, it becomes the agent of the birth.
commentary: The doctrine of 3.7.104 applied to its hardest case: origination. Utpatteh prag asad-bhavah buddhy-avastha-nibandhanah: the *prior non-existence* before the *arising* is *grounded in a state of the intellect* — the pot's not-yet-being is a way the mind holds the situation, not a condition the pot is in (3.3.79's finding: 'the prior state of a non-thing' is not a describable condition; neither the priority nor the state exists in a non-thing). Avisistah sata anyena karta bhavati janmanah: *not distinguished* from another *existent* thing, it *becomes the agent of the birth*. So 'the pot comes to be' has the pot as agent — and the pot, at that moment, is a buddhi-posting indistinguishable from any existing thing, because buddhi does not distinguish them (3.3.86's mind that will not halt in non-being). This is 3.3.43-48's dilemma of origination dissolved by the karaka-analysis: nothing is born, and the birth's agent is a state of the intellect, and the grammar conjugates it.

translation: Or, as a cause stands in the form of the effect — then, having obtained the word 'effect', it thereupon arises as the effect.
commentary: The alternative account of origination. Karanam karya-bhavena yatha va vyavatisthate: *or*, as a *cause* stands in the *form of the effect* — the clay standing as the pot, the satkarya-vadin's picture (3.7.49's sat va janmana yat prakasyate). Karya-sabdam tada labdhva karyatvena atha jayate: *then*, *having obtained the word 'effect'*, it *thereupon arises as the effect*. The order is the verse's shock: the thing obtains the *word* first, and *then* arises as the effect. Naming is prior to being. This is not a slip — it is 3.1.6's doctrine (every word expresses its own universal first, and superimposes it on the object) applied to origination: what makes the clay into a pot is its receiving the word 'pot', and the arising follows the naming. The Padakanda's constructivism reaches its limit here, and the limit is that things come to be by being called.

translation: As the snake's becoming-coiled, and the wholeness of things dispersed — just so, some declare, is the birth-form of existent things.
commentary: Two similes for a birth that is no birth. Yatha aheh kundali-bhavah: as the *snake's becoming-coiled* — the snake does not acquire a new thing by coiling; it arranges itself, and 'the coil' comes to be without anything coming to be. Vyagranam ca samagrata: and the *wholeness* of things *dispersed* — scattered things gathered, and the 'whole' arises without any new entity: the heap is not a further thing beside the grains. Tatha eva janma-rupatvam satam eke pracaksate: *just so*, some declare, is the *birth-form* of *existent* things. So birth is rearrangement and gathering — configuration, not creation. The similes are chosen for cases where everyone already sees that the 'new thing' is a way of taking the old: nobody thinks a coil was manufactured. And they complete 3.3.61's finding (non-being is not born as being; the two are constructions, not other than one self) with a picture of what birth then *is*.

translation: The effect which proceeds from its causes, having a divided womb — its own universal also stands established in the form of the individual.
commentary: The effect's universal, and the verse closes the movement by returning to the Jati-samuddesa. Vibhakta-yoni yat karyam karanebhyah pravartate: the *effect* which proceeds *from its causes*, having a *divided womb* — its origin is in several causes, each contributing (yoni: 3.1.3's womb-vocabulary, 2.58's samhita as the womb of the words). Sva jatih vyakti-rupena tasya api vyavatisthate: *its own universal* also stands established *in the form of the individual*. So the effect, however divided its origin, has its own universal, and that universal stands *as* the individual — the pot's pot-hood is not a further item in the pot but the pot's own standing (3.1.29's face manifested by water: the universal itself is what is expressed). This connects the two ends of the Padakanda: the universals of samuddesa 1 and the causal analysis of samuddesa 7 meet in the effect, whose being-a-thing-of-a-kind is the same fact as its having been made.

mula_deva: भावष्वेव पदन्यासः प्रज्ञाया वाच एव वा ।
mula_iast: bhāvaṣveva padanyāsaḥ prajñāyā vāca eva vā |
translation: The setting-down of a foot is only among existent things — whether of cognition or of speech; even 'it does not exist' does not exist in a footless place, nor is the non-existent thereby differentiated.
commentary: The footless station (3.3.80's apada-sthana) recalled and applied to the karaka-system. Bhavesu eva pada-nyasah: the *setting-down of a foot* is only among *existent* things — prajnaya vacah eva va: whether of *cognition* or of *speech*: neither thought nor word can get a footing where nothing is (3.3.78's karana that cannot set its foot in a non-place). Na asti iti api apade na asti: even *'it does not exist'* does *not exist* in a *footless place* — the very denial needs a floor, and where none is, the denial cannot be made either (3.3.80: no ground for saying 'it exists' or 'it does not exist'). Na ca asad bhidyate tatah: nor is the *non-existent* thereby *differentiated*: our denials do not carve nothing into kinds. So the whole apparatus — every karaka, every case, every construal — operates only where there is standing-room, and 3.3.80's station remains where speech stops.

translation: As intellect and word proceed among things that have come to be — of those there is no practice by any other reality.
commentary: The consequence of 3.7.109, and it is a boundary-marker. Buddhi-sabdau pravartete yatha bhutesu vastusu: as *intellect* and *word* proceed among things *that have come to be* — the two operate on the arisen, the constructed, the standing (3.3.82's beings raised up by construction). Tesam anyena tattvena vyavaharo na vidyate: of *those* there is *no practice* by any *other reality*. There is no second reality in which thought and speech could transact — no access to things as they are apart from having come to be. This is 2.297's charter (words are grounded in the world) and 3.3.71's cost (when reality and otherness are both abandoned, practice withdraws) stated together: the domain of buddhi and sabda is the arisen, and it has no outside that they could reach.

translation: As the division of ether, and as the moving of a shadow — so, though there is no difference, birth and destruction are constructed by some.
commentary: Two similes for constructed events. Akasasya yatha bhedah: as the *division of ether* — akasa is partless, and we speak of its regions (3.1.15's places constructed by the properties of what is conjoined). Chayayah calanam yatha: as the *moving of a shadow* — a shadow does not move; the light and the object move, and the shadow's 'motion' is a way of describing their change. Nothing travels. Janma-nasau abhede 'pi tatha kaiscit prakalpitau: so, *though there is no difference*, *birth and destruction* are *constructed* by some. Both similes are cases where a real appearance is generated by nothing doing what it seems to do — and both are cases nobody disputes. The shadow is especially apt: its motion is perfectly determinate, perfectly predictable, and there is no mover. So with birth: the event is exact and nothing is born (3.7.107's coiling snake).

translation: Just as the non-existence of ether is determined by a non-existent corporeality — so the non-existence of corporeality rests upon a non-existent ether.
commentary: The mutual constitution of absences, and its symmetry is the argument. Yatha eva akasa-nastitvam asan-murti-nirupitam: the *non-existence of ether* is *determined* by a *non-existent* corporeality — 'there is no space here' means 'this is occupied', and the occupying body is itself constructed. Tatha eva murti-nastitvam asad-akasa-nisrayam: so the *non-existence of corporeality* rests upon a *non-existent ether* — 'there is nothing here' means 'this is empty space', and the space is constructed too. Each absence is defined by the other's presence, and each presence is itself an absence's construction. So the pair spins: space's absence needs body, body's absence needs space, and neither is anywhere independently. This is 3.3.60's mutual dependence (resting on being, absence is followed; resting on absence, being is followed) given its most concrete case — and the concreteness is the point: emptiness and occupancy, the plainest facts about a place, are each other's shadows.

translation: As the self of the action is designated by the operations that are for its sake — this sequence of cause and effect is from the grasping of non-difference.
commentary: The causal sequence explained by a grasp. Yatha tad-arthaih vyaparaih kriya-atma vyapadisyate: as the *self of the action* is *designated* by the *operations that are for its sake* — the cooking is named by the fetching, lighting, stirring that serve it; the whole is designated through its parts (2.448's uncounted sub-actions displayed by a single verb). Abheda-grahanad esa karya-karanayoh kramah: *this sequence* of *cause and effect* is from the *grasping of non-difference*. The causal order exists because we grasp the parts as *not different* from the whole they serve: the stirring is the cooking, and so the stirring is its cause. Take them as different and no sequence appears — just events. So causation is generated by the same abheda-grahana that generates the action's unity, and cause-and-effect is the inside of a whole we have taken as one (3.7.107's samagrata: the wholeness of things dispersed).

translation: When there is doubt whether the modification is the agent of the birth, or the material cause — the view of the comprehenders is differentiated by the seeing of marks.
commentary: The open question, and its resolution is left to evidence. Vikaro janmanah karta prakrtir va iti samsaye: when there is *doubt* whether the *modification* is the *agent of the birth* — the pot, coming to be, being the agent of its own arising (3.7.105) — *or* the *material cause*: the clay, being what does the arising. Bhidyate pratipattrnam darsanam linga-darsanaih: the *view* of the comprehenders is *differentiated* by the *seeing of marks* — different hearers construe differently, according to what indications they find (2.73's linga; 2.315's fourteen determinants). So the question has no answer of its own: the karaka-assignment for origination follows the marks the context supplies, and comprehenders differ. This is 2.135's doctrine (launched in one way, divided out among differing comprehenders each according to his own cognition) applied to the most metaphysically loaded construction in the language.

mula_deva: क्लृपि सम्पद्यमाने या चतुर्थी सा विकारतः ।
mula_iast: klṛpi sampadyamāne yā caturthī sā vikārataḥ |
translation: In 'klp', when there is a becoming, the fourth case is from the modification; in the lump of gold, the material cause, the statement rests upon the earring.
commentary: The grammar of becoming, and it assigns by the outcome. Klpi sampadyamane ya caturthi sa vikaratah: in the root *klp*, when there is a *becoming*, the *fourth case* (dative) is *from the modification* — 'suvarna-pindah kundalaya kalpate', 'the gold-lump serves for an earring': the dative goes to the *product*, not to the material. Suvarna-pinde prakrtau vacanam kundala-asrayam: in the *lump of gold*, the *material cause*, the *statement* rests upon the *earring*. So when Sanskrit says a material 'becomes' something, the sentence is oriented toward the product: the earring gets the dative, and the gold is the subject. The case-assignment records which end of the transformation the speaker is facing — and 3.7.48 already established that whether the material is in view at all is a matter of vivaksa. The dative here is a small piece of evidence for a large thesis: the grammar of becoming is a grammar of attention.

translation: In a sentence, the agent 'becomes'; of a form ending in 'cvi' with 'sangha', it is stated; in the derivative formation, 'they become united' — the independence of the brahmins.
commentary: The cvi-construction (2.279's) revisited for its karaka-structure. Vakye sampadyate karta: in a *sentence*, the *agent* 'becomes' — the analytic phrase makes the transforming thing the agent: 'the brahmins become a group'. Sangha-cvy-antasya kathyate: of a form ending in *cvi* with 'sangha' — the synthetic form, sanghi-bhavanti, where the cvi-affix compresses the becoming. Vrttau sanghi-bhavanti iti brahmananam svatantrata: in the *derivative formation*, 'they *become united*' — the *independence of the brahmins*. The point is that the brahmins remain svatantra, agents, through both constructions: the analytic phrase and the compressed cvi-form assign agency identically, though their morphology differs entirely. So the karaka-structure survives the form-change — which is 2.415's doctrine (the sentence and its meaning shining forth undivided from words that possess sequence) at the level of a single derivation.

mula_deva: अत्वं सम्पद्यते यस्त्वं न तस्मिन् युष्मदाश्रया ।
mula_iast: atvaṃ sampadyate yastvaṃ na tasmin yuṣmadāśrayā |
translation: You who become 'you' — in him there is no material cause resting on 'yusmad'; the man has a material nature, and he is prescribed as natural.
commentary: A puzzle in the cvi-construction's pronominal cases. Atvam sampadyate yah tvam: *you* who *become 'you'* — the construction 'tvam-bhavati' (becomes you) faces a difficulty: na tasmin yusmad-asraya prakrtih asti — in him there is *no material cause* resting on *'yusmad'* (the pronominal stem for 'you'): there is no such stuff as you-ness for someone to be made of. The cvi-construction normally has a material becoming a product (3.7.115's gold and earring), and here the 'product' is a person and there is no material. Prakrtih purusasya asti prakrtah sa vidhiyate: the *man* has a *material nature*, and he is *prescribed as natural* — the material is the person himself, and the grammar treats him as the prakrti. So the construction is saved by locating the material in the person rather than in the pronoun's sense — which is a technical fix with a philosophical shadow: becoming-you has no stuff, and the grammar supplies one by fiat.

translation: Not abandoning the prior state, touching the subsequent property — the meaning's self, as though congealed, is spoken of as 'being born'.
commentary: The verse that resolves the origination-problem within the karaka-system, and its central word is chosen with care. Purvam avastham ajahat: *not abandoning* the *prior state* — the thing does not leave what it was (3.3.74's the self of being is not abandoned). Samsprsan dharmam uttaram: *touching* the *subsequent property* — it reaches the new condition without having left the old. Samurcchita iva artha-atma: the *meaning's self*, *as though congealed* — samurcchita: coagulated, curdled, thickened into shape (the word is used of milk becoming curds, and of things taking form). Jayamano 'bhidhiyate: is *spoken of as 'being born'*. So birth is a congealing between two states, holding both — which is 3.3.39's interval (fallen from the prior property, not having attained the subsequent, and in that interval birth is spoken of) now given a positive image. The iva marks it: as though congealed. Nothing thickens; the thickening is how the two-stateness is taken.

translation: Somewhere a certain property is understood as more possessed of operation; and the capacities of things, though possessing difference, are commingled.
commentary: Why one property among many gets the karaka-assignment. Sa-vyaparataraah kascit kvacid dharmah pratiyate: *somewhere* a certain *property* is understood as *more possessed of operation* — sa-vyaparatara: comparatively more operative. Among the sword's sharpness, weight, hardness and shape (3.7.31), one stands out as doing the work, and it takes the instrumental. Samsrjyante ca bhavanam bheda-vatyo 'pi saktayah: and the *capacities* of things, *though possessing difference*, are *commingled*. The properties are distinct and they act together, and the assignment picks one — the more-operative — from a fused ensemble. So the karana-assignment is a selection from a commingling (3.4.1's samsarga-rupat sambhutah), made by comparative operativeness, and the comparison is the speaker's (3.7.91's sthalya pacyate: the pot, which does nothing, chosen).

translation: In the inversion, the man's operating in a contrary meaning would be understood; for here the means is understood as possessed of operation.
commentary: The cvi-construction inverted. Viparita-artha-vrttitvam purusasya viparyaye: in the *inversion*, the *man's* *operating in a contrary meaning* would be understood — reverse the construction and the person is construed as acting oppositely. Gamyeta sadhanam hi atra sa-vyaparam pratiyate: for *here* the *means* is understood as *possessed of operation* — the inverted construction makes the means operative where it was not. The technicality is dense and the principle is 3.7.119's: which element is taken as operative decides the assignment, and inverting the construction inverts the taking. The verse is preparing 3.7.121's example, where a king becomes a servant and the grammar must decide who is doing what.

translation: 'You become another' — that would be the construction there; as when a king has come to servanthood, the movement would be like that.
commentary: The example that makes 3.7.120 vivid. Tvam anyo bhavasi iti esa tatra syat parikalpana: *'you become another'* — *that* would be the *construction* there. And the illustration: rajni bhrtyatvam apanne yatha tadvad gatir bhavet — as when a *king* has come to *servanthood*, the *movement* would be *like that*. The king who becomes a servant is the same man and is another; the construction 'the king becomes a servant' has him as agent of his own demotion, and what he becomes is what he was not. The example is chosen because the identity is undeniable (he is the same man) and the otherness is real (he is not a king now) — so 'you become another' is exactly right and exactly paradoxical. And it is 3.7.118's congealing: not abandoning the prior state, touching the subsequent, the meaning's self as though thickened between them.

translation: (122) From possibility, in the accomplishing of the action, he is resorted to as agent; in an action to be accomplished by himself, he is the occasioner of the means. (123) In mere employment, he is settled in subordination by his very independence; he becomes undistinguished from other independent ones whose doubt is removed.
commentary: The agent's two faces. (122): sambhavanat kriya-siddhau kartrtvena samasritah — from *possibility*, in the accomplishing of the action, he is *resorted to as agent*: agency is conferred on whoever could do it. Kriyayam atma-sadhyayam sadhananam prayojakah: in an action *to be accomplished by himself*, he is the *occasioner of the means* — he sets the instruments going (3.7.101's six marks). (123) gives the paradox: prayoga-matre nyag-bhavam svatantryad eva nisritah — in *mere employment*, he is *settled in subordination* *by his very independence*. His independence is what subordinates him — because to be the free one is to stand back and let the instruments touch the work (3.7.94's arat, 3.7.95's nyag-bhava in the agent). Avisisto bhavaty anyaih svatantraih mukta-samsayaih: he becomes *undistinguished* from *other independent ones* whose *doubt is removed*. All the free ones are alike in being at one remove. The verse is the samuddesa's last word on the agent, and it is that freedom and efficacy are inversely related.

translation: All alike proceed from their occasioning grounds for their own becoming; and even the accommodation of another's intention is for the accomplishing of one's own purpose.
commentary: The samuddesa's motivational realism, and it is bracing. Nimittebhyah pravartante sarva eva sva-bhutaye: *all alike* proceed from their *occasioning grounds* *for their own becoming* — every participant acts for itself: the axe for its own cutting, the fire for its own burning, the servant for his own ends (3.1.25's universal deploying the causes for the manifestation of its own self). Abhipraya-anurodho 'pi sva-arthasya eva prasiddhaye: and even the *accommodation of another's intention* is for the *accomplishing of one's own purpose*. The soldier fights the king's war for his own reasons; the instrument serves the agent by doing what instruments do. So the hierarchy of 3.7.22 (the soldiers fight, the king conquers) is not a hierarchy of purposes: each has his own, and the subordination is grammatical rather than motivational. Every karaka is an agent (3.7.18) and every agent acts for itself — which is why the roles must be *assigned* rather than found (3.7.104's buddhy-avastha), since the world offers only a crowd of self-interested operations and no organizing centre.

translation: One who, doing the directing and the requesting, and performing what is capable of that — being an agent alone, obtains the designation 'hetu' taught in the science.
commentary: The hetu located within agency. Presana-adhyesane kurvan: one *doing* the *directing* and the *requesting* — presana is command, adhyesana is request: the two ways one gets another to act. Tat-samarthani ca acaran: and *performing what is capable of that* — doing whatever brings the other's action about. Karta eva vihitam sastre hetu-samjnam prapadyate: *being an agent alone*, he *obtains* the *designation 'hetu'* taught in the *science*. The point is karta eva: the hetu is not a separate karaka standing beside the agent; it is an *agent*, receiving a further designation when his agency consists in moving another agent (3.7.25's karta kartr-antara-apeksah). So the causative's structure is agency-over-agency (3.7.63's stacking), and hetu names the upper storey rather than a new kind of thing.

mula_deva: सक्रियस्य प्रयोगस्तु यदा स विषयो णिचः ॥ १२६ ॥
mula_iast: sakriyasya prayogastu yadā sa viṣayo ṇicaḥ || 126 ||
translation: But in the directing of a mere substance, the imperative of 'prcch' and the like is prescribed; the employment of one possessed of action, however — then that is the domain of the causative affix.
commentary: Two kinds of directing, morphologically distinguished. Dravya-matrasya tu praise: in the *directing of a mere substance* — commanding something that is not itself an agent — prcchy-ader lod vidhiyate: the *imperative* of 'prcch' (ask) and the like is prescribed: one uses the imperative directly. Sa-kriyasya prayogah tu yada sa visayo nicah: but the *employment* of *one possessed of action* — directing something that *does* act — *then* that is the *domain of the causative affix*. So the nic-affix is reserved for directing agents, and mere imperatives suffice for directing things. The distinction is exact and it depends on 3.7.18's doctrine: the causative presupposes a second agent, and where the directed party has no action of its own, there is no second agency for the affix to mark.

translation: From independence in the action that is a quality, one who has come to objecthood in the directing — from the restriction of the object-designation, is expressed by his own property.
commentary: The caused agent's double status. Guna-kriyayam svatantryat: from *independence* in the action that is a *quality* — the caused agent is svatantra in his own act (Devadatta genuinely cooks). Presane karmatam gatah: in the *directing*, he has *come to objecthood* — but relative to the director, he is the object ('he makes Devadatta cook'). Niyamat karma-samjnayah sva-dharmena abhidhiyate: from the *restriction* of the *object-designation*, he is expressed *by his own property*. So he is agent below and object above, and the grammar's rule fixes which designation the morphology records. This is 3.7.20-21 exactly (subordinated capacities furnished with their own complete operations; agency regained at the next level down), and the causative construction is where the two-level structure is morphologically visible.

mula_deva: क्रियायाः प्रेरकं कर्म हेतुः कर्तुः प्रयोजकः ।
mula_iast: kriyāyāḥ prerakaṃ karma hetuḥ kartuḥ prayojakaḥ |
translation: The object is the impeller of the action; the hetu is the occasioner of the agent; and the arising of the action is for the sake of the object, through refinements and apprehensions.
commentary: The three-term structure of purposive action. Kriyayah prerakam karma: the *object* is the *impeller* of the action — what one wants is what sets the act going (3.7.45's ipsitatama; 3.7.71's object as the action's occasioner). Hetuh kartuh prayojakah: the *hetu* is the *occasioner of the agent* — the reason moves the doer. Karma-artha ca kriya-utpattih samskara-pratipattibhih: and the *arising of the action* is *for the sake of the object*, through *refinements* and *apprehensions*. So the causal order runs: hetu moves agent, agent acts, action is for the object — and the object, being what is wanted, was the impeller all along. The circle is teleological and it is not vicious: the wanted end starts the process that reaches it, which is what purposive action *is*. Samskara-pratipattibhih — through refinements and apprehensions — names the machinery: the instruments are prepared (3.7.92) and the situation is grasped, and between them the action arises.

translation: From the agent's not refusing, what is desired by the object is a limb of the giving; or, from impelling and from consenting, it obtains the state of being the recipient.
commentary: The sampradana — the recipient, the dative's karaka — defined by three alternatives, and all three are relational. Anirakaranat kartuh: from the *agent's not refusing* — the recipient is defined *negatively*: he is the one who does not decline. Tyaga-angam karmana ipsitam: what is *desired by the object* is a *limb of the giving*. Or: prerana-anumatibhyam va labhate sampradanatam: from *impelling* and from *consenting*, it obtains the state of being the *recipient* — he asked for it, or he agreed to it. So the recipient is constituted by not-refusing, or by asking, or by consenting: three attitudes, no action. The sampradana is the karaka that does nothing at all — it merely fails to object — and Panini's own definition (1.4.32: karmana yam abhipraiti sa sampradanam, whom the agent intends by the object) is equally attitudinal. This is 3.7.104's thesis at its cleanest: a karaka constituted wholly by a stance.

translation: In hetu-hood, in the object-designation, or even in remainder-hood, the karaka is called 'sampradana' by the science, in the case of 'ruc' and the rest.
commentary: The sampradana's assignment shown to override its competitors. In verbs like ruc (to please), the thing pleased could be construed as hetu (it is why the pleasing happens), as karman (it is what is affected), or as sesa (the residue-case: 3.7.44's sasthi sese). Hetutve karma-samjnayam sesatve va api karakam: in *hetu*-hood, in the *object*-designation, or even in *remainder*-hood — three available construals. Rucy-arthadisu sastrena sampradana-akhyam ucyate: the karaka is called *'sampradana'* *by the science* in the case of 'ruc' and the rest. Panini legislates (1.4.33: rucy-arthanam priyamanah): the pleased one takes the dative, whatever else he might have been. So the sampradana-assignment here is a *stipulation* — the science choosing among available construals and fixing one. This is 3.7.103's finding (the restriction is in the word, not in the thing) exhibited: the grammar decides, and there was nothing to discover.

mula_deva: भेदस्य च विवक्षायां पूर्वा पूर्वा क्रियां प्रति ।
mula_iast: bhedasya ca vivakṣāyāṃ pūrvā pūrvā kriyāṃ prati |
translation: And when a difference is intended, each prior one with respect to the action — since the subsequent limb is an object, the mention of 'action' has not been made.
commentary: Why Panini's rule omits a word. Bhedasya ca vivaksayam purva purva kriyam prati: when a *difference is intended*, *each prior one* with respect to the action — in a chain of sub-actions (3.7.16-17's seeing, seeking, resolving, undertaking), each earlier is means to the later. Parasya angasya karmatvat na kriya-grahanam krtam: since the *subsequent limb* is an *object*, the mention of 'action' has *not been made*. The rule does not need to say 'action' because the later stage is already an object, and the object-rule covers it. This is grammatical economy (2.343's laghava) explained by semantic structure: the sutra is short because the chain of 3.7.16-17 makes each stage an object of the last, and the karman-provisions already reach them. The verse shows the samuddesa's doctrine paying for itself in Panini's brevity.

translation: But when oneness is intended in an aggregate of actions — then the object, from its connection with the action, is figuratively spoken of by its own designation.
commentary: The complement of 3.7.131. Kriyanam samudaye tu yada ekatvam vivaksitam: when *oneness* is intended in an *aggregate of actions* — the chain taken as one act rather than as stages (2.448's uncounted sub-actions displayed by a single verb). Tada karma kriya-yogat sva-akhyaya eva upacaryate: *then* the object, from its *connection with the action*, is *figuratively* spoken of by its *own designation*. Upacaryate — figuratively (3.3.39's aupacariki satta; 2.253's upacara): where the chain is taken as one, the intermediate objects lose their objecthood and the designation is transferred. So whether the stages have objects depends on whether the chain is taken as many acts or one — which is vivaksa (3.7.2's kvacit kimcit vivaksyate), and the pair 3.7.131-132 shows both takings licensed and both grammatical.

translation: And the intending of difference and non-difference stands settled by its own nature; therefore, in the objecthood of verbs of motion, no deviation is seen.
commentary: The stability underneath the relativity. Bheda-abheda-vivaksa ca svabhavena vyavasthita: the *intending of difference and non-difference* stands settled *by its own nature* — the two takings of 3.7.131-132 are not arbitrary; each has its own settled character, and speakers do not confuse them. Tasmat gaty-artha-karmatve vyabhicaro na drsyate: *therefore*, in the *objecthood* of verbs of *motion*, no *deviation* is seen. The gati-verbs ('goes to the village') take their objects invariably, without wobble — because though the assignment is a taking, the taking is stable. This is the samuddesa's version of 3.6.22 (relative to the limit, no inversion) and 3.7.11 (the capacities are of fixed apprehension): constructivism without arbitrariness. Vyabhicara — deviation — is the logician's word for a mark that strays, and its absence here is what makes the grammar statable at all.

mula_deva: आरंभेण न योगस्य प्रत्याख्यानं समं भवते ॥ १३४ ॥
mula_iast: āraṃbheṇa na yogasya pratyākhyānaṃ samaṃ bhavate || 134 ||
translation: If both designations were to obtain everywhere by option — then, by the undertaking, the rejection of the connection would not be equal.
commentary: An argument against free option in karaka-assignment. Vikalpena eva sarvatra samjne syatam ubhe yadi: if *both* designations were to obtain *everywhere* by *option* — if the grammar simply let one choose between karman and sampradana, or between hetu and karana, wherever both fit. Arambhena na yogasya pratyakhyanam samam bhavet: then, by the *undertaking*, the *rejection of the connection* would not be *equal*. The reasoning is compressed: if option ran everywhere, the rules that *reject* certain connections (that bar a construal) would be pointless, since option would restore what they barred. Panini's system contains such rejections; therefore option does not run everywhere. This is the argument from the science's economy that 2.443 ran (Panini does not legislate vacuously) — and it protects 3.7.133's stability: the assignments are takings, and the takings are constrained, or the sastra would not need its prohibitions.

mula_deva: आस्थितं कर्म यत् तत्र द्वैरूप्यं भजते क्रिया ॥ १३५ ॥
mula_iast: āsthitaṃ karma yat tatra dvairūpyaṃ bhajate kriyā || 135 ||
translation: In what is to be abandoned there is the form of relinquishing; in what is to be attained, the seeing of conjunction; where the object is settled there, the action takes on a two-fold form.
commentary: The two directions an action can face. Tyaga-rupam prahatavye: in *what is to be abandoned* there is the *form of relinquishing* — the action faces away, and its object is a source. Prapye samsarga-darsanam: in *what is to be attained*, the *seeing of conjunction* — the action faces toward, and its object is a goal. Asthitam karma yat tatra dvairupyam bhajate kriya: where the *object* is *settled* there, the *action* takes on a *two-fold form*. One action, two faces: every movement is a leaving and an arriving, and which the sentence records decides whether the noun takes the ablative or the accusative. The verse introduces the apadana — the ablative's karaka — by locating it in the same action that yields the accusative, differently faced. Bhajate: takes on, wears (3.2.8's tattvam eva avikalpitam vikalpa-rupam bhajate).

translation: Something whose domain is specified; likewise something whose domain is taken up; and something whose action is expected — thus the ablative is said to be three-fold.
commentary: The apadana's taxonomy, and it is Panini's. Nirdista-visayam kimcit: something whose *domain is specified* — the source explicitly named ('he comes from the village'). Upatta-visayam tatha: likewise something whose *domain is taken up* — the source supplied by the construction ('he fears the thief': the thief is the source of the fear, unnamed as a source). Apeksita-kriyam ca iti: and something whose *action is expected* — the source of an action not stated but required ('he abstains from wine': the drinking is expected and absent). Tridha apadanam ucyate: thus the *ablative* is said to be *three-fold*. The three cover the ablative's whole range — physical source, emotional source, abstained-from action — and they are ordered by how much the construction supplies: named, taken up, expected. This is 3.7.78's method (one karaka expounded by way of difference) applied to the case that most obviously needs it.

translation: The self of 'gami' is differentiated by difference of what is conjoined — just as 'bhrami' is; the separation too, having a fixed limit, is inherent likewise in the unfixed.
commentary: The ablative's limit-condition examined. Samyogi-bhedad bhinnatma gamir eva bhramir yatha: the self of *'gami'* (going) is *differentiated* by difference of *what is conjoined* — as *'bhrami'* (whirling) is: the going is one action, and it differs according to what it goes from and to, as whirling differs by what whirls. Dhruva-avadhih apayo 'pi: the *separation* too has a *fixed limit* — Panini's rule (1.4.24: dhruvam apaye 'padanam: the fixed point in a separation is the apadana). Samavetah tatha adhruve: it is *inherent* likewise in the *unfixed*. So the ablative's 'fixed point' is found even where nothing is fixed — the departure-point of two moving things, the source that is itself in motion. The rule says dhruva, fixed, and the case applies to the unfixed: which means dhruva names a role in the construal (what is taken as the reference-point) and not a property of anything (3.6.2's avadhi; 3.7.103's restriction in the word, not in the thing).

translation: 'The state of being a substance is not fixity' — this is understood in the sutra; but the fixity whose domain is the separation is what is intended.
commentary: The rule's key word disambiguated. Dravyatva-bhavo na dhrauvyam iti sutre pratiyate: '*the state of being a substance is not fixity*' — this is understood in the *sutra*: dhruva does not mean 'is a substance' or 'is immobile'. Apaya-visayam dhrauvyam yat tu tavad vivaksitam: but the *fixity* whose *domain is the separation* is what is *intended* — dhruva means: fixed *for the purposes of this separation*, taken as the point from which. So the ablative's 'fixed point' is fixed relative to the departure being described, and nothing more. The verse is precise grammatical exegesis and it delivers the samuddesa's thesis exactly: what looked like a metaphysical requirement in Panini's rule (the source must be *fixed*) turns out to be a construal-relative role. 3.7.139 gives the examples.

mula_deva: सरणे देवदत्तस्य ध्रौव्यं पाते तु वाजिनः ।
mula_iast: saraṇe devadattasya dhrauvyaṃ pāte tu vājinaḥ |
translation: In Devadatta's running there is fixity; but in the horse's falling, of the horse. That which is pervaded by the separation — of that they declare the non-fixity.
commentary: The relativity of 3.7.138 exhibited. Sarane devadattasya dhrauvyam: in *Devadatta's running* there is *fixity* — 'the horse falls from running Devadatta': Devadatta is running, and he is the dhruva, the fixed point of the falling. Pate tu vajinah: but in the *horse's falling*, of the *horse* — from the horse's side, the horse is what departs. Avistam yad apayena tasya adhrauvyam pracaksate: *that which is pervaded by the separation* — of *that* they declare the *non-fixity*. So the runner is 'fixed' while running and the faller is 'unfixed' while stationary-relative-to-him: fixity is entirely a matter of which party the separation is predicated of. The example is chosen for its absurdity-as-physics and its exactness-as-grammar: a running man is the fixed point, and Sanskrit says so, and the rule is dhruvam apaye 'padanam. Nothing could show more plainly that the karakas are construals (3.7.91's sthalya pacyate, in the ablative).

mula_deva: विभागे प्रविभक्ते तु क्रिये तत्र विवक्षिते ॥ १४० ॥
mula_iast: vibhāge pravibhakte tu kriye tatra vivakṣite || 140 ||
translation: Both rams are unfixed, even though the separation is produced by the action of both; but when the division is divided out, the two actions are intended there.
commentary: The limit-case: two things parting from each other. Ubhau api adhruvau mesau: *both rams* are *unfixed* — two rams butting and springing apart: neither is stationary, and each departs from the other. Yady api ubhaya-karma-je vibhage: even though the *separation* is *produced by the action of both* — the parting is jointly caused; there is no asymmetry in the event whatever. Pravibhakte tu kriye tatra vivaksite: but when the *division is divided out*, the *two actions* are *intended there*. The speaker splits the one parting into two departures, and each ram becomes the other's dhruva: 'this one springs from that', 'that one springs from this'. Two sentences, two ablatives, one symmetrical event. This is 3.7.19's parents (both agents, roles by intention) at the ablative, and it closes the apadana's analysis where the samuddesa closes everything: the world offers a symmetry, the grammar requires an asymmetry, and vivaksa supplies it.

mula_deva: मेषयोः स्वक्रियापेक्षं कर्तृत्वं च पृथक् पृथक् ॥ १४१ ॥
mula_iast: meṣayoḥ svakriyāpekṣaṃ kartṛtvaṃ ca pṛthak pṛthak || 141 ||
translation: (141) Limit-hood, depending on the action of the other ram, belongs to the two rams severally; and agency, depending on their own action, belongs to them severally. (142) But if one action, to be accomplished by two, is intended by way of non-difference — the two rams are agents in the separation, if some other limit exists.
commentary: The rams of 3.7.140 analysed at both takings. (141) gives the divided taking: mesa-antara-kriya-apeksam avadhitvam prthak prthak — *limit-hood*, depending on the *other* ram's action, belongs to them *severally*: each is the other's dhruva, in his own sentence. Sva-kriya-apeksam kartrtvam ca prthak prthak: and *agency*, depending on their *own* action, *severally*. Two sentences, each ram agent in one and limit in the other. (142) gives the undivided taking: abhedena kriya eka tu dvi-sadhya ced vivaksita — if *one* action, *to be accomplished by two*, is intended *by way of non-difference*: one parting, jointly done. Then: mesau apaye kartarau yady anyo vidyate avadhih — the two rams are *agents* in the separation, *if some other limit exists*. Both agents, and the ablative must go elsewhere — to a third thing, or nowhere. So the same event yields two grammars: divided, each is the other's source; undivided, both are agents and the source-slot is vacant. Vivaksa decides, and the case-frame follows.

translation: But without a limit, motion is not understood as a separation; 'the leaf falls from the tree' — thus it is exhibited in the Bhasya.
commentary: The apadana's necessary condition, cited from Patanjali. Gatir vina tu avadhina na apayah iti gamyate: *without a limit*, *motion* is not understood as a *separation* — a falling is just a falling until something is fallen *from*; apaya requires an avadhi, or it is not apaya. And the citation: vrksasya parnam patati iti evam bhasye nidarsitam — '*the leaf falls from the tree*' — thus it is *exhibited in the Bhasya*. Patanjali's own example, and the genitive in it (vrksasya) rather than the expected ablative is the puzzle the Mahabhasya discusses. Bhartrhari cites rather than argues: the point that separation needs a limit is the tradition's, and the example is the tradition's, and 3.7.140-142's analysis of the rams was an extension of it. The samuddesa's method throughout — 2.475's bhasye eva upavarnitah, 'described in the Bhasya itself' — is to find its doctrines already in the gurus.

translation: Difference and non-difference, separateness and standing — these being contradictory, all the properties are not intended simultaneously in a cloud.
commentary: Why one situation cannot bear all its construals at once. Bheda-abhedau prthag-bhavah sthitih ca iti virodhinah: *difference* and *non-difference*, *separateness* and *standing* — these are *contradictory*. Yugapan na vivaksyante sarve dharmah valahake: *all* the properties are *not intended simultaneously* in a *cloud*. The cloud is the example: it is one and many, gathering and dispersing, moving and standing — every contradictory pair applies to it, and no single utterance can intend them all. So vivaksa selects, and must: the properties are all there (3.7.2's sarvada sarvatha bhavat: existing always and in every way), and speech takes one set at a time. This is 2.251's mechanism (transcending simultaneity, the word's plenitude stands settled in succession) applied to the world rather than the word — and it explains why every karaka-assignment excludes the others it could have made.

translation: In 'he pierces with a bow', without the intention of separation, since instrument-hood does not exist — therefore those two are together.
commentary: A case where two karaka-construals must coexist. Dhanusa vidhyati iti atra: in '*he pierces with a bow*' — the bow is instrumental. Vina apaya-vivaksaya karanatvam yatah na asti: *without* the *intention of separation*, *instrument-hood* does *not exist* — the bow is an instrument only because the arrow *leaves* it: take away the departure and the bow does nothing. So the instrumental presupposes an ablative sense that the case does not express. Tasmat tad ubhayam saha: *therefore* those *two are together* — instrument-hood and separation-hood coexist in the one bow, in the one case-ending. This is a genuine exception to 3.7.144's rule that contradictory construals cannot be simultaneous: here two construals are *both* required, because one is the condition of the other. The verse is careful evidence that the karaka-system's exclusivity has limits.

translation: That one and the same existing capacity stands two-formed; being the occasioning ground for the two designations there, the later annuls the earlier.
commentary: The resolution of 3.7.145. Eka eva sa sati saktih dvi-rupa vyavatisthate: that *one and the same* existing capacity stands *two-formed* — the bow's single capacity wears both faces (instrument and source), and there are not two powers in it. Nimittam samjnayoh tatra: it is the *occasioning ground* for the *two designations* *there* — one capacity, two available names. Paraya badhyate 'para: the *later* *annuls* the *earlier*. The badha-machinery adjudicates (2.66's annulment; 2.73's hierarchy; 3.7.97's kartrtvam badhakam): where two designations compete for one capacity, the rule ordering decides, and the instrumental wins in 'dhanusa vidhyati'. So the coexistence of 3.7.145 is real at the level of capacity and resolved at the level of designation: the bow is both, and the grammar says one. This is the samuddesa's settled shape — plenitude below, selection above, and the selection made by rule.

mula_deva: निर्धारणे विभक्ते यो भीत्रादीना च यो विधिः ।
mula_iast: nirdhāraṇe vibhakte yo bhītrādīnā ca yo vidhiḥ |
translation: The rule for the ablative in determination, and that for 'bhi', 'tra' and the rest, has its separation taken up or expected — that is for the comprehension of the unlearned.
commentary: The apadana's extended cases placed as pedagogy. Nirdharane vibhakte yah: the rule for the ablative in *determination* — 'the best of cows' (nirdharana: singling out from a class, which takes the ablative or genitive). Bhi-tradinam ca yo vidhih: and that for '*bhi*' (fear) and '*tra*' (protect) and the rest — 'he fears the thief', where the thief is grammatically a source (3.7.136's upatta-visaya). Upatta-apeksita-apayah: its *separation* is *taken up* or *expected* (3.7.136's second and third kinds). Sah abudha-pratipattaye: *that* is *for the comprehension of the unlearned*. The extended ablative-rules are teaching-devices: they let the learner see that fearing has a source-structure, that singling-out has a departure-structure. The separations are constructed for instruction (2.13's divisions for the avidvan; 3.7.78's one apadana exhibited by way of difference). The samuddesa marks its own taxonomy as ladder.

translation: Bearing, not directly, an action that is separated by agent and object; assisting in the accomplishing of the action — that is remembered in the science as the locus.
commentary: The adhikarana — the locus, the locative's karaka — defined, and the definition is by remoteness. Kartr-karma-vyavahitam asaksat dharayat kriyam: *bearing*, *not directly*, an action that is *separated* by *agent and object* — the locus holds the action at two removes: the agent and object stand between. Asaksat: not directly (3.7.94's arat, the agent assisting only from a distance — and the locus is further still). Upakurvat kriya-siddhau: *assisting* in the *accomplishing* of the action. Sastre adhikaranam smrtam: that is *remembered in the science* as the *locus*. So the adhikarana is the most remote karaka: it supports the whole configuration without touching any part of it. The mat is where Devadatta sits, and the mat does nothing to the sitting except be under it. Bharing-at-a-remove is the whole of its contribution — and it is a contribution (upakurvat), which is why it is a karaka at all.

translation: And the non-difference of contact is in sesame, ether, mat and the like; but the assistances of the conjoined and the inherent are differentiated.
commentary: The locus's kinds. Upaslesasya ca abhedah tila-akasa-katadisu: the *non-difference of contact* is in *sesame*, *ether*, *mat* and the like — three loci: the oil in the sesame (pervading), the thing in space (containing), the sitter on the mat (supporting). In all three the contact is undifferentiated: 'in' is 'in'. Upakarah tu bhidyante samyogi-samavayinam: but the *assistances* of the *conjoined* and the *inherent* are *differentiated*. The oil's being in the seed is inherence; the man's being on the mat is conjunction; and though the locative is the same, what the locus *does* differs. So the case-ending flattens a real distinction — one locative for pervasion, containment and support — and the karaka-analysis recovers it. This is 3.1.17-18's distinction (cognitions running along among the conjoined, difference withdrawing among the inherent) applied to the locus.

translation: The non-destruction of weight; independence in obstruction; delimitation by a particular direction — such are the causes of difference.
commentary: Three marks by which loci differ. Avinasah gurutvasya: the *non-destruction of weight* — the mat bears the man, and his weight is not annulled: the locus supports without absorbing. Pratibandhe svatantrata: *independence in obstruction* — the locus can resist, and its resisting is its own (3.7.29's wall, whose capacity is in obstructing). Dig-visesad avacchedah: *delimitation by a particular direction* — the locus locates by direction (3.6.13's bhaga-prakalpana-sakti, the first capacity). Ity-adyah bheda-hetavah: such are the *causes of difference*. Three ways loci vary: how they bear weight, how they resist, how they orient. Each is a mode of the assisting-at-a-remove that 3.7.148 defined, and together they explain why the single locative case covers so heterogeneous a family.

translation: For some, ether alone, from its construction of a difference of places, is held to be the first locus-capacity of all conjoined things.
commentary: The locus reduced to ether. Akasam eva kesamcid: for *some*, *ether alone* — desa-bheda-prakalpanat: from its *construction of a difference of places* (3.1.15's places constructed by the properties of what is conjoined; 3.6.5's ether-portions serving as direction's adjunct). Adhara-saktih prathama sarva-samyoginam mata: is held to be the *first* *locus-capacity* of *all conjoined* things. Every locus is ether, ultimately: the mat is where the man is because the mat is somewhere, and being-somewhere is ether's construction. Prathama — first: the same word 3.6.13 gave direction (the *first* capacity, of constructing parts). So there are two claimants for primacy — direction constructing parts, ether constructing places — and they are the same construction differently named. The verse reports the reduction as one school's (kesamcid) and does not adopt it.

translation: 'This is here' — the designation is not constructed, from the non-existence of things; they call that the occasioning ground which is ether.
commentary: The argument for 3.7.151's reduction. Idam atra iti: '*this is here*' — the plainest locative there is. Bhavanam abhavat na prakalpate vyapadesah: the *designation* is *not constructed*, from the *non-existence of things* — if there were no things, there would be no 'here': the locative needs a located thing and a locating one, and both are constructions (3.6.14's things without place; 3.7.104's buddhy-avastha). Tam akasa-nimittam sampracaksate: they call *that* — the ground of the 'here' — *the occasioning ground which is ether*. So what makes 'this is here' sayable is akasa, and akasa is what has no differentiation of its own (3.1.15). The locative rests on the undifferentiated, differentiated by what it locates — which is 3.1.16's structure (places belonging to what is not a place, conferred by relata) reaching the grammar's most basic spatial expression.

translation: Actions are divided by time; all corporeal things by ether. And just this much is that difference, which rests upon non-difference.
commentary: The two great dividers, and their common ground. Kalat kriyah vibhajyante: *actions* are *divided by time* — one cooking from another by when (3.6.4's krama-rupe tu kalatah). Akasat sarva-murtayah: *all corporeal things* by *ether* — one pot from another by where. Two principles of individuation, and between them they carve everything: events by time, bodies by space. And then the deflation: etavan ca eva bhedah ayam abheda-upanibandhanah — *just this much* is that *difference*, which *rests upon non-difference*. Both dividers divide what is undivided (time and ether being themselves partless: 3.1.15, 3.2.8), and so all difference whatever — every event distinguished from every other, every thing from every other — rests on abheda. Etavan ca eva: just this much, and no more. The whole world's plurality is space and time carving the uncarved, and they are constructions.

translation: Although 'upavas' conforms to a particular place — from the property of the word's operation, it takes hold of time alone.
commentary: A case where the karaka follows the word rather than the situation. Yady api upavasir desa-visesam anurudhyate: although *'upavas'* (to fast, to dwell near) *conforms to a particular place* — the fast is kept somewhere; a place is unmistakably involved. Sabda-pravrtti-dharmat tu kalam eva avalambate: from the *property of the word's operation*, it *takes hold of time alone* — the verb's own behaviour makes its locus temporal ('he fasts for three nights'), not spatial, whatever the situation contains. So the word's usage overrides the situation's structure in fixing the karaka: the fast has a place, and 'upavas' will not take it as its adhikarana. This is 3.7.103's finding at its sharpest (the restriction is in the word, not in the thing) — and 3.7.66's two schools meet here, with the sabda-party winning: the action is where the words put it.

translation: Even where 'vas' is not employed, place is therefore the locus; and 'three nights' and the rest, though unemployed, is remembered as the object in 'upavas'.
commentary: The consequence of 3.7.154, and it is a double displacement. Vasatau aprayukte 'pi desah adhikaranam tatah: even where *'vas'* (to dwell) is *not employed*, *place* is *therefore* the locus — the dwelling-verb is absent and its locus survives, supplied to the construction. Aprayuktam tri-ratradi karma ca upavasau smrtam: and *'three nights'* and the rest, *though unemployed*, is remembered as the *object* in *'upavas'* — the time-phrase is the object of an unstated verb. So 'he fasts three nights' has: a place-locus from an unuttered 'dwell', and a time-object from an unuttered action, and neither verb is there. The karaka-frame outlives the verbs that would license it, supplied by the buddhi that holds the situation (3.7.3). Aprayukta — unemployed, twice: the samuddesa ends its analysis of the locus by showing the case-frame running on words nobody said.

translation: The relation is other than the karakas, and is preceded by action and karaka; whether the action is heard or unheard, it is expressed.
commentary: The genitive placed outside the karaka-system entirely. Sambandhah karakebhyo 'nyah: the *relation* is *other than* the karakas — the sixth case is not a karaka at all; it is the sesa, the remainder (3.7.44's sasthi sese). And its genesis: kriya-karaka-purvakah — it is *preceded by action and karaka*: a relation is what a completed karaka-configuration leaves behind. The king's man is the king's because some appointing happened. Srutayam asrutayam va kriyayam so 'bhidhiyate: whether the action is *heard* or *unheard*, it is *expressed* — the genitive stands whether or not its generating action is stated, and usually it is not (2.197's action that, having generated the connection, withdraws). So the genitive is the case of relations that outlive their actions, and its exclusion from the karakas is principled: karakas are participants *in* an action, and the genitive marks what is left when the action has gone.

translation: Though resting in two, it is set apart among the subordinates because of its being for another; being expressed there, it is enjoyed in the principal as well.
commentary: The genitive's peculiar double life. Dvistho 'py asau: though *resting in two* — a relation needs two terms; 'the king's man' holds both. Para-arthatvad gunesu vyatiricyate: it is *set apart* among the *subordinates* because of its *being for another* — the relation is parartha (2.113's term), serving something beyond itself, so it sits among the subordinate elements. Tatra abhidhiyamanah san pradhane 'py upabhujyate: being *expressed there*, it is *enjoyed in the principal* as well. Upabhujyate — enjoyed, consumed, made use of: the relation, though expressed in the subordinate term, does its work for the principal. So the genitive is expressed at one end and works at the other, which is what it is to be a relation — and 2.395's joint-property model (each owner having the whole, undiminished) is the right picture for how it can be wholly in both.

translation: A restriction of the occasioning ground for the relation is not grasped from the word; but by the karmapravacaniyas, it is confined to a particular.
commentary: The genitive's vagueness and its remedy. Nimitta-niyamah sabdat sambandhasya na grhyate: a *restriction of the occasioning ground* for the *relation* is *not grasped from the word* — 'the king's man' does not say *how* he is the king's: servant, son, subject, sculptor? The genitive marks relation and specifies none. Karma-pravacaniyaih tu sa visese 'varudhyate: but by the *karmapravacaniyas*, it is *confined to a particular* — the class of words (anu, prati, adhi) that Panini defines as speaking *about* the action, and whose office 2.204 identified exactly: sambandhasya bhedakah, *differentiators of the relation*. So the genitive opens a relation and the karmapravacaniya closes it. This is 2.199-204's analysis reappearing in the Padakanda, and the two treatments agree precisely: a relation left standing by a withdrawn action, and a particle that pins which relation it is.

translation: And when a thing is designated by the means, and the action is being heard again — the sixth case is stated word by word, for the exclusion of the compound.
commentary: Why the genitive is prescribed where a karaka would serve. Sadhanaih vyapadiste ca sruyamana-kriye punah: when a thing is *designated by the means*, and the *action is being heard* again — where the verb is present and the karaka-relation is live. Prokta pratipadam sasthi samasasya nivrttaye: the *sixth case* is stated *word by word*, *for the exclusion of the compound*. The genitive is prescribed individually so as to block compounding — keep 'rajnah purusah' from collapsing into 'rajapurusah', because the analysis must stay visible. This is 2.198 verbatim, and its recurrence here is not repetition but placement: the Vakyakanda gave the rule's motive, and the Padakanda now shows it operating inside the karaka-system. The genitive is posted where the science needs the relation *displayed* — an operational reason, not a semantic one (3.7.103's niyamah sabde na tu vastuni).

translation: In the nistha-form, the sixth case whose domain is the object is not prohibited; the compound with a sixth case characterized as remainder is not desired there.
commentary: A technical case, and it discriminates two genitives. Nisthayam karma-visaya sasthi na pratisidhyate: in the *nistha*-form (the past participle in -ta) the *sixth case* whose *domain is the object* is *not prohibited* — 'krtam asya', 'done by him', where the genitive marks the object-relation and is allowed to compound. Sesa-laksanaya sasthya samasah tatra na isyate: the *compound* with a sixth case *characterized as remainder* is *not desired* there. So two genitives: the karaka-genitive (marking a role, compoundable) and the sesa-genitive (marking bare relation, not compoundable). The grammar distinguishes them by their compounding behaviour, and nothing in the form distinguishes them at all. This is 2.12's distributional method certifying a distinction the ear cannot hear — and it confirms 3.7.156: the genitive spans both the karaka-system and its remainder, and only its behaviour tells you which side it is on.

mula_deva: व्यतिरेकः स धर्मौ द्वौ लभते विषयान्तरे ॥ १६१ ॥
mula_iast: vyatirekaḥ sa dharmau dvau labhate viṣayāntare || 161 ||
translation: Of that which is designated by another, when a separation arises elsewhere — that one obtains two properties in another domain.
commentary: How a term can hold two relational roles. Anyena vyapadistasya: of that which is *designated by another* — something already standing in a relation. Yasya anyatra upajayate vyatirekah: when a *separation* arises *elsewhere* — it enters a second relation, in another context. Sa dharmau dvau labhate visayantare: *that one* obtains *two properties* in *another domain*. So a term can be the king's man and the village's headman at once, holding two relations without conflict, because the relations belong to different domains. This is 3.7.68's bhinna-kaksya (objecthood of a different level: the strata not colliding) applied to the genitive — and it explains why relational terms stack indefinitely where karaka-terms do not: karakas compete for one action's slots, and relations do not compete at all.

mula_deva: प्राधान्यं स्वगुणे लब्ध्वा प्रधाने याति शेषताम् ।
mula_iast: prādhānyaṃ svaguṇe labdhvā pradhāne yāti śeṣatām |
translation: Having obtained principality in its own subordinate, it goes to remainder-hood in the principal; therefore, in joint connection and in its own connection, principality is not lost.
commentary: The relational hierarchy, and it mirrors the karaka one exactly. Pradhanyam sva-gune labdhva: having obtained *principality* in its *own subordinate* — a term is principal relative to what depends on it. Pradhane yati sesatam: it goes to *remainder-hood* in the *principal* — and subordinate relative to what it depends on. Saha-yoge sva-yoge atah pradhanatvam na hiyate: *therefore*, in *joint connection* and in its *own* connection, *principality* is *not lost*. Each term is principal below and remainder above, and neither status cancels the other. This is 3.7.20-23's nesting (the soldiers fighting, the king conquering; agency retained under subordination) transposed from karakas to relations — and the transposition is exact, which confirms that the samuddesa's structural insight is not about the karakas specifically but about how hierarchies of dependence work.

translation: They know the vocative to be the mere facing-toward of what is accomplished; for the meaning's self, having attained facing-toward, is applied to the actions.
commentary: The vocative defined, and it is barely a case at all. Siddhasya abhimukhi-bhava-matram sambodhanam viduh: they know the *vocative* to be the *mere facing-toward* of what is *accomplished* — matra: merely, nothing more. The vocative does not assign a role; it turns something to face. And siddhasya: of what is *accomplished*, already standing (3.7.79's siddha-rupa: the object presented as complete). Prapta-abhimukhyo hi artha-atma kriyasu viniyujyate: for the *meaning's self*, *having attained facing-toward*, is *applied to the actions*. So the vocative is prior to the karaka-system: it turns a thing toward the discourse, and *then* the actions can use it. 'Devadatta! Come!' — the calling makes him available, the imperative gives him a role. The vocative is the doorway into the sentence, and it is not a room in it.

mula_deva: संबोधनं न वाक्यार्थ इति वृद्धेभ्य आगमः ।
mula_iast: saṃbodhanaṃ na vākyārtha iti vṛddhebhya āgamaḥ |
translation: (164) 'The vocative is not a sentence-meaning' — such is the tradition from the elders; the meanings of the case-endings, by way of indication, have been abstracted from the sentence-meaning. (165) In the meaning of a case-ending, let it be ascertained from the statement about the avyayibhava; the case-ending's meaning is other than the substance, and it is expressed by the indeclinable.
commentary: The vocative's exclusion, on authority, and then the case-endings' status. (164): sambodhanam na vakyarthah iti vrddhebhya agamah — '*the vocative is not a sentence-meaning*' — such is the *tradition from the elders* (vrddha: 3.2.7's vrddhebhya agamah; 2.485's counsel about waiting upon them). The vocative does not contribute to what the sentence says, because it is prior to the saying (3.7.163). And the general placement: uddesena vibhakty-arthah vakyarthat samapoddhrtah — the *meanings of the case-endings*, *by way of indication*, have been *abstracted from the sentence-meaning*. Apoddhrta: extracted (2.10, 3.1.1, 3.4.1 — the kanda's constant). So all the karaka-meanings this samuddesa has spent 165 verses anatomizing are extracts from the one sentence-meaning, indicated for the analysis's sake. (165) confirms it from the avyayibhava-compounds, where a case-meaning is carried by an indeclinable: anyo dravyad vibhakty-arthah — the case-ending's meaning is *other than the substance*, and it can be borne by a word that is not a noun at all. The role floats free of the thing.

mula_deva: द्रव्यं तु यद् यथाभूतं तदत्यन्तं तथा भवेत् ।
mula_iast: dravyaṃ tu yad yathābhūtaṃ tadatyantaṃ tathā bhavet |
translation: But the substance, being such as it is, would be so utterly; even in a difference of action, that substance-self of it is not abandoned.
commentary: The samuddesa's penultimate verse, and it reasserts what all its constructivism did not touch. Dravyam tu yad yatha-bhutam tad atyantam tatha bhavet: the *substance*, being *such as it is*, would be so *utterly* — atyantam: absolutely, without qualification. Kriya-bhede 'pi tasya asau dravya-atma na avahiyate: *even in a difference of action*, that *substance-self* of it is *not abandoned*. The pot is a pot whether one makes it, breaks it, sees it or sits beside it; the karaka-role changes with every sentence and the thing does not. This is not a retraction of 3.7.104 (the roles arise from states of the intellect): the roles are constructed *and* the thing that wears them is not the roles. Dravya, as 3.2.11 established, is what stands when the configurations are withdrawn — and no amount of role-assignment reaches it. The verse marks exactly what the samuddesa's analysis has and has not dissolved.

mula_deva: तस्माद् यत् करणं द्रव्यं तत् कर्म न पुनर्भवेत् ।
mula_iast: tasmād yat karaṇaṃ dravyaṃ tat karma na punarbhavet |
translation: Therefore that substance which is the instrument would not again be the object; and there would be an otherwise-becoming of that whole substance-self.
commentary: The samuddesa's last verse, and it is a difficulty rather than a conclusion. Tasmat yat karanam dravyam tat karma na punar bhavet: *therefore* that *substance* which is the *instrument* would *not again* be the *object* — if the substance-self is unabandoned through every difference of action (3.7.166), then a thing that is an instrument cannot also be an object; the roles would have to be fixed in the things.
