Sthitasya anugrahah taih dharmaih samsargibhih tatah: of *what stands*, there is *favouring* by those *commingling* properties — a standing thing is assisted by what it is with (3.3.5's upakara; 3.7.12's whatever assists is the means). And then three privations: pratibandhah — *obstruction* (held back); tirobhavah — *disappearance* (hidden); prahanam — *abandonment* (let go). Iti ca atmanah: these *of its self*. So a thing's career is: standing, favoured, obstructed, hidden, abandoned — and every one of the five is a relation to what it is commingled with or to the time that holds it. Nothing in the list is the thing's own doing. This is the six vikaras (1.3, 3.1.36) redistributed as time's operations on a persisting thing, and it prepares the identification of 3.9.12.

mula_deva: काल एव हि विश्वात्मा व्यापार इति कथ्यते ॥ १२ ॥
mula_iast: kāla eva hi viśvātmā vyāpāra iti kathyate || 12 ||
translation: But at each state, time's operation stands settled here; for time alone, whose self is the universe, is spoken of as 'operation'.
commentary: The samuddesa's central identification, and it overturns 3.9.1 entirely. Praty-avastham tu kalasya vyaparo 'tra vyavasthitah: at *each state*, *time's operation* stands settled *here* — every one of 3.9.11's five moments is time operating. And then: kala eva hi visva-atma vyapara iti kathyate — for *time alone*, *whose self is the universe*, is *spoken of as 'operation'*. Time *is* operation. The rival opened by declaring time vyapara-vyatirekena, *apart from operation* (3.9.1), and the samuddesa answers: kala eva vyaparah — time is nothing else. What we call an operation is time operating; what we call time is the operating. And visva-atma: whose self is the universe (3.7.41's sky, earth, wind, sun as portions of the inner-organ reality standing outside). So time is not a container of processes nor a measure of them; it is the processing, and it is everything. The Kriya-samuddesa's action (3.8.36's operation resorting to nothing) and this samuddesa's time are the same doctrine.

translation: By it, the accumulations and diminutions of corporeal things, which are divided, are severally marked out — through transformation, which is joined with difference for all of them.
commentary: Time's work on bodies. Murtinam tena bhinnanam: of *corporeal things*, *divided* by it — bodies, differentiated by time (3.9.8's divisions borrowed and handed back). Acaya-apacayah prthak laksyante: their *accumulations and diminutions* are *severally marked out* — growth and decay, the two middle vikaras (vardhate, apaksiyate). Parinamena sarvasam bheda-yogina: through *transformation*, which is *joined with difference* for *all* of them. So time marks every body's waxing and waning, and the marking is parinama — real transformation, the Samkhya's word (3.2.15's prakrti-vikara relation, there accepted as simile and its metaphysics declined). The verse grants the bodies their real change and locates the marking of it in time — which on 3.9.12's identification means: the changing *is* time. Nothing changes *in* time; the changing is what time is.

mula_deva: स कलाः कलयन् सर्वाः कालाख्यां लभते विभुः ॥ १४ ॥
mula_iast: sa kalāḥ kalayan sarvāḥ kālākhyāṃ labhate vibhuḥ || 14 ||
translation: By operations resembling the whirling-motion of a water-machine, that all-pervading one, reckoning all the moments, obtains the name 'time'.
commentary: The samuddesa's second great image, and its etymology is the doctrine. Jala-yantra-bhrama-avesa-sadrsibhih pravrttibhih: by *operations* resembling the *whirling-motion* of a *water-machine* — the araghatta, the Persian wheel with its chain of buckets: turning endlessly, each bucket rising full and descending empty, the whole driven by the water it lifts. Sa kalah kalayan sarvah kala-akhyam labhate vibhuh: that *all-pervading one*, *reckoning* all the *moments*, obtains the *name 'time'*. Kalayan — reckoning, counting, impelling: the etymology derives kala from kal, and the derivation is exact for once (against 2.170-175's general scepticism about etymologies): time is *what reckons*. So time's name says what 3.9.12 said: it is not a thing that gets counted but the counting. And the water-wheel is chosen because it is a machine whose motion is its whole reality — stop it and there is no wheel-ness left over.

mula_deva: ताः स एवानुजानाति यथा तन्तुः शकुन्तिकाः ॥ १५ ॥
mula_iast: tāḥ sa evānujānāti yathā tantuḥ śakuntikāḥ || 15 ||
translation: And those variegated operations of the universe which are held back by it — those same it permits, as a string does the birds.
commentary: The sutradhara-image (3.9.4) refined, and the refinement is beautiful. Pratibaddhah ca yah tena citrah visvasya vrttayah: the *variegated operations* of the *universe* which are *held back* by it — everything possible, restrained. Tah sa eva anujanati: *those same* it *permits*. Yatha tantuh sakuntikah: as a *string* does the *birds* — the fowler's tethered birds, each on its string: held from flying free, released to the length the string allows. The image is more precise than the puppeteer's: the birds have their own power of flight (3.7.124's all proceeding for their own becoming), and the string neither gives it nor takes it — it only permits so much and no more. So time does not move things; it lets them move, to the extent it lets them. Pratibandha and anujna (3.9.4's two operations), and the bird supplies its own wings.

translation: (16) From connection with a particular time, when the capacities have obtained ripeness, the eternal action is manifested by the act called employment. (17) But in that, the manifestation of the fruit is understood as impelled by the universal; by some wonderful operation, it is restricted by the capacities.
commentary: The mechanism of ripening. (16): visista-kala-sambandhat labdha-pakasu saktisu — from connection with a *particular time*, the *capacities* having *obtained ripeness*: paka, ripening — the word 1.84 used for the seed-traces maturing into comprehension, and 2.148 for the wine's power arising by fermentation. Kriya abhivyajyate nitya prayoga-akhyena karmana: the *eternal action* is *manifested* by the act called *employment* — the action is eternal and its manifesting is timed (3.8.20's universal-theory, which held action asadhya and apprehended as though sadhya). (17): jati-prayukta tasyam tu phala-vyaktih pratiyate — the *manifestation of the fruit* is understood as *impelled by the universal* (3.1.25's jati as prayojika of the causes). Kuto 'pi adbhutaya vrttya saktibhih sa niyamyate: by some *wonderful* operation, it is *restricted* by the capacities. Adbhuta — wonderful: the word 1.142 gave the supreme station of speech and 3.3.81 gave the shining-forth at the footless station. Where the mechanism gives out, the samuddesa says so.

translation: But thereafter, the capacity called inherence, which annuls difference, brings those individuals, by their causes, to something like oneness.
commentary: Inherence recast as a temporal power. Tatah tu samavaya-akhya saktir bhedasya badhika: the *capacity called inherence*, which *annuls difference* — samavaya, the Vaisesika's intimate relation (3.3.13's machinery), here not a category but a *sakti*, and one whose office is to annul difference. Ekatvam iva ta vyaktir apadayati karanaih: it *brings* those *individuals*, *by their causes*, to *something like oneness*. Ekatvam iva: something *like* oneness — the iva marks it, as always. So inherence is the power by which a manifold of causes yields one thing: the threads become a cloth, the moments become a life. And it is a *capacity*, working in time (3.3.10's samavaya transcending difference and non-difference and standing in quite another way). What the Vaisesika made a static bond, the samuddesa makes a temporal operation of unification.

mula_deva: ताः सर्वा व्यक्तिमायान्ति स्वच्छे छाया इवाम्भसि ॥ १९ ॥
mula_iast: tāḥ sarvā vyaktimāyānti svacche chāyā ivāmbhasi || 19 ||
translation: Then, after this restriction, those universals which are the occasioners — all of them come to manifestation, like shadows in clear water.
commentary: The universals' arrival, with an image of great delicacy. Athasmat niyamad urdhvam: *after this restriction* — once time's holding has released (3.9.4's pratibandha lifted). Jatayo yah prayojikah: those *universals* which are the *occasioners* (3.1.25's jati deploying the causes for its own manifestation). Tah sarvah vyaktim ayanti: *all* of them *come to manifestation*. Svacche chaya iva ambhasi: like *shadows in clear water* — chaya in svaccha ambhas: reflections appearing on still water, arriving all at once, present without having travelled, and vanishing if the water stirs. So the universals manifest as reflections do: not by moving into place but by the medium's becoming fit. And the medium's fitness is time's releasing. The image completes 3.9.15's birds: there, held things let go; here, what fills the space the moment it is permitted.

translation: Then, from their conforming to the causes, the qualities — preceded by the causes — are apprehended there, being the grounds of the manifestation of their own universals.
commentary: The qualities' place in the sequence. Karana-anuvidhayitvat: from their *conforming to the causes* — the qualities follow the causes' lead; they do not arrive independently. Atha karana-purvakah gunah tatra upalabhyante: *then*, *preceded by the causes*, the *qualities* are *apprehended* there. Sva-jati-vyakti-hetavah: being the *grounds of the manifestation of their own universals* — the redness is what makes redness-hood manifest (3.1.29's face manifested by water: the universal itself is what is expressed). So the sequence runs: time releases, universals manifest (3.9.19), causes operate, qualities appear conforming to them, and the qualities ground their own universals' manifestation. Each stage is a manifesting and none is a making. The whole causal order is a choreography of appearings, timed.

translation: And from the eternality of the substrata and the eternality of what rests on them — it favours those individuals; thereby standing is constructed.
commentary: How persistence is produced. Asrayanam ca nityatvam asritanam ca nityata: the *eternality of the substrata* and the *eternality of what rests on them* — both the bearers and the borne are eternal (3.1.2's nityau eva; 3.1.41's universal standing in a non-eternal substratum by the unbroken succession). Ta vyaktir anugrhnati: it *favours* those *individuals* — time assists them (3.9.11's anugraha). Sthitih tena prakalpyate: *thereby standing* is *constructed*. So persistence is not a thing's own inertia but time's continued favouring of it — prakalpyate, constructed. This is 3.9.10's sthitir niyama-purvika (standing is preceded by restriction) given its positive form: a thing stands because time keeps assisting it, and stops when time stops. The next verse draws the consequence.

translation: As, for the non-eternal, there is dependence in its arising — so also in its standing; they know that its being loosed for destruction alone is its self-dependent standing.
commentary: The verse's second half is one of the samuddesa's sharpest sayings. Anityasya yatha utpade paratantryam tatha sthitau: as, for the *non-eternal*, there is *dependence* in its *arising* — so also in its *standing*: a thing depends on others to arise, and equally to persist (3.9.21's favouring). And then: vinasaya eva tat srsta-svadhina-sthitim viduh — they know that its being *loosed* *for destruction alone* is its *self-dependent standing*. A thing stands on its own only when it has been released for destruction. To be independent is to be dying. So long as it persists it is held (3.9.4's pratibandha); the moment the holding stops it is its own and it perishes. Svadhina-sthiti — self-dependent standing — is thus not a good state but the last one, and the verse's irony is exact: autonomy is what a thing gets when time has let it go.

translation: Standing, it is favoured in its actions by the commingling entities; the operation of things possessing birth is not remembered without taking up their existence.
commentary: The condition of a thing's operating. Sthitah samsargibhih bhavaih sa kriyasu anugrhyate: *standing*, it is *favoured in its actions* by the *commingling* entities — a thing acts only as assisted by what it is with (3.3.5's upakara; 3.7.12's whatever assists is the means). Na esam sattam anudgrhya vrttir janmavatam smrta: the *operation* of things *possessing birth* is *not remembered* without *taking up their existence*. Anudgrhya — without taking up, without appropriating: a thing cannot operate without first having taken up its own existence, and that taking-up is what time's favouring gives it (3.9.21). So the born must be granted being before they can do anything, and the granting is temporal. The verse locates the whole karaka-system (3.7's crowd of self-interested operations) inside time's economy: nothing assists anything until time has let each participant be.

mula_deva: सा शक्तिं प्रतिबध्नाति जायन्ते च विरोधिनः ॥ २४ ॥
mula_iast: sā śaktiṃ pratibadhnāti jāyante ca virodhinaḥ || 24 ||
translation: That capacity of time which is called old-age, which is opposed to other capacities — it obstructs the capacity, and the opposing ones are born.
commentary: Decay named as a capacity of time. Jara-akhya kala-saktih ya: that *capacity of time* which is called *old-age* — jara: aging, the fourth of the six vikaras' declining phase (apaksiyate). Sakty-antara-virodhini: which is *opposed to other capacities* — it is a power whose whole nature is to be against powers. Sa saktim pratibadhnati: it *obstructs* the capacity — 3.9.4's pratibandha, now aimed at a thing's own powers. Jayante ca virodhinah: and the *opposing ones* are *born* — and as the thing's powers are held, their opposites arise. So aging is not the wearing-out of a thing but time's holding-back of its capacities while their contraries are released. The account is exact and it is entirely within 3.9.4's two operations: nothing is done to the aging thing; its powers are obstructed and their enemies permitted. Decay is a schedule.

mula_deva: प्रयोजकास्तु ये भावाः स्थितिभा गस्य हेतवः ।
तिरोभवन्ति ते सर्वे यत आत्मा प्रहीयते ॥ २५ ॥
mula_iast: prayojakāstu ye bhāvāḥ sthitibhā gasya hetavaḥ |
tirobhavanti te sarve yata ātmā prahīyate || 25 ||
translation: But those entities which are the occasioners, the causes of the portion of standing — all of them disappear, whereby the self is abandoned.
commentary: The mechanism of death. Prayojakah tu ye bhavah sthiti-bhagasya hetavah: those *entities* which are the *occasioners*, the *causes of the portion of standing* — whatever was favouring the thing (3.9.21, 3.9.23's samsargibhih bhavaih). Tirobhavanti te sarve: *all of them* *disappear* — they do not attack; they withdraw. Yata atma prahiyate: *whereby* the *self* is *abandoned*. So a thing dies because its supporters vanish, not because anything destroys it — 3.8.27's finding (in destruction there is non-thinghood; destruction is not a thing) given its positive mechanism. Prahiyate: is abandoned, let go (3.9.11's prahanam; 3.9.22's srsta, loosed). Death is desertion. And on 3.9.12's identification (time *is* operation), the deserting is time — which is to say that nothing kills anything, and things stop being held.

translation: Just as, by a wonderful operation, everything is born motionless, without ground, footless — so its self is abandoned.
commentary: Birth and death given the same three privations, and the vocabulary is 3.3's. Yatha eva adbhutaya vrttya: just as, by a *wonderful* operation (3.9.17's adbhuta, and 3.3.81's atyadbhuta tv iyam vrttih — 'this operation is utterly wonderful', where the self-reality of things not previously existent shines forth). Niskramam nirnibandhanam apadam jayate sarvam: *everything* is *born* *motionless*, *without ground*, *footless* — apada: 3.3.80's apada-sthana, the footless station of speech, where language has no floor to set a foot on. Tatha asya atma prahiyate: *so* its *self* is *abandoned*. Birth and death are the same wonder, and both happen where nothing supports them. So the samuddesa's account of death (3.9.25's desertion) and of birth converge on 3.3.80-81: at the place where speech has no footing, things arise and depart, and the arising is wonderful. The mechanism has run out, and the verse says so rather than inventing more.

translation: Of two actions ending in completion, inherent in different objects — how could there be delimitation, without some one relatum?
commentary: The problem that time solves. Kriyayoh apavarginyoh nana-artha-samavetayoh: of *two actions* *ending in completion*, *inherent in different objects* — two processes, in different things, each running to its own end. Sambandhina vina ekena paricchedah katham bhavet: *how* could there be *delimitation*, *without some one relatum*? How can one say which is longer, which came first, whether they overlapped? Two independent processes have no common measure unless something relates them. The question is genuine and it is the strongest argument for time's reality: something must be shared, or no comparison of durations is possible. And it is exactly the office 3.9.1's rival assigned time (parimanam kriyavatam, the measure of things possessing action). The next verse answers with an image — and the answer will grant the office and refuse the substance.

translation: As weight, established in various substances, would be measured on a balance or in the hand — so the course of action, by time.
commentary: The answer to 3.9.27, and the simile does exactly the required work. Yatha tulayam haste va nana-dravya-vyavasthitam gurutvam parimiyeta: as *weight*, established in *various substances*, would be *measured* on a *balance* or in the *hand* — different things, incommensurable in themselves, brought to comparison by a common instrument. Kalad evam kriya-gatih: so the *course of action*, *by time*. So time is the balance: what lets independent processes be compared. And notice what a balance is — it is not a substance the weights are in; it is an *operation* that brings them into relation, and it has no weight of its own that it contributes. Or the hand: even less a thing, more an act of hefting. So the office of 3.9.27 is granted (time measures) and the substance of 3.9.1 is refused (a measure is not a container). This is 3.9.12's identification given its argument: time is the weighing, not the scale-pan.

translation: It abandons the actions that stand co-occurrent with it; as rice abandons water — thereby it obtains the name 'year'.
commentary: The samuddesa's most compressed image, and its transcription is difficult. Jahati saha-vrttah ca kriyah sa samavasthitah: it *abandons* the *actions* that stand *co-occurrent* with it — time lets go of what accompanies it (3.9.25's tirobhavanti; 3.9.22's srsta). Vrihir yatha udakam: as *rice* abandons *water* — the boiling rice, which takes up water and gives it off as steam; or the ripening grain, which draws water and dries. Tena hayana-akhyam prapadyate: *thereby* it obtains the name *'year'* — hayana, the year, whose etymology the tradition connects with ha, to abandon: the year is what lets go. So time gets its name from its abandoning, as at 3.9.14 it got its name from its reckoning — two etymologies, two operations, and both are 3.9.4's: obstruction and permission, holding and letting go. The year is a letting-go, and the rice giving up its water is the figure for it.
contested: the rice-and-water simile is compressed and the transcription uncertain; whether the picture is boiling (rice releasing steam) or ripening (grain drying) cannot be settled, and the hayana-etymology (from ha, to abandon) is the traditional one the verse evidently invokes

translation: That operation of it which consists of obstruction and permission is everlasting; being divided by that, it takes on the form of sequence.
commentary: Time's self-division, and it is reflexive. Pratibandha-abhyanujnabhyam vrttir ya tasya sasvati: that *operation* of it consisting of *obstruction and permission* (3.9.4's two) is *everlasting* — the holding-and-releasing never stops, and it is what time *is* (3.9.12's kala eva vyaparah). Taya vibhajyamano 'sau bhajate krama-rupatam: *being divided by that*, it *takes on* the *form of sequence*. So time is divided by its own operation: the holding-and-releasing is what carves time into before and after, and time is the holding-and-releasing. The circle is exact and it is not vicious — it is what a self-operating power looks like. Bhajate: takes on, wears (3.2.8's tattvam eva avikalpitam vikalpa-rupam bhajate: reality itself, unconstructed, takes on the form of construction). Sequence is time's own garment, worn by its own act.

mula_deva: समत्वं विषमत्वं वा स एकः प्रतिपद्यते ॥ ३१ ॥
mula_iast: samatvaṃ viṣamatvaṃ vā sa ekaḥ pratipadyate || 31 ||
translation: From difference of agents, having gone to excellence and diminution in respect of their purposes — that one thing attains sameness or unevenness.
commentary: Time's apparent variability. Kartr-bhedat tad-arthesu prakarsa-apacayau gatah: from *difference of agents*, having gone to *excellence and diminution* in respect of *their purposes* — the same hour is long to the waiting and short to the busy: time's felt extent varies with whose purposes are in play. Samatvam visamatvam va sa ekah pratipadyate: *that one* thing attains *sameness or unevenness*. So time is one and is met as even or uneven, according to the agents and their ends. The verse does not psychologize this away: the unevenness is real, and it belongs to time-as-met, since time is nothing but its operating on somebody's purposes (3.9.12). And prakarsa — excellence, the graded more-ness of 3.5.2-3 — is here temporal: an hour can be more.

translation: As, from difference of action, the designation 'carpenter' and the rest proceeds with respect to one thing — so, from difference of action, the designation 'season' and the rest arises with respect to one thing.
commentary: The seasons explained by an occupational analogy. Kriya-bhedad yatha ekasmin taksady-akhya pravartate: as, from *difference of action*, the designation *'carpenter'* and the rest proceeds with respect to *one* thing — one man is carpenter, cook, father: the names track what he does, and he is one. Kriya-bhedat tatha ekasmin rtv-ady-akhya upajayate: so, from difference of action, the designation *'season'* and the rest arises with respect to *one* thing. Spring and autumn are one time under different doings — as teacher and uncle are one man under different relations (3.8.63's closing simile). So the calendar is an occupational list: time named by what it is doing. And the analogy's force is that nobody thinks the carpenter and the cook are two men, or that carpentry is a substance the man is in.

translation: 'Commencement', 'action', and 'completion' — these are spoken of, from a difference of superimposition of other properties, upon what is of existent-and-non-existent self.
commentary: The three phases of any process, and their status. Arambhah ca kriya ca eva nistha ca iti abhidhiyate: *'commencement'*, *'action'*, and *'completion'* — the three moments every act is analysed into. Dharmantaranam adhyasa-bhedat: from a *difference of superimposition* of *other properties* — adhyasa: superimposition (3.1.6's central term; 3.8.11's asad-adhyasa, the superimposition of what is not there). Sad-asad-atmanah: upon what is of *existent-and-non-existent self* — the action, whose phases are existent and non-existent by sequence (3.8.6, 3.8.19's atma eka iva grhyate). So the three phases are superimposed on something that is neither wholly there nor wholly not, and they differ only in which properties are laid on. This is the Kriya-samuddesa's analysis (3.8.4's construed collection) restated as time's doing: the phases are time's superimpositions.

mula_deva: न ह्यात्मा कस्यचिद् भेत्तुं प्रचेतुं वापि शक्यते ॥ ३४ ॥
mula_iast: na hyātmā kasyacid bhettuṃ pracetuṃ vāpi śakyate || 34 ||
translation: And as much as it is for the dyad and the rest, so much is it also for a mountain; for the self of anything whatever cannot be split or accumulated.
commentary: Time's indifference to magnitude. Yavan ca dvy-anukadinam tavan bimavato 'py asau: as much as it is for the *dyad* and the rest — the smallest composite of the atomists — *so much* is it also for a *mountain*. Time is not more for big things. Na hi atma kasyacid bhettum pracetum vapi sakyate: for the *self* of anything whatever cannot be *split* or *accumulated*. Nothing's self can be divided or added to — which is 3.6.15's great leveling (partlessness is equal for the atom and for the pot) now applied to time: as nothing has parts of its own, nothing has more time than anything else. So the mountain's age and the dyad's are not different quantities of a stuff. Duration is not a possession.

translation: But by other entities, an accumulation is constructed for others; thereby it is understood: 'this slowly, this quickly'.
commentary: Where duration comes from, then. Anyaih tu bhavaih anyesam pracayah parikalpyate: by *other entities*, an *accumulation* is *constructed* for *others* — durations are conferred by comparison with something else: the mountain is old *relative to* the flower's seasons. Sanaih idam idam ksipram iti tena pratiyate: *thereby* it is understood: '*this slowly*, *this quickly*'. So fast and slow are relational constructions, borrowed from a comparison-term, and neither belongs to the process. This is 3.9.28's balance (weight measured by an instrument that contributes none) and 3.9.34's leveling (no thing has its own duration) combining: durations are conferred, comparisons confer them, and time is the conferring.

translation: And being conditioned by action, it attains past-ness, future-ness and present-ness, divided by eleven forms.
commentary: The tense-doctrine, announced. Kriya-upadhih ca san: *being conditioned by action* — upadhi: the limiting adjunct (3.2.2's asatya-upadhi; 1.75's grahana-upadhi-bheda). Time takes its tenses from the actions that condition it, not from itself. Bhuta-bhavisyad-vartamanatah: *past-ness*, *future-ness* and *present-ness* — the three tenses, and they are *-ta* abstractions: past-*ness*, conditions rather than times. Ekadasabhih akaraih vibhaktah pratipadyate: *divided by eleven forms* it attains them. Eleven: Panini's ten lakaras plus one, or the traditional enumeration the next verses give. So the three tenses are eleven forms, and all of them are time conditioned by action. Time has no tense of its own — which follows from 3.9.12: if time is the operating, its tenses are the operations'.

translation: And being conditioned by action, it attains past-ness, future-ness and present-ness, divided by eleven forms.
commentary: The source transmits this verse identically with 3.9.36 — the same two lines under two page-labels. Whether the wisdomlib edition has duplicated a single karika across two pages, or whether the text genuinely repeats it (a not-unknown scribal or recensional event), cannot be settled from the transcription alone. We record the duplication rather than silently collapsing it, and the substantive commentary is given at 3.9.36.
contested: the verse is transmitted identically with 3.9.36 in the source; this is very likely a pagination or transcription fault rather than a genuine repetition in the text, and readers consulting other editions should expect the numbering here to diverge

translation: The past there is five-fold, and the future four-fold; the present is declared two-fold — thus eleven constructions.
commentary: The eleven enumerated. Bhutah panca-vidhah tatra: the *past* is *five-fold* — Sanskrit's several past forms (the aorist, the imperfect, the perfect, and the periphrastics, distinguished by remoteness and witness). Bhavisyan ca catur-vidhah: the *future* *four-fold*. Vartamano dvidha akhyatah: the *present* is declared *two-fold*. Ity ekadasa kalpanah: thus *eleven* *constructions*. And the last word is the doctrine: kalpanah — *constructions*, not times. The tense-system is eleven ways of construing, not eleven regions of a substance. So Sanskrit's remarkably fine past-tense apparatus (five forms where a modern European language has two or three) is not a finer map of a real past; it is a richer set of postings (3.7.103's niyamah sabde na tu vastuni: the restriction is in the word, not in the thing).

mula_deva: काले निधाय स्वं रूपं प्रज्ञया यन्निगृह्यते ।
mula_iast: kāle nidhāya svaṃ rūpaṃ prajñayā yannigṛhyate |
translation: Having deposited their own form in time, what is grasped by the understanding — from that, entities withdraw, their capacities having passed over into it.
commentary: The past, and it is a deposit-account. Kale nidhaya svam rupam: *having deposited their own form in time* — things leave their shape with time as one leaves goods in trust. Prajnaya yan nigrhyate: what is *grasped by the understanding* — what memory holds is that deposit. Bhavas tato nivartante: *from that*, entities *withdraw* — they go, and the form stays. Tatra sankranta-saktayah: their *capacities having passed over into it* — sankranta: transferred, migrated. So the past is where things leave their forms and powers when they withdraw, and what we grasp of the past is the deposit, not the things. This is a genuinely strange and beautiful account: the past is a repository, the deposits are real, and the depositors are gone. And it explains how the past is knowable (its forms are there) without being present (its things are not).

mula_deva: भाविनां चैव यद्रूपं तस्य च प्रतिबिम्बकम् ।
mula_iast: bhāvināṃ caiva yadrūpaṃ tasya ca pratibimbakam |
translation: And the form of things to come — the reflection of that, too, is possible in time alone, as in a well-polished mirror.
commentary: The future, and the image is exact. Bhavinam ca eva yad rupam: the *form* of *things to come*. Tasya ca pratibimbakam: the *reflection* of that. Sunirmrsta iva adarse: as in a *well-polished mirror* — sunirmrsta: thoroughly wiped, burnished. Kala eva upapadyate: is possible *in time alone*. So the future is time reflecting what has not arrived — a mirror-image with no object yet before it. The image completes 3.9.39's pair: the past is a deposit, the future is a reflection, and time holds both. And the reflection-figure carries all its earlier freight (1.49's borrowed properties; 2.294's reflection failing the effect-test; 3.9.19's shadows in clear water): the future is *there* in time as a face is in a mirror — present to be seen, doing nothing, and not a second thing. Sunirmrsta — well-polished — is the condition: a clouded mirror shows no future, and time's clarity is what makes anticipation possible.

translation: As a current draws along grass, leaves, creepers and the like — so time too impels the measures of things possessing measure.
commentary: The current, and it is the samuddesa's third great image. Trna-parna-lata-adini yatha srotah anukarsati: as a *current* *draws along* *grass*, *leaves*, *creepers* and the like — the river carrying its debris: each thing moving at the water's speed, none of them swimming. Pravartayati kalo 'pi matra matravatam tatha: so *time* too *impels* the *measures* of things *possessing measure*. So things are carried, not travelling: their durations are the current's, as the leaf's speed is the river's. The image was already used at 3.7.33 (the capacity, in a single substratum, draws downward like a current) and here it is time's. And what a current is matters: not a thing in the water but the water's own moving — which is 3.9.12's identification exactly. Time is not what carries things along; it is the carrying.

translation: As the wind, having entered them as it were, connects up the motions of things possessing motion — so the self of time brings about the form of sequence.
commentary: The wind, and it is the fourth image and the most intimate. Avisya iva anusandhatte yatha gatimatam gatih vayuh: as the *wind*, having *entered* them *as it were*, *connects up* the *motions* of things possessing motion — the wind that carries the leaves does not push them from outside; it enters and moves them from within, and their separate motions become one motion. Anusandhatte: connects up, threads together, joins into a sequence (the same root as the sutradhara's thread, 3.9.4). Tatha eva kala-atma vidhatte krama-rupatam: so the *self of time* *brings about* the *form of sequence*. So time works as wind does: entering (avisya *iva*: as it were — the iva is careful) and threading the many motions into one order. This is the puppeteer (3.9.4), the string (3.9.15), the current (3.9.41) and now the wind — four images for one operation, and each more inward than the last.

translation: The division of the solstices, and the fixed motion of the luminaries; and the ceasings and originations of beings — these are grounded in that.
commentary: The observable order, grounded. Ayana-pravibhagah ca: the *division of the solstices* — the year's great turning-points, by which the calendar is fixed. Gatih ca jyotisam dhruva: and the *fixed motion* of the *luminaries* — the stars' unfailing courses, the most reliable regularity anyone knew. Nivrtti-prabhavah ca eva bhutanam: and the *ceasings and originations* of *beings* — birth and death. Tan-nibandhanah: these are *grounded in that* — in time. So the astronomical order and the biological one have one ground, and it is the holding-and-releasing (3.9.4). The verse is quietly ambitious: it claims that what makes the sun turn at the solstice and what makes a man die are the same operation, differently scheduled. And nibandhana is the grounding-word the whole work has used since 1.13.

mula_deva: नक्षत्राख्या पृथक् तेषु चिह्नमात्रं तु तारकाः ॥ ४४ ॥
mula_iast: nakṣatrākhyā pṛthak teṣu cihnamātraṃ tu tārakāḥ || 44 ||
translation: (44) Those measures of the moments which follow upon time's operation are severally called 'naksatra' among them; but the stars are a mere mark. (45) By the cries of beasts and birds, by the operations of stationary things, and by the transformations of shadow and the rest — the season's abode is determined.
commentary: How time is read, and the epistemology is careful. (44): matranam parimanah ye kala-vrtty-anuyayinah: the *measures* of the *moments* which *follow upon time's operation* — the divisions that track the operating. Naksatra-akhya prthak tesu: they are severally called *'naksatra'* — the lunar mansions, the sky's calendar. And the correction: cihna-matram tu tarakah — the *stars* are a *mere mark*. The stars do not constitute the naksatras; they mark them. What is real is the operation; the constellations are its dial. (45) then lists the other dials: rutaih mrga-sakuntanam — by the *cries* of beasts and birds; sthavaranam ca vrttibhih — by the *operations* of *stationary* things (the flowering, the leaf-fall); chaya-adi-parinamaih ca — and by the *transformations of shadow* and the rest (the gnomon's shadow, the day's lengthening). Rtu-dhama nirupyate: the *season's abode* is *determined*. So one reads time from birdsong, budding and shadow as much as from stars — and all of them equally are marks (cihna-matra), none of them time.

translation: This coming-to-appearance which is seen as though possessing sequence — of a universe that is sequenceless — that is time's activity.
commentary: The samuddesa's thesis, stated in one line. Nirbhasa-upagamo yo 'yam kramavan iva drsyate: this *coming-to-appearance* which is *seen* *as though possessing sequence* — the iva marks it, as it has marked every claim of this kind since 1.48. Akramasya api visvasya: *of a universe* that is *sequenceless* — the world does not run; nothing is in order. Tat kalasya vicestitam: *that* is *time's activity*. So the whole appearance of sequence, in a sequenceless universe, is what time does. Vicestita: activity, exertion, the writhing or working of something — a strong word, and it fits 3.9.12 (time is operation): time does not exist and then act; the acting is all there is, and its product is the look of order. Everything the samuddesa has built converges here: the puppeteer, the string, the current, the wind — four figures for one activity, and this is what the activity produces.

translation: As the settled arrangement of far and near has the road for its locus — so the settled arrangement of long and quick has time for its locus.
commentary: The parallel that places time among the constructions. Duranti ka-vyavasthanam adhva-adhikaranam yatha: as the *settled arrangement* of *far and near* has the *road* for its *locus* — distance is measured along a road, and the road is where far-and-near live. Cira-ksipra-vyavasthanam kala-adhikaranam tatha: so the settled arrangement of *long and quick* has *time* for its *locus*. The parallel is exact and it is deflationary: as a road is not distance but where distance is reckoned, time is not duration but where duration is reckoned. And a road, notoriously, is not a thing — it is a way, made by going. Adhikarana: the locus, the karaka that assists at two removes without touching anything (3.7.148). So time is the locus of durations as the road is of distances: indispensable, reckoned along, and not an item.

mula_deva: भेदा इव त्रयः सिद्धा यांल्लोको नातिवर्तते ॥ ४८ ॥
mula_iast: bhedā iva trayaḥ siddhā yāṃlloko nātivartate || 48 ||
translation: Of that undivided time, in practice, three differences — as it were, made by action — are established, which the world does not transgress.
commentary: The three tenses, and every qualification matters. Tasya abhinnasya kalasya: of that *undivided* time — one, partless (3.9.7's neither one nor many). Vyavahare: *in practice* — the divisions belong to transaction, not to time (2.297's sabdah loka-nibandhanah). Kriya-krtah bheda iva trayah siddhah: *three* differences, *as it were*, *made by action*, are *established* — kriya-krta: action-made (3.9.36's kriya-upadhih: time conditioned by action), and iva: as though. Yan loko na ativartate: which the *world does not transgress* — the phrase 2.462 used of sequence and simultaneity (which the world does not transgress). So the three tenses are practical, action-made, apparent divisions of an undivided time — and nobody escapes them. Both halves stand: constructed, and inescapable (3.6.18's dik-kala-parikalpana standing like consciousness itself).

translation: Three capacities of the one time stand established, by connection with which there is the seeing and the non-seeing of entities that exist.
commentary: The tenses recast as saktis, and this is the samuddesa's central move. Ekasya saktayah tisrah kalasya samavasthitah: *three capacities* of the *one* time stand established — not three times but three powers of one (1.2's Brahman resting on its differentiated powers; 3.7.37's agency alone, sixfold). And their office: yat-sambandhena bhavanam darsana-adarsane satam — by *connection with which* there is the *seeing* and the *non-seeing* of entities *that exist*. Satam — *that exist*: the entities exist throughout; what the tenses govern is whether they are *seen*. So past and future things are not absent; they are unseen. The tense-system is an optics, not an inventory — and this single word satam is the hinge of the whole doctrine. Everything is; the three powers decide what shows.

mula_deva: द्वाभ्यां स किल शक्तिभ्यां भावानां वरणात्मकः ।
mula_iast: dvābhyāṃ sa kila śaktibhyāṃ bhāvānāṃ varaṇātmakaḥ |
translation: By two of its capacities it is, indeed, of the nature of a screen for entities; but the capacity called 'present' is the illuminator of the entities' form.
commentary: The three sorted, two against one. Dvabhyam sa kila saktibhyam bhavanam varanatmakah: by *two* of its capacities it is *of the nature of a screen* — varana: a covering, a curtain, a screening-off. Past and future are screens: they hide what exists (3.9.49's satam). Saktih tu vartamana-akhya bhava-rupa-prakasini: but the capacity called *'present'* is the *illuminator* of the entities' *form*. So of time's three powers, two conceal and one reveals; and 'the present' names not a stretch of time but a lighting. This is the samuddesa's most economical statement of its tense-doctrine, and it inverts the ordinary picture entirely: things do not come into the present and pass out; they stand, and time's screens draw back and close.

mula_deva: अतीताख्या तु या शक्तिस्तया जन्म विरुध्यते ॥ ५१ ॥
mula_iast: atītākhyā tu yā śaktistayā janma virudhyate || 51 ||
translation: The capacity called 'future' is not the obstructor of the capacity of birth; but that capacity called 'past' — by it, birth is opposed.
commentary: The two screens distinguished by their relation to birth. Anagata janma-sakteh saktir apratibandhika: the capacity called *'future'* is *not the obstructor* of the *capacity of birth* — the future screens without preventing: what is future is unborn and *may* be born; the screen does not bar the door. Atita-akhya tu ya saktih taya janma virudhyate: but that capacity called *'past'* — by *it*, *birth* is *opposed*. The past screens and bars: what is past cannot be born again. So the two screens differ in modality, and the difference is exactly the asymmetry we feel between past and future — the future is open and the past is closed. And the samuddesa has derived that asymmetry from time's powers rather than assuming it: pratibandha (3.9.4's obstruction) is what the past does, and the future merely conceals.

translation: These three roads stand established, having the nature of darkness and light; they are sequenceless, and in them the sequence of entities is apprehended.
commentary: The image that gathers 3.9.50-51. Tamah-prakasavat tv ete trayo 'dhvano vyavasthitah: these *three roads* stand established, having the nature of *darkness and light* — adhvan: road, the word 3.9.47 used for the locus of far-and-near; and the three roads are two dark and one lit. Akramah tesu bhavanam kramah samupalabhyate: they are *sequenceless*, and *in them* the *sequence of entities* is *apprehended*. So the three tenses are themselves without sequence — the past is not before the future — and the sequence we find is the entities' as apprehended in them. The image is precise: a lit stretch of road between two dark ones, and things showing as they pass through the light. Nothing moves the road; the road does not run. And 3.9.46's finding stands: the sequence is the appearance, and the appearing is time's activity.

translation: But two of them there are of the form of darkness; of one, the standing is like light. And for some, even the past turns round again.
commentary: The image confirmed and then complicated. Dvau tu tatra tamo-rupau: *two* of them are of the *form of darkness* — past and future, the screens (3.9.50's varanatmakah). Ekasya alokavat sthitih: of *one*, the *standing* is *like light* — the present. Then: atitam api kesamcit punar viparivartate — and for *some*, even the *past* *turns round again*. Some hold that the past returns — the cyclical cosmologies, the recurring ages, the doctrine that what has been will be again. So 3.9.51's asymmetry (the past bars birth) is not universally granted: on a cyclical view the past's screen is not final. Bhartrhari reports it (kesamcit) without adopting it, and its presence here is honest doxography: the tense-doctrine he has built assumes an asymmetry his tradition does not unanimously hold.

translation: What has that property attains present-ness simultaneously; and from the present-ness of some, it likewise goes to past-ness.
commentary: The cyclical view of 3.9.53 developed. Yugapad vartamanatvam tad-dharma pratipadyate: what has *that* property attains *present-ness* *simultaneously* — on the recurrence-view, the returning past becomes present all at once. Kesamcid vartamanatvat ca eti tadvad atitatam: and from the *present-ness* of *some*, it *likewise goes to past-ness*. So things cycle through the three, and the cycling is what the recurrence-doctrine describes. Bhartrhari is reporting a view in which the three saktis are not fixed stations but phases each thing passes through and re-enters — and it is compatible with his own account (the three are powers of one time, not regions) while its cyclicality is an addition he does not endorse. The report is careful and the reader is left to weigh it.

translation: Drawn forth by the assistance of a cause, it has come to present-ness; the cause's assistance having subsided, it does not again come to seeing.
commentary: The linear account, against 3.9.53-54's cyclical one. Hetu-upakarad aksiptah vartamanatvam agatah: *drawn forth* by the *assistance of a cause*, it has come to *present-ness* — a thing enters the light because something assists it (3.9.23's favouring by commingling entities). Santa-hetupakarah san punar na upaiti darsanam: the cause's assistance having *subsided*, it does *not again* come to *seeing*. Santa: quieted, stilled. So on this account the present is where a thing stands while its causes assist, and when they subside it goes dark and does not return — 3.9.25's death (the supporters withdrawing) as the general law of pastness. This is the asymmetry of 3.9.51 given its mechanism, and it is set here against 3.9.53's recurrence without adjudication. Two accounts of whether the dark road behind is final.

translation: For some, there are only two capacity-paths of the all-pervading time, by which it effects the opening and closing of the eyes of entities.
commentary: A rival reduction, and its image is lovely. Dve eva kalasya vibhoh kesamcit sakti-vartmani: only *two* *capacity-paths* of the *all-pervading* time, for *some*. Karoti yabhyam bhavanam unmilana-nimilane: by which it effects the *opening and closing of the eyes* of entities — unmilana and nimilana: the eye's opening and shutting; also the blossoming and closing of a flower. So on this account there are not three powers but two — a showing and a hiding — and past and future collapse into one darkness. The reduction is economical: 3.9.50 already made two of the three screens, and this view asks why two screens are two powers. And the image is the samuddesa's gentlest: things blink open and shut, and time is the blinking.

translation: Some declare that they are divided, by their own nature, by times which have separate meanings, and are characterized by the intellect's gathering-together.
commentary: The idealist option. Kalabhih prthag-arthabhih pravibhakte svabhavatah: divided, *by their own nature*, by *times* which have *separate meanings* — the tenses as genuinely distinct times, each with its own content. Kecid buddhy-anusamhara-laksane te pracaksate: *some* declare them to be *characterized by the intellect's gathering-together*. Buddhy-anusamhara: the mind's drawing-together — 2.1's sixth view of the sentence (buddhy-anusamhrtih, the gathering-together in cognition, which the Vakyakanda's whole argument favoured). So the tenses are the mind's gatherings: past is what the intellect has drawn together, future what it projects. The view makes tense wholly cognitive — and it is very close to Bhartrhari's own (3.7.104's buddhy-avastha carving the karakas), differing only in making the gathering constitutive of time rather than of its divisions.

mula_deva: ज्ञानानुगतशक्तिं वा बाह्यं वा सत्यतः स्थितम् ।
mula_iast: jñānānugataśaktiṃ vā bāhyaṃ vā satyataḥ sthitam |
translation: Whether a capacity that follows upon cognition, or something external standing in truth — without resorting to a self of time, it is not possible to transact.
commentary: The dispute of 3.9.57 declined, and the declining is the samuddesa's method. Jnana-anugata-saktim va: whether a *capacity that follows upon cognition* — the idealist option (3.9.57); bahyam va satyatah sthitam: or something *external* *standing in truth* — the realist option (3.9.1's substance). Kala-atmanam anasritya vyavhartum na sakyate: *without resorting to a self of time*, it is *not possible to transact*. So: whichever it is, one cannot do without it. The office is undeniable and the ontology is undecided — which is 1.28's move (eternal or produced, no beginning either way), 2.433's (all-natured or self-less, the word is sovereign either way), 3.1.107's (whatever the substrate, the doctrine stands). Vyavahartum: to transact — and 3.6.19 already said the theorist who abandons these entities takes hold of them again.

mula_deva: ताभिः स्वशक्तिभिः सर्वं सदैवास्ति च नास्ति च ॥ ५९ ॥
mula_iast: tābhiḥ svaśaktibhiḥ sarvaṃ sadaivāsti ca nāsti ca || 59 ||
translation: For some, there are three being-capacities of each and every entity; by those own capacities, everything always both is and is not.
commentary: The three saktis relocated from time to things. Tisro bhavasya bhavasya kesamcid bhava-saktayah: *three* *being-capacities* of *each and every* entity — every thing carries its own past, present and future as powers, rather than passing through time's three. Tabhih sva-saktibhih sarvam sada eva asti ca na asti ca: by those *own capacities*, *everything* *always* both *is* and *is not*. Sada eva: always — and both. So on this view nothing ever changes tense; each thing is permanently existent-and-non-existent by its own three powers, and the appearance of passage is the shifting of which power shows. This is 3.3.87's finding (the eternal one, whose self is both being and non-being, shines forth as multiform) applied to each thing severally — and it dissolves time into the things, as 3.9.12 dissolved it into operation.

translation: Being not different from existence, those three also stand established; and from their non-difference from that, sequence and existence-and-non-existence are not differentiated.
commentary: The reduction completed, and it lands where the Padakanda always lands. Sattvad avyatirekena tah tisro 'pi vyavasthitah: *being not different from existence*, those *three* also stand established — the three capacities are not other than satta (3.1.33's sole universal, in which all words stand established). Kramah tah tad-abhedac ca sad-asattvam na bhidyate: and from their *non-difference* from that, *sequence* and *existence-and-non-existence* are *not differentiated*. So: the three tenses are satta's powers, satta is one, and therefore sequence and being/non-being are not two things but one Existence variously showing. This is 3.3.84 exactly (in reality there is no difference whatever of simultaneity from sequence, just as no non-being is determined as other than being) — and its arrival here completes the Kala-samuddesa's absorption into the Padakanda's single doctrine. Time, action, being: one satta, carved.

translation: By seeing and non-seeing it is one; but that very thing is seen and unseen. There is no oneness of the roads, and nothing whatever withdraws.
commentary: The three roads (3.9.52) audited, and the audit is severe. Darsana-adarsanena ekam: by *seeing* and *non-seeing* it is *one* — the thing is one, whether shown or screened (3.9.49's satam: the entities exist throughout). Drstadrstam tad eva tu: but *that very thing* is *seen and unseen* — one thing, both. Adhvanam ekata na asti: there is *no oneness of the roads* — the three are not one road; the metaphor of a single track through darkness and light is refused. Na ca kimcin nivartate: and *nothing whatever* *withdraws* — nothing goes away, ever. So the picture of things passing along a road out of the light is dismantled: nothing travels, nothing leaves, and the three roads are not a road. What is left is 3.9.50's screens — powers that show and hide what stands unmoving.

translation: The seeing of time is differentiated by the positions of capacity, of self, and of deity; that is first in nescience — which does not exist in knowledge.
commentary: The three schools of time named, and then placed. Sakty-atma-devata-paksaih bhinnam kalasya darsanam: the *seeing of time* is differentiated by the positions of *capacity* (time as sakti — Bhartrhari's own, 3.9.49), of *self* (time as atman, a substance: 3.9.1's Vaisesika), and of *deity* (time as a god — Kala the destroyer, the Mahabharata's dark deity). Three doctrines, three darsanas. And then the placing: prathamam tad avidyayam yad vidyayam na vidyate — *that* is *first in nescience*, *which does not exist in knowledge*. All three belong to avidya — the domain of constructed difference (2.233's sciences describing only avidya) — and none survives into vidya. So the samuddesa's own doctrine, the sakti-paksa, is named as one of three and consigned with the others. This is the most unsparing thing in the samuddesa: it classifies its own position as nescience.

translation: (63) If, time being undivided, an appearance of difference is seen in short, long, prolated and the rest — that is like the cognition of slow and quick. (64) By the repetition of short, long and prolated, in water-clocks and the like — how could there be a connection with accumulation, caused by mere construction?
commentary: An objection about measured time. (63): if time is *undivided* (3.9.48's abhinna), whence the *appearance of difference* in the vowel-quantities — hrasva, dirgha, pluta, the measured units by which recitation is timed? The answer offered: sa cira-ksipra-buddhi-vat — that is *like the cognition of slow and quick* (3.9.35's constructed durations; 2.23's cognitions of slow and quick, which do not themselves differ in time). But (64) presses: hrasva-dirgha-pluta-avrttya nalika-salilaadisu — by the *repetition* of short, long, prolated, in *water-clocks* and the like — katham pracaya-yogah syat kalpana-matra-hetukah: *how* could there be a *connection with accumulation*, caused by *mere construction*? Water accumulates in the clock; the accumulation is measured; and a mere construction does not fill a vessel. The objection is genuine and it is the strongest against the whole doctrine: constructed time does not explain the clepsydra. 3.9.70 will answer it.

translation: (65) It is accumulated by the accumulation of the occasioning ground of manifestation; but the word's reality, though undivided, is not of the nature of accumulation. (66) Thus a difference of a quarter-mora, or of ten such, by an option of measure, does not exist in the word's self. (67) Those words which stand, as it were, in the interval, by a kind of arising-in-consequence, are the means of comprehension for the comprehenders.
commentary: The answer for the word, before the answer for time. (65): abhivyakti-nimittasya pracayena praciyate: it is *accumulated* by the *accumulation of the occasioning ground of manifestation* — what accumulates is the manifesting sound, not the word; abhinnam api sabdasya tattvam apracaya-atmakam: the word's *reality*, though *undivided*, is *not of the nature of accumulation*. So the morae pile up in the utterance and not in the sphota (1.76-77's prakrta and vaikrta dhvani, the quantities lodged in the manifesting sound). (66): hence no *quarter-mora* difference, nor of ten, exists *in the word's self* — the measures are the medium's. (67): and the intermediate words — antarale iva sthitah, standing *as it were in the interval*, arising anunispadi (2.121's entailed) — are *the means of comprehension*: upayah pratipattaye, the ladder (2.38's discardable means). So the measured time of recitation is the manifesting stream's, and the word is untouched. The parallel is being laid for time itself.

translation: Taking up this or that particular limit, the one time of things possessing moments is constructed as partaking of the differences of instant, month and season.
commentary: How time's units are made. Visistam avadhim tam tam upadaya prakalpyate: taking up *this or that particular limit* — avadhi: the limit, the reference-point (3.6.2's avadhi from which direction separates; 3.7.137's dhruva). Kalah kalavatam ekah: the *one* time of things possessing moments. Ksana-masa-rtu-bheda-bhak: constructed as *partaking of* the differences of *instant*, *month*, *season*. So the units are made by taking up limits: pick a reference-point and a return, and you have a unit. The instant, the month, the season differ only in which avadhi is taken — the heartbeat, the moon, the solstice. And prakalpyate: constructed. Time is one; the units are chosen limits; and nothing in time favours one choice over another (3.9.47's road, along which distances are reckoned).

translation: And from a difference in the intellect's grasping, in the standing that is of the nature of practice — just so much is the instant, the time, or the aeons and the world-ages.
commentary: The units' relativity, stated without hedging. Buddhy-avagraha-bhedat ca: from a difference in the *intellect's grasping* — the units follow how the mind takes hold (3.7.104's buddhy-avastha carving the karakas; 3.7.3's sadhana-vyavahara grounded in a state of the intellect). Vyavahara-atmani sthiteh: in the *standing* that is *of the nature of practice*. Tavan eva ksanah kalo yugam anvantarani va: *just so much* is the *instant*, the *time*, or the *aeons* and the *world-ages*. Tavan eva: just so much — the instant and the aeon are made the same way, by a grasp, and differ only in the grasp's scale. So there is no smallest unit and no largest: the ksana is not time's atom, and the manvantara is not its total. Both are how long a mind held something.

translation: By obstruction and permission, in that which rests in the aperture of the tube — that trickling in the water: that is time's very activity.
commentary: The water-clock, and it answers 3.9.64's objection exactly. The nalika is the clepsydra: a pierced vessel set in water, filling through its hole and sinking at a fixed interval. Pratibandha-abhyanujnabhyam: by *obstruction* and *permission* — 3.9.4's two operations, and here they are visible: the tube's wall obstructs, the aperture permits. Nalaka-vivara-asrite yad ambhasi praksaranam: that *trickling* in the *water* which *rests in the aperture of the tube*. Tat kalasya eva cestitam: *that* is *time's very activity* — cestita: 3.9.46's word for what time does. So the clepsydra does not measure time; the trickling *is* time — obstruction and permission, made of brass and water. The objection of 3.9.64 asked how a mere construction fills a vessel, and the answer is that time was never a construction laid over the trickle: the trickle is the holding-and-releasing, which is what time is (3.9.12). The clock is not time's instrument; it is time's portrait.

mula_deva: अल्पे महति वा छिद्रे तत्संबन्धे न भिद्यते ।
mula_iast: alpe mahati vā chidre tatsaṃbandhe na bhidyate |
translation: Whether the hole be small or large, in connection with that it does not differ; time's operation, and its self too, follows just that of it.
commentary: The clepsydra pressed, and the result is the samuddesa's. Alpe mahati va chidre tat-sambandhe na bhidyate: whether the *hole* be *small or large*, in connection with that it does *not differ* — time does not run faster through a wide aperture; the clock is calibrated to the hole, and time is not. Kalasya vrttir atma api tam eva asya anuvartate: time's *operation*, and *its self* too, *follows just that of it*. So time's operation follows the trickling and time's *self* follows it too — atma api: even its self. There is nothing behind the trickle that the trickle measures. This completes 3.9.70: the clock is not a portrait of time but time itself, and asking whether the clock is accurate is asking whether the trickling trickles. The verse is the samuddesa's flattest identification, and it is delivered in plumbing.

mula_deva: आक्रीड इव कालस्य दृश्यते यः स्वशक्तिभिः ।
mula_iast: ākrīḍa iva kālasya dṛśyate yaḥ svaśaktibhiḥ |
translation: That which is seen as it were a playground of time, of the many-formed one, by its own capacities — by that it is manifoldly differentiated among the entities.
commentary: Time's variety, and the image is a play-space. Akrida iva kalasya drsyate yah sva-saktibhih: that which is *seen* as it were a *playground* of *time* — akrida: a pleasure-garden, a place of sport; the word carries lila, the divine play. Bahu-rupasya bhavesu bahudha tena bhidyate: of the *many-formed* one, it is *manifoldly differentiated* among the *entities*. So the world is time's playground, and time's many forms are its games. The image softens the puppeteer (3.9.4's sutradhara: a stage-manager working the strings) into something less coercive — not a machine driven but a garden played in. And both are true on the doctrine: the holding-and-releasing is a discipline and a sport, and 3.9.15's tethered birds are exactly a game.

mula_deva: त्वचिसारस्य वा वृद्धिं तृणसारस्य वा दधत् ।
mula_iast: tvacisārasya vā vṛddhiṃ tṛṇasārasya vā dadhat |
translation: Or bearing the growth of a bark-pithed tree, or of a grass-pithed one — to just that extent, by connection with their growth, time's reality is optioned.
commentary: Time read from growth-rates. Tvaci-sarasya va vrddhim trna-sarasya va dadhat: *bearing* the *growth* of a *bark-pithed* tree, or of a *grass-pithed* one — two kinds of plant, growing at their own rates: the hardwood slowly, the reed fast. Tavad tad-vrddhi-yogena kala-tattvam vikalpate: to *just that extent*, by *connection with their growth*, *time's reality* is *optioned*. Vikalpate: is optioned, constructed as an alternative (3.9.69's tavan eva ksanah: just so much is the instant). So time's reality is measured out by whatever is growing, and the hardwood's year and the reed's are different lengths of the same year. This is 3.9.35's relational duration (accumulation constructed for one thing by another) with living clocks — and it belongs with 3.9.44-45's birdsong and shadow: time is read from anything that changes, and every reading is a marking.

translation: Even in the transgression of the measures, there is no transgression of it; there is no difference whatever of the road by a difference of the goer's going.
commentary: Time's invariance under all its measurements. Vyatikrame 'pi matranam tasya na asti vyatikramah: even in the *transgression of the measures* — even when the units are exceeded, miscounted, or run over — there is *no transgression of it*: time is not violated by a bad clock. Na gantr-gati-bhedena marga-bhedo 'sti kascana: there is *no difference whatever* of the *road* by a *difference of the goer's going*. The road does not change when travellers walk at different speeds. So the measures vary (3.9.73's trees) and the measured does not — and the road-image (3.9.47's adhva-adhikaranam; 3.9.52's three roads) does the work: what is reckoned along is untouched by the reckoning. This is the invariance that 3.9.71's clepsydra seemed to threaten, restored: the trickle is time, and a different hole is a different trickle and the same time.

translation: By the repetition of the risings and settings of the luminaries, established in the world — though there is no lapsing of time, a likeness of property, as it were, is obtained.
commentary: The astronomical clock, and its status. Udaya-astamaya-avrttya jyotisam loka-siddhaya: by the *repetition* of the *risings and settings* of the *luminaries*, *established in the world* — the day, the tradition's most fundamental unit, given by the sun's return. Kalasya avyatipate 'pi: *though there is no lapsing of time* — time does not pass; nothing withdraws (3.9.61's na ca kimcin nivartate). Tad-dharmyam iva labhyate: a *likeness of property*, *as it were*, is *obtained*. So the sun's returning lends time an appearance of passing, and the appearance is a borrowed property (1.49's reflection following the water's movement; 3.9.30's krama-rupata worn). The day is not a piece of time; it is a resemblance conferred on the unlapsing by something that comes back. Iva, as always.

translation: But others — knowers of time — know time as the pulsation of sun, planets and constellations, differentiated by a difference of revolutions.
commentary: The astronomers' reduction, reported. Aditya-graha-naksatra-parispandam: the *pulsation* of *sun*, *planets* and *constellations* — parispanda: throbbing, vibration, the regular beat of the heavens. Atha apare bhinnam avrtti-bhedena kalam kala-vido viduh: *others* — *knowers of time*, the kala-vids, which is to say the astronomers — know *time* as *that*, *differentiated by a difference of revolutions*. So for the professionals, time simply *is* the celestial motion: no substance behind it, no sakti, just the turning. And the samuddesa reports this without objection, because it is very nearly its own view — 3.9.71 said time's self follows the trickle, and this says it follows the stars. The difference is only that the astronomer stops at the heavens and 3.9.44's correction stands: the stars are a mere mark (cihna-matram tu tarakah), and what they mark is the operating.

translation: That action which proceeds toward the delimitation of another action, and whose measure is well ascertained — that is expressed as 'time'.
commentary: The functional definition, and it makes time an action. Kriyantara-pariccheda-pravrtta ya kriyam prati: that *action* which *proceeds toward* the *delimitation* of *another action* — one process used to bound another: the sun's course delimiting the day's work, the clepsydra's trickle (3.9.70) delimiting the recitation. Nirjnata-parimana sa: and whose *measure is well ascertained* — the delimiting process must itself be known-in-measure, or it delimits nothing. Kala iti abhidhiyate: *that* is *expressed* as '*time*'. So time is any well-measured action used to bound another. Not a substance (3.9.1 refused), not a container: an *action*, doing a job. This is 3.9.12's identification (kala eva vyaparah) given as a definition — and it means that anything regular enough can *be* time, which is what 3.9.44's stars, 3.9.73's trees and 3.9.70's water-clock have all been showing.

mula_deva: अतः क्रियान्तराभावे सा क्रिया काल इष्यते ॥ ७८ ॥
mula_iast: ataḥ kriyāntarābhāve sā kriyā kāla iṣyate || 78 ||
translation: In cognition there is a passing-over of the form; by cognition alone there is the gathering-together. Therefore, in the absence of another action, that action is held to be time.
commentary: Time's limiting case: when there is nothing to measure by. Jnane rupasya sankrantih: in *cognition* there is a *passing-over* of the *form* — sankranti: migration, the word 3.9.39 used for capacities passing over into time. Jnanena eva anusamhrtih: by *cognition alone* there is the *gathering-together* — anusamhrti: 2.1's sixth view of the sentence, the mind's drawing-together; 3.9.57's buddhy-anusamhara. Atah kriyantara-abhave sa kriya kala isyate: *therefore*, in the *absence of another action*, *that* action is held to be *time*. So where no external process delimits, cognition's own gathering does — and cognition's gathering is then time. This completes 3.9.77: time is the delimiting action, and when nothing outside delimits, the mind's own act does, and the mind's act is time. Which is why 3.9.69 could say that the instant and the aeon are both *just so much* as the intellect grasped.

mula_deva: भूतो घट इतीयं च सत्ताया एव भूतता ।
mula_iast: bhūto ghaṭa itīyaṃ ca sattāyā eva bhūtatā |
translation: And this 'the pot was' is the past-ness of Existence alone; 'Existence was' — thus the existence of Existence is called 'past'.
commentary: The tenses reduced to Existence's tenses. Bhuto ghata iti iyam sattayah eva bhutata: '*the pot was*' is the *past-ness* of *Existence alone* — not the pot's pastness: the pot is nothing over satta (3.1.33), so what was past was Being, potwise. Bhuta satta iti sattayah satta bhuta abhidhiyate: '*Existence was*' — thus the *existence of Existence* is called '*past*'. So every past-tense sentence says: Being was. And the reduction is complete — subject, tense and predicate all collapse into satta and its temporal powers (3.9.60's three saktis not different from existence). This is the Kala-samuddesa reaching what the Jati- and Kriya-samuddesas reached (3.1.33's sole universal; 3.8.62's every root meaning satta): there is one thing, and the tenses are its powers, and 'the pot was' is Being wearing pastness.

mula_deva: परतो भिद्यते सर्वमात्मा तु न विकल्पते ।
mula_iast: parato bhidyate sarvamātmā tu na vikalpate |
translation: Everything is differentiated by another; but the self is not optioned. Therefore the standing of a mountain and the rest is differentiated by another's form.
commentary: The principle behind 3.9.79. Paratah bhidyate sarvam: *everything* is *differentiated by another* — nothing differentiates itself (3.9.8's time divided by its commingled things; 3.1.15-16's places conferred by relata). Atma tu na vikalpate: but the *self* is *not optioned* — the thing's own self admits no alternatives; it is not one of several ways it might be. Parvatadi-sthitih tasmat para-rupena bhidyate: *therefore* the *standing* of a *mountain* and the rest is *differentiated by another's form*. The mountain's endurance is measured by what passes, and the passing is not the mountain's. So a mountain does not have a long duration; other things have short ones. This is 3.9.35's relational accumulation and 3.9.73's growth-clocks stated as a general law — and it is why 3.9.79 could reduce the pot's pastness to Being's: the pot never had a tense of its own to lose.

translation: Operations whose difference is well established, and whose part-actions are of different form, are differentiated by accompaniment from those whose part-actions are of like form.
commentary: How actions are told apart. Prasiddha-bheda vyaparah virupa-avayava-kriyah: *operations* whose *difference* is *well established*, and whose *part-actions* are of *different form* — cooking and walking: their sub-actions differ visibly, and nobody confuses them. Sahacaryena bhidyante sarupa-avayava-kriyah: those whose *part-actions are of like form* are differentiated *by accompaniment* — two walkings, two cookings: identical in their parts, told apart only by what accompanies them (where, when, whose). So actions of unlike parts are distinguished by their parts, and actions of like parts by their company. And the second class is where time does its work: two identical processes are two only because they are accompanied differently — which is to say, timed differently. The verse quietly explains why time is needed at all: without it, repetitions would be indistinguishable.

mula_deva: सादृश्यात् सति भेदे तु तदङ्गत्वेन गृह्यते ॥ ८४ ॥
mula_iast: sādṛśyāt sati bhede tu tadaṅgatvena gṛhyate || 84 ||
translation: (82) The collection of actions such as eating comes, as it were, to interruption; it is seen as though ceased, by reason of the operations in the interval. (83) And though its form is severed, it does not withdraw, from want of a stopping; for every action is apprehended as though intermixed with another. (84) Or every part-action seen in that interval, though a difference exists, is grasped as a limb of it, from likeness.
commentary: The problem of interrupted actions. (82): a meal is interrupted — one pauses, talks, resumes: the collection of eatings *comes as it were to interruption*, vyavadhanam iva upaiti, and is *seen as though ceased*, nivrtta iva drsyate, because of what happened in the interval. Two iva's: the interruption is apparent. (83): na ca vicchinna-rupo 'pi so 'viramat nivartate — though its *form is severed*, it does *not withdraw*, from *want of a stopping*: the eating did not stop, only its manifestation did (3.9.50's screens). And the general truth: sarva eva hi kriya anyena sankirna iva upalabhyate — *every* action is apprehended as though *intermixed* with another; there are no pure processes. (84) then explains the resumption: the interval's part-actions are *grasped as limbs of it*, from *likeness* — sadrsyat, and sati bhede, *though a difference exists*. So an interrupted meal is one meal because likeness lets the intervals be annexed. This is 3.8.4's construed unity, and the interruption shows the construing at work.

mula_deva: सदसद् वापि वस्तु स्यात् तृतीयं नास्ति किञ्चन ।
तेन भूतभविष्यन्तौ मुक्त्वा मध्यं न विद्यते ॥ ८५ ॥
सदसद् वापि तेनैकं क्रमरूपं कथं भवेत् ॥ ८६ ॥
कार्योत्पत्तौ समर्थं वा स्वेन धर्मेण तत् तथा ।
mula_iast: sadasad vāpi vastu syāt tṛtīyaṃ nāsti kiñcana |
tena bhūtabhaviṣyantau muktvā madhyaṃ na vidyate || 85 ||
sadasad vāpi tenaikaṃ kramarūpaṃ kathaṃ bhavet || 86 ||
kāryotpattau samarthaṃ vā svena dharmeṇa tat tathā |
translation: (85) A thing must be either existent or non-existent; there is no third whatever. Therefore, apart from past and future, no middle exists. (86) The form of completion, for one thing, is not construed, from the absence of difference; how then could one thing, existent or non-existent, have the form of sequence? (87) And from the non-standing of many, only one is apprehended; and there, remembrance according to apprehension is also possible. (88) On the construction of oneness for everything, the one would be of existent-and-non-existent form; or the form of completion would be the general of completion. (89) Or, being capable in the arising of the effect by its own property — it would be grasped so in its self-reality, and that is present-ness in it. (90) That form of the continuum of action which is inwardly grasped, its reflection having passed over into one place — that they call present-ness.
commentary: The samuddesa's hardest problem: whether a present exists. (85) states it with brutal economy: sad asad vapi vastu syat trtiyam na asti kimcana — a thing must be *existent or non-existent*; there is *no third whatever*. Tena bhuta-bhavisyantau muktva madhyam na vidyate: *therefore*, apart from *past and future*, *no middle exists*. The present is squeezed out: what has happened is past, what has not is future, and there is nothing between. This is the classical argument that eliminates the present, and Bhartrhari states it at full strength. (86-87) press: no completion-form for one thing, no sequence for what is simply existent or non-existent; and from many things' *non-standing*, only *one* is apprehended, with remembrance following. (88-90) then rebuild. (88): construct oneness and the one is *of existent-and-non-existent form* (3.9.59's every entity always both is and is not). (89): or a thing *capable in the arising of the effect by its own property* is grasped so in its *self-reality* — and *that* is *present-ness*: the present is capability-in-act. (90) gives the final account, and its image is the mirror again: kriya-prabandha-rupam yad adhyatmam vinigrhyate — the *continuum of action* which is *inwardly grasped*, sankranta-bimbam ekatra, its *reflection having passed over into one place* — tam ahur vartamanatam: *that* they call *present-ness*. So the present is not a stretch of time but the inward grasping of a continuum, its image gathered into one — which is 3.9.78's cognition-gathering, and 3.9.40's mirror. The present is where the mind's mirror holds the process.
contested: the six-verse passage is the samuddesa's most compressed and its argumentative structure is genuinely hard to reconstruct; we read 85-87 as the objection (no present) and 88-90 as the answer (present-ness as capability, and as the inwardly-gathered reflection of a continuum). Readers who take 88-89 as further horns of the objection will find 90 the sole answer, which the text also permits

translation: The lapsing of an action has, as its mark, the utter non-arising of the action; and the past is not un-arisen — nor is the future of that kind.
commentary: A third category distinguished from both tenses. Kriya-atipattih atyantam kriya-anutpatti-laksana: the *lapsing of an action* has as its *mark* the *utter non-arising* of the action — the kriya-atipatti is the grammarian's category for the action that was to have happened and did not (Panini's conditional, the lin of unfulfilled condition: 'had he come, he would have seen'). Its mark is atyantam anutpatti: *utter* non-arising — not merely absent but never to arrive. Na ca bhutam anutpannam: and the *past* is *not un-arisen* — the past arose and is screened (3.9.50). Na bhavisyat tatha-vidham: *nor* is the *future* of that kind — the future has not arisen and may (3.9.51's future not obstructing birth). So the lapsed action is neither past nor future: it is what will never have been. And Sanskrit has a tense for it. The verse identifies a genuine fourth category that 3.9.85's dichotomy (existent or non-existent, no third) cannot accommodate.

translation: From the arising of a contrary action beforehand, or when a contrary operation has been completed — the domain there is differentiated by a difference of limit.
commentary: How the lapsed action of 3.9.91 comes about. Prag-viruddha-kriya-utpadat: from the *arising* of a *contrary action* *beforehand* — something incompatible happened first, and the action could not arise: the messenger died, so the message never came. Nirvrtte va virodhini vyapare: or when a *contrary operation* has been *completed* — the incompatible thing finished, and the window closed. Avadhi-bhedena visayas tatra bhidyate: the *domain* there is *differentiated* by a *difference of limit* — avadhi: the limit, the samuddesa's constant (3.9.68's units made by taking up limits). So a lapsing is fixed by where one sets the limit: relative to this moment the action is merely future; relative to that one, it has lapsed forever. The category of the never-to-be is thus limit-relative like every other temporal category — and 3.9.68's doctrine (take up a limit and the units follow) reaches even the tense of what will never have happened.

mula_deva: स्वकाल एव साधुश्चेत् कालभेदे गतिः कथम् ।
mula_iast: svakāla eva sādhuścet kālabhede gatiḥ katham |
translation: (93) When the occasioning ground deviates, correctness is not constructed; by the sutra 'bhavya asit', it is taught elsewhere, at that time. (94) If it is correct only in its own time, how is there passage in a difference of time? From the sentence-meaning, distinctness is not established in respect of things not of that meaning.
commentary: A grammatical problem about tense-mismatch. (93): vyabhicare nimittasya sadhutvam na prakalpyate — when the *occasioning ground deviates*, *correctness* is *not constructed*: if a form's licensing condition fails, the form is incorrect (1.27's sadhutva established from the sistas). The sutra cited (bhavya asit — 'what was to be' plus a past 'was') licenses a future-participle with a past verb: a form whose two halves point opposite ways in time. (94) presses: sva-kale eva sadhus cet kala-bhede gatih katham — if it is *correct only in its own time*, *how is there passage* in a *difference of time*? A form correct only in its own tense should not survive transplantation into another. Vakya-arthad atad-arthesu visistatvam na sidhyati: from the *sentence-meaning*, *distinctness* is not established in respect of things *not of that meaning*. So the tense-mismatched construction is a genuine puzzle: the grammar licenses it, and no account of why is forthcoming. The next verses supply one.
contested: the sutra invoked at 93 is compressed to 'bhavya asit' and its identification is uncertain; the construction at issue — a future-sense stem under a past-tense finite verb — is clear enough from 94-101's discussion

translation: If the part is of that meaning, there would be no understanding of past-ness for what is future; utter past-ness alone would be possible there.
