So two rule-failures from the same cause: where the number has fused (3.14.99's abheda-ekatva), the rules that need a difference to work on have nothing. Bhartrhari is cataloguing the doctrine's costs as carefully as its benefits (3.14.106's benefit, this verse's cost), and he does not net them out.

translation: Difference is seen in 'gargyatara', and likewise in 'gargatarah'; 'yusmat-pita' and 'tvat-pita' — thus the two substitutes are settled.
commentary: The counter-evidence, admitted. Drsto gargyatare bhedah: *difference* is *seen* in *'gargyatara'* — the comparative, which requires the difference 3.14.122 said was absent. Tatha gargatara iti: and *likewise* in *'gargatarah'*. Yusmat-pita tvat-piteti tatha adesau vyavasthitau: *'yusmat-pita'* and *'tvat-pita'* — thus the *two substitutes* are *settled*: the plural-based and singular-based substitutes both occur, each in its own domain. Drstah is again the operative word (3.14.114's drsyate): the forms exist and the theory must accommodate them. So difference does survive in some derivatives, and 3.14.124 must say how — which it does, by relocating the number into the base.

translation: That number which, being an adjunct, is settled in the base, is manifested by substitutes, or by a technical term, even without a case-ending.
commentary: The resolution of 3.14.122-123. Upadhi-bhuta ya sankhya prakrtau samavasthita: that *number* which, being an *upadhi* — an adjunct, a qualifying condition rather than a constituent — is *settled in the base*. Adesaih samjnaya vapi vibhaktya vyajyate vina: is *manifested* by *substitutes*, or by a *technical term*, *even without a case-ending*. Vyajyate: is *manifested*, revealed — not said (3.14.98's dyotika, and the word is chosen). So the number that 3.14.98 said withdraws from the compound has not gone anywhere: it has become an adjunct of the base, where no ending expresses it and the substitutes betray it. This is why 3.14.123's forms come out: 'tvat-pita' shows its singular through its substitute, having no ending to show it with. Number in a compound is not spoken; it is given away.

translation: In 'saurpika' and in 'masa-jata', the measure, by its own nature, resting on the number which is an adjunct, operates by way of difference.
commentary: The upadhi-number of 3.14.124 at work. Saurpike masa-jate ca parimanam svabhavatah: in *'saurpika'* (measured by a surpa) and *'masa-jata'* (a month old), the *measure*, by its *own nature*. Upadhi-bhutam asritya sankhyam bhedena vartate: *resting on* the *number* which is an *adjunct*, operates *by way of difference*. So measure-words carry their difference through the adjunct-number in the base, not through any ending: 'masa-jata' says one month and no ending says it. Svabhavatah is doing work — the measure is *by its own nature* quantitative, so it needs no help to reach the number that is sitting in its base. This finishes the account 3.14.84-86 began, and the two now agree: measure is certified by machinery and carried by the base.

mula_deva: वयस्विनि परिच्छेदः क्रीते चापि न गम्यते ।
mula_iast: vayasvini paricchedaḥ krīte cāpi na gamyate |
translation: In 'vayasvin' and in 'krita', the delimitation is not understood; the sense intended, apart from difference — there the measure is purposeless.
commentary: The contrast case. Vayasvini paricchedah krite capi na gamyate: in *'vayasvin'* (aged, possessing age) and in *'krita'* (bought), the *delimitation* is *not understood* — pariccheda: the cutting-off, the definite quantity. 'Vayasvin' says one has age, not how much; 'krita' says bought, not for how much. Isto bhedad rte tatra parimanam anarthakam: the sense *intended*, *apart from difference* — there the *measure* is *purposeless*. Anarthaka: without purpose. So these words carry no adjunct-number at all (3.14.124), and reaching for one gets nothing. The pair with 3.14.125 is the point: some derivatives have quantity in their base and some have only quality, and no rule can tell which — one has to know the word. Which is 3.14.43 once more: the world's settled arrangement, and the science following after.

translation: From the expression of non-difference of what is different, the rule of 'sas' after 'prastha' and the rest; but from non-difference, because that is its property, it is not seen after 'ghata' and the rest.
commentary: Bhinnasya abheda-vacanat prasthadibhyah saso vidhih: from the *expression of non-difference* of what is *different*, the rule of *'sas'* after *'prastha'* and the rest — the distributive affix, giving 'prastha-sah', prastha by prastha: many prasthas said as one distributive sweep. Tad-dharmatvad abhedat tu ghatadibhyo na drsyate: but from *non-difference*, *because that is its property*, it is *not seen* after *'ghata'* and the rest. So the distributive attaches to measure-words and not to pots: a measure is intrinsically a repeatable unit — abheda is *its property* — and a pot is not. This is 3.14.125-126's finding again (quantity is in some bases and not others), now settling which stems take an affix. The grammar's gana is not arbitrary; it tracks which words have a unit built into them.

mula_deva: निवर्तते यद् वचनं तस्य भोवो न विद्यते ॥ १२८ ॥
mula_iast: nivartate yad vacanaṃ tasya bhovo na vidyate || 128 ||
translation: Where the number-ending is heard, there the state is specified; that ending which withdraws — of that, there is no being.
commentary: A methodological principle, stated with force. Sruyate vacanam yatra bhavas tatra visisyate: *where* the *number-ending* is *heard*, *there* the *state* is *specified* — an audible ending does specifying work. Nivartate yad vacanam tasya bhavo na vidyate: that ending which *withdraws* — of *that*, there is *no being*. Bhavo na vidyate: it has no existence at all. So Bhartrhari refuses the halfway house: an elided ending is not a quiet ending, it is *not there*. This is stronger than 3.14.87's asadhutva-nivrtty-artham (where the elided matup was allowed to exist in order to certify) and stands in tension with it — the samuddesa is exploring both, and the tension is the subject-matter (3.14.113's two modes). What is not heard does not specify.

translation: The operation rests on existence; from the science, non-application is non-appearance. What is seen utterly in the sentence — of that there is absence in derivative formations.
commentary: The consequence of 3.14.128. Karyam satta-asrayam: the *operation* rests on *existence* — a rule cannot act on what is not there. Sastrad apravrttir adarsanam: from the *science*, *non-application* is *non-appearance* — the rule's failing to apply *is* the thing's not showing up; there is no further fact. Vakye drstam yad atyantam abhavas tasya vrttisu: what is *seen utterly* in the *sentence* — of that there is *absence* in *derivative formations*. So the sentence's visible articulation is *absent* — not hidden, not suppressed — in the compound. This is the samuddesa's core claim (3.14.49's atyanta-bheda) stated in the vocabulary of rule-application: nothing withdrew into the compound, because nothing was there to withdraw.

translation: For the sake of differentiating the domain of the technical term, the non-appearance of what was applicable is taught; but the ending which is heard is apprehended as specified.
commentary: Samjna-visaya-bhedartham prasakta-adarsanam smrtam: for the sake of *differentiating the domain* of the *technical term*, the *non-appearance* of what was *applicable* is *taught* — prasakta: what would have applied; adarsana: its not showing. The grammar deliberately teaches that an applicable thing fails to appear, and the teaching's purpose is to carve the term's domain. So the elision is a demarcation-device, not a report (3.14.42's rules as nidarsana). Sruyamanam tu vacanam visistam upalabhyate: but the ending which *is heard* is apprehended as *specified*. The audible one does real work; the elided one draws a boundary. Both are the grammar's; only one is the word's.
contested: Pada c is transmitted in our source as 'ksuyamanam', which yields no sense here; we read 'sruyamanam' ('being heard'), which the contrast with 3.14.128's 'sruyate' and with pada d's 'upalabhyate' requires. We note the source reading rather than silently normalising it.

mula_deva: अभावो वा लुको यत्र रूपवान् वा विधीयते ।
mula_iast: abhāvo vā luko yatra rūpavān vā vidhīyate |
translation: Where absence, or elision, or a substitute possessing form is prescribed — there, from the deviation of the cause, incorrectness follows.
commentary: The cost of the whole apparatus. Abhavo va luko yatra rupavan va vidhiyate: where *absence*, or *luk*, or a *substitute possessing form* is *prescribed* — the grammar's three ways of handling what is not audibly there. Vyabhicaran nimittasya tatra asadhuh prasajyate: *there*, from the *deviation* of the *cause*, *incorrectness* *follows*. Vyabhicara: straying, failure of invariable concomitance — the occasioning cause does not hold steady across the cases. So wherever the grammar posits an absence, the conditioning breaks down somewhere and a bad form is generated. This is 3.14.76's vyutpattir anavasthita made concrete: the derivational machinery leaks, and it leaks precisely at the points where it posits what is not there (3.14.74's imagined parts).

translation: Difference, number, or particular has been expounded for the derivative formation and the sentence; but the particular need not everywhere be of that kind.
commentary: A caution against over-generalizing what has been established. Bhedah samkhya viseso va vyakhyato vrtti-vakyayoh: *difference*, *number*, or *particular* has been *expounded* for the *derivative formation* and the *sentence* — the samuddesa's results so far. Sarvatra eva visesas tu na avasyam tadrso bhavet: but the *particular* need *not everywhere* be *of that kind*. Na avasyam: not necessarily. So the analysis was of certain cases and does not automatically extend. Bhartrhari is guarding his own conclusions against the very error he charged others with (3.14.76's taking the upayas for true): a result reached on some forms is an upaya for those forms, and carrying it everywhere would make it a doctrine. The samuddesa polices itself by the standard it set.

mula_deva: प्रधानं मृगदुग्धादौ गार्गीपुत्रे न स क्रमः ॥ १३३ ॥
mula_iast: pradhānaṃ mṛgadugdhādau gārgīputre na sa kramaḥ || 133 ||
translation: And from the universal's being the cause of difference, the principal is not qualified by gender in 'mrga-dugdha' and the rest; in 'gargi-putra' that sequence does not hold.
commentary: Jates ca bheda-hetutvat: from the *universal's* being the *cause of difference* — the jati is what differentiates, so it does the qualifying. Na lingena visesyate pradhanam mrga-dugdhadau: the *principal* is *not* qualified by *gender* in *'mrga-dugdha'* (deer's milk) and the rest — the milk is not made feminine by the doe: gender does not reach across into the principal. Gargi-putre na sa kramah: in *'gargi-putra'* (Gargi's son) that *sequence* does *not hold* — here the feminine of the first member is doing something, and the sequence reverses. So two compounds, alike in form, differ in whether gender penetrates. This is 3.14.132's caution being exercised at once: the particular is not everywhere of the same kind, and here is a pair to prove it.

translation: In non-difference, from connection with gender and number, 'suklam patah' being applicable — the science is undertaken for the establishing of gender and number.
commentary: Why the grammar has gender-and-number rules at all. Abhede linga-samkhyabhyam yogat: in *non-difference*, from *connection* with *gender and number*. Suklam pata iti prasakte: *'suklam patah'* — the ungrammatical mixture, a neuter singular adjective with a masculine plural noun — being *applicable*: without a rule, that is what would come out, since abheda leaves the qualifier with no reason to agree. Sastram arabdham siddhaye linga-samkhyayoh: the *science* is *undertaken* for the *establishing* of *gender and number*. So agreement is not a fact of the meaning — the meaning is undivided and has no agreement in it (3.14.92's anusyuta) — and the grammar must impose it. This is a striking admission: concord is the sastra's contribution, not the world's.

translation: That quality which, for another's sake, attains the state of remainder in derivative formations — that is designated in the sutra by way of being a qualifier.
commentary: Pararthaam sesa-bhavam yo vrttisu pratipadyate: that *quality* which, *for another's sake*, attains the *state of remainder* in *derivative formations* — sesa: what is left over, the residue after the principal has taken what it needs. Guno visesanatvena sa sutre vyapadisyate: that is *designated* in the *sutra* by way of being a *qualifier*. Vyapadisyate: is *designated* — the samuddesa's careful verb for a name that is apt without being a description (3.14.105's vyapadesarha). So Panini's 'visesana' names whatever is left over serving another's purpose, and the naming is a convenience. The qualifier is not a thing in the meaning; it is the grammar's label for a residue — which is why 3.14.134's concord had to be imposed on it.

translation: Although particulars are heard in sentences, from their being other words — from the derivative formation's non-different form, in them there is no derivative formation.
commentary: Sabdantaratvad vakyesu visesa yady api srutah: although *particulars* are *heard* in *sentences*, from their being *other words* — the phrase has audible particulars, because the phrase is a different word (3.14.49's atyanta-bheda; 3.14.64). Vrtter abhinna-rupatvat tesu vrttir na vidyate: from the *derivative formation's* *non-different form*, in *them* there is *no* derivative formation. So one cannot find the compound inside the phrase: the phrase's particulars are not the compound's members, and there is no compounding going on in a phrase at all. This closes the door 3.14.52's rival wanted open. The compound is not a phrase that has undergone anything; the two are different words, and the particulars one hears belong to one of them only.

mula_deva: तस्मात् तदाश्रयं लिङ्गं वचनं च प्रसज्यते ॥ १३७ ॥
सलिङ्गं च ससङ्ख्यं च ततो द्रव्याभिधायिना ।
mula_iast: tasmāt tadāśrayaṃ liṅgaṃ vacanaṃ ca prasajyate || 137 ||
saliṅgaṃ ca sasaṅkhyaṃ ca tato dravyābhidhāyinā |
translation: (137) And since the word's formation, from its form, has the general for its domain — therefore gender and number resting on that follow. (138) Then the word, with gender and with number, is connected with what expresses the substance; there, the hearing of those two would be separate.
commentary: How gender and number get into a word at all. (137): rupac ca sabda-samskarah samanya-visayo yatah — the *word's formation*, from its *form*, has the *general* for its *domain*: the grammar operates on shapes, and shapes are general (3.14.78's science made of notation). Tasmat tad-asrayam lingam vacanam ca prasajyate: *therefore* *gender* and *number* resting on *that* *follow* — they attach to the general form. (138): salingam ca sasamkhyam ca tato dravyabhidhayina sambadhyate padam — *then* the *word*, with gender and number already on it, is *connected* with what *expresses the substance*. Tatra tayor bhinna srutir bhavet: *there*, the *hearing* of those two would be *separate*. So gender and number arrive with the form, before the substance is in view, and one would then hear them twice — which is the difficulty 3.14.139 answers.

translation: The gender and number of the qualities, which come from the statement of the future, external substratum — those are taught by the sutra.
commentary: The answer. Bhavino bahirangasya vacanad asrayasya ye lingasankhye gunanam: the *gender and number* of the *qualities*, which come from the *statement* of the *substratum* — and the substratum is *bhavin*, *future* (not yet in view when the form was made), and *bahiranga*, *external* (settled later: 3.7.182's antaranga/bahiranga). Te sutrena pratipadite: *those* are *taught* by the *sutra*. So the double hearing of 3.14.138 does not arise: the qualifier's gender and number are not its own at all — they are borrowed from a substratum that comes afterwards and from outside, and the borrowing is what the rule prescribes. This is 3.14.134's admission completed: concord is imposed, and it is imposed by making the qualifier answer to something not yet there.

translation: And even though it operates in the particular, since the particular is unmarked, from the non-difference of form — therefore here the difference-operation is not conceivable.
commentary: Visesa-vrtter api ca rupabhedad alaksitah yasmad visesah: even though it *operates in the particular* (3.14.37's visesa-vrttitva), the *particular* is *unmarked* — alaksita: uncharacterized, bearing no sign — from the *non-difference of form*: the form does not show which particular it has got. Tena atra bheda-karyam na kalpate: *therefore* here the *difference-operation* is *not conceivable*. So a rule that needs a difference to operate on finds none, because the difference — though present — has left no mark on the shape, and the grammar reads shapes (3.14.137's samanya-visaya). This is the deepest version of the samuddesa's recurring finding: the science's blindness to particulars is not an oversight but a consequence of its being a science of forms.

mula_deva: विशेष एव सामान्यं विशेषाद् भिद्यते यतः ।
mula_iast: viśeṣa eva sāmānyaṃ viśeṣād bhidyate yataḥ |
translation: The general is the particular itself, since it is differentiated from the particular; for the non-difference of the particulars, when resorted to, is exclusive.
commentary: One of the boldest lines in the kanda. Visesa eva samanyam: the *general* *is* the *particular itself* — eva: nothing else. Visesad bhidyate yatah: *since* it is *differentiated from* the particular — and here is the paradox stated on purpose: the general is the particular, and it is what the particular is marked off from. The general is not a further thing; it is the particular seen as excluding others. Abhedo hi visesanam asrito vinivartakah: for the *non-difference* of the *particulars*, when *resorted to*, is *exclusive* — vinivartaka: what turns others away. So sameness *is* exclusion: to take several particulars as non-different just is to turn away everything else. There is no positive general over and above the turning-away. This is very close to what the Buddhists would build apoha out of, arrived at from the other side — Bhartrhari's samanya is real, and its reality consists in excluding.

translation: Whatever is resorted to, that excludes something else; but the division into difference and non-difference is not determined in the general.
commentary: The principle generalized, and then withheld. Yad yad asriyate tat tad anyasya vinivartakam: *whatever* is *resorted to*, *that* *excludes* something *else* — every resort is an exclusion, without exception. To take up anything at all is to turn away what it is not (3.14.101's bhedapohena vartate; 2.129's the mind's fixing on one thing being its exclusion of the rest). Bhedabheda-vibhagas tu samanye na nirupyate: *but* the *division* into *difference and non-difference* is *not determined* in the *general*. Na nirupyate: is not ascertained, cannot be settled. So the question 'is the general the same as its particulars or different?' has no answer — not because we lack information, but because the general is constituted by exclusion and exclusion is not one of the two. Bhartrhari states his own doctrine and then declines to metaphysicalize it: the general excludes, and asking whether it is one with what it excludes is asking the wrong thing.

translation: And the general is an extraction — so it stands only in the condition of a cause; therefore it is grasped by its own property.
commentary: The general's status settled by naming its origin. Apoddharas ca samanyam: the *general* is an *apoddhara* — an *extraction*, the work's technical term for what the analyst lifts out of an undivided whole (2.10's words extracted from sentences as suffixes from words; 3.14.75's three storeys of extraction). So the samanya is not found; it is pulled out. Nimittavastham eva atah: *therefore* it stands *only in the condition of a cause* — nimitta-avastha: it exists in the state of being an occasioning condition, and in no other state. Tat sva-dharmena grhyate: it is *grasped by its own property*. So the general is real exactly as far as it causes, and no further — which is 3.14.142's refusal to say whether it is one with its particulars, now given its ground. An extraction that occasions has nothing left over to be same or different.
contested: Pada b in our source reads 'tasyorakarinah', which we cannot construe and take to be corrupt. We translate the verse from padas a, c and d, whose sense is secure, and leave b unrendered rather than emend on conjecture. Something like 'tasya upakarinah' ('of that which assists') would fit the syntax, but we do not press it.

translation: From the non-ascertainment of their properties, the differences alone, imagined, are designated by their causes, qualified by the name of the general.
commentary: What is actually going on when one uses a general term. Anirdharita-dharmatvad: from the *non-ascertainment* of their *properties* — one does not have the particulars' properties settled; one never does. Bheda eva vikalpitah: the *differences alone*, *imagined* — vikalpita: constructed (3.14.74's bhagair iva vikalpitaih). Nimittair vyapadisyante samanyakhya-visesitah: are *designated* by their *causes*, *qualified* by the *name of the general*. So there are only particulars, ill-known and constructed, and the general's name is a qualification laid over them by way of their occasioning conditions. Nothing general is being referred to. This is 3.14.143's apoddhara cashed out: the extraction leaves the particulars where they were and adds a name.

translation: But when gender and number are designated by their own nature, then correctness is commenced in the sentence, in usages alone.
commentary: Yada tu vyapadisyete linga-samkhye svabhavatah: *when* gender and number are designated *by their own nature* — not imposed by rule (3.14.134's sastram arabdham) but belonging to the words themselves. Prayogesv eva sadhutvam vakye prakramyate tada: *then* *correctness* is *commenced* in the *sentence*, in *usages* alone. Prayogesv eva: in usages *and nothing else*. So on this alternative, concord is not the grammar's imposition: it is a fact of how words are used, and sadhutva starts from usage and stays there. The verse offers the rival to 3.14.134-139's account, and it is the more deflationary of the two — there is no mechanism, only what the sistas say (3.14.43's loka-vyavastha).

translation: There, the usage of the qualities with their substrata is unfixed; that general which is utter is settled there alone.
commentary: Tatra prayogo 'niyato gunanam asrayaih saha: *there*, the *usage* of the *qualities* with their *substrata* is *unfixed* — aniyata: not settled, varying: which quality-word goes with which substratum is not determined by anything. Samanyam yat tad atyantam tatraiva samavasthitam: that *general* which is *utter* is *settled* *there alone* — atyanta: complete, unqualified; tatra eva: in *that* — in the usage itself, and nowhere else. So on 3.14.145's view the general has no seat but the practice: the usage is unfixed, and the general is wholly resident in it. This is the most radically usage-based moment in the samuddesa, and Bhartrhari lets it stand alongside the rule-based account without adjudicating (3.14.97's upayas unsettled among persons).

translation: The cowness of Sabaleya is not designated as 'cow'; the whiteness of Bahuleya is designated as 'white'.
commentary: An asymmetry between universals and qualities, exhibited in two words. Na gotvam sabaleyasya gaur iti vyapadisyate: the *cowness* of *Sabaleya* — the dappled ox, the tradition's stock individual — is *not* designated as *'cow'*: 'gauh' names the animal, never the cowness. Suklatvam bahuleyasya sukla ity apadisyate: the *whiteness* of *Bahuleya* *is* designated as *'white'* — 'sukla' does reach the quality itself. So jati-words point past the universal to the individual, and guna-words do not: the two behave oppositely, and the grammar treats them alike. This is why 3.14.133's gender did not penetrate 'mrga-dugdha' and did penetrate 'gargi-putra'; and it is 3.14.132's warning made good — the particular is not everywhere of the same kind.

mula_deva: व्यतिरेके च सत्येवं मतुपः श्रवणं भवेत् ।
mula_iast: vyatireke ca satyevaṃ matupaḥ śravaṇaṃ bhavet |
translation: And when there is thus a difference, the hearing of 'matup' would occur; therefore elision is expounded, and after 'rasa' and the rest it does not exist.
commentary: Vyatireke ca saty evam matupah sravanam bhavet: when there is thus a *difference* — between the quality and its possessor — the *hearing* of *'matup'*, the possessive affix, *would occur*: if 'sukla' means whiteness and the cloth *has* it, the affix should be audible. Lug anvakhyayate tasmat: *therefore* *luk* is *expounded* — the grammar posits the affix and deletes it (3.14.87's matub-lopa, for the same reason). Rasadibhyas ca na asti sah: and after *'rasa'* (taste) and the rest, *it does not exist* — no matup, elided or otherwise. So the device is applied to some quality-words and withheld from others, and the withholding needs its own explanation. 3.14.149 gives it.

translation: Since, from the connection 'this is that', it operates by non-difference of form, like 'sukla' and the rest — therefore the elision does not exist in 'rasa' and the rest.
commentary: Yat so 'yam iti sambandhad rupabhedena vartate: since, from the *connection* 'this is that', it operates by *non-difference of form* — the identification runs directly, with no formal gap between quality and possessor. Sukladivat: *like* 'sukla' and the rest. Tato lopas tad rasadau na vidyate: *therefore* the *elision* does *not exist* in *'rasa'* and the rest. So the argument is: where the identification is immediate, no affix was ever needed, and so none can be elided (3.14.128's what withdraws has no being). 'Rasa' just identifies with its possessor; 'sukla' has to be got there by a suppressed matup. The grammar's uneven treatment tracks a real unevenness in how the words work — which is 3.14.147's asymmetry again, one level down.

translation: The entry by gender and number stands somewhere, like 'the platforms' and the rest; in the connection 'this is that', it does not exist in 'prastha' and the rest.
commentary: Avesah linga-samkhyabhyam kvacin mancadivat sthitah: the *entry* by *gender and number* stands *somewhere*, *like 'manca'* and the rest — 'manca hasanti', *the platforms laugh*: the platforms take the gender and number of the people on them, and the metonymy is complete enough to govern agreement. Avesa: entering, taking possession. So 'this is that' can be strong enough that one thing wears another's gender. So 'yam ity abhisambandhe sa prasthadau na vidyate: in the connection 'this is that', *it does not exist* in *'prastha'* and the rest. But not always: the measure-words do not take on what they measure. So the identification comes in strengths, and the grammar has to know which strength a word commands.

translation: The gender, in the abandonment of gender, is in the sutra the teaching of the affix; from the connection 'this is that', in a masculine word, the one expressing the feminine.
commentary: Lingam linga-parityage sutre pratyaya-sasanam: the *gender*, in the *abandonment of gender*, is in the *sutra* the *teaching of the affix* — where a word has dropped its gender, what the rule supplies is not a gender but an affix: the grammar deals in morphology and lets the gender follow. So 'yam ity abhisambandhat pum-sabde stry-abhidhayini: from the connection 'this is that', in a *masculine word*, the one *expressing the feminine*. So a feminine affix lands on a masculine stem, carried by the identification. This is 3.14.150's avesa in a specific case, and it shows how the grammar handles what it cannot state: it prescribes a suffix and lets the semantics arrive by identification.

translation: In the substratum, what is resorted to is designated by gender and number; and by the settled arrangement of the science, 'of qualifiers, and not of a universal'.
commentary: Asraye linga-samkhyabhyam asritam vyapadisyate: in the *substratum*, what is *resorted to* is *designated* by *gender and number* — the qualifier takes its gender and number from where it rests (3.14.139's borrowed concord). Visesananam ca ajater iti sastra-vyavasthaya: and by the *settled arrangement* of the *science*, *'of qualifiers, and not of a universal'* — the rule's own words, restricting the provision to qualifiers and excluding universals. So the borrowing is not universal: a jati-word does not take its substratum's gender, and the sutra says so. This is 3.14.147's asymmetry finding its way into Panini's wording — the grammar had noticed that universals and qualities behave differently, and had written the difference into a rule.

translation: The cognition that 'those properties which are in the difference-causes, from their conforming to the cause, exist also in the substratum' — is set aside.
commentary: An error blocked. Nimitta-anuvidhayitvad ye dharma bheda-hetusu: *those properties* which are in the *difference-causes*, from their *conforming to the cause* — anuvidhayin: following after, taking their shape from. Ta asraye 'pi vidyanta iti buddhir nivartyate: the *cognition* that they *exist also in the substratum* — is *set aside*. Nivartyate: is turned back. So one must not infer that because a qualifier's properties follow its cause, those properties are also in the thing qualified. The properties belong to the conditions, and the substratum does not acquire them by being conditioned. This guards 3.14.152's restriction from the obvious slide, and it is a small instance of the samuddesa's constant discipline: what the analysis puts somewhere for its own reasons must not be allowed to migrate into the object.

translation: And the operation of gender and number in 'goda' and the rest — established in the world by its own nature, similar to the cause — is stated by the science.
commentary: The relation of science and usage, stated in one line. Akhyayate ca sastrena: is *stated* by the *science* — akhyayate: is *reported*. Loka-rudha svabhavatah: *established in the world* *by its own nature* — rudha: settled, grown into place, the word for what convention has made unanalysable (3.14.55's rudhi). Nimitta-tulya godadau pravrttir linga-samkhyayoh: the *operation* of gender and number in *'goda'* and the rest, *similar to the cause*. So the order is: the world settles it by its own nature, the operation resembles the causes, and the science *reports*. The grammar is not the source; it is downstream (3.14.81's anugamyate, correctness *followed out*). This is 3.14.145-146's usage-account and 3.14.134's rule-account reconciled — the rules are true reports of what usage settled.

translation: In 'haritaki' and the rest, the individual; in 'khalatika' and the rest, number; of the particulars of 'manusya' and of the luk, the pair rests on what is expressed.
commentary: Three cases sorted. Haritakyadisu vyaktih: in *'haritaki'* (the myrobalan) and the rest, the *individual* — the word reaches the individual fruit. Sankhya khalatikadisu: in *'khalatika'* and the rest, *number*. Manusya-lub-visesanam abhidheyasrayam dvayam: of the *particulars* of *'manusya'* and of the *luk*, the *pair* — gender and number — *rests on what is expressed*. Abhidheyasraya: resting on the expressed, i.e. taking their character from the meaning rather than the form. So three word-classes and three different anchors: individual, number, and the meaning itself. The catalogue is unglamorous and it is the samuddesa's method — 3.14.132 said the particular is not everywhere the same, and this is what checking looks like.

translation: If, in the use of the universal, the qualifier comes into connection with the universal — then it acquires the properties of the universal.
commentary: Jati-prayoge jatya cet sambandham upagacchati visesanam: *if*, in the *use of the universal*, the *qualifier* comes into *connection* with the *universal*. Tato dharman jates tat pratipadyate: *then* it *acquires* the *properties* of the *universal*. So a qualifier attached to a jati-word takes on the jati's characteristics — including, presumably, the absence of number that 3.14.107 established (jatau dvivacanabhavat). The verse is conditional throughout: *if* the connection is with the universal. Because it need not be — the qualifier might attach to the individual instead, and then everything changes. 3.14.157 takes the other branch.

translation: A qualifier other than the universal, having fallen upon a form ending in luk — from that form's being principal, it is designated by its properties.
commentary: The other branch. Lubante sannipatitam jater anyad visesanam: a qualifier *other than the universal*, having *fallen upon* a form ending in *luk* — sannipatita: alighted, come to rest on. Lubantasya pradhanatvat tad-dharmair vyapadisyate: from *that* form's being *principal*, it is *designated by its properties*. So where the qualifier lands on a luk-ending word rather than on the universal, it takes that word's properties instead — because the luk-ending word is principal there. The pair 3.14.156-157 is a clean statement of the samuddesa's general finding: a qualifier has no properties of its own; it takes whichever ones the thing it rests on has (3.14.139's borrowed concord, 3.14.152's asraye). What varies is only what it lands on.

translation: Those differences of gender and number which occur in the non-bahuvrihi, the dvandva, the feminine and the superlative — are to be stated in accordance with the Bhasya.
commentary: The gender-and-number stretch closed by deferral. Abahurvrihi-dvandva-stry-atisayesu ye bheda: those *differences* which are in the *non-bahuvrihi*, the *dvandva*, the *feminine* and the *superlative* — four remaining classes, each with its own gender-and-number irregularities. Bhasyanusarena vacyas te linga-samkhyayoh: are *to be stated* in *accordance with the Bhasya*. Bhasyanusarena: following Patanjali. So Bhartrhari declines to redo what the Bhasya has done and points the reader there (3.14.34's citing the Bhasya's admission; 2.475). The gesture is characteristic: the karikas are not a complete grammar and do not pretend to be — they are a philosophy of one, and where the grammar has already spoken well, Bhartrhari says so and moves on.
contested: Pada a is metrically short in our source (reading 'abahurvihi-dvandva-stry-atisayesu ye'), and the compound's first member is transmitted as 'abahurvihi' rather than the expected 'abahuvrihi'. We render the sense the four-item list requires. The list's membership, not the metre, carries the verse.

translation: If 'tarap' and the rest came after the inferior thing ending in genitive or accusative, then, in the case of the superior which does the humbling, there would be a gender-discrepancy of the base.
commentary: The comparative's derivation, and a difficulty. Yadi sasthi-dvitiyantan nikrstat tarabadayah: *if* *'tarap'* and the rest — the comparative affixes — came after the *inferior* thing ending in *genitive* or *accusative*: i.e. if 'X-tara' were derived from the one X surpasses. Nyakkarini syur utkrste prakrteh syad vilingata: in the case of the *superior* which does the *humbling*, there would be a *gender-discrepancy* of the *base* — vilingata: the base would end up with the wrong gender, since the surpassed and the surpassing need not share one. So the derivation would make the comparative's gender depend on whoever is being outdone, which is plainly wrong. The next verses spell out how wrong.

mula_deva: काल्यां कालाद् द्वितीयान्तात् काले काल्यास्तरब् भवेत् ।
न्यक्कर्तरि तथा गार्ग्ये गर्गेभ्यः प्रत्ययो भवेत् ॥ १६० ॥
न्यक्कर्तृषु च गर्गेषु गार्ग्यात् स्यात् तच्च नेष्यते ।
कुमार्याः स्वार्थिके ङीप् स्यात् प्रकृत्यर्थो हि नाधिकः ॥ १६१ ॥
mula_iast: kālyāṃ kālād dvitīyāntāt kāle kālyāstarab bhavet |
nyakkartari tathā gārgye gargebhyaḥ pratyayo bhavet || 160 ||
nyakkartṛṣu ca gargeṣu gārgyāt syāt tacca neṣyate |
kumāryāḥ svārthike ṅīp syāt prakṛtyartho hi nādhikaḥ || 161 ||
translation: (160) In 'kali', 'tarap' would come from 'kala' ending in the accusative; in 'kala', from 'kali'. Likewise, when Gargya does the humbling, the affix would come from the Gargas. (161) And when the Gargas do the humbling, it would come from Gargya — and that is not desired. For 'kumari', in the svarthika, 'nip' would apply; for the base-meaning is not in excess.
commentary: The absurdity of 3.14.159 spelled out on four forms. (160): kalyam kalad dvitiyantat kale kalyas tarab bhavet — in the feminine *'kali'*, 'tarap' would come from masculine *'kala'*; and in *'kala'*, from feminine *'kali'*: each would take the other's gender, and the derivation would deliver 'kalitara' where 'kalatara' is wanted. Nyakkartari tatha gargye gargebhyah pratyayo bhavet: likewise where *Gargya* humbles, the affix would come from the *Gargas* — the plural. (161): nyakkartrsu ca gargesu gargyat syat tac ca nesyate — and conversely; *and that is not desired*. Kumaryah svarthike nip syat prakrtyartho hi nadhikah: for *'kumari'*, in the *svarthika*, *'nip'* would apply, for the *base-meaning* is *not in excess* — the affix adds nothing, so nothing licenses the change. Four forms, four wrong results.

translation: Therefore it is prescribed after a genitive-ending, when the quality residing in its own substratum is in excess — or in an action inhering in the superior.
commentary: The correct derivation. Sasthyantad adhike tasmad gune svasraya-vartini: *therefore* after a *genitive*-ending, when the *quality* residing in its *own substratum* is *in excess* — the comparative is built on the *quality's* excess, and the quality stays in its own seat: svasraya-vartin. Utkrsta-samavetayam kriyayam va vidhiyate: *or* in an *action* *inhering* in the *superior*. So 'tarap' attaches where the excess is — in the quality, or in the action — and not to whoever is surpassed. The gender-discrepancy of 3.14.159-161 never arises, because the base was never the inferior party. The whole objection came from putting the affix in the wrong place, and the repair is to locate the excess correctly.

translation: And what is taken up — the base-meaning — the substance alone is the substratum of those two; from the connection 'this is that', it is cognized by non-difference.
commentary: Upattam ca prakrtyartho dravyam eva asrayas tayoh: what is *taken up*, the *base-meaning* — the *substance alone* is the *substratum* of those *two* (gender and number). Dravyam eva: the substance and nothing else; qualities and universals do not hold gender or number in their own right. So 'yam ity abhisambandhad abhedena pratiyate: from the connection 'this is that', it is *cognized by non-difference*. So the substance is where gender and number live, and everything else that seems to have them has borrowed them by identification (3.14.150's avesa). This is the resolution of the whole gender-and-number stretch: there is one bearer, and the rest is 'this is that'.

translation: And from non-difference of form, that substance is cognized as having expectancy; it is joined with particulars of different forms, as though with substrata.
commentary: Rupabhedac ca tad dravyam akanksavat pratiyate: from *non-difference of form*, that *substance* is cognized as *having expectancy* — akanksavat: with a gap in it, reaching (2.4's sakanksa). Because its form shows no differences, the substance presents itself as incomplete and wanting qualification. Visesair bhinna-rupais tad asrayair iva yujyate: it is *joined* with *particulars* of *different forms*, *as though* with *substrata*. Iva: as though. So the substance takes on particulars as if they were its supports — when in fact it is the support and they are the borrowers (3.14.163). The relation inverts in the cognition, and iva marks the inversion. This is 3.14.9-11's two open generals drawing away each other's doubt, now stated for substance and quality.

mula_deva: संख्या च ताभ्यां द्रव्यात्मा सोऽभिन्नो व्यपदिश्यते ॥ १६५ ॥
mula_iast: saṃkhyā ca tābhyāṃ dravyātmā so'bhinno vyapadiśyate || 165 ||
translation: That gender which is settled in the particulars of different forms, and number — by those two the substance-self, undivided, is designated.
commentary: Bhinna-rupesu yal lingam visesesu vyavasthitam samkhya ca: the *gender* *settled* in the *particulars* of *different forms*, *and number* — the two live in the particulars. Tabhyam dravyatma so 'bhinno vyapadisyate: by *those two* the *substance-self*, *undivided*, is *designated*. Abhinnah: undivided (3.14.99's abheda-ekatva; 3.14.93's tad-atmany avibhakte). So the traffic runs both ways: the substance lends its gender and number to the particulars (3.14.163), and the particulars are what designate the substance by means of them. And the substance so designated is undivided — it does not acquire the particulars' divisions by being pointed at through them. Vyapadisyate again: designated, not described (3.14.135).

translation: The substratum, and what inheres, are the cause of gender and number; therefore 'si', whose state resides in the agent, is cited in the Bhasya.
commentary: Asrayah samavayi ca nimittam linga-samkhyayoh: the *substratum*, and what *inheres*, are the *cause* of gender and number — two causes, not one: where the thing rests, and what rests in it. Kartr-stha-bhavakah setir ato bhasya udahrtah: *therefore* *'si'* (to lie down), whose *state resides in the agent*, is *cited in the Bhasya*. So Patanjali's example is chosen because 'si' is the clean case: its action does not go out to an object but stays in the one who lies (3.7.115's kartr-stha-bhava; 3.8's intransitive action). With nothing but the agent in view, one can see that the agent's gender and number are doing all the work. Bhartrhari is reading the Bhasya's choice of example as an argument — which it was.

translation: If the cause were grasped as the substratum — the means — then there would arise, in that case, the gender and number stated for the object.
commentary: The reason 3.14.166 needed an intransitive example. Nimittam asrayatvena grhyeta yadi sadhanam: *if* the *cause* were grasped as the *substratum* — namely the *means*, the sadhana. Karma-apadistayoh praptis tatra syal linga-samkhyayoh: then there would arise, in that case, the *gender and number* stated for the *object*. So if any karaka could serve as the seat, a transitive verb would let the object's gender and number in, and the form would come out wrong. Hence 'si': no object, no leak. This is a small, exact piece of reasoning about why an example is the example it is, and it shows the samuddesa's respect for the Bhasya's craft (3.14.158's bhasyanusarena).

translation: In the science, the feminine-meaning is extracted from the aggregate by way of being a cause; its connection, at one's pleasure, is with the base or with the affix.
commentary: The feminine begins, and it begins by being named an extraction. Sastre nimitta-bhavena samudayad apoddhrtah stry-arthah: in the *science*, the *feminine-meaning* is *extracted* from the *aggregate* by way of being a *cause* — apoddhrta: pulled out (3.14.143's apoddharas ca samanyam, in the same words). So femininity is not found in the word; it is lifted out of an undivided whole because the grammar needs a condition to hang rules on. Tasya icchaya yogah prakrtya pratyayena va: its *connection*, *at one's pleasure*, is with the *base* or with the *affix*. Icchaya: as one wishes. So having extracted it, one may put it wherever one likes — in the stem or in the suffix — and nothing decides. That is what being an extraction means (3.14.96's upaya-matram nanatvam).

translation: The word 'stri', being a quality-word, has properties similar to 'sita' and the rest; it is employed for the quality alone, or for the substratum possessing condensation.
commentary: Stri-sabdo guna-sabdatvat tulya-dharma sitadibhih: the word *'stri'*, from being a *quality-word*, has *properties similar* to *'sita'* (white) and the rest — it behaves like a colour-term, not like a jati-term (3.14.147's asymmetry: guna-words reach the quality, jati-words do not). Guna-matre prayujyate samstyanavati va asraye: it is employed for the *quality alone*, *or* for the *substratum* possessing *samstyana*. Samstyana: *condensation*, coagulation — from the root sty~ai, and the grammarians' etymology of 'stri'. So the word is ambiguous exactly as 'sukla' is: it may name the quality or the thing that has it. Everything in the following verses turns on which.

translation: If the feminine-meaning is the substance possessing condensation, and that is the base-meaning — then condensation has the purpose of indicating the substance.
commentary: The first horn of 3.14.169's ambiguity. Stry-arthah samstyanavad dravyam prakrtyarthas ca yady asau: *if* the *feminine-meaning* is the *substance* possessing *samstyana*, and *that* is the *base-meaning*. Dravya-upalaksanarthatvam samstyanasya tatha sati: then *samstyana* has the *purpose of indicating the substance* — upalaksana: an indicator, a mark that identifies without characterizing (3.14.55's saptaparna, whose seven leaves identify a tree that has none). So on this horn, condensation is not what 'stri' means; it is only how the word finds its object. The femininity is the substance, and the coagulation is a pointer. Bhartrhari sets out the consequence without comment and turns, in the next verses, to what it costs.

translation: Somewhere a substance is seen indicated by condensation; from the derivative formation of that, condensation not being accepted, the affix would come.
commentary: Samstyanena kvacid dravyam drstam yad upalaksitam: *somewhere* a *substance* is *seen* *indicated* by *samstyana* — the indicator does work, in some cases. Anangikrta-samstyanat tad-vrtteh pratyayo bhavet: from the *derivative formation* of *that*, *samstyana* not being *accepted*, the *affix* would come. So where one derives from such a form without granting the condensation, the feminine affix attaches anyway — and it attaches for the wrong reason, since what licensed it has been denied. The verse is registering a leak in 3.14.170's account: an indicator that is not part of the meaning cannot be relied on to condition affixation, and yet the affixes come. 3.14.172 gives the case.

translation: And when 'bhuta' and the rest, the six designations, operate on 'brahmani' and the like, indicated by condensation — then the affixes would come from them.
commentary: Bhutadayah sad akhyas ca: *'bhuta'* and the rest, the *six designations* — a fixed set of terms in the grammar. Samstyanena upalaksite brahmanyadau yada vrttah: when they *operate on* *'brahmani'* and the like, *indicated by samstyana* (3.14.170's upalaksana). Tebhyah syuh pratyayas tada: *then* the *affixes* would come *from them*. So the feminine affix would be conditioned by the six designations rather than by the femininity — the indicator has been allowed to do the licensing, and the derivation has slipped its moorings. This is 3.14.131's vyabhicara in a specific instance: posit a condition that is only an indicator, and it will stray. The problem 3.14.171 announced now has a name and a form.

translation: For those possessing that, being principal, are the occasioners of the affixes; therefore even in co-reference, 'tap' and the rest is possible.
commentary: The repair. Tadvanto hi pradhanatvat pratyayanam prayojakah: those *possessing that* — the substances that have the samstyana, not the samstyana itself — being *principal*, are the *occasioners* of the *affixes*. Prayojaka: what sets them in motion. So the affix is conditioned by the possessor, not by the indicator, and 3.14.172's slippage is blocked: the six designations were never the licensers, because they were not principal. Samanadhikaranye 'pi tasmat tabadi-sambhavah: *therefore* *even in co-reference*, *'tap'* and the rest is *possible* — even where two words share a referent (3.14.9-11's krsnas tilah), the feminine affix can attach, because the possessor is there to license it. The principal does the work; the mark only points.

translation: When the word 'stri' is described as expressing the quality alone, and the base-meaning is condensation — then the affixes are svarthika.
commentary: The second horn of 3.14.169. Guna-matra-abhidhayitvam stri-sabde varnyate yada: *when* the word *'stri'* is described as expressing the *quality alone* — not the substance that has it. Prakrtyartham ca samstyanam: and the *base-meaning* is *samstyana* — the condensation itself is what the stem says. Svarthikah pratyayas tada: *then* the *affixes* are *svarthika* — affixes that add nothing to the base's meaning, mere formal furniture. So on this horn the feminine suffixes are semantically empty: the stem already means femininity, and the suffix only marks what is there. The two horns of 3.14.169 thus give two quite different jobs to the same affixes — which is 3.14.168's point exactly: the extraction may be lodged in the base or in the affix, *icchaya*, as one pleases, and the grammar comes out either way.

mula_deva: संस्त्याने केवले वृत्तिः प्रकृतीनां न विद्यते ।
mula_iast: saṃstyāne kevale vṛttiḥ prakṛtīnāṃ na vidyate |
translation: In mere condensation, the operation of the bases does not exist; therefore they are grasped as settled in the substance pervaded by it.
commentary: The cost of 3.14.174's horn. Samstyane kevale vrttir prakrtinam na vidyate: in *mere* condensation, the *operation* of the *bases* does *not exist* — kevala: bare, by itself. If 'stri' names only the quality, the stems have nothing to work on: one cannot decline a coagulation. Tad-aviste tato dravye grhyante samavasthitah: *therefore* they are *grasped* as *settled* in the *substance* *pervaded* by it — avista: entered, permeated. So the bases must after all be lodged in the substance, and the second horn collapses back into the first (3.14.170). Which is 3.14.163's finding once more: dravyam eva asrayah — the substance alone is the seat, and every account that tries to do without it comes back.

translation: And in those words in which condensation is required as assisting, from them come 'tap' and the rest; and that is not intended for expression in 'bhuta' and the rest.
commentary: Upakari ca samstyanam yesu sabdesv apeksitam: in those words in which *condensation* is *required* as *assisting* — upakarin: helping, contributing; apeksita: needed. Tebhyas tabadayah: *from them* come *'tap'* and the rest — the feminine affixes attach where the condensation is a live contributor. Tac ca bhutadisv avivaksitam: and that is *not intended for expression* in *'bhuta'* and the rest — avivaksita: not meant to be said (3.14.103's standing device). So the same element is contributory in one class of words and unmeant in another, and the affixation follows the contribution. This settles 3.14.171-172's leak on principle rather than by patch: the affix goes where the condensation is doing something.

translation: When pure condensation is resorted to as the affix's meaning — then the dual and the multiplicity of affixes do not succeed.
commentary: The second horn's second cost. Samstyanam pratyayasya arthah suddham asriyate yada: when *pure* condensation is resorted to as the *affix's* meaning — the svarthika account of 3.14.174, taken strictly. Tada dvivacana-aneka-pratyayatvam na sidhyati: *then* the *dual* and the *multiplicity of affixes* do *not succeed*. So one cannot get 'two women': a quality has no dual (3.14.107's jatau dvivacanabhavat, and a bare guna is no better off), and there is nothing for a second affix to attach to. The horn fails on morphology as 3.14.175 showed it failing on the bases. Bhartrhari is not refuting the account so much as walking it to its end and letting it stop.

translation: If femininity is a universal, then it is that alone; difference is elsewhere unintended, since, though the substances differ, that, being one, is specified.
commentary: A third possibility. Jatis cet stritvam eva asau: *if* femininity is a *universal*, then it is *that alone* — a single jati, striitva, present wherever a woman is. Bhedo 'nyatra avivaksitah: *difference* is *elsewhere* *unintended*. Yasmad bhinnair api dravyais tad ekam sad visisyate: *since*, though the *substances differ*, *that*, being *one*, is *specified* — the many women specify the one femininity. So this account explains what 3.14.177's account could not: the differences are in the substances, the femininity is one, and the plural is theirs, not its. It is the strongest of the three and the samuddesa tests it too — 3.14.179 immediately.

mula_deva: मात्राणां हि तिरोभावे परिमाणं न विद्यते ।
कुमार्य इति तेन स्यात् कुमार्यां भेदसम्भवात् ॥ १७९ ॥
mula_iast: mātrāṇāṃ hi tirobhāve parimāṇaṃ na vidyate |
kumārya iti tena syāt kumāryāṃ bhedasambhavāt || 179 ||
translation: For in the disappearance of the measures, the measure does not exist; therefore 'kumaryah' would come about, from the possibility of difference in 'kumari'.
commentary: The test of 3.14.178. Matranam hi tirobhave parimanam na vidyate: in the *disappearance* of the *measures*, the *measure* does *not exist* — where the units go, the quantity goes: no units, no measure (3.14.127's the unit is the measure-word's own property). Kumarya iti tena syat kumaryam bheda-sambhavat: *therefore* *'kumaryah'* — the plural — *would come about*, from the *possibility of difference* in *'kumari'*. So on the universal-account, the plural has to come from somewhere, and the only place left is the individual girls; the femininity contributes nothing to it. Which is exactly what 3.14.178 wanted — and the verse notes the consequence rather than objecting. The account survives the test.

translation: (180) Just as actions operate in an accompanying substance by means of universal, number and aggregate, even where oneness of self is required — (181) so, from the seeing of the non-difference of the properties of forms from the forms, both co-reference and connection with action are conceivable.
commentary: The analogy that licenses the whole account. (180): jati-sankhya-samaharair yathaiva sahacarini dravye kriyah pravartante — as *actions* operate in an *accompanying substance* by means of *universal*, *number* and *aggregate*: three routes by which an action reaches a thing. Ekatmatve 'py apeksite: *even where* *oneness of self* is *required* — even where the thing must be one, the three routes work. (181): murtibhyo murti-dharmanam tatha 'bhedasya darsanat — from the *seeing* of the *non-difference* of the *properties of forms* from the *forms*: the shape's properties are not other than the shape. Samanadhikaranyam ca kriya-yogas ca kalpate: *co-reference* and *connection with action* are *conceivable*. So because a form's properties are not separate from it, both agreement and predication go through — without any of it requiring real division (3.14.92's anusyuta).

translation: But in co-reference, when the elision of 'matup' is required, 'luk taddhita-luki' would apply; there too the luk is an indicator.
commentary: Samanadhikaranye tu matub-lopad apeksite: in *co-reference*, when the *elision of matup* is *required* (3.14.148's device). Luk taddhita-lukiti syat: *'luk taddhita-luki'* would apply — Panini's rule chaining one elision to another. Luk tatrapy upalaksanam: *there too* the *luk* is an *indicator* — upalaksana: a mark, not a constituent (3.14.170's upalaksanartha). So the elision, having been posited to certify the form, turns out to be functioning as an indicator, and the rule that chains to it is chaining to a pointer. Tatrapi — *there too* — is the point: this is the same thing happening again, and by now one should recognize it. The grammar's absences do indicative work throughout, and Bhartrhari's contribution is to say so each time.

translation: For some, 'tap' and the rest is prescribed only in substances possessing condensation whose differences are abandoned, from concomitance by non-difference.
commentary: Kesancit tyakta-bhedesu dravyesv eva vidhiyate samstyanavatsu tabadih: for *some*, *'tap'* and the rest is prescribed *only* in *substances* possessing *condensation* whose *differences are abandoned* — tyakta-bheda: the differences given up (3.14.101's bhedanam parityagat). Abhedena samanvayat: from *concomitance* by *non-difference*. So a fourth position: the feminine affix requires substances that have shed their differences, and it attaches by the concomitance of what is left. This keeps 3.14.163's dravya as the seat while getting the unity that 3.14.178's universal supplied. The samuddesa now has four accounts of the feminine on the table and has refuted none — which is 3.14.97 again: the upayas are unsettled among persons, because they are upayas.

translation: The substance-self, having become general, its grasp delimited, is joined with actions, with the differences; and the portion remains.
commentary: Samanya-bhuto dravyatma: the *substance-self*, having *become general* — samanya-bhuta: not a universal, but a substance gone general (3.14.141's visesa eva samanyam: the general *is* the particular). Paricchinna-parigrahah: its *grasp delimited* — pariccheda: the cutting-off (3.14.126). Kriyabhir yujyate bhedaih: is *joined* with *actions*, with the *differences*. Bhagas ca avatisthate: and the *portion* *remains* — avatisthate: stands, persists. So the generalized substance takes on actions and differences and something is left over that does not: the bhaga persists through. This is the samuddesa's picture of how a thing can be one and still be caught in the traffic of predication — the joining happens at the edges, and a portion stands out of it.

translation: In 'sukla' and the rest, the substratum-substance is expressed as principal; but femininity, since it is the affix's meaning, is the domain of expression.
commentary: The disanalogy that has been threatening since 3.14.169. Sukladisv asraya-dravyam pradhanyena abhidhiyate: in *'sukla'* and the rest, the *substratum-substance* is *expressed as principal* — 'sukla' says the white *thing*, and the whiteness rides along. Stritvam tu pratyayarthatvad abhidha-visayo yatah: *but* femininity, since it is the *affix's meaning*, is the *domain of expression* — it is what gets said. So the parallel with the colour-words breaks: in 'sukla' the substance is principal and the quality subordinate; in the feminine the quality is what the affix expresses, and the relation inverts. 3.14.169 proposed the analogy; here it is withdrawn where it fails. This is 3.14.132's discipline being applied to the samuddesa's own working assumption.

translation: From the connection 'this is that', femininity attains a substratum; or else the reversal of subordination is established by nature.
commentary: Two ways out of 3.14.185's inversion. So 'yam ity abhisambandhad asrayam pratipadyate stritvam: from the connection 'this is that', *femininity* *attains a substratum* — the identification hands it a seat it does not have of its own (3.14.150's avesa). Or: svabhava-siddho va guna-bhava-viparyayah: the *reversal of subordination* is *established by nature* — svabhava-siddha: settled by the thing's own nature; the inversion is simply how it is, and needs no mechanism. So either the identification supplies what is missing, or nothing is missing and the inversion is a fact. Bhartrhari offers both and endorses neither. By this point in the samuddesa that is not evasion but method (3.14.119's showing the result survives the disputed premise).

translation: Or the general is taught as a quality, from its having expectancy; that is a property of the self of the individuals, having a single presentation for its basis.
commentary: A fifth account, and the most interesting. Sakanksatvad gunatvena samanyam va upadisyate: *or* the *general* is *taught as a quality*, from its *having expectancy* — sakanksa: reaching, incomplete (3.14.164's akanksavat). The general is classed as a guna because, like a quality, it cannot stand alone. Vyaktinam atma-dharmo 'sau: *that* is a *property of the self* of the *individuals* — not a thing over them but their own nature. Eka-prakhya-nibandhanah: having a *single presentation* for its *basis* — eka-prakhya: one appearing, one showing-forth. So the general is: the individuals' own property, grounded in the fact that they present alike. Not a universal, not a name, not an exclusion merely — a single presentation, and the property is that. This is as close as the work comes to a positive account of samanya, and it is built out of appearing (3.14.141's the general is the particular itself).

translation: (188) And that state is of such a kind, when the grasping of the portion-difference has been made by the intellect alone, the substratum-hood of the differences being conceived — (189) then, even when the differences are extracted, in the substratum having individual-form, by the reflective reference to gender it attains gender and number.
commentary: The whole gender-and-number analysis referred to the intellect. (188): evambhuta ca sa avastha bhaga-bheda-parigrahe krte buddhya eva — that *state* is of *such a kind*, when the *grasping* of the *portion-difference* has been *made by the intellect alone*. Buddhya eva: by the intellect and by nothing else — the divisions were never in the object (3.14.15's buddhya ekam bhidyate; 3.14.93's buddhy-antaram upasritah). Bhedanam asrayatve 'vakalpite: the *substratum-hood* of the *differences* being *conceived* — avakalpita: constructed. (189): niskrstesv api bhedesu vyakti-rupasraye tatah — *even when* the *differences* are *extracted* (niskrsta: pulled out, as in apoddhara), then, in the *substratum* having *individual-form*. Linga-pratyavamarsena linga-samkhye prapadyate: by the *reflective reference* to *gender*, it *attains* gender and number. Pratyavamarsa: the mind's turning back on what it laid down. So gender and number arrive by the intellect referring back to its own extraction — which is 3.14.74's imagined parts, and the whole samuddesa, in one word.

translation: Without the employment of the word 'ca', the operating in an unspecified meaning of those which are to be a dvandva is cognized, from non-difference of form.
commentary: The dvandva returns, and the samuddesa's method is by now familiar. Antarena ca-sabdasya prayogam: *without* the *employment* of the word *'ca'* — no 'and' is spoken. Dvandva-bhavinam avisistartha-vrttitvam: of those which are *to be* a *dvandva* — bhavin: future, not yet compounded — the *operating in an unspecified meaning*: before the compounding, the members mean indeterminately. Rupabhedat pratiyate: is *cognized*, from *non-difference of form*. So the members that will make a dvandva already operate unspecifiedly, and one knows it because their form shows no difference. The togetherness of 3.14.38 (the dvandva's saha-vivaksa, lost the moment one says 'ca') is thus present before the compound exists — which is why no 'ca' is needed to make it.

translation: Or the derivative formation, in a meaning that has alternation, or is to be excluded, or is accumulated — of those whose powers are unknown, it is restricted by the indicator.
commentary: What 'ca' is for. Vikalpavati va vrttir nivartye 'rthe samuccite: the formation, in a meaning that has *alternation*, or is *to be excluded*, or is *accumulated* — three of the particle's senses, and no form shows which. Tesam ajnata-saktinam dyotakena niyamyate: of those whose *powers are unknown*, it is *restricted* by the *indicator* — ajnata-sakti: the words' capacities are not known from their shapes; the dyotaka narrows them. So the particle does not add a meaning; it *restricts* a range that was already there and unreadable. This is the dyotaka doctrine at its clearest (2.190's vacaka against dyotaka): 'ca' reveals which of the members' latent powers is in play, and reveals nothing that was not latent.

translation: In the derivative formation, from its specified form, the word 'ca' withdraws; even where the meaning differs, from likeness of form that is designated by the meaning.
commentary: Why the dvandva has no 'and'. Vrttau visista-rupatvat ca-sabdo vinivartate: in the *derivative formation*, from its *specified form*, the word *'ca'* *withdraws* — the compound's own shape already specifies what 'ca' would have restricted, so the restrictor is redundant (3.14.191's niyamyate). Artha-bhede 'pi sarupyat tac ca arthena apadisyate: even where the *meaning differs*, from *likeness of form*, that is *designated* by the *meaning*. So the compound does the particle's work by being the shape it is, and the particle drops out — not elided (3.14.128's what withdraws has no being) but unnecessary. 'Rama-krsnau' needs no 'and' because its form is already the restriction.

mula_deva: तद्धर्मत्वं ततो द्वन्द्वे चादिष्वर्थकृतं हि तत् ॥ १९३ ॥
mula_iast: taddharmatvaṃ tato dvandve cādiṣvarthakṛtaṃ hi tat || 193 ||
translation: And if the non-substantial meaning of 'ca' is itself resorted to — then in the dvandva there would be the property of that; for in 'ca' and the rest, that is made by the meaning.
commentary: Casya ca asattva-bhuto 'rthah sa eva asriyate yadi: *if* the *non-substantial* meaning of *'ca'* — asattva-bhuta: not a thing, the standard characterization of what particles mean — is *itself* resorted to as the dvandva's meaning. Tad-dharmatvam tato dvandve: *then* in the *dvandva* there would be *the property of that* — the compound would inherit ca's non-substantiality, and so would have no gender and no number, since only substances have those (3.14.163's dravyam eva asrayah). Cadisv artha-krtam hi tat: for in *'ca'* and the rest, *that* is *made by the meaning*. So making the dvandva mean what 'ca' means would make it an indeclinable. The consequence is unacceptable and the following verses look for a way round.

mula_deva: चार्थः शक्तेः क्वचिद् भेदात् कथञ्चित् समवस्थितः ।
mula_iast: cārthaḥ śakteḥ kvacid bhedāt kathañcit samavasthitaḥ |
translation: The meaning of 'ca', from a difference of power somewhere, is somehow settled; 'ca' and the rest are its indicators, but the dvandva is the expresser of those which possess it.
commentary: The way round 3.14.193, and it is precise. Carthah sakteh kvacid bhedat kathancit samavasthitah: the *meaning of 'ca'*, from a *difference of power* somewhere, is *somehow* settled — kathancit: somehow; Bhartrhari does not pretend to have located it. Dyotakas cadayas tasya: *'ca'* and the rest are its *indicators*. Vakta dvandvas tu tadvatam: *but* the *dvandva* is the *expresser* of *those which possess it* — tadvat: the *possessors* of ca's meaning, i.e. the things conjoined, not the conjoining. So the distinction that saves the account: 'ca' indicates the togetherness; the dvandva *says the things that have it*. The compound therefore means substances after all, and takes gender and number (3.14.193's difficulty dissolved). Dyotaka against vacaka, doing real work.

translation: The state of being another's word-meaning, belonging to ca's meaning — which is to be expressed as alternation and so on — would not be conceivable, since it is an indicator; therefore, existing, it is indicated.
commentary: Vikalpady-abhidheyasya carthasya anya-padarthata: the state of being *another's word-meaning* (3.14.38's bahuvrihi-relation), belonging to *ca's meaning* — which is *to be expressed* as *alternation* and so on. Dyotakatvan na kalpeta: would *not be conceivable*, since it is an *indicator*: an indicator cannot be somebody else's word-meaning, because it is not a meaning at all. Tasmat sad upalaksyate: *therefore*, *existing*, it is *indicated*. Sat: it *is* there — Bhartrhari does not make ca's sense unreal; he makes it unsayable. It exists and is pointed at, and no word says it. This is the exact boundary the dyotaka doctrine draws, and 3.14.194's dvandva lives on the other side of it.

translation: There, the gender is natural, when a word-property is required; some word somehow attains that very meaning.
commentary: Tatra svabhavikam lingam sabda-dharme vyapeksite: *there*, the *gender* is *natural* — svabhavika: belonging to the thing's own nature (3.14.186's svabhava-siddha) — when a *word-property* is *required*. Sabdah kascit tam eva artham kathancit pratipadyate: *some* word *somehow* attains *that very* meaning. Kascit... kathancit: *some* word, *somehow* — the vagueness is not carelessness; it is the claim. Which word reaches which meaning, and by what route, is not derivable: it happens, and the grammar reports it (3.14.154's akhyayate ca sastrena). So gender is natural, the reaching is contingent, and the science stands at its distance (3.14.74's atidure). The verse's diffidence is its content.

translation: Meanings are spread out from the word; it is the ordainer of the differences. Other than the word, there is no inference of the intention to speak.
commentary: One of the summit verses of the work. Sabdad arthah pratayante: *meanings* are *spread out* from the *word* — pratayante, from pra-tan: woven forth, extended like a fabric from its warp. Not caused by, not indicated by: *spread out from*. Sa bhedanam vidhayakah: *it* is the *ordainer* of the *differences* — vidhayaka: the one who ordains, lays down (3.14.15's sa hi arthasya vidhayika, of the intellect; the same word, now of the word). All the divisions this samuddesa has been unmaking — parts, members, genders, numbers — are the word's own ordinances. Anumanam vivaksayah sabdad anyan na vidyate: there is *no inference* of the *intention to speak* *other than the word*. So one cannot get behind the word to the speaker's meaning: the word is the only evidence there is of what was meant, and there is no independent access to vivaksa. This is the whole doctrine — 1.123's no cognition without word; 2.32's the word is the measure — and it is why 3.14.196 could only say kathancit. The word ordains, and nothing stands behind it to be consulted.

translation: (198) The dvandva's meaning would be the accumulated, with the accumulation subordinate; or else the accumulation, with the accumulated subordinate. (199) Where the accumulated is principal, gender and number are by nature; where the accumulation is principal, the science would be the teacher.
commentary: The fork, cleanly drawn. (198): samuccitah syad dvandvartho guna-bhuta-samuccayah — the dvandva means the *accumulated things*, with the *accumulation* subordinate. Samuccayo vapi bhaved guna-bhuta-samuccitah: *or* the *accumulation*, with the *things* subordinate. (199): samuccitasya pradhanye linga-samkhye svabhavatah — where the *things* are principal, gender and number are *by nature*: substances have them, and nothing need be said (3.14.163). Samuccayasya pradhanye sastram syat pratipadakam: where the *accumulation* is principal, the *science* would be the *teacher* — the accumulation is no substance, so the grammar must supply what the meaning cannot (3.14.134's sastram arabdham). So the fork is exactly the fork of 3.14.145 against 3.14.134: does concord come from the world or from the rules? And the answer depends on what a dvandva is about.

translation: Others know that, even where the meaning possessing accumulation is principal, gender and number are not established — from their conforming to the cause.
commentary: An objection to the first horn. Samuccayavato 'rthasya pradhanye 'py apare viduh: *others* know that *even where* the *meaning possessing accumulation* is *principal* — i.e. even on 3.14.198's first branch, where the things are what is meant. Nimitta-anuvidhayitvad asiddhim linga-samkhyayoh: gender and number are *not established*, from their *conforming to the cause* — anuvidhayin: following after (3.14.153's word). If gender and number must follow their occasioning cause, and the cause is the accumulation, then they cannot come 'by nature' from the substances after all. So even the natural horn needs the science. The objection is good and 3.14.201-202 must meet it.

translation: If the accumulation is the cause, there would be conformity to the cause; by agreement and difference, ca's meaning is the basis of the dvandva.
commentary: The objection's premise examined. Samuccayo nimittam cet syan nimitta-anuvartanam: *if* the *accumulation* is the *cause*, there would be *conformity to the cause* — the conditional is granted, and its antecedent is now the question. Anvaya-vyatirekabhyam cartho dvandva-nibandhanah: by *agreement and difference* — the distributional test (2.12's method) — *ca's meaning* is the *basis* of the *dvandva*: where the togetherness is, the compound is; where it is not, not. So the test says the accumulation *is* the basis, and 3.14.200's antecedent holds. Bhartrhari is running the objection's own argument to its strongest and then, in 3.14.202, showing that the conclusion still does not follow.

translation: Whether the accumulated is the cause, or even where ca's meaning has departed — the dvandva's gender and number stand settled, established by nature.
commentary: The answer. Samuccita-nimittatve carthasya apagame 'pi va: *whether* the *accumulated* is the *cause*, *or even* where *ca's meaning* has *departed* — both branches, including the one where the togetherness is simply gone. Svabhava-siddhe dvandvasya linga-samkhye vyavasthite: the dvandva's gender and number *stand settled*, *established by nature*. So 3.14.200's inference fails not because its premise is false but because the conclusion does not depend on it: however the causation is arranged, the compound's gender and number are there. Svabhava-siddha and vyavasthita together: settled by nature, and standing. This is 3.14.119's move once more — show the result survives every branch, and the branching stops mattering.

translation: The particle, being an indicator of a meaning residing in another word, is not joined with gender and number; but the dvandva is the expresser of the meaning.
commentary: The ground of 3.14.202, stated plainly. Padantara-sthasya arthasya dyotakatvan na yujyate nipato linga-sankhyabhyam: the *particle*, being an *indicator* of a meaning *residing in another word*, is *not joined* with *gender and number*. Padantara-stha: the meaning it indicates is not its own — it sits in someone else's word (3.14.191's restricting the members' latent powers). So a particle can have no gender: it has no meaning of its own to bear one. Dvandvas tv arthasya vacakah: *but* the *dvandva* is the *expresser* of the *meaning* — vacaka, not dyotaka. So the compound is categorially unlike the particle whose work it does, and 3.14.193's threatened contagion cannot occur: the dvandva says things, and things have gender and number (3.14.194).

translation: And in conformity to the cause, from the non-requiring of the substance's properties, connection with action by way of subordinate and principal is not conceivable.
commentary: The cost of 3.14.200's position, now charged. Nimitta-anuvidhane ca dravya-dharma-anapeksanat: in *conformity to the cause*, from the *non-requiring* of the *substance's properties* — if gender and number merely follow the accumulation, the substance's own properties are not needed and drop out of view. Guna-pradhana-bhavena kriya-yogo na kalpate: *connection with action* by way of *subordinate and principal* is *not conceivable*. So the compound could not be predicated of: an action needs a substance with properties to attach to, and this account has dispensed with them. The objection of 3.14.200, followed out, costs the dvandva its ability to appear in a sentence — which 3.14.205 makes fatal.

mula_deva: यस्य नास्ति क्रियायोगः स्वतन्त्रोऽसौ न विद्यते ।
mula_iast: yasya nāsti kriyāyogaḥ svatantro'sau na vidyate |
translation: That which has no connection with action does not exist independently; the dvandva's meaning would there be — and the taking-up is purposeless.
commentary: The fatal step. Yasya nasti kriya-yogah svatantro 'sau na vidyate: that which has *no connection with action* does *not exist independently* — a thing that cannot enter into action has no standing of its own; being is being caught up in action (3.8's the whole world is a weave of actions; 3.7.3's the karakas as what is capable). Artho dvandvasya tatra syad upadanam anarthakam: the *dvandva's meaning* would *there* be — and the *taking-up* is *purposeless*: anarthaka (3.14.126's word). So 3.14.200's account leaves the dvandva with a meaning that cannot act and therefore is not, and the grammar's taking it up is pointless. The refutation is complete, and its principle is worth marking: for this work, to be is to be able to be predicated.

translation: The expresser of the meaning possessing accumulation does not conform; and its meaning, being also the cause, is therefore joined with its own properties.
commentary: The settled position. Samuccayavato 'rthasya vacako na anuvartate: the *expresser* of the meaning *possessing accumulation* does *not conform* — na anuvartate: the dvandva, being a vacaka (3.14.203), does not follow after its cause the way an indicator would. Nimittam api ca asya arthah sva-dharmair yujyate tatah: and its *meaning*, being *also the cause*, is *therefore joined with its own properties*. So the compound's meaning is both what it says and what conditions it, and it therefore carries its own gender and number rather than borrowing them — which is 3.14.202's svabhava-siddha, now derived rather than asserted. The dvandva-analysis closes: 'ca' indicates and has nothing; the dvandva says and has everything.

mula_deva: बाह्यो नास्त्याश्रयो द्वन्द्वे विशेषौ तत्र हि श्रुतौ ।
mula_iast: bāhyo nāstyāśrayo dvandve viśeṣau tatra hi śrutau |
translation: In a dvandva there is no external substratum, for the two particulars are heard there; the accumulation, having them for its support, is designated by their properties.
commentary: Why the dvandva is not a bahuvrihi. Bahyo nasty asrayo dvandve: in a *dvandva* there is *no external substratum* — nothing outside it that it is about (3.14.38's anya-padartha, which the bahuvrihi has and this does not). Visesau tatra hi srutau: for the *two particulars* are *heard* *there* — the dvandva says its own members, audibly. Samuccayas tad-adharas tad-dharmair vyapadisyate: the *accumulation*, having *them* for its *support*, is *designated by their properties*. So the accumulation rests on the members and takes its gender and number from them — no outside seat is needed, because the members are right there. This grounds 3.14.206's svabhava-siddha: the dvandva has its own properties because it has its own members.

mula_deva: एकः समूहो धर्मान् स भागयोः प्रतिपद्यते ॥ २०८ ॥
mula_iast: ekaḥ samūho dharmān sa bhāgayoḥ pratipadyate || 208 ||
translation: Or that one aggregate which is followed, as it were, by the two member-differences which possess difference — acquires the properties of the two portions.
commentary: The same result by the other route. Yo va avayava-bhedabhyam bhedavadbhyam iva anvitah: that which is *followed*, *as it were*, by the *two member-differences* which *possess difference* — iva again (3.14.68's asadbhir bhagair anugata iva, in nearly the same words): the aggregate is only *as though* accompanied by its members.
