Ekah samuho dharman sa bhagayoh pratipadyate: *one* *aggregate*, it *acquires* the *properties* of the *two portions*. Ekah samuhah: one aggregate (3.14.96's samudayas tv eka eva sah). So the compound is one and takes two members' properties without having two members — the iva carries the whole weight. This is the samuddesa's settled shape: unity in fact, duality as device.

translation: And that meaning is one and of two selves, endowed with difference and non-difference; resting on the two differences, it attains the gender and number residing in them.
commentary: Ekas ca dvayatmako 'rtho 'sau: that *meaning* is *one* and *of two selves* — the paradox stated without apology. Bhedabheda-samanvitah: *endowed with difference and non-difference* — bhedabheda, the doctrine that will bear Bhartrhari's name in the later tradition, here in its native habitat: a compound's meaning. Yau bhedav asritas tat-sthe linga-sankhye prapadyate: *resting on* the *two differences*, it *attains* the *gender and number* *residing in them*. So the dvandva is genuinely both — one meaning with two selves — and it draws its concord from the side of itself that is two. Note that 3.14.142 refused to settle whether the general is same or different from its particulars; here the refusal has become a positive characterization. What cannot be resolved into either is what a compound is.

translation: As the word 'Caitra' is not employed when the Caitra-meaning is expressed by its own word — so there would be non-employment in the bahuvrihi.
commentary: The bahuvrihi's problem, opened. Yatha sva-sabdabhihite caitrarthe na prayujyate caitra-sabdah: as the word *'Caitra'* is *not employed* when the Caitra-*meaning* is already *expressed by its own word* — one does not say a thing twice. Bahuvrihav aprayogas tatha bhavet: *so* there would be *non-employment* in the *bahuvrihi*. So if 'citragu' expresses its possessor, the possessor's own name should become unusable alongside it — one could not say 'citragur devadattah'. But one can. So either the compound does not express the possessor, or the principle is wrong. The following verses take the first branch.

translation: As by 'gauh' there is no expression of 'sukla' and the rest — so, of whomever there is a connection, that much is cognized by 'go'.
commentary: The first branch. Yatha gaur iti sukhlader abhidhanam na vidyate: as by *'gauh'* there is *no expression* of *'sukla'* and the rest — 'cow' does not say white, though every cow has some colour. Evam yasya abhisambandho gobhis tavat pratiyate: *so*, of *whomever* there is a *connection*, *that much* is cognized by *'go'*. Tavat: *that much* and no more. So 'citragu' conveys only that someone stands connected to variegated cows — it does not name him, any more than 'gauh' names a colour. The possessor is reached and not expressed, and 3.14.210's difficulty dissolves: the compound never said Caitra, so Caitra's name remains free.

mula_deva: सम्बन्धी नियतो रूढश्चित्राणां न च विद्यते ।
mula_iast: sambandhī niyato rūḍhaścitrāṇāṃ na ca vidyate |
translation: And there is no fixed, conventionally established relatum of the variegated cows — as 'vajrapani' or 'tryaksa' is settled.
commentary: The proof of 3.14.211. Sambandhi niyato rudhas citranam na ca vidyate: there is *no fixed*, *conventionally established* relatum of the *variegated* ones — rudha: grown into place by convention (3.14.55). Nobody in particular is *the* owner of dappled cows. Gavam yatha vajrapanis tryakso vapi vyavasthitah: as *'vajrapani'* — thunderbolt-in-hand, which means Indra and nobody else — *or* *'tryaksa'* — three-eyed, which means Siva — is *settled*. So some bahuvrihis have hardened into names and pick out one being by convention; 'citragu' has not. And that is the evidence: if the compound *expressed* its possessor, every bahuvrihi would be a proper name. Only the ones convention has frozen are, and the freezing is extraneous to the form.

translation: Although particulars are heard in sentences, from their being other words — this derivation-word is quite another, an expresser of the general.
commentary: Sabdantaratvad vakyesu visesa yady api srutah: although *particulars* are *heard* in *sentences*, from their being *other words* — the analytic phrase 'citra gavo yasya' does name its parts. Vrtti-sabdo 'nya eva ayam: this *derivation-word* is *quite another* — anya eva: simply a different word (3.14.49's atyanta-bheda, and 3.14.136 in nearly these words). Samanyasya abhidhayakah: an *expresser of the general*. So the compound says a general — someone-or-other-with-dappled-cows — where the phrase says particulars. This is why 3.14.212 found no fixed relatum: the compound was never in the business of picking one out. It expresses a general and leaves the individual to be found.

translation: It excludes the non-cow and the non-variegated-cowed alike, from difference of form; and thus, from non-difference of form, it is the expresser of the citragu-particulars.
commentary: How a general expression reaches particulars, and the mechanism is exclusion. Agor acitragos ca eva rupa-bhedan nivartakah: it *excludes* the *non-cow* and the *non-citragu*, from *difference of form* — nivartaka: what turns away (3.14.141's vinivartakah; 3.14.142's whatever is resorted to excludes something else). Where the form differs, the word turns it away. Evam citragu-visesanam rupabhedat tu vacakah: *thus*, from *non-difference of form*, it is the *expresser* of the *citragu-particulars*. So the same fact — form-difference — does both jobs: where it obtains the word excludes, where it fails the word expresses. The general is nothing but this two-sided working of form, and every particular that survives the exclusion is expressed. Bhartrhari's samanya is an exclusion that leaves particulars standing (3.14.187's eka-prakhya).

mula_deva: एवं यदि स्यात् सामान्यं तस्य न स्यात् प्रतिश्रुतिः ॥ २१५ ॥
mula_iast: evaṃ yadi syāt sāmānyaṃ tasya na syāt pratiśrutiḥ || 215 ||
translation: As this 'citragu' is not employed when [the thing] has been employed — so, if it were a general, there would be no re-hearing of it.
commentary: Yatha citragur ity etat prayukte na prayujyate: as this *'citragu'* is *not employed* when [the thing] has been *employed*. Evam yadi syat samanyam tasya na syat pratisrutih: *so*, *if* it were a *general*, there would be *no re-hearing* of it — pratisruti: hearing again, echo. So the objection: on the general-account, the word should not bear repetition — one general once said is said. But 'citragu' can be said again of the same man, and heard again. Hence, allegedly, it is no general. The verse is compressed and it is an objection, not a doctrine; 3.14.216 answers by showing that generals and particulars exclude each other in both directions.
contested: Pada b, 'prayukte na prayujyate', has no expressed subject; we supply 'the thing' from 3.14.210, whose construction it echoes. Alternatively the locative absolute may resume 'citragu' itself (the word not being employed once employed). The objection's force — that a general should not tolerate repetition — is the same on either supply.

translation: But 'sarva' and the rest, the particulars, are excluders of the specific instances — as the specific instances are annullers of the general and of other instances.
commentary: The answer to 3.14.215, and it makes exclusion mutual. Sarvadayo visesas tu pradesanam nivartakah: *'sarva'* (all) and the rest, the *particulars*, are *excluders* of the *specific instances* — pradesa: a particular case, an instance. So the general terms turn away the instances. Yatha pradesah samanya-pradesantara-badhakah: *as* the *instances* are *annullers* of the *general* and of *other instances* — badhaka: what sublates, the strong word. So the traffic runs both ways: generals exclude instances, and instances annul both the general and each other. Nothing is privileged; every term turns away whatever it is not (3.14.142's yad yad asriyate). And so 3.14.215's difficulty fails: a general is not consumed by being said, because its being said is an exclusion, and exclusions repeat.

translation: Or, from the case-meaning's being expressed, the genitive is not employed afterwards; but from the substance's not being expressed, the word 'tad' is employed afterwards.
commentary: Two facts about how bahuvrihis are used. Vibhakty-arthabhidhanad va sasthi na anuprayujyate: from the *case-meaning's* being *expressed*, the *genitive* is *not employed* afterwards — the compound has already said the possessive relation, so no 'whose' need follow. Dravyasya anabhidhanat tu tac-chabdo 'nuprayujyate: *but* from the *substance's* *not being expressed*, the word *'tad'* *is* employed afterwards. So the compound says the relation and does not say the possessor — and usage reflects both halves precisely: no genitive (the relation is in), a pronoun (the substance is out). This is 3.14.211's tavat confirmed from the outside: what a word expresses can be read off what one must add to it.

translation: If co-reference is conceivable from the elision of 'matup' — since 'matup' also has that meaning, an infinite regress follows.
commentary: The elision-device, finally broken. Samanadhikaranyam cen matub-lopat prakalpate: *if* *co-reference* is conceivable from the *elision of matup* — the device of 3.14.87, 3.14.148, 3.14.182. Matupo 'pi tad-arthatvad anavastha prasajyate: since *matup* *also* has *that* meaning, an *infinite regress* follows — anavastha: no standing, the endless recession. Because if possession must be carried by a matup, then the matup's own possession needs another matup, and so without end. So the device that has been quietly doing the work all through this stretch is now shown to be viciously regressive. Bhartrhari has let it run and run and then pulled it — which is 3.14.76's vyutpattir anavasthita made literal: the derivation has no footing, and here is the proof.

translation: And of the relation, another relation as relatum follows; and where the case-meaning is principal, connection with action is not conceivable.
commentary: A second regress and a second failure. Sambandhasya ca sambandhi sambandho 'nyah prasajyate: of the *relation*, *another relation* as *relatum* *follows* — the relation needs relating to its terms, and that relating needs relating (3.3's the sambandha-samuddesa's central difficulty: relation cannot be a relatum without regress). Vibhakty-artha-pradhane ca kriya-yogo na kalpate: and where the *case-meaning* is *principal*, *connection with action* is *not conceivable*. So the second failure is the fatal one of 3.14.205: a meaning that cannot enter into action does not independently exist. Making the relation principal in a bahuvrihi thus both regresses and de-realizes. The account is finished.

translation: From the case-meaning's being principal, therefore 'there' — there is no action; 'drs' and the rest is conceivable as the cause of object, agent and the rest.
commentary: Vibhakty-artha-pradhanatvat tatas tatreti na kriya: from the *case-meaning's* being *principal*, *therefore* 'tatra' — 'there' — there is *no action*: a locative standing on its own has no verb to be locative to, and a bare relation is such a locative. Drsyadih karma-kartradi-nimittatvaya kalpate: *'drs'* (to see) and the rest is *conceivable* as the *cause* of *object*, *agent* and the rest — it is the *verb* that generates the karakas (3.7.1's the sadhanas are what the action needs; 3.8.2's the action is the principal). So relations cannot be principal because karakas are made by verbs, and a compound with a relation at its centre has no verb in it. The refutation is now complete on principle: relation is not a thing one can put in the middle of anything.

translation: And how could a relation be included, being expressed as principal? And how could that, being such, be the meaning of a nominal base?
commentary: Two rhetorical questions, and they close the case. Antarbhavec ca sambandhah pradhanyabhihitah katham: *how* could a *relation* be *included* — antarbhu: contained within something — while being *expressed as principal*? What is principal is not contained; the two are contraries. Sa pratipadikarthas ca tatha-bhutah katham bhavet: and *how* could *that*, being *such*, be the meaning of a *nominal base*? A pratipadika means a thing; a relation is not a thing (3.3's relation as neither substance nor quality). So a bahuvrihi cannot have its relation as its meaning, because a nominal stem cannot mean a relation and nothing that is principal can be contained. Katham... katham: the questions have no answers, and are not meant to.

translation: But from the impossibility in the relation, there is possibility in what accompanies the relation — as of the operations of universal, number and aggregate.
commentary: The resolution. Asambhavat tu sambandhe: from the *impossibility* in the *relation* — 3.14.219-221's demonstration that nothing can be predicated of a relation. Sambandha-sahacarini sambhavah: in what *accompanies* the relation, there is *possibility* — sahacarin: the accompanier (3.14.31's bhinnesu sahacarisu, where the samuddesa began). Jati-sankhya-samahara-karyanam iva: *as* of the *operations* of *universal*, *number* and *aggregate* (3.14.180's three routes). So the grammar's operations do not fall on the relation, which cannot bear them; they fall on what goes along with it, exactly as universal, number and aggregate reach a substance by accompanying it. The bahuvrihi means neither its members nor its relation nor its possessor: it means what accompanies the relation, and the possessor is found by that.

translation: From the connection 'this is that', of those expressing a specified substratum — as with 'sukla' and the rest, gender and number will come about from the undertaking of the science.
commentary: So 'yam ity abhisambandhad visistasraya-vacinam: from the connection 'this is that', of those *expressing a specified substratum* — the bahuvrihis, which reach a substratum without naming it (3.14.211's tavat). Sukladivat linga-sankhye sastrarambhad bhavisyatah: as with *'sukla'* and the rest, *gender and number* will come about from the *undertaking of the science*. Sastrarambhat: because the grammar takes it up (3.14.134's sastram arabdham, in the same idiom). So the bahuvrihi's concord is not natural (against 3.14.202's dvandva, which had it by svabhava): it must be legislated, because there is no substance in the compound to bear it. The two compound-types are thus opposite in exactly the place one would expect.

mula_deva: भेदेन तु विवक्षायां सामान्ये वा विवक्षिते ।
mula_iast: bhedena tu vivakṣāyāṃ sāmānye vā vivakṣite |
translation: But when the intention to express is by way of difference, or when the general is intended — there would be no reaching of a word-meaning possessing gender and number.
commentary: Bhedena tu vivaksayam samanye va vivaksite: when the *intention to express* is *by way of difference*, *or* when the *general* is *intended* — vivaksa, the will to say (3.14.197's the word is the only inference of it). Salingasya sasamkhyasya padarthasya agatir bhavet: there would be *no reaching* — agati: no access, no way through — of a *word-meaning* possessing *gender and number*. So on either intention, differentiating or generalizing, one cannot get to a gendered and numbered meaning: the differentiated has no unity to bear them, the general has no substance. This is why 3.14.223 had to call in the sastra: neither vivaksa delivers concord, and something must.

translation: Correctness is not seen of what operates in the case-meaning alone; 'correctness belongs to what operates in the whole meaning' — thus the taking-up of the word 'artha' is made.
commentary: A word in Panini's rule explained. Sadhutvam na vibhakty-artha-matre vrttasya drsyate: *correctness* is *not seen* of what operates in the *case-meaning alone* — a form that reaches only the relation is not sadhu (3.14.219-221's demonstration that a relation cannot be the meaning). Krtsnartha-vrtteh sadhutvam ity artha-grahanam krtam: 'correctness belongs to what operates in the *whole meaning*' — thus the *taking-up of 'artha'* is *made*. So the word 'artha' in the sutra is there to require that the form reach the *whole* meaning, relation and relata together. Bhartrhari is reading a single word of Panini's as carrying the doctrine of 3.14.222 — the operations fall on what accompanies the relation, and the rule says so by saying 'artha'.

translation: When, from the connection 'this is that', this operates in the substance — then correctness is stated of what possesses gender and number.
commentary: So 'yam ity abhisambandhad dravya-vrttir ayam yada: when, from the connection 'this is that', this *operates in the substance* — the compound reaches a substance by identification, not by naming (3.14.211). Salingasya sasamkhyasya tada sadhutvam ucyate: *then* *correctness* is stated of what *possesses gender and number*. So the order is: identification first, substance reached, concord acquired, correctness pronounced. Nothing here is in the compound's meaning; all of it is in what the compound gets to. This completes 3.14.225: 'artha' in the rule means the whole reach of the word, and the reach ends in a substance (3.14.163's dravyam eva asrayah, for the last time in this stretch).

translation: As the genitive is not heard when the case-meaning is included — so the non-hearing of the expressers of gender and number would follow.
commentary: An objection to 3.14.226. Antarbhuta-vibhakty-arthe sasthi na sruyate yatha: as the *genitive* is *not heard* when the *case-meaning* is *included* — 3.14.217's fact: the compound has swallowed the relation, so no 'whose' follows. Tatha asrutih prasajyate linga-sankhyabhidhayinam: *so* the *non-hearing* of the *expressers* of *gender and number* would *follow*. So if inclusion silences the genitive, inclusion should silence the gender-and-number affixes too — and then no bahuvrihi could inflect. The objection is exact and it uses the samuddesa's own finding against it. 3.14.228 states the consequence in its full severity.

translation: The bahuvrihi would then have similarity with an indeclinable, from the removal of the formation which is the cause of gender and number.
commentary: The consequence, and it is the one Bhartrhari has been avoiding since 3.14.193. Sadharmyam avyayena syad bahuvriheh tatha sati: the *bahuvrihi* would then have *similarity* with an *indeclinable*. Linga-sankhya-nimittasya samskarasya apavartanat: from the *removal* of the *formation* which is the *cause* of gender and number — apavartana: the turning away, the withdrawal of the very derivational process that would have supplied concord. So 3.14.227's objection makes the bahuvrihi an avyaya — uninflectable, like 'ca' (3.14.193's threatened contagion, arriving from the other direction). The result is plainly false: 'citragum pasya' is good Sanskrit. So something in the objection is wrong, and 3.14.229 finds it.

mula_deva: विना विभक्त्या सम्बन्धो विभक्त्या विद्यते विना ॥ २२९ ॥
mula_iast: vinā vibhaktyā sambandho vibhaktyā vidyate vinā || 229 ||
translation: And from connection with what is employed, the hearing of 'Caitra' and the rest would occur; the relation exists without a case-ending — it exists without a case-ending.
commentary: The reply. Prayuktena ca sambandhac caitradi-sravanam bhavet: from *connection* with what is *employed*, the *hearing* of *'Caitra'* and the rest would *occur* — the possessor's name does turn up alongside the compound, and that is what a bahuvrihi is for (3.14.210-211: the compound reaches him without saying him, so his name stays sayable). Vina vibhaktya sambandho vibhaktya vidyate vina: the *relation* exists *without a case-ending*. So the disanalogy 3.14.227 missed: a relation can subsist unexpressed, and gender cannot. The genitive is silent because the relation survives its silence; the gender-affixes are audible because the gender does not. Inclusion silences only what can survive being silent.
contested: Pada d as transmitted ('vibhaktya vidyate vina') repeats pada c's content with the words reordered, which may be emphatic or may be a transmission fault. We render the sense once and note the duplication rather than reconstruct a different fourth pada.

translation: Even in the expression of number, numberhood does not withdraw; but in the expression of the genitive's meaning, there would be the state of being a nominal-base-meaning.
commentary: Abhidhane 'pi sankhyayah sankhyatvam na nivartate: *even in the expression* of *number*, *numberhood* does *not withdraw* — say a number and it is still a number; expressing it does not consume it. Sasthy-arthasya abhidhane tu syat pratipadikarthata: *but* in the *expression* of the *genitive's meaning*, there would be the state of being a *nominal-base-meaning*. And that is impossible — 3.14.221 asked how a relation could be a pratipadika's meaning, and the question had no answer. So the asymmetry of 3.14.229 is now grounded: number survives expression, relation cannot be expressed at all. The two are different in kind, and the rules treat them differently because they are.

translation: The imagining of the case-meaning is not for the sake of establishing the subsequent employment; another thing has been hinted at — so some declare.
commentary: Anuprayoga-siddhy-artham na vibhakty-artha-kalpana: the *imagining* of the *case-meaning* is *not* for the sake of *establishing* the *subsequent employment* — kalpana: construction (3.14.96's bheda-samsarga-kalpana). One does not posit the case-meaning merely to license the 'tad' that follows (3.14.217). Vastv-antaram upaksiptam iti kecit pracaksate: *another thing* has been *hinted at* — so *some* declare. Upaksipta: thrown out, alluded to, gestured toward. So on this view the posited case-meaning is pointing at something else altogether, and the anuprayoga is not the reason for it. Bhartrhari reports without endorsing (kecit), and 3.14.232 sets out what the something else might be.

translation: The causality belongs to the relations qualified by the relata; or the causality would belong to the possessors of those, qualified by the relations.
commentary: The fork that organizes the rest of the stretch. Sambandhibhir visistanam sambandhanam nimittata: the *causality* belongs to the *relations* *qualified by the relata* — relation principal, terms qualifying. Sambandhair va visistanam tadvatam syan nimittata: *or* the causality would belong to the *possessors of those*, *qualified by the relations* — terms principal, relation qualifying. So: which is the bahuvrihi's occasioning cause, the relation-as-qualified or the possessor-as-qualified? Everything 3.14.219-221 established says the second must win — a relation cannot be principal. But Bhartrhari puts both, because the tradition holds both, and 3.14.233-234 name the holders.

translation: Some say the affixes come, in the possessor of that, from 'danda' for the conjoined and from 'visana' for the inherent; and the bahuvrihi likewise.
commentary: The second horn's partisans. Kecit samyogino dandad visanat samavayinah: *some* say — from *'danda'* (staff) for the *conjoined*, giving 'dandin', the staff-bearer whose staff is merely in contact with him; and from *'visana'* (horn) for the *inherent*, giving 'visanin', the horned one whose horns inhere in him. Two relations, samyoga and samavaya, the Vaisesika pair. Tadvati pratyayan ahuh: they say the *affixes* come *in the possessor of that*. Bahuvrihim tathaiva ca: and the *bahuvrihi* *likewise*. So the possessive affixes attach to the possessor, and the compound works the same way: possessor principal, relation qualifying — 3.14.232's second horn, with two well-known relations to instance it.

translation: Others know the relation, differentiated by the difference of the relata, as the cause; what bears the case-meaning is expressed by the compound.
commentary: The first horn's partisans. Bhinnam sambandhi-bhedena sambandham apare viduh: *others* know the *relation*, *differentiated by the difference of the relata*, as the *cause* — the relation is principal, and it is many because its terms are many: staff-bearing and horn-having are different relations. Nimittam savibhakty-arthah samasena abhidhiyate: what *bears the case-meaning* is *expressed by the compound*. So on this view the compound says the relation-with-its-terms, and that is what conditions the affixes. It is 3.14.232's first horn, and it has 3.14.219-221 against it. Bhartrhari sets both parties out and lets the earlier argument do the deciding — he does not repeat it.

translation: The principal, differentiated by its own subordinates through being for another's purpose, is required last of all — whether as cause or as what is expressed.
commentary: Pradhanam anyarthataya bhinnam svair upasarjanaih: the *principal*, *differentiated* by its *own subordinates* through *being for another's purpose* — the bahuvrihi's members, which serve an outside possessor (3.14.63's apujyamana: unhonoured, none of them principal). Nimittam abhidheyam va sarva-pascad apeksyate: is *required* *last of all* — sarva-pascat: after everything — whether as *cause* or as what is *expressed*. So the possessor comes last: the members are said, the relation is included, and only at the end is the one they were all for required. This is the bahuvrihi's peculiar temporal shape, and it explains 3.14.139's bhavin, bahiranga substratum — the thing that governs the compound's concord arrives after the compound is finished.

translation: And although difference is seen in the owner in the sentence — the bahuvrihi is desired only when his principality is intended.
commentary: Svamini vyatirekas ca vakye yady api drsyate: although *difference* is *seen* in the *owner* in the *sentence* — in 'citra gavo yasya', the owner stands apart, distinct, named by a relative pronoun. Pradhanya eva tasya isto bahuvrihir vivaksite: the *bahuvrihi* is *desired* *only* when *his principality* is *intended*. So the phrase and the compound differ in what they make principal: the phrase foregrounds the cows and appends the owner; the compound is chosen precisely when the owner is to be principal. This is 3.14.49's atyanta-bheda given its motive — one does not compound to abbreviate, one compounds to shift what is principal. Vivaksa decides (3.14.224), and the form follows.

translation: When the possessor operates as the qualifier of the cows — in that meaning, 'these are his', there is no bahuvrihi.
commentary: The test of 3.14.236, negative half. Gavam visesanatvena yada tadvan pravartate: when the *possessor* operates as the *qualifier* of the *cows* — the owner qualifying the herd rather than the reverse. Asya eta iti tatra arthe bahuvrihir na vidyate: in *that* meaning — *'these are his'* — there is *no bahuvrihi*. So where the cows are principal and the owner merely tells which cows, the compound is unavailable: one must use the phrase. The bahuvrihi is not a way of saying 'his cows'; it is a way of saying 'the man with the cows', and the difference is which way the qualification runs. This is as sharp a statement of the compound's semantics as the samuddesa gives.

translation: But when the reflective reference is 'he is the owner of those cows' — then the connection with the cows is conceivable as the cause.
commentary: The positive half. Yada pratyavamarsas tu tasam svami gavam iti: when the *reflective reference* is *'he is the owner of those cows'* — pratyavamarsa: the mind's turning back on what it has laid down (3.14.189's linga-pratyavamarsa, in the same sense). Gobhis tada abhisambandho nimittatvaya kalpate: *then* the *connection with the cows* is *conceivable as the cause*. So when the mind's backward glance lands on the *owner*, the cow-connection becomes the occasioning condition and the bahuvrihi is licensed. The whole determination is thus in the pratyavamarsa — in which way the cognition turns — and not in the world, where a man and his cows stand in one relation regardless (3.14.188's buddhya eva: by the intellect alone). Which is where this samuddesa has said everything is.

translation: Requiring the relation, for the removal of conventionality — from conforming to the cause, the meaning possessing that property follows.
commentary: Apeksamanah sambandham rudhitvasya nivrttaye: *requiring* the *relation*, for the *removal of conventionality* — rudhitva: the state of having hardened into a name (3.14.212's vajrapani and tryaksa, frozen by convention). A compound that keeps requiring its relation cannot congeal into a proper name: the requirement holds it open. Nimitta-anuvidhayitvat tad-dharmarthah prasajyate: from *conforming to the cause*, the meaning *possessing that property* *follows*. So the live relation both prevents rudhi and delivers the properties. This explains why most bahuvrihis are not names: they go on requiring, and requiring keeps them general (3.14.213's samanyasya abhidhayakah).

translation: As 'nana-citrah' conforms to its cause — so the bahuvrihi, though operating in what has become manifold, would be.
commentary: Nana-citra iti yatha nimittam anurudhyate: as *'nana-citrah'* — the manifoldly variegated — *conforms* to its *cause*: the plural is there because the variegation is many, and the form follows the fact. Nana-bhute 'pi vrttah san bahuvrihis tatha bhavet: *so* the *bahuvrihi*, though *operating* in what has *become manifold*, would be. So the compound may reach a plurality and still take its concord from its cause rather than from the plurality — as 'nana-citrah' does. The point is that manifoldness in the object does not force manifoldness in the form: the form answers to what conditions it (3.14.153's the properties belong to the conditions and do not migrate).
contested: The source records a variant in pada b: 'anurudhyate' ('conforms to') against 'vartate' ('operates in'). We translate the former, which the argument requires — the verse is about conformity to the nimitta, as pada b of 3.14.239 has just established. On the variant the sense would be that 'nana-citrah' merely operates in its cause, which is weaker but not incompatible.

mula_deva: सम्बन्धिनि निमित्ते तु द्रव्यधर्मो न हीयते ।
mula_iast: sambandhini nimitte tu dravyadharmo na hīyate |
translation: But when the relatum is the cause, the substance's property is not lost; for the absence of gender operates as the contradictory of gender.
commentary: Sambandhini nimitte tu dravya-dharmo na hiyate: when the *relatum* is the *cause* — 3.14.232's second horn, the one that survives — the *substance's property* is *not lost*. Lingabhavo hi lingasya virodhitvena vartate: for the *absence of gender* operates as the *contradictory* of gender. So the choice is stark: either the cause is a substance and gender comes with it, or the cause is a relation and gender is *contradicted* — not merely absent but excluded. Virodhitvena: as a contrary. This is why 3.14.228's avyaya-consequence was so threatening: relation does not merely fail to supply gender, it repels it. Hence the relatum must be the cause, and 3.14.232's fork is settled.

translation: And the meaning possessing number and gender, of undivided properties, from its cause — being near, from substantiality, is not contradicted by its own properties.
commentary: Samkhyavaml lingas ca artho 'bhinna-dharma nimittatah: the meaning possessing *number* and *gender*, of *undivided properties*, from its *cause* — abhinna-dharma: its properties not split off from it (3.14.181's murti-dharmas not other than the murti). Asanna eva dravyatvat tad-dharmair na virudhyate: *being near*, from *substantiality*, it is *not contradicted* by *its properties*. Asanna: close at hand, adjacent. So because the meaning is substance-like it is *near* its gender and number, and no contradiction arises — where 3.14.241's relation was far and repellent. The bahuvrihi's meaning is thus a near-substance: not a substance, near enough to hold concord. That is as precise as this can be made.

mula_deva: विभक्त्यर्थेन चाविष्टं शुद्धं चेति द्विधा स्थितम् ।
द्रव्यं शुद्धस्य यो धर्मः स न स्यादन्यधर्मणः ॥ २४३ ॥
mula_iast: vibhaktyarthena cāviṣṭaṃ śuddhaṃ ceti dvidhā sthitam |
dravyaṃ śuddhasya yo dharmaḥ sa na syādanyadharmaṇaḥ || 243 ||
translation: Substance stands in two ways: pervaded by the case-meaning, and pure. That property which belongs to the pure would not belong to what has another property.
commentary: A distinction with consequences. Vibhakty-arthena ca avistam suddham ceti dvidha sthitam dravyam: *substance* stands *in two ways* — *pervaded by the case-meaning* (avista: entered into, 3.14.175's word), and *pure*. Suddhasya yo dharmah sa na syad anya-dharmanah: that *property* which belongs to the *pure* would *not* belong to what has *another property*. So the two substances have different properties and one cannot argue from the one to the other: the substance caught up in a relation is not the substance simpliciter, and what holds of the second need not hold of the first. This is the ground of 3.14.242's asanna: the bahuvrihi's meaning is the pervaded substance, near the pure one and not it.

translation: In the indication of mere substance, this difference is not intended; in the earlier part of the work, the difference; but in the second it has been shown.
commentary: A rare internal cross-reference. Dravya-matrasya nirdese bhedo 'yam avivaksitah: in the *indication* of *mere substance*, *this difference* — 3.14.243's pure against pervaded — is *not intended*: where one says simply 'substance', the distinction is not being drawn (3.14.103's avivaksita). Granthe purvatra bhedas tu dvitiye 'nupradarsitah: *in the earlier part of the work*, the *difference*; but in the *second* it has been *shown*. So Bhartrhari is pointing his reader back — the Dravya-samuddesa (3.2) and the Dravyalaksana-samuddesa (3.4) treated substance one way, and the second kanda another. Granthe purvatra: *earlier in the book*. The Vakyapadiya knows itself as a single structure and says so here.

translation: And here the taking-up of the word 'substance' is the qualifier of gender and number; for those two rest on substance, and do not succeed for anything else.
commentary: Dravyasya grahanam ca atra linga-samkhya-visesanam: the *taking-up* of the word *'substance'* — its appearance in the rule — is the *qualifier* of *gender and number*: the word is there to specify which gender and number are meant, namely a substance's. Dravyasritatvam hi tayoh: for those *two* *rest on substance*. Tato 'nyasya na sidhyatah: and for *anything else* they do *not succeed*. So the samuddesa's long gender-and-number enquiry ends where 3.14.163 said it would: dravyam eva asrayah, and every other candidate — quality, universal, relation, accumulation — has been tried and has failed. The word 'dravya' in the sutra is Panini registering the same finding.

translation: The compound operates in the substance-general, delimited by relations differentiated by their relata, conforming to the specified meaning.
commentary: The bahuvrihi's meaning, finally stated in full. Sambandhi-bhinna-sambandha-paricchinne: *delimited* by *relations* *differentiated by the relata* (3.14.234's bhinnam sambandhi-bhedena, now used affirmatively). Pravartate samaso dravya-samanye: the *compound* operates in the *substance-general* — dravya-samanya: not a substance and not a universal, but substance-in-general, the near-substance of 3.14.242. Visistartha-anupatini: *conforming to the specified meaning* — anupatin: running after, following on. So: 'citragu' means substance-in-general, cut down by the cow-relation, running toward whatever specified thing satisfies it. Every word of this has been earned across forty verses, and none of it is spare.

translation: Not transgressing the substance's property, settled in the difference-properties, it attains gender and number which depend on a future substratum.
commentary: Dravya-dharma-anatikrantah: *not transgressing* the substance's property — it stays within what a substance may do, and so may hold concord (3.14.245). Bheda-dharmesv avasthitah: *settled in* the *difference-properties*. Bhavisyad-asraya-apekse linga-sankhye prapadyate: it *attains* *gender and number* which *depend* on a *future substratum*. Bhavisyat: the substratum is *yet to come* — 3.14.139's bhavin and bahiranga, and 3.14.235's sarva-pascat: the possessor arrives last. So the bahuvrihi's concord is owed to something that does not yet exist when the compound is formed, and the compound holds the place open. That is the whole peculiarity of the form, and here it is stated exactly.
contested: Pada b is transmitted with a variant: 'bheda-dharmesv avasthitah' ('settled in the difference-properties') against 'bheda-dharmesv asaktitah' ('from incapacity in the difference-properties'). We translate the former. The variant would reverse the pada's force — the compound failing at the differences rather than resting in them — and would fit 3.14.241's repulsion-argument; but 'avasthitah' better suits pada a's 'anatikrantah', which describes staying within bounds rather than falling short.

translation: Even where the science's operation differs, the worldly meaning is not differentiated; since, in the negative compound, three positions have been considered there.
commentary: The negative compound begins, and it begins with the samuddesa's constant refrain. Sastra-pravrtti-bhede 'pi laukiko 'rtho na bhidyate: *even where* the *science's operation* *differs*, the *worldly meaning* is *not differentiated* — however many ways the grammar derives it, 'abrahmana' means what it means (3.14.74's the science stands very far off; 3.14.97's the upayas unsettled among persons). Nan-samase yatas tatra trayah paksa vicaritah: since, in the *nan-compound*, *three positions* have been *considered* there. So three rival accounts are on record and the word is indifferent to all of them. This is the frame for everything that follows: the analyses will multiply and nothing in the language will move.

mula_deva: अब्राह्मणादिषु नञः प्रयोगो न हि विद्यते ॥ २४९ ॥
mula_iast: abrāhmaṇādiṣu nañaḥ prayogo na hi vidyate || 249 ||
translation: Even where it is another word, the consideration proceeds only by resorting to oneness; for in 'abrahmana' and the rest, the employment of 'nañ' does not exist.
commentary: Sabdantare 'pi ca ekatvam asritya eva vicarana: *even where* it is *another word*, the *consideration* proceeds *only* by *resorting to oneness* — the analysis treats the compound and the phrase as one thing in order to discuss them, though 3.14.49 established they are utterly different words. The unity is the analyst's, assumed to make the discussion possible. Abrahmanadisu nanah prayogo na hi vidyate: for in *'abrahmana'* and the rest, the *employment* of *'nañ'* does *not exist* — the technical particle 'nañ' is a grammarian's object; what one says is 'a-'. So the three positions of 3.14.248 are being debated about a thing that is not in the word: nañ is notation (3.14.78's anubandha-svaradi).

translation: Before the compound, the exclusion of the word-meanings is indicated by 'nañ'; of those excluded by their own nature, unmarked from non-difference of form.
commentary: The first position, and it is the dyotaka one. Prak samasat padarthanam nivrttir dyotyate nana: *before* the compound, the *exclusion* of the *word-meanings* is *indicated* by *'nañ'* — dyotyate: indicated, not expressed (3.14.191's the particle restricts what was latent). Svabhavato nivrttanam rupabhedad alaksita: of those *excluded* *by their own nature*, *unmarked* from *non-difference of form*. So the exclusion was already there — everything excludes what it is not, by its own nature (3.14.142's yad yad asriyate tat tad anyasya vinivartakam) — and it went unmarked because the form did not show it (3.14.140's alaksitah). 'Nañ' does not negate; it marks an exclusion that every word was already performing. This is a beautiful position and it follows from everything the samuddesa has said about generals.

mula_deva: क्रियया यस्य सम्बन्धो वृत्तिस्तस्य न विद्यते ॥ २५१ ॥
mula_iast: kriyayā yasya sambandho vṛttistasya na vidyate || 251 ||
translation: That which has connection with the action residing in 'brahmana' and the rest, expressed by the verb-word in sentences — of that there is no derivative formation.
commentary: Brahmanadi-sthaya vakyesv akhyata-pada-vacyaya kriyaya yasya sambandhah: that which has *connection* with the *action* residing in *'brahmana'* and the rest, *expressed by the verb-word* in *sentences* — the being-a-brahmin that the phrase 'na brahmanah' denies by its verb. Vrttis tasya na vidyate: of *that* there is *no derivative formation*. So the sentence's negation attaches to an action said by a verb, and no compound can do that: compounds have no verbs (3.14.220's vibhakty-artha-pradhanatvat... na kriya). Hence 'abrahmana' is not the compressed 'na brahmano bhavati' — it cannot be, because what the phrase negates is not available to be compounded.

translation: If the action residing in 'pacaka' and the rest is connected with 'nañ' — there, from the non-taking-up of existence, the three-fold position is not established.
commentary: Pacakadi-pada-stha cen nana sambandhyate kriya: *if* the *action* residing in *'pacaka'* (cook) and the rest is *connected with 'nañ'* — 'apacaka', the non-cook: here the base does contain an action, cooking, so perhaps the negation reaches it. Tatra satta-anupadanat tri-paksi na upapadyate: *there*, from the *non-taking-up of existence*, the *three-fold position* is *not established*. So the three accounts of 3.14.248 all assumed that nañ negates an *existence*, and 'apacaka' negates a cooking without touching any existence — the cook still is. So the trio does not apply here, and its generality is broken. Bhartrhari is dismantling the framework of the debate rather than joining it.

translation: If connection with existence alone is imagined everywhere — then in this compound 'asat', let another existence be imagined.
commentary: The reductio, and it is very fine. Sattaya eva abhisambandho yadi sarvatra kalpyate: *if* connection with *existence alone* is *imagined* *everywhere* — kalpyate: imagined, constructed (3.14.231's kalpana). Asann iti samase 'smin satta anya parikalpyatam: then in *this* compound *'asat'* — the non-existent — let *another existence* be *imagined*. Parikalpyatam: let it be imagined — the imperative is sardonic. So if every nañ-compound negates an existence, then 'asat' negates one too, and one must find an existence for it to negate, and then another to be negated in that, and so on. The account destroys itself on its own crucial word. The argument is three lines long and it is complete.

mula_deva: क्त्वान्ते च तुमुनन्ते च नञ्समासे न दृश्यते ।
mula_iast: ktvānte ca tumunante ca nañsamāse na dṛśyate |
translation: In the ktva-ending and in the tumun-ending, in the negative compound, the qualifier-qualified relation of 'nañ' with those expressing existence is not seen.
commentary: Two more counter-cases. Ktvante ca tumun-ante ca nan-samase na drsyate: in the *ktva*-ending (the gerund: 'akrtva', not having done) and the *tumun*-ending (the infinitive), in the *nan-compound*, it is *not seen*. Visesana-visesyatvam nanah satta-abhidhayinam: the *qualifier-qualified* relation of *'nañ'* with *those expressing existence*. So in 'akrtva' the nañ stands in no qualifier-qualified relation to anything existence-expressing — there is no existence in a gerund to qualify. Na drsyate: it is *not seen* (3.14.114's drsyate: the argument is observation). So the framework fails again, on forms everyone uses. Between 3.14.252 and this verse the existence-account has been refuted from four directions, and the samuddesa has not yet said what it thinks nañ does.

translation: 'Nañ' is settled in the general of the substratum which is the action's means; then it is joined with specified substrata — 'brahmana' and the rest.
commentary: The samuddesa's own account of the negative, at last. Kriyayah sadhana-adhara-samanye nan vyavasthitah: *'nañ'* is *settled* in the *general* of the *substratum* which is the *action's means* — not in an existence (3.14.252-254's refuted framework), but in the general of a *seat*. Tato visistair adharair yujyate brahmanadibhih: *then* it is *joined* with *specified substrata* — *'brahmana'* and the rest. So the order is: nañ occupies a general first, and is *afterwards* fitted to a specific base. It does not negate the brahmin; it settles in a general seat and then that seat turns out to be brahmin-shaped. This is why 'abrahmana' means a ksatriya and not simply anything-not-a-brahmin: the general was a seat, and seats are furnished.

translation: As, in the derivative formation, 'nir' and the rest, having taken up the meaning 'gone' and so on, are joined with the substrata which are means — in the negative compound too, that is the sequence.
commentary: The precedent for 3.14.255. Vrttau yatha gatady-artham upadaya niradayah: as in the *derivative formation*, *'nir'* and the rest, having *taken up* the meaning *'gone'* and so on — 3.14.39's preverbs, whose transcending-sense exists only in composition. Yujyante sadhana-adharaih: are *joined* with the *substrata* which are *means*. Nan-samase 'pi sa kramah: in the *nan-compound* too, *that* is the *sequence*. So nañ works exactly as 'nir' does: it acquires a sense in the composing and is then fitted to a base. Neither is a negation or a preposition operating on something already there. Sa kramah — *that* sequence: the ordering is the whole of the explanation.

translation: There, in 'asat', from the derivation of 'nañ', the qualifier-qualified relation with 'brahmana', 'ksatriya' and the rest is imagined — as with 'hunchbacked' and 'lame'.
commentary: Tatra asati nano vrtteh: *there*, in *'asat'*, from the *derivation* of *'nañ'*. Brahmana-ksatriyadibhir visesana-visesyatvam kalpyate: the *qualifier-qualified* relation with *'brahmana'*, *'ksatriya'* and the rest is *imagined* — kalpyate: constructed, not found. Kubja-khanjavat: as with *'kubja'* (hunchbacked) and *'khanja'* (lame) — two attributes of one man, neither qualifying the other, held together only by co-reference (3.14.9-11's krsnas tilah). So the relation between the negation and the base is like that between a hunchback's two defects: they meet in a subject and not in each other. The negative compound has no internal qualification, only a shared seat.
contested: Pada b's syntax is strained: 'asati' and 'vrtteh' do not construe smoothly together. We read 'asati' as locative of 'asat' (picking up 3.14.253's example) with 'vrtteh' ablative. Read instead as a locative absolute — 'the derivation of nañ not existing' — the verse would say that the qualifier-qualified relation is imagined precisely where nañ has no derivation, which also suits 'kalpyate'. The comparison to kubja-khanja stands on either construal.

mula_deva: गुणत्वमितरेषां च तेषां वा स्यात् प्रधानत् ॥ २५८ ॥
mula_iast: guṇatvamitareṣāṃ ca teṣāṃ vā syāt pradhānat || 258 ||
translation: And where there is thus free play, 'asat' would be principal and the others subordinate; or they would be principal.
commentary: The cost of 3.14.257's imagined relation. Kama-care ca saty evam: where there is thus *free play* — kama-cara: doing as one likes, the state of a question nothing settles (3.14.168's icchaya: at one's pleasure). Asatah syat pradhanata gunatvam itaresam ca: *'asat'* would be *principal* and the *others* *subordinate*. Tesam va syat pradhanata: *or* *they* would be principal. So if the relation is merely imagined, nothing decides which side is principal, and both assignments are available. This is exactly what one should expect of a construct (3.14.96's upaya-matram nanatvam), and it is a defect only if one thought the construct was a description.

translation: 'Brahmana', 'ksatriya' and the rest — resorted to as principal at first, operating in the general because of what is heard — are commenced upon precisely in the particular.
commentary: Pradhanyena asritah purvam: resorted to as *principal* *at first* — the base-word comes first and comes as principal. Sruteh samanya-vrttayah: *operating in the general* because of *what is heard* — the audible word 'brahmana' means the general, brahmin-hood at large. Visesa eva prakranta brahmana-ksatriyadayah: they are *commenced upon* *precisely in the particular* — prakranta: begun, set out from. So the movement is general-to-particular within a single word: 'brahmana' sounds the general and the discourse begins from a particular. This is 3.14.37's visesa-vrttitva (in composition the member means the particular) shown as a movement rather than a state, and it is what 3.14.255's 'then' was describing.

translation: As their delimitation is effected by 'gaura' and the rest — so, by 'asat' too, an unmanifested identity is manifested.
commentary: The positive characterization of what a negative does. Yatha gauradibhis tesam avacchedo vidhiyate: as their *delimitation* is effected by *'gaura'* (fair) and the rest — an ordinary adjective cuts a class down. Asata api anabhivyaktam tadatmyam vyajyate tatha: *so*, by *'asat'* too, an *unmanifested identity* is *manifested*. Tadatmya: identity, being-that-self; anabhivyakta: unmanifested; vyajyate: is made manifest. So the negative does what the adjective does — it delimits — and what it delimits into view is an *identity* that was there unmanifested. 'Abrahmana' does not remove a brahmin; it manifests a ksatriya-identity that was in the field unshown. This is 3.14.250's position perfected: exclusion is how things get shown, and nañ shows by excluding.

translation: As the existent meaning is imagined for the expression of existence — so the indefinable is constructed for the expression of non-existence.
commentary: The parity argument. Yatha satta-abhidhanaya sann arthah parikalpyate: as the *existent meaning* is *imagined* for the *expression of existence* — even to say 'it is', one constructs an existent for the saying to be about. Tatha satta-abhidhanaya nirupakhyo vikalpyate: so the *indefinable* — nirupakhya: that of which no account can be given, the technical term for the utterly unreal — is *constructed* for the expression of non-existence. So both existence and non-existence require a posit, and the posit for non-existence is a thing defined as having no definition. Neither side is innocent. This is 3.14.253's reductio turned into a general observation: reference to being and to non-being are equally constructions (3.14.197's the word ordains the differences).
contested: Pada c reads 'satta-abhidhanaya' in our source, duplicating pada a. The parallel ('yatha... tatha') plainly requires 'asatta-abhidhanaya' — for the expression of *non*-existence — which is what we translate; otherwise the verse says the same thing twice and the nirupakhya has no work. We note the transmitted reading rather than pass over the emendation.

translation: The intellect, having made a footing in 'ksatriya' and the rest, resting on another existence, then draws away the applicable existence differentiated by the universal.
commentary: The negative described as a mental movement — and this is the samuddesa's real answer. Ksatriyadau padam krtva buddhih: the *intellect*, having *made a footing* — padam krtva: having taken a step, planted itself — in *'ksatriya'* and the rest. Satta-antarasraya: *resting on another existence*. Jatya bhinnam tatah sattam prasaktam apakarsati: *then* *draws away* the *applicable* *existence* *differentiated by the universal*. Apakarsati: pulls off, drags away (3.14.11's apakarsati, of the two generals drawing away each other's doubt). So: the mind plants itself on a ksatriya, stands on that existence, and from there *pulls away* the brahmin-existence that was pressing. The negation is a shift of footing followed by a withdrawal — done by the buddhi, in two moves, and there is no negative fact anywhere in it.

translation: When the negation of being is intended as 'non-being' — without resorting to definability, negation is not conceivable.
commentary: Abhava iti bhavasya pratisedhe vivaksite: when the *negation of being* is *intended* as *'non-being'* — the hardest case, where what is denied is being itself. Sopakhyatvam anasritya pratisedho na kalpate: *without resorting to definability* — sopakhya: that of which an account *can* be given, the opposite of 3.14.261's nirupakhya — *negation* is *not conceivable*. So even to deny being, one must have something definable to plant one's foot on (3.14.262's padam krtva). A negation of the utterly indefinable is not a negation, because there is nowhere to stand. This is why 3.14.261's posit was unavoidable: the mind's withdrawal requires a footing, and a footing must be describable.

translation: By words expressing many properties, expressing an aggregate — they operate in the parts, similar in form by their own nature.
commentary: Aneka-dharma-vacanaih sabdaih sangha-abhidhayibhih: by words *expressing many properties*, *expressing an aggregate* — words like 'brahmana', which name a bundle: birth, study, conduct, and the rest. Ekadesesu vartante tulya-rupah svabhavatah: they *operate in the parts*, *similar in form* by their *own nature*. So an aggregate-word applies to its parts as readily as to the whole, and the form does not distinguish the two applications: they are tulya-rupa, alike in shape, by nature. This is the premise of the paradox that 3.14.265 is about to spring, and it is not a trick — it is a fact about every word that names a bundle.

translation: As, from the doing of one part, an aggregate is spoken of as 'done' and also as 'not done' — that same sequence is in 'abrahmana'.
commentary: The paradox, and it is superb. Yatha ekadesa-karanat krta ity abhidhiyate akrtas ceti sanghatah: as, from the *doing of one part*, an *aggregate* is spoken of as *'done'* *and* as *'not done'* — a half-built house is built and unbuilt, and both are true, because 3.14.264's words apply to parts and wholes alike. Sa eva abrahmane kramah: *that same* sequence is in *'abrahmana'*. So a man with some of the brahmin-properties and not others is a brahmana and an abrahmana at once. This is not a puzzle to be dissolved; it is what aggregate-words do, and 3.14.266 draws the consequence without flinching.

translation: From that presentation, a brahmana would be a non-brahmana; or, in the undone, from the attachment of the done, it is not different from the done-and-undone.
commentary: The consequence. Brahmano 'brahmanas tasmad upanyasat prasajyate: from *that presentation*, a *brahmana* would be a *non-brahmana* — the contradiction, stated as such. Akrte va krtasangad avisistam krtakrtat: *or*, in the *undone*, from the *attachment* of the *done*, it is *not different* from the *done-and-undone*. So either the brahmin is a non-brahmin, or the undone thing is indistinguishable from the half-done. Both are unacceptable, and both follow from 3.14.264's premise, which is true. So something must be added — and 3.14.267 adds it: the grammar's own decision.

translation: There, when a non-primary is possible, 'nañ' — employed at the time of the science's exposition — is a differentiator, for the exclusion of the primary.
commentary: The resolution, and it is frankly conventionalist. Amukhya-sambhave tatra mukhyasya vinivrttaye: *there*, when a *non-primary* is *possible*, for the *exclusion of the primary* — where a word might be taken in its full or its partial sense, the full sense must be got out of the way. Sastra-anvakhyana-samaye nan prayukto visesakah: *'nañ'*, *employed* at the *time of the science's exposition*, is a *differentiator*. Anvakhyana-samaye: at the moment of the grammarian's *exposition* (3.14.71's anvakhyanaya bhedah). So nañ is the grammar's device for forcing the non-primary reading, deployed when the science is expounding. It resolves 3.14.266's contradiction by decision, not by discovery — which is what a science that stands atidure (3.14.74) has to do.

translation: And a qualifier is seen not to damage the word-meaning. Now, some property of the meaning possessing a universal has been excluded.
commentary: Two steps toward a new difficulty. Padartha-anupaghatena drsyate ca visesanam: a *qualifier* is *seen* *without damaging* the *word-meaning* — anupaghata: no injury: adding 'blue' to 'lotus' does not destroy lotus-hood, and qualifiers generally leave their bases intact (3.14.260's delimitation, which cuts down without cutting up). Atha jatimato 'rthasya kascid dharmo nivartitah: *now*, *some property* of the meaning *possessing a universal* has been *excluded*. So nañ, if it is a qualifier (3.14.257), must likewise leave the base intact while excluding one of its properties. And that raises the question of 3.14.269: which property, and does it matter?

mula_deva: अवश्यं ब्राह्मणे कश्चित् क्वचिद् धर्मो न विद्यते ।
mula_iast: avaśyaṃ brāhmaṇe kaścit kvacid dharmo na vidyate |
translation: Necessarily, some property somewhere does not exist in a brahmana; there, from the non-statement of the particular, the hearing of 'nañ' is purposeless.
commentary: The difficulty, and it is sharp. Avasyam brahmane kascit kvacid dharmo na vidyate: *necessarily* — avasyam — *some* property *somewhere* does *not exist* in a *brahmana*: no one has every property; every brahmin lacks something. Visesa-avacanat tatra nanah srutir anarthika: *there*, from the *non-statement of the particular*, the *hearing of 'nañ'* is *purposeless* — anarthika (3.14.126, 3.14.205's word). So if nañ merely excludes some property or other, it says nothing: every brahmin already fails some property, and 'abrahmana' would be true of all of them. Unless nañ says *which* property — and it does not. The account of 3.14.268 collapses into vacuity.

translation: The nañ-qualified would become a synonym of the unqualified; for correctness in such a case is cognized from the exposition.
commentary: The consequence of 3.14.269. Avisistasya paryayo nan-visistah prasajyate: the *nañ-qualified* would become a *synonym* of the *unqualified* — 'abrahmana' and 'brahmana' would mean the same, since every brahmin lacks something. Paryaya: a synonym — the reductio's final form, and it is worse than 3.14.266's contradiction. Anvakhyanad dhi sadhutvam evambhute pratiyate: for *correctness* in *such a case* is cognized from the *exposition* — anvakhyana: the grammarian's exposition (3.14.267's samaya). So what saves the word from synonymy is not anything in it: it is the science's exposition, which decides how it is to be taken. The negative compound is legible because the grammarians settled how to read it — and Bhartrhari, having demonstrated that nothing else could settle it, says so.

translation: Although here the qualifier does not damage the word-meaning — that state of the meaning which exists by figurative transfer is indicated by 'nañ'.
commentary: The answer to 3.14.268-270. Padartha-anupaghatena yady apy atra visesanam: *although* here the *qualifier* does *not damage* the *word-meaning* (3.14.268's principle, conceded). Upacara-sato 'rthasya sa avastha dyotyate nana: that *state* of the meaning which *exists by figurative transfer* is *indicated* by *'nañ'*. Upacara-sat: existing by upacara — by transferred usage, the mode of being that belongs to what is only figuratively so (3.3.82's beings raised up by construction). Dyotyate: *indicated*, not expressed (3.14.250's dyotyate nana). So nañ points at the base's *figurative* condition — 'abrahmana' shows brahmin-hood being held in a transferred way — and 3.14.269's vacuity is avoided: nañ does not exclude some property or other, it marks a mode of holding.

translation: As the word-meaning stands settled in the qualified things — in such a one, the being-such is understood through the difference-causes.
commentary: Visesyesu yathabhutah padarthah samavasthitah: as the *word-meaning* stands *settled* in the *qualified things* — however it happens to sit in its instances. Tathabhute tathabhavo gamyate bheda-hetubhih: in *such a one*, the *being-such* is *understood* through the *difference-causes*. So the mode in which the meaning sits — fully, partially, figuratively — is read off the differentiating conditions, and nothing else reveals it. This grounds 3.14.271: nañ can indicate a figurative holding because the mode of holding is what the bheda-hetus disclose anyway. The negative is one such cause among others, doing what they all do.

translation: When that word-meaning is excluded, how does the member operate? For a word's operation without a cause is not possible.
commentary: The objection that will not go away. Nivrtte 'vayavas tasmin padarthe vartate katham: when that *word-meaning* is *excluded*, *how* does the *member* operate? If 'abrahmana' has excluded brahmin-hood, what is the 'brahmana' in it doing? Na animitta hi sabdasya pravrttir upapadyate: for a *word's operation* *without a cause* is *not possible*. Animitta: causeless. So the base cannot just sit there: a word operates only when something occasions it, and its occasion has just been denied. This is the sharpest form of the negative compound's puzzle, and 3.14.274-275 test the obvious answers.

translation: If, like the word 'arat', a single word by its own nature operates in a contradictory meaning — then the hearing of 'nañ' is purposeless.
commentary: The first answer, and its cost. Arac-chabdavad ekasya viruddhe 'rthe svabhavatah sabdasya vrttir yady asti: *if*, like the word *'arat'* — which means both *near* and *far*, Sanskrit's most famous auto-antonym — a *single* word *by its own nature* operates in a *contradictory* meaning. Nanah srutir anarthika: then the *hearing of 'nañ'* is *purposeless*. So one might say that 'brahmana' natively means both brahmin and non-brahmin, as 'arat' means both near and far — and then the base needs no cause from outside (3.14.273 answered). But then nañ is doing nothing, since the base already had the sense. The answer buys the base's operation at the price of the negative.

translation: Now, the nature deserves to be expounded from the statement; that is to be expressed, from its not being well-known; the meaning is excluded by 'nañ'.
commentary: The second answer. Atha svabhavo vacanad anvakhyeyatvam arhati: *now*, the *nature* *deserves to be expounded* from the *statement* — anvakhyeya: to be expounded (3.14.74's anvakhyeyas tatha). The word's dual nature is not self-evident and must be taught. Tad vacyam aprasiddhatvat: *that* is *to be expressed*, from its *not being well-known* — aprasiddha: not established in usage, unlike 'arat', whose double sense everyone knows. Nana artho vinivartyate: the *meaning* is *excluded* *by 'nañ'*. So: 'brahmana' does not natively mean non-brahmin (which is why 3.14.274's parallel fails), and nañ is what excludes — it has work precisely because the base's contrary sense is not established. The negative earns its keep from the base's not being 'arat'.

mula_deva: प्रस्थानं गम्यते शुद्धे तदर्थेऽपि न तिष्ठतौ ॥ २७६ ॥
mula_iast: prasthānaṃ gamyate śuddhe tadarthe'pi na tiṣṭhatau || 276 ||
translation: Although there is operation in both, the principal is cognized; setting-out is understood in the pure root, and even in that meaning, not in 'tisthati'.
commentary: A parallel from the preverbs. Yady apy ubhaya-vrttitvam pradhanam tu pratiyate: *although* there is *operation in both*, the *principal* is *cognized* — where a form could go two ways, one of them is what is understood. Prasthanam gamyate suddhe tad-arthe 'pi na tisthatau: *setting-out* is understood in the *pure* root, and *even in that meaning*, *not in 'tisthati'*. So 'pra-stha' means setting out, and the sense belongs to the compounded root — while 'tisthati', the same root uncompounded, means standing still and never means departure. The preverb's sense is not in the root and does not stay in it (3.14.39's niradinam krantady-arthesv asambhavah; 3.14.256's sa kramah). Nañ is the same: what it contributes is not recoverable from the base, and does not survive decomposition.

translation: For what purpose, when the primary meaning is possible in what is not such, is the operation of the word 'brahmana' accepted in difference?
commentary: Kim artham atathabhute sati mukhyartha-sambhave: *for what purpose*, when the *primary meaning* is *possible* in what is *not such* — the ksatriya is not a brahmin, and yet the word's primary sense somehow reaches him. Bhede brahmana-sabdasya vrttir abhyupagamyate: is the *operation* of the word *'brahmana'* *accepted* *in difference*? So the question is put bluntly: why let the word run into the very thing it is not? Kim artham: to what end? The samuddesa has been circling this and here demands the point of the whole construction. 3.14.278 answers: because that is how one is told anything.

mula_deva: अयं पदार्थ एतस्मिन् क्षत्रियादौ न विद्यते ।
mula_iast: ayaṃ padārtha etasmin kṣatriyādau na vidyate |
translation: 'This word-meaning does not exist in this ksatriya and the like' — thus the word expressing that is employed, for the sake of cognition.
commentary: The answer, and it is the practical one. Ayam padartha etasmin ksatriyadau na vidyate: *'this word-meaning does not exist in this ksatriya'* and the like — the content of what one wants conveyed. Iti tad-vacanah sabdah pratyayaya prayujyate: *thus* the word *expressing that* is *employed*, *for the sake of cognition* — pratyayaya: so that one may know. So 'brahmana' runs into the ksatriya (3.14.277's question) because that is the only way to say that brahmin-hood is *not* there: one must bring the brahmin-hood into view to remove it (3.14.262's padam krtva, planting the foot in order to pull away; 3.14.263's sopakhyatvam, needing something definable to deny). The word goes where it does not belong because negation has no other route.

translation: When the meaning, from the word, has attained the state of being the intellect's object — either operation or exclusion is cognized; for the meaning is attached by the hearing.
commentary: The general principle behind 3.14.278. Buddher visayatam prapte sabdad arthe pratiyate: when the *meaning*, *from the word*, has attained the state of being the *intellect's object* — the word first makes the meaning an object of thought; that is what a word does (3.14.197's sabdad arthah pratayante). Pravrttir va nivrttir va: *either* *operation* *or* *exclusion* is cognized — only then, and equally either way. Srutya hy artho 'nusajyate: for the *meaning* is *attached* by the *hearing* — anusajyate: is annexed, comes along with. So affirmation and denial are alike downstream of the word's presenting: one cannot deny what one has not first been given, and the giving is the hearing. This is the whole doctrine of negation in one line, and it is the doctrine of 1.123 (no cognition without word) applied to absence.

translation: Or from incorrect teaching, or from a cause of doubt — a word does operate; but there is no reversal in the case of 'clod' and the rest.
commentary: The limit of 3.14.279's principle. Asamyag-upadesad va nimittat samsayasya va sabda-pravrttih: from *incorrect teaching*, or from a *cause of doubt*, a *word* does *operate* — words are applied wrongly, out of bad instruction or genuine uncertainty, and the misapplication is real. Na tv asti lostadisu viparyayam: *but* there is *no reversal* in *'losta'* (a clod of earth) and the rest. So the errors have a range: one may call a ksatriya a brahmin by ignorance or doubt, and nobody calls a clod one. The word's straying is bounded by something — the resemblance that made the doubt possible. This is 3.14.214's rupa-bheda: where the form differs enough, the word turns it away, and no mistake can reach it.

translation: From the many, 'as' — it succeeds where there is principality; the dependence of principals is thus proper in the 'atal'-rule.
commentary: A rule-technical case, and its reading is not easy. Anekasmad asa iti pradhanye sati sidhyati: from the *many*, *'as'* — it *succeeds* where there is *principality*: the form comes out when the relevant element is principal, and not otherwise. Sapeksatvam pradhananam evam yuktam tv atal-vidhau: the *dependence* of *principals* is *thus proper* in the *'atal'*-rule. Sapeksatva: mutual dependence — and the point is that even principals may depend, which the atal-rule requires. So the samuddesa notes that principality does not entail independence: a principal can still be sapeksa (3.14.47's relatum-words, whose expectancy is their meaning). The nañ-compound will need this, since its base is principal and dependent at once.
contested: Padas a-b are highly compressed and we render them with reserve. 'Anekasmad asa iti' appears to cite a rule-fragment; without the rule's identification the construction of 'asa' (whether the root 'as', a form of it, or a pratyaya) cannot be fixed. The verse's usable content — that principals may nonetheless be sapeksa, and that the atal-rule requires this — rests on padas c-d, which are clear.

translation: And from one's being principal, in the presence of its qualifier — the recurrence even of the principal's property; therefore there is no other statement.
commentary: Ekasya ca pradhanatvat tad-visesana-sannidhau: from *one's* being *principal*, in the *presence* of *its qualifier* — sannidhi: proximity, the qualifier standing by. Pradhana-dharmapy avrttih: the *recurrence* *even* of the *principal's property* — avrtti: coming round again, repetition. Ato na vacanantaram: *therefore* there is *no other statement* — no further rule is needed. So when a principal has its qualifier alongside, the principal's own property recurs, and the grammar need not say so separately. This is rule-economy of the kind 3.14.106 found (a rule made idle by a doctrine), and it prepares the number-analysis of 3.14.283-286: the principal's property recurring is exactly what will happen to number in 'advi'.

translation: Here the principal, from being what is differentiated, is the one-meaning modified by 'nañ'; and 'dvi' and the rest too, having gone to oneness, operate having abandoned their own properties.
commentary: Pradhanam atra bhedyatvad ekartho vikrto nana: *here* the *principal*, from being *what is differentiated* — bhedya: the thing to be cut into, not the cutter — is the *one-meaning* *modified* by *'nañ'*. Ekartha: 3.14.44's ekarthibhava, becoming-one-meaning; vikrta: altered. So the negative compound is one meaning that nañ has modified, not two meanings in relation. Hitva sva-dharman vartante dvyadayo 'py ekatam gatah: and *'dvi'* and the rest too, having *gone to oneness*, operate having *abandoned their own properties*. So the numerals in a nañ-compound drop their numbers — 'advi' has no two in it — because they have gone into the one-meaning (3.14.101's bhedanam parityagat; 3.14.115's the numeral's outer difference withdrawing).

translation: As 'ksatriya' and the rest, joined with 'nañ', have attained brahmana-hood — so in 'dvitva' and the rest, oneness is figuratively applied, from the connection with 'nañ'.
commentary: The parallel that organizes the numeral-case. Brahmanatvam yatha apanna nan-yuktah ksatriyadayah: as *'ksatriya'* and the rest, *joined with 'nañ'*, have *attained brahmana-hood* — the striking formulation: in 'abrahmana' the ksatriya has *acquired* brahmin-hood, figuratively, in order to have it denied (3.14.271's upacara-sat; 3.14.278's the word must reach him to remove it). Dvitvadisu tatha ekatvam nan-yogad upacaryate: *so* in *'dvitva'* and the rest, *oneness* is *figuratively applied* from the *connection with 'nañ'* — upacaryate: is transferred. So the negated numeral acquires a figurative oneness exactly as the negated ksatriya acquires a figurative brahmin-hood. Nañ confers before it removes, both times.

translation: Having attached the connection with oneness, that property is negated; from 'dvi' and the rest, in those the word 'tad' operates, as with 'brahmana' and the rest.
commentary: Ekatva-yogam asajya sa dharmah pratisidhyate: having *attached* the *connection with oneness*, *that property* is *negated* — asajya: having fastened it on. So the sequence is explicit: attach, then deny. The oneness is put there in order to be taken away, which is what 3.14.284's upacara amounted to. Dvyadibhyas tesu tac-chabdo vartate brahmanadivat: from *'dvi'* and the rest, in *those* the word *'tad'* operates, as with *'brahmana'* and the rest — the pronoun follows, as it did at 3.14.217, because the substance is not expressed. So the numeral-negation behaves in every respect like the caste-negation, down to the pronoun. The parallel is complete.

mula_deva: वृत्तौ तस्य प्रधानत्वात् सा सङ्ख्या न निवर्तते ॥ २८६ ॥
mula_iast: vṛttau tasya pradhānatvāt sā saṅkhyā na nivartate || 286 ||
translation: As, in a sentence, that which is pervaded by number is employed in 'dvi' and the rest — in the derivative formation, from its being principal, that number does not withdraw.
commentary: The stretch's conclusion, and it inverts 3.14.283. Avista-sankhyo vakye 'sau yatha dvyadau prayujyate: as, *in a sentence*, that *pervaded by number* — avista: entered into (3.14.243's word) — is employed in *'dvi'* and the rest. Vrttau tasya pradhanatvat sa sankhya na nivartate: in the *derivative formation*, from *its being principal*, that *number* does *not withdraw*. So where the numeral is *principal*, its number survives the composition — against 3.14.283, where the numerals abandoned their properties on going into the one-meaning. The two verses are not in conflict: they describe the numeral subordinate and the numeral principal, and 3.14.282 has already established that the principal's property recurs. What a compound does to its members depends on which of them is principal — which is where this samuddesa began (3.14.95's upasarjana).

translation: The thing to be negated is annexed exactly as it is; for in connection with another number, that meaning is not negated.
commentary: Pratisedhyo yathabhutas tathabhuto 'nusajyate: the *thing to be negated* is *annexed* *exactly as it is* — yathabhutas tathabhutah: however it is, so it comes along (3.14.279's srutya hy artho 'nusajyate, the same verb). One cannot deny a thing under a description it does not have. Vacanantara-yoge hi na so 'rthah pratisidhyate: for in connection with *another number*, that *meaning* is *not negated* — change the number and the denial misses. So negation is exact: it strikes the thing as presented and nothing else, which is why 3.14.286's number mattered. Deny 'two' and you have not denied 'twos'.

translation: As by 'non-white' the meaning 'black' and the like is cognized — so in 'not-one' too, another number is expressed.
commentary: The paryudasa principle, in two examples. Asukla iti krsnadir yatha arthah sampratiyate: as by *'asukla'* (non-white) the meaning *'black'* and the like is *cognized* — one does not hear 'not white' and think of virtue or Tuesday: one thinks of another colour. The negation lands inside the genus. Sankhyantaram tatha aneka ity atrapi abhidhiyate: *so* in *'aneka'* (not-one) too, *another number* is *expressed*. So 'aneka' means some other number, not the absence of number. This is paryudasa — the negation that selects a sibling rather than annihilating — and Bhartrhari establishes it by pointing at what one actually understands (3.14.260's asata api tadatmyam vyajyate: the negative manifests an identity).

translation: And from the action's applicability, all the objects being accepted — even when one is negated, another, being applicable, is cognized.
commentary: Why paryudasa works. Kriya-prasangat sarvesu karmasv angikrtesu ca: from the *action's applicability*, *all the objects* being *accepted* — the verb has already brought the whole field of its possible objects into view (3.14.291 will spell this out). Ekasmin pratisiddhe 'pi praptam anyat pratiyate: *even when one* is *negated*, *another*, being *applicable*, is *cognized*. So the reason 'asukla' delivers black is that the colour-field was already open when white was denied: deny one member of an open field and the field's remainder presents itself. Paryudasa is not a special kind of negation; it is ordinary negation performed inside a field that the verb had already spread out.

translation: The action and what is heard are within what has been commenced, by prasajya-negation; but in paryudasa, a determinate other countable is stated.
commentary: The two negations distinguished, and this is the tradition's most consequential distinction. Kriya srutis ca prakrante prasajya-pratisedhena: the *action* and *what is heard* are within what has been *commenced*, by *prasajya*-negation — prasajya-pratisedha: the negation that denies the predication itself ('he does not cook'), and it stays inside what was begun: the verb is what is denied. Paryudase tu niyatam sankhyeyantaram ucyate: *but* in *paryudasa*, a *determinate other* countable is *stated* — niyatam: fixed. So prasajya denies the act; paryudasa asserts a sibling. The first says no, the second says another. And Bhartrhari's contribution here is 3.14.289's ground: paryudasa can name a sibling only because the action had already laid out the family.

translation: The root-meaning, having objects for its domain, designated by its own means — by implication, having first implied all objects, stands.
commentary: The ground of 3.14.289, stated in full. Dhatv-arthah karma-visayo vyapadistah sva-sadhanaih: the *root-meaning*, having *objects for its domain*, *designated by its own means* — the verb comes with its karakas (3.7.1's the action requires its sadhanas). Arthat sarvani karmani prag aksipya avatisthate: *by implication*, having *first implied* *all objects*, it *stands*. Aksipya: having drawn in, implicated; prak: beforehand. So the verb, before any object is specified, has already implicated *all* its possible objects and stands over them. That standing field is what a paryudasa-negation carves. This is a fine piece of semantics: the verb's transitivity is a prior claim on a whole domain, and particular objects are selections from it.

mula_deva: साध्यत्वात् तत्र सिद्धेन क्रिया द्रव्येण लक्ष्यते ।
mula_iast: sādhyatvāt tatra siddhena kriyā dravyeṇa lakṣyate |
translation: (292) Where 'aneka' is employed in a verb whose means and substratum are known, and afterwards 'tisthati' is annexed — (293) there, since the action is to be accomplished, it is indicated by an accomplished substance; the substance was accepted already beforehand, and therefore differs from the earlier one.
commentary: (292): nirjnata-sadhana-adhare yatra akhyate prayujyate aneka iti — where *'aneka'* is employed in a *verb* whose *means and substratum* are *known*. Pascac ca tisthatity anusajyate: and *afterwards* *'tisthati'* is *annexed* — 'aneke tisthanti', not-one stand. (293): sadhyatvat tatra siddhena kriya dravyena laksyate — since the *action* is *to be accomplished* (sadhya: not yet done), it is *indicated* by an *accomplished* substance (siddha: already there). The standing contrast of this work (3.8's sadhya-kriya against the siddha karakas; 3.1's the accomplished indicating the accomplishable). Prag eva angikrtam dravyam atah purvena bhidyate: the *substance* was *accepted already beforehand*, and *therefore differs from the earlier* one. So the substance that indicates the action is not the one the verb had implicated at 3.14.291: it was accepted earlier and is a different thing. The negation's 'other' is thus genuinely other.
