Tesam pratyaya-rupena sarvesam sabda-vacyata: of *all* of them there is *expressibility by the word*, *in the form of the cognition* — pratyaya-rupena: under the cognition's own shape. So everything mingled in the cognition is expressed — but only as the cognition has it, not as it is in itself. Sabda-vacyata is granted to all and qualified by pratyaya-rupena: words say everything, and say it all in the shape thought gave it. This is 3.14.470's tattva-avivaksa stated positively.

translation: But of bare states, the form is not determined; and in them, whose form is undetermined, the word does not operate.
commentary: The conclusion, and it is one of the work's central sentences. Kevalanam tu bhavanam na rupam avadharyate: of *bare* states, the *form* is *not determined* — kevala: alone, uncombined; avadharyate: is ascertained, fixed. A thing by itself has no determinate form, because determination is what its accompaniers do (3.14.350's the principal has no own-form, from non-determination). Anirupita-rupesu tesu sabdo na vartate: in *them*, whose *form* is *undetermined*, the *word* does *not operate*. So words cannot reach anything bare — not because words are weak but because bare things have no shape to be reached. Every word-meaning is a meaning-in-company (3.14.472), and there is no such thing as the thing itself for language to fail at.

translation: And from the employment of the earlier word, it does not withdraw from the aggregate; or else it operates in the member; it never abandons what has been taken up.
commentary: Purva-sabda-prayogac ca samuhan na nivartate: from the *employment of the earlier word*, it does *not withdraw* from the *aggregate*. Vartate 'vayave vapi: *or else* it operates in the *member*. Na upattam tyajati kvacit: it *never* *abandons* what has been *taken up* — kvacit: ever. So a word may sit on the whole or on the part, and whichever it took, it keeps: nothing it has once grasped is let go. This is the principle behind 3.14.264-266's paradox (the half-done thing is done and not done): an aggregate-word retains both applications, and the retention is what generates the contradiction. Here it is stated as a law: words do not release.

translation: And if what expresses the aggregate were to qualify the difference — there would be non-equal case-endings, as in 'the fore-body' and the rest.
commentary: Samudayabhidhayi ca yadi bhedam visesayet: *if* what *expresses the aggregate* were to *qualify the difference* — the whole-word made into a qualifier of a part. Tatra atulya-vibhaktitvam purva-kayadivad bhavet: there would be *non-equal case-endings*, as in *'purva-kaya'* (the fore-part of the body) and the rest — the class of compounds where the members take different cases in the analysis ('kayasya purvam'). So the qualification would force a case-mismatch and turn every aggregate-word into a purva-kaya. The consequence is formal and it blocks the reading. This is the samuddesa's habitual test: propose a semantics, run it through the case-system, see what breaks.

mula_deva: समूहे च प्रदेशे च पञ्चावा इति दृश्यते ।
mula_iast: samūhe ca pradeśe ca pañcāvā iti dṛśyate |
translation: In the aggregate and in the specific instance alike, 'five sheep' is seen; thus the qualifier 'all' is proper.
commentary: Samuhe ca pradese ca pancava iti drsyate: in the *aggregate* and in the *specific instance* alike, *'five sheep'* is *seen* — the phrase serves for the flock and for the individual beasts, indifferently: 3.14.475's the word does not abandon either. Tatha visesanam sarva ity etad upapadyate: *thus* the *qualifier* *'all'* is *proper* — 'all five sheep' construes, and it construes because the phrase already holds both applications. So 'sarva' can qualify because there is a plurality in there to be totalized, and there is a plurality because the aggregate-word kept its distributive grip. The little word 'sarva' is evidence for the law of 3.14.475.

mula_deva: अर्धं च पिप्पली चेति खण्डे शब्दः प्रयुज्यते ॥ ४७८ ॥
mula_iast: ardhaṃ ca pippalī ceti khaṇḍe śabdaḥ prayujyate || 478 ||
translation: So in 'half a pepper': for the exclusion of another class, 'a half, and a pepper', the word is employed for the piece.
commentary: Tatha ardha-pippali ity atra: so in *'ardha-pippali'* — half a long-pepper. Jaty-antara-nivrttaye: for the *exclusion of another class* — the half must still be pepper and not something else: cut a pepper in half and each half is pepper (against 3.14.298's dewlapless cow, which was a different animal). Ardham ca pippali ca iti khande sabdah prayujyate: *'a half, and a pepper'* — the *word* is *employed for the piece*. So 'pippali' applies to the fragment, and the compound's analysis makes both members co-refer to it. The class survives the cutting, and it survives because 3.14.475's word does not release its grip on the member when the whole is broken.

mula_deva: मात्राणां हि तिरोभावे परिमाणं न विद्यते ।
कुमार्य इति तेन स्यात् कुमार्यां भेदसम्भवात् ॥ १७९ ॥
mula_iast: mātrāṇāṃ hi tirobhāve parimāṇaṃ na vidyate |
kumārya iti tena syāt kumāryāṃ bhedasambhavāt || 179 ||
translation: For in the disappearance of the measures, the measure does not exist; therefore 'kumaryah' would come about, from the possibility of difference in 'kumari'.
commentary: This verse is identical to 3.14.179, and our source numbers it '179' here — the second such doubling in this samuddesa (see 3.14.421, which repeats 3.14.121). Its argument belongs to the number-analysis of 3.14.175-179, where it drew the consequence that on the universal-account the plural must come from the individual girls. It has no work to do among the aggregate-and-part verses that surround it here. We print it where the source prints it, and record the repetition.
contested: The doubling at 3.14.421/121 and here at 3.14.479/179 stands at an interval of 300 verses in both cases, which suggests a mechanical fault in the transmission — a repeated exemplar-page or a systematic mis-numbering — rather than two independent scribal slips. A reader with the critical editions to hand should establish whether either verse is attested at its second position.

translation: But not being established specifically, they stand settled in the aggregate; their being seen in the specific instance is by meaning, context and the rest.
commentary: Asiddhas tu visesena samudaye vyavasthitah: *not established specifically*, they stand *settled in the aggregate* — a word's default seat is the whole, and the particular application is not fixed by anything in the word. Pradese darsanam tesam artha-prakaranadibhih: their being *seen* in the *specific instance* is by *meaning*, *context* and the rest — the meaning-fixing conditions (3.14.444's arthat prakaranad vapi; 2.316's classical list). So: aggregate by default, particular by context. This is the settled form of 3.14.475's the word never abandons what it took: it holds the whole always and the part when the situation asks.

translation: (481) That indication of the universal, and the accompanier in the actions, or what has a conventional connection there, which is for the most part unmarked — (482) 'aggregate or specific instance': thus, when that is not resorted to, 'brahmana' and the rest operate in the meaning-self without specification.
commentary: (481): yad upavyanjanam jateh sahacari ca karmasu — the *indication* of the *universal* (upavyanjana: 3.14.83's accompanying sign), and the *accompanier* in the *actions*. Tatra va rudha-sambandham yat prayena apalaksitam: or what has a *conventional connection* there (3.14.440's rudha-yoga), which is *for the most part unmarked* — prayena apalaksita: mostly leaving no mark (3.14.140's alaksitah). (482): samudayah pradeso va ity evam tasminn anasrite — *'aggregate or specific instance'*: when *that* — the choice — is *not resorted to*. Arthatmany avisesena vartante brahmanadayah: *'brahmana'* and the rest operate in the *meaning-self* *without specification*. So where nothing forces the choice, the word does not make it: it sits on the meaning-self, unspecified. Avisesena is the resting state of every word, and specification is always something done to it.

translation: And that which, being of like hearing, is seen settled in the aggregate — by it a figurative oneness is apprehended in the specific instance too.
commentary: Yas ca tulya-srutir drstah samudaye vyavasthitah: that which, being of *like hearing* — the same audible word — is *seen settled* in the *aggregate*. Tena upacaritaikatvam pradese 'py upalabhyate: by *it*, a *figurative oneness* is *apprehended* in the *specific instance* too — upacarita-ekatva: a oneness by transfer (3.14.284's upacaryate). So because the word sounds the same on the whole and on the part, one takes the two to be one — figuratively. The identity of whole and part is an effect of the word's identity: the sound is one, and so the thing seems one. This is 3.14.325's reflection in a homelier setting — a likeness of form producing an apparent transfer.

translation: From preparation or from damage, operating in what has unstated measure — the universal-word in 'oil' and the rest is here determined from capacity.
commentary: Samskarad upaghatad va vrtto 'kta-parimanake: from *preparation* or from *damage*, operating in what has *unstated measure* — akta-parimanaka: no quantity said. Tailadau jati-sabdo 'tra samarthyad avasiyate: the *universal-word* in *'oil'* and the rest is *determined* *from capacity*. So 'oil' names a kind and no amount, and whether a given use means the pot of it or the drop is settled by capacity — by whether it was prepared or spoiled, which tells you how much was in question. Mass-terms have no built-in individuation and the context supplies it (3.14.480's artha-prakaranadibhih). The verse is a small, exact observation about a class of nouns the grammar had no separate category for.

translation: In universal-words and quality-words, difference of bodily form is not intended; they rest on mere difference of connection with universal and with quality.
commentary: Na jati-guna-sabdesu murti-bhedo vivaksitah: in *universal-words* and *quality-words*, *difference of bodily form* is *not intended* — murti: the shaped body; and it is avivaksita (3.14.176's device). Te jati-guna-sambandha-bheda-matra-nibandhanah: they rest on *mere difference of connection* with *universal* and with *quality*. Bheda-matra: nothing but the difference in the connecting. So 'cow' and 'white' do not care about bodies: what differentiates their applications is only how the kind or the quality is connected, and the physical individual is not in view. This is why 3.14.484's oil worked — a mass-term has no murti to be differentiated by, and none of these words needed one.

translation: And the designation 'black' and the rest is by qualities operating in all the members; those too, residing in one part, are differentiators of cloths and the rest.
commentary: Krsnadi-vyapadesas ca sarva-avayava-vrttibhih gunaih: the designation *'black'* and the rest is by *qualities* operating in *all the members* — the cloth is black because every thread is. Te 'py ekadesa-sthah patadinam visesakah: *those too*, residing in *one part*, are *differentiators* of *cloths* and the rest — a quality in a single part still differentiates the whole cloth. So the quality qualifies from everywhere and from anywhere: total presence and partial presence both do the job. Which is 3.14.475's law again — the word does not release either application — now for quality-terms.

translation: But when 'cloth' and the rest operate in the cloth's members — then, like 'oil' and the rest, their being universal-words is stated.
commentary: Pata-avayava-vrttas tu yada tatra patadayah: when *'pata'* and the rest operate in the *cloth's members* — one calls a scrap of cloth 'cloth'. Tada tailadivat tesam jati-sabdatvam ucyate: *then*, like *'taila'* and the rest, their being *universal-words* is stated. So 'cloth' behaves as a mass-term when it applies to its own fragments: any piece of cloth is cloth, as any drop of oil is oil (3.14.484). The parallel is sharp — a count-noun turns into a mass-noun when it distributes over parts, and the grammar registers the shift by calling it a jati-sabda. The observation is precise and modern-sounding, and it is 3.14.478's ardha-pippali generalized.

translation: Those whose hearing has exclusion for its purpose — in them difference is not required; in the specific instance or in the aggregate, the quality is the excluder of others.
commentary: Nivrtty-artha srutir yesam bhedas tesv anapeksitah: those whose *hearing* has *exclusion* for its *purpose* — in them *difference* is *not required*: where a word is there to exclude, one need not settle which thing it applies to (3.14.142's yad yad asriyate tat tad anyasya vinivartakam). Pradese samudaye va guno 'nyesam nivartakah: in the *specific instance* *or* in the *aggregate*, the *quality* is the *excluder of others*. So the exclusion works either way — on the part or on the whole, indifferently — because excluding does not require knowing what one is excluding *from*. This is why 3.14.482's avisesena was tolerable: an unspecified word still excludes.

translation: There, the word 'brahmana' operates by the brahmin's studying; for similarity is seen there with the ksatriya's studying and the rest.
commentary: The vati-comparison, resumed from 3.14.431. Brahmana-adhyayanena tatra vartate brahmana-srutih: the word *'brahmana'* — in 'brahmanavat', in the brahmin's manner — operates *by the brahmin's studying*. Sadrsyam tatra drstam hi ksatriya-adhyayanadibhih: for *similarity* is *seen* there with the *ksatriya's studying* and the rest. So the word reaches the brahmin's studying and the comparison is between the two studyings. This is 3.14.443's structure — one action, two seats — and the question 3.14.490 asks is what makes the two studyings alike.

translation: If the word 'brahmana' operated in the brahmin's studying — it would have to be stated by what property the two actions are similar.
commentary: Brahmana-adhyayane vrttir yadi syad brahmana-sruteh: *if* the word *'brahmana'* *operated in* the *brahmin's studying*. Vaktavyam kena dharmena tulyatvam kriyayor iti: it would have to be *stated* *by what property* the *two actions* are *similar*. Vaktavyam: it must be said — and it cannot be (3.14.442: same class, nothing to compare; different class, nothing shared). So locating the word in the action leaves the resemblance groundless. The demand kena dharmena is unanswerable, and 3.14.491 responds by moving the word off the action altogether.

translation: When the word 'brahmana' is said to operate in the studier — then the action residing in the studier is itself what attains causality.
commentary: The resolution. Adhyetari yada vrttir ucyate brahmana-sruteh: when the word *'brahmana'* is said to operate in the *studier* — the person, not the studying: which is what the word means anyway. Nimittatvam tada upaiti kriya eva adhyetari sthita: *then* the *action* *residing in the studier* *itself* attains *causality* — nimittatva (3.14.113's word). So the word sits on the man and the action, seated in him, does the occasioning. 3.14.490's unanswerable question does not arise, because the two actions are never compared directly: each is in its man, and the men are compared. This is 3.14.431's opening claim, arrived at by exhausting the alternative.

translation: In connection with the word 'lion' as expressing mere valour — the genitive would follow after 'Caitra', as in connection with 'valour' and the rest.
commentary: Simha-sabdena sambandhe saurya-matrabhidhayina: in connection with the word *'lion'* as expressing *mere valour* — if 'simha' in 'caitra-simha' just means bravery. Caitrat sasthi prasajyeta yoge sauryadibhir yatha: the *genitive* would *follow* after *'Caitra'*, as in connection with *'valour'* and the rest. So one would have to say 'caitrasya simhah' — Caitra's lion — as one says 'caitrasya sauryam', Caitra's valour: abstract nouns take a genitive possessor. And one does not. So 'simha' does not mean mere valour: it means the animal, and the man is called it (3.14.428's the word standing for the man-meaning). The case-system settles a semantic question in one line.

translation: 'It should be given to the vaisya as to a brahmin' — in such cases, connection with a recipient and the rest is not conceivable in a mere action.
commentary: Brahmanaya iva datavyam vaisyaya ity evam adisu: *'it should be given to the vaisya as to a brahmin'* and the like — the vati-comparison with a dative. Sampradanadi-yogas ca kriya-matre na kalpate: connection with a *recipient* and the rest is *not conceivable* in a *mere action* — sampradana: the karaka of the one given to (3.7's the karakas). So the dative in 'brahmanaya iva' needs a person to be the recipient, and a bare action cannot be one. Hence again the word must be sitting on the man (3.14.491). The karaka-system, like the case-system at 3.14.492, will not let an action stand where a person is wanted.

translation: And from its expressing mere action, the listing of 'vati' among the indeclinables should never be made; since both alternatives are equal.
commentary: Kriya-matrabhidhayitvad avyayesu vater na ca pathah kadacit kartavyah: from its expressing *mere action*, the *listing* of *'vati'* among the *indeclinables* should *never* be *made* — patha: the recitation, the entry in the gana. Tulyau paksav ubhau yatah: since *both alternatives* are *equal*. So: if 'vati' expressed a bare action it would be an avyaya and belong in the list; and it does not, so it should not be listed. And the reason offered is that the two positions are *equal* — tulyau: neither is better. So Bhartrhari declines to list it because the question is undecidable, not because he has decided it. The Vrtti-samuddesa's long examination of the vati-rule ends with a shrug that is also a principle: where the alternatives are equal, do not legislate.

mula_deva: जहाति जातिं द्रव्यं वा तस्मान्नावयवे स्थितः ।
mula_iast: jahāti jātiṃ dravyaṃ vā tasmānnāvayave sthitaḥ |
translation: It abandons the universal or the substance; therefore it does not reside in the member. But since it is the hearing of an action, it stands in the meaning possessing that.
commentary: Jahati jatim dravyam va tasman na avayave sthitah: it *abandons* the *universal* or the *substance*; *therefore* it does *not reside* in the *member* — jahati: lets go (3.14.94's tyaga, the jahat-svartha doctrine). In 'brahmanavat' the word has dropped both what it named and what it meant, so it is not sitting on the compound's member. Kriyayas tu srutir yasmat tadvaty arthe 'vatisthate: *but* since it is the *hearing of an action*, it stands in the meaning *possessing* that. Tadvati arthe: in the *possessor*. So having let go of universal and substance, the word lands on the one who has the action — 3.14.491's conclusion, restated as the word's own movement.

translation: And since here the hearing of the action has the exclusion of non-actions for its purpose — therefore the action-word is cognized in what is indicated by the action.
commentary: Akriyanam nivrtty-artha yatas ca atra kriya-srutih: since here the *hearing of the action* has for its *purpose* the *exclusion of non-actions* — nivrtty-artha: it is there to turn things away (3.14.488's nivrtty-artha sruti). The rule's word 'kriya' is not describing; it is fencing out. Kriya-upalaksite tasmat kriya-sabdah pratiyate: *therefore* the *action-word* is cognized in what is *indicated by the action* — upalaksita: marked out by it, not characterized (3.14.170). So 'kriya' in the rule excludes non-actions and indicates a possessor, and it never means an action at all. The rule's most puzzling word (3.14.434) turns out to be a fence and a pointer.

translation: And since in 'hotavya' and the rest another action, like 'brahmana' and the rest, has to be required — therefore, in the pure meaning, there is no operation of anything.
commentary: Hotavyadisu yasmac ca kriya anya brahmanadivat apeksaniya: in *'hotavya'* and the rest, *another action*, *like 'brahmana'* and the rest, has to be *required* — one must reach for a second action, as one reaches for a second person. Suddhe 'rthe tasmad vrttir na kasyacit: *therefore*, in the *pure meaning*, there is *no operation of anything* — na kasyacit: of nothing whatever. So nothing operates in a bare, unaccompanied meaning. This is 3.14.474's kevalanam tu bhavanam na rupam avadharyate, arriving as the conclusion of the vati-analysis: purity is not a state anything can be in and be spoken of.

translation: Whether the whole or a part is what is resorted to somewhere — that whose operation is in the particular, all call settled in difference.
commentary: Sarvam vapy ekadeso va yasminn asriyate kvacit: *whether* the *whole* or a *part* is *resorted to* somewhere — either way (3.14.475's the word does not release either). Visesa-vrttim tam sarvam ahur bhede vyavasthitam: that whose operation is *in the particular*, *all* call *settled in difference*. So the criterion of being 'in difference' is operating in a particular, regardless of whether the particular is a whole or a part — the whole/part question is orthogonal to the general/particular one. This clears an ambiguity that has been latent since 3.14.475, and it does so by noting that everyone (sarvam ahuh) already uses the criterion this way.

mula_deva: समुच्चयो विकल्पो वा प्रकाराः सर्व एव वा ।
mula_iast: samuccayo vikalpo vā prakārāḥ sarva eva vā |
translation: Accumulation, or alternation, or all the modes — are described as particulars; or else the general, unconstructed.
commentary: Samuccayo vikalpo va prakarah sarva eva va: *accumulation*, or *alternation*, or *all the modes* (3.14.191's senses of the particle). Visesa iti varnyante: are described as *particulars*. Samanyam va avikalpitam: *or* the *general*, *unconstructed* — avikalpita: not built up, not made into an alternative (3.14.144's vikalpita, negated). So the modes of combination are themselves either particulars or an unconstructed general, and there is nothing else they could be. The samuddesa is placing its own machinery on the general-particular scale it has been using throughout: accumulation and alternation are particulars, and what is left when one stops constructing is the general.

mula_deva: न हि ब्राह्मण इत्यत्र भेदः कश्चिदुपाश्रितः ।
mula_iast: na hi brāhmaṇa ityatra bhedaḥ kaścidupāśritaḥ |
translation: For in 'brahmana' no difference is resorted to, nor is any rejected; therefore this stands settled in the aggregate.
commentary: Na hi brahmana ity atra bhedah kascid upasritah apakrto va: in *'brahmana'* *no* difference is *resorted to*, *nor* is any *rejected* — neither taken up nor thrown out: the word simply does not engage the question. Tena ayam samudaye vyavasthitah: *therefore* this stands *settled in the aggregate*. So the default seat (3.14.480's asiddhas tu visesena samudaye vyavasthitah) is where a word rests when it has done nothing about difference at all — not chosen the whole, but not chosen anything, which comes to resting on the whole. Neither upasrita nor apakrta: the word's indifference is its position.

translation: But that in which the action is resorted to — that difference is determined; 'thus', 'otherwise', 'in every way' — in non-employment it does not exist.
commentary: Kriya tv asriyate yasmin sa bhedo 'dhyavasiyate: that in which the *action* is *resorted to* — *that* difference is *determined*: the action's seat is what fixes which particular is meant, where 'brahmana' alone fixed nothing (3.14.500). Tatha anyatha sarvatha ceti aprayoge na vidyate: *'thus'*, *'otherwise'*, *'in every way'* — in *non-employment* it does *not exist*. So the manner-words: unless one of them is *said*, no manner is there. Aprayoge na vidyate — where it is not employed, it does not exist: 3.14.128's what withdraws has no being, applied to modes. The action seats the word, and only an uttered manner-word gives it a manner.

translation: The hearing of the action, conveying in the comparandum the action's operation in the comparans — as it were makes the difference a word-meaning.
commentary: Upamane kriya-vrttim upameye kriya-srutih pratyayayanti: the *hearing of the action*, *conveying* — in the *comparandum* — the *action's operation* in the *comparans*. Bhedasya karoti iva padarthatam: *as it were* *makes* the *difference* a *word-meaning*. Iva: as though. So the vati-word transfers the standard's action onto the compared thing, and in doing so it *seems* to make the difference itself into something meant. But only iva: the difference is not a word-meaning, and the appearance that it is comes from the transfer. This is 3.14.465's finding (the relation is said by no word) protected against the illusion that 'brahmanavat' finally says it.

translation: When the similarity of an operation with an operation is intended — the affix is taught from a word expressing something possessing action.
commentary: Vyaparena eva sadrsye vyaparasya vivaksite: when the *similarity* of an *operation* *with an operation* is *intended* — the case the whole vati-analysis is about. Kriyavad vacanac chabdat pratyayah pratipadyate: the *affix* is *taught* from a word expressing something *possessing action* — kriyavat: an action-*possessor*, not an action. So even where two operations are being compared, the affix attaches to a word for a possessor. The rule never touches an action directly, and 3.14.434's paradox — a nominal stem expressing an action — never arises, because the stem expresses a possessor and the action rides in him (3.14.491).

mula_deva: अध्येता ब्राह्मण इव प्रत्ययो न निवर्तते ॥ ५०४ ॥
mula_iast: adhyetā brāhmaṇa iva pratyayo na nivartate || 504 ||
translation: Even when the similarity of an action-possessor with an action-possessor is desired to be stated — 'the student is like a brahmin' — the affix does not withdraw.
commentary: Kriyavato 'pi sadrsye vaktum iste kriyavata: even when the similarity of an *action-possessor* with an *action-possessor* is desired to be stated. Adhyeta brahmana iva: *'the student is like a brahmin'* — persons compared to persons, no actions in view. Pratyayo na nivartate: the *affix* does *not withdraw*. So the vati-affix serves this case too: it does not require actions to be the compared terms. Between 3.14.503 and this verse the affix's range is fixed — it attaches to possessor-words always, and what is compared may be their actions or themselves. The rule is simpler than the debate about it.

translation: 'He studies similarly' — thus the qualifier is masculine, for the action-possessor; but in the action, the word 'tulya' is neuter.
commentary: A grammatical test, and it is decisive. Adhite tulya ity evam pumllingena visesanam: *'adhite tulyah'* — he studies, similar — the *qualifier* is *masculine*, agreeing with the *action-possessor*: the man. Kriyavati kriyayam tu tulya-sabde napumsakam: but in the *action*, the word *'tulya'* is *neuter* — 'tulyam adhite', he studies similarly, where the neuter agrees with the studying. So the gender of 'tulya' shows which term the similarity is predicated of, and both are available. The language distinguishes what the theorists were arguing about, by one vowel. This is the samuddesa's best instrument: where a doctrine is in doubt, look at the concord.

mula_deva: पुत्रेण तुल्यः कपिल इति वृत्तिः प्रसज्यते ॥ ५०६ ॥
mula_iast: putreṇa tulyaḥ kapila iti vṛttiḥ prasajyate || 506 ||
translation: Even though the base-meaning is specified, from the affix-meaning's not being qualified — 'Kapila is like a son' — the derivation would follow.
commentary: Prakrty-arthe visiste 'pi pratyayartha-avisesanat: even though the *base-meaning* is *specified*, from the *affix-meaning's* *not being qualified*. Putrena tulyah kapila iti vrttih prasajyate: *'Kapila is like a son'* — the *derivation* would *follow*. So one would get 'putravat kapilah' meaning Kapila resembles a son — in what respect, nothing says, because the affix-meaning was left unqualified. The over-generation is real: the affix will attach wherever a comparison is wanted, and it does not require the respect to be specified. This is the vati-rule's looseness, and 3.14.507-508 explain why it is tolerable.

translation: Those actions conventionally connected with a son, intended in the world — by those, 'vati' would come after 'putra', of an action-possessor, in what is similar in quality.
commentary: Yah putre rudha-sambandhah kriya loke vivaksitah: those *actions* *conventionally connected* with a *son*, *intended in the world* — rudha-sambandha (3.14.440's rudha-yoga): what everyone associates with sonhood — obedience, service, care in old age. Tabhih kriyavatah putrad gunatulye vatir bhavet: *by those*, *'vati'* would come after *'putra'*, of an *action-possessor*, in what is *similar in quality*. So 'putravat' means: doing what sons conventionally do. The looseness of 3.14.506 is filled by convention — the respect is not stated because everyone knows it (3.14.415's the moon's beauty and not its eclipses). Loke vivaksitah: what the world means by it.

translation: Although the cause is included in conventional words — yet the accompanying actions, conventionally established, exist like word-meanings.
commentary: Antarbhutam nimittam ca rudhi-sabdesu yady api: although the *cause* is *included* — antarbhuta: swallowed inside (3.14.311's abhyantarikrta) — in *conventional words*: a rudhi-word has digested whatever occasioned it and shows nothing (3.14.55's saptaparna). Kriyas tu sahacarinyo rudhah santi padarthavat: *yet* the *accompanying actions*, *conventionally established*, *exist* *like word-meanings* — padarthavat: as good as meanings. So even in a word whose ground is buried, the associated actions are there and available, functioning as if they were the meaning. This is what makes 3.14.507's convention usable: it is not vague background, it is as determinate as a word-meaning, and it is why 'putravat' conveys something exact.

translation: But if, setting aside the sequence, the qualification of the affix-meaning stands — from the favouring of the principal, from equality, and from the case-ending —
commentary: Kramam tu yadi badhitva pratyayartha-visesanam avatisthate: if, *setting aside* the *sequence* — badhitva: having annulled it (3.14.216's badhaka) — the *qualification of the affix-meaning* *stands*. And three reasons why it might: pradhana-anugrahat — from the *favouring of the principal*; samyat — from *equality*; vibhakteh — from the *case-ending*. So one could let the affix-meaning be qualified out of order, and there would be grounds for it. The verse is a protasis and 3.14.510 supplies what follows — which is a mess.

translation: From the base's not being specified, it would follow in what is similar in action; in 'putra' and the rest, from quality-words, in the reverse of what was stated before.
commentary: The apodosis of 3.14.509, and it is a reductio. Prakrter avisistatvat kriya-tulye prasajyate: from the *base's* *not being specified*, it would *follow* in what is *similar in action*. Putradau guna-sabdebhyah purvoktasya viparyaye: in *'putra'* and the rest, from *quality-words*, in the *reverse* of what was *stated before*. Viparyaya: the inversion. So setting aside the sequence inverts 3.14.506-507's result exactly: the affix would attach to quality-words and mean action-similarity, where before it attached to action-words and meant quality-similarity. Everything comes out backwards. So the sequence must not be set aside — and the argument for keeping it is that abandoning it turns the rule inside out.

translation: 'He goes like a stout man' — there the hearing of the action is external, no occasion for 'vati'; in 'he goes similarly', 'vati' is accepted.
commentary: Sthulena tulyo yati iti bahiranga kriya-srutih: *'he goes like a stout man'* — the *hearing of the action* is *external*: bahiranga (3.7.182's word) — the going is outside the comparison, which is between a man and a stout man. Animittam vateh: *no occasion* for *'vati'* — so 'sthulavat' will not do here. Tulyam yati ity atra isyate vati: in *'he goes similarly'*, *'vati'* *is* accepted. So the affix comes only when the manner of going is what is compared, not when the persons are. The contrast with 3.14.504 is precise: there persons were compared and vati stood, because the actions were latent in them; here the action is stated and external, and vati falls.

translation: By that, two are qualified — that which is a qualifier in one place; for the word 'tulya' requires that property as residing in both.
commentary: Dvayam visesyate tena yad ekatra visesanam: *two* are *qualified* by that which is a *qualifier* *in one place* — the property is stated once and qualifies twice (3.14.386's one is heard, the other is cognized; 3.14.424's sakrc-chrutau). Tulya-sabdo hi tam dharmam ubhaya-stham apeksate: for the word *'tulya'* *requires* that property as *residing in both* — ubhaya-stha: in the two. So 'tulya' is a word that demands a two-seated property and is satisfied by one saying. That is what makes it the comparison-word: it does the doubling itself, and the speaker need not.

translation: If there is one common property of comparans and comparandum — 'tulya' means 'measured out by a balance' — that is proper there.
commentary: Ekah samano dharmas ced upamana-upameyayoh: *if* there is *one common property* of *comparans and comparandum*. Tulaya sammitam tulyam iti tatra upapadyate: *'tulya'* means *'measured out by a tula'* — a *balance*, a pair of scales — and *that* is *proper* there. So the word's own etymology is the argument: 'tulya' is what has been weighed against something, and a balance needs two pans and one weight. The word is built out of the very structure 3.14.512 described. This is Bhartrhari doing what the Nirukta tradition did — reading the doctrine out of the word — and here it works perfectly.

translation: And, being heard in the sutra, that two-seated property is cognized by non-difference; and from its being a general word, an unheard action is not understood.
commentary: Sutre srutas ca dvistho 'sav abhedena pratiyate: *heard in the sutra*, that *two-seated* property is cognized *by non-difference* — the rule states it and one takes it as one (3.14.362's asrita-ekatva). Na ca samanya-sabdatvad asruta gamyate kriya: and from its being a *general word*, an *unheard action* is *not understood*. So a general word does not smuggle in an unstated action: 'tulya' says likeness and does not tell you the respect. Which is why 3.14.506's over-generation was real and why 3.14.507's convention had to be called in. The rule is honest about what it does not supply.

mula_deva: ये धर्मा नियतास्तेषां पुत्रादिशु न विद्यते ॥ ५१५ ॥
mula_iast: ye dharmā niyatāsteṣāṃ putrādiśu na vidyate || 515 ||
translation: And unheard, the properties 'standing in command' and the rest are cognized; those properties which are fixed — of them, none exists in 'putra' and the rest.
commentary: Asrutas ca pratiyante nidesa-sthayitadayah: *unheard*, the properties *'standing in command'* and the rest *are* cognized — some properties do arrive unsaid (3.14.507's rudha-sambandha: what everyone associates). Ye dharma niyatas tesam putradisu na vidyate: those properties which are *fixed* — of *them*, *none* exists in *'putra'* and the rest. Niyata: settled, determinate. So: some unstated properties come along, and none of them is *fixed* — 'putravat' brings a cloud of associations and no definite one. This qualifies 3.14.508's padarthavat: the conventional actions are as good as meanings, and they are not determinate meanings.

translation: Therefore one who has not resorted to an action is not similar to an action-possessor; and in the hearing of the action, that action-possessing too is cognized.
commentary: Anasrita-kriyas tasman na tulyo 'sti kriyavata: therefore one who has *not resorted to an action* is *not similar* to an *action-possessor* — likeness requires both parties to have the action; an actionless thing resembles no agent. Kriyayah sravane sapi kriyavatta pratiyate: and in the *hearing of the action*, that *action-possessing* too is *cognized* — say the action and the possessing comes with it. So the two are inseparable in the hearing: name an action and you have named someone's having it (3.14.474's nothing bare is reachable by a word). Which is why the vati-rule can afford to speak of actions and mean possessors.

translation: And from the provision for two, superiority is stated; from its always expressing non-substance, in the qualification of the affix-meaning.
commentary: Dvayoh pratividhanac ca jyayastvam abhidhiyate: from the *provision for two*, *superiority* is *stated* — jyayastva: the greater merit; where a reading handles both cases it is preferred. Nitya-asattva-abhidhayitvat pratyayartha-visesane: from *always expressing non-substance*, in the *qualification of the affix-meaning*. So the reading that makes the affix-meaning qualified is superior because it covers both cases and because the affix always expresses asattva (3.14.435's tinam asattva-vacitvat). The samuddesa is adjudicating between two readings of the rule on the grounds of coverage — an unusually decisive moment.

translation: Since in the affix there is only the operation, which is non-substantial — therefore the action-hearing has no indicative purpose.
commentary: Asattva-bhuto vyaparah kevalam pratyaye yatah: since in the *affix* there is *only* the *operation*, which is *non-substantial* — the affix carries an activity and no thing (3.14.193's asattva-bhuta, of the particle). Vidyate laksanarthatvam nasti tena kriya-sruteh: *therefore* the *action-hearing* has *no indicative purpose* — laksanarthatva: the pointing-function of 3.14.451 and 3.14.496. So on *this* reading the rule's 'kriya' does not point at a possessor: the affix already has the operation and needs no indication. Which is the opposite of what 3.14.496 concluded — and both readings are on the table, because the samuddesa is showing what each costs.

translation: But from the taking-up of the action-possessor, in the qualification of the base-meaning — where similarity is by mere action, the expressing of non-substance is established.
commentary: Kriyavatas tu grahanat prakrty-artha-visesane: from the *taking-up of the action-possessor*, in the *qualification of the base-meaning* — the other reading: the possessor is what the rule takes up, and it qualifies the *base*. Kriya-matrena tulyatve siddha asattva-abhidhayita: where similarity is by *mere action*, the *expressing of non-substance* is *established*. So this reading too delivers asattva-abhidhayita — the thing 3.14.517 wanted. Both readings reach it, by different routes. The adjudication of 3.14.517 was therefore premature, and the samuddesa is undoing it — which is its habit (3.14.494's tulyau paksav ubhau).

mula_deva: यदा क्रियानिमित्तं तु सादृश्यं स्यात् क्रियावतोः ।
mula_iast: yadā kriyānimittaṃ tu sādṛśyaṃ syāt kriyāvatoḥ |
translation: But when the similarity of two action-possessors has the action for its cause — then, since an action-possessor is what is expressed, there is non-substance-expressing.
commentary: Yada kriya-nimittam tu sadrsyam syat kriyavatoh: when the *similarity* of *two action-possessors* has the *action* for its *cause* — 3.14.503's case: two persons, alike by reason of their actions. Kriyavato 'bhidheyatvat tada adravya-abhidhayita: *then*, since an *action-possessor* is what is *expressed*, there is *non-substance-expressing* — adravya-abhidhayita: it does not express a thing. So even where a possessor is expressed, what is expressed is not a substance: the possessor is reached under the action, and the action is asattva. This closes the sequence 3.14.517-520: every reading of the rule yields asattva, and the affix cannot express a thing however one construes it.

translation: There, the listing of 'vati' among the indeclinables should be made, like 'svar' and the rest; and in 'the student is equal to a brahmin' the affix would occur.
commentary: Avyayesu vateh pathah karyas tatra svaradivat: *there* — on the reading just reached — the *listing* of *'vati'* among the *indeclinables* should be *made*, like *'svar'* and the rest: the svaradi-gana. Brahmanena samo 'dhyeta ity atra ca pratyayo bhavet: and in *'the student is equal to a brahmin'* the *affix* would *occur*. So if the affix always expresses asattva (3.14.520), it belongs in the indeclinable list — reversing 3.14.494's refusal to list it. The samuddesa has argued itself to the opposite conclusion in twenty-seven verses, and it does not conceal the reversal: 3.14.494 declined because the alternatives were equal, and they have stopped being equal.

translation: And co-reference is designated with the vati-meaning; otherwise, for 'tulyam', an unheard sentence-remainder would have to be imagined.
commentary: Samanadhikaranyam ca vaty-arthena apadisyate: *co-reference* is designated with the *vati-meaning* — the affix's sense co-refers with what it attaches to. Tulyam ity anyatha kalpyo vakya-sesa asruto bhavet: *otherwise*, for *'tulyam'*, an *unheard* *sentence-remainder* would have to be *imagined* — vakya-sesa: what must be supplied to make the sentence construe (3.14.463's sopaskara). So the co-reference reading saves one from inventing words: on any other reading, 'tulyam' needs a supplement nobody says. The argument is economy — prefer the reading that requires no invention — and it is the same argument as 3.14.469's, that unemployed elements should be indicated rather than supplied.

translation: And in the similarity of two action-possessors, in the qualification of the affix-meaning — in 'the student is like a student', there is no rule for 'vati'.
commentary: Kriyavatos ca sadrsye pratyayartha-visesane: in the *similarity of two action-possessors*, in the *qualification of the affix-meaning* — the reading of 3.14.517. Adhyetra sadrso 'dhyeta ity atra nasti vater vidhih: in *'the student is like a student'*, there is *no rule* for *'vati'*. So the affix will not attach where both terms are the same kind of agent — because there is nothing to compare (3.14.394's the cause in the cognition 'cow' exists in another cow; 3.14.442's an action of the same class is not compared). The rule's silence here is not a gap: it registers that identical terms make no simile.

translation: That third case which comes by 'with words meaning tulya' — its meaning is not differentiated, even where there is difference, by any of the other case-endings.
commentary: Tulyarthair iti ya tasyas trtiyayah: that *third case* which comes by *'tulyarthaih'* — Panini's rule prescribing the instrumental with words meaning 'similar'. Na bhidyate artho bhede 'pi sarvabhir itarabhir vibhaktibhih: its *meaning* is *not differentiated*, even where there is *difference*, by *all the other* case-endings. So: whatever case the compared thing would have taken in the underlying construction, the 'tulya'-construction puts it in the instrumental, and the meaning does not vary. The claim is strong and 3.14.525-527 proves it by running through the cases one by one.

translation: (525) 'It is eaten as by a brahmin' — 'it is eaten similarly-with a twice-born'. 'He sees, as a brahmin' — 'he sees similarly-with a brahmin'. (526) 'It is known, as by a brahmin' — 'it is known similarly-with a twice-born'. 'Let it be given, as to a brahmin' — 'let it be given similarly-with a brahmin'. (527) 'Study much, as from a brahmin, from a vaisya' — by these and the like, there is no difference at all of the third case.
commentary: The proof of 3.14.524, and it is a demonstration rather than an argument. Five constructions, in five different cases — instrumental (bhujyate brahmanena iva), accusative (pasyati brahmanam iva), instrumental again (brahmanena iva vijnatam), dative (diyatam brahmanaya iva), ablative (brahmanad iva vaisyat) — and each is rendered by *tulyam* with an *instrumental*, and none of the meanings shifts. Ity evam adibhir bhedas trtiyayah na kascana: by these and the like, there is *no difference at all* of the *third case*. So the instrumental prescribed with 'tulya' is case-neutral: it absorbs agent, object, recipient and source alike, and expresses none of them. It is not a karaka-marker at all — it is the mark of the compared term, and the karaka it would otherwise have borne is simply gone. The examples are laid out and one sees it; Bhartrhari adds nothing, which is the right decision.

mula_deva: तुल्यं मधुरयाधीये मात्रा तुल्यं स्मरामि ताम् ।
mula_iast: tulyaṃ madhurayādhīye mātrā tulyaṃ smarāmi tām |
translation: 'I study similarly-with Madhura', 'I remember her similarly-with mother' — how is a construction of similarity of Madhura and of mother to be had?
commentary: The difficulty in 3.14.524's doctrine. Tulyam madhuraya adhiye: *'I study similarly-with Madhura'* — where the underlying sense is 'I study as one does in Madhura': a locative absorbed into the instrumental. Matra tulyam smarami tam: *'I remember her similarly-with mother'* — 'as I remember mother': an object absorbed. Madhurayas ca matus ca katham sadrsya-kalpana: *how* is a *construction of similarity* of *Madhura* and of *mother*? So the instrumental of 3.14.525-527 has swallowed a place and a person, and now literally says that a city and a mother are being compared to something. Katham: how can that be? The absorption has produced nonsense, and 3.14.529 must explain it away.

translation: The recitation whose domain is Madhura, and the remembering whose object is mother — are expressed by the words 'Madhura' and 'mother' by way of non-difference.
commentary: Madhura-visayah pathah: the *recitation* whose *domain* is *Madhura* — the studying done there. Smaranam matr-karmakam: the *remembering* whose *object* is *mother*. Madhura-matr-sabdabhyam abhedena abhidhiyate: are *expressed* by the *words* *'Madhura'* and *'mother'* *by way of non-difference*. So the words do not name the city and the woman: they name the *studying-at-Madhura* and the *remembering-of-mother*, by non-difference — the action and its seat taken as one (3.14.483's upacaritaikatva). So the comparison is between two studyings and two rememberings after all, and 3.14.528's absurdity was an artifact of reading the words as naming their nominal referents.

translation: As the word 'camel' operates in faces resembling a camel's part; and the word 'Madhura' in a palace resembling a house.
commentary: This verse repeats 3.14.430 verbatim, and our source numbers it '430' — the third such doubling in this samuddesa, after 3.14.421 (=121) and 3.14.479 (=179). Here the repetition at least has a context: 3.14.529 has just been about the word 'Madhura' operating in what is not the city, and the camel-and-palace verse illustrates exactly that. Whether the redactor placed it here deliberately or the transmission duplicated it, we print it where the source does.
contested: This and the two verses following it (3.14.531 = 3.14.431, 3.14.532 = 3.14.432) form a block of three consecutive verses repeated from an earlier position, retaining their original numbers 430-432 in our source. Taken with the isolated doublings at 3.14.421 and 3.14.479, the pattern suggests a mechanical fault in the transmission — a repeated exemplar-leaf — rather than authorial repetition. The critical editions should be consulted.

translation: As the similarity of two studyings is designated of the two students — so the substrata of the means are spoken of by properties belonging to the action.
commentary: A verbatim repetition of 3.14.431, numbered '431' in our source (see the note at 3.14.530). Its argument — that an action's properties are predicated of the agents who bear them — is the same one 3.14.529 has just used to dissolve the Madhura-and-mother difficulty, so the block's placement here is at least intelligible. But it adds nothing that 3.14.431 did not already establish, and the retained numbering marks it as the transmission's doing rather than the redactor's.

mula_deva: इवार्थे यच्च वचनं पूर्वसूत्रे च यो विधिः ।
क्रियाशब्दश्रुतौ भेदो न कश्चित् विद्यते तयोः ॥ ४३२ ॥
mula_iast: ivārthe yacca vacanaṃ pūrvasūtre ca yo vidhiḥ |
kriyāśabdaśrutau bhedo na kaścit vidyate tayoḥ || 432 ||
translation: The statement in the sense of 'iva', and the rule in the previous sutra — in the hearing of an action-word there is no difference at all between those two.
commentary: A verbatim repetition of 3.14.432, numbered '432' in our source, and the last of the three-verse block (see 3.14.530). Its subject — the coincidence of the iva-rule and the previous sutra where an action-word is heard — is in fact what 3.14.535-538 are about to take up in detail, so a reader coming to this stretch fresh will find the verse doing useful work here. That does not make it any less a repetition.

translation: Although the fixed adjunct is not employed elsewhere — from non-difference of form, an unknown action is here heard again.
commentary: Yady apy upadhir anyatra niyato na prayujyate: although the *fixed adjunct* is *not employed* elsewhere — the conditioning element that does not appear (3.14.464's na kadacit prayujyate; 3.14.469's aprayuktaih). Rupabhedat tv anirjnata kriya atra sruyate punah: from *non-difference of form*, an *unknown* action is *here* *heard again* — anirjnata: not known, unidentified. So because the forms are alike, an action one cannot identify gets heard a second time. The verse describes the mechanism by which an unemployed adjunct nevertheless makes itself audible: form-identity smuggles it back in (3.14.483's tulya-sruti producing a figurative oneness).

translation: As 'sudara' and the rest, ending in 'kyan' — although there is an affix-meaning, they are cited from the absence of difference.
commentary: Kyan-ante sudaradayah: *'sudara'* and the rest, ending in *'kyan'* — the denominative affix. Saty api pratyayarthatve bhedabhavad udahrtah: *although* there is an *affix-meaning*, they are *cited* from the *absence of difference*. So these forms have an affix that means something, and they are nevertheless cited as cases where no difference obtains — the affix's contribution has vanished into the whole (3.14.44's ekarthibhava). This is the precedent 3.14.533 needed: an affix-meaning present and undetectable is a familiar thing, and the unknown action of 3.14.533 is another instance.
contested: Pada a is transmitted as 'yatha vyutparayr pucchau', which we cannot construe and take to be corrupt beyond safe conjecture. We translate from padas b-d, whose sense is clear. The lost example presumably belonged with the kyan-forms of pada b.

mula_deva: नियमे वाभिधाने वा भिद्यते न क्रियाश्रुतिः ॥ ५३५ ॥
mula_iast: niyame vābhidhāne vā bhidyate na kriyāśrutiḥ || 535 ||
translation: And this being so, the rule for 'vati' here too is established by the previous sutra; whether in restriction or in expression, the action-hearing is not differentiated.
commentary: Evam ca sati purvena siddho 'trapi vater vidhih: this being so, the rule for *'vati'* *here too* is *established by the previous* sutra — the later rule is unnecessary; the earlier one already covers the case. Niyame va abhidhane va bhidyate na kriya-srutih: whether in *restriction* or in *expression*, the *action-hearing* is *not differentiated* — niyama: a rule that restricts what would otherwise be free; abhidhana: one that states. So the 'kriya' in the rule does the same thing on either construal, and the second rule is redundant. This is 3.14.432's finding (= 3.14.532's), now argued rather than asserted.

translation: In the rule for 'iva', the affix whose domain is substance and the rest is stated again; in the previous sutra it is prescribed only for the similarity of actions.
commentary: Ive dravyadi-visayah pratyayah punar ucyate: in the rule for *'iva'*, the affix whose *domain* is *substance* and the rest is *stated again* — punah: over again. Kriyanam eva sadrsye purva-sutre vidhiyate: in the *previous* sutra it is prescribed *only* for the *similarity of actions*. So the two rules divide the field: the earlier covers actions, the later substances and qualities — and if that is right, 3.14.535's redundancy-charge fails, because the later rule has territory of its own. The samuddesa has stated the objection to its own conclusion immediately and is about to test it.

translation: 'Houses as in Madhura', 'white as a brahmin's' — here, for substance and for quality, 'vati' would not come by the previous rule.
commentary: The evidence for 3.14.536. Madhurayam iva grha: *'houses as in Madhura'* — a substance-comparison. Brahmanasya iva panduarah: *'white as a brahmin's'* — a quality-comparison. Ity atra dravya-gunayoh purvena vatir na bhavet: here, for *substance* and *quality*, *'vati'* would *not* come by the *previous* rule — since that rule speaks only of actions. So the later rule is needed, and 3.14.535's economy was false. The two examples are exactly chosen: one substance, one quality, neither with any action in view, both taking 'vati' in ordinary usage.

translation: If the undertaking is not for the sake of the action, there is no purpose in the rule; apart from the taking-up of 'kriya', it is established by the previous rule.
commentary: Arambhasya akriyarthatve narthah yogena vidyate: if the *undertaking* is *not* for the sake of the *action*, there is *no purpose* in the *rule* — arambha: the rule's being framed at all. Rte kriyaya grahanat purva-yogena sidhyati: *apart from* the *taking-up* of *'kriya'*, it is *established* by the *previous* rule. So the later rule earns its place only by its word 'kriya': strike that word out and everything it does is done already. This settles the question 3.14.535-537 opened — the second rule is not redundant, and what saves it is one word. Bhartrhari has been reading Panini this way throughout (3.14.225's artha-grahanam krtam; 3.14.446's atin-grahanam).

translation: The operation of the word 'Madhura' in a part of Madhura has been explained; the word 'brahmana' will state the teeth, which are parts of a brahmin.
commentary: Madhuravayave vrttir vyakhyata madhura-sruteh: the *operation* of the word *'Madhura'* in a *part* of Madhura has been *explained* — 3.14.529-530: the word reaches what is in the city, and what resembles a part of it. Brahmanavayavan dantan vaksyati brahmana-srutih: the word *'brahmana'* *will state* the *teeth*, which are *parts* of a *brahmin* — 'brahmanavat dantah', teeth like a brahmin's: the word reaches the teeth. So the same part-reaching happens with persons: 'brahmana' says the teeth. This is 3.14.487's law (a word distributes over its parts) applied where it is least expected, and it is what licenses 3.14.537's brahmanasya iva panduarah.

mula_deva: न काचिदिवयोगे तु बाह्यात् सम्बन्धिनो विना ।
षष्ठी विधीयते तत्र पूर्वेण प्रत्ययो भवेत् ॥ ५४० ॥
mula_iast: na kācidivayoge tu bāhyāt sambandhino vinā |
ṣaṣṭhī vidhīyate tatra pūrveṇa pratyayo bhavet || 540 ||
translation: But in connection with 'iva' no genitive is prescribed without an external relatum; there the affix would come by the previous rule.
commentary: Na kacid iva-yoge tu bahyat sambandhino vina sasthi vidhiyate: in connection with *'iva'* *no* genitive is *prescribed* *without* an *external relatum* — bahya: outside the construction (3.14.207's word). So 'brahmanasya iva' needs someone outside — whose teeth? — before the genitive can stand. Tatra purvena pratyayo bhavet: *there* the *affix* would come by the *previous* rule. So where the external relatum is absent, one falls back to the earlier rule and gets 'vati' anyway. The two rules interlock rather than compete, and the division of 3.14.536 is confirmed by watching where each one is forced.

translation: With the word 'tulya', an excess of connection arises; there the genitive and the instrumental both exist, for 'tulya' is an expresser.
commentary: Adhikyam tulya-sabdena sambandha upajayate: with the word *'tulya'*, an *excess* of *connection* *arises* — adhikya: something more, a surplus relation. Sasthi-trtiye tatra stah: *there* the *genitive* and the *instrumental* both *exist* — both cases are available with 'tulya', where 3.14.524 said only the instrumental was. Tulya-sabdo hi vacakah: for *'tulya'* is an *expresser* — a vacaka, not a dyotaka. So 'tulya' *says* the likeness, and because it says it, it can govern a case of its own, and a surplus connection appears. The vacaka/dyotaka distinction (3.14.194, 3.14.203) is doing the work again, and it is the samuddesa's most reliable tool.

translation: But in the employment of the word 'iva', without an external relatum, there is no excess in the comparans; an indicator is employed.
commentary: The contrast that 3.14.541 set up. Iva-sabda-prayoge tu bahyat sambandhino vina: in the employment of the word *'iva'*, *without* an *external relatum*. Na adhikyam upamane 'sti: there is *no excess* in the *comparans*. Dyotakah samprayujyate: an *indicator* is *employed* — 'iva' is a dyotaka. So 'iva' brings no surplus because it says nothing: it only reveals what the words already had (3.14.191's the indicator restricts the latent powers). And that is precisely why 'iva' governs no case and 'tulya' governs two. The whole difference between the two comparison-words is that one speaks and one points.

translation: The difference which is here in 'iva' is caused by 'palace' and the rest; in 'tulya', an excess dependent on that domain arises.
commentary: Ive yo vyatireko 'tra sa prasadadi-hetukah: the *difference* which is here in *'iva'* is *caused by* *'palace'* and the rest — 3.14.530's palace resembling a house: whatever differentiation 'iva' seems to carry comes from the words around it, not from itself (3.14.542: it says nothing). Tulye tad-visaya-apeksam adhikyam upajayate: in *'tulya'*, an *excess* *dependent on that domain* *arises*. So 'tulya' generates its own surplus, in dependence on the same domain that 'iva' merely borrows from. The pair is now fully drawn: 'iva' takes all its content from its neighbours; 'tulya' adds. And the long examination of the comparison-words ends here, on the distinction it began with.

translation: 'The ox is equal to a gavaya' — then there would be a derivation; but in 'like a cow' there is no difference to serve, as it were, as a substratum.
commentary: Gavayena samo 'nadvan iti vrttis tada bhavet: *'the ox is equal to a gavaya'* — then there would be a *derivation*: 'gavayavat', since the two are different species and there is something to compare. Na tv asti gaur iva ity atra vyatireka iva asrayah: but in *'like a cow'* — said of an ox — there is *no difference* to serve, *as it were*, as a *substratum*. So the gavaya-comparison stands and the cow-comparison does not, because ox and cow are too close: the difference the comparison needs as its footing is missing (3.14.394's the cause in the cognition 'cow' exists in another cow). Vyatireka iva asrayah: the difference is what the simile *stands on*, and here there is no floor.

translation: But prior to connection with the comparandum, in a difference caused by 'palace' and the rest, 'vati' occurs — not in one caused by the tulya-meaning.
commentary: Upameyena sambandhat prak tu prasadadi-hetuke vyatireke vater bhavah: *prior to* connection with the *comparandum*, in a *difference caused by 'palace'* and the rest (3.14.543's prasadadi-hetukah), there is the *occurrence* of *'vati'*. Na tu tulyartha-hetuke: *not* in one caused by the *tulya-meaning*. So the affix attaches to a difference that the surrounding words made, before the comparandum is even connected — and not to a difference that 'tulya' itself generates. The distinction of 3.14.541-543 is thus doing rule-technical work: 'iva'-differences take vati, 'tulya'-differences do not, because the latter are the word's own surplus and not the situation's.

translation: In connection with the word 'iva' the third case is not prescribed; therefore, abandoning the one under discussion, another case-ending is resorted to.
commentary: Iva-sabdena sambandhe na trtiya vidhiyate: in connection with the word *'iva'*, the *third case* is *not prescribed* — 'iva' governs nothing (3.14.542: it is a dyotaka and brings no surplus, so it can demand no case). Prakrtam tam atas tyaktva vibhakty-antaram asritam: *therefore*, *abandoning* the one *under discussion* — the instrumental of 3.14.524's rule — *another* case-ending is *resorted to*. So with 'iva' one drops the prescribed instrumental and takes whatever case the underlying construction wanted: 'brahmanam iva pasyati', accusative (3.14.525). The whole absorption of 3.14.525-527 was 'tulya''s doing, and 'iva' undoes it.

mula_deva: सप्तम्यपि न तत्रास्ति ज्ञापनार्था तु सा कृता ।
mula_iast: saptamyapi na tatrāsti jñāpanārthā tu sā kṛtā |
translation: The seventh is also not there; but it has been made for the sake of indication; it is desired in the domain of the remainder, among the fixed case-endings.
commentary: Saptamy api na tatra asti: the *seventh* — the locative — is *also not* there. Jnapanartha tu sa krta: but it has been *made* for the sake of *indication* — jnapana: the grammarians' term for a rule that exists to signal something rather than to operate (3.14.130's the elision taught to demarcate a domain). Ista sa sesa-visaye niyatasu vibhaktisu: it is *desired* in the *domain of the remainder*, among the *fixed* case-endings. So the locative in the rule is not prescribing a locative: it is a jnapaka, and what it indicates is that the sesa-domain is where the fixed cases operate. The rule's cases are notation about cases (3.14.78's the science made of its own devices).

translation: But if the case-endings operated in this domain by way of difference — the third alone would show the deviation.
commentary: Yadi tu vyatirekena visaye 'smin vibhaktayah pravarteran: *if* the case-endings *operated* in this *domain* *by way of difference* — each case marking a distinct relation, as cases normally do. Trtiya eva vyabhicaram pradarsayet: the *third alone* would *show* the *deviation* — vyabhicara: straying, the failure of invariable concomitance (3.14.131's word). So the instrumental would be the odd one out: every other case would mark its own relation and the instrumental would be marking all of them (3.14.525-527). The instrumental's absorption is thus visible as an anomaly within the case-system, and the anomaly is the evidence.

mula_deva: बाधिता विनिवर्तेत षष्ठी सा गृह्यते पुनः ॥ ५५० ॥
mula_iast: bādhitā vinivarteta ṣaṣṭhī sā gṛhyate punaḥ || 550 ||
translation: (549) The deviation being thus established, then, without the taking-up of the seventh, the seventh alone would be stated and none of the other case-endings would exist. (550) In the domain of the utter one, the genitive, annulled by the seventh which is there for indication, would withdraw; therefore it is taken up again.
commentary: (549): vyabhicare tatha asiddhe saptami-grahanad vina saptamy eva ucyate sarva na santy anya vibhaktayah — the *deviation* being thus *established*, *without* the *taking-up of the seventh*, the *seventh alone* would be *stated* and *none* of the *other* case-endings would *exist*. So without the locative's explicit mention, the rule would over-reach and swallow every case — the mirror of the instrumental's over-reach at 3.14.548. (550): atyantasya visaye saptamya jnapanarthaya badhita vinivarteta sasthi — in the *domain of the utter one*, the *genitive*, *annulled* by the *seventh* which is there for *indication*, would *withdraw*. Sa grhyate punah: *therefore* it is *taken up again*. So the genitive has to be re-stated to survive the locative's annulling. Three cases, each over-reaching or being over-reached, and each held in place by an explicit mention. The rule is a truce.

translation: But when difference itself is cognized through difference of substratum — then, of the akrti, on the substance-view, there is no difference.
commentary: This verse repeats 3.14.331 verbatim, and our source numbers it '331' — the fifth doubling in this samuddesa (after 3.14.421, 3.14.479, and the block 3.14.530-532). Unlike the 530-532 block, it has no evident bearing on the case-technical argument of 3.14.546-550 that surrounds it, and no bearing on the king-comparisons that follow. Its home is the akrti-analysis of 3.14.329-331. We print it where the source prints it and record the repetition.
contested: This is the fifth verse in the Vrtti-samuddesa which our source repeats verbatim from an earlier position while retaining the earlier number (121/421, 179/479, 430-432/530-532, 331/551). Five such faults in one samuddesa, all of the same kind, point to a defect in this witness rather than to the text. Readers should treat any of these five as unreliable evidence for the karika-count.

translation: From the applicability of 'similar to that', the action-adjunct is established; as in 'the king behaves like a king', when difference is intended.
commentary: Tena tulyam iti prapteh kriyopadhih prasidhyati: from the *applicability* of *'tena tulyam'* — the rule's own words — the *action-adjunct* is *established*. Rajavad vartate raja ity atra bhede vivaksite: as in *'rajavad vartate raja'* — *the king behaves like a king* — when *difference* is *intended*. So the strange sentence is the test-case: a king compared to a king, which 3.14.544 said was impossible for want of a difference. It works only if a difference is *intended* — this actual king against kingship's exemplars. And 3.14.553 names them.

translation: Those kings well-known for kingship, from Prthu down to Yudhisthira — they are the comparans for other rulers of the earth.
commentary: Rajatvena prasiddha ye prthu-prabhrtayo nrpah yudhisthirantah: those kings *well-known* for *kingship* — prasiddha (3.14.360, 3.14.373's word: famous, established) — *from Prthu* — the first king, who levelled the earth — *down to Yudhisthira*. Te 'nyesam upamanam mahiksitam: *they* are the *comparans* for *other* rulers of the earth. So 'rajavad vartate raja' means: he behaves as Prthu and Yudhisthira did. The difference 3.14.552 required is the difference between a living king and the exemplary dead, and the comparison is possible because fame has made a class of them (3.14.373's the comparans exceeds everywhere, from its being well-known).

translation: The difference of comparans and comparandum is made by establishment and non-establishment; since everywhere, the unestablished is compared with the well-known.
commentary: The principle, stated for the last time and in its plainest form. Siddhy-asiddhi-krto bheda upamana-upameyayoh: the *difference* of comparans and comparandum is *made by* *establishment and non-establishment* — 3.14.397's siddhy-asiddhibhyam, and 3.14.398's sampurnatvat: the general becomes a particular by being complete. Sarvatra eva yatah asiddham prasiddhena upamiyate: since *everywhere*, the *unestablished* is *compared with* the *well-known*. Sarvatra eva: everywhere, without exception. So there is no comparison between equals: one term is always famous and the other always is not, and that asymmetry *is* what makes them two. The whole comparison-doctrine rests on fame.

translation: 'His form is like a king's' — when a king alone is intended, it will occur by the second rule, which is not for the sake of the action.
commentary: Rajavad rupam asya iti rajany eva vivaksite: *'his form is like a king's'* — when a *king alone* is *intended*: no action in view, only the man and his bearing. Akriyarthena yogena dvitiyena bhavisyati: it will occur by the *second* rule, which is *not for the sake of action* — 3.14.536-538's division: the earlier rule covers actions, the later substances and qualities. So this case falls to the later rule, and the division holds. The samuddesa is checking its own settlement against a fresh example, and it passes.

translation: When the comparans is not intended, this is stated as having restriction for its purpose; the property is the agent of the action 'befits'; the statement is again for that purpose.
commentary: Upamana-avivaksayam niyamartho 'yam ucyate: when the *comparans* is *not intended*, this is stated as having *restriction* for its purpose — niyama: a rule that narrows rather than enables (3.14.535's niyame va abhidhane va). Dharmo 'rhati-kriya-karta: the *property* is the *agent* of the action *'arhati'* — befits, is worthy of: and what does the befitting is a property, not a person. Tad-artham vacanam punah: the *statement* is *again* for that purpose. So where no comparison is meant, the rule restricts, and what it restricts is which properties may be said to befit. The vati-affix has a second life outside comparison altogether.

translation: 'Like a trained hand' is seen only in the well-known; and 'like a king' too, in a king well-known for kingship.
commentary: Krta-hastavad ity etat prasiddhesv eva drsyate: *'krta-hastavat'* — like a trained hand, said of a skilled archer — is *seen* *only in the well-known*: one says it only of the acknowledged master. Rajatvena prasiddhe ca rajni rajavad ity api: and *'rajavat'* too, in a *king* *well-known for kingship*. So both idioms require their subject to be already famous — the comparison does not confer the standing, it presupposes it. This is 3.14.554's principle at work on the comparandum's side: the compared thing must be established enough to be worth comparing, and only then does fame's surplus have anywhere to land.

translation: Those properties of which utter impossibility is seen in a non-king — they are restricted to a king; the deviating ones are abandoned.
commentary: Arajni yesam dharmanam drsto 'tyantam asambhavah: those properties of which *utter impossibility* is *seen* in a *non-king* — the things nobody but a king can do or be. Te rajani niyamyante: *they* are *restricted* to a *king* — niyamyante: fixed on him. Tyajyante vyabhicarina: the *deviating* ones are *abandoned* — vyabhicarin: what strays, what a non-king might also have (3.14.548's vyabhicara). So 'rajavat' means: with the properties no one but a king could have — and the ones a commoner might share are dropped. The selection is by exclusivity, and 3.14.415's problem (which of the moon's properties does 'moon-faced' take?) here gets a criterion: whatever nothing else could have.

mula_deva: अर्हतेश्च क्रिया कर्त्री या तस्यां वतिरिष्यते ।
mula_iast: arhateśca kriyā kartrī yā tasyāṃ vatiriṣyate |
translation: And that action which is the agent of 'befits' — in it 'vati' is accepted; but not in cases like 'the umbrella befits the king'.
commentary: Arhates ca kriya kartri ya tasyam vatir isyate: that *action* which is the *agent* of *'arhati'* — where an action does the befitting — in *it* *'vati'* is *accepted*: 'rajavad arhati', it befits as it befits a king. Rajanam arhati chatram iti na tv evam adisu: *but not* in *'the umbrella befits the king'* and the like — where the umbrella is the agent and a person the object. So the affix comes only when what befits is an action, not when it is a thing. The distinction is fine and it is the last of the vati-rule's boundaries: the affix lives on actions and their possessors, and a befitting umbrella is neither.

mula_deva: छत्राद्यर्थे तु वचने प्रत्याख्यानं न सम्भवेत् ॥ ५६० ॥
mula_iast: chatrādyarthe tu vacane pratyākhyānaṃ na sambhavet || 560 ||
translation: For the science follows out words that exist and are employed; but if there were a statement in the sense of 'umbrella' and the rest, rejection would not be possible.
commentary: Prayuktanam hi sabdanam sastrena anugamah satam: the *science* *follows out* words that *exist* and are *employed* — prayukta and sat: said, and there. Anugamah: it follows *after* (3.14.81's anugamyate; 3.14.154's akhyayate ca sastrena). Chatrady-arthe tu vacane pratyakhyanam na sambhavet: but if there were a *statement* in the sense of *'umbrella'* and the rest, *rejection* would *not* be possible. So one cannot reject a rule that covers actually-occurring usage: if 'the umbrella befits the king' (3.14.559) were provided for, the provision could not be thrown out. The science's authority over its rules stops where usage begins.

translation: The sutra 'tad-arham' is not undertaken in another grammar; and even in the mere comparison, the construction of difference is possible.
commentary: Tad-arham iti na arabdham sutram vyakaranantare: the sutra *'tad-arham'* is *not undertaken* in *another grammar* — vyakaranantara: a rival system, which manages without the rule. Sambhavaty upamatrapi bhedasya parikalpana: and even in the *mere comparison*, the *construction of difference* is *possible* — parikalpana: a construction (3.14.75's word). So the rule is dispensable: other grammars do without it, and the difference it was meant to secure can be constructed anyway. Bhartrhari cites a rival system's economy against Panini's — rarely, and here without apology.

translation: From knowing the operation of one thing, established in another domain — when possession of that property is intended, difference is constructed by the intellect.
commentary: Ekasya karya-nirjnanat siddhasya visayantare: from *knowing the operation* of *one* thing, *established* in *another domain* — one knows what a king does, and the king belongs to another sphere than this man. Tad-dharmatva-vivaksayam buddhya bhedah prakalpyate: when *possession of that property* is *intended*, *difference* is *constructed* *by the intellect*. Buddhya: by the intellect (3.14.188's buddhya eva; 3.14.15's the intellect is the ordainer of the meaning). So the difference the comparison stands on (3.14.544's asraya) is not found in the things: it is built by the mind when it wants to say that this man has that man's property. This is the thesis the next eight verses unfold, and it is the samuddesa's last great argument.

mula_deva: प्रयोगः सोऽपि चैतस्य विषये विद्यते वतेः ॥ ५६३ ॥
mula_iast: prayogaḥ so'pi caitasya viṣaye vidyate vateḥ || 563 ||
translation: And from this sutra's undertaking, no employment of the word 'iva' obtains; and that too exists in the domain of this 'vati'.
commentary: Sutrarambhan na ca etasmad iva-sabdasya vidyate prayogah: from *this sutra's* *undertaking*, no *employment* of the word *'iva'* obtains — where the rule gives 'vati', 'iva' is not used. So 'py etasya visaye vidyate vateh: and *that too* — 'iva' — *exists* in the *domain* of this *'vati'*. So the two do coexist after all: the affix does not banish the particle from its territory. The observation matters because 3.14.542-546 had drawn them sharply apart, and here they overlap — which is what one should expect of a dyotaka and a vacaka that reveal and say the same thing.

translation: 'Slayer of dasyus, like Indra' — this is employed in a mantra to Indra; elsewhere, when 'as Indra, whose deeds are seen' is intended.
commentary: Dasyu-hendra iva ity etad aindre mantre prayujyate: *'slayer of dasyus, like Indra'* — this is employed in a *mantra to Indra*: the god is compared to himself, in his own hymn. Anyatra drsta-karmendro yatha ity asmin vivaksite: *elsewhere*, when *'as Indra, whose deeds are seen'* is *intended* — of a man, with the god's famous exploits in view. So the same phrase does two quite different things: in the mantra Indra is likened to Indra, and outside it a man is likened to Indra. The first is 3.14.552's rajavad vartate raja — a thing compared to itself — and the Veda does it, which settles that it can be done.

translation: Resorting to a former state, the state which is designated — 'you are like your own self' — there it is thus expressed.
commentary: Purvam avastham asritya ya avastha vyapadisyate: *resorting to* a *former state*, the *state* which is *designated*. Sadrsas tvam tava eva iti tatra evam abhidhiyate: *'you are like your own self'* — *there* it is thus *expressed*. Tava eva sadrsah: like *yourself alone* — the highest praise the language has, and it is a comparison of a thing to itself. And the analysis: what it compares is two *states* of one person, the present to the former, and the difference is temporal. So 3.14.564's self-comparison is not paradoxical: the intellect has divided one thing into its states (3.14.562's buddhya bhedah prakalpyate), and the states are two.

mula_deva: तेनास्मिन् विषये भिन्नमभिन्नं वा न विद्यते ॥ ५६७ ॥
mula_iast: tenāsmin viṣaye bhinnamabhinnaṃ vā na vidyate || 567 ||
translation: (566) Where no other comparans of established difference exists — there the comparandum's own self is divided by one's own cognition. (567) Even the difference which is natural — that too is grounded in cognition; therefore in this domain there is neither the different nor the non-different.
commentary: (566): prasiddha-bhedam yatra anyad upamanam na vidyate — where no *other* comparans of *established difference* exists: nothing outside is good enough, as nothing outside is good enough for Indra. Upameyasya tatra atma sva-buddhya pravibhajyate: *there* the *comparandum's own self* is *divided* *by one's own cognition* — pravibhajyate: is cut apart. So one cuts the thing in two and compares the halves. (567): yo 'pi svabhaviko bhedah so 'pi buddhi-nibandhanah — *even* the difference which is *natural* — svabhavika: belonging to the thing's own nature (3.14.186's word) — *that too* is *grounded in cognition*. Tena asmin visaye bhinnam abhinnam va na vidyate: *therefore*, in *this domain*, *neither* the *different* *nor* the *non-different* *exists*. So the division of Indra from Indra is not a special expedient: *every* difference is the mind's, including the ones that look built-in, and once that is seen, neither difference nor identity is available as a fact about things. This is 3.14.142's na nirupyate given its ground.

translation: Showing Caitra by the difference-causes 'bracelet-wearing' and 'ear-ringed' — one says 'he is such', from grasping a state of the intellect.
commentary: Angadi kundali ca iti darsayan bheda-hetubhih: *showing* by the *difference-causes* — *'bracelet-wearing'*, *'ear-ringed'*: the ornaments by which one picks a man out. Caitram idrsa ity aha: one says of *Caitra*, *'he is such'*. Buddhy-avastha-parigrahat: from *grasping* a *state of the intellect* — buddhy-avastha: a condition of the mind, not of Caitra. So the man is identified by his ornaments and what one has actually grasped is a mental state. Idrsah — *such* — points at a cognition, not a man. This is 3.14.567's thesis in the plainest possible case: even 'that one, with the bracelet' reaches only a buddhy-avastha.

translation: The cognition-self which arises by these words, as it is — the object too comes about in conformity with that cognition.
commentary: Etaih sabdair yathabhutah pratyayatma upajayate: the *cognition-self* which *arises* by *these words*, *as it is* — the words make a cognition, and the cognition has whatever shape they gave it. Tat-pratyaya-anukarena visayo 'py upapadyate: the *object* too *comes about* *in conformity with* that *cognition* — anukara: following after, imitating. So the object *follows* the cognition, and the cognition follows the words: the order of dependence runs word, then thought, then thing. This is 3.14.197's sabdad arthah pratayante — meanings are spread out from the word — and 1.123's no cognition without word, arriving as the conclusion of an argument about jewellery.

translation: By the division of the intellect's states, the difference-operation is cognized; all the meanings of words are, as it were, produced by the intention to speak.
commentary: One of the summit verses of the Vrtti-samuddesa, and of the work.
