Buddhy-avastha-vibhagena bheda-karyam pratiyate: by the *division* of the *intellect's states*, the *difference-operation* is *cognized* — every rule that needs a difference to operate on is fed by the mind's dividing itself (3.14.567's even natural difference is buddhi-nibandhana). Janyanta iva sabdanam arthah sarve vivaksaya: *all* the *meanings* of *words* are, *as it were*, *produced* by the *intention to speak*. Janyante: are *generated*, brought into being. Sarve: all of them. Vivaksaya: by the wish to say. And iva — *as it were* — which is the whole of Bhartrhari's caution and the whole of his boldness: he will not say the world is made by speaking, and he will say it is *as though* made by speaking, and he will not take the iva back. 3.14.197 said the word ordains the differences; this says the wish to speak generates the meanings. Between them the samuddesa's method — analyses are devices, divisions are the mind's, the science stands far off — is not scepticism at all. It is this.

mula_deva: न तत्र कश्चित् सादृश्यं सदपि प्रतिपाद्यते ॥ ५७१ ॥
mula_iast: na tatra kaścit sādṛśyaṃ sadapi pratipādyate || 571 ||
translation: Even where the external object is of the same kind, wherever the cognitions differ — there no one conveys a similarity, though it exists.
commentary: The test of 3.14.570, negative. Tathavidhe 'pi bahye 'rthe: even where the *external object* is *of the same kind* — the things really are alike, out there. Bhidyante yatra buddhayah: wherever the *cognitions* *differ*. Na tatra kascit sadrsyam sad api pratipadyate: *there* *no one* conveys a *similarity*, *though it exists* — sad api: though it is there. So an actual resemblance in the world goes unsaid if the cognitions have not converged: nobody makes the comparison, and nobody could. The external likeness is real and inert. This is as sharp a statement as the work gives of what it means for meanings to be generated by vivaksa: the world's own resemblances do not get into speech unless the mind has already made them.

mula_deva: अत्यन्तं विषये भिन्ने यावत् प्रख्या न भिद्यते ।
mula_iast: atyantaṃ viṣaye bhinne yāvat prakhyā na bhidyate |
translation: Even when the object is utterly different, so long as the presentation is not differentiated — so long does no one's recognition withdraw.
commentary: The converse test, and it is the stronger. Atyantam visaye bhinne: even when the *object* is *utterly different* — atyantam: completely; two different things, out there. Yavat prakhya na bhidyate: so long as the *presentation* is *not differentiated* — prakhya: the showing-forth (3.14.187's eka-prakhya; 3.14.403's jati-bhagasraya prakhya). Na tavat pratyabhijnanam kasyacid vinivartate: *so long* does *no one's* *recognition* *withdraw* — pratyabhijnana: the recognition 'this is that'. So one goes on recognizing a thing as the same across an utter difference, provided the presentation has not split. With 3.14.571 the pair is complete: real likeness without mental likeness conveys nothing, and real difference without mental difference does not stop recognition. The mind, not the world, is where sameness and difference are.

mula_deva: अयमेव तु सूत्रेण भेदो भेदेन दर्शितः ।
mula_iast: ayameva tu sūtreṇa bhedo bhedena darśitaḥ |
translation: This very difference is shown by the sutra by way of difference; the unlearned apprehends what is well-known but hard to know.
commentary: Ayam eva tu sutrena bhedo bhedena darsitah: *this very difference* — the mind-made one of 3.14.562-572 — is *shown* by the *sutra* *by way of difference*: the rule exhibits it as though it were a difference among things. Darsitah: exhibited (3.14.42's bheda-abheda-nidarsanam krtam). Prasiddham api durjnanam abudhah pratipadyate: the *unlearned* *apprehends* what is *well-known* but *hard to know* — prasiddham api durjnanam: familiar and hard: everyone lives by these differences and nobody can say what they are. So the rule is a teaching-device for the abudha (3.14.49's abudhan praty upayas ca vicitrah), exhibiting as objective a difference that is the mind's. The samuddesa's whole account of grammar, in one verse, applied to its own last doctrine.

translation: One who is not always a grammarian speaks like a grammarian; 'speak like a grammarian' — therefore he too is expressed.
commentary: Vaiyakaranavad brute na vaiyakaranah sada: one who is *not always a grammarian* speaks *like a grammarian* — na sada: not always: even a real grammarian is only sometimes grammatical, and so 'vaiyakaranavat' is said of him too. Vaiyakaranavad brusva ity atah so 'py abhidhiyate: *'speak like a grammarian'* — *therefore* *he too* is *expressed*. So one may tell a grammarian to speak like a grammarian, and the injunction is not empty: it asks him to be, now, what he is only sometimes. This is 3.14.565's tava eva sadrsah — like your own self — in a professional key, and there is a joke in it that Bhartrhari, of all people, was entitled to make.

mula_deva: केचित् पुमांसो भाषन्ते स्त्रीवत्, पुंवच्च योषितः ।
mula_iast: kecit pumāṃso bhāṣante strīvat, puṃvacca yoṣitaḥ |
translation: Some men speak like women, and women like men; in the deviation, one's own property too is again taught by that.
commentary: Kecit pumamso bhasante strivat, pumvac ca yositah: *some men* speak *like women*, and *women* *like men* — the vati-affix used of what one is. Vyabhicare sva-dharmo 'pi punas tena upadisyate: in the *deviation*, one's *own property* too is *again* *taught* by that — vyabhicara: straying (3.14.548, 3.14.558's word). So where a man speaks womanishly, the word 'strivat' also teaches what his own property was — you cannot say he speaks like a woman without saying he is not one. The comparison, in marking the straying, re-teaches the norm it strayed from. The observation is exact, and it holds for every 'X-vat' said of someone who is not X.

translation: 'You are like your own self' — what is said in the world; there, another comparans, which was applicable, withdraws.
commentary: 3.14.565's phrase given its logic. Sadrsas tvam tava eva iti loke yad abhidhiyate: *'you are like your own self'* — what is *said in the world*: not a grammarian's construct but the language's own idiom. Upamanantaram tatra prasaktam vinivartate: *there*, *another* comparans, which was *applicable*, *withdraws* — prasakta: it was available, and it goes. So the praise works by *dismissing* every other standard: to say a man is like himself is to say that all the famous exemplars have been considered and found short. The self-comparison is not a failure to find a comparans; it is the refusal of the ones on offer, and the refusal is the compliment.

translation: 'It is fitting, suitable for a king' — in illustrating the meaning; when the comparans is not intended, 'tad-arham' is recited.
commentary: Yuktam aupayikam rajna ity arthasya nidarsane: *'it is fitting, suitable for a king'* — in the *illustration* of the *meaning*: what 'rajarham' comes to. Upamana-avivaksayam tad-arham iti pathyate: when the *comparans* is *not intended*, *'tad-arham'* is *recited* — the sutra's name. So the rule has two employments: with a comparans in view it gives comparison, without one it gives fitness (3.14.556's niyamartho 'yam ucyate). The two are distinguished by vivaksa alone, and Panini gave them one rule — which 3.14.561 noted another grammar does without.

translation: (578) The remainder of 'vati', occasioned incidentally, is stated; from connection with the comparans, 'vati' is cited in this. (579) In the absence of any construction of a principal, from the seeing of a quality-word — in 'vati' after a preverb, the construction of the root-meaning in the root is established.
commentary: (578): prasakta-anuprasaktas tu vati-seso 'bhidhiyate — the *remainder* of 'vati', *occasioned incidentally*: prasakta-anuprasakta, one applicability dragging in another. Upamana-abhisambandhad asmin vatir udahrtah: from *connection with the comparans*, 'vati' is *cited* in this. (579): pradhana-kalpana-abhave guna-sabdasya darsanat — in the *absence* of any *construction of a principal*, from the *seeing of a quality-word*. Upasargad vatau siddha dhatau dhatv-artha-kalpana: in *'vati'* after a *preverb*, the *construction of the root-meaning* in the *root* is *established*. So where no principal is constructed, the root's meaning has to be constructed into the root — which is 3.14.68's roots with imagined selves, and 3.14.276's the preverb's sense not being in the root. Kalpana at every level, exactly as 3.14.75 said.

translation: And in this, by 'its own form', the meaning too is included; being indicated as the form is — therefore the near meaning will be taken.
commentary: Svam rupam iti ca etasminn arthasya api parigrahah: by *'svam rupam'* — Panini's rule that a word in the grammar stands for itself — the *meaning* too is *included*. So the rule that makes a word self-referring does not exclude its sense: the form is meant *and* the meaning comes along. Rupavaj jnapitas tasmad asanno 'rtho grahisyate: being *indicated* as the *form* is, *therefore* the *near* meaning will be *taken*. Asanna: the *nearby* one (3.14.242's asanna eva; 3.14.295's asatti). So one takes the closest sense, not any sense — the same nearness-principle that bounded paryudasa's drift. Even self-reference does not escape it.

translation: The means, produced by the root-meaning as a means — 'made by the root' — thus it is cognized in this sutra.
commentary: Dhatv-arthena upajanitam sadhanatvena sadhanam: the *means*, *produced by the root-meaning* *as a means* — the karaka is generated by the action and generated *as* a karaka: it has no prior standing (3.7.3's the sadhanas as capacities the action confers; 3.14.220's the verb makes object and agent). Dhatuna krtam ity evam asmin sutre pratiyate: *'made by the root'* — thus it is cognized in *this sutra*. So Panini's wording is read as saying that the root makes its means. Krtam: *made*. This is the karaka-doctrine's strongest formulation and Bhartrhari finds it in a rule about affixes.

translation: That word which, its purpose being served, is never employed — its elision is stated, from its non-appearance in the domain.
commentary: Yah sabdas carita-arthatvad atyantam na prayujyate: that word which, its *purpose being served* — carita-artha: having done its work — is *never* employed. Visaye 'darsanat tatra lopas tasya abhidhiyate: from its *non-appearance* in the *domain*, its *elision* is *stated*. So the grammar declares an elision for a word that was never there — the word had already done its job (as a conditioning presence) and was never audible (3.14.468's upadanam prayogesu tasya atyantam na vidyate). Lopa is thus the technical name for a word that finished before it was spoken, and 3.14.128's what withdraws has no being applies: the elision is bookkeeping.

mula_deva: क्रियायां साधने द्रव्ये प्रादयो ये व्यवस्थिताः ।
mula_iast: kriyāyāṃ sādhane dravye prādayo ye vyavasthitāḥ |
translation: Those 'pra' and the rest which are settled in an action, in a means, or in a substance — from those, as expressing substance, 'vati' is prescribed in its own meaning.
commentary: Kriyayam sadhane dravye pradayo ye vyavasthitah: those *'pra'* and the rest — the preverbs — *settled* in an *action*, in a *means*, or in a *substance*: the three places a preverb can lodge. Tebhyah sattva-abhidhayibhyo vatih svarthe vidhiyate: from *those*, as *expressing substance*, *'vati'* is prescribed *in its own meaning* — svarthe: adding nothing (3.14.174's svarthika). So the affix attaches to preverbs when they have gone over to expressing substance, and it adds nothing when it does. The preverb, that most verbal of elements, becomes a substance-word and takes a nominal affix — 3.14.308's nan changing category by composition, happening again.

translation: There, 'pra' and the rest is employed without an affix, along with the meaning; but in the designation by division, the division is constructed.
commentary: Pratyayena vina pradis tatra arthena prayujyate: *there*, *'pra'* and the rest is employed *without an affix*, *with the meaning* — the preverb carries its sense unaided. Bhedena tu samakhyane vibhagah parikalpitah: but in the *designation by division*, the *division* is *constructed* — parikalpita. So the affix of 3.14.583 is not needed and the analysis that requires it is a construct. This is 3.14.35's samudayam upakramya padam tasyam prayujyate, vibhagena samakhyane — having begun with the aggregate, the division is what the designation makes. The samuddesa's oldest finding, in its last technical application.

translation: But if the means were grasped without substance being accepted — there would be no connection with case-endings, as in 'tasil' and the rest.
commentary: Anangikrta-sattvam tu yadi grhyeta sadhanam: if the *means* were *grasped* *without substance being accepted* — a karaka with no thing in it. Vibhaktibhir na yogah syad yatha eva tasiladisu: there would be *no connection with case-endings*, as in *'tasil'* and the rest — the affix giving 'tatah', 'from there': an indeclinable, taking no case. So a substanceless means would be an adverb. The argument is 3.14.163's dravyam eva asrayah in its karaka-form: strip the substance out and the case-system has nothing to attach to, and one is left with indeclinables.

translation: By those who follow, from the recitation, the case-endinglessness of vati-ending forms — for them, in 'udvat', the having-of-a-case-ending must be stated.
commentary: Pathad yair avibhaktitvam vaty-antesv anugamyate: by those who *follow*, from the *recitation* — the gana-list — the *case-endinglessness* of *vati*-ending forms: those who list vati among the avyayas (3.14.521's proposal). Tesam udvat ity atra vaktavya savibhaktita: for *them*, in *'udvat'*, the *having-of-a-case-ending* must be *stated* — vaktavya: they must say it. So the listing costs them an extra rule: 'udvat' does take endings, and if vati-forms are indeclinable by the list, an exception must be written. This is the samuddesa's standing audit again: every doctrine is charged for the rules it adds (3.14.318's the prohibition is purposeless; here, a rule becomes necessary).

translation: 'Nañ' and 'snañ' do not enter into the vati-meaning, from the seeing of 'pumvat'; that irregular form is the annuller of the exception.
commentary: Vaty-artham na avagahete pumvad ity asya darsanat: *'nañ'* and *'snañ'* do *not enter into* the *vati-meaning*, from the *seeing* of *'pumvat'* — the form is there and it has no nañ or snañ in it. Nan-snanav apavadasya badhakam tan nipatanam: that *irregular form* — nipatana: a form Panini simply lists as given — is the *annuller* of the *exception*. So an irregularly-listed form defeats a rule that would otherwise have applied. Nipatana beats apavada: what the grammar simply records overrides what it derives. This is a real principle of the system and it is 3.14.560's in another key — the science follows what is said.

translation: Thus, 'nañ' and 'snañ' surely transgressing the vati-meaning — in their operation the general rule, being annulled, is not possible.
commentary: Evam utkramato nunam vaty-artham nan-snanav iti: thus *'nañ'* and *'snañ'* *surely* *transgressing* the *vati*-meaning — utkramatah: overstepping, going past their bounds. Tayoh pravrttav utsargo badhanan na upapadyate: in *their operation*, the *general rule*, being *annulled*, is *not possible*. Utsarga: the general provision; badhana: its annulment by the special (3.14.216's badhaka). So if the two affixes did overstep into vati's territory, the general rule would be annulled there and could not operate. The consequence is unacceptable, and the argument is the standard one of rule-conflict — special defeats general, and one must therefore keep the special off the general's ground.

mula_deva: नञ्स्नञौ विहितौ येन स योगो नावगाहते ।
mula_iast: nañsnañau vihitau yena sa yogo nāvagāhate |
translation: That rule by which 'nañ' and 'snañ' are prescribed does not enter into the vati-making; for that is justified as a mere indication.
commentary: Nan-snanau vihitau yena sa yogo na avagahate vati-karanam: that *rule* by which *'nañ'* and *'snañ'* are *prescribed* does *not enter into* the *vati-making* — the two rules do not touch. Tad dhi lingam eva samarthyate: for *that* is *justified* as a *mere indication* — linga: a sign, a jnapaka; eva: nothing more. So the rule that seemed to conflict is not a rule at all in the operative sense: it is a marker (3.14.547's jnapanartha; 3.14.469's nirdisyanta upadhayah). The conflict of 3.14.587-588 dissolves because one of the parties was never a competitor. This is the last of the samuddesa's many findings that a rule is notation.

translation: By non-difference, the comparans' falling upon a different meaning; and thus the modification of comparans-words, as with 'anga', is not found.
commentary: Abhedena upamanasya bhinnartha-upanipatita: by *non-difference*, the *comparans'* *falling upon* a *different meaning* — upanipatita: it lands there, uninvited (3.14.428's the word coming to stand for the man-meaning). Uhas tatha upamana-nam angavan na upalabhyate: *thus* the *uha* of *comparans*-words, as with *'anga'*, is *not found*. Uha: the Mimamsakas' term for the modification of a mantra when it is carried to a new rite — one substitutes the new deity's name for the old. So comparans-words do not undergo uha: one cannot swap the standard out and keep the comparison, as one swaps a deity's name. The comparans is welded to its comparison, and the reason is that it landed there by non-difference and not by substitution.

translation: In the gavedhuka oblation, seen in 'go-vikarta' and 'aksa-vapa'; 'these two are like Rudra's beast' — thus the singular is heard.
commentary: The ritual illustration of 3.14.590. Gavedhuke carau drsta go-vikarta-aksa-vapayoh: in the *gavedhuka* oblation — the offering of coix-seed to Rudra — *seen* in *'go-vikarta'* (the ox-carver) and *'aksa-vapa'* (the dice-thrower): two persons the rite excludes. Pasu rudra iva hy etav iti eka-vacana-srutih: *'these two are like Rudra's beast'* — and the *singular* is *heard*: 'pasu', not 'pasu' dual. So two men are compared to Rudra's beast, singular, and the number does not shift to match them. The comparans keeps its own number — which is 3.14.590's point exactly: it does not undergo modification to fit its new employment. And Bhartrhari proves it from the srauta ritual, where the Mimamsakas' uha lives.

mula_deva: कश्यपा इति लोपः स्यात् तथा प्रतिकृतिष्वपि ॥ ५९२ ॥
mula_iast: kaśyapā iti lopaḥ syāt tathā pratikṛtiṣvapi || 592 ||
translation: And from difference of the comparans, the rule of 'añ' would apply in the plural; there would be elision in 'kasyapah'; and likewise in the case of images.
commentary: Upamanasya bhedac ca bahusu syad ano vidhih: from *difference* of the *comparans*, the rule of *'añ'* would apply *in the plural* — if the standard were plural, the patronymic affix would take its plural form. Kasyapa iti lopah syat: there would be *elision* in *'kasyapah'* — the affix would drop, as it does in patronymic plurals. Tatha pratikrtisv api: and *likewise* in the case of *images* — the pratikrti-affix, for statues and likenesses (a comparison-affix of a kind: an image is *like* what it depicts). So the comparans' number is about to become the question, and it runs through both the patronymics and the images.

translation: But thus, from 'treatment as though joined', the singular would occur here; 'luk in the case of a human' would be so stated, for the establishing of a single gender.
commentary: Evam tu yuktavad-bhavad atra eka-vacanam bhavet: from *yuktavad-bhava* — the treatment of a derivative as though it were its analysed phrase (3.14.110, 3.14.318) — the *singular* would occur here. Lum manusye tatha uktam syal lingasya ekasya siddhaye: *'luk in the case of a human'* — the rule for images of men — would be so *stated*, for the *establishing of a single gender*. So the rule about human images exists to fix one gender, and yuktavad-bhava would give the singular. Two provisions, both about number and gender in likenesses, and 3.14.594 asks what governs them.

translation: When the comparanda are different, some one thing operates; in the prescription of the affix there, 'as though joined' is always accepted.
commentary: Upameyesu bhinnesu kincid ekam pravartate: when the *comparanda* are *different*, *some one thing* operates — many things compared, one standard. Pratyayasya vidhau tatra nityam yuktavad isyate: in the *prescription of the affix* there, *yuktavad* is *always* accepted — nityam: invariably. So where one standard serves many, the affix is prescribed on the yuktavad-bhava, and the compound answers to its analysis. The rule is uniform, and 3.14.595 gives the case where it is not.

translation: But when it is settled one by one, for each comparandum — then, by difference of external meaning, the taddhita-ending form is multiplied.
commentary: Yada praty-upameyam tu tad ekaikam avasthitam: when *that* is *settled one by one*, *for each* comparandum — a standard apiece, not one for all. Tada bahyartha-bhedena taddhitantam praciyate: *then*, by *difference of external meaning*, the *taddhita*-ending form is *multiplied* — praciyate: is heaped up, pluralized. So distributed standards give a plural derivative, and a shared standard (3.14.594) gives a singular. The difference is not in the words: it is in whether one standard or many is intended (3.14.570's vivaksaya).

translation: As in the multiplication of aggregates, dvigus have different numbers — in 'five bundles' and the rest — so it would be in the multiplication of case-ending forms.
commentary: Yatha samuha-pracaye dvigunam bhinna-samkhyata: as in the *multiplication of aggregates*, *dvigus* have *different numbers* — the numeral-compounds: 'panca-puli', a five-bundle, and several of them. Panca-pulyadisu tatha sub-anta-pracaye bhavet: *so* it would be in the *multiplication of case-ending forms*. So the precedent is the collective numeral: one can have many five-bundles, and the compound's own number is one while the count of them is another. The comparison-derivative of 3.14.595 does the same. The parallel is well chosen — a dvigu is a collection with its own number, exactly as a comparison-derivative is.

mula_deva: प्रचये भिद्यमाने तु संख्या पूलेषु भिद्यते ।
mula_iast: pracaye bhidyamāne tu saṃkhyā pūleṣu bhidyate |
translation: But when the multiplication is differentiated, the number in the bundles is differentiated; whereas no difference of meaning at all is seen in luk-ending forms.
commentary: Pracaye bhidyamane tu samkhya pulesu bhidyate: when the *multiplication* is *differentiated*, the *number* in the *bundles* is differentiated — many five-bundles means many bundles, and the count runs down into them. Artha-bhedo lubantesu na eva kascana drsyate: whereas *no* difference of meaning *at all* is *seen* in *luk*-ending forms — na eva kascana. So the parallel of 3.14.596 breaks: the dvigu's plurality reaches its members, and the luk-form's does not. The images of Kasyapa (3.14.592) are many and there is no plurality of Kasyapa in them. So the two cases are not alike after all, and Bhartrhari retracts his own analogy in the next verse but one.

translation: Those in which no other word expressing the comparandum is employed — there, others accept a difference of number in the comparans.
commentary: Yesu upameya-vacanah sabdo 'nyo na prayujyate: those in which *no other word* expressing the *comparandum* is *employed* — the compared thing is not separately named: 'kasyapah', images of Kasyapa, with no word for the images. Upamanasya tatra anyaih samkhyaya bheda isyate: *there*, *others* accept a *difference of number* in the *comparans*. So when the compared thing goes unnamed, the number attaches to the standard by default — and that is why 3.14.592's difficulty arose. The plural of 'kasyapah' looks like a plurality of Kasyapas, because there is no other word for it to be a plurality of.

translation: As from the employment of 'jaggery-sesame' and the rest there is a single number; but when 'cooking' and the rest is not employed, a different number is expressed.
commentary: Yatha guda-tiladinam prayogad eka-samkhyata: as from the *employment* of *'guda-tila'* — jaggery-and-sesame, the sweetmeat — and the rest, there is a *single number*: the compound names one thing. Pakader aprayoge tu bhinna-samkhya abhidhiyate: but when *'paka'* — the cooking, the preparation — and the rest is *not employed*, a *different number* is *expressed*. So: name the confection and it is one; leave the preparation-word out and the plural of the ingredients shows through. The number tracks which word is present, not what is in the bowl. Which confirms 3.14.598: an unnamed thing cannot carry a number, and the number goes to whatever is named.

mula_deva: यः संबन्धिगतो भेदः स प्रयोगे प्रतीयते ।
mula_iast: yaḥ saṃbandhigato bhedaḥ sa prayoge pratīyate |
translation: The difference which resides in the relatum is cognized in the employment; therefore the difference of the relata is not understood in the comparandum.
commentary: Yah sambandhi-gato bhedah sa prayoge pratiyate: the *difference* which *resides in the relatum* is cognized *in the employment* — where the related term is *said*, its difference is available. Sambandhinam ato bheda upameye na gamyate: *therefore* the *difference of the relata* is *not understood* in the *comparandum*. So an unsaid relatum's difference does not transfer: the images' plurality is not Kasyapa's, and it does not become his by his being the only word present. This blocks the slide 3.14.598 identified, and it does so by a principle: differences are cognized where they are employed, and nowhere else (3.14.501's aprayoge na vidyate).

translation: Therefore, to avoid the consequence of being a general word — the difference residing in the comparandum is seen in the comparanda.
commentary: Tasmat samanya-sabdatva-prasanga-vinivrttaye: *therefore*, to *avoid* the *consequence* of being a *general word* — if the derivative carried no difference at all, it would be a mere general and would not pick out these images (3.14.425's samanya-vacanah katham). Upameya-gato bhedah upamanesu drsyate: the *difference* residing in the *comparandum* is *seen* in the *comparanda*. So the images' difference is *seen in* the standard — displaced there, because the standard is the only word present. This is the resolution: the plural of 'kasyapah' is the images' plurality, seen in Kasyapa's word, and no Kasyapas are multiplied. Drsyate: it is *seen* there, which is not the same as being there.

translation: Somewhere the comparans of all of them together is heard undivided; or else each one of the differentiated comparanda is compared.
commentary: Upamanam samastanam abhinnam sruyate kvacit: *somewhere* the *comparans* of *all of them together* is *heard undivided* — one standard, for the whole company, and it stays single. Bhinnanam upameyanam ekaikam va upamiyate: *or else* *each one* of the *differentiated comparanda* is *compared*. So two readings, always available: the collective and the distributive. 3.14.594-595 gave the rule for each; here they are set side by side as the two things a comparison can be doing. And 3.14.603-605 give three examples that make the difference vivid.

translation: As 'garuda' is employed with reference to a battle-array, where the elephants' similarity is with one Vainateya.
commentary: Yatha garuda ity etad vyuha-apeksam prayujyate: as *'garuda'* is employed with reference to a *vyuha* — the garuda-vyuha, the eagle-formation of the epic battlefields, in which an army is drawn up in the shape of the bird. Ekena yatra sadrsyam vainateyena hastinam: where the *similarity* of the *elephants* is with *one* *Vainateya* — Garuda, son of Vinata. So: many elephants, one eagle, and the likeness is of the whole formation to the single bird. This is 3.14.602's first reading — samastanam abhinnam, the undivided standard for all together — and the example is perfect: no elephant resembles Garuda, and the army does.

translation: Even of one, differentiated images are cognized together — 'of Kasyapa'; therefore this stands distributively.
commentary: Ekasya api pratiyate bhinna pratikrtih saha: even of *one*, *differentiated images* are cognized *together* — one Kasyapa, many statues, and one hears them all at once in 'kasyapah'. Tena ayam pratyekam avatisthate: *therefore* this stands *distributively* — pratyekam: one by one. So the images-case is 3.14.602's second reading: the plural distributes over the statues, though the man is one. Set against 3.14.603's garuda — one bird, many elephants, collective — the pair shows that the same grammatical shape does both, and only vivaksa tells which.

translation: When 'the clouds are like a mountain' is said, the similarity of all of them together with one mountain is cognized; therefore it is differentiated distributively.
commentary: Meghah saila iva ity ukte samastanam pratiyate sadrsyam girina ekena: when *'the clouds are like a mountain'* is said, the *similarity* of *all together* with *one mountain* is cognized. Pratyekam tena bhidyate: *therefore* it is *differentiated* *distributively*. So this one does both: the massed clouds resemble the single mountain collectively, and the resemblance is then distributed over them. It is 3.14.603 and 3.14.604 at once. The example is the best of the three because it is what one actually sees — a bank of cloud on the horizon, one great shape, and each cloud in it — and it shows the collective and the distributive as two moments of one cognition rather than two doctrines.

translation: The state of having that domain, which depends on 'cha', is not understood, since it is what is to be prescribed; for in 'the crow and the palm-fruit' the indication is well-known.
commentary: Cha-apeksa tad-visayata vidheyatvan na gamyate: the state of *having that domain*, which *depends on 'cha'* — the affix — is *not understood*, since it is *what is to be prescribed*: one cannot use the affix's domain to determine the affix, because the domain is what the rule is for (the circularity of 3.14.78's uhyau). Kaka-taliyam ity atra prasiddham hy upalaksanam: for in *'kakataliya'* — the crow-and-palm-fruit, the crow that alights just as the fruit falls, Sanskrit's byword for coincidence — the *indication* is *well-known*. So the compound is understood by everyone and the affix's domain is not thereby fixed: usage runs ahead of the rule (3.14.560's the science follows). The example is chosen for its fame, which is the point: what everyone knows does not tell the grammarian what to write.

mula_deva: तेन छस्य विधानात् प्राग् व्यपदेशो न विद्यते ॥ ६०७ ॥
mula_iast: tena chasya vidhānāt prāg vyapadeśo na vidyate || 607 ||
translation: And whether the domain is 'rajasva' and the rest or something else is undetermined; therefore, prior to the prescription of 'cha', there is no designation.
commentary: Rajasvadis ca visayah syad anyo va ity aniscitam: whether the *domain* is *'rajasva'* and the rest, *or* something *else*, is *undetermined* — aniscita: unsettled. Tena chasya vidhanat prag vyapadeso na vidyate: *therefore*, *prior to* the *prescription of 'cha'*, there is *no designation* — vyapadesa: the technical naming (3.14.135's word). So the domain cannot be named before the rule that creates it, and the rule cannot be framed before the domain is named. The circle is the same one 3.14.78 exhibited — base and affix each conjectured from the other — and here it closes on a single affix. The Vrtti-samuddesa's method-argument and its rule-technical argument have arrived at the same place.

translation: Here the causality of two 'iva'-meanings is cognized; the member is joined with one, the affix with the other.
commentary: The anatomy of 'kakataliya'. Dvayor iva-arthayor atra nimittatvam pratiyate: the *causality* of *two* *'iva'-meanings* is cognized here — two likenesses, not one. Ekena avayavo yuktah pratyayo 'nyena yujyate: the *member* is joined with *one*, the *affix* with the *other*. So the compound carries a double comparison: one of its members answers to one likeness and the affix to another. This is unlike anything else in the samuddesa — a compound with two comparisons inside it — and 3.14.609 unpacks them.

translation: Caitra's arriving there is like the crow's arriving; but the bandit's falling upon him is like the palm-fruit's falling.
commentary: The two likenesses of 3.14.608, and the image is exact. Caitrasya tatra agamanam kakasya agamanam yatha: *Caitra's arriving* there is *like* the *crow's arriving* — he came for his own reasons, as the crow did. Dasyor abhinipatas tu talasya patanam yatha: but the *bandit's falling upon him* is like the *palm-fruit's falling* — the fruit was ripe and would have dropped anyway. So neither event is for the other's sake, and their meeting is the whole of the misfortune. 'Kakataliya' compounds two independent likenesses and means their coincidence — which no member of it says.

translation: In their conjunction, that other action which arises there — killing and the rest, in the comparandum-meaning — by that the prescription of 'cha' is accepted.
commentary: Sannipate tayor ya anya kriya tatra upajayate: in their *conjunction*, that *other* action which *arises* there — sannipata: the falling-together; and the third action, which neither party performed. Vadhadir upameye 'rthe: *killing* and the rest, in the *comparandum-meaning*. Taya cha-vidhir isyate: *by that* the *prescription of 'cha'* is *accepted*. So what licenses the affix is the action that came out of the meeting and belonged to neither — the killing that nobody did. Upajayate: it *arises*. This is the finest thing in the last stretch: the compound is licensed by an emergent event, and a coincidence is precisely an event with no agent.

translation: The substance-word stands in the inherent action; thus the words 'crow' and 'palm' stand for the arriving and the falling.
commentary: Kriyayam samavetayam dravya-sabdo 'vatisthate: the *substance-word* stands in the *inherent action* — the noun lodges in the action that inheres in what it names. Pata-agamanayoh kaka-tala-sabdau tatha sthitau: the words *'kaka'* and *'tala'* stand for the *falling* and the *arriving*. So 'crow' in the compound means the crow's coming and 'palm' means the fruit's dropping: two nouns, and each has become an event. This is 3.14.529's madhura and matr all over again — the word names the action at its seat, not the seat — and it is why 'kakataliya' can compound two happenings out of two things.

translation: The sentence which is expository is constructed in this way; the sentence of usage in the world is not employed in this way.
commentary: Yad anvakhyayakam vakyam tad evam parikalpyate: the sentence which is *expository* — anvakhyayaka, the grammarian's paraphrase (3.14.71's anvakhyanaya) — is *constructed* in this way: parikalpyate, built. Prayoga-vakyam yal loke tad evam na prayujyate: the *sentence of usage* in the *world* is *not employed* in this way. So everything 3.14.608-611 has just unpacked belongs to the exposition and to nothing anyone says. Nobody utters 'as the crow's arriving and the palm-fruit's falling'; they say 'kakataliyam', and they mean coincidence. The analysis is a construction and the word is a word — 3.14.49's atyanta-bheda, held to the very end.

translation: Of those two whose meeting is seen to be unreasoned, like the crow and the palm-fruit — the derivation of the compound-base is accepted.
commentary: Yayor atarkita praptir drsyate kaka-talavat: of those two whose *meeting* is seen to be *atarkita* — *unreasoned*, not to be argued out, arriving without a why — *like the crow and the palm-fruit*. Tayoh samasa-prakrter vrttir abhyupagamyate: of *those* the *derivation* of the *compound-base* is *accepted*. So the rule's condition is *atarkita prapti*: an unreasoned meeting. This is one of the loveliest phrases in the samuddesa and one of the most precise: a coincidence is exactly a conjunction for which no reasoning is available, and Sanskrit grammar has an affix for it. Drsyate: it is *seen* to be unreasoned — one sees the absence of a reason, and that seeing is what the compound reports.

mula_deva: काकस्य तालेन यथा वधो यस्य तु दस्युना ।
mula_iast: kākasya tālena yathā vadho yasya tu dasyunā |
translation: As the crow's killing by the palm-fruit — but whose killing is by a bandit; there, when the other comparandum is made striking, 'cha' is accepted.
commentary: Kakasya talena yatha vadho yasya tu dasyuna: as the *crow's* killing *by the palm-fruit* — but *whose* killing is *by a bandit*: Caitra's. Tatra citrikrte 'nyasminn upameye cha isyate: *there*, when the *other* comparandum is *made striking*, *'cha'* is *accepted*. Citrikrta: made vivid, made a picture of, rendered arresting. So the affix comes when the compared event has been made *striking* — and that is an aesthetic condition, not a semantic one. Bhartrhari has been describing a figure of speech all along, and here he says what figures are for: 'kakataliya' is used because it makes the thing vivid, and the grammar accommodates the vividness.

translation: And others: 'cañcatka', of the flickering kind, and 'brhatka' — they describe the gem, the frog and the firefly by similarity.
commentary: Cancat-prakaras cancatko brhatka iti ca apare: *'cancatka'*, of the *flickering kind*, and *'brhatka'* — the great-ish one — and *others* say. Mani-manduka-khadyotan sadrsyena pracaksate: they describe the *gem*, the *frog* and the *firefly* *by similarity*. So three small pulsing things, named by a mode-affix, and each of them likened to something. The set is unexpected and Bhartrhari has chosen it: a jewel, a frog, a firefly — things that throb. 3.14.616 says what each is likened to, and the verse is a small poem.

translation: There the firefly is compared by its opening and closing; the frog by its succession of breaths; the gem, its light throbbing.
commentary: One of the most beautiful verses in the kanda, and it is doing analytic work. Tatra unmesa-nimesabhyam khadyota upamiyate: the *firefly* is compared by its *opening and closing* — unmesa-nimesa: the eye's opening and shutting, the standard Sanskrit measure of an instant. Svasa-prabandhair mandukah: the *frog* by its *succession of breaths* — prabandha: an unbroken series; the throat going in and out. Spandamana-prabho manih: the *gem*, its *light throbbing* — spandamana: pulsing, quivering. So the three are alike in *pulsing*, and each is compared by its own kind of pulse. What the mode-affix catches is a rhythm — and 3.14.321's pravrtti said that a thing's general character *is* a rhythm: manifestation, disappearance, persistence. The firefly is that doctrine made visible.

mula_deva: बृहत्क इति तत्रैव मणौ शब्दः प्रयुज्यते ॥ ६१७ ॥
mula_iast: bṛhatka iti tatraiva maṇau śabdaḥ prayujyate || 617 ||
translation: That which, though small, is apprehended as great, its light expanding — there, in that very gem, the word 'brhatka' is employed.
commentary: Pravikasi-prabho 'lpo 'pi mahan ya upalabhyate: that which, *though small*, is *apprehended as great*, its *light expanding* — pravikasin: unfolding, blooming outward. A little stone that throws a great blaze. Brhatka iti tatra eva manau sabdah prayujyate: *there*, in that very *gem*, the word *'brhatka'* is *employed*. So the affix says: great, in the way this small thing is great. It records the discrepancy between the size and the apprehension — and it is the apprehension that the word takes (3.14.569's the object comes about in conformity with the cognition). A jewel is brhatka because of how one sees it.

translation: By some, 'mode' everywhere is accepted as just similarity; by others, the designation 'mode' is accepted even in difference.
commentary: Sadrsyam eva sarvatra prakarah kaiscid isyate: by *some*, *'prakara'* — mode, kind, manner — *everywhere* is accepted as *just similarity*: to be of a kind is to be like. Bhede 'pi tu prakarakhya kaiscid abhyupagamyate: by *others*, the designation *'prakara'* is accepted *even in difference*: a mode may be a mode without any likeness. So the last doctrinal question of the samuddesa: is a kind a resemblance? Two answers, both held, neither adjudicated — the work's manner from first to last (3.14.97's the upayas are unsettled among persons).

translation: Some word expressing a mode stands in what possesses the mode; some, having operated in the mode alone, passes beyond it.
commentary: Prakara-vacanah kascit prakaravati samsthitah: *some* word expressing a *mode* stands in what *possesses* the mode — it lodges in the thing that has the manner. Prakara-matre vartitva kascid tad vyativartate: *some*, having operated in the *mode alone*, *passes beyond* it — vyativartate: goes past, overshoots. So mode-words do two things: some settle on the possessor, some touch the mode and travel on. The pair is 3.14.354-355's two permutations once more, and it is the last time the samuddesa runs its most characteristic move — set two readings side by side, change one word, and let the reader see that nothing decides.

translation: (620) The taking-up of 'similarity' in the sutra is an indication of what is similar; in the avyayibhava of two equals, the word 'saha' is the expresser. (621) That derivation of what expresses the sense of 'as' — in repetition and in similarity: and this difference in the avyayibhava has been shown by way of difference.
commentary: (620): sadrsya-grahanam sutre sadrsasya upalaksanam — the taking-up of *'sadrsya'* in the *sutra* is an *indication* of what is *similar*: the rule's word points at the similar thing and does not describe similarity (3.14.170's upalaksana). Tulyayor avyayibhave saha-sabdo 'bhidhayakah: in the *avyayibhava* of *two equals*, the word *'saha'* is the *expresser* — a vacaka, where 'sadrsya' was only a pointer. (621): vipsa-sadrsyayor vrttir ya yatharthabhidhayinah — the *derivation* of what expresses the sense of *'yatha'*, in *repetition* (vipsa: 'gramam gramam', village by village) and in *similarity*. Sa ca ayam avyayibhave bhedo bhedena darsitah: and this *difference* in the avyayibhava *has been shown by way of difference* — darsitah, exhibited (3.14.573's the sutra shows the difference by way of difference). So the rule exhibits as a real distinction what is a distinction of the exposition. The samuddesa's last word about rules is the one it began with.

mula_deva: यत् तु मूर्तिगतं साम्यं तत् सहेनाभिधीयते ॥ ६२२ ॥
mula_iast: yat tu mūrtigataṃ sāmyaṃ tat sahenābhidhīyate || 622 ||
translation: By some, similarity in 'anu' is accepted as fitness; but that similarity which resides in bodily form is expressed by 'saha'.
commentary: Sadrsyam yogyata kaiscid anav abhyupagamyate: by *some*, *similarity* in *'anu'* is accepted as *yogyata* — *fitness*, aptness, the capacity to go together (2.317's yogyata, one of the sentence's meaning-conditions). So 'anu' marks not likeness but suitability. Yat tu murti-gatam samyam tat sahena abhidhiyate: but that *similarity* which *resides in bodily form* is expressed by *'saha'* — murti: the shaped body (3.14.485's murti-bheda, which jati- and guna-words did not intend). So the division: 'anu' takes fitness, 'saha' takes bodily likeness. Two particles, two kinds of togetherness, and the difference is whether shape is in question.

translation: Even in 'being-thus', similarity is grounded in a state of the intellect; where mere difference is grasped, another thing altogether is expressed.
commentary: The work's thesis, sounded once more before the end. Itthambhave 'pi sadrsyam buddhy-avastha-nibandhanam: even in *itthambhava* — being-thus, the condition of being in a certain way — *similarity* is *grounded in a state of the intellect*: buddhy-avastha-nibandhana, the exact words of 3.14.567 and 3.14.570. Grahane bheda-matrasya tatra anya eva abhidhiyate: where *mere difference* is *grasped*, *another thing altogether* is *expressed*. So even here, at the last technical question, the answer is the one the samuddesa reached at its summit: the likeness is in the mind's condition, and where the mind grasps only difference, something else is being said. Api — *even* — carries it: there is no case left where this does not hold.

translation: 'The Vahika is an ox' — in the duality, similarity is cited as a counter-instance; when 'white' and the rest is present and accomplished, it is said twice with 'Vahika'.
commentary: The last verse of the Vakyapadiya, and Bhartrhari ends on the metaphor every Sanskrit grammarian ends on. Gaur vahika iti dvitve sadrsyam pratyudahrtam: *'gaur vahikah'* — *the Vahika is an ox* — the Bhasya's own example, the countryman called an ox for his dullness. In the *duality*, *similarity* is cited as a *pratyudaharana* — a *counter*-instance: the case brought to show what the rule does *not* cover. Suklad au sati nispanne bahikena dvir ucyate: when *'white'* and the rest is *present and accomplished*, it is said *twice* with *'Vahika'*. So: 'the white Vahika is an ox' — and 'Vahika' is said twice over, once for the man and once inside the metaphor, because the qualifier has fixed him and the ox is still there. The two do not merge and they do not part. It is the right ending. The work opened at 1.1 with the Word-Brahman, without beginning or end, undivided, appearing as many; and it closes on a man who is an ox, where a metaphor is a duality that will not resolve into one thing or two — 3.14.367's vyatikirna, intermingled past separating. And the last thing the samuddesa does is call it a counter-instance: the case the rule does not reach. Bhartrhari has spent six hundred verses showing that the science stands very far off (3.14.74), that the divisions are the intellect's (3.14.570), that the upayas must not be taken for true (3.14.76) — and he ends by pointing at an ordinary insult that his grammar cannot derive, and stopping there.
